2. **No Global Clock** — Each node maintains its own local clock; coordination is achieved via logical clocks (Lamport) or vector clocks.
3. **Independent Failures** — Each node can fail independently without bringing the whole system down (partial failure model).
4. **Heterogeneity** — Varied hardware (x86, ARM), OS (Linux, Windows), and network protocols (TCP/IP, UDP) coexist.
5. **Openness** — Systems expose well-defined interfaces for extension and interoperability.

## 2.2 Architectural Models of Distributed Computing

### 2.2.1 Client–Server Model
- **Server**: Hosts services, resources, or data; listens on a port.
- **Client**: Initiates requests; consumes services.
- **Variants**: Multi-tier (2-tier, 3-tier, n-tier), Thin vs. Fat client.

### 2.2.2 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model
- All nodes are equal; each node can act as both client and server.
- Examples: BitTorrent, Blockchain networks, early Skype.

### 2.2.3 Three-Tier / Multi-Tier Model
- **Presentation Layer** (UI) → **Application Layer** (logic) → **Data Layer** (DB).

### 2.2.4 Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) / Microservices
- Loosely coupled services communicating over standard protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC, SOAP).

## 2.3 Goals of Distributed Computing (KTU High-Yield)

| Goal | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Resource Sharing** | Sharing hardware/software across users | Shared printer, distributed DB |
| **Openness** | Standard interfaces for heterogeneity | POSIX, HTTP, ODBC |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple users simultaneously served | Web servers handling 10K requests |
| **Scalability** | Ability to grow with demand | Adding web servers behind a load balancer |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Continues despite failures | RAID, replicated databases |
| **Transparency** | Hides distribution from user | Distributed file systems (HDFS) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|------|
| **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | Max speedup with $N$ processors; $p$ = parallel fraction | Dimensionless |
| **Parallel Efficiency** | $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | Measures utilization of processors | Dimensionless |
| **Scalability Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} \times 100\%$ | Percentage efficiency | % |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Uptime ratio | % |
| **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Total Jobs}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Jobs completed per unit time | jobs/s |
| **Network Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times D$ | Bits in flight on a link | bits |
| **CAP Theorem Constraint** | Choose 2 of $\{C, A, P\}$ | Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance | Logical |
| **Lamport Logical Clock Update** | $L_i = \max(L_i, L_j) + 1$ | On receiving a message | Scalar |
| **Replication Factor** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Number of Copies}}{\text{Total Nodes}}$ | Data redundancy | Dimensionless |
| **Load Imbalance Factor** | $L_{imb} = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{avg}}{T_{avg}}$ | Measures load distribution | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Amdahl's Law is the **single most important equation** in distributed/parallel computing. It tells engineers that even with infinite processors, the serial portion of a program ($1-p$) becomes the **bottleneck**. This is why modern cloud systems (Spark, MapReduce) aggressively minimize serial code.

## 2.5 Challenges in Distributed Computing

- **Heterogeneity** — Different network, hardware, OS.
- **Openness** — Monolithic vs open systems.
- **Security** — Firewalls, encryption, authentication, authorization.
- **Scalability** — Both vertical (scale up) and horizontal (scale out).
- **Failure Handling** — Detecting, masking, tolerating, recovery.
- **Concurrency** — Shared resource contention.
- **Transparency** — Achieving 8 forms simultaneously is hard.
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Maintaining non-functional requirements (latency, jitter).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (A KTU Examiner's Favourite)

Amdahl's Law gives the theoretical maximum speedup achievable when only a portion $p$ of a task can be parallelized, using $N$ processors.

### Step 1: Define the total task time
Let the total execution time on a single processor be $T(1) = 1$ (normalized to 1 unit for simplicity).

### Step 2: Decompose the task into serial and parallel parts
- **Serial portion** (cannot be parallelized): $1 - p$
- **Parallel portion** (can be parallelized): $p$

### Step 3: Compute the parallel execution time
When $N$ processors share the parallel part, the time spent on the parallel portion becomes $\dfrac{p}{N}$.

### Step 4: Total execution time with $N$ processors
The total time is the sum of the serial part and the parallel part:

$$T(N) = (1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}$$

### Step 5: Define Speedup
Speedup is the ratio of original time to new time:

$$S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$$

### Step 6: Examine the limit as $N \to \infty$
Taking the limit as the number of processors approaches infinity:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

This proves that the **maximum achievable speedup is bounded by** $\dfrac{1}{1 - p}$, **independent of the number of processors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If 90% of a program is parallelizable ($p = 0.9$), the **absolute maximum speedup** is $1 / (1 - 0.9) = 10\times$, even with infinite processors. This is why software refactoring to reduce serial code is critical.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Numerical Type)

**Problem:** A distributed application has 75% parallelizable code. Compute the speedup when using 8 processors. Also calculate the parallel efficiency.

**Given:** $p = 0.75$, $N = 8$

**Step 1: Apply Amdahl's Law**
Substitute into the speedup formula:

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{8}}$$

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375}$$

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.91$$

**Step 2: Compute Parallel Efficiency**

$$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{N} = \frac{2.91}{8} \approx 0.364 = 36.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with 8 processors, we only get ~2.91× speedup, and the efficiency is just 36.4%. This clearly illustrates the **serial bottleneck problem** in distributed computing.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Parallel Computing | Distributed Computing | Cloud Computing |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Coupling** | Tight | Loose | Very Loose |
| **Memory** | Shared (mostly) | Distributed | Virtualized/Abstracted |
| **Network** | High-speed bus / interconnect | LAN/WAN | Internet |
| **Synchronization** | Clock-based | Logical clocks (Lamport) | Eventual consistency |
| **Failure Mode** | Whole system | Partial failure | Multi-tenant failure |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Moderate | Massive (elastic) |
| **Resource Type** | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Virtualized |
| **Typical Use** | HPC, simulations | Cluster/Grid/Web apps | SaaS, IaaS, PaaS |
| **Cost** | High CAPEX | Moderate CAPEX | OPEX-based (pay-per-use) |
| **Examples** | MPI on supercomputers | Google FS, Hadoop | AWS EC2, Azure, GCP |

## 3.4 Logical Clock Update — Worked Walkthrough (Lamport's Algorithm)

The Lamport Logical Clock ensures causal ordering of events in a distributed system with no global clock.

**Rules:**
1. Each process $P_i$ maintains an integer counter $L_i$, initially $L_i = 0$.
2. On an **internal event** at $P_i$: $L_i = L_i + 1$.
3. On **sending** a message $m$ from $P_i$ to $P_j$: increment $L_i$, then timestamp $m$ with $L_i$.
4. On **receiving** a message $m$ at $P_j$ with timestamp $T_m$:

$$L_j = \max(L_j, T_m) + 1$$

**Example Walkthrough:**
- $P_1$ executes locally: $L_1 = 1$.
- $P_1$ sends message to $P_2$ with timestamp $1$: $L_1 = 2$.
- $P_2$ receives message: $L_2 = \max(0, 1) + 1 = 2$.
- $P_2$ sends reply with timestamp $2$: $L_2 = 3$.
- $P_1$ receives reply: $L_1 = \max(2, 2) + 1 = 3$.

The total ordering preserves causality.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Distributed System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientA[Client A]
        appA[Application Layer]
    end
    subgraph clientB[Client B]
        appB[Application Layer]
    end
    subgraph clientC[Client C]
        appC[Application Layer]
    end
    subgraph networkLayer[Network Layer TCP/IP HTTP REST]
        nw[Network Fabric Switches Routers]
    end
    subgraph middlewareLayer[Middleware Layer]
        rpc[Remote Procedure Call]
        mom[Message Oriented Middleware]
        obj[Object Broker RMI CORBA]
    end
    subgraph serverPool[Server Pool Distributed Nodes]
        s1[Node 1 App DB]
        s2[Node 2 App DB]
        s3[Node 3 App DB]
        s4[Node 4 App DB]
    end
    appA --> nw
    appB --> nw
    appC --> nw
    nw --> rpc
    nw --> mom
    nw --> obj
    rpc --> s1
    mom --> s2
    obj --> s3
    rpc --> s4
    s1 <-.->|Replication| s2
    s2 <-.->|Replication| s3
    s3 <-.->|Replication| s4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Clients interact only with the **middleware** (RPC, MOM, Object Brokers). Middleware abstracts away the heterogeneity of underlying nodes. Nodes replicate data among themselves for fault tolerance. This is the canonical layered architecture of a distributed system.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User A]:::user --> proxy1[Proxy Layer]
    userB[User B]:::user --> proxy1
    userC[User C]:::user --> proxy1
    proxy1 -->|Load Balanced Request| auth[Authentication Module]
    auth -->|Authorized| sched[Scheduler Dispatcher]
    sched --> n1[Compute Node N1]:::node
    sched --> n2[Compute Node N2]:::node
    sched --> n3[Compute Node N3]:::node
    n1 --> ds[Distributed Storage]
    n2 --> ds
    n3 --> ds
    ds -->|Result Aggregation| sched
    sched -->|Final Response| proxy1
    proxy1 --> userA
    classDef user fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Master–Worker Distributed Computation Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    master[Master Node Job Coordinator]:::master
    w1[Worker 1]:::worker
    w2[Worker 2]:::worker
    w3[Worker 3]:::worker
    w4[Worker 4]:::worker
    master -->|Task Chunk 1| w1
    master -->|Task Chunk 2| w2
    master -->|Task Chunk 3| w3
    master -->|Task Chunk 4| w4
    w1 -->|Result 1| master
    w2 -->|Result 2| master
    w3 -->|Result 3| master
    w4 -->|Result 4| master
    classDef master fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef worker fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition:** This is the **MapReduce / Spark master-worker model** in essence. The master dissects the problem into sub-tasks, distributes them, and aggregates results. This is the backbone of **Hadoop MapReduce**, **Apache Spark**, and most cloud-based data analytics platforms.

## 4.4 Transparency Layers in a Distributed System (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userL[User Application]:::top
    accessT[Access Transparency]:::layer
    locT[Location Transparency]:::layer
    migT[Migration Transparency]:::layer
    relT[Relocation Transparency]:::layer
    repT[Replication Transparency]:::layer
    conT[Concurrency Transparency]:::layer
    failT[Failure Transparency]:::layer
    perT[Persistence Transparency]:::layer
    hardwareL[Heterogeneous Hardware Network OS]:::hw
    userL --> accessT
    accessT --> locT
    locT --> migT
    migT --> relT
    relT --> repT
    repT --> conT
    conT --> failT
    failT --> perT
    perT --> hardwareL
    classDef top fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef layer fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 1, 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a distributed system. List any four characteristics of distributed systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system. The components are interconnected via a network and communicate through message passing.

**Four characteristics:**
1. **Concurrency** — multiple processes run simultaneously.
2. **No global clock** — coordination via logical clocks.
3. **Independent failures** — partial failure model.
4. **Heterogeneity** — varied hardware, OS, and protocols.

*(Examiner note: Any 4 valid characteristics accepted; 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for characteristics.)*

---

### Question 2 (Module 1, 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between tightly coupled and loosely coupled distributed systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Tightly Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Memory** | Shared memory | Distributed memory |
| **Communication** | High-speed bus | Network-based |
| **Speed** | Very high | Moderate |
| **Coupling** | Strong | Weak |
| **Example** | SMP, multi-core CPUs | Cluster, Grid, Cloud |

*(3 marks — 1 for concept of each, 1 for tabular differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the various architectural models of distributed systems with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major challenges faced in designing distributed systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The major architectural models of distributed systems are:

1. **Client–Server Model**
   - Centralized server hosts services; clients send requests and receive responses.
   - Variants: 2-tier, 3-tier, n-tier.
   - Example: Web browser (client) ↔ Web server.

2. **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model**
   - All nodes are equal; no central server.
   - Types: Structured (DHT-based like Chord) and Unstructured (Gnutella).
   - Example: BitTorrent, blockchain networks.

3. **Multi-Tier / Three-Tier Model**
   - Presentation tier (UI) → Logic tier (business) → Data tier (DB).
   - Used in modern enterprise apps.

4. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**
   - Services communicate via standard protocols (SOAP, REST).
   - Foundation for **microservices** and **cloud-native apps**.

*(Diagrams expected: minimum one schematic for client-server and P2P; 1–2 marks reserved for diagrams.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

Major challenges in designing distributed systems:

1. **Heterogeneity** — Diverse hardware, OS, networks, programming languages.
2. **Openness** — Need for standardized interfaces and protocols.
3. **Security** — Confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorization across networks.
4. **Scalability** — Cost-effective growth in users, data, and transactions.
5. **Failure Handling** — Detecting, masking, tolerating, and recovering from partial failures.
6. **Concurrency** — Multiple clients accessing shared resources simultaneously → race conditions.
7. **Transparency** — Achieving 8 transparencies without performance penalty.
8. **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Maintaining latency, throughput, jitter constraints.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the client–server architecture. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Amdahl's Law? A distributed application has 80% parallel code. Calculate the speedup obtained with 16 processors. Also find the maximum achievable speedup. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Client–Server Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Client 1]:::cl --> s[Server]:::sv
    c2[Client 2]:::cl --> s
    c3[Client 3]:::cl --> s
    s -->|Response| c1
    s -->|Response| c2
    s -->|Response| c3
    classDef cl fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef sv fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Advantages:**
- Centralized management, easy maintenance.
- Strong security control on server.
- Scalable through server clustering.

**Disadvantages:**
- Single point of failure.
- Server becomes bottleneck under high load.
- Higher cost for powerful server hardware.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$$

**Given:** $p = 0.80$, $N = 16$

**Step 1: Compute the parallel part divided by N**

$$\frac{p}{N} = \frac{0.80}{16} = 0.05$$

**Step 2: Add the serial part**

$$(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N} = 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.25$$

**Step 3: Compute the speedup**

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0$$

**Step 4: Compute maximum speedup (as $N \to \infty$)**

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

**Result:** With 16 processors, speedup = **4×**; maximum theoretical speedup = **5×**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly **forget the serial part $(1-p)$** while writing Amdahl's formula — this loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. Many confuse **speedup with efficiency** — efficiency = speedup / $N$, a separate calculation.
> 3. In diagrams, students must **label the server, clients, request, and response arrows** explicitly. Unlabeled arrows = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. Avoid writing "distributed system is a network of computers" — too vague. Use Tanenbaum-style precise definition to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: A distributed system is a collection of independent computers appearing as one coherent system (Tanenbaum).
- **Key Characteristics**: Concurrency, no global clock, independent failures, heterogeneity, openness.
- **Architectural Models**: Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Multi-Tier, SOA/Microservices.
- **8 Forms of Transparency**: Access, Location, Migration, Relocation, Replication, Concurrency, Failure, Persistence.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$; max speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
- **Efficiency**: $E(N) = S(N) / N$.
- **CAP Theorem**: Choose any 2 of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.
- **Lamport's Logical Clock**: On receive, $L_i = \max(L_i, T_m) + 1$.
- **Middleware**: RPC, MOM, Object Brokers (RMI, CORBA).
- **Goal Hierarchy**: Resource sharing → Openness → Concurrency → Scalability → Fault tolerance → Transparency.
- **Challenges**: Heterogeneity, security, scalability, failure handling, concurrency, QoS, transparency trade-offs.
- **Parallel vs Distributed**: Parallel = tightly coupled + shared memory; Distributed = loosely coupled + message passing.
- **Master–Worker Pattern**: Backbone of MapReduce, Spark, Hadoop.
- **High-Yield Numericals**: Amdahl's Law problems (expect 7-mark computation questions).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utility Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Utility Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Utility Computing** is a service-provisioning model in which computing resources—such as compute power, storage, applications, networking, and infrastructure services—are packaged, metered, and delivered to consumers on a **demand-driven, pay-per-use basis**, analogous to traditional public utilities such as electricity, water, or natural gas.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Cloud Computing, Utility Computing represents the foundational conceptual model from which modern **Cloud Computing** has evolved. It emphasizes the abstraction of underlying IT infrastructure so that end-users consume resources as a transparent, metered commodity rather than owning and managing physical hardware.

### Key Terminology Mapping

| Term | Meaning in Utility Computing Context |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Resource Metering** | Continuous measurement of consumption (CPU-hours, GB stored, I/O ops) |
| **Pay-Per-Use (PPU)** | Billing aligned strictly with actual consumption |
| **Elasticity** | Dynamic scaling of resources in response to load |
| **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** | Contractual guarantee of uptime, performance, and availability |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant resource sharing model |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Utility Computing exactly like your home's Electricity Connection.**

When you need electricity at home, you do **not** build your own power plant. You simply plug into the grid (KSEB in Kerala, for example), and:

1. **You Pay Only for What You Consume** — billed per "unit" (kWh) on your monthly bill.
2. **It is Always Available** — you can turn the switch on any time, 24x7x365.
3. **It Scales Automatically** — switch on one bulb or ten air-conditioners; the grid handles the load.
4. **You Don't Maintain Infrastructure** — wires, transformers, generators are owned by the utility provider.
5. **You Pay for the Service, Not the Hardware** — there is no upfront capital cost for a power plant.

**Utility Computing applies the exact same philosophy to IT resources** — instead of buying servers, you rent compute. Instead of buying hard disks, you rent storage. Instead of buying software licenses permanently, you rent them by the hour. The "electricity grid" is replaced by the "Cloud Data Center", and your "electricity bill" is your **cloud bill** (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP invoices).

### The Core Conceptual Shift

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **From CAPEX to OPEX**: Utility Computing shifts IT spending from **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** — buying hardware upfront — to **Operational Expenditure (OPEX)** — paying for usage as an ongoing operating cost. This is the most critical paradigm shift introduced by Utility Computing.

### Standard Metrics Used in Utility Computing

- **Compute** → measured in **vCPU-hours** or **EC2 instance-hours**
- **Storage** → measured in **GB-month** or **TB-month**
- **Bandwidth** → measured in **GB transferred** or **Mbps**
- **Requests** → measured in **per 1,000 API calls** (e.g., AWS Lambda)
- **Availability Target** → standard metric is **99.9% ("three nines")** or **99.99% ("four nines")** per month

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Utility Computing

The architecture of Utility Computing rests on five foundational pillars. Each pillar has a "Why" (rationale) and a "How" (mechanism):

### Pillar 1: On-Demand Self-Service
- **Why?** Eliminates human interaction with the provider for resource provisioning, enabling rapid deployment.
- **How?** The user interacts with a self-service portal or API; resources (VMs, storage) are provisioned automatically by orchestration software (e.g., OpenStack, VMware vRealize).

### Pillar 2: Resource Pooling (Multi-Tenancy)
- **Why?** Maximizes hardware utilization and economic efficiency by sharing physical resources across many tenants.
- **How?** Virtualization (Hypervisors like KVM, Xen, Hyper-V) partitions one physical server into multiple isolated Virtual Machines (VMs), each assigned to a different tenant.

### Pillar 3: Rapid Elasticity
- **Why?** Workloads are dynamic; a system must scale out (add resources) under load and scale in (remove resources) when idle to optimize cost.
- **How?** Auto-scaling groups monitor metrics (CPU > 70%) and trigger the launch or termination of VM instances programmatically.

### Pillar 4: Measured Service (Metering)
- **Why?** Fair billing and capacity planning require accurate, transparent measurement of resource consumption.
- **How?** Metering agents (e.g., CloudWatch, Ceilometer) collect usage data at the hypervisor level and feed it to the billing system.

### Pillar 5: Pay-Per-Use Pricing
- **Why?** Aligns cost with value received; no waste, no upfront investment.
- **How?** Tiered pricing models — Reserved Instances (cheaper, committed), On-Demand (flexible, premium), Spot Instances (cheapest, interruptible).

## 2.2 Utility Computing vs. Related Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Distinction** — KTU frequently tests the difference between Utility, Grid, and Cloud Computing.

| Parameter | Utility Computing | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Goal** | Metered IT-as-a-service | Distributed problem-solving | On-demand, elastic, pooled resources |
| **Resource Scope** | Compute, storage, apps | Mainly compute cycles | Compute, storage, platform, software |
| **Billing Model** | Pay-per-use (utility-style) | Often free / shared | Pay-per-use (inherits from utility) |
| **Virtualization** | Optional but typical | Rare | Mandatory and central |
| **Audience** | Business + Enterprise | Academic / Scientific | Universal (B2B, B2C, B2G) |
| **Relationship** | **Parent concept** | Sibling / parallel concept | **Direct descendant** of Utility Computing |

## 2.3 The Utility Computing Stack (Layered Architecture)

Utility Computing is delivered through a layered stack. Each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer beneath it.

| Layer | Service Type | What is Provided | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L5 — Application** | SaaS | Ready-to-use software | Gmail, Microsoft 365 |
| **L4 — Platform** | PaaS | Runtime + Middleware + DB | Heroku, Google App Engine |
| **L3 — Software Infra** | IaaS | VMs, Storage, Network | AWS EC2, Azure VM |
| **L2 — Physical Infra** | Data Center | Servers, Racks, Power, Cooling | AWS Region, Azure Datacenter |
| **L1 — Hardware** | Raw Resources | CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC | Intel Xeon, Samsung SSD |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Metering & Billing Math)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Break Rule**: All absolute values use `\vert` notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Monthly Cost** | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \times U_i) \times (1 + T)$ | $R_i$ = unit rate, $U_i$ = units used, $T$ = tax | Final customer invoice |
| **Effective Cost per Request** | $C_{req} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{req}}$ | $N_{req}$ = total requests served | API pricing optimization |
| **Availability Uptime %** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures (hrs), MTTR = Mean Time To Repair (hrs) | SLA compliance calculation |
| **Annual SLA Downtime** | $D_{annual} = 8760 \times (1 - A)$ | $A$ as decimal (e.g., 0.999), result in hours | Capacity planning |
| **Cost per vCPU-hour** | $C_{vCPU} = \dfrac{C_{server}}{v \times h \times 24 \times 30}$ | $C_{server}$ = server cost, $v$ = vCPUs, $h$ = hours billed | Internal TCO analysis |
| **Storage Cost (GB-month)** | $C_{storage} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB\text{-}month}$ | $S_{GB}$ = storage in GB, $P$ = price per GB-month | Backup/storage billing |
| **Elasticity Headroom Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{R_{max} - R_{avg}}{R_{avg}}$ | $R_{max}$ = peak resources, $R_{avg}$ = average | Right-sizing workloads |
| **Tenancy Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{tenants}}{N_{servers}}$ | Tenants per physical server | Data center efficiency |
| **Return on Investment (Cloud)** | $ROI = \dfrac{(C_{on\text{-}prem} - C_{cloud})}{C_{cloud}} \times 100$ | Both costs annualized | Migration business case |
| **Mean Cost per Tenant** | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k$ | $C_k$ = cost of tenant $k$, $N$ = total tenants | Fairness analysis |

### Standard Engineering Constants

- **Hours per year** = **8,760** (used in SLA calculations)
- **Minutes per month (avg)** = **43,200** (used in "four nines" SLA: 4.32 min downtime)
- **Standard SLA tiers** = **99.9%** (8.77 hrs/yr), **99.99%** (52.6 min/yr), **99.999%** (5.26 min/yr)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Utility Computing underpins virtually every modern IT delivery model:

- **Enterprise IT** — Companies like Netflix migrated entirely off owned data centers to AWS, paying purely for streaming-driven usage.
- **Telecommunications** — Vodafone, Jio use utility models to deliver virtualized network functions (NFV).
- **Scientific Research** — CERN's Large Hadron Collider generates petabytes of data; utility storage makes this affordable.
- **Startups** — Indian startups (Flipkart, Ola in early days) leveraged utility AWS/Azure to avoid capital investment in servers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Step Mandate**: Every algebraic step is shown in full. No skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SLA Downtime Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A cloud provider advertises a monthly availability SLA of **99.95%**. Compute the maximum permitted downtime per month (in minutes) and verify it is consistent with the annual downtime budget.

### Step 1: Convert SLA percentage to a decimal fraction.

$$
A = 99.95\% = 0.9995
$$

*Textual Logic:* The percentage is divided by 100 to obtain the proportion of time the service is "up".

### Step 2: Compute the downtime fraction per month.

$$
D_{fraction} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

*Textual Logic:* If 99.95% of the month is "up", the remaining fraction represents allowable "down" time.

### Step 3: Convert fraction to total minutes in a standard month.

$$
M_{month} = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hrs/day} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 43{,}200 \text{ min}
$$

*Textual Logic:* A 30-day month is the de-facto industry standard for SLA accounting (some providers use 31 or 28-day months, but 30 is the KTU convention).

### Step 4: Compute permitted monthly downtime in minutes.

$$
D_{minutes} = M_{month} \times D_{fraction} = 43{,}200 \times 0.0005
$$

### Step 5: Perform the multiplication explicitly.

$$
D_{minutes} = 43{,}200 \times \frac{5}{10{,}000} = \frac{216{,}000}{10{,}000} = 21.6 \text{ min}
$$

*Textual Logic:* Therefore, a 99.95% SLA permits a maximum of **21.6 minutes** of downtime per month.

### Step 6: Cross-check against the annual budget.

$$
A_{annual} = 0.9995^{12} \approx 0.99401 \text{ (i.e., 99.401\% per year)}
$$

$$
D_{annual} = 8760 \times (1 - 0.9995^{12}) \approx 8760 \times 0.00599 \approx 52.47 \text{ hrs/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check**: 12 months × 21.6 min = **259.2 min/year** = **4.32 hours/year**. This is more conservative than the compounded 52.47 hours because the per-month SLA is non-compounding (a 21.6-min outage one month is forgiven next month). Both perspectives are valid and KTU accepts either.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Utility Computing Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

**Problem Statement:** A startup needs 100 vCPUs of compute. Option A (On-Premise) requires buying 2 servers at **₹8,00,000 each** (each has 64 vCPUs, 5-year life). Option B (Cloud) costs **₹4 per vCPU-hour** with 24×7 usage. Compute the 5-year TCO for each and identify the break-even point.

### Step 1: On-Premise Capital Cost (CAPEX).

$$
C_{capex} = 2 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

*Textual Logic:* 2 physical servers × per-server price = total upfront spend.

### Step 2: On-Premise Annual Power + Cooling Cost (assume 30% of CAPEX/yr).

$$
C_{opex\_annual} = 0.30 \times 16{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

*Textual Logic:* Industry rule of thumb: power, cooling, and admin costs equal ~30% of hardware cost annually.

### Step 3: On-Premise 5-Year TCO.

$$
TCO_{onprem} = 16{,}00{,}000 + (5 \times 4{,}80{,}000) = 16{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

*Textual Logic:* CAPEX is paid once at year 0; OPEX is paid yearly for 5 years.

### Step 4: Cloud Compute vCPU-hours per year.

$$
H_{year} = 100 \text{ vCPUs} \times 24 \text{ hrs/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 8{,}76{,}000 \text{ vCPU-hr/yr}
$$

*Textual Logic:* 100 vCPUs running continuously = 100 vCPU-hours per hour = 876,000 per year.

### Step 5: Cloud Annual Cost.

$$
C_{cloud\_annual} = 8{,}76{,}000 \times 4 = 35{,}04{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

### Step 6: Cloud 5-Year TCO.

$$
TCO_{cloud} = 5 \times 35{,}04{,}000 = 1{,}75{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 7: Compute Savings.

$$
\text{Savings} = 1{,}75{,}20{,}000 - 40{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}35{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: Under steady 24×7 utilization, **on-premise is significantly cheaper**. However, if utilization drops below ~25%, cloud becomes the winner. This is the core insight of **Right-Sizing** in Utility Computing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Break-Even Utilization

**Problem Statement:** Find the utilization percentage $U$ (as a fraction of 24×7) at which On-Premise TCO = Cloud TCO over 5 years.

### Step 1: Set up the equation.

$$
TCO_{onprem} = TCO_{cloud} = 5 \times (8{,}76{,}000 \times 4) \times U
$$

*Textual Logic:* Cloud cost scales linearly with utilization $U \in [0, 1]$.

### Step 2: Substitute and solve.

$$
40{,}00{,}000 = 5 \times 35{,}04{,}000 \times U
$$

$$
40{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}75{,}20{,}000 \times U
$$

$$
U = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{1{,}75{,}20{,}000} = \frac{40}{175.2} \approx 0.2283
$$

### Step 3: Convert to percentage.

$$
U_{\%} = 0.2283 \times 100 = 22.83\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: Below **~23% utilization**, Utility/Cloud Computing is economically superior. Above it, on-premise wins. This is why the cloud is favored by **bursty workloads** (e-commerce, OTT streaming, exam portals) and resisted by **steady-state high-utilization workloads** (e.g., on-prem HPC clusters).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Utility Computing Trade-offs

| Dimension | Benefit | Challenge / Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost** | No CAPEX; pay for what you use | Uncontrolled spend if not monitored (bill shock) |
| **Scalability** | Elastic up/down in minutes | Cold-start latency during scale-out |
| **Availability** | Multi-AZ redundancy built-in | Vendor lock-in to a single provider |
| **Security** | Provider invests in cutting-edge security | Data leaves your physical perimeter |
| **Maintenance** | Patches, updates are provider's job | Less direct control over infrastructure |
| **Performance** | Latest hardware (e.g., Graviton, GPU instances) | Network latency to remote data centers |
| **Compliance** | Provider offers compliance certifications | Shared-responsibility model is complex |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Utility Computing Conceptual Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER TENANT] -->|Self-Service Portal| B[UTILITY COMPUTING PLATFORM]
    B --> C[METERING AND BILLING ENGINE]
    B --> D[RESOURCE POOL VIRTUALIZATION LAYER]
    B --> E[SLA MONITORING DASHBOARD]
    D --> F[PHYSICAL SERVER FARM]
    D --> G[STORAGE ARRAY TIER]
    D --> H[NETWORK FABRIC]
    C --> I[MONTHLY ITEMIZED INVOICE]
    E --> J[ALERTS AND REMEDIATION]
    F --> K[DATA CENTER POWER AND COOLING]
    G --> K
    H --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The user (left) interacts only with the Utility Platform. The platform hides the physical data center (right) and exposes three feedback channels: Billing, SLA, and Resource Pool. This is the **abstraction principle** that defines Utility Computing.

## 4.2 Layered Architecture Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5 APPLICATION SAAS"] --> L4["LAYER 4 PLATFORM PAAS"]
    L4 --> L3["LAYER 3 INFRASTRUCTURE IAAS"]
    L3 --> L2["LAYER 2 VIRTUALIZATION HYPERVISOR"]
    L2 --> L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL HARDWARE SERVERS STORAGE NETWORK"]
    L5 -. Metering .-> M["METERING SERVICE LAYER CROSS CUTTING"]
    L4 -. Metering .-> M
    L3 -. Metering .-> M
    M --> B["BILLING AND REPORTING MODULE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading**: The Metering Service is **cross-cutting** — it taps into every layer to capture consumption data. This is why utility pricing is granular and accurate.

## 4.3 Request Lifecycle in a Utility Computing System

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Portal
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant V as Virtualization
    participant M as Metering Agent
    participant B as Billing
    U->>P: Request 4 vCPUs for 30 days
    P->>O: Forward provisioning request
    O->>V: Allocate 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM
    V-->>O: VM created with ID VM-7821
    O-->>P: Provisioning confirmed
    P-->>U: Resource ready, login credentials
    loop Every 1 hour
        M->>V: Sample utilization
        V-->>M: 4 vCPU-hr, 16 GB-hr
        M->>B: Log usage record
    end
    U->>P: Terminate instance
    P->>O: Release VM
    O->>V: Deallocate resources
    V-->>O: VM destroyed
    O-->>B: Final bill = sum of hourly records
    B-->>U: Invoice generated
```

## 4.4 Utility Computing vs. On-Premise Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Compute Resources] --> B{Average Utilization?}
    B -->|Less than 23 percent| C[Choose Utility or Cloud]
    B -->|More than 23 percent| D{Data Sensitivity?}
    D -->|Low to Medium| E[Reserved Instances Cloud]
    D -->|High or Regulated| F[Choose On-Premise Private]
    C --> G[Design with Auto-Scaling]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Plan 5-Year Hardware Refresh]
    G --> I[End: Cost-Optimized Architecture]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Use Case**: This decision flow is a direct application of the break-even derivation in Section 3.3. It is highly likely to appear as a 7-mark or 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Utility Computing. List any four of its key characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Utility Computing** is a service-provisioning model in which computing resources such as compute, storage, and applications are delivered to consumers on a **metered, pay-per-use basis**, similar to traditional public utilities like electricity or water.

**Four Key Characteristics:**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources are provisioned automatically without human intervention.
2. **Resource Pooling** — Physical resources serve multiple tenants via virtualization.
3. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capacity can be scaled up or down quickly to match demand.
4. **Measured Service** — Usage is continuously monitored, metered, and reported for billing.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Utility Computing and Grid Computing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Utility Computing | Grid Computing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Deliver IT as a metered service | Solve large-scale distributed problems |
| **Billing** | Pay-per-use | Usually free or subsidized (academic) |
| **Virtualization** | Core enabling technology | Optional / not central |
| **Workload** | Business + Enterprise apps | Scientific / research jobs |
| **Resource Type** | Compute, storage, apps | Mostly compute cycles |

> Utility Computing is **commercial**; Grid Computing is **collaborative-academic**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Provision**: KTU 2024 ESE allows the student to choose EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**. Both are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the layered architecture of Utility Computing with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in delivering metered IT services.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A cloud provider offers 99.99% monthly availability. Calculate the maximum permitted downtime per month (in minutes) and per year (in hours). Also discuss the impact of this SLA on customer trust and business continuity.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Layered Architecture of Utility Computing** consists of five layers, each abstracting the complexity of the layer below it.

| Layer | Function | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Application (SaaS)** | Delivers ready-to-use software | Gmail, Salesforce |
| **Platform (PaaS)** | Provides runtime, middleware, databases | Google App Engine |
| **Infrastructure (IaaS)** | Provides virtual machines, storage, network | AWS EC2, S3 |
| **Virtualization** | Partitions physical hardware into isolated VMs | KVM, VMware ESXi |
| **Physical Hardware** | Servers, storage arrays, network switches | Data center racks |

**Role Discussion:**

- The **Hardware layer** provides raw physical capacity (CPU, RAM, Disk).
- The **Virtualization layer** enables **multi-tenancy** by creating isolated VMs, the foundation of resource pooling.
- The **IaaS layer** exposes these VMs through APIs, allowing programmatic provisioning.
- The **PaaS layer** adds developer tools (databases, message queues) so customers can deploy apps without managing OS.
- The **SaaS layer** delivers finished applications consumable by end-users.

A **cross-cutting Metering Service** captures consumption at every layer, feeding the Billing module. This is what makes the model "utility-like".

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2)** — A five-layer stack with a vertical metering plane.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Convert SLA to fraction.**

$$
A = 99.99\% = 0.9999
$$

**Step 2: Compute monthly downtime fraction.**

$$
D_{fraction} = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

**Step 3: Compute minutes per month.**

$$
M = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}200 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4: Compute permitted monthly downtime.**

$$
D_{month} = 43{,}200 \times 0.0001 = 4.32 \text{ min/month}
$$

**Step 5: Compute annual downtime (non-compounded).**

$$
D_{annual} = 4.32 \times 12 = 51.84 \text{ min/yr} \approx 0.864 \text{ hours/yr}
$$

**Step 6: Discuss business impact.**

- A **"four nines" SLA (99.99%)** permits only **~52 minutes of downtime per year**.
- This level of reliability is **essential for mission-critical workloads** such as banking, healthcare, and e-commerce, where even brief outages cause revenue loss and reputational damage.
- SLAs build **customer trust** by providing financial credits if the provider fails to meet the target.
- They drive **business continuity** by forcing providers to architect for redundancy (multi-AZ, multi-region).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare and contrast Utility Computing, Grid Computing, and Cloud Computing. Highlight which concept is the parent and which are the descendants.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A startup requires 200 vCPUs for a project. On-premise CAPEX = ₹30,00,000 (5-year life) with 25% annual OPEX. Cloud cost = ₹3.5 per vCPU-hour, expected utilization 15%. Compute the 5-year TCO for both and recommend a choice with justification.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Utility Computing | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Metered IT service | Distributed compute | Elastic, on-demand resources |
| **Billing** | Pay-per-use | Often free | Pay-per-use |
| **Virtualization** | Core | Not central | Mandatory |
| **Audience** | Business | Academic | Universal |
| **Evolution** | **Parent** concept | Parallel concept | **Descendant** of Utility |

**Conceptual Hierarchy:**

$$
\text{Utility Computing} \xrightarrow{\text{evolved into}} \text{Cloud Computing}
$$

$$
\text{Grid Computing} \;\parallel\; \text{Utility Computing} \quad (\text{independent trajectory})
$$

- **Utility Computing** introduced the **pay-per-use** philosophy.
- **Cloud Computing** inherited this and added **self-service, elasticity, and virtualization at scale**.
- **Grid Computing** emerged separately to solve **scientific distributed computing** problems; it later influenced cloud architecture but is not its ancestor.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute On-Premise 5-Year TCO.**

$$
C_{opex\_yr} = 0.25 \times 30{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
TCO_{onprem} = 30{,}00{,}000 + (5 \times 7{,}50{,}000) = 30{,}00{,}000 + 37{,}50{,}000 = 67{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Cloud vCPU-hours per year (adjusted for 15% utilization).**

$$
H_{yr} = 200 \times 8760 \times 0.15 = 2{,}62{,}800 \text{ vCPU-hr/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Cloud Annual Cost.**

$$
C_{cloud\_yr} = 2{,}62{,}800 \times 3.5 = 9{,}19{,}800 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Cloud 5-Year TCO.**

$$
TCO_{cloud} = 5 \times 9{,}19{,}800 = 45{,}99{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5: Compare and Decide.**

$$
TCO_{cloud} - TCO_{onprem} = 45{,}99{,}000 - 67{,}50{,}000 = -21{,}51{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation**: **Choose Cloud**. Savings = **₹21,51,000** over 5 years. At only 15% utilization, the cloud's OPEX model dominates. Additionally, the startup avoids upfront capital risk, gains instant elasticity, and can shut down resources when the project ends — flexibility that on-premise CAPEX cannot match.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> - **Do NOT** confuse Utility Computing with Cloud Computing. Utility is the **parent philosophy**; Cloud is the **modern implementation**. Many students lose 2-3 marks by stating "Utility Computing is another name for Cloud Computing" — this is **incorrect**.
> - **Do NOT** confuse **MTBF/MTTR** with **SLA availability**. MTBF/MTTR are component-level reliability metrics; SLA is a contractual uptime promise.
> - **Always show units** in your final answer for numeric questions (minutes, hours, ₹). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units.
> - **Always state the formula first**, then substitute, then compute. Inverting this order costs method marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Utility Computing = IT delivered like electricity** — metered, pay-per-use, on-demand, elastic.
- **Core shift**: From **CAPEX** (buy hardware) → **OPEX** (rent by the hour).
- **Five pillars**: On-Demand Self-Service, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service, Pay-Per-Use.
- **Parent of Cloud Computing**; sibling of Grid Computing.
- **Virtualization** is the technological foundation; **Metering** is the billing foundation.
- **Standard SLA metrics**: 99.9% = 8.77 hrs/yr, 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr, 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr.
- **Break-even utilization** for cloud vs. on-premise is typically **~20-25%**; below that, cloud wins.
- **Total Cost** = Sum of (Rate × Usage) + Tax; **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- **Layered stack**: Hardware → Virtualization → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, with a cross-cutting metering plane.
- **KTU-favorite question pattern**: Numerical SLA downtime calculation (3-7 marks) + conceptual comparison (7 marks).
- **Always remember the three standard references** for Utility Computing roots: **IBM On-Demand Computing (2002)**, **Sun Microsystems "The Network is the Computer" (1984)**, and **HP Utility Computing Initiative**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing. NIST Reference Model - Basic Terminology and Concepts. Cloud Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing. NIST Reference Model - Basic Terminology and Concepts. Cloud Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (NIST SP 500-145)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official NIST Definition of Cloud Computing (SP 500-145, Sept 2011):**
>
> *"Cloud computing is a model for enabling **ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access** to a shared pool of **configurable computing resources** (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with **minimal management effort or service provider interaction**."*

This definition, formulated by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**, is the **globally accepted canonical definition** referenced by KTU, IEEE, ISO/IEC, and the academic community. Every KTU answer on cloud fundamentals MUST anchor to this definition.

---

## 1.2 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing

These are the **non-negotiable, defining traits** that distinguish a true cloud from traditional hosting, virtualization, or out-sourcing.

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Essence |
|---|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **On-Demand Self-Service** | Provision resources automatically, without human interaction with the provider |
| 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Accessible over the network via standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST, etc.) used by thin/thick clients |
| 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Provider's resources are pooled to serve **multiple tenants** using a multi-tenant model |
| 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities can be **scaled out/in rapidly** (and sometimes automatically) with demand |
| 5 | **Measured Service** | Cloud systems **automatically control and optimize resource use** by leveraging metering at an appropriate level (storage, CPU, bandwidth, active users) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electricity Grid"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Cloud = Electricity Grid**
>
> Imagine a city 100 years ago. Every factory had its own diesel generator. Then the **electricity grid** arrived. Today, you simply plug in a switch — you do **not** know which power plant generated your electricity, you don't manage the generator, you pay only for the units (kWh) you consume, and you can scale consumption up or down instantly.
>
> **Cloud computing is the "electricity grid" of computing:**
> - **On-demand self-service** = flipping a switch
> - **Broad network access** = every socket in every building
> - **Resource pooling** = one huge grid serving all factories
> - **Rapid elasticity** = plug in more appliances, no paperwork
> - **Measured service** = the monthly kWh bill

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Why NIST?

Before NIST's 2011 publication, **every vendor had its own definition** of "cloud" (Amazon, Google, IBM, Microsoft all differed). This created chaos for buyers and researchers.

**NIST stepped in as the neutral federal authority** (under the U.S. Department of Commerce) and produced two foundational documents:

1. **NIST SP 500-145** — *"The NIST Definition of Cloud Computing"* (Sept 2011) — defines the model.
2. **NIST SP 500-292** — *"NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture"* (Sept 2011) — defines the reference model/architecture.

KTU 2024 Scheme **directly tests** both, with **emphasis on SP 500-145** in Module 1.

---

## 1.5 Basic Terminology Cheat-Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glossary of Terms You Must Know (KTU Module-1 syllabus):**
>
> - **Cloud** — A pool of abstract, virtualized, dynamically scalable managed resources (compute, storage, services) delivered over the Internet.
> - **Provider** — The entity that owns and operates the cloud infrastructure (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP).
> - **Consumer** — The person/organization that uses the cloud services.
> - **Tenancy / Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple consumers sharing the same physical resources, logically isolated.
> - **Workload** — A collection of application/software components that consume cloud resources.
> - **Virtualization** — The abstraction layer that enables resource pooling and multi-tenancy.
> - **Provisioning** — The act of deploying and configuring cloud resources.
> - **Elasticity** — The ability to scale resources up/down in near real-time.
> - **Scalability** — The ability to handle increasing (or decreasing) load by adding/removing resources.
> - **Metering / Pay-per-use** — Billing based on actual consumption (utility model).
> - **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — Formal contract defining QoS guarantees (uptime %, latency, etc.).

---

## 1.6 How a Cloud Differs from a Traditional Data Centre

| Attribute | Traditional Data Centre | Cloud Computing |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Acquisition | Months (procurement, install) | Minutes (self-service portal) |
| Capacity Planning | Manual, over-provisioned | Elastic, on-demand |
| Cost Model | CapEx-heavy (buy hardware) | OpEx (pay-as-you-go) |
| Utilization | Often **15–20 %** | High (multi-tenant pooling) |
| Management | Customer manages everything | Provider manages infrastructure |
| Location | Known, fixed | Abstracted / location-transparent |

---

## 1.7 Pedagogical Roadmap of This Note

This note covers **three syllabus pillars** in Module 1:

1. The **Definition of Cloud Computing** (above).
2. The **NIST Reference Model** (actors, roles, architecture) — covered in Section 4 with diagrams.
3. The **Essential Characteristics** — covered in detail in Section 2.

You will be exam-ready on:
- ✅ Writing the NIST definition **verbatim** (2 marks is common in Part A).
- ✅ Listing & explaining **all 5 characteristics** (favourite 7- or 14-mark question).
- ✅ Drawing / labelling the **NIST Reference Architecture diagram** (Part B, 14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Five Essential Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Any single essential characteristic appearing in a 7-mark question requires **definition + technical elaboration + a real-world example**. Memorizing a one-liner will fetch only 2 out of 7 marks.

---

### Characteristic 1 — On-Demand Self-Service

**Definition (KTU standard phrasing):** A consumer can **unilaterally provision** computing capabilities, such as server time and network storage, **automatically without requiring human interaction** with each service provider.

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**
- The **why**: Removes human bottleneck — provisioning must not require opening a ticket or making a phone call.
- The **how**: Implemented through a **self-service portal / API / CLI** backed by an orchestration engine (e.g., AWS Console, Azure Portal, OpenStack Horizon).
- The **consumer experience**: Log in → click "Launch Instance" → VM is ready in seconds.

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- Enables **DevOps automation** — CI/CD pipelines can spin up ephemeral test environments without ops team intervention.
- Reduces provisioning latency from **days/weeks → seconds**.

---

### Characteristic 2 — Broad Network Access

**Definition:** Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through **standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms** (e.g., mobile phones, laptops, PDAs, workstations).

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why**: Cloud is not bound to a particular device or OS — must be platform-agnostic.
- **How**: Relies on **standard, open protocols** — HTTP/HTTPS, REST APIs, SOAP, gRPC.
- Thick clients (full apps) **and** thin clients (browsers) both work.

**Real-world utility:**
- Enables **BYOD (Bring Your Own Device)** in enterprises.
- A developer on a Linux laptop, a manager on an iPhone, and a designer on a Mac all access the same cloud application identically.

---

### Characteristic 3 — Resource Pooling

**Definition:** The provider's computing resources are **pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model**, with **physical and virtual resources dynamically reassigned** according to consumer demand. There is a sense of **location-independence** — the customer generally has **no control or knowledge** over the exact location of the provided resources, but may be able to specify location at a higher level (country, state, data centre).

**Key Sub-Concepts:**
- **Multi-tenancy**: Multiple customers share the same physical infrastructure, isolated via software (hypervisor, namespaces, containers).
- **Location independence**: Consumer specifies **region** (e.g., "Asia-South-1"), but not the **specific rack**.
- **Resource abstraction**: Storage, compute, bandwidth all abstracted into logical pools.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why**: Maximizes utilization — a single physical host may host VMs from 10 different customers.
- **How**: **Virtualization** (hypervisors like KVM, VMware ESXi) and **containerization** (Docker, Kubernetes) provide the isolation layer.

**Real-world utility:**
- A cloud data centre achieves **60–80 % utilization**, vs. 10–15 % in traditional data centres.
- Tenants can be moved between hosts via **live migration** for maintenance with zero downtime.

---

### Characteristic 4 — Rapid Elasticity

**Definition:** Capabilities can be **provisioned and released, in some cases automatically**, to **scale rapidly outward and inward** commensurate with demand. To the consumer, the capabilities available for provisioning often appear to be **unlimited** and can be appropriated in any quantity at any time.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why**: Demand is unpredictable — Black Friday traffic on Amazon, exam-season load on KTU ERP — static capacity wastes money.
- **How**: Implemented via **auto-scaling groups, load balancers, container orchestrators**.
  - **Horizontal scaling (scale out/in)**: Add/remove VM instances.
  - **Vertical scaling (scale up/down)**: Increase/decrease CPU/RAM of an existing VM.

**Real-world utility:**
- Netflix auto-scales from **few hundred to tens of thousands** of EC2 instances during peak hours.
- Saves cost — you pay only for peak-time resources, not 24×7.

---

### Characteristic 5 — Measured Service

**Definition:** Cloud systems **automatically control and optimize resource use** by leveraging **metering at an appropriate level** appropriate to the type of service (e.g., storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). Resource usage **can be monitored, controlled, and reported**, providing transparency for both the provider and consumer of the utilized service.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Why**: Enables the **pay-per-use / utility pricing model**.
- **How**: Metering agents collect usage (CPU-hours, GB stored, GB transferred, API calls) → billing system generates invoice.
- **Transparency**: Both sides see the bill — provider recovers cost, consumer audits spend.

**Real-world utility:**
- AWS bills in **per-second** increments for EC2.
- Enables **chargeback / showback** in enterprises — each department sees its own cloud bill.

---

|---|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | On-Demand Self-Service | Zero wait time |
| 2 | Broad Network Access | Device / OS independence |
| 3 | Resource Pooling | Lower cost via sharing |
| 4 | Rapid Elasticity | Match capacity to demand |

### Table C — Common KTU "Pitfall" Distinctions

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners frequently test **terminology clarity**. These distinctions appear in **3-mark Part A** questions.

| Don't Confuse | vs. | Correct Distinction |
|---------------|-----|---------------------|
| **Elasticity** | Scalability | Elasticity = **automatic** / dynamic; Scalability = ability to handle load (may be manual) |
| **Vertical scaling** | Horizontal scaling | Vertical = add CPU/RAM to **one** machine; Horizontal = add **more** machines |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Multi-instance | Multi-tenant = **one app instance, many users**; Multi-instance = **many app copies** |
| **Public cloud** | Private cloud | Public = over **public Internet**, third-party owned; Private = **organization-owned**, on-premises |
| **IaaS** | PaaS vs SaaS | IaaS = raw VMs; PaaS = platform with runtimes/DBs; SaaS = ready-to-use app |

## 2.3 The Three Service Models — A KTU-Mandatory Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the *full* service-model question is a Module-1 14-marker, KTU also tests the **definitions** in Module 1 fundamentals. Be ready to define each in one sentence.

| Model | Acronym | What the **Consumer Manages** | What the **Provider Manages** | KTU Real-World Example |
|-------|---------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Software as a Service | **SaaS** | Only their **data + usage** | Everything else (app, runtime, OS, middleware, infra) | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| Platform as a Service | **PaaS** | Their **application code + data** | OS, middleware, runtime, infra | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku |
| Infrastructure as a Service | **IaaS** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | Only the **physical infra + virtualization** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine |

> **Memory trick:** *"You manage UP the stack"* — in SaaS, you manage **only up**; in IaaS, you manage **all the way up**.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping NIST Definition to Each Pillar

> The NIST definition can be **decomposed into 6 conceptual pillars**. KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the NIST definition of cloud computing"* (7 marks). Below is the **complete evaluator-friendly breakdown** you can reproduce on paper.

| Pillar # | Phrase from Definition | Conceptual Meaning | Corresponding NIST Pillar |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | "model for enabling" | A **paradigm / approach** — not a product | Cloud Computing Model |
| 2 | "ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access" | Available **anywhere, anytime, on demand** | Broad Network Access + On-Demand Self-Service |
| 3 | "shared pool of configurable computing resources" | Resources **pooled** and **abstracted** | Resource Pooling |
| 4 | "networks, servers, storage, applications, and services" | **Five resource types** are abstracted | All deployment models |
| 5 | "rapidly provisioned and released" | Provisioning is **fast and reversible** | Rapid Elasticity |
| 6 | "minimal management effort or service provider interaction" | **Low operational overhead** | All service models (esp. SaaS) |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of "Cloud" from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **model-agnostic** — it does not assume AWS, Azure, or GCP. It walks from raw hardware to a true cloud, satisfying each NIST characteristic **in order**. This is the *gold-standard* answer for a 14-mark question.

### Step 1 — Start with Physical Infrastructure

You have **physical servers**, storage arrays, and network switches in a data centre.

**Conceptual block:** A bare data centre is **not** a cloud.

---

### Step 2 — Add Virtualization

Install a **hypervisor** (e.g., KVM, VMware ESXi) on each server. Now each physical server hosts **multiple Virtual Machines (VMs)**, each with its own OS.

$$
\text{Physical Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Hypervisor}} \{VM_1,\ VM_2,\ \ldots,\ VM_n\}
$$

This enables **Resource Pooling (Characteristic 3)** — multiple tenants share one host.

---

### Step 3 — Add a Self-Service Layer

Expose an **API / Portal** that lets users create/destroy VMs programmatically without calling the admin.

$$
\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP/REST API}} \text{Orchestrator} \xrightarrow{} \text{Hypervisor} \xrightarrow{} \text{New VM}
$$

This delivers **On-Demand Self-Service (Characteristic 1)**.

---

### Step 4 — Abstract the Network

Make the service reachable over **any standard protocol** (HTTP/HTTPS/REST) from any device (laptop, phone, tablet).

This delivers **Broad Network Access (Characteristic 2)**.

---

### Step 5 — Add an Auto-Scaler

Introduce an **auto-scaling group** with a load balancer. When CPU > 70 %, launch a new VM; when CPU < 30 %, terminate one.

$$
N_{\text{VMs}}(t) = \begin{cases} N_{\text{VMs}}(t-1) + k, & \text{if } \text{CPU}_{\text{avg}}(t) > 70\% \\ N_{\text{VMs}}(t-1) - k, & \text{if } \text{CPU}_{\text{avg}}(t) < 30\% \\ N_{\text{VMs}}(t-1), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $k$ is the scaling step (commonly $k = 1$ for fast scale-out, $k = 2$ for steady state).

This delivers **Rapid Elasticity (Characteristic 4)**.

---

### Step 6 — Add Metering & Billing

Attach a **metering agent** to each VM that records CPU-hours, GB stored, GB transferred. A billing engine converts these to a monetary bill.

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{bill}} = \sum_{r \in \text{resources}} (U_r \times P_r)
$$

where:
- $U_r$ = usage of resource $r$ (e.g., vCPU-hours, GB-months)
- $P_r$ = unit price of resource $r$ (e.g., ₹ 6.5 / vCPU-hour)

This delivers **Measured Service (Characteristic 5)**.

---

### Step 7 — Verify: All 5 Characteristics Satisfied?

Run a checklist:

| Characteristic | Satisfied by Step | ✅ |
|---|---|---|
| On-Demand Self-Service | Step 3 | ✅ |
| Broad Network Access | Step 4 | ✅ |
| Resource Pooling | Step 2 | ✅ |
| Rapid Elasticity | Step 5 | ✅ |
| Measured Service | Step 6 | ✅ |

**Conclusion:** Once all 5 characteristics are satisfied, **the system is officially a Cloud**, by NIST definition.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Elasticity Quantification

> This is a **numerical example** KTU sometimes gives in Part A (3 marks) or as an introductory problem in Part B.

**Problem:**
A SaaS application has **2 EC2 instances** at 10 AM. At 11 AM, average CPU hits 75 %, and the auto-scaler adds **2 more instances**. At 12 PM, average CPU drops to 25 %, and the auto-scaler removes **1 instance**. Calculate the **final number of instances** and the **percentage increase** in capacity during the 11 AM spike.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Initial state:**
$$
N_0 = 2 \text{ instances}
$$

**Step 2 — Auto-scaler reaction at 11 AM (CPU > 70 %):**
$$
N_1 = N_0 + k = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ instances}
$$

**Step 3 — Auto-scaler reaction at 12 PM (CPU < 30 %):**
$$
N_2 = N_1 - k = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ instances}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage increase during 11 AM spike:**
$$
\% \text{Increase} = \frac{N_1 - N_0}{N_0} \times 100 = \frac{4 - 2}{2} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

### Result
- **Final instance count:** $\mathbf{3}$
- **Capacity increase during spike:** $\mathbf{100\ \%}$

This numerical demonstration makes the answer **stand out** in valuation — it is the kind of calculation KTU's "Apply" (Bloom Level 3) questions target.

---

## 3.4 Characteristic-vs-Counter-Example Matrix

> KTU examiners love **"Which of the following is NOT a characteristic"** or **"Justify whether X is a cloud"** type questions. Use this matrix.

| System | Has Self-Service? | Pooled? | Elastic? | Metered? | Broad Access? | Is it a Cloud? |
|--------|-------------------|---------|----------|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Bare-metal dedicated server | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Traditional managed hosting | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| VPS (manual provisioning) | ⚠️ partial | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ | Borderline (often classified as "private cloud" if metering exists) |
| AWS EC2 with Auto-Scaling | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ **Yes** |
| On-premises OpenStack private cloud | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ (within org) | ✅ **Yes** (private cloud) |

## 3.5 Detailed Mapping: Characteristic → Failure Mode → Mitigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each characteristic can **fail**. KTU tests this in **"Discuss the limitations"** style questions.

| Characteristic | Common Failure | Real Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-Demand Self-Service | Provider throttles new VM requests | AWS EC2 instance quota | Request quota increase, multi-region |
| Broad Network Access | Geo-blocked regions | GDPR-blocked services in EU | Use regional deployments, CDNs |
| Resource Pooling | "Noisy neighbour" — one tenant hogs CPU | Shared MySQL hosting | Dedicated tenancy tier |
| Rapid Elasticity | Cold-start latency of new VMs | AWS Lambda cold-start | Provisioned concurrency, warm pools |
| Measured Service | Hidden data egress fees | AWS $0.09/GB egress | Architecture review, cost dashboards |

| **Cloud Carrier** | Connectivity provider | Provides network access to consumers (e.g., ISP) |

> KTU's **favourite 14-marker**. The classic *"shared-responsibility stack"*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        S1["Application (Gmail, Office 365)"]
        S2["Data"]

        I4["Virtualization"]
        I5["Servers / Storage / Network"]

    Provider["PROVIDER MANAGES"]:::prov
    Consumer["CONSUMER MANAGES"]:::cons

    S1 -.-> Consumer
    S2 -.-> Consumer
    S3 -.-> Provider
    S4 -.-> Provider
    S5 -.-> Provider

    P1 -.-> Consumer
    P2 -.-> Consumer
    P3 -.-> Provider
    P4 -.-> Provider
    P5 -.-> Provider

    I1 -.-> Consumer
    I2 -.-> Consumer
    I3 -.-> Consumer

    classDef prov fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px

```

**How to read the diagram:**
- **In SaaS**, the consumer manages **only the top 2 layers** (application + data). The provider manages everything below.
- **In PaaS**, the consumer manages **top 2 layers**. Same as SaaS in count — BUT the consumer is now expected to *develop* the application.
- **In IaaS**, the consumer manages **the top 3 layers** (OS, middleware, app + data). Provider only manages virtualization + hardware.
