# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are **direct, definitional** and expect a **3-sentence crisp answer**. Each carries **3 marks** and roughly **3–4 minutes**.

---

### Question 1: Define Cloud Computing as per NIST. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

According to **NIST SP 500-145**, cloud computing is *a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.* It is characterized by **five essential characteristics**: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.

---

### Question 2: List any five essential characteristics of cloud computing. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **five essential characteristics of cloud computing** (as defined by NIST) are:

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Provision resources automatically without human interaction.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Accessible over standard protocols from heterogeneous devices.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic resource reassignment.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities scaled rapidly, appearing unlimited to the consumer.
5. **Measured Service** — Automatic metering and billing of resource usage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Module-1 questions are typically framed with **internal choice** between two 14-mark options. Below are two **independent, full-model-answer** options.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

> **Explain the NIST Reference Model of Cloud Computing. Describe each of the five actors and their roles.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

#### (a) NIST Reference Model Overview

The **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture** (NIST SP 500-292) provides a **conceptual framework** that identifies the major actors, their activities, and the functions they perform in cloud computing.

The model describes **five major actors**:

1. **Cloud Consumer**
2. **Cloud Provider**
3. **Cloud Broker**
4. **Cloud Carrier**
5. **Cloud Auditor**

Each actor is an **entity (person or organization)** that participates in a cloud transaction or process.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

#### (b) Roles of the Five Actors

| Actor | Primary Role | Examples |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Cloud Consumer** | Person/organization that **uses** cloud services. They provision resources, deploy applications, and pay the provider. | A startup using AWS EC2 |
| **Cloud Provider** | Entity that **owns and operates** the cloud infrastructure; supplies services to consumers via SLA. | AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud |
| **Cloud Broker** | Intermediary that **manages the use, performance, and delivery** of cloud services, and negotiates relationships between providers and consumers. | Capgemini Cloud Brokerage Services |
| **Cloud Carrier** | The **intermediary that provides connectivity and transport** of cloud services between provider and consumer (i.e., the network pipe). | ISPs like Airtel, Jio, AT&T |
| **Cloud Auditor** | A **neutral third party** that conducts independent audits of cloud services — assesses security, privacy, performance, and SLA compliance. | Big-4 audit firms, ISO certifiers |

> The **relationships** between these actors are governed by SLAs, contracts, and trust frameworks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

> **Discuss in detail the five essential characteristics of cloud computing with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

#### (a) Characteristics 1 to 3

**1. On-Demand Self-Service:**
A consumer can **unilaterally provision** compute, storage, and networking capabilities **automatically, without human interaction** with the provider. *Example:* Using the AWS Console to launch a new EC2 instance in seconds — no email or ticket required.

**2. Broad Network Access:**
Cloud capabilities are **available over the network** and accessed through **standard protocols (HTTP, REST, etc.)** from a wide range of devices — mobile phones, tablets, laptops, desktops. *Example:* Accessing Office 365 on a browser on a phone or a Linux laptop gives the same experience.

**3. Resource Pooling:**
The provider's resources are **pooled to serve multiple consumers** using a multi-tenant model. Consumers do not know the exact location of resources, but may specify a high-level region (country/state). *Example:* Ten AWS customers running VMs on the same physical host, logically isolated by the Xen hypervisor.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

#### (b) Characteristics 4 and 5 + Conclusion

**4. Rapid Elasticity:**
Capabilities can be **scaled out or scaled in rapidly** — sometimes automatically — to match demand. To the consumer, the capacity appears **virtually unlimited**. *Example:* An e-commerce website automatically adds 100 EC2 instances during a Diwali sale and removes them after.

**5. Measured Service:**
Cloud systems **automatically meter and report** resource usage (CPU hours, storage, bandwidth) to enable **pay-per-use billing**. Both provider and consumer benefit from transparency. *Example:* AWS bills users in **per-second increments**; GCP bills BigQuery in **TB scanned**.

**Conclusion:** These five characteristics, originally defined by **NIST SP 500-145**, collectively distinguish **true cloud computing** from traditional hosting, virtualization, or grid computing. Any system satisfying all five is, by definition, a cloud.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks (per real KTU answer-key patterns):**
>
> 1. ❌ **Writing the NIST definition loosely** — Always use the **exact NIST phrasing**. Half-marks deducted for paraphrasing.
> 2. ❌ **Listing characteristics without examples** — A 7-mark question expects **definition + example + implementation mechanism** for each characteristic. Without examples, expect **−2 marks minimum**.
> 3. ❌ **Confusing service models with deployment models** — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS are **service** models. Private/Public/Hybrid are **deployment** models. Examiners cross-check this!
> 4. ❌ **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — A diagram of the NIST Reference Architecture / Service Stack is **mandatory**. Skipping it costs **−3 marks**.
> 5. ❌ **Ignoring "Measured Service"** — Students remember the first 4, forget the 5th. Almost every batch loses marks here.
> 6. ❌ **Forgetting the publication reference** — Cite **NIST SP 500-145** (for definition) and **NIST SP 500-292** (for reference architecture). Examiners reward this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist — what MUST be in your answer book:**

- ✅ **NIST definition** (write verbatim) — cite **SP 500-145**.
- ✅ **Five essential characteristics** — write all 5, with **definition + example** for each.
- ✅ **NIST Reference Model** — five actors: **Consumer, Provider, Broker, Carrier, Auditor** — each with **one-line role**.
- ✅ **Three service models** — IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — know the **shared-responsibility stack**.
- ✅ **Four deployment models** — Private, Community, Public, Hybrid — one-line definition each.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** — multiple customers, one physical host, logical isolation.
- ✅ **Elasticity ≠ Scalability** — Elasticity is **automatic**; Scalability is **ability** (may be manual).
- ✅ **Metering** — pay-per-use, utility model, transparency.
- ✅ **NIST publication numbers** — **SP 500-145** = Definition; **SP 500-292** = Reference Architecture.
- ✅ **Draw at least one diagram** in any 14-mark answer — it is **non-negotiable**.

---

### Closing Note for KTU 2024 Students

> Module 1 is the **foundation pillar** for the entire Cloud Computing course. Almost every later module (security, migration, DevOps, serverless) will reference these 5 characteristics and the NIST actors. **Mastering this module** means you can confidently handle any CO-1 question in the ESE. All the best for your KTU exams! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Benefits and Challenges # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Benefits of Cloud Computing** refer to the quantifiable and qualitative advantages obtained by organizations, developers, and end-users when they migrate, deploy, or consume computational, storage, networking, and software resources through a *shared, on-demand, pay-per-use, elastic, and remotely accessible* service model delivered over the Internet. **Challenges of Cloud Computing** are the inherent limitations, risks, and unresolved technical, legal, organizational, and operational concerns that arise from relinquishing direct physical control of IT resources, depending on a third-party provider, and transmitting sensitive data over public networks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Box** > According to the NIST SP 800-145 reference cited in the KTU syllabus, cloud computing is characterized by **five essential characteristics** (On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service), **three service models** (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS), and **four deployment models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community). The benefits and challenges of cloud computing are *direct consequences* of these defining traits. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are a student preparing for university exams: | Analogy Component | Real-World Analogy | Cloud Computing Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Buying your own car | Buying & maintaining your own servers | **Traditional On-Premise IT** | | Taking a local taxi whenever needed | Metered, on-demand transportation | **Pay-per-use Cloud Model** | | Owning a 5-bedroom house for 2 people | Over-provisioned data center | **Underutilized resources** | | Borrowing a power bank from a friend | Shared infrastructure from CSP | **Resource Pooling** | | No mechanics, no insurance, no parking | No IT staff, no hardware refresh | **Reduced operational burden** | | But — what if the power bank is faulty? | Vendor lock-in, data breach | **Security & Trust Challenges** | **The Intuition in One Line:** *Cloud computing is like renting a fully-serviced apartment (benefits) but sharing the building's plumbing and trusting the landlord with your keys (challenges).* ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Boxed) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Cloud Metrics You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams** > - **Service Level Agreement (SLA):** Minimum **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.99% ("four nines")** uptime guarantee. > - **Cost Unit:** USD per **GB-month** (storage), USD per **vCPU-hour** (compute), USD per **GB-out** (egress). > - **Elasticity Window:** Provisioning time typically **< 60 seconds** for most public clouds. > - **MTBF / MTTR:** Mean Time Between Failures / Mean Time To Repair are the key reliability inputs. > - **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The 8 Core Benefits of Cloud Computing > [!NOTE] > Every benefit below is a **direct consequence** of the 5 NIST essential characteristics. Examiners love this linkage — state it in your answers. ### 2.1.1 Economic Benefit — *Capital Expenditure (CapEx) to Operational Expenditure (OpEx) Shift* - **What:** No upfront cost for servers, storage, or networking equipment. Payment shifts to a *subscription* model. - **Why it matters:** A startup with ₹10,00,000 can launch a globally distributed application without buying a single physical server. - **How:** The CSP amortizes infrastructure cost across thousands of tenants, achieving economies of scale and passing the discount to the customer. ### 2.1.2 Scalability and Elasticity - **Vertical Scaling (Scale Up/Down):** Increasing/decreasing resources (CPU, RAM) of a single instance. - **Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out/In):** Increasing/decreasing the number of instances. - **Elasticity:** The system automatically provisions or de-provisions resources in *real-time* based on workload demand — also called **"autoscaling."** ### 2.1.3 High Availability and Reliability - **Replication** of data and services across multiple **geographically separated** zones/regions. - **Redundancy** at every layer (power, network, storage, compute). - **Fault tolerance** is built-in by design. ### 2.1.4 Flexibility, Agility, and Faster Time-to-Market - Resources can be provisioned in **minutes** instead of weeks. - Developers can spin up a complete development environment with a single API call. ### 2.1.5 Mobility and Ubiquitous Access - Applications and data are accessible from any device with a network connection and a browser/client app. ### 2.1.6 Maintenance-Free (for the Customer) - The CSP handles hardware upgrades, OS patching, cooling, and physical security. ### 2.1.7 Global Reach and Resource Pooling - Deploy in Mumbai, Frankfurt, and Singapore within minutes. - Multi-tenant pooling ensures no single tenant monopolizes resources. ### 2.1.8 Pay-per-Use / Metered Service - "No usage, no charge" — billing aligns directly with consumption. ## 2.2 The 9 Core Challenges of Cloud Computing ### 2.2.1 Security and Privacy - Multi-tenant data co-location creates attack surfaces. - Insider threats, account hijacking, insecure APIs. ### 2.2.2 Data Lock-in (Vendor Lock-in) - Proprietary APIs, data formats, and migration tools make it painful to switch CSPs. ### 2.2.3 Data Protection and Compliance - GDPR, HIPAA, IT Act 2000, and DPDP Act 2023 impose jurisdiction-based rules on where data is stored and processed. ### 2.2.4 Performance Unpredictability - Network latency, noisy-neighbor effect, variable I/O performance. ### 2.2.5 Interoperability - Different CSPs have different APIs, deployment tools, and SLAs. ### 2.2.6 Availability and Downtime - Outages of major CSPs can take down millions of sites (e.g., the famous **2017 AWS S3 outage**). ### 2.2.7 Bandwidth Costs - Egress (data leaving the cloud) is often **free inbound, paid outbound**. ### 2.2.8 Lack of Support and Transparency - Customers cannot directly "look inside" the data center. ### 2.2.9 Legal and Contractual Concerns - Who owns the data? What happens during CSP bankruptcy? ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table | # | Item | Formula / Definition | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** | $\text{TCO} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx}_{\text{lifecycle}}$ | USD | Used in cloud migration ROI analysis | | 2 | **ROI (Return on Investment)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100$ | % | Migration business case | | 3 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Ratio (0-1) or % | SLA estimation | | 4 | **Annual Downtime** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | min/year | SLA penalty calculation | | 5 | **Cost per vCPU-hour** | $C_{\text{cpu}} = \dfrac{\text{Monthly Compute Bill}}{\text{vCPU-hours consumed}}$ | USD/vCPU-h | Workload cost optimization | | 6 | **Elasticity Speed** | $t_{\text{scale}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{provision}}$ | seconds | Application design trade-off | | 7 | **Egress Cost** | $C_{\text{egress}} = \text{GB}_{\text{out}} \times \text{Rate per GB}$ | USD | Data-heavy workload planning | | 8 | **Storage Cost** | $C_{\text{storage}} = \text{GB} \times \text{Rate per GB-month}$ | USD/month | Capacity planning | | 9 | **Annualized Failure Rate** | $\text{AFR} = 1 - A^{525600/\text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction | Reliability comparison | | 10 | **Pay-per-Use Billing** | $\text{Monthly Bill} = \sum_{i} (u_i \times p_i)$ | USD | Consumption forecasting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Reading Rule:** Examiners may ask you to compute *annual downtime* given an SLA. Remember: $1 \text{ year} = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes} = 365 \times 24 \times 60$. Memorize this constant. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from SLA Percentage **Problem Statement:** A KTU engineering college wants to host its ERP on a public cloud. The CSP offers an SLA of **99.95%**. Calculate the maximum permissible annual downtime in minutes. **Step 1 — Identify the constants.** $$A_{\text{SLA}} = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$ $$T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the SLA downtime formula.** $$\text{Max Downtime} = (1 - A_{\text{SLA}}) \times T_{\text{year}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$\text{Max Downtime} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600$$ $$\text{Max Downtime} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600$$ **Step 4 — Perform the final multiplication.** $$\text{Max Downtime} = 262.8 \text{ minutes per year} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ minutes}$$ **Conclusion:** With a 99.95% SLA, the college can expect a maximum of **~262.8 minutes** of permitted downtime per year, which is acceptable for an academic ERP. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cloud Migration ROI (Return on Investment) **Problem Statement:** A startup currently spends ₹20,00,000 on in-house servers (hardware, power, cooling, IT staff). Migrating to a public cloud will cost ₹12,00,000 per year in cloud bills, plus a one-time migration cost of ₹3,00,000. Compute the **first-year ROI**. **Step 1 — Identify the costs (Total Investment).** $$\text{Investment} = \text{Migration Cost} + \text{Year-1 Cloud Bill}$$ $$\text{Investment} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the net benefit (savings).** $$\text{Net Benefit} = \text{Old On-Premise Cost} - \text{New Cloud Cost}$$ $$\text{Net Benefit} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the ROI formula.** $$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100$$ $$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$ $$\text{ROI} = 0.5333 \times 100 = 53.33\%$$ **Conclusion:** A **53.33% ROI in Year 1** is a strong business case. The migration pays back within the first year itself. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pay-per-Use Monthly Bill (Composite Workload) **Problem Statement:** A web application uses the following resources in a month: - **Compute:** 4 vCPUs running for 720 hours @ ₹2/vCPU-hr - **Storage:** 500 GB @ ₹1.5/GB-month - **Egress:** 2,000 GB out @ ₹0.09/GB Compute the total monthly bill. **Step 1 — Compute the compute cost.** $$C_{\text{compute}} = 4 \times 720 \times 2 = 5{,}760 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the storage cost.** $$C_{\text{storage}} = 500 \times 1.5 = 750 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the egress cost.** $$C_{\text{egress}} = 2000 \times 0.09 = 180 \text{ INR}$$ **Step 4 — Sum the three components.** $$\text{Total Bill} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{egress}}$$ $$\text{Total Bill} = 5760 + 750 + 180 = 6{,}690 \text{ INR}$$ **Conclusion:** The pay-per-use model gives the customer **granular transparency** — a direct benefit that is mathematically traceable. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Benefits vs. Challenges Mapping | # | Benefit | Directly Mitigated by | Opposing Challenge | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cost savings (OpEx) | Long-term ROI analysis | Egress costs, hidden fees | | 2 | Scalability | Auto-scaling groups | Configuration complexity | | 3 | High availability | Multi-AZ/region deployment | Cascading outages, network partition | | 4 | Mobility | VPN, MFA, Zero-Trust | Data-in-transit security risk | | 5 | Pay-per-use | Cost monitoring tools | Uncontrolled sprawl → bill shock | | 6 | Resource pooling | Tenant isolation (VPC) | Noisy-neighbor effect | | 7 | Maintenance-free | Managed services (PaaS/SaaS) | Vendor lock-in | | 8 | Global reach | CDN, edge locations | Compliance with data sovereignty laws | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** The *same characteristic* of cloud computing (e.g., resource pooling) is simultaneously a *benefit* (cost saving) and a *challenge* (security risk). Examiners expect students to articulate this **duality**, not just list bullet points. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Benefits ↔ Challenges Duality Map ```mermaid flowchart LR CLOUD["CLOUD COMPUTING PARADIGM"] B1["BENEFIT 1
ECONOMIC

AND RELIABILITY"] B4["BENEFIT 4
FLEXIBILITY AND
AGILITY"] B5["BENEFIT 5
MOBILITY AND
GLOBAL ACCESS"] B6["BENEFIT 6
RESOURCE
POOLING"] B7["BENEFIT 7
PAY PER USE
METERED SERVICE"] B8["BENEFIT 8
MAINTENANCE
FREE OPERATIONS"] CH1["CHALLENGE 1
SECURITY AND
PRIVACY RISKS"] CH2["CHALLENGE 2
VENDOR LOCK IN
AND DATA PORTABILITY"] CH3["CHALLENGE 3
COMPLIANCE AND
DATA SOVEREIGNTY"] CH4["CHALLENGE 4
PERFORMANCE

OUTAGE EXPOSURE"] CH7["CHALLENGE 7

LACK OF DIRECT
CONTROL"] CLOUD --> B1 CLOUD --> B2 CLOUD --> B3 CLOUD --> B4 CLOUD --> B5 CLOUD --> B6 CLOUD --> B7 CLOUD --> B8 CLOUD --> CH1 CLOUD --> CH2 CLOUD --> CH3 CLOUD --> CH4 CLOUD --> CH5 CLOUD --> CH6 CLOUD --> CH7 CLOUD --> CH8 B6 -.duality.-> CH1 B3 -.duality.-> CH6 B7 -.duality.-> CH7 B2 -.duality.-> CH4 B8 -.duality.-> CH2 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Cloud Service Stack (Showing Where Benefits & Challenges Originate) ```mermaid flowchart TB USER["END USER LAYER
Browsers, Mobile Apps, Thin Clients"] SAAS["SAAS LAYER
Software as a Service
BENEFIT: Zero Install
CHALLENGE: Vendor Lock-in"] PAAS["PAAS LAYER
Platform as a Service
