```mermaid
    TOP["SaaS - Highest Abstraction - End User Software"]
    MID["PaaS - Medium Abstraction - Developer Platform"]
    BOT["IaaS - Lowest Abstraction - Raw Infrastructure"]

    TOP --> MID --> BOT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** As you move **upward**, the user manages **less** and the provider manages **more**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS). List any FOUR characteristics of PaaS.**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition:** Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) is a cloud computing service model that provides a pre-configured platform—including runtime, middleware, OS, development tools, and databases—over the internet, allowing developers to build, test, and deploy applications without managing the underlying infrastructure.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Pre-built Runtime & Middleware** — Supports multiple language runtimes (Java, Python, Node.js) out-of-the-box.
2. **Integrated Development Environment** — Provides web-based IDEs, CLI tools, and CI/CD pipelines.
3. **Auto-Scaling & Load Balancing** — Dynamically adjusts the number of application instances based on traffic.
4. **Pay-per-use Pricing** — Charged based on actual resource consumption (compute, storage, bandwidth).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for crisp definition + 2 marks for 4 valid characteristics)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | IaaS | **PaaS** | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What user manages** | OS, Runtime, Middleware, App | **App & Data** | Nothing (just usage) |
| **What provider manages** | Virt, Servers, Storage, Network | **OS, Middleware, Runtime, Virt, Servers, Storage, Network** | Everything |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs | **AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku** | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| **Target User** | System Admins | **Application Developers** | End Users |
| **Abstraction** | Lowest | **Medium** | Highest |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for clarity of differentiation + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for completeness)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model** with a neat diagram. Discuss the three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and their responsibilities. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable case study, explain how **Google App Engine (GAE)** acts as a Platform-as-a-Service. Discuss its architecture, supported runtimes, auto-scaling, and billing model. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**NIST Reference Model Overview:**
The NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Model defines five essential characteristics (**On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service**), three service models (**IaaS, PaaS, SaaS**), and four deployment models (**Public, Private, Hybrid, Community**).

**IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):**
- Provides raw compute, storage, and networking resources.
- User has full control over the OS, middleware, and applications.
- **Example:** Amazon EC2.
- **User manages:** Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS.
- **Provider manages:** Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking.

**PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):**
- Provides a complete development and deployment platform.
- User only manages the application code and data.
- **Example:** Google App Engine.
- **User manages:** Application, Data.
- **Provider manages:** OS, Middleware, Runtime, Virt, Servers, Storage, Networking.

**SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):**
- Delivers ready-to-use software applications.
- User simply consumes the software via a web browser.
- **Example:** Microsoft Office 365, Gmail.
- **User manages:** Nothing (or limited user-specific configuration).
- **Provider manages:** Everything.

**Diagram (NIST Stack):**

| User Responsibility | SaaS | PaaS | IaaS | Provider Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | Provider | User | User | Application |
| Data | Provider | User | User | Data |
| Runtime | Provider | Provider | User | Runtime |
| Middleware | Provider | Provider | User | Middleware |
| OS | Provider | Provider | User | OS |
| Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider | Virtualization |
| Servers | Provider | Provider | Provider | Servers |
| Storage | Provider | Provider | Provider | Storage |
| Networking | Provider | Provider | Provider | Networking |

*(7 marks: 2 for NIST overview + 2 for three service models + 2 for diagram + 1 for examples)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Case Study: Google App Engine (GAE) as PaaS**

**Scenario:** A startup "FreshEats" wants to launch a food delivery web app. Instead of buying servers, they deploy on GAE.

**GAE Architecture:**

1. **Frontend (Instance):** Receives web requests; runs in a sandboxed environment.
2. **Application Code:** Written in Python, Java, Go, Node.js, PHP, Ruby. (Standard & Flexible Environments.)
3. **Middleware Services:** GAE provides built-in services — Memcache, Task Queues, Cron, Mail, User Auth, Cloud Datastore (NoSQL), Cloud SQL (RDBMS).
4. **Auto-Scaling:** GAE automatically creates more instances when request rate/rate of CPU usage/latency exceeds thresholds. Idle instances are scaled to zero (Standard env) to save cost.
5. **Load Balancer:** Google's global L7 load balancer routes requests across regions.
6. **Storage Layer:** Google-managed distributed file system (Colossus) and databases.
7. **Monitoring:** Stackdriver (now Google Cloud Operations) provides logs, traces, and alerts.

**Supported Runtimes (Standard Env):** Python 3.7+, Java 11/17, Node.js, Go 1.16+, PHP 7.4+, Ruby 2.7+.

**Auto-Scaling Mechanism:**
- **Basic Scaling:** Instance created when request arrives; shut down after idle period.
- **Manual Scaling:** Fixed number of instances always running.
- **Automatic Scaling:** Dynamically adjusts based on target CPU utilization, throughput, and latency.

**Billing Model:**
- Pay only for **instance hours**, **outgoing bandwidth**, and **stored data**.
- Free quota: 28 instance-hours/day, 1 GB storage, 5 GB egress/day.
- Beyond free quota: ~\$0.05–\$0.10 per instance-hour depending on memory class (F1=128MB, F2=256MB, ..., B4=1024MB, B8=2048MB).

**Benefits Realized by FreshEats:**
- Zero infrastructure management.
- Automatic scaling during lunch/dinner rush hours.
- Built-in HTTPS, DDoS protection, and global CDN.
- Reduced time-to-market from months to weeks.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture + 1 for runtimes + 2 for auto-scaling + 1 for billing + 1 for benefits)*

---

### **Question B: [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **layered architecture of PaaS** with a neat block diagram. List the responsibilities of the user and the cloud provider in each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Microsoft Azure App Service, and Red Hat OpenShift** as PaaS platforms. Discuss at least **four comparison parameters**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**PaaS Layered Architecture (7 Layers):**

| Layer # | Layer Name | Managed By | Example Technologies |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Application** | **User** | Custom code, business logic, UI |
| 2 | **Data** | **User / Provider (shared)** | SQL/NoSQL databases, blob storage |
| 3 | **Runtime** | **Provider** | JVM, CPython, Node.js, Go runtime |
| 4 | **Middleware** | **Provider** | Tomcat, JBoss, IIS, Kafka |
| 5 | **Operating System** | **Provider** | Linux (Ubuntu, RHEL), Windows Server |
| 6 | **Virtualization** | **Provider** | VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V, Docker |
| 7 | **Physical Infrastructure** | **Provider** | Bare-metal servers, SAN, network switches |

**Key Insight:** The dividing line in PaaS lies between **Data (Layer 2)** and **Runtime (Layer 3)**. The user owns the application logic and (typically) the data schema/contents, while everything from the runtime downward is the provider's responsibility.

**Block Diagram:**

```
+-----------------------------------+
|    APPLICATION (User)             |
+-----------------------------------+
|    DATA (User / Provider)         |
+-----------------------------------+
|    RUNTIME (Provider)             |
+-----------------------------------+
|    MIDDLEWARE (Provider)          |
+-----------------------------------+
|    OPERATING SYSTEM (Provider)    |
+-----------------------------------+
|    VIRTUALIZATION (Provider)      |
+-----------------------------------+
|    PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE (Provider) |
+-----------------------------------+
```

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram + 4 for explaining layers + 2 for responsibility demarcation)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison: AWS Elastic Beanstalk vs. Azure App Service vs. Red Hat OpenShift**

| Parameter | AWS Elastic Beanstalk | **Azure App Service** | **Red Hat OpenShift** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloud Lock-in** | High (AWS-only) | **High (Azure-only)** | **Low (runs anywhere — on-prem, AWS, Azure, GCP)** |
| **Deployment Model** | Public cloud only | **Public cloud only** | **Public, Private, Hybrid** |
| **Supported Languages** | Java, .NET, Python, Node.js, Go, Ruby, PHP | **.NET, Java, Node.js, Python, PHP, Ruby** | **Any language via containers (Docker)** |
| **Container Support** | Docker via ECS integration; supports ECS/EKS | **Built-in; integrates with AKS** | **Native (Kubernetes-based)** |
| **Developer Tools** | AWS Toolkit, CLI, Eclipse plugin | **Visual Studio, VS Code, Azure CLI, Azure Portal** | **OpenShift Web Console, oc CLI, VS Code** |
| **Pricing Model** | Pay for underlying AWS resources (EC2, RDS, ELB) | **Pay per App Service Plan tier (Free, Basic, Standard, Premium, Isolated)** | **Subscription-based; included with Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform license** |
| **Best For** | AWS-centric startups/mid-size apps | **Microsoft-stack enterprises (.NET-heavy)** | **Enterprise hybrid cloud, regulated industries, Kubernetes-native** |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Yes, based on CloudWatch metrics | **Yes, built-in (rules-based or metric-based)** | **Yes, via Kubernetes HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)** |
| **Vendor Support** | AWS (24/7) | **Microsoft (24/7)** | **Red Hat + joint with chosen hyperscaler** |

**Conclusion:**
- Choose **AWS Elastic Beanstalk** if you are AWS-native and want minimal configuration.
- Choose **Azure App Service** for .NET/Windows-heavy enterprise applications.
- Choose **Red Hat OpenShift** for **vendor-neutral, hybrid, container-first** deployments with maximum portability.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for the comparison table + 2 marks for justified conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
> 1. **Confusing PaaS with SaaS:** Many students wrongly list Gmail as a PaaS example. Remember: **Gmail = SaaS** (end-user software); **App Engine = PaaS** (developer platform).
> 2. **Wrong responsibility layer:** Students often claim the user manages the OS in PaaS. **No — in PaaS, the OS is provider-managed.** This single error can cost **2–3 marks** in a "shared responsibility" question.
> 3. **Forgetting the NIST context:** KTU examiners value NIST-specific terminology (5 characteristics, 3 service models, 4 deployment models). Always cite the **NIST Reference Model (SP 500-292)** when defining service models.
> 4. **Numerical errors in SLA:** A 99.9% SLA does **NOT** mean 0.1% downtime in hours — you must convert to minutes (525,600 min/year). Show the **full calculation**.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** KTU 2024 scheme requires a diagram for any architecture question. **Omitting the diagram = −2 to −3 marks guaranteed.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist for the night before your exam!**

- ✅ **PaaS = Platform-as-a-Service** — provides a managed application development & deployment platform.
- ✅ PaaS is the **middle layer** of cloud service models: **IaaS → PaaS → SaaS**.
- ✅ **NIST** defines 3 service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS**; 4 deployment models: **Public, Private, Hybrid, Community**; 5 characteristics: **On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service**.
- ✅ In PaaS, the **user manages** only the **Application** and **Data**; the **provider manages** everything from **Runtime downward**.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for PaaS layers (top → bottom):** **A-D-R-M-O-V-P** (Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Physical).
- ✅ **SLA uptime formula:** $T_{\text{down}} = 525600 \times (1 - U/100)$ minutes/year.
- ✅ **99.9% SLA** = ~**8.76 hours downtime/year**; **99.99% SLA** = ~**52.56 minutes downtime/year**.
- ✅ **Top PaaS Examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift, IBM Bluemix, Oracle Cloud Platform.
- ✅ **PaaS Sub-Categories:** Public, Private, Hybrid, **CPaaS** (Twilio), **DBaaS** (Amazon RDS), **mPaaS** (Firebase), **iPaaS** (MuleSoft).
- ✅ **Key Benefits:** Faster time-to-market, reduced OpEx, auto-scaling, built-in security, multi-language support.
- ✅ **Key Limitations:** Vendor lock-in, reduced infrastructure control, data sovereignty concerns, hidden costs at scale.
- ✅ **Auto-Scaling Types:** Basic, Manual, Automatic (target-tracking / scheduled).
- ✅ **PaaS deployment workflow:** Code → Git → Build → Test → Containerize → Orchestrate → Route → Scale.
- ✅ **Famous Cold Start Penalty** in serverless PaaS = trade-off for elasticity; warm containers mitigate it.
- ✅ KTU exam weightage: Expect **1 question (7–14 marks)** on PaaS/IaaS/SaaS comparison in almost every attempt.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — block diagram of NIST stack or PaaS layers is the safe bet.

---

> **End of Module 1 — PaaS Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) examination standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of SaaS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which a third-party cloud provider hosts, manages, and delivers software applications to customers over the Internet on a subscription or pay-per-use basis. The consumer does **not** manage the underlying cloud infrastructure (servers, operating systems, storage, or application code); they simply access the application remotely via a web browser, mobile app, or API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (NEP-Aligned):** SaaS is one of the **three foundational service models** of cloud computing (alongside **IaaS** and **PaaS**), positioned at the **topmost layer of the cloud computing stack**. The consumer's responsibility ends at the *user-level application configuration*; everything below the application is owned by the SaaS provider.

In the SPI model (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS), SaaS represents the **highest abstraction level**, where the user's only interaction is with the *finished application*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Analogy Extended"

Imagine cloud computing as a four-tier utility supply system for a city:

| Utility | Self-Supply (Traditional IT) | Cloud Service Model |
|---|---|---|
| Electricity | You own a diesel generator | **IaaS** – Rent a power plant, use raw electricity |
| Wiring & Switchboards | You hire electricians to wire your house | **PaaS** – Get pre-wired sockets & switches |
| Appliances (fridge, TV) | You buy and own them | **SaaS** – Just press "ON" and use a ready appliance |
| TV Channels / Streaming | You install a satellite dish | **SaaS** – Stream Netflix, Hotstar, YouTube |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** When you watch Netflix or use Gmail, you are *not* thinking about which server is running the code, which OS is installed, or how memory is allocated. You simply **log in and use it**. That "use-it-and-forget-the-infrastructure" philosophy is the *essence of SaaS*.

### The "Pizza-as-a-Service" Analogy (KTU Favourite)

- **On-Premise (Traditional):** You buy flour, cheese, tomatoes — make pizza at home from scratch.
- **IaaS:** You buy raw dough from the store, add toppings, bake at home.
- **PaaS:** You get a pre-made base; you add toppings and bake.
- **SaaS:** You **order a fully cooked pizza** delivered to your door. You only eat it. 🍕

SaaS is the **"fully cooked pizza"** — the provider does *everything*; the user simply consumes.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of SaaS (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NIST SP 800-145** definition mandates the following essential characteristics for all cloud models (incl. SaaS): **On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service**. SaaS additionally exhibits these domain-specific traits:

1. **Multi-Tenancy Architecture** — A single application instance serves *multiple customer organizations (tenants)* simultaneously with logical isolation.
2. **Centralized Remote Hosting** — The application lives in the provider's data center, not on the user's machine.
3. **Browser-Based / Thin-Client Access** — Most SaaS apps run in a browser (zero local install).
4. **Subscription-Based Pricing** — Typically billed monthly/yearly per user (e.g., **₹125/user/month** for Google Workspace).
5. **Automatic Updates & Patching** — Provider pushes upgrades transparently.
6. **High Availability & Scalability** — Built on redundant cloud infrastructure.
7. **API-Driven Extensibility** — SaaS apps expose **RESTful APIs** for integration.
8. **Metered Service** — Usage is monitored and billed.

---

## 1.4 Popular Real-World SaaS Examples (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Application | Provider | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Gmail, Google Docs, Google Drive | **Google Workspace** | Productivity / Collaboration |
| Microsoft 365 (Word, Excel, Teams) | **Microsoft** | Productivity / Office Suite |
| Salesforce CRM | **Salesforce** | Customer Relationship Management |
| Dropbox, OneDrive | Various | Cloud Storage / File Sync |
| Zoom, Slack, Microsoft Teams | Various | Communication / Conferencing |
| AWS Chime, Amazon WorkDocs | **Amazon** | Enterprise SaaS |
| GitHub, GitLab | Various | DevOps / Source Code Mgmt |
| Netflix, Spotify | Various | Media Streaming (consumer SaaS) |
| Zoho CRM, Freshworks | Various | Indian SaaS Providers |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Give two examples of SaaS applications."* — Always cite **at least one productivity tool (Gmail/Docs)** and **one enterprise tool (Salesforce/CRM)** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cloud Computing Service Model Stack

SaaS sits at the **highest layer** of the cloud abstraction stack. Each successive layer hides more complexity from the consumer.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SaaS  ← User only manages DATA + ACCESS        │  ← Consumer Responsibility
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PaaS  ← User manages APPLICATIONS + DATA       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  IaaS  ← User manages OS, MIDDLEWARE, APPS, DATA│
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  On-Premise  ← User manages EVERYTHING incl.    │
│                servers, networking, facilities   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Responsibility Matrix (HIGH-YIELD for KTU — 14-mark questions!)

| Component | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | **SaaS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | User | User | User | **Provider** |
| Data | User | User | User | **User** |
| Runtime / Middleware | User | User | Provider | **Provider** |
| Operating System | User | User | Provider | **Provider** |
| Virtualization | User | Provider | Provider | **Provider** |
| Servers | User | Provider | Provider | **Provider** |
| Storage | User | Provider | Provider | **Provider** |
| Networking | User | Provider | Provider | **Provider** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In SaaS, the **only two things the user "owns"** are: **(1) their own DATA** and **(2) their USER ACCESS credentials**. Everything else is the provider's responsibility. This is the most-tested SaaS concept in KTU board exams.

---

## 2.2 SaaS Architecture: Multi-Tenancy Models

Multi-tenancy is the architectural cornerstone of SaaS. It refers to a **single software instance serving multiple tenants (customers)**. KTU syllabus emphasizes **four levels of multi-tenancy** (originally from the **Microsoft SaaS Maturity Model**):

### Level 1 — Ad-Hoc / Custom (Mature/Per-tenant)
- Each customer gets a **dedicated, customized instance**.
- No real multi-tenancy.
- High cost, low scalability.

### Level 2 — Configurable
- A **single instance** serves all tenants.
- Each tenant has **isolated customizations** stored as configuration metadata.
- Moderate scalability.

### Level 3 — Configurable, Multi-Tenant Efficient
- **Single instance, single database, shared schema**.
- Tenant data segregated using a **Tenant_ID column**.
- Most common modern SaaS architecture.

### Level 4 — Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant Efficient
- **Single instance, single database, shared schema**.
- Built on **load-balanced, horizontally scalable** infrastructure.
- Includes **automated provisioning, metering, and self-service portals**.
- The *true* cloud-native SaaS ideal (e.g., Salesforce, Workday).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *"Explain SaaS maturity levels"*, always number them 1→4 and include the defining feature of each: (1) Custom, (2) Configurable, (3) Multi-tenant, (4) **Scalable multi-tenant with load balancing**.

---

## 2.3 SaaS Delivery & Deployment Variants

### By Delivery:
- **Public SaaS** — Hosted on a public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP). E.g., Gmail.
- **Private SaaS** — Hosted on a private cloud for one organization.
- **Hybrid SaaS** — Combines public + private; sensitive data stays private, rest is public.

### By Tenant Isolation:
- **Single-Tenant SaaS** — One customer per instance. (e.g., Hosted Exchange dedicated).
- **Multi-Tenant SaaS** — Many customers per instance. (e.g., Salesforce).

---

## 2.4 SaaS Pricing Models (Frequently Tested)

| Pricing Model | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-User / Per-Month (Subscription)** | Fixed fee per active user | Microsoft 365: ~**$12.50/user/month** |
| **Tiered Subscription** | Plans like Basic / Pro / Enterprise | Dropbox Basic, Plus, Family |
| **Usage-Based (Pay-as-you-go)** | Billed by API calls, storage, transactions | AWS Lambda, Twilio SMS |
| **Freemium** | Free basic tier, paid premium features | Slack, Zoom (40-min limit) |
| **Per-Feature** | Pay only for the modules used | Salesforce Sales Cloud / Service Cloud |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for SaaS over **N years** can be estimated as:
>
> $$\text{TCO}_{SaaS} = \sum_{t=1}^{N}\Big[(C_{sub,t} \times U_t) + C_{int,t} + C_{train,t}\Big]$$
>
> Where:
> - $C_{sub,t}$ = subscription cost per user in year $t$
> - $U_t$ = number of users in year $t$
> - $C_{int,t}$ = integration/customization cost in year $t$
> - $C_{train,t}$ = user training cost in year $t$
> - $N$ = total contract duration in years
>
> Compare against **On-Premise TCO**:
>
> $$\text{TCO}_{On-Prem} = C_{license} + \sum_{t=1}^{N}\Big[C_{HW,t} + C_{SW,t} + C_{IT-staff,t} + C_{power,t} + C_{cooling,t}\Big]$$
>
> SaaS becomes economical when $\text{TCO}_{SaaS} < \text{TCO}_{On-Prem}$ over the chosen horizon.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SaaS TCO (N years) | $\sum_{t=1}^{N}(C_{sub,t} U_t + C_{int,t} + C_{train,t})$ | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | Breakeven users | $U_{breakeven} = \dfrac{C_{OnPrem}}{C_{SaaS/user/yr}}$ | Users |
| 3 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 4 | Annual Downtime | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes/year |
| 5 | Multi-Tenant DB query | `SELECT * FROM data WHERE tenant_id = X` | Logical isolation |
| 6 | Pay-per-use bill | $B = \sum_{i} (Q_i \times P_i)$ | ₹ |
| 7 | SaaS Maturity | Levels 1→4 (Custom → Scalable Multi-tenant) | Categorical |
| 8 | Service Stack | SaaS > PaaS > IaaS > On-Premise | Layer order |
| 9 | User Responsibility | **DATA + ACCESS** only (in SaaS) | Conceptual |
| 10 | SLA Guarantee | Typical 99.9% ("three nines") | ≈ 8.76 hr/yr downtime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, **memorize the responsibility matrix** (Section 2.1) and the **4 maturity levels**. These appear every year in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks) questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of SaaS Concepts

- **Healthcare:** Hospital SaaS like *Epic Systems* for Electronic Health Records (EHR).
- **Banking:** SaaS-based core banking platforms (e.g., Mambu).
- **Education:** Google Classroom, Moodle Cloud — used by Kerala KTU universities.
- **Government:** IndiaStack, DigiLocker — citizen-service SaaS.
- **Startups:** SaaS lowers entry barriers; no CapEx on servers.
- **DevOps:** SaaS CI/CD tools (GitHub Actions, CircleCI).

> [!NOTE]
> India is currently the **3rd largest SaaS market globally** (after the US and China), with Bengaluru being called the *"SaaS capital of India"*. KTU examiners appreciate such contemporary context in answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Problems

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1: SaaS vs On-Premise TCO Comparison (7-Mark Style)

### **Problem (KTU Pattern):**
A startup has **100 employees** and needs an Office productivity suite for **5 years**.

- **On-Premise:** One-time license = **₹15,00,000**, annual hardware/IT/maintenance = **₹4,00,000/year**, training = **₹50,000/year** (year 1 only).
- **SaaS (Microsoft 365 Business Premium):** **₹1,500/user/month**, integration = **₹1,00,000/year**, training = **₹25,000 in year 1 only**.

Compute total TCO of both models and recommend the cheaper option.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute On-Premise TCO over 5 years.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{OnPrem} &= C_{license} + \sum_{t=1}^{5}(C_{HW} + C_{train,t}) \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 4{,}00{,}000 + 1 \times 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{35{,}50{,}000 \ \text{INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute SaaS TCO over 5 years.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sub,annual} &= 1500 \text{ ₹/user/month} \times 100 \text{ users} \times 12 \text{ months} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}500 \times 1200 = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{SaaS} &= \sum_{t=1}^{5}\Big[18{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000\Big] + 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times 19{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 95{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{95{,}25{,}000 \ \text{INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Recommendation.**

$$
\Delta \text{TCO} = \text{TCO}_{SaaS} - \text{TCO}_{OnPrem} = 95{,}25{,}000 - 35{,}50{,}000 = \mathbf{+59{,}75{,}000 \ \text{INR (extra)}}
$$

**Conclusion:** On-premise is **₹59.75 lakhs cheaper** over 5 years for 100 users. However, SaaS still wins on:
- **Zero upfront CapEx** (only OpEx)
- **Automatic updates & zero IT staff**
- **Immediate deployment (hours vs. weeks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** TCO alone doesn't capture *time-to-value* and *opportunity cost*. Mentioning these qualitative factors in your answer fetches full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2: Breakeven Analysis for SaaS Migration (7-Mark Style)

### **Problem:**
A firm currently spends **₹50,00,000** in Year 0 on on-premise setup (one-time). Annual on-prem maintenance is **₹8,00,000/year**. SaaS costs **₹60,000/user/year** with no setup cost. How many users make SaaS and On-Premise **equally costly** in a 5-year horizon?

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Write TCO equations.**

$$
\text{TCO}_{OnPrem} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000 \ \text{₹}
$$

$$
\text{TCO}_{SaaS} = 5 \times (60{,}000 \times U) = 3{,}00{,}000 \times U \ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 2: Equate to find breakeven users.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3{,}00{,}000 \times U &= 90{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
U_{breakeven} &= \dfrac{90{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{300 \ \text{users}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

| User Count | Cheaper Option |
|---|---|
| $U < 300$ | **SaaS** (cheaper) |
| $U = 300$ | Tie |
| $U > 300$ | **On-Premise** (cheaper at scale) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always include the *interpretation table* or a graph. Solutions without interpretation are penalized 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3: SaaS Availability & Downtime Calculation

### **Problem:**
A SaaS provider advertises **99.95% uptime SLA**. Compute the **maximum permissible annual downtime** in minutes and assess if it meets the requirement of a hospital EHR system needing 99.99%.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute allowed downtime for 99.95% SLA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} &= (1 - 0.9995) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0005 \times 5{,}25{,}600 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{262.8 \ \text{minutes/year}} \ (\approx 4.38 \text{ hours})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Required downtime for 99.99% SLA.**

$$
D_{hospital} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525600 = 0.0001 \times 525600 = \mathbf{52.56 \ \text{minutes/year}}
$$

**Step 3: Gap Analysis.**

$$
\Delta D = 262.8 - 52.56 = \mathbf{210.24 \ \text{minutes extra}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 99.95% SaaS does **NOT** meet the hospital's 99.99% requirement. The hospital must negotiate a **premium tier** with the provider, or use a **multi-region failover** deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Reference (SLA → Annual Downtime):**
> - 99% ("two nines") → **5256 min** (3.65 days)
> - 99.9% ("three nines") → **525.6 min** (8.76 hours)
> - 99.95% ("three-and-a-half nines") → **262.8 min**
> - 99.99% ("four nines") → **52.56 min**
> - 99.999% ("five nines") → **5.26 min**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SaaS Multi-Tenant Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA["Tenant A - Acme Corp"]:::tenant
    userB["Tenant B - BetaTech"]:::tenant
    userC["Tenant C - GammaInc"]:::tenant
    userD["Tenant D - Delta Ltd"]:::tenant
    
    lb["Load Balancer"]:::infra
    
    appInst["SaaS Application Instance - Shared"]:::app
    
    db["Shared Database with tenant_id column"]:::data
    
    auth["OAuth 2.0 / SAML Auth Service"]:::sec
    api["REST API Gateway"]:::api
    monitor["Metering and Billing Service"]:::sec
    config["Tenant Configuration Metadata"]:::config
    
    userA --> lb
    userB --> lb
    userC --> lb
    userD --> lb
    
    lb --> auth
    auth --> appInst
    appInst --> api
    api --> db
    appInst --> config
    appInst --> monitor
    
    classDef tenant fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block illustrates **Level 4 SaaS maturity** — a single shared application instance, single database with **tenant_id**-based row-level isolation, served through a load balancer, with separate authentication, API, configuration, and metering services.

---

## 4.2 Cloud Service Stack — Responsibility Ownership

```mermaid

    subgraph OnPrem["On-Premise: User Manages EVERYTHING"]
        op1["Application"]

```

---

## 4.3 SaaS Request Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as End User Browser
    participant CDN as CDN and Edge Cache
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant AUTH as Identity Provider
    participant APP as SaaS Application Server
    participant DB as Multi-Tenant Database
    participant BILL as Metering and Billing

    U->>CDN: GET https://app.saas.com/login
    CDN-->>U: Cached static assets (HTML, CSS, JS)
    U->>AUTH: Submit credentials (OAuth/SAML)
    AUTH-->>U: JWT token (signed)
    U->>LB: API call with JWT
    LB->>APP: Route to healthy instance
    APP->>DB: Query with tenant_id filter
    DB-->>APP: Tenant-specific rows
    APP-->>U: Rendered response
    APP->>BILL: Emit usage metric (CPU/Storage/API call)
    BILL-->>U: Monthly invoice (pay-per-use)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Expectation:** A well-labeled SaaS architecture showing **tenants → load balancer → application instance → multi-tenant database** with a **tenant_id** separator fetches full marks in the diagram question (typically 4–5 marks of a 14-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define Software-as-a-Service (SaaS). List any four characteristics of SaaS.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) is a cloud computing model in which a third-party provider hosts software applications and makes them available to end-users over the Internet, typically on a subscription basis. The provider manages the underlying infrastructure, middleware, runtime, OS, and the application itself; the user only manages their data and access.

**Characteristics (1.5 marks — list any 4):**
1. Multi-tenancy — single instance serves multiple customers
2. Browser-based thin-client access
3. Subscription-based pricing
4. Automatic updates and patching by the provider
5. Scalable and elastic resource provisioning
6. Measured/metered service

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`
**Differentiate between SaaS and PaaS with respect to user responsibility, examples, and target audience.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | SaaS | PaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **User manages** | Data + Access only | Applications + Data |
| **Provider manages** | Everything else incl. OS, runtime, app | OS, runtime, middleware |
| **Target user** | End-users / business users | Developers |
| **Examples** | Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365 | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku |
| **Skill required** | None (just login) | Programming / DevOps |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Answer any one full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **cloud computing service models** (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with a neat diagram showing the responsibility split. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **multi-tenant architecture** of SaaS. Explain the different levels of SaaS maturity with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Service Models (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Cloud computing is classified by NIST into three primary service models — IaaS (Infrastructure), PaaS (Platform), and SaaS (Software). They differ in **what the user manages** and **what the provider manages**.

**IaaS (2 marks):**
- Provider supplies raw compute, storage, and networking (virtualized).
- User installs OS, middleware, runtime, and applications.
- Examples: **Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets**.
- Best for: System administrators, IT architects.

**PaaS (2 marks):**
- Provider supplies the *platform* — OS, middleware, runtime, databases.
- User only writes code and uploads it.
- Examples: **Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Microsoft Azure App Service**.
- Best for: Application developers.

**SaaS (2 marks):**
- Provider supplies the *finished application*.
- User simply logs in via browser.
- Examples: **Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Zoom**.
- Best for: End-users / business users.

*(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for a 1-mark visual.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Multi-Tenant SaaS Maturity (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** **Multi-tenancy** means a *single application instance* serves *multiple customer organizations (tenants)*, with each tenant's data and configuration kept logically isolated.

**Why multi-tenant? (1 mark):** Maximizes **resource utilization**, lowers per-tenant cost, simplifies **patching/upgrades** (one deploy reaches all), and enables **horizontal scaling**.

**Four Maturity Levels (4 marks):**

| Level | Name | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ad-Hoc / Custom | Each tenant gets a *customized, separate* instance | Legacy enterprise software |
| 2 | Configurable | Single instance, per-tenant configuration in metadata | Early Salesforce |
| 3 | Multi-Tenant Efficient | Single instance, single database, shared schema, **tenant_id** column | Modern Salesforce, Workday |
| 4 | Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant | Adds **load balancing, auto-scaling, metering, self-service** | Salesforce Lightning, AWS Marketplace SaaS |

**Challenges of Multi-Tenancy (1 mark):** Data leakage prevention, noisy-neighbor effect, per-tenant customization limits, regulatory compliance (GDPR, DPDP Act 2023).

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Past Pattern]`

**(a)** Compare **SaaS and On-Premise software deployment** with respect to cost, control, scalability, maintenance, and security. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SaaS provider advertises **99.9% uptime SLA**. A retail e-commerce company claims downtime costs them **₹50,000 per hour**. Compute the **(i)** maximum allowed annual downtime and **(ii)** the maximum tolerable business loss in a year due to SLA-permitted downtime. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — SaaS vs On-Premise (7 Marks)

| Parameter | On-Premise | SaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **Upfront Cost (CapEx)** | High (servers, licenses, setup) | **Zero** (subscription only) |
| **Operating Cost (OpEx)** | IT staff, electricity, cooling, AMC | **Pay-per-use subscription** |
| **Control** | **Full** control over hardware, OS, data | Limited (vendor lock-in risk) |
| **Scalability** | Manual, slow (procure new hardware) | **Elastic, near-instant** |
| **Maintenance** | Customer's IT team | **Provider's responsibility** |
| **Security** | Customer's responsibility; isolated | Provider-managed; shared responsibility model |
| **Updates / Patches** | Manual, scheduled | **Automatic, transparent** |
| **Time to Deploy** | Weeks to months | **Hours to days** |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Customer must build | **Provider offers built-in DR** |
| **Best For** | Regulated industries (defense, banking core) | SMEs, startups, fast-scaling teams |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** SaaS is preferred for agility, low CapEx, and rapid scaling; on-premise remains relevant for highly regulated or data-sovereignty-critical workloads.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — SLA Downtime & Loss Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- SLA = **99.9%**
- Business loss rate = **₹50,000 per hour**

**Step 1: Compute maximum allowed annual downtime in minutes (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 99.9\% = 0.999 \\[4pt]
D_{max} &= (1 - 0.999) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\[4pt]
&= 0.001 \times 525600 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{525.6 \ \text{minutes/year}} \ (\approx 8.76 \text{ hours})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert downtime to hours (1 mark).**

$$
D_{hours} = \dfrac{525.6}{60} = \mathbf{8.76 \ \text{hours/year}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute maximum tolerable business loss (2 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= D_{hours} \times 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 8.76 \times 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{4{,}38{,}000 \ \text{INR/year}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks).**
- The provider is contractually allowed to be down for **525.6 minutes** (≈ 8.76 hours) per year.
- The retail company can suffer a maximum revenue impact of **₹4,38,000/year** under the SLA.
- To reduce this loss, the company should negotiate a **higher SLA tier (e.g., 99.95% or 99.99%)** or use **multi-region active-active deployment** to exceed the SLA's permitted downtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert SLA percentage to decimal before subtracting from 1. *(Use $1 - 0.999 = 0.001$, not $1 - 99.9$.)* **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $365 \times 24$ only, forgetting to multiply by 60 to get minutes. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting minutes → hours before multiplying by hourly loss rate. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the formula but skipping the interpretation/justification. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - **Pro Tip:** Always end a numerical answer with a *one-line managerial interpretation* — this differentiates a 6-mark answer from a 7-mark one.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **SaaS = Software hosted by provider, accessed via browser, paid by subscription.**
- ✅ **User responsibility in SaaS = DATA + USER ACCESS only** *(everything else is the provider's).*
- ✅ **SaaS Maturity Levels (1→4):** Custom → Configurable → Multi-tenant Efficient → **Scalable Multi-tenant**.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy key feature:** single instance, **tenant_id**-based row isolation.
- ✅ **Examples to quote:** Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Zoom, Dropbox, Google Workspace.
- ✅ **SLA Quick Table:** 99% → 3.65 days; 99.9% → 8.76 hr; 99.99% → 52.56 min; 99.999% → 5.26 min.
- ✅ **TCO formula:** $\sum (C_{sub} \times U) + C_{int} + C_{train}$ over $N$ years.
- ✅ **Breakeven users:** $U_{be} = \dfrac{\text{TCO}_{OnPrem}}{C_{SaaS/user/yr} \times N}$.
- ✅ **Cloud service stack (top → bottom):** SaaS → PaaS → IaaS → On-Premise.
- ✅ **Pricing models:** Per-user subscription, Tiered, Pay-per-use, Freemium, Per-feature.
- ✅ **Delivery models:** Public, Private, Hybrid SaaS.
- ✅ **NIST essential characteristics:** On-demand, Broad network, Pooling, Elasticity, Measured.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — it fetches 1–2 bonus marks.
- ✅ **Always end numerical answers with a managerial interpretation** — full marks guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: XaaS (Anything-as-a-Service) - Cloud Deployment Models - Public Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** XaaS (Anything-as-a-Service) - Cloud Deployment Models - Public Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 XaaS — Anything-as-a-Service

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
**XaaS (Anything-as-a-Service)** is the umbrella cloud-computing paradigm in which *any* product, tool, technology, or computing resource that can be delivered over the internet is provisioned to a consumer on a pay-per-use / subscription basis, abstracting away the underlying infrastructure, platform, runtime, and management overhead. It is the superset that encompasses SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, and the newer "as-a-Service" categories (DaaS, STaaS, SECaaS, AIaaS, FaaS, DBaaS, MBaaS, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"XaaS refers to the delivery of any IT-related capability — hardware, software, storage, network, platform, database, security, desktop, AI/ML model, or even business process — as a service over the Internet, where the consumer consumes the capability on demand without owning or managing the underlying stack."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Electricity Grid"**
> Think of XaaS exactly like the **electricity you receive at home**. You do **not** own a power plant, do not lay transmission lines, do not maintain transformers. You simply plug in a device and pay the utility company only for the kilowatt-hours you actually consume. The *electricity* is the "service," the *wires/grid* is the "infrastructure," and the *meter* is the "metering/billing system." If we replace *electricity* with *computing power / software / storage / AI model / database*, we get **XaaS**.

---

### 1.2 Cloud Deployment Models

A **Cloud Deployment Model** defines *who owns the cloud infrastructure, where it physically resides, who can access it, and how it is operated* (i.e., the boundary of tenancy, ownership, and exposure).

The four canonical KTU-defined deployment models are:

1. **Public Cloud** — owned by a third-party provider, open to the general public/public-paying tenants.
2. **Private Cloud** — provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (public + private) bound by standardized technology.
4. **Community Cloud** — provisioned for exclusive use by a specific community of consumers with shared concerns (e.g., mission, security, policy).

---

### 1.3 Public Cloud — Core Definition

**Formal Definition:**
A **Public Cloud** is a cloud deployment model in which the cloud infrastructure is *owned, managed, and operated* by a **third-party Cloud Service Provider (CSP)**, and the computing resources (servers, storage, networks, applications, services) are *provisioned for open use by the general public or a broad industry group* over the public Internet, on a pay-per-use consumption model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Public Cloud is a cloud infrastructure made available to the public or a large industry group and is owned by an organization selling cloud services. The provider is responsible for the creation and ongoing maintenance of the public cloud and its IT resources."* — *Module 1, KTU Cloud Computing (PEEET637) Syllabus, 2024 Scheme.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "A Public Bus Service"**
> A **public cloud** is to IT what a **public bus service (KSRTC / DTC)** is to transport:
> - The **bus, depot, driver, fuel** are owned by the *transport corporation* (CSP — e.g., AWS).
> - You, the **passenger** (tenant), only **buy a ticket** (pay-as-you-go).
> - The bus route is **shared** with hundreds of other passengers (multi-tenancy).
> - You can **board and alight at any stop** (elastic on-demand provisioning).
> - You do **not** control where the depot is, who the driver is, or how the engine is maintained (no infrastructure control).

---

### 1.4 Standard Public Cloud Metrics (KTU Must-Know Bold Terms)

- **Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple customers share the *same physical resources* in a logically isolated fashion.
- **Elasticity** — Ability to *scale resources out/in* in response to demand in near real-time.
- **Pay-per-Use (Metered Billing)** — Billing is based on *actual consumption* (compute-seconds, GB-month, requests).
- **Self-Service Provisioning** — Tenant can provision resources *without human interaction* from the CSP.
- **Resource Pooling** — Provider's resources are pooled to serve *multiple consumers* using a multi-tenant model.
- **Broad Network Access** — Capabilities are available over the *public Internet* via standard protocols on heterogeneous thin/heavy clients.
- **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically *control and optimize resource use* by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the type of service.

> [!TIP]
> **NIST SP 800-145 — Five Essential Characteristics of a Cloud (Public Cloud must satisfy all):**
> 1. On-demand self-service
> 2. Broad network access
> 3. Resource pooling
> 4. Rapid elasticity
> 5. Measured service
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The XaaS Hierarchy — Layered Service Stack

XaaS is best understood as a **layered abstraction stack**. As you move *up* the stack, the consumer manages less and the provider manages more. As you move *down* the stack, the consumer gets more control and flexibility.

**Layered Logic (Bottom → Top):**

- **Layer 0 — On-Premises (No Cloud)**
  *Consumer manages*: Applications + Data + Runtime + Middleware + OS + Virtualization + Servers + Storage + Networking.
  *Provider manages*: Nothing.

- **Layer 1 — IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)**
  *Consumer manages*: Applications + Data + Runtime + Middleware + OS.
  *Provider manages*: Virtualization + Servers + Storage + Networking.
  *Examples*: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine (GCE).

- **Layer 2 — PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)**
  *Consumer manages*: Applications + Data.
  *Provider manages*: Runtime + Middleware + OS + Virtualization + Servers + Storage + Networking.
  *Examples*: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Heroku.

- **Layer 3 — SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)**
  *Consumer manages*: Only user-level configuration and data entry.
  *Provider manages*: Everything else (the full stack).
  *Examples*: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox, Zoom.

- **Layer 4 — FaaS / Serverless (Function-as-a-Service)**
  *Consumer manages*: Only the function code and event triggers.
  *Provider manages*: Entire execution environment, scaling, patching.
  *Examples*: AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.

- **Layer 5 — XaaS (Any Emerging "*aaS*")**
  Anything *beyond* the above is XaaS — DBaaS, SECaaS, AIaaS, IoTaaS, DaaS, STaaS, MBaaS, etc.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind XaaS

> [!NOTE]
> **Why XaaS exists — the underlying business & engineering drivers:**

1. **CapEx → OpEx shift** — Capital expenditure becomes predictable operating expenditure.
2. **Elasticity of demand** — Traditional IT cannot scale from 10 to 10,000 users in minutes; XaaS can.
3. **Time-to-Market** — Pre-built services (e.g., Twilio for SMS, Stripe for payments) eliminate months of development.
4. **Specialization** — Providers (AWS, Azure) operate at a scale where their per-unit cost is unbeatable.
5. **Innovation Democratization** — A startup gets the *same* AI infrastructure as a Fortune 500 company.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Cloud Cost & SLA Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Formulas (Use these in numerical problems):**

**Formula 1 — Pay-per-Use Total Cost:**

$$
\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( P_{i} \times U_{i} \right)
$$

Where:
- $P_{i}$ = per-unit price of resource $i$ (e.g., ₹/GB-month, ₹/compute-hour)
- $U_{i}$ = units consumed of resource $i$
- $n$ = number of distinct metered resources

**Formula 2 — Cloud Service Availability (Downtime Tolerance):**

$$
\text{Availability (\%)} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Annual Downtime} = (1 - \text{Availability}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Formula 3 — SLA Compensation:**

$$
\text{Refund \%} = f(\text{Actual Availability}, \text{Committed Availability})
$$

**Formula 4 — Elastic Scaling Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{scale}} = \frac{U_{\text{actual}}}{U_{\text{provisioned}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where $U_{\text{actual}}$ is the utilized resources and $U_{\text{provisioned}}$ is the peak provisioned capacity.

**Formula 5 — Storage Cost Over Time:**

$$
\text{Storage Cost} = D \times C_{s} \times T + B_{\text{out}} \times C_{bw}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = data size in GB
- $C_{s}$ = storage cost per GB-month
- $T$ = time in months
- $B_{\text{out}}$ = egress bandwidth in GB
- $C_{bw}$ = bandwidth cost per GB

---

### 2.4 Public Cloud Architecture (Layered Reference Model)

**Operational Layers of a Public Cloud:**

- **Physical Layer** — Datacenters with servers, storage arrays, network switches, power & cooling.
- **Virtualization / Hypervisor Layer** — VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen, Hyper-V; abstracts physical hardware into VMs.
- **Resource Pool Layer** — Pools of compute, storage, and network; supports multi-tenancy.
- **Service Orchestration Layer** — Manages provisioning, monitoring, billing (e.g., AWS CloudFormation, Azure Resource Manager).
- **Service Layer (API Gateway)** — Public REST/SDK endpoints exposed to tenants.
- **Consumer Layer** — Tenants access via Web Console, CLI, or API.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> This layered model is the **mental blueprint** for every KTU numerical on cloud cost, SLA, and capacity planning. It also appears verbatim in 14-mark questions on "Explain the public cloud architecture with a neat diagram."

### 2.5 KTU Quick-Reference Comparison Table — Service Models

| Service Model | Consumer Manages | Provider Manages | Provisioning Unit | KTU Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **On-Premises** | Full Stack | None | Bare-metal server | University Datacenter |
| **IaaS** | OS, Middleware, App, Data | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | VM / Bare-metal | AWS EC2 |
| **PaaS** | App, Data | Runtime to Network | Application container / platform | Google App Engine |
| **SaaS** | User config only | Full Stack | Software license subscription | Microsoft 365 |
| **FaaS** | Function code, triggers | Full runtime, scaling, patching | Event-driven function | AWS Lambda |
| **XaaS** | Domain-specific config | Domain-specific service | Domain unit | DBaaS, SECaaS, AIaaS |

### 2.6 KTU Quick-Reference Comparison Table — Deployment Models

| Property | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Owner** | Third-party CSP | Single Organization | Org + CSP | Community of Orgs |
| **Location** | Off-premise (anywhere) | On-premise / hosted | Both | On / Off-premise |
| **Tenants** | Open to public | Single | Multiple (linked) | Shared concern group |
| **Cost Model** | OpEx (pay-per-use) | CapEx heavy | Mixed | Shared CapEx/OpEx |
| **Security Level** | Medium (shared) | High (dedicated) | Configurable | High (regulated) |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by private infra | Burst to public | Limited |
| **KTU Example** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack on-prem | AWS + on-prem DC | Govt. cloud for banks |
| **Typical SLA** | 99.9% – 99.99% | 99.99% (custom) | Tiered | 99.95% (custom) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — "Who-Manages-What" Responsibility Distribution

**Logic of the matrix:**
For each *service model* (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), the **IT stack** is partitioned vertically between *Consumer* and *Provider*. The diagonal shifts *up* as we move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.

Let the IT stack be the set $S = \{App, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virt., Server, Storage, Network\}$.

Let $C_{IaaS}, C_{PaaS}, C_{SaaS}$ denote the *consumer's responsibility set* for each model.

$$
C_{IaaS} = \{App, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS\}
$$

$$
C_{PaaS} = \{App, Data\}
$$

$$
C_{SaaS} = \{\text{User-level config}, \text{User data entry}\}
$$

And the *provider's responsibility set* is simply the complement:

$$
P_{X} = S \setminus C_{X}
$$

**Worked-Out Numerical — Pay-per-Use Billing:**

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A startup uses AWS EC2 at **₹8 per compute-hour**, EBS storage at **₹2 per GB-month**, and S3 egress at **₹0.50 per GB**. In March 2024, they consumed **720 compute-hours**, stored **500 GB** for the month, and downloaded **300 GB** of data. Calculate the total bill.

**Step 1 — Identify the meter units.**

Compute: hours, Storage: GB-month, Bandwidth: GB.

**Step 2 — Apply the per-resource cost formula:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{compute} = P_{c} \times U_{c} = 8 \times 720
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{compute} = 5760 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Storage cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{storage} = P_{s} \times U_{s} = 2 \times 500
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{storage} = 1000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Egress bandwidth cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{bw} = P_{b} \times U_{b} = 0.50 \times 300
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{bw} = 150 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all components:**

$$
\text{Total Bill} = \text{Cost}_{compute} + \text{Cost}_{storage} + \text{Cost}_{bw}
$$

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 5760 + 1000 + 150 = 6910 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Final Answer:** **₹6,910**.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical — SLA Availability & Downtime

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A public cloud provider commits to **99.95% monthly availability** for a managed database service. In a 30-day month, the service experienced **two outages** of **12 minutes** and **25 minutes**. Verify whether the SLA was violated, and if so, compute the refund percentage as per the policy: **10% credit if availability < 99.95%, 25% if < 99.0%, 50% if < 95.0%**.

**Step 1 — Total minutes in the billing month:**

$$
T_{total} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43200 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Total downtime observed:**

$$
T_{down} = 12 + 25 = 37 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the actual availability:**

$$
A_{actual} = \frac{T_{total} - T_{down}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
A_{actual} = \frac{43200 - 37}{43200} \times 100
$$

$$
A_{actual} = \frac{43163}{43200} \times 100 = 99.9144\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with committed SLA (99.95%):**

$$
99.9144\% < 99.95\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SLA is VIOLATED.}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the refund tier:**

Since $95.0\% \le 99.9144\% < 99.95\%$, the **10% credit tier** applies.

**Step 6 — Compute the refund (if monthly bill = ₹50,000):**

$$
\text{Refund} = 0.10 \times 50000 = 5000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Final Answer:** **SLA violated; 10% credit applicable; Refund = ₹5,000.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — XaaS Sub-Categories (Memorize for KTU)

| XaaS Sub-Type | Full Form | What is Delivered | KTU Industry Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Ready-to-use software | Salesforce, Google Workspace |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Application runtime platform | Heroku, Google App Engine |
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | VMs, storage, network | AWS EC2, Azure VMs |
| **FaaS** | Function-as-a-Service | Event-driven functions | AWS Lambda |
| **DaaS** | Desktop-as-a-Service | Virtual Desktop (VDI) | Amazon WorkSpaces, Citrix Cloud |
| **DBaaS** | Database-as-a-Service | Managed database engines | Amazon RDS, Azure Cosmos DB |
| **STaaS** | Storage-as-a-Service | Object / block / file storage | AWS S3, Azure Blob |
| **SECaaS** | Security-as-a-Service | Security controls (WAF, IAM) | AWS Shield, Cloudflare |
| **AIaaS** | AI-as-a-Service | Pre-trained ML models / APIs | AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI |
| **MBaaS** | Mobile-Backend-as-a-Service | Mobile app backends | Firebase, AWS Amplify |
| **IoTaaS** | IoT-as-a-Service | IoT device + data ingestion | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub |
| **BaaS** | Blockchain-as-a-Service | Distributed ledger | IBM Blockchain Platform |
| **NaaS** | Network-as-a-Service | Virtual networks, SD-WAN | Cisco Meraki, AWS VPC |
| **DRaaS** | Disaster-Recovery-as-a-Service | Backup + DR orchestration | Azure Site Recovery |
| **MaaS** | Monitoring-as-a-Service | Metrics, logs, traces | Datadog, New Relic |
