<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Hybrid Cloud. List any two advantages of a hybrid cloud over a pure public cloud deployment.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Cloud** is a cloud computing deployment model that combines **private cloud infrastructure** (on-premises or hosted) with **public cloud services**, bound together by **standardized technologies** (APIs, VPNs, container orchestrators) that enable **data and application portability** between the two environments (NIST SP 500-292).

**Two advantages over pure public cloud:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance & Data Sovereignty:** Sensitive data (e.g., medical records, financial transactions) can remain on private infrastructure under strict regulatory control, while non-sensitive workloads leverage public-cloud elasticity. Pure public cloud forces all data into the provider's jurisdiction, which is unacceptable in sectors like BFSI and defense.

2. **Lower Latency for Edge Workloads:** Workloads requiring **sub-10 ms response times** (factory automation, trading systems, hospital equipment) can run on private cloud close to the data source, while still benefiting from public-cloud burst capacity for non-latency-critical tasks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is a "cloud burst"? Explain in the context of hybrid cloud.**

**Model Answer:**

A **cloud burst** is a configuration pattern in which a **private cloud application** automatically offloads ("bursts") excess compute demand to a **public cloud** when private-cloud capacity is exhausted, and de-provisions those public resources when demand subsides.

**Context in hybrid cloud:** The hybrid architecture provides the **interconnect (VPN/Direct Connect)** and **orchestration (Kubernetes, Anthos)** needed to make cloud bursting seamless. For example, a private e-commerce site running steady traffic of 1000 users may **burst to AWS** during a festival sale when traffic spikes to 100,000 users. The hybrid model combines the **cost efficiency of private** for baseline load with the **elasticity of public** for peaks — a hallmark use case.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Components Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a hybrid cloud**. Enumerate the **key components** at each layer and justify why a unified orchestration layer is essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Hybrid Cloud** with **Public Cloud** and **Private Cloud** along **any seven parameters** (e.g., cost, security, scalability, compliance, latency, control, complexity). Conclude with a recommendation on when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

A hybrid cloud architecture has the following layers (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

**Layer 1 — Private Cloud Substrate:** Hosts legacy ERP, core databases, and sensitive workloads. Built on **VMware vSphere, OpenStack, or Hyper-V**. Protected by **NGFW, IDS/IPS, and segmentation**.

**Layer 2 — Public Cloud Substrate:** Provides elastic compute (EC2, Azure VMs), managed Kubernetes (EKS, AKS), object storage (S3, Blob), and serverless functions (Lambda). Chosen for **burst, ML training, and global CDN**.

**Layer 3 — Secure Interconnect:** Two options:
- **IPsec VPN** over the public internet (cheap, ~50–100 ms RTT).
- **Dedicated interconnect** like AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute (expensive, ~5–15 ms RTT, guaranteed bandwidth).

**Layer 4 — Unified Orchestration Layer:** Tools like **Anthos, Azure Arc, OpenShift** provide a **single control plane** spanning both private and public Kubernetes clusters. This is essential because:
- It enables **GitOps deployments** where the same manifest deploys to both environments.
- It provides **consistent security policy** (Pod Security Admission, OPA Gatekeeper).
- It abstracts **infrastructure differences** so developers treat both environments uniformly.

**Layer 5 — Identity Federation:** A **SAML/OIDC broker** (Azure AD, Okta) issues a single identity honored by both private AD and public-cloud IAM.

**Layer 6 — Data Integration:** Tools like **Apache Kafka, AWS DataSync, Azure Data Factory** implement **ETL/ELT** pipelines.

**Layer 7 — Observability:** **Prometheus + Grafana + Loki** (or commercial tools like Datadog) deliver **unified metrics, logs, and traces**.

> A block diagram (see Section 4.1) is mandatory for full marks.

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost Model** | Pure OpEx (pay-per-use) | CapEx-heavy (upfront purchase) | Mixed CapEx + OpEx |
| **Security & Isolation** | Shared responsibility, multi-tenant | Full physical & logical isolation | Mixed: sensitive on private, rest on public |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by physical hardware | Combined — private baseline + public burst |
| **Compliance** | Limited (data residency issues) | Full control, easy audits | Best of both — comply on private, scale on public |
| **Latency** | Higher (WAN hops) | Very low (LAN) | Tunable per workload |
| **Control & Customization** | Low (provider-driven) | Very high (full stack) | High where it matters (private) |
| **Operational Complexity** | Lowest (provider manages) | Highest (you manage) | Medium (both layers + interconnect) |

**Recommendation:**
- **Public:** Startups, unpredictable workloads, global SaaS.
- **Private:** Banks, defense, hospitals, highly regulated enterprises with steady load.
- **Hybrid (preferred for most):** Enterprises with mixed compliance + burst needs — typical large-scale KTU-case-study answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Economic & Strategic Focus (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** A company is evaluating whether to host a workload on a **private server** (CapEx = ₹30 lakh, OpEx = ₹8 lakh/year, useful life 5 years, discount rate 12%) or on a **public cloud** at ₹200/hour. The workload requires **5000 compute-hours/year**. Compute the **TCO for both options** and recommend. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **cloud bursting** and **data gravity** in a hybrid cloud context. Describe how an organization can use **Kubernetes federation** to implement seamless bursting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Private Cloud TCO:**

Using $TCO_{\text{private}} = C_0 + C_{\text{opex}} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r}$:

$$
TCO_{\text{private}} = 30,00,000 + 8,00,000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12}
$$

Compute the annuity factor:

$$
\frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048
$$

$$
TCO_{\text{private}} = 30,00,000 + 8,00,000 \times 3.6048 = 30,00,000 + 28,83,840 = 58,83,840 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Public Cloud TCO:**

$$
TCO_{\text{public}} = 5 \text{ years} \times 5000 \text{ hrs/yr} \times 200 \text{ ₹/hr} = 50,00,000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
\Delta TCO = 58,83,840 - 50,00,000 = 8,83,840 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Recommendation:** **Public cloud is cheaper by ₹8.83 lakh** over 5 years. However, the organization may still choose a **hybrid approach** — keep a small private footprint for compliance-sensitive data and burst the bulk of compute to the public cloud. Pure public cloud is recommended only if there is no compliance barrier.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Cloud Bursting:** As defined in Q2 (Part A), cloud bursting is the dynamic offload of compute load from a saturated private cloud to a public cloud. In a hybrid setup, the **load balancer detects capacity exhaustion** and routes excess requests to a **public-cloud auto-scaling group**. When load subsides, the public instances are **terminated** to stop billing. The user experiences **uninterrupted service** and the organization pays only for peak-time public usage.

**Data Gravity:** Coined by **Dave McCrory (2010)**, data gravity is the phenomenon where **large datasets attract applications and services toward them**, because moving large data across the network is expensive and slow. In a hybrid cloud, this means:
- Massive datasets (e.g., 10 PB of genomic data) should be **co-located with the compute** that processes them — either fully in the public cloud or fully in the private cloud, not split.
- Architectural decisions should **bring compute to data**, not the other way around.
- Tools like **AWS Snowmobile** (physical data truck) or **Azure Data Box** address data-gravity challenges by physically moving bulk data.

**Kubernetes Federation for Seamless Bursting:**

**Kubernetes Federation (Karmada / Kubefed v2):**

1. **Federated control plane** manages member clusters in both private and public clouds.
2. **ReplicaSchedulingPreference** distributes replicas across clusters based on cost, latency, and capacity rules.
3. A **horizontal pod autoscaler** is configured to spill new pods to the public-cloud cluster when the private cluster's CPU > 70%.
4. **Cluster Autoscaler** in the public cloud **provisions new nodes** to host the spilled pods.
5. **Istio service mesh** routes traffic between pods regardless of which cluster they reside in.
6. When load subsides, **Cluster Autoscaler terminates** public nodes, and HPA scales down replicas — cost goes back to baseline.

This delivers a **truly elastic hybrid experience**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Hybrid with Multi-Cloud:** Hybrid = private + public tied together with orchestration. Multi-Cloud = multiple public providers (e.g., AWS + Azure). Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the Interconnect:** A common 7-mark answer describes "private + public" but omits the **VPN / Direct Connect / API Gateway layer**. Always mention the **glue**.
> 3. **Skipping the Diagram:** Any architecture question without a labelled block diagram loses at least **2 marks** in the 7-mark sub-parts.
> 4. **Ignoring TCO Math:** A pure narrative answer on cost comparison without **TCO formula or numerical computation** gets partial credit at best. Always show the math.
> 5. **Missing Real Tools:** Mentioning only generic "VPN" and "API" without naming **AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, Anthos, Azure Arc, OpenShift** weakens the answer.
> 6. **Ignoring Compliance Angle:** RBI, HIPAA, GDPR, ITAR, MeitY rules must be cited to justify *why* hybrid exists.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hybrid Cloud = Private Cloud + Public Cloud + Interconnect + Orchestration.** Never reduce it to just "two clouds."
- **NIST SP 500-292** is the formal reference — cite it in definition questions.
- **Three primary drivers:** Compliance, cost optimization, latency + scale.
- **Seven architectural layers:** Private substrate, Public substrate, Interconnect, Orchestration, IAM, Data integration, Observability.
- **Two interconnect options:** IPsec VPN (cheap, higher latency) vs. Direct Connect (expensive, low latency).
- **Cloud Bursting** is the canonical workload-mobility pattern: private handles baseline, public handles peak.
- **Data Gravity** is a key constraint — bring compute to data, not vice versa.
- **TCO Formula:** $TCO = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{C_{\text{opex}}(t)}{(1+r)^{t}}$. Always show this for cost-based questions.
- **Break-even point** $BEP_{\text{hrs}}$ tells you the threshold above which private becomes cheaper.
- **Composite availability:** $A_{\text{hybrid}} = 1 - (1-A_p)(1-A_{pub})$ — two redundant components yield exponentially higher reliability.
- **Unified Orchestration** is provided by **Anthos, Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Red Hat OpenShift** — name at least one.
- **Identity Federation** uses **SAML 2.0 / OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect** brokers like **Azure AD or Okta**.
- **Hybrid ≠ Multi-Cloud** — memorize this distinction.
- **Use Cases to Memorize:** Banking (HDFC), Healthcare (Apollo), E-Commerce (Flipkart), Research (CERN), Government (Smart Cities).
- **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level:** Expect **L1 (Remember) – L4 (Analyze)** questions; TCO calculations and architecture diagrams are **L4 / L5 (Evaluate/Create)**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for any 7+ mark question on hybrid cloud.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Virtualization - Virtualizing Physical Computing Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Virtualization - Virtualizing Physical Computing Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtualization** is the strategic abstraction and logical partitioning of physical computing resources (CPU, Memory, Storage, and Network) into multiple virtual instances, each behaving as an independent, isolated computing environment, thereby enabling efficient resource utilization, workload isolation, and on-demand scalability.

In the context of **Cloud Computing**, virtualization is the foundational technology that allows a single physical machine (the **Host**) to be transformed into multiple independent virtual machines (the **Guests**), each with its own operating system, applications, and dedicated virtual hardware resources.

### 1.2 What are "Physical Computing Resources"?

The four primary physical resources that are virtualized in any cloud data center are:

| # | Resource | Physical Entity | Virtual Counterpart |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Compute** | Physical CPU cores, sockets, threads | **vCPU (Virtual CPU)** |
| 2 | **Memory** | Physical RAM (DDR DIMMs) | **Virtual Memory / Guest RAM** |
| 3 | **Storage** | HDDs, SSDs, NVMe drives | **Virtual Disks (VMDK, VHD, QCOW2)** |
| 4 | **Network** | Physical NICs, switches, routers | **vNIC, Virtual Switches, VLANs** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Virtualizing Physical Computing Resources" specifically means mapping *abstract* virtual resources onto *concrete* physical hardware using a software layer called the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** or **Hypervisor**.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Apartment Building vs. Independent House**
>
> Imagine you have a large plot of land. **Without virtualization (Non-virtualized era):** Each family needs an entire independent house. 10 families = 10 houses, 10 kitchens, 10 power connections, 10 water tanks — even if most houses are mostly empty.
>
> **With Virtualization (Cloud era):** You build **one large apartment building (Physical Server)** with a professional manager (**Hypervisor**) who allocates specific apartments (**Virtual Machines**) to each family. Each family gets:
> - A private, locked apartment (Isolation)
> - Their own kitchen, bathroom (Dedicated virtual resources)
> - A share of the building's generator, water supply (Pooled physical resources)
> - The flexibility to expand or shrink their apartment anytime (Elasticity)
>
> The land (physical hardware) is used **far more efficiently**, and the manager ensures families don't disturb each other.

### 1.4 Why Virtualization? The Core Motivations

1. **Server Consolidation** — Reducing the "server sprawl" problem. Industry average consolidation ratio is **8:1 to 15:1**.
2. **Resource Utilization** — Bare-metal servers typically run at only **5%–15%** CPU utilization. Virtualization pushes this to **60%–80%**.
3. **Hardware Independence** — VMs are portable files (`OVF`, `OVA`) that can move between physical hosts.
4. **Cost Reduction** — Fewer physical servers = lower CapEx (hardware), OpEx (power, cooling, space).
5. **Disaster Recovery** — VMs can be snapshotted, copied, and restored in seconds.
6. **Foundation for Cloud Computing** — Without virtualization, there is no IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Virtualization ≠ Emulation. Emulation translates instructions from one architecture to another (slow). Virtualization directly executes guest instructions on the host CPU (near-native speed) using hardware-assisted features like **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of Virtualization

The virtualization stack is composed of the following logical layers (from bottom to top):

1. **Physical Hardware (Bare Metal)** — The actual CPU, RAM, disk, NIC.
2. **Host Operating System (optional, in Type-2 only)** — A standard OS like Windows/Linux.
3. **Hypervisor (VMM)** — The core software that creates and runs VMs. Allocates physical resources to virtual ones.
4. **Virtual Machines (Guests)** — Each contains:
   - Virtual CPU (vCPU)
   - Virtual Memory
   - Virtual Disk
   - Virtual NIC
   - Guest Operating System (can be different from host)
5. **Applications** — End-user workloads running inside the Guest OS.

### 2.2 The Two Types of Hypervisors (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a compulsory KTU 2-mark or 5-mark question in Module 2.**

| Feature | **Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted)** |
|---------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Position** | Runs directly on hardware | Runs on a host OS |
| **Performance** | Near-native, high performance | Slower (double scheduling) |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| **Used By** | Enterprise data centers, AWS EC2 (historically) | Developers, students, testing |
| **Stability** | High (no host OS failure) | Dependent on host OS |
| **Drivers** | Has its own hardware drivers | Uses host OS drivers |

### 2.3 Virtualization of Each Physical Resource

#### A. CPU Virtualization
- The physical CPU's time is divided into slices using a **scheduler** (Credit Scheduler in Xen, CFS in KVM).
- **vCPU-to-pCore mapping:** One physical core (pCore) may be time-shared by multiple vCPUs.
- **Hardware-assisted virtualization** uses Intel VT-x / AMD-V to allow guest instructions to execute directly on the CPU.
- **Full Virtualization vs. Para-Virtualization:**
  - *Full:* Guest OS unmodified; uses binary translation (older) or VT-x.
  - *Para:* Guest OS is "aware" it is virtualized and makes hypercalls (e.g., Xen early versions).

#### B. Memory Virtualization
- The Hypervisor maintains a **shadow page table** mapping Guest Virtual → Guest Physical → Host Physical addresses.
- This adds a level of indirection known as the **GVA → GPA → HPA** translation:
  - **GVA** = Guest Virtual Address
  - **GPA** = Guest Physical Address (from the VM's perspective)
  - **HPA** = Host Physical Address (real RAM chip)
- Memory can be **over-committed** — i.e., total allocated virtual memory can exceed physical RAM, using swap space on disk.

#### C. Storage Virtualization
- A physical disk is abstracted into a **Virtual Disk File** (e.g., `.vmdk`, `.vhd`, `.qcow2`).
- The guest sees a logical block device, while the hypervisor handles actual I/O to the underlying LUN, RAID array, or SAN.
- Thin Provisioning: Allocate 100 GB virtual, but use only 20 GB physical until needed.

#### D. Network Virtualization
- A physical NIC is virtualized into multiple **vNICs**.
- A **Virtual Switch (vSwitch)** inside the hypervisor connects VMs to each other and to the outside network.
- VLAN tagging, traffic shaping, and firewalls are enforced virtually.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems, especially consolidation and overcommit ratio calculations.

| # | Metric | Formula | Typical Value | Description |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Consolidation Ratio (CR)** | $CR = N_{VM} \,/\, N_{Host}$ | **8:1 to 15:1** | VMs per physical host |
| 2 | **Server Reduction %** | $SR = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{Host,new}}{N_{Host,old}}\right) \times 100$ | **85%–95%** | % reduction in physical servers |
| 3 | **CPU Utilization Gain** | $U_{gain} = U_{after} - U_{before}$ | **+50%** | Old 10% → New 65% |
| 4 | **Memory Overcommit Ratio (MOR)** | $MOR = \dfrac{M_{vAlloc}}{M_{pRAM}}$ | **1.5:1 to 2:1** | Virtual vs. physical RAM |
| 5 | **CPU Overcommit Ratio (COR)** | $COR = \dfrac{vCPU_{total}}{pCore_{total}}$ | **4:1 to 8:1** | Virtual vs. physical cores |
| 6 | **Storage Overcommit (Thin)** | $SOR = \dfrac{V_{provisioned}}{V_{physical}}$ | **2:1 to 5:1** | Provisioned vs. used |
| 7 | **Total vCPUs in Cluster** | $vCPU_{total} = pCore \times COR$ | — | Cluster capacity |
| 8 | **Power Saving %** | $P_{save} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{old}}{P_{new}} \times (1-CR)$ | **70%–80%** | Energy efficiency gain |
| 9 | **VM Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{VM}}{N_{Host} \times pCore}$ | **1 to 4** | VMs per physical core |
| 10 | **Hardware-Assisted Speedup** | $S = \dfrac{T_{emulated}}{T_{native-vt}}$ | **1.5× to 3×** | VT-x vs. emulation gain |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **AWS EC2** uses Xen (and now Nitro System) as its Type-1 hypervisor.
- **OpenStack Nova** uses KVM (Linux Kernel-based Virtual Machine).
- **VMware vSphere** dominates enterprise data centers (~75% market share).
- **Containers (Docker)** are *not* full virtualization — they share the host kernel, which is why KTU explicitly distinguishes "Virtualization" from "Containerization."

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1: Consolidation Ratio & Server Reduction

> **Problem Statement (Sample KTU Numerical):**
> A company currently operates **120 physical servers**, each running at an average **12% CPU utilization**. After virtualization, each new physical server runs **16 VMs** at an average **75% utilization**.
> Calculate: (a) Total physical servers required after virtualization. (b) Server Reduction percentage. (c) Total CPU cycles utilized before vs. after.

**Given Data:**
- $N_{Host,old} = 120$
- $U_{old} = 12\% = 0.12$
- $N_{VM/new\_host} = 16$ (consolidation ratio = 16)
- $U_{new} = 75\% = 0.75$

#### Step 1: Compute Total Useful Compute Capacity Before Virtualization

We assume each server has the same hardware capacity. The *useful* work (in server-utilization units) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{useful,old} &= N_{Host,old} \times U_{old} \\
&= 120 \times 0.12 \\
&= 14.4 \text{ "server-equivalents" of work}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute Total Workload Demand

The actual workload (business demand) is **fixed**. Total demand must be preserved:

$$
W_{demand} = 14.4 \text{ units}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute New Physical Servers Required

Each new server can now deliver 0.75 units of useful work:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{Host,new} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{W_{demand}}{U_{new}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{14.4}{0.75} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 19.2 \rceil \\
&= 20 \text{ servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute Server Reduction Percentage

$$
\begin{aligned}
SR &= \left( 1 - \dfrac{N_{Host,new}}{N_{Host,old}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( 1 - \dfrac{20}{120} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( 1 - 0.1667 \right) \times 100 \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Server reduction is **83.33%** (i.e., **100 physical servers are eliminated**).

#### Step 5: Verification Using Consolidation Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{Host,new} &= \dfrac{N_{VM,total}}{CR} = \dfrac{120}{16} = 7.5 \rightarrow \text{⚠ Mismatch!}
\end{aligned}
$$

The consolidation ratio method only works when VMs map 1:1 to old servers. Since we have demand-based sizing, the **utilization method is more accurate**.

---

### 3.2 Problem 2: Memory Overcommit & Effective Memory

> **Problem Statement:** A hypervisor host has **128 GB physical RAM**. The administrator provisions 3 VMs with **64 GB, 48 GB, and 32 GB** of virtual RAM respectively. Calculate the Memory Overcommit Ratio (MOR). If one VM is idle, how much physical RAM is free for the hypervisor?

#### Step 1: Sum of Allocated Virtual Memory

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{vAlloc} &= M_{VM1} + M_{VM2} + M_{VM3} \\
&= 64 + 48 + 32 \\
&= 144 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute MOR

$$
\begin{aligned}
MOR &= \dfrac{M_{vAlloc}}{M_{pRAM}} \\
&= \dfrac{144}{128} \\
&= 1.125 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Used Physical RAM (Active VM Scenario)

If only VM2 and VM3 are actively using memory (say at 50% each, while VM1 is idle at 0%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{used} &= (0.0 \times 64) + (0.5 \times 48) + (0.5 \times 32) \\
&= 0 + 24 + 16 \\
&= 40 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Free Physical RAM

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{free} &= M_{pRAM} - M_{used} \\
&= 128 - 40 \\
&= 88 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** The hypervisor uses techniques like **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)** and **Memory Ballooning** to reclaim idle pages, achieving effective overcommit ratios of **1.5:1 to 2:1** safely.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Virtualization Techniques Comparison

| Property | Full Virtualization | Para-Virtualization | Hardware-Assisted (VT-x/AMD-V) | OS-Level (Containers) |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| Guest OS Modification | **Not Required** | **Required** | Not Required | Shares host kernel |
| Performance | Moderate | High | **Highest** | Highest |
| Hypervisor Examples | IBM CP/CMS, early VMware | Xen (classic) | KVM, modern Xen, ESXi | Docker, LXC |
| Isolation | Strong | Strong | **Strong** | Weak (shared kernel) |
| Overhead | High (binary translation) | Low (hypercalls) | **Near-zero** | Negligible |
| Use Case | Legacy OS support | Performance-critical VMs | Modern cloud | Microservices |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: CPU Scheduling in Virtualization

In a **Type-1 hypervisor with credit-based scheduling** (e.g., Xen):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Credit}_i &= \text{Weight}_i \times \Delta t \\
\text{If Credit}_i &\geq 0 : \text{VM}_i \text{ runs} \\
\text{If Credit}_i &< 0 : \text{VM}_i \text{ is preempted and decayed}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Weight}_i$ is the priority weight assigned to VM $i$.
- $\Delta t$ is the time slice (typically **30 ms** in Xen).
- VMs with higher weights consume credits faster, ensuring fair-share scheduling.

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Resource Virtualization Pipeline

> This block diagram shows the **lifecycle of a CPU instruction** as it travels from a Guest VM down to the physical hardware, traversing every virtualization layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Guest Application User Process"] --> B["Guest OS Kernel Mode"]

    C --> D["Hypervisor Trap or VT-x Transition"]

    E --> F["Hardware CPU Executes Instruction Native"]
    F --> G["Result Returns via EPT Shadow Page Table"]
    G --> A

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The hypervisor is the *single point of mediation* between every virtual resource request and the physical hardware. This is why it is the **trusted computing base (TCB)** of the entire cloud infrastructure.
