## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define virtualization. List any four advantages of virtualizing physical computing resources in a cloud data center.*

**Model Answer:**

Virtualization is the abstraction of physical computing resources (CPU, memory, storage, network) into logical, isolated virtual instances, managed by a software layer called the hypervisor, enabling multiple operating systems and applications to share a single physical machine.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Server Consolidation** — Increases server utilization from ~12% to ~75%, reducing hardware count by 80–90%.
2. **Cost Reduction** — Lower CapEx (fewer servers) and OpEx (less power, cooling, and rack space).
3. **Isolation & Security** — Each VM runs in its own sandbox; a crash in one VM does not affect others.
4. **Portability & Migration** — VMs are files (`.vmdk`, `.qcow2`) that can be live-migrated across hosts without downtime (e.g., VMware vMotion).

*(Any other valid points: snapshots, faster provisioning, disaster recovery, environment replication for testing.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 (Hosted)** |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------------------|
| Installation | Installed directly on hardware | Installed inside a host OS |
| Performance | High, near-native | Lower, due to double scheduling |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| Use case | Enterprise data centers, production cloud | Development, learning, testing |
| Reliability | Independent of host OS | Depends on host OS stability |

Type-1 hypervisors are preferred in production cloud environments (e.g., AWS, Azure) because they eliminate the host OS layer and provide direct hardware access.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **architecture of a Type-1 hypervisor**. With a neat block diagram, describe how a physical server's CPU, memory, storage, and network resources are virtualized and allocated to multiple guest VMs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A university data center has **80 physical servers** each running at **10% average CPU utilization**. The IT team plans to virtualize using a Type-1 hypervisor with a target average utilization of **70%** and a consolidation ratio of **12 VMs per host**. Calculate:
- (i) Total workload in server-equivalents
- (ii) New number of physical hosts required
- (iii) Percentage server reduction
- (iv) Total vCPUs required if each VM needs 2 vCPUs.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Type-1 Hypervisor Architecture — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

A Type-1 hypervisor (also called a **bare-metal hypervisor**) is installed directly onto the physical server hardware without any intervening host operating system. It acts as a thin, specialized OS whose sole purpose is to manage VMs and arbitrate access to physical resources.

**Key Components:**

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Provides device drivers for CPU, RAM, disk controllers, and NICs. It abstracts physical hardware into a unified resource pool.
2. **CPU Scheduler:** Allocates physical CPU cores/time-slices to vCPUs of each VM. Common algorithms include:
   - **Credit Scheduler (Xen)**
   - **Completely Fair Scheduler (KVM)**
   - **Proportional Share (ESXi)**
3. **Memory Manager:** Implements **GVA → GPA → HPA** translation using either shadow page tables or Extended Page Tables (EPT). Supports **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)**, **Memory Ballooning**, and **Memory Compression**.
4. **Storage Virtualization Module:** Creates virtual disks (`.vmdk`, `.vhd`, `.qcow2`) backed by physical storage (DAS, NAS, SAN). Supports thin provisioning, snapshots, and cloning.
5. **Network Virtualization Module:** Implements a **virtual switch (vSwitch)** that connects vNICs of VMs to physical NICs. Supports VLAN tagging, traffic shaping, and port mirroring.
6. **VM Control Plane:** Handles VM lifecycle — create, start, pause, resume, snapshot, migrate, destroy.

**Architectural Block Diagram:**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  VM1  |  VM2  |  VM3  |  VM4  |  VM5  |  VM6   |
| (App) | (App) | (App) | (App) | (App) | (App)  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|    Guest OS    |    Guest OS    |    Guest OS    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
| vCPU | vRAM | vDisk | vNIC (per VM)              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|       HYPERVISOR (VMM) - Type 1                  |
|  [Scheduler | Memory Mgr | Storage Mgr | vSwitch]|
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Hardware Abstraction Layer (Device Drivers)     |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  PHYSICAL HARDWARE                               |
|  CPU | RAM | DISK | NIC | USB | GPU              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Working Principle:**
- The hypervisor boots directly from the server's firmware (BIOS/UEFI).
- It loads its own microkernel and device drivers.
- Each VM's vCPU is mapped onto a physical pCore through the scheduler.
- Memory translation happens through nested page tables (EPT) using hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V).
- I/O requests from the guest's virtual devices are intercepted by the hypervisor and translated into physical device operations.

**Examples:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core mode), Citrix XenServer, Red Hat KVM (acts as Type-1 on Linux kernel).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $N_{Host,old} = 80$
- $U_{old} = 10\% = 0.10$
- $U_{new} = 70\% = 0.70$
- $CR = 12$ VMs per host
- vCPUs per VM = 2

**(i) Total workload in server-equivalents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= N_{Host,old} \times U_{old} \\
&= 80 \times 0.10 \\
&= 8.0 \text{ server-equivalents}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) New number of physical hosts (utilization method):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{Host,new} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{W}{U_{new}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{8.0}{0.70} \right\rceil = \lceil 11.43 \rceil = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii-alt) Verification using Consolidation Ratio (if all 80 servers map to VMs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{VM,total} &= 80 \text{ (assuming 1 VM per old server)} \\
N_{Host,new} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{12} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.67 \rceil = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Both methods are accepted, but students must clearly state assumptions.

**(iii) Server Reduction Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SR &= \left(1 - \dfrac{12}{80}\right) \times 100 = 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Total vCPUs Required:**

If we assume 80 VMs (one per old server), each needing 2 vCPUs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
vCPU_{total} &= 80 \times 2 = 160 \text{ vCPUs}
\end{aligned}
$$

If we use 7 new hosts with, say, 16 cores each (typical), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
pCore_{total} &= 7 \times 16 = 112 \\
COR &= \dfrac{160}{112} = 1.43 : 1 \quad (\text{healthy, well below 4:1 safety limit})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **memory virtualization** mechanism in detail. Explain **GVA → GPA → HPA** address translation with the help of a diagram. What are **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS)**, **Memory Ballooning**, and **memory overcommit**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A virtualized host has **256 GB physical RAM** and runs **10 VMs**, each allocated **32 GB** virtual RAM. The hypervisor's memory overcommit ratio is **1.5:1**.
- (i) Calculate the maximum total virtual memory that can be allocated safely.
- (ii) Determine the total RAM required if every VM fully uses its allocated memory.
- (iii) If the workload uses only **40% of allocated RAM on average**, what is the actual physical RAM consumed?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Memory Virtualization Mechanism (7 marks)**

Memory virtualization introduces an additional level of indirection in address translation. Each VM believes it owns a contiguous physical memory range, but in reality, this is a virtual view managed by the hypervisor.

**The Three Address Spaces:**

1. **GVA (Guest Virtual Address):** Generated by the application inside the guest. Translated by the Guest OS using its own page tables.
2. **GPA (Guest Physical Address):** The address the guest OS *thinks* is physical. In reality, it is a virtual address in the hypervisor's view.
3. **HPA (Host Physical Address):** The actual address of a real RAM chip on the motherboard.

**Translation Process:**

```
Guest Application
        |
        v
  [GVA: 0x0040 1234]  -- Guest Page Table -->
  [GPA: 0x0000 8000]  -- EPT / Shadow Page Table -->
  [HPA: 0x1A2B 4F00]  -- Real RAM chip
```

**Implementation Techniques:**

- **Shadow Page Tables:** The hypervisor maintains a hidden copy of each guest's page table, directly mapping GVA → HPA. The hardware TLB caches this mapping. Disadvantage: complex to maintain for every VM.
- **Extended Page Tables (EPT) / Nested Page Tables (NPT):** A hardware-assisted feature (Intel EPT, AMD NPT) where the CPU walks two-level page tables automatically. Modern, fast, and widely used (VT-x + EPT).

**Key Optimization Techniques:**

- **Transparent Page Sharing (TPS):** The hypervisor scans for identical memory pages across VMs (e.g., identical OS kernels) and stores only one copy, sharing it across VMs. Typical savings: 20–40%.
- **Memory Ballooning:** A driver inside the guest (balloon driver) inflates under hypervisor pressure, forcing the guest OS to swap out its own pages to its own virtual disk, freeing host RAM.
- **Memory Overcommit:** Allocating more virtual memory than physical RAM, relying on TPS, ballooning, and swapping to disk. Typical ratios: 1.5:1 to 2:1.
- **Memory Compression:** The hypervisor compresses idle pages in RAM (instead of swapping to disk) for faster access.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $M_{pRAM} = 256$ GB
- $N_{VM} = 10$
- $M_{vAlloc/VM} = 32$ GB
- $MOR_{allowed} = 1.5:1$

**(i) Maximum total virtual memory that can be safely allocated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{vAlloc,max} &= M_{pRAM} \times MOR \\
&= 256 \times 1.5 \\
&= 384 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total RAM required if every VM fully uses its allocated memory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{vAlloc,requested} &= 10 \times 32 = 320 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 320 GB < 384 GB (the safe overcommit limit), the allocation is **valid**.

**(iii) Actual physical RAM consumed (with 40% usage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{used,actual} &= N_{VM} \times M_{vAlloc/VM} \times U_{RAM} \\
&= 10 \times 32 \times 0.40 \\
&= 128 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Free physical RAM for hypervisor/buffer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{free} &= 256 - 128 = 128 \text{ GB (50% free)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The configuration is healthy, with 50% physical RAM headroom, allowing for burst workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Virtualization" with "Cloud Computing"** — Virtualization is a *technology*; Cloud is a *service model* that *uses* virtualization. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Writing "Hypervisor" without explaining Type-1 vs Type-2** — KTU specifically expects a comparison table for full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the GVA → GPA → HPA distinction in memory questions** — This is the most tested concept in 7-mark questions.
> 4. **Numerical mistakes in MOR and Consolidation Ratio** — Always include the formula *and* the units. Marks are split: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for final answer, 1 for unit/correctness.
> 5. **Drawing full virtualization ≠ containers** — Docker is OS-level virtualization, not hardware virtualization. KTU explicitly tests this distinction.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- [x] **Virtualization** = abstraction of physical resources into multiple isolated virtual instances.
- [x] **Hypervisor (VMM)** = the software layer that creates and manages VMs.
- [x] **Type-1** = Bare-metal (ESXi, Xen, KVM); **Type-2** = Hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- [x] **Four virtualized resources:** CPU → vCPU, RAM → Virtual RAM, Disk → Virtual Disk File, NIC → vNIC.
- [x] **Memory Translation:** GVA → GPA → HPA (via EPT / Shadow Page Tables).
- [x] **CPU Translation:** Guest instructions executed via VT-x / AMD-V (hardware-assisted).
- [x] **Consolidation Ratio (CR)** = VMs per physical host (typical: 8:1 to 15:1).
- [x] **Server Reduction %** = $\left(1 - \dfrac{N_{Host,new}}{N_{Host,old}}\right) \times 100$
- [x] **Memory Overcommit (MOR)** = $\dfrac{M_{vAlloc}}{M_{pRAM}}$ (safe: 1.5:1 to 2:1).
- [x] **CPU Overcommit (COR)** = $\dfrac{vCPU_{total}}{pCore_{total}}$ (safe: 4:1 to 8:1).
- [x] **Benefits:** Consolidation, Utilization (5%→75%), Isolation, Portability, Cost ↓, DR, Foundation for IaaS.
- [x] **Virtualization ≠ Containerization** — VMs share hardware, Containers share kernel.
- [x] **Key vendors:** VMware (ESXi), Microsoft (Hyper-V), Red Hat (KVM), Citrix (Xen), AWS (Nitro).
- [x] **Optimization techniques:** TPS, Ballooning, Memory Compression, Thin Provisioning, Live Migration.
- [x] **Hardware requirements:** Intel VT-x, AMD-V, EPT/NPT, IOMMU (Intel VT-d) for I/O virtualization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-emulated, logically isolated abstraction of a physical computing system that behaves exactly like a real hardware platform, possessing its own virtual CPU, memory, storage, and network interfaces, executing its own operating system and applications as though it were an independent physical machine.

In the context of **Machine Virtualization**, a **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** — commonly known as a **Hypervisor** — is the software layer that creates, manages, and arbitrates the execution of multiple VMs on a single physical host by multiplexing the underlying physical hardware resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Machine Virtualization forms the foundational pillar of Cloud Computing (IaaS delivery model). Mastery of VM types, hypervisor architectures, and virtualization techniques is mandatory for Module 2.

### The Three Canonical Properties of a Virtual Machine (Popek & Goldberg Criteria, 1974)

| Property | Description | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Fidelity** | The VM must behave identically to the underlying physical machine | Software running inside a VM is unaware of virtualization |
| **Safety / Isolation** | The VMM must retain complete control over physical resources | One VM cannot interfere with another or the host |
| **Efficiency** | Most VM instructions must be executed directly by the hardware without VMM intervention | Near-native performance, minimal overhead |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Physical Server as a Piece of Land and a Virtual Machine as an Apartment in a High-Rise Building built on that land.**

Imagine you own a large plot of land (the **physical server**). Instead of letting a single family use the entire land inefficiently, you build a high-rise apartment complex (**hypervisor + VMs**) on it. Each apartment (**Virtual Machine**) has its own:
- Walls, doors, and rooms (isolated **virtual CPU, RAM, Disk**)
- Independent electric and water meters (dedicated **virtual NIC and storage I/O**)
- Independent occupants running their own lifestyle (a separate **Guest OS + Applications**)

The building's architect and security (**the Hypervisor**) ensures that:
- No tenant can break into another apartment (**isolation**)
- Each apartment feels exactly like an independent house (**fidelity**)
- The land is utilized to its maximum capacity (**efficiency and consolidation**)

The land beneath is still one, but multiple families happily coexist, each believing they have a house of their own. **That is Machine Virtualization.**

## 1.3 Classification of Virtual Machines

There are two broad classes of VMs in the KTU framework:

### A. Process Virtual Machine (System-Level Process VM)
- Designed to run a **single process / application**
- Provides an abstract, platform-independent programming environment
- Examples: **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, Microsoft .NET CLR, Parrot VM
- Used for language portability and sandboxed execution

### B. System Virtual Machine (Hardware-Level VM)
- Provides a **complete system environment** capable of running a full OS
- Multiplexes a single physical machine among many guest OS instances
- Examples: **VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen, VirtualBox**
- This is the dominant form used in **Cloud Data Centers (IaaS)**

## 1.4 Why Machine Virtualization? (Motivations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is virtualization the backbone of cloud computing?"* The answer revolves around the following four drivers.

1. **Server Consolidation** — Multiple underutilized servers are merged onto one physical host, raising utilization from typical **5–15%** to **60–80%**.
2. **Hardware Independence** — VMs are encapsulated into files that can be migrated across heterogeneous hardware without modification.
3. **Rapid Provisioning** — A new VM can be instantiated in **seconds** versus hours/days for procuring and racking a physical server.
4. **Fault Isolation & Security** — A crash or compromise inside one VM does not propagate to peers running on the same host.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture Stack of Machine Virtualization

The virtualization stack consists of four logical layers. We examine them from the **bottom (hardware)** upward.

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function | Real-World Examples |
|:-------:|------------|----------|---------------------|
| L4 | **Guest Application Layer** | End-user software running inside the VM | MS Word, Apache HTTP, Oracle DB |
| L3 | **Guest Operating System** | The OS installed *inside* the VM | Windows Server 2022, Ubuntu 22.04 |
| L2 | **Virtual Machine (Guest)** | The encapsulated emulated hardware (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC) | VM1.vmdk, VM2.vdi |
| L1 | **Hypervisor (VMM)** | Creates, schedules, and isolates VMs; controls physical resources | VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM, Hyper-V |
| L0 | **Host Physical Hardware** | Real CPU (with VT-x/AMD-V), RAM, Storage, NIC | Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC, DDR5 |

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Hypervisors

### Type 1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal / Native)
- Installed **directly on the host's hardware**
- Acts as a thin, purpose-built OS
- Used in **enterprise data centers and public clouds**
- Examples: **VMware ESXi**, Microsoft **Hyper-V (Server Core mode)**, Citrix **XenServer**, Linux **KVM**

### Type 2 Hypervisor (Hosted)
- Runs as an application **on top of a conventional host OS**
- Used in **desktops, labs, development environments**
- Examples: **Oracle VirtualBox**, VMware **Workstation/Player**, Parallels Desktop

### The Hybrid / Microkernel Hypervisor
- A modern evolution: a small microkernel (hypervisor core) handles only CPU/memory, while a privileged "driver domain" handles I/O
- Example: **Xen** with its Dom0 (control domain)

## 2.3 The Three Techniques for Achieving Virtualization

### (i) Full Virtualization
The guest OS is **completely unmodified**. The VMM translates sensitive (privileged) instructions at runtime using either:
- **Binary Translation (BT)** — pre-Hardware-Assisted era; VMware used this
- **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** — modern Intel VT-x / AMD-V traps privileged instructions to the VMM

### (ii) Para-Virtualization
The guest OS is **explicitly modified** to make hypercalls to the VMM instead of issuing privileged instructions directly.
- **Faster** than full virtualization but requires OS source-code access
- Example: **Xen** running a para-virtualized Linux

### (iii) Hardware-Assisted Virtualization
Modern CPUs provide **new privilege levels** and **shadow page tables / Extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT)** that allow the VMM to trap and emulate sensitive instructions in silicon.
- Industry standard today
- Combined with **SR-IOV** for direct NIC virtualization and **VT-d / AMD-Vi** for IOMMU

## 2.4 Resource Virtualization (Sub-Domains)

| Resource | Virtualization Mechanism | Purpose |
|----------|-------------------------|---------|
| **CPU** | Time-sharing via scheduler; VT-x ring de-privileging | Multiplex vCPUs onto pCPUs |
| **Memory** | Shadow Page Tables, EPT/NPT, Ballooning | Share physical RAM among VMs |
| **Storage** | Virtual disk files (.vmdk, .vhd, .qcow2); thin/thick provisioning | Abstract physical disks |
| **I/O** | Emulated devices, para-virtualized drivers (virtio), SR-IOV pass-through | Virtualize NICs and HBAs |
| **Network** | Virtual switches (vSwitch), VLAN tagging, overlay networks (VXLAN, GENEVE) | Create virtual topologies |

## 2.5 Live Migration

**Live Migration** is the process of moving a running VM from one physical host (source) to another (destination) **without downtime** and with **no perceived service disruption** to the end user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Live migration is what enables elastic load balancing in clouds like AWS EC2, Azure, and OpenStack. It uses the **pre-copy** technique: memory pages are iteratively copied while the VM runs, and a final stop-and-copy phase transfers the residual dirty pages.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **mathematically verifiable** formulas a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 2 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|:-:|---------|---------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)** | $SCR = N_{VM} / N_{Host}$ | $N_{VM}$ = number of VMs, $N_{Host}$ = number of physical hosts | $SCR \geq 1$ |
| 2 | **VM Density** | $\rho_{VM} = N_{VM} / R_{Host}$ | $R_{Host}$ = total physical RAM (GB) | $\rho_{VM} \leq R_{Host} / R_{min}$ |
| 3 | **Memory Overhead per VM** | $M_{overhead} = M_{VMM} + (N_{VM} \times M_{shadow})$ | All in MB | Depends on shadow page table size |
| 4 | **CPU Utilization Gain** | $U_{gain} = (U_{after} - U_{before}) / U_{before} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in % | $0\% \leq U_{gain} \leq 100\%$ |
| 5 | **CPU Scheduling Time Slice** | $T_{slice} = T_{quantum} / N_{vCPU}$ | $T_{quantum}$ in ms | $T_{slice} > 0$ |
| 6 | **Effective VM Throughput** | $Th_{eff} = Th_{native} \times (1 - O_{VM})$ | $O_{VM}$ = virtualization overhead fraction | $0 \leq O_{VM} < 1$ |
| 7 | **Live Migration Downtime** | $D_{down} = T_{stop-copy} + T_{restart}$ | In seconds | $D_{down} < 1$ s typical |
| 8 | **Total Pre-Copy Iterations** | $N_{iter} = \lceil \log_{R} (D_{thresh} / D_{0}) \rceil$ | $R$ = convergence rate, $D$ = dirty page rate | Stops when page set fits in $D_{thresh}$ |
| 9 | **Total Data Transferred** | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{N_{iter}} D_{i}$ | In MB | Sum of all pre-copy rounds |
| 10 | **Hypervisor Trap Cost** | $C_{trap} = C_{exit} + C_{service} + C_{entry}$ | All in CPU cycles | $C_{trap} \gg C_{native}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Restriction Reminder:** In your written exam answer books, never use the `|` character inside table cells for absolute value — always write $\vert x \vert$ explicitly. KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Cloud Platform | Hypervisor Technology | VM Brand |
|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| Amazon AWS EC2 | Xen (legacy), Nitro KVM (current) | t3, m5, c5 instance families |
| Microsoft Azure | Hyper-V (custom Windows hypervisor) | Dv5, Ev5, M-series |
| Google Cloud Platform (GCP) | KVM (Linux kernel-based) | N2, C2, M2 instance families |
| OpenStack (private cloud) | KVM / QEMU | Nova-managed instances |

Virtualization is the **substrate** of public IaaS clouds. Without efficient machine virtualization, concepts like *elasticity, pay-per-use, multi-tenancy* would be commercially infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Popek and Goldberg Virtualization Criteria — Formal Derivation

The Popek–Goldberg theorem (1974) is the **mathematical foundation** of machine virtualization. A given ISA (Instruction Set Architecture) is **virtualizable** if and only if its set of **sensitive instructions** is a **subset** of its set of **privileged instructions**.

### Definitions

- **Privileged Instructions (P):** Instructions that trap to the VMM if executed in user mode; they execute successfully in kernel/ring 0.
- **Sensitive Instructions (S):** Instructions whose behavior or side-effects depend on the current privilege level (i.e., they either change hardware configuration or expose privileged state).
- **Innocuous Instructions (I):** Instructions that are neither sensitive nor control-sensitive; they always behave identically regardless of privilege.

### Theorem Statement (Formal)

A machine $M$ with instruction set $I_{set}$ is **virtualizable** under a VMM if and only if:

$$S \subseteq P$$

Equivalently, every sensitive instruction must be privileged so that any attempt by the guest OS to execute it forces a trap to the VMM, allowing correct emulation.

### Logical Walk-Through of the Derivation

> **Step 1 — Necessity ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $M$ is virtualizable. Assume for contradiction that $\exists$ an instruction $x \in S$ such that $x \notin P$. Then the guest could execute $x$ in user mode without trapping to the VMM. Because $x$ is sensitive, its behavior in user mode differs from supervisor mode, breaking **fidelity**. Contradiction. Hence $S \subseteq P$.

> **Step 2 — Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$):** If $S \subseteq P$, every sensitive instruction will trap to the VMM when the guest attempts to run it. The VMM can then emulate the privileged behavior, **preserving fidelity** while maintaining control. The VMM dispatches innocuous instructions directly, **preserving efficiency**.

$$
\therefore \quad \text{M is virtualizable} \iff S \subseteq P
$$

### Counter-Example: The x86 Problem

The original Intel x86 (pre-VT-x, pre-AMD-V) contained **19 sensitive but non-privileged instructions** (e.g., `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `SMSW`). Thus:

$$S \not\subseteq P \quad \text{(cardinality of } S \setminus P = 19\text{)}$$

Hence classical x86 was **not virtuallyizable** in the Popek–Goldberg sense. Solutions:
1. **Binary Translation** (VMware) — rewrite non-trapping instructions at runtime
2. **Para-Virtualization** (Xen) — patch the guest OS to avoid those instructions
3. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) — extend ISA to make sensitive instructions privileged in a new execution mode (VMX root vs. VMX non-root)

## 3.2 Memory Overhead Calculation — Full Worked Numerical

### Problem Statement
A physical host has **32 GB RAM**. The VMM itself consumes **512 MB** of RAM for its own kernel and metadata. Each VM is allocated **4 GB** of guest virtual memory. The shadow page table mechanism adds an **8% memory overhead** per VM. Compute:
1. Maximum number of VMs that can be launched
2. Total memory overhead of the VMM + shadow tables
3. Utilization of the physical RAM

### Given Data
- $R_{Host} = 32 \text{ GB} = 32768 \text{ MB}$
- $M_{VMM} = 512 \text{ MB}$
- $M_{VM} = 4 \text{ GB} = 4096 \text{ MB}$
- $O_{shadow} = 8\% = 0.08$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Compute effective per-VM memory footprint including shadow overhead.**

$$
M_{VM,eff} = M_{VM} \times (1 + O_{shadow})
$$

$$
M_{VM,eff} = 4096 \times (1 + 0.08)
$$

$$
M_{VM,eff} = 4096 \times 1.08
$$

$$
M_{VM,eff} = 4423.68 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step (b) — Compute memory available for VMs after deducting the VMM reservation.**

$$
R_{avail} = R_{Host} - M_{VMM}
$$

$$
R_{avail} = 32768 - 512 = 32256 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step (c) — Compute maximum number of VMs that fit in $R_{avail}$.**

$$
N_{VM,max} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{avail}}{M_{VM,eff}} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{VM,max} = \left\lfloor \frac{32256}{4423.68} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{VM,max} = \lfloor 7.293 \rfloor = 7
$$

**Step (d) — Compute total shadow-page-table overhead across all 7 VMs.**

$$
M_{shadow,total} = N_{VM,max} \times M_{VM} \times O_{shadow}
$$

$$
M_{shadow,total} = 7 \times 4096 \times 0.08
$$

$$
M_{shadow,total} = 2293.76 \text{ MB} \approx 2.24 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step (e) — Compute physical RAM utilization.**

$$
R_{used} = M_{VMM} + (N_{VM,max} \times M_{VM,eff})
$$

$$
R_{used} = 512 + (7 \times 4423.68) = 512 + 30965.76 = 31477.76 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
U_{RAM} = \frac{R_{used}}{R_{Host}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
U_{RAM} = \frac{31477.76}{32768} \times 100\% = 96.06\%
$$

### Final Answer
- **Maximum number of VMs = 7**
- **Total shadow page table overhead ≈ 2293.76 MB (≈ 2.24 GB)**
- **Physical RAM utilization ≈ 96.06%**

## 3.3 Live Migration — Convergence of Pre-Copy Iterations

### Problem Statement
A VM has **4 GB RAM**. During pre-copy live migration, the dirty page generation rate is $D_0 = 100 \text{ MB/s}$ initially, decreasing by a convergence factor of $R = 0.5$ each iteration. The network bandwidth is $B = 200 \text{ MB/s}$. The migration must converge (i.e., residual dirty pages $\leq 50 \text{ MB}$) before stop-and-copy.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Write the dirty-rate recurrence.**

$$
D_{i+1} = R \times D_{i} = R^{i+1} \times D_{0}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute iterations until $D_{i} \leq 50 \text{ MB}$.**

$$
D_{i} \leq 50
$$

$$
R^{i} \times D_{0} \leq 50
$$

$$
0.5^{i} \times 100 \leq 50
$$

$$
0.5^{i} \leq 0.5
$$

$$
i \geq 1
$$

So theoretically **1 iteration** of pre-copy suffices under ideal convergence.

**Step 3 — Compute total data transferred across 4 iterations (for richer analysis).**

$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} R^{i} \times D_{0}
$$

$$
D_{total} = D_{0} \times \frac{1 - R^{4}}{1 - R}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 100 \times \frac{1 - 0.5^{4}}{1 - 0.5}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 100 \times \frac{1 - 0.0625}{0.5}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 100 \times \frac{0.9375}{0.5}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 100 \times 1.875
$$

$$
D_{total} = 187.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total migration time including stop-and-copy.**

$$
T_{mig} = T_{pre-copy} + T_{stop-copy}
$$

$$
T_{mig} = \frac{D_{total}}{B} + T_{stop-copy}
$$

$$
T_{mig} = \frac{187.5}{200} + 0.5 = 0.9375 + 0.5 = 1.4375 \text{ s}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Iterations to converge = 1**
- **Total data transferred (over 4 iters) = 187.5 MB**
- **Total migration time ≈ 1.44 seconds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When asked to "design" or "analyze" a live-migration scenario, you **must** mention the **three phases** explicitly: *Iterative Pre-Copy → Stop-and-Copy → VM Resume on Destination*. Examiners award 2 marks for phase identification alone.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Full vs. Para vs. Hardware-Assisted

| Attribute | Full Virtualization (BT) | Para-Virtualization | Hardware-Assisted (VT-x/AMD-V) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Guest OS modification** | None | Required (kernel patched) | None |
| **Performance** | Medium (translation overhead) | High (direct hypercalls) | Near-Native |
| **Compatibility** | Any OS | Only open-source / cooperative OS | Any OS |
| **Security boundary** | Strong | Moderate (modified kernel exposes API) | Strong (enforced in silicon) |
| **Trap cost** | High (BT loop) | Low (hypercalls) | Low (hardware intercept) |
| **Example** | VMware Workstation (legacy) | Xen (early versions) | KVM, Hyper-V, modern ESXi |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Type 1 vs. Type 2 Hypervisor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Type 1 Bare Metal Hypervisor Architecture"]
        direction TB
        h1App1["Guest App 1"]:::app
        h1App2["Guest App 2"]:::app
        h1OS1["Guest OS Windows"]:::guest
        h1OS2["Guest OS Linux"]:::guest
        h1VM1["Virtual Machine 1"]:::vm
        h1VM2["Virtual Machine 2"]:::vm
        h1VMM["Hypervisor ESXi or Xen or KVM"]:::hyper
        h1HW["Host Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC"]:::hw

        h1App1 --> h1OS1 --> h1VM1 --> h1VMM --> h1HW
        h1App2 --> h1OS2 --> h1VM2 --> h1VMM
    end

    subgraph T2["Type 2 Hosted Hypervisor Architecture"]
        direction TB
        h2App1["Guest App 1"]:::app
        h2App2["Guest App 2"]:::app
        h2OS1["Guest OS Windows"]:::guest
        h2OS2["Guest OS Linux"]:::guest
        h2VM1["Virtual Machine 1"]:::vm
        h2VM2["Virtual Machine 2"]:::vm
        h2VMM["Hypervisor VirtualBox or VMware Workstation"]:::hyper
        h2HostOS["Host Operating System Windows or Linux"]:::hostos
        h2HW["Host Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC"]:::hw

        h2App1 --> h2OS1 --> h2VM1 --> h2VMM --> h2HostOS --> h2HW
        h2App2 --> h2OS2 --> h2VM2 --> h2VMM
    end

    classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef guest fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef vm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hyper fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hostos fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice how the Type 1 hypervisor sits **directly on the hardware**, eliminating the host-OS layer. This single fact is what gives Type 1 its performance and stability edge in production cloud environments.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Live Migration Workflow (Pre-Copy Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source Host VM Running"]:::vm --> B["Iter 1 Pre Copy Transmit Clean Pages"]:::step
    B --> C["Iter 2 Re Scan Re transmit Dirty Pages"]:::step
    C --> D{"Dirty Page Set Less Than Threshold"}:::decision
    D -- "No" --> B
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Stop and Copy Phase Suspend VM"]:::step
    E --> F["Transfer Residual State CPU Registers Device State"]:::step
    F --> G["Destination Host Resume VM"]:::step
    G --> H["VM Now Running on Destination Ack to Source"]:::vm
    H --> I["Source Releases Resources"]:::step

    classDef vm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cloud VM Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR["User Space Inside VM"]
        UA1["User Application Layer e g MS Office Apache Tomcat Oracle DB"]:::usr
    end

    subgraph KRN["Kernel Space Inside VM"]
        GU["Guest OS Kernel Scheduler File System Network Stack Drivers"]:::krn
    end

    subgraph VIRT["Virtualization Layer"]
        VCPU["Virtual CPU vCPU Emulation"]:::virt
        VMEM["Virtual Memory Shadow Page Table or EPT"]:::virt
        VIO["Virtual IO Devices Disk NIC Console virtio Backends"]:::virt
        VNET["Virtual Network vSwitch Overlay VXLAN or GENEVE"]:::virt
    end

    subgraph HYP["Hypervisor Core"]
        SCH["Scheduler Fair Share or Credit Based"]:::hyp
        ISO["Isolation Engine Ring De privilege VT x or AMD V"]:::hyp
        MIG["Live Migration Engine pre copy"]:::hyp
    end

    subgraph HW["Physical Host Hardware"]
        PCPU["Physical CPU Cores"]:::hw
        PRAM["Physical DDR Memory DIMMs"]:::hw
        PDSK["Physical Storage SSD or NVMe"]:::hw
        PNIC["Physical Network Adapter 10G or 25G"]:::hw
    end

    UA1 --> GU
    GU --> VCPU
    GU --> VMEM
    GU --> VIO
    GU --> VNET
    VCPU --> SCH
    VMEM --> ISO
    VIO --> MIG
    VNET --> SCH
    SCH --> PCPU
    ISO --> PRAM
    MIG --> PDSK
    VIO --> PNIC

    classDef usr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef krn fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef virt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hyp fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**. The four vertical bands — User, Kernel, Virtualization, Hypervisor, and Hardware — show the **direction of privilege descent**. Every privileged operation originating in the User Space must travel downward, becoming intercepted and arbitrated by the Hypervisor before reaching the Physical Hardware.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. The marks shown are the **simulated maximum marks** for each part, matching the official KTU template.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Virtual Machine. List the three Popek–Goldberg properties of an efficient VMM. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-defined emulation of a physical computing system that behaves as an independent, isolated logical machine, with its own virtual CPU, memory, storage, and I/O, and is capable of running a complete operating system and applications.

The three Popek–Goldberg properties of an efficient VMM are:

1. **Fidelity:** The behavior of a program inside a VM must be identical to its behavior on the native physical machine.
2. **Safety / Isolation:** The VMM must retain total control over all system resources; a guest VM must not be able to directly manipulate or access resources belonging to another VM or to the host.
3. **Efficiency:** A statistically large subset of instructions issued by the guest VM must be executed directly on the hardware without VMM intervention, ensuring near-native performance.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for naming the 3 properties with one-line description each)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Installation** | Installed directly on the physical hardware | Installed as an application on top of a host OS |
| **Performance** | Higher — direct hardware access | Lower — extra host-OS indirection |
| **Use case** | Enterprise data centers, public clouds | Desktops, development, labs |
| **Reliability** | Higher (no host-OS failure surface) | Depends on host OS stability |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for correct differentiation + 1 mark for valid examples + 0.5 mark for conclusion)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has an **internal choice** (Or option). We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternative selections.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain in detail the three techniques of machine virtualization: Full Virtualization, Para-Virtualization, and Hardware-Assisted Virtualization. Compare their performance and compatibility. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Full Virtualization**
In full virtualization, the guest OS is **not modified** in any way. The VMM creates a complete emulation of the underlying hardware, allowing any OS to run unaware of the virtualization layer. Sensitive (privileged) instructions issued by the guest are either:
- Caught and translated at runtime using **Binary Translation (BT)**, OR
- Trapped directly in modern CPUs via **VT-x/AMD-V** hardware extensions

The guest OS sees a perfect replica of the hardware, hence full compatibility with legacy, proprietary, and closed-source operating systems. The trade-off is the runtime translation overhead of BT, which can be mitigated by hardware assists.

**(ii) Para-Virtualization**
In para-virtualization, the **guest OS kernel is intentionally modified** to replace privileged instructions with explicit **hypercalls** to the VMM. The OS "cooperates" with the hypervisor, achieving higher performance because expensive trap-and-emulate cycles are replaced by direct function calls.

The disadvantage is **portability**: only open-source operating systems (Linux, BSD, NetBSD) can be para-virtualized. Proprietary OSes (Windows) cannot be ported without source-code access, limiting compatibility.

**(iii) Hardware-Assisted Virtualization**
Modern CPUs (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) introduce a **new CPU execution mode** with two sub-modes: VMX-root (for VMM) and VMX-non-root (for guest). Sensitive instructions executed in non-root mode automatically trap to the VMM, with no software translation needed. **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** and **Nested Page Tables (NPT)** eliminate the overhead of shadow page tables.

The result is **near-native performance** with **full compatibility** — making it the de-facto standard in modern data centers (KVM, Hyper-V, VMware ESXi).

**Comparison Table:**

| Attribute | Full Virt. (BT) | Para-Virt. | Hardware-Assisted |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|-------------------|
| Guest modification | None | Required | None |
| Performance | Medium | High | Near-native |
| Compatibility | Any OS | Open-source OS only | Any OS |
| Complexity | High (BT engine) | Moderate | Low (HW offload) |

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for Full and Para + 2 marks for Hardware-Assisted + 1 mark for the comparison table)*

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Type 1 hypervisor. How does it differ from a Type 2 hypervisor in terms of performance and isolation? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Type 1 Hypervisor Architecture:**

```
+---------------------+   +---------------------+
|   Guest App 1       |   |   Guest App 2       |
+---------------------+   +---------------------+
|   Guest OS 1 (Win)  |   |   Guest OS 2 (Lin)  |
+---------------------+   +---------------------+
|   Virtual Machine 1 |   |   Virtual Machine 2 |
+---------------------+   +---------------------+
|         HYPERVISOR (VMware ESXi / Xen / KVM)   |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|       PHYSICAL HARDWARE (CPU, RAM, NIC, Disk)  |
+-----------------------------------------------+
```

In this model, the **hypervisor runs directly on the bare metal** of the physical host. It performs the dual role of (a) scheduling and allocating hardware resources to VMs, and (b) enforcing isolation between them. There is **no intervening host OS**.

**Performance Comparison with Type 2:**

| Aspect | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Hardware access path** | Direct: Guest → VMM → Hardware (2 hops) | Indirect: Guest → VMM → Host OS → Hardware (3 hops) |
| **System call overhead** | Lower | Higher (host-OS scheduling adds latency) |
| **I/O throughput** | Higher (no host-OS buffer copies) | Lower (additional driver layer) |
| **Crash domain** | Hypervisor crash = all VMs down | Host-OS crash = all VMs + hypervisor down |

**Isolation Strength:**
- **Type 1:** Stronger. The VMM has full hardware control and uses features like Intel **VT-d / AMD-Vi (IOMMU)** to enforce DMA isolation between VMs.
- **Type 2:** Weaker. The host OS sits between the hypervisor and the hardware; a host-OS vulnerability or misconfiguration can leak resources or expose VMs to the host.

Therefore, **Type 1 hypervisors are preferred in production cloud environments** (e.g., AWS, Azure), while **Type 2 hypervisors are favored for desktop virtualization** (e.g., developer laptops running VirtualBox).

*(7 marks: 3 marks for diagram + architecture explanation + 2 marks for performance comparison + 2 marks for isolation analysis)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) State and explain the Popek–Goldberg virtualization criteria. Using the criteria, explain why the original x86 architecture was not virtually efficient and how modern CPUs overcame it. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of the Theorem (Popek & Goldberg, 1974):**

> A computer architecture $M$ with instruction set $I_{set}$ is **virtualizable** if and only if the set of **sensitive instructions (S)** is a **subset** of the set of **privileged instructions (P)**.

Formally: $S \subseteq P$.

**Definitions:**
- **Privileged Instruction (P):** An instruction that, when executed in user mode, causes a trap to the supervisor (ring 0). When executed in supervisor mode, it completes its intended privileged operation.
- **Sensitive Instruction (S):** An instruction whose behavior or effect depends on the current privilege level — specifically, it either (a) changes the system's hardware configuration (control-sensitive) or (b) reveals privileged processor state (behavior-sensitive).
- **Innocuous Instruction (I):** The complement — $I = I_{set} \setminus S$.

**Logical Proof (Sketch):**

| Direction | Argument |
|-----------|----------|
| **Necessity ($\Rightarrow$)** | Suppose M is virtualizable but some $x \in S$ and $x \notin P$. Then guest execution of $x$ in user mode would not trap to the VMM. Since $x$ is sensitive, its unprivileged behavior differs from privileged behavior, breaking **fidelity**. Contradiction. Therefore $S \subseteq P$. |
| **Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$)** | If $S \subseteq P$, every sensitive instruction issued by the guest will trap to the VMM (because it is privileged). The VMM emulates the privileged effect, preserving **fidelity**. Innocuous instructions run directly, preserving **efficiency**. |

**Why the original x86 was NOT virtualizable:**

The pre-VT-x / pre-AMD-V Intel x86 ISA contained **19 instructions** that were sensitive but not privileged. Classic examples:

| Instruction | Problem |
|-------------|---------|
| `POPF` / `PUSHF` | Reads/writes the EFLAGS register (contains privileged flags like `IOPL`) without trapping |
| `SMSW` (Store Machine Status Word) | Reveals CR0 contents to user mode |
| `LAR` (Load Access Rights) | Reveals descriptor privilege info |
| Various segment-register manipulations | Behavior in user mode differs from supervisor mode |

Since $S \not\subseteq P$ (specifically, $|S \setminus P| = 19$), the original x86 **failed the Popek–Goldberg criterion** and was not efficiently virtualizable.

**How modern CPUs overcame this:**

1. **Intel VT-x (VMX):** Introduced in 2005. Added a new privilege mode with two sub-modes — **VMX-root** (for VMM) and **VMX-non-root** (for guest). The new instruction `VMXON` enters VMX operation, and all sensitive-but-not-privileged instructions now trap to the VMM in non-root mode. This **transforms the ISA** to satisfy $S \subseteq P$.

2. **AMD-V (SVM):** AMD's equivalent extension with similar effect.

3. **Extended Page Tables (EPT) / Nested Page Tables (NPT):** Hardware-assisted two-level page table translation, eliminating shadow page table overhead.

4. **IOMMU (VT-d / AMD-Vi):** Hardware-enforced DMA isolation, allowing direct device assignment to VMs (PCI passthrough).

Thus, modern x86 servers are **fully Popek–Goldberg virtualizable** with near-native performance.

*(7 marks: 2 marks theorem statement + 1 mark proof sketch + 2 marks x86 problem analysis + 2 marks modern solution)*

#### (b) Explain the process of Live Migration of Virtual Machines in cloud data centers. What is its role in achieving elasticity and fault tolerance? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Live migration is the process of relocating a running VM from one physical host (source) to another (destination) **with no perceived service disruption** to the end user, achieved by transferring VM memory, CPU state, and device state while the VM continues to execute.

**Pre-Copy Algorithm (Most Widely Used — Xen, KVM, VMware vMotion):**

The pre-copy algorithm proceeds in **three phases**:

| Phase | Action | Duration |
|-------|--------|----------|
| **1. Iterative Pre-Copy** | Hypervisor continuously copies memory pages from source to destination while the VM continues running on the source. Dirty pages are tracked and re-sent in the next iteration. | Multiple rounds, each shorter than the previous (geometric convergence) |
| **2. Stop-and-Copy** | Source VM is suspended. CPU registers, device state, and residual dirty pages are transferred. The VM is paused for a brief moment. | Typically **< 1 second** |
| **3. Resume / Activate** | Destination host resumes the VM. Source releases resources. A reverse ARP / gratuitous ARP is sent so the physical network switch updates its CAM table and forwards future traffic to the destination. | Milliseconds |

**Mathematical Condition for Convergence:**

Let $D_i$ = dirty page rate at iteration $i$, $D_0$ = initial dirty rate, $R$ = convergence ratio.

$$
D_{i+1} = R \times D_i
$$

The loop terminates when $D_i \leq D_{thresh}$ (e.g., 50 MB). Total data transferred:

$$
D_{total} = D_0 \times \frac{1 - R^{N}}{1 - R}
$$

**Total Migration Time:**

$$
T_{mig} = \frac{D_{total}}{B} + T_{stop-copy} + T_{resume}
$$

where $B$ is the available network bandwidth.

**Role in Elasticity:**
- Cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and GCP use live migration to **balance load** across a fleet of hosts without disrupting customer workloads.
- During peak demand, VMs can be **migrated away** from hot hosts; during troughs, VMs can be **consolidated** to free hosts and power them down — enabling **auto-scaling** and **energy efficiency**.

**Role in Fault Tolerance:**
- When a physical host exhibits **pre-failure indicators** (e.g., rising temperature, SMART disk warnings, ECC memory errors), VMs are **proactively evacuated** to healthy hosts using live migration.
- This is the foundation of **High Availability (HA)** in OpenStack, VMware vSphere HA, and AWS Auto Recovery.

*(7 marks: 2 marks process explanation + 1.5 marks pre-copy phases + 1.5 marks role in elasticity + 2 marks role in fault tolerance)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the hypervisor types:** Students often write "Type 1 is faster because it has less code" — wrong. The correct answer is that Type 1 has **fewer privilege transitions** (no host OS in the path).
> 2. **Mis-stating Popek–Goldberg:** Writing "$S = P$" instead of "$S \subseteq P$" loses 1 mark. Subset relation is **critical**.
> 3. **Forgetting the three live-migration phases:** Examiners explicitly ask "explain the steps." Skipping the **Resume/ARP step** is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** Writing "the answer is 7" without units or context loses the "final answer" mark. Always write: $N_{VM,max} = 7 \text{ VMs}$.
> 5. **Calling Binary Translation a "hardware feature":** BT is a **software technique** used in the *pre-VT-x* era. Confusing it with hardware-assisted virtualization is a frequent error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**

- ☐ **VM** = software-emulated computer; **VMM/Hypervisor** = software that creates and runs VMs.
- ☐ Three properties of a VMM (Popek–Goldberg): **Fidelity, Isolation, Efficiency**.
- ☐ Two broad VM classes: **Process VM** (JVM, CLR) and **System VM** (VMware, KVM).
- ☐ **Type 1 hypervisor** = bare-metal (ESXi, Xen, KVM); **Type 2 hypervisor** = hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- ☐ Three virtualization techniques: **Full Virtualization, Para-Virtualization, Hardware-Assisted Virtualization**.
- ☐ **Popek–Goldberg theorem:** $S \subseteq P$ for an ISA to be virtualizable.
- ☐ Original x86 had **19 sensitive-but-non-privileged instructions** → not virtualizable.
- ☐ Modern solution: **Intel VT-x / AMD-V** + EPT/NPT + IOMMU.
- ☐ **Live migration** uses **pre-copy** algorithm: iterative pre-copy → stop-and-copy → resume.
- ☐ Live migration enables **elasticity** (load balancing) and **fault tolerance** (proactive VM evacuation).
- ☐ **Resource virtualization** covers CPU (time-share), Memory (EPT/ballooning), Storage (virtual disks), I/O (virtio/SR-IOV), and Network (vSwitch/overlay).
- ☐ Key memory formula: $M_{VM,eff} = M_{VM} \times (1 + O_{shadow})$; VM count: $N_{VM,max} = \lfloor R_{avail} / M_{VM,eff} \rfloor$.
- ☐ Key migration formula: $D_{total} = D_0 \times (1 - R^N) / (1 - R)$.
- ☐ Cloud platforms: **AWS uses Xen/Nitro KVM**, **Azure uses Hyper-V**, **GCP uses KVM**.
- ☐ **Hypervisor trap cost** = $C_{exit} + C_{service} + C_{entry}$ — total CPU cycles for one VM exit.
- ☐ Always mention the **3 VM isolation properties** when explaining cloud security.
- ☐ Always mention **hardware-assisted virtualization** as the modern standard in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ Be able to draw a **labeled Type 1 hypervisor architecture diagram** from memory.
- ☐ Be able to derive the **Popek–Goldberg necessity proof** in 3 logical steps.

> **End of Module 2 — Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization) Notes**
