topics.title: Non-virtualized v/s Virtualized Machine Environments. Types of VMs - Process VM v/s System VM. Emulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-virtualized v/s Virtualized Machine Environments. Types of VMs - Process VM v/s System VM. Emulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Machine Environment

A **Machine Environment** refers to the complete software-hardware stack that enables an application to execute. The stack consists of four canonical abstraction layers, ordered from hardware (bottom) to user (top):

1. **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** — the contract between hardware and software (e.g., x86-64, ARMv8, RISC-V).
2. **Hardware / Firmware Abstractions** — the actual silicon + microcode + BIOS/UEFI.
3. **Operating System / Runtime** — process management, memory management, device drivers, syscall interface.
4. **Application / User-level Programs** — the workload that the end user actually wants to run.

The **"Environment"** of a workload is therefore the *aggregate* of these four layers. Whether this environment is *virtual* or *non-virtual (physical)* determines everything from performance, isolation, portability, to security guarantees.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *Machine Environment* is said to be **virtualized** when the application-level behaviour is preserved while the actual physical resources (CPU, RAM, disk, NIC) are abstracted, multiplexed, and arbitrated by a piece of control software. If the workload runs directly on the bare hardware with a single OS image, the environment is **non-virtualized**.

### 1.2 Non-Virtualized (Physical / Native) Machine Environment

In a **non-virtualized environment**, a single operating system is installed directly on the bare-metal hardware and has **unmediated, exclusive control** over all physical resources. The OS assumes it owns the entire machine. There is no layer of indirection between the OS and the hardware.

**Key Properties:**
- **Single OS image** boots from the firmware.
- **No Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** is present in the software stack.
- The application is compiled for the **host ISA** and runs natively.
- Device drivers in the OS talk **directly to the hardware** registers.
- Performance overhead = **near zero** (baseline).

### 1.3 Virtualized Machine Environment

In a **virtualized environment**, a software layer called the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)**, popularly known as a **Hypervisor**, is inserted between the hardware and one or more guest operating systems. Each guest OS believes it has the entire machine to itself, but in reality the VMM multiplexes the underlying physical resources.

**Key Properties:**
- **Multiple OS instances** can co-exist on a single physical host.
- The VMM **arbitrates** access to physical CPU, memory, I/O devices.
- Each guest operates on a **virtual hardware clone** — a software-emulated CPU, RAM, disk, and NIC.
- A VMM must satisfy the **Popek & Goldberg (1974) equivalence, efficiency, and resource-control** properties to be considered a true VMM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goldberg's Classical Definition (1973):** A *Virtual Machine* is "an efficient, isolated duplicate of the real machine." The three pillars are: **(i) Fidelity** (software in the VM behaves as on native hardware), **(ii) Performance** (majority of instructions run with **no** VMM intervention), and **(iii) Safety / Isolation** (the VMM fully mediates access to physical resources).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Building Model

Imagine a plot of land with a single house on it.

- **Non-virtualized = Single-Family House on a Plot:** One family owns the land, builds one house, lives there alone. The family has 100% access to the plot's resources (water, electricity, garden). There is no shared infrastructure, no landlord, no rules. *Performance is optimal because there is no middleman.*

- **Virtualized = High-Rise Apartment Building:** The plot of land is still one, but a builder (the **Hypervisor**) constructs multiple floors. Each floor (a **Virtual Machine**) is a self-contained apartment that looks like a complete house — has its own kitchen (CPU), its own water tank quota (RAM), its own parking slot (Disk). The builder controls the elevator, water pumps, and electricity distribution. *Each tenant (Guest OS) believes they have an independent house, but resources are physically shared and arbitrated.*

The cost of the "landlord" (VMM) is a small percentage of throughput, but the benefit is **multi-tenancy, isolation, and elasticity** — exactly what a cloud needs.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Performance overhead of full virtualization | $\eta$ | **2 % – 10 %** of native |
| Performance overhead of emulation | $\eta_{emu}$ | **10× – 1000×** slower than native |
| Number of VMs per physical core (practical) | $N_{v/c}$ | **4 – 32** depending on workload |
| Memory overhead per VM | $M_{oh}$ | **128 MB – 1 GB** of hypervisor reservations |

> [!NOTE]
> The values above are empirical, not theoretical constants. They are the *expected ballpark* in modern production hypervisors such as VMware ESXi, KVM, and Microsoft Hyper-V. Always cite your specific environment in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Differences: Layer-by-Layer Walkthrough

#### Layer 1 — Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)

- **Non-virtualized:** The CPU executes the *native* ISA of the silicon (e.g., x86-64). The OS image contains binaries compiled for that exact ISA. The system boots, firmware enumerates the CPU, hands control to the bootloader, and the OS kernel initializes the hardware.
- **Virtualized:** The guest OS still believes it is running on the native ISA, but the VMM may *intercept* certain "privileged" or "sensitive" instructions. The instruction is either:
  - **Emulated** in software (slow path), or
  - **Directly executed** on the CPU (fast path), or
  - **Binary-translated** into a safe equivalent sequence.

#### Layer 2 — Hardware / Memory

- **Non-virtualized:** The OS uses the Memory Management Unit (MMU) directly. Page tables are *real* and point to physical RAM.
- **Virtualized:** The VMM introduces a second level of indirection. The guest maintains *guest-physical* page tables; the VMM maintains *machine-physical* page tables. This is called **shadow paging** (older technique) or **EPT / NPT (Extended / Nested Page Tables)** (modern technique).

#### Layer 3 — I/O Devices

- **Non-virtualized:** Drivers in the OS communicate directly with PCIe / USB / SATA controllers.
- **Virtualized:** Each guest sees *virtual* devices (e.g., a virtual NIC, virtual disk). The VMM traps I/O accesses and routes them to a *backend driver* that talks to the real hardware. This indirection is the primary source of I/O overhead.

#### Layer 4 — Application

- **Non-virtualized:** Native binary, direct syscall into the host OS.
- **Virtualized:** Identical experience from the application's perspective — calls the guest OS's syscall interface as if it were the only OS in the world.

### 2.2 The Three Properties of a True VMM (Popek & Goldberg Theorem)

For software to be a valid VMM on a given ISA, three conditions must hold:

| # | Property | Formal Statement | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fidelity / Equivalence** | Behaviour of a program in a VM must be identical (except for timing and resource availability) to its behaviour on the bare machine. | No instruction should "fail" inside the VM that would succeed on hardware. |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | A statistically dominant subset of the virtual processor's instructions must be executed **without VMM intervention**. | "Trap-and-emulate" should be the *exception*, not the norm. |
| 3 | **Resource Control / Safety** | The VMM must be able to gain complete control over all system resources at any time. | The VMM must run at a higher privilege level than any guest. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Instructions (Popek & Goldberg):** An instruction is *privileged* if it traps to the supervisor in user mode. It is *sensitive* if it can affect the system's configuration. For a VMM to be implementable purely via trap-and-emulate, the ISA must satisfy: **{Privileged Instructions} ⊇ {Sensitive Instructions}**. x86 historically *violated* this rule (instructions like `POPF` were sensitive but not always privileged), which forced VMware to invent **Binary Translation**.

### 2.3 Types of Virtual Machines — Taxonomy

The most cited taxonomy comes from **Smith & Nair (2005), "The Architecture of Virtual Machines"**, and it has been adopted by KTU reference texts.

#### A. Process Virtual Machine (Process VM / Application VM)

A **Process VM** exists only for the lifetime of a *single process*. Its job is to provide a runtime environment in which a program — typically compiled to a *virtual* (abstract) ISA — can execute *portably* across different host hardware.

**Examples:** Java Virtual Machine (JVM), Microsoft .NET CLR, Parrot VM, LLVM bitcode.

**Sub-classes:**
1. **Multiprogrammed VM** — provides OS-like services to one process (e.g., the JVM heap manager).
2. **High-Level Language VM** — abstracts the source-language semantics (e.g., the JVM abstracting Java bytecode).
3. **System-Level VM (in Smith's terminology — different from the "System VM" below)** — emulates a foreign ISA for a single process (e.g., a Linux binary running under QEMU-user-mode on Windows).

#### B. System Virtual Machine (System VM / Platform VM)

A **System VM** provides a *complete system environment* capable of running an *entire operating system* and all its applications. It emulates a full hardware platform (CPU, memory, disks, NIC, BIOS) and boots a guest OS from scratch.

**Examples:** VMware ESXi guests, KVM guests, VirtualBox VMs, Microsoft Hyper-V VMs, Xen HVM guests.

**Sub-classes (based on hypervisor placement):**
- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor:** VMM runs directly on hardware (ESXi, Hyper-V Server, Xen).
- **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor:** VMM runs as an application on a host OS (VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, Parallels).

### 2.4 Emulation — Definition and Mechanics

**Emulation** is the most general form of instruction-set translation: the VMM (or emulator) mimics the behaviour of a *foreign* ISA on the *host* ISA. Each guest instruction is *interpreted* — fetched, decoded into an internal representation, and then executed as a sequence of native host instructions.

**Distinction from Virtualization:**

| Property | Virtualization | Emulation |
|---|---|---|
| Guest ISA | Same as host ISA | Different from host ISA |
| Example | Linux on x86-via-KVM | ARM Linux on x86-via-QEMU |
| Speed | Near-native (2-10% overhead) | 10×–1000× slower |
| Mechanism | Direct execution + trap | Instruction-by-instruction interpretation |
| Hardware assistance | Common (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) | Not possible (ISA mismatch) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virtualization Overhead | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{vm} - T_{native}}{T_{native}} \times 100$ | **%** | $T_{vm}$ is wall-clock task time in VM. |
| Effective VM CPU count | $C_{eff} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} vCPU_i \times \eta_{cpu}$ | **vCPUs** | Sum of allocated virtual CPUs. |
| Memory overcommit ratio | $R_{mc} = \dfrac{\sum M_{vm}}{M_{phys}}$ | **dimensionless** | Can exceed 1.0 (e.g., 1.5) with page sharing. |
| Disk thin-provision savings | $S_{disk} = P_{prov} - P_{used}$ | **GB** | $P_{prov}$ is provisioned, $P_{used}$ is actually written. |
| Emulation speedup factor | $\sigma_{emu} = \dfrac{T_{native}}{T_{emu}}$ | **dimensionless** | Typically $0.001 \le \sigma_{emu} \le 0.1$. |
| Popek-Goldberg efficiency | $\epsilon = \dfrac{N_{direct}}{N_{total}}$ | **fraction** | $N_{direct}$ = instructions executed without VMM. |
| Type-1 vs Type-2 VMM syscall cost | $C_{sys} = C_{trap} + C_{emulate} + C_{return}$ | **CPU cycles** | Type-1 has $C_{trap}$ only; Type-2 has additional $C_{mode-switch}$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Tip:** In the table above, I deliberately used the LaTeX fraction `\dfrac{...}{...}` instead of the literal pipe `|` symbol (as you can see inside the `\vert x \vert` rule). This avoids breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Where It Matters in Industry |
|---|---|
| Non-virtualized | HPC clusters, real-time embedded controllers, GPUs that need bare-metal performance. |
| Type-1 Virtualization | **Cloud IaaS** — AWS EC2 Nitro, Azure Hyper-V, Google Cloud KVM. |
| Type-2 Virtualization | Developer laptops running multiple OSes, lab environments. |
| Process VM (JVM/.NET) | **Write once, run anywhere** — Android (ART/Dalvik), enterprise Java backends. |
| Emulation (QEMU) | **Cross-compile testing**, OS development, malware analysis sandboxes, **Apple Silicon Rosetta 2** (AArch64 emulating x86-64). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How a Guest Instruction Executes in an Emulator (Software Translation)

In a *pure* software emulator, **every** guest instruction is fetched, decoded, and executed as a sequence of host instructions. Let us walk through the full life-cycle.

**Step 1 — Fetch the guest instruction.**
The emulator's *fetch-decode-execute* main loop reads a fixed number of bytes from the guest's code memory.

$$
\text{PC}_{guest} \leftarrow \text{PC}_{guest} + \text{len}(\text{opcode})
$$

where $\text{PC}_{guest}$ is the guest's program counter. The length of the opcode in bytes depends on the guest ISA (e.g., 2 bytes for ARM Thumb, 1–15 bytes for x86 variable-length encoding).

**Step 2 — Decode the opcode.**
A large `switch` statement (or a decoded instruction cache) maps the opcode to a C handler function. For an ARM `ADD R0, R1, R2`, the decoder identifies it as an integer ALU operation.

**Step 3 — Fetch the operands.**
Operands are read from the emulated register file (which is a struct in C) or from emulated memory. Memory access goes through an MMU emulator that consults shadow page tables.

**Step 4 — Execute the operation.**
The host C function performs the addition on a 64-bit host integer:

$$
R0_{host} = R1_{host} + R2_{host}
$$

**Step 5 — Update flags / condition codes.**
For ARM, flags such as **N (negative)**, **Z (zero)**, **C (carry)**, **V (overflow)** must be recomputed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{sign bit of } R0_{host} \\
Z &= (R0_{host} == 0) \;\; ? \;\; 1 : 0 \\
C &= \text{carry out of the most significant bit} \\
V &= \text{two's-complement overflow flag}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Write back the result.**
The new value of $R0_{host}$ is stored back into the emulated register file.

**Step 7 — Increment PC and loop.**
The emulated PC is advanced by the instruction length, and the loop returns to Step 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The cost of one emulated instruction in cycles is roughly:
> $$ T_{emu} = T_{fetch} + T_{decode} + T_{operand} + T_{exec} + T_{writeback} + T_{dispatch} $$
> which on a modern x86 host typically yields **10 to 1000 host cycles per single guest instruction**, explaining the order-of-magnitude slowdown.

### 3.2 Derivation: Virtualization Overhead of a Type-1 Hypervisor

Assume a task runs in $T_{native}$ seconds on bare metal. In a virtualized environment, every privileged instruction executed in the guest causes a **VM exit** to the hypervisor, which then emulates it and performs a **VM entry** to resume the guest. The number of such exits per unit of work is $E$ (exits/second).

**Step 1 — Add the cost of VM exits and entries.**

$$
T_{vm} = T_{native} + E \times T_{round-trip}
$$

where $T_{round-trip}$ is the time cost of one VM-exit + VM-entry cycle. For Intel VT-x, this is approximately **500–1000 ns** in 2024-era hardware.

**Step 2 — Compute the fractional overhead.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{T_{vm} - T_{native}}{T_{native}} = \dfrac{E \times T_{round-trip}}{T_{native}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
If a CPU-bound workload makes $E = 1000$ exits per second (e.g., due to CR3 reloads on every page-table switch), and $T_{round-trip} = 500\,\text{ns}$, then:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1000 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{T_{native}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{T_{native}}
$$

For a workload with $T_{native} = 1$ second, the overhead is **0.05 %** — negligible. For a workload with $T_{native} = 10^{-3}$ s, the overhead is **50 %** — significant. This is why virtualization is most damaging to **I/O-heavy** and **syscall-heavy** micro-benchmarks, not to long CPU-bound tasks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Execution Techniques

| Dimension | Direct Execution (Native) | Trap & Emulate (Full Virt.) | Binary Translation (Full Virt.) | Emulation (Pure Software) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guest ISA = Host ISA? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Mechanism | CPU runs guest insns. directly | Sensitive insns. trap; VMM emulates | VMM rewrites guest binary to safe form | Each guest insn. interpreted in software |
| Hardware assist needed? | No | **Yes** (VT-x / AMD-V) | No (works on pre-2005 CPUs) | No |
| Speed vs. native | **1.00×** (baseline) | **0.90× – 0.98×** | **0.85× – 0.95×** | **0.001× – 0.1×** |
| Example | Bare-metal OS | KVM on AMD-V | VMware (pre-2006) | QEMU, Rosetta 2 |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process VM vs. System VM

| Dimension | Process VM | System VM |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifetime** | One process | Many processes (entire OS) |
| **Provides** | Application-level ABI | Full hardware-platform ABI |
| **Guest is** | A program binary (bytecode / p-code) | An operating system |
| **Typical users** | Developers, language-runtime users | Cloud consumers, sysadmins |
| **Examples** | JVM, .NET CLR, Dalvik/ART | VMware ESXi guests, KVM, Hyper-V |
| **Resource control** | Limited to the process sandbox | **Full** (VMM owns all hardware) |
| **Isolation** | Process-level (lightweight) | **VM-level** (hardware-isolated) |
| **Performance cost** | Typically 10-30% (JIT-compiled) | 2-10% with HW assist |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Non-Virtualized vs. Virtualized Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NonVirt["NON VIRTUALIZED ENVIRONMENT"]
        direction TB
        A1[Application 1]
        A2[Application 2]
        B1[Host Operating System]
        C1[Bare Metal Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC]
        A1 --> B1
        A2 --> B1
        B1 --> C1
    end

    subgraph VirtEnv["VIRTUALIZED ENVIRONMENT"]
        direction TB
        V1[Application A]
        V2[Guest OS 1 Linux]
        V3[Application B]
        V4[Guest OS 2 Windows]
        V5[Hypervisor VMM]
        V6[Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC]
        V1 --> V2
        V3 --> V4
        V2 --> V5
        V4 --> V5
        V5 --> V6
    end

    style NonVirt fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#900
    style VirtEnv fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#069
```

**Reading the diagram:** The left side has *one* application-OS pair directly on hardware. The right side has *two* guest OSes arbitrated by a single VMM sitting on the physical hardware. The VMM is the additional layer that defines virtualization.

### 4.2 Process VM (Application VM) — JVM Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC[Java Source Code MyApp.java]
    BYTE[Java Bytecode MyApp.class]
    JITC[JIT Compiler C1 C2]
    CLS[Class Loader Subsystem]
    RT[Runtime Data Areas Heap Method Area Stack PC Registers]
    EXEC[Execution Engine Interpreter]
    GC[Garbage Collector]
    OS[Host Operating System]
    HW[Physical Hardware]

    SRC -->|javac compile| BYTE
    BYTE --> CLS
    BYTE --> EXEC
    CLS --> RT
    EXEC --> JITC
    JITC -->|native code| HW
    EXEC --> RT
    RT --> GC
    EXEC --> OS
    OS --> HW
```

**Reading the diagram:** Java source is compiled *once* into **bytecode** (a virtual ISA). The JVM's class loader brings the bytecode into runtime data areas. The execution engine either *interprets* the bytecode or uses a **JIT compiler** to translate "hot" bytecode into host-native instructions. The JIT path approaches native speed for warm code; cold (un-JITted) code is interpreted and slow. The Garbage Collector reclaims heap memory. The entire JVM is itself *a process running on the host OS*.

### 4.3 System VM — Type-1 Bare-Metal Hypervisor

```mermaid
graph TB
    G1A[App A on Linux]
    G1B[Guest OS 1 Linux Kernel]
    G2A[App X on Windows]
    G2B[Guest OS 2 Windows Kernel]
    HV[Type 1 Hypervisor ESXi Xen Hyper V Bare Metal]
    DRV[Native Hardware Drivers]
    PHW[Physical Server CPU Memory NIC Storage]

    G1A --> G1B
    G2A --> G2B
    G1B --> HV
    G2B --> HV
    HV --> DRV
    DRV --> PHW
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Type-1 hypervisor is installed directly on the physical server. It contains the *control domain* (dom0 in Xen, or the VMkernel in ESXi) and the *native drivers* that talk to real hardware. The two guest OSes above it never see real devices — they see virtual disks, virtual NICs, virtual consoles provided by the hypervisor.

### 4.4 Emulation Flow — QEMU Running an ARM Guest on an x86 Host

```mermaid
graph LR
    ARMBIN[ARM Binary ARMv7 ELF]
    QEMU[QEMU Emulator Running as x86 Process]
    TB[Translation Block Cache]
    HOST[X86 Host CPU Native Execution]
    ARMREG[Emulated ARM Register File C Struct]
    ARMMEM[Emulated ARM Memory Byte Array]
    HOSTOS[Host Operating System]

    ARMBIN -->|loaded into| ARMMEM
    ARMMEM --> QEMU
    QEMU -->|decode| TB
    TB -->|emit host insns| HOST
    HOST -->|results| QEMU
    QEMU -->|update| ARMREG
    QEMU --> HOSTOS
```

**Reading the diagram:** The guest ARM binary is loaded into an emulated memory buffer inside the QEMU process. QEMU decodes blocks of guest instructions into a **Translation Block (TB) cache** — these are sequences of *host-x86* instructions that perform the same effect as a contiguous block of guest instructions. The host CPU executes the translated block natively. After the block, QEMU regains control, updates the emulated register file, and dispatches the next block. This is the **dynamic binary translation** approach used by QEMU's TCG (Tiny Code Generator) mode.

### 4.5 Full Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Virtualization Style

```mermaid
graph TD
    START{Need to run software?}
    SAMEISA{Guest ISA same as host ISA?}
    OSLEVEL{Need a full OS or just one app?}
    APPONLY[Use Process VM JVM .NET CLR]
    TYPE1[Use Type 1 Hypervisor KVM ESXi]
    TYPE2[Use Type 2 Hypervisor VirtualBox]
    EMU[Use Emulation QEMU BOCHS]
    PERF{Performance critical?}
    HWASSIST{Hardware virtualization available?}

    START --> SAMEISA
    SAMEISA -->|Yes| OSLEVEL
    SAMEISA -->|No| EMU
    OSLEVEL -->|Just one app| APPONLY
    OSLEVEL -->|Full OS| PERF
    PERF -->|Yes, production| HWASSIST
    PERF -->|No, dev laptop| TYPE2
    HWASSIST -->|Yes| TYPE1
    HWASSIST -->|No| EMU
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a decision flowchart a cloud architect would walk through when choosing the right execution substrate. Note the three terminal leaves: *Process VM* (single app, portably), *Type-1 / Type-2 Hypervisor* (full OS, same ISA), and *Emulation* (foreign ISA, performance-tolerant).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Differentiate between a non-virtualized machine environment and a virtualized machine environment. State one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Non-Virtualized | Virtualized |
|---|---|---|
| **Software layer between OS and hardware** | None (OS owns the hardware) | VMM/Hypervisor sits in between |
| **Number of OS instances** | One | Many (concurrent) |
| **Resource control** | Sole control by the host OS | Arbitrated by VMM |
| **Performance overhead** | Near zero (native) | 2 – 10 % (full virt.) or higher |
| **Example** | Bare-metal Linux on a laptop | VMware ESXi server hosting 10 Linux VMs |

*(Examiner's Note: A crisp 3-row table + 1-line example fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
**What is a Process Virtual Machine? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Process Virtual Machine** is a virtual execution environment created for the lifetime of a *single application process*. It provides an abstract ISA (often called *bytecode* or *p-code*) and a runtime that translates the abstract instructions into the host machine's native instructions, either by interpretation or JIT compilation. The process VM abstracts away the underlying hardware and operating system, giving the program *portability* across heterogeneous hosts.

**Examples:** (1) **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** — runs Java bytecode on any host with a JVM port. (2) **Microsoft .NET Common Language Runtime (CLR)** — runs CIL/MSIL bytecode on Windows, Linux, and macOS.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE YOU WRITE:**
> 1. **Never skip the comparison table.** A 14-mark question on "Process VM vs System VM" *must* have a table; missing it costs **at least 3 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Do not confuse "System VM" (Smith & Nair) with "System-level VM"** — they are different sub-classes in different parts of the taxonomy. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Diagrams are mandatory.** Even a rough hand-drawn architecture diagram fetches **2 bonus marks** under the KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. **Cite examples.** Bare statements like "VMs run in the cloud" lose marks. Always give named, real-world products: KVM, ESXi, QEMU, JVM.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Slot]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture and operation of a Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor. With a neat diagram, show how multiple guest OSes are arbitrated by the hypervisor. Compare it with a Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor.**

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of a Process Virtual Machine. Use the Java Virtual Machine as a reference.**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

A **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** is a virtualization control layer that is installed *directly* on the physical server's hardware — there is no host operating system beneath it. The hypervisor itself includes a small, hardened *control domain* (VMkernel in VMware ESXi, *dom0* in Xen) that hosts the management plane, and a set of *native device drivers* that directly control the physical CPU, memory, NICs, and storage controllers.

**Architecture walkthrough:**
1. The hypervisor boots from firmware and takes exclusive ownership of all physical resources.
2. It initializes a **scheduler** that multiplexes physical CPU cores among virtual CPUs (vCPUs).
3. It sets up a **memory allocator** that carves physical RAM into isolated partitions.
4. It exposes **virtual hardware** (virtual CPU, virtual disk, virtual NIC, virtual console) to each guest.
5. Each guest OS boots inside its partition, believing it owns the whole machine.

**Type-1 vs Type-2 Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| **Runs on** | Bare hardware | As an application on a host OS |
| **Performance** | Near-native (2–5% overhead) | Moderate (5–15% overhead due to extra OS layer) |
| **Use case** | Production cloud, data centers | Developer laptops, labs |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels |
| **Driver model** | Hypervisor has its own drivers | Relies on host OS drivers |
| **Security surface** | Smaller (no host OS) | Larger (host OS = additional attack surface) |

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(See SECTION 4.3 above for the exact architecture; student should redraw a similar block diagram.)*

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Type-1 hypervisors are the de-facto standard in cloud IaaS because they eliminate the host-OS performance penalty and reduce the security surface. Type-2 hypervisors are still preferred for desktop virtualization.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

A **Process Virtual Machine** is a runtime engine that provides an *abstract execution environment* for a single process. We use the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** as the canonical example.

**Internal components of the JVM (5 marks):**

1. **Class Loader Subsystem** — reads `.class` files containing Java bytecode, verifies them, and brings them into the runtime data areas. Uses a delegation model: *Bootstrap → Extension → Application* classloader.
2. **Runtime Data Areas:**
   - **Heap** — shared memory for all objects and instance variables. Subject to **Garbage Collection**.
   - **Method Area** — stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode.
   - **JVM Stacks** — one per thread; holds stack frames for active method calls.
   - **PC Registers** — one per thread; points to the current bytecode instruction.
   - **Native Method Stacks** — supports JNI calls into C/C++ libraries.
3. **Execution Engine** — the heart of the JVM. It has two parts:
   - **Interpreter** — reads bytecode one instruction at a time and dispatches to a native handler.
   - **JIT Compiler** (C1, C2, Graal) — compiles "hot" bytecode to native host instructions at runtime for performance.
4. **Garbage Collector (GC)** — reclaims unreachable heap objects. Modern GCs (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah) use concurrent and parallel techniques.
5. **JNI / Native Method Interface** — bridges JVM-managed code with host-OS native libraries (e.g., `System.currentTimeMillis()`).

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.2 Mermaid diagram — student should redraw the same block architecture.)*

**Conclusion:** The Process VM provides *portability* (write once, run anywhere), *safety* (sandboxed execution, GC-managed memory), and *managed performance* (JIT approaching native speed). The cost is increased memory footprint and occasional GC pauses.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Slot]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Define emulation in the context of virtualization. Explain the steps involved in emulating a foreign instruction set with a suitable example. Compare emulation with full virtualization.**

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the two main types of virtual machines — Process VM and System VM. Give two real-world examples for each type.**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Emulation* is the software-based imitation of an *entire foreign hardware platform* (CPU + memory + I/O) by interpreting guest instructions on a host with a *different* ISA. Unlike virtualization, the guest ISA and host ISA are not the same. The emulator translates each guest instruction (or block of instructions) into an equivalent sequence of host instructions at runtime.

**Example:** **QEMU (Quick EMUlator)** emulating an **ARMv7** guest on an **x86-64** host. Each ARM `ADD R0, R1, R2` is decoded by QEMU's *TCG (Tiny Code Generator)* and translated into a small block of x86 instructions that perform the same addition and update the emulated ARM flags.

**Step-by-Step Emulation Process (3 marks):**

1. **Load** the guest binary into an emulated memory buffer.
2. **Fetch** the next guest instruction from the emulated PC.
3. **Decode** the opcode into an internal micro-operation.
4. **Read** the operands from the emulated register file.
5. **Execute** the operation using a host C function (or via JITted host code).
6. **Update** flags, condition codes, and the emulated register file.
7. **Write back** the result to emulated memory if required.
8. **Increment** the emulated PC and **loop**.

**Comparison Table — Emulation vs Full Virtualization (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Emulation | Full Virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| **Guest ISA = Host ISA?** | **No** (foreign ISA) | **Yes** (same ISA) |
| **Speed** | 10×–1000× slower | 2%–10% overhead |
| **Hardware assist** | Not possible | Yes (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) |
| **Primary technique** | Interpretation / dynamic binary translation | Trap-and-emulate + hardware assists |
| **Example** | QEMU, BOCHS, Apple Rosetta 2 | VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Process Virtual Machine (3.5 marks):**
A Process VM exists for the lifetime of *one* process. It provides an *abstract ISA* and a runtime that translates it to the host ISA. The host OS and hardware are abstracted away from the program.

- **Examples:** **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** (executes Java bytecode), **.NET CLR** (executes CIL/MSIL).
- *Diagram: See SECTION 4.2.*

**System Virtual Machine (3.5 marks):**
A System VM provides a *complete hardware-platform emulation* capable of running an *entire OS and its applications*. The guest OS boots inside a hardware-isolated partition managed by a VMM.

- **Examples:** **VMware ESXi guests** (Type-1, production), **KVM guests** (Linux kernel as hypervisor).
- *Diagram: See SECTION 4.3.*

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **non-virtualized** environment has *no* VMM; a single OS owns the hardware directly.
- [ ] A **virtualized** environment has a **VMM/Hypervisor** that multiplexes hardware among guests.
- [ ] **Popek & Goldberg (1974)** defined the three VMM properties: **Fidelity, Efficiency, Resource Control**.
- [ ] **Type-1 (bare-metal)** hypervisors run on hardware directly (ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM).
- [ ] **Type-2 (hosted)** hypervisors run as apps on a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- [ ] **Process VM** = runtime for *one process* (JVM, .NET CLR). Abstract ISA, JIT-compiled.
- [ ] **System VM** = complete hardware platform for *an entire OS* (VMware guests, KVM guests).
- [ ] **Emulation** runs a *foreign ISA* on the host; uses software interpretation (QEMU, BOCHS, Rosetta 2).
- [ ] Emulation is **10× – 1000× slower** than native; full virtualization is **2 % – 10 %** overhead.
- [ ] **Trap-and-emulate** works only when **{Privileged} ⊇ {Sensitive}** instructions — x86 historically violated this, hence the need for **binary translation** (VMware).
- [ ] **Modern hardware assists** (Intel VT-x, AMD-V, ARM Virtualization Extensions) close the gap and allow efficient full virtualization.
- [ ] The **virtualization overhead formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{T_{vm} - T_{native}}{T_{native}} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] For exam answers: always include a **comparison table** + a **diagram** + at least **two real examples** per concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interpretation and Binary Translation. Virtualization Layers. Hypervisors/VMM - Types of Hypervisors. Full Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interpretation and Binary Translation

### Interpretation
**Interpretation** is a code translation technique used in virtualization where a virtual machine monitor (VMM) reads each instruction (or block of instructions) of the guest operating system's binary code and executes an equivalent sequence of instructions on the host hardware *one at a time*. No static translation is performed — the guest code remains in its original form in memory and is dynamically decoded at runtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Interpretation is a runtime code translation method in which each guest instruction is fetched, decoded, and emulated by the host VMM through software routines, without modifying the original guest binary.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of interpretation like a **real-time human translator** at a multilingual conference. The speaker (guest CPU instruction) says something, and the translator (VMM interpreter) immediately converts it word-by-word into the listener's language (host ISA) and delivers it. It is flexible but slow, because the translation happens on the fly.

### Binary Translation (BT)
**Binary Translation** is a more advanced technique where blocks (or entire segments) of guest binary code are *statically or dynamically* translated into an equivalent block of host-native instructions *before* execution. The translated code is then cached and executed directly, eliminating the per-instruction emulation overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Binary Translation is a virtualization mechanism in which chunks of guest machine code are rewritten into host ISA instructions, either ahead-of-time (AOT) or dynamically (DBT), to enable direct native execution on the host hardware.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Unlike interpretation, binary translation is like a **professional document translator** who translates the entire book (block of guest code) once, publishes a translated copy, and lets the reader consume it directly. The book doesn't need to be re-translated every time it's read — the cache acts like the published translated book.

---

## 1.2 Virtualization Layers

A **Virtualization Layer** (also called the *abstraction layer*) sits between the physical hardware and the guest workloads (OS + applications). It abstracts the underlying resources — CPU, memory, storage, network — and presents them as logical/virtualized pools to the guest.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU-2024 mandated hierarchy of Virtualization Layers (top to bottom):**
> 1. **Application Layer** — End-user apps running inside VMs/containers.
> 2. **Guest OS / Workload Layer** — OS instances that believe they own the hardware.
> 3. **Virtualization Layer (VMM/Hypervisor)** — The abstraction engine.
> 4. **Host Operating System / Bare-Metal Firmware** (depending on Type-1 or Type-2 hypervisor).
> 5. **Physical Hardware Layer** — CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC.

---

## 1.3 Hypervisors / VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor)

A **Hypervisor** or **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** is the software, firmware, or hardware component that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines by enforcing isolation, allocating physical resources, and intercepting privileged operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *A Hypervisor is a control program that establishes a controlled, isolated execution environment for guest operating systems, arbitrating their access to the underlying physical hardware through virtualization primitives.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** A hypervisor is the **manager of a multi-tenant office building** (physical hardware). The manager allocates floors (CPU cycles), rooms (RAM), and desks (storage) to different companies (VMs), ensures they cannot access each other's files (isolation), and handles complaints (system calls/traps) by passing them to building services (hardware).

---

## 1.4 Types of Hypervisors

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates two primary classifications:

### Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal / Native)
Runs **directly on the host's hardware** without an underlying host OS. Examples: **VMware ESXi**, **Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core mode)**, **Xen**, **Citrix XenServer**.

### Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted)
Runs as an **application on top of a conventional host operating system**. Examples: **VMware Workstation**, **Oracle VirtualBox**, **Parallels Desktop**, **QEMU**.

---

## 1.5 Full Virtualization

**Full Virtualization** is a virtualization technique in which the guest OS is **completely unaware** that it is being virtualized. The guest executes its original, unmodified binary directly. The VMM traps and emulates all privileged/sensitive operations transparently using either **BT + Direct Execution** (preferred) or pure **Interpretation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Full Virtualization is a technique in which the entire physical hardware is emulated/simulated for the guest OS, such that no guest modification is required and all sensitive instructions are transparently handled by the VMM.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Full Virtualization is like a **perfect theme-park replica of a city** (a movie set). The actors (guest OS) genuinely believe they are in the real city and perform normally — they never realize the buildings behind them are just facades (emulated hardware).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: Interpretation

When a guest instruction is fetched, the interpreter loop performs the following:

1. **Fetch** the next guest instruction from the guest's instruction pointer (IP).
2. **Decode** the opcode and operands.
3. **Emulate** the instruction by executing an equivalent sequence of host-ISA code from a software handler table.
4. **Update** guest registers and flags in the in-memory VCPU state structure.
5. **Increment** the guest IP and return to step 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind slowness:** Each guest instruction causes 2 or more host-level operations (decode + emulate). For an N-instruction guest program, the host executes approximately `N × k` operations, where `k ≥ 2`. This is the **interpretation overhead factor**.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown: Binary Translation

Binary Translation operates on **basic blocks** (a straight-line sequence of guest code ending in a branch/jump). The process:

1. **Identify** a basic block starting at the current guest IP.
2. **Translate** the entire block from guest ISA to host ISA, producing a *Translated Code Block (TCB)*.
3. **Store** the TCB in a **Translation Cache** indexed by the source guest address.
4. **Execute** the TCB natively on the host CPU.
5. On a cache miss (a new block), repeat from step 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Binary Translation is faster:** Once a block is translated and cached, it executes at near-native speed. The amortized cost per instruction drops from `k` (interpretation) to nearly `1`.

## 2.3 Sensitive vs. Privileged Instructions (Popek & Goldberg Theorem Foundation)

Full Virtualization relies on the **Popek & Goldberg virtualization requirements (1974)**, which state that a VMM can be built for a given ISA only if the set of *sensitive instructions* is a *subset* of the *privileged instructions*. For x86, this is **not naturally true** (17 sensitive-but-non-privileged instructions exist), which is why techniques like BT and interpretation are used.

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Privileged Instruction** | An instruction that traps (raises an exception) if executed in user mode. |
| **Sensitive Instruction** | An instruction whose behavior in user mode depends on the current configuration / resource mapping. |
| **Control-Sensitive** | A sensitive instruction that changes the privilege level or mode. |
| **Behavior-Sensitive** | A sensitive instruction whose result depends on the resource configuration. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is fundamentally a systems/architecture topic, formulas are limited to performance & overhead metrics. The KTU-2024 board examiners expect these expressions.

| Metric / Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Interpretation Overhead** | $$T_{\text{host}} = k \cdot T_{\text{guest}}$$ | `k` ≥ 2 ; `T` in CPU cycles |
| **BT Overhead (with cache)** | $$T_{\text{BT}} = (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + h \cdot T_{\text{native}}$$ | `h` = cache hit ratio (0 ≤ h ≤ 1) |
| **Effective MIPS (Guest)** | $$\text{MIPS}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\text{MIPS}_{\text{host}}}{k}$$ | Reduction due to interpretation |
| **Total Memory Overhead** | $$M_{\text{overhead}} = n \cdot (M_{\text{VCPU}} + M_{\text{cache}})$$ | `n` = number of VMs |
| **Translation Cache Hit Ratio** | $$h = \frac{\text{Total Execs} - \text{Misses}}{\text{Total Execs}}$$ | Dimensionless |
| **Popek-Goldberg Condition** | $$\text{Sensitive} \subseteq \text{Privileged}$$ | Required for efficient VMM |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe symbol inside a table has been intentionally replaced with `subseteq` / parentheses to comply with the KTU engine's table-parsing safety rules. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if you must render absolute value in LaTeX.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Interpretation** is the fallback for **legacy / non-virtualizable ISAs** (e.g., older x86 without VT-x).
- **Binary Translation** powers **Apple Rosetta 2**, **Transmeta Crusoe**, and **VMware's Monolithic Hypervisor** (early versions).
- **Full Virtualization** is the **foundation of public cloud IaaS** — AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, and Azure VMs all use full virtualization at the compute layer (with hardware assists like Intel VT-x / AMD-V).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Performance Ratio of Binary Translation vs. Interpretation

We derive the relationship showing when Binary Translation outperforms Interpretation.

**Let:**
- $N$ = total number of guest instructions in a program
- $k$ = interpretation cost factor (host cycles per guest instruction)
- $T_{\text{trans}}$ = average host cycles to translate one guest instruction
- $h$ = translation cache hit ratio
- $T_{\text{native}}$ = average host cycles to execute one native instruction ≈ 1

**Step 1: Total host cycles using pure interpretation**

$$
T_{\text{interp}} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{native}}
$$

**Step 2: Total host cycles using Binary Translation**

A miss on a block of $b$ instructions costs $b \cdot T_{\text{trans}}$ for translation plus $b$ cycles for execution. A hit costs only $b$ cycles (native). If there are $B$ total blocks, with hit ratio $h$:

$$
T_{\text{BT}} = B \cdot \left[ (1 - h) \cdot (b \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + b) + h \cdot b \right]
$$

$$
T_{\text{BT}} = B \cdot b \cdot \left[ (1 - h) \cdot (T_{\text{trans}} + 1) + h \right]
$$

**Step 3: Substituting $N = B \cdot b$**

$$
T_{\text{BT}} = N \cdot \left[ (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + (1 - h) + h \right]
$$

$$
T_{\text{BT}} = N \cdot \left[ (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + 1 \right]
$$

**Step 4: Speedup of BT over Interpretation**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{interp}}}{T_{\text{BT}}} = \frac{N \cdot k}{N \cdot \left[ (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + 1 \right]}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{k}{(1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{trans}} + 1}
$$

**Step 5: Realistic substitution**

For a typical VMM: $k = 4$, $T_{\text{trans}} = 3$ cycles/instruction, and after warmup $h = 0.95$.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{4}{(1 - 0.95) \cdot 3 + 1} = \frac{4}{0.15 + 1} = \frac{4}{1.15} \approx 3.48 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $h \to 1$ (steady state), the speedup approaches $k$, the full benefit of translation. This is why VMMs aggressively pre-warm translation caches during VM boot.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Interpretation | Binary Translation | Hardware-Assisted (VT-x/AMD-V) | Para-Virtualization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS modification** | None | None | None | **Required** |
| **Performance** | Low | Medium-High | **Near-Native** | Near-Native |
| **Complexity in VMM** | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Translation Cache** | Not used | Required | Not used | Not used |
| **Handles Sensitive Instructions** | By emulating | By rewriting | By trapping in ring -1 | By hypercalls |
| **Example VMM** | QEMU (TCG mode) | Transmeta Crusoe, QEMU TCG | KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi | Xen (classic) |
| **Deployment Cost** | Very Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Isolation Strength** | Medium | Strong | **Very Strong** | Strong |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: How a Privileged x86 Instruction is Handled in Full Virtualization (Software BT Approach)

Consider the guest executes `OUT 0x3F8, AL` (a sensitive I/O instruction).

**Step 1: Guest execution attempt**

The guest CPU (virtual) is at privilege level CPL=3. The `OUT` instruction is sensitive-but-not-privileged on legacy x86, so it does **not** trap automatically.

**Step 2: VMM scan and rewrite**

The binary translator scans the guest basic block. It detects `OUT` and rewrites the translated host block to:

```text
mov [vcpu_io_buffer], AL       ; save guest state
call vmm_trap_io_port          ; invoke VMM handler
```

**Step 3: VMM handler execution**

The VMM checks the I/O bitmap, validates the port (0x3F8 is allowed), emulates the side effect on the virtual device, and returns.

**Step 4: Resume guest execution**

The VMM restores the guest's saved registers from the in-memory VCPU structure and returns control to the next guest instruction in the translated cache.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
