# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Overhead Comparison (Container vs VM)

**Problem Statement:** A cloud host has 16 GB of physical RAM. It must host **N = 10** web-server instances. Each guest OS consumes **M_guest = 1.5 GB**, each application consumes **M_app = 512 MB**, the hypervisor footprint is **M_hyp = 256 MB**, the host kernel uses **M_kern = 200 MB**, and the container runtime uses **M_run = 50 MB** per instance. Compare the *total memory overhead* under hypervisor vs. OS-level virtualization.

### Step 1: Hypervisor-Based Total Memory

$$O_{mem}^{VM} = M_{hyp} + N \times (M_{guest} + M_{app})$$

Substituting the values:

$$O_{mem}^{VM} = 256 + 10 \times (1.5 \times 1024 + 512)$$

Convert GB to MB for consistency ($1\ \text{GB} = 1024\ \text{MB}$):

$$M_{guest} = 1.5 \times 1024 = 1536\ \text{MB}$$

So:

$$O_{mem}^{VM} = 256 + 10 \times (1536 + 512)$$

$$O_{mem}^{VM} = 256 + 10 \times 2048$$

$$O_{mem}^{VM} = 256 + 20\,480$$

$$\boxed{O_{mem}^{VM} = 20\,736\ \text{MB} \approx 20.25\ \text{GB}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 16 GB host **cannot** run 10 hypervisor-based VMs. Memory is **insufficient by ~4.25 GB** and would trigger swapping or VM boot failures.

### Step 2: OS-Level (Container) Total Memory

$$O_{mem}^{OS} = M_{kern} + N \times (M_{run} + M_{app})$$

Substituting:

$$O_{mem}^{OS} = 200 + 10 \times (50 + 512)$$

$$O_{mem}^{OS} = 200 + 10 \times 562$$

$$O_{mem}^{OS} = 200 + 5620$$

$$\boxed{O_{mem}^{OS} = 5820\ \text{MB} \approx 5.68\ \text{GB}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The same 16 GB host easily runs 10 containers, with **~10.3 GB free** for buffers, cache, and burst workloads.

### Step 3: Memory Savings Ratio

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{O_{mem}^{VM} - O_{mem}^{OS}}{O_{mem}^{VM}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{20\,736 - 5820}{20\,736} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{14\,916}{20\,736} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = 0.7194 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Savings} \approx 71.94\%}$$

**Conclusion:** OS-level virtualization saves **~72%** of memory by eliminating 10 redundant guest kernels. This is the **primary engineering justification** for the Docker/Kubernetes revolution in modern cloud computing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Overlay Network Throughput with VXLAN Encapsulation

**Problem Statement:** A 10 Gbps (10,000 Mbps) physical link carries VXLAN-encapsulated traffic. Each original packet has payload **P = 1450 bytes**. The VXLAN overhead adds 50 bytes (outer IPv4 = 20, UDP = 8, VXLAN = 8, Ethernet = 14). Compute the **effective application throughput** and the **encapsulation penalty** $P_{encap}$.

### Step 1: Total Frame Size After Encapsulation

$$F_{total} = P + O_{vxlan}$$

$$F_{total} = 1450 + 50 = 1500\ \text{bytes}$$

### Step 2: Throughput Penalty Ratio

The penalty is the ratio of overhead to total frame:

$$P_{encap} = \frac{O_{vxlan}}{F_{total}} = \frac{50}{1500}$$

$$P_{encap} = 0.0333 \quad (3.33\%)$$

### Step 3: Effective Application Throughput

$$T_{eff} = T_{phys} \times \left(1 - P_{encap}\right)$$

$$T_{eff} = 10\,000 \times (1 - 0.0333)$$

$$T_{eff} = 10\,000 \times 0.9667$$

$$\boxed{T_{eff} \approx 9666.7\ \text{Mbps} \approx 9.67\ \text{Gbps}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The VXLAN overlay costs only **~3.33%** of the physical bandwidth — a small price for **16 million** isolated logical networks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Technology | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Microservices deployment on a single host | **OS-Level (Docker)** | Lowest overhead, sub-second boot |
| Multi-tenant cloud requiring strict isolation | **Hypervisor (KVM/Xen)** | Hardware-level isolation, runs different OS families |
| Need to run Windows + Linux side by side | **Hypervisor (VMware/VirtualBox)** | Containers need the same kernel family |
| On-prem to AWS connectivity | **VXLAN Overlay** | Scales beyond 4094 VLANs |
| 5G network slicing | **SDN + NFV** | Programmable, dynamic slice provisioning |
| Branch office secure tunnel | **VPN (IPsec/SSL)** | Encryption + L3 tunneling |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 OS-Level Virtualization — Container Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hwLayer[Physical Hardware CPU RAM NIC SSD] --> hostKern[Host OS Kernel Linux Single Instance]
    hostKern --> cgroupCtrl[cgroup Controller Resource Limits]
    hostKern --> nsCtrl[Namespace Manager Isolation Views]
    hostKern --> engine[Container Engine Docker containerd]
    cgroupCtrl --> ctr1
    cgroupCtrl --> ctr2
    cgroupCtrl --> ctr3
    nsCtrl --> ctr1
    nsCtrl --> ctr2
    nsCtrl --> ctr3
    engine --> ctr1[Container 1 App A Bins Libs]
    engine --> ctr2[Container 2 App C Bins Libs]
    engine --> ctr3[Container 3 App D Bins Libs]
    fsLayer[Union File System OverlayFS Layered Images] --> ctr1
    fsLayer --> ctr2
    fsLayer --> ctr3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A *single* host kernel simultaneously serves **three** containers. The `cgroup Controller` enforces CPU/RAM/I/O quotas, the `Namespace Manager` provides per-container process/network/filesystem views, and the `Union File System` shares the read-only base image across all containers while giving each a thin writable top layer.

---

## 4.2 Hypervisor vs. OS-Level Virtualization — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HYP[Hypervisor Based]
        hw1[Hardware] --> hyp[Hypervisor ESXi Xen]
        hyp --> gk1[Guest OS 1]
        hyp --> gk2[Guest OS 2]
        hyp --> gk3[Guest OS 3]
        gk1 --> a1[App 1]
        gk2 --> a2[App 2]
        gk3 --> a3[App 3]
    end
    subgraph OSL[OS Level Based]
        hw2[Hardware] --> hk[Host Kernel Single]
        hk --> r1[Container Runtime]
        r1 --> c1[Container 1 App 1]
        r1 --> c2[Container 2 App 2]
        r1 --> c3[Container 3 App 3]
    end
```

> **Comparison takeaway:** Notice the **eliminated layer** of `Guest OS` in the OS-level stack. This is the source of the **~72% memory savings** derived in §3.1.

## 4.3 Network Virtualization — SDN + Overlay Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APPS[Application Plane]
        app1[Load Balancer App]
        app2[Firewall App]
        app3[Monitoring App]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Plane SDN Controller]
        topo[Topology Discovery Module]
        path[Path Computation Module]
        flow[Flow Policy Engine]
    end
    subgraph DATA[Data Plane Physical Underlay]
        leaf1[Leaf Switch 1]
        leaf2[Leaf Switch 2]
        spine1[Spine Switch 1]
    end
    subgraph OVR[Overlay Logical Networks]
        vx1[VXLAN 10001 Tenant A]
        vx2[VXLAN 10002 Tenant B]
        vx3[VXLAN 10003 Tenant C]
    end
    app1 --> CTRL
    app2 --> CTRL
    app3 --> CTRL
    CTRL -->|OpenFlow southbound| leaf1
    CTRL -->|OpenFlow southbound| leaf2
    leaf1 --- spine1
    spine1 --- leaf2
    leaf1 --> vx1
    leaf1 --> vx2
    leaf2 --> vx2
    leaf2 --> vx3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The SDN **Controller** (control plane) tells the **leaf and spine switches** (data plane) how to forward packets. The **VXLAN overlays** (10001, 10002, 10003) create three isolated logical networks on the same physical fabric, one per tenant.

---

## 4.4 Network Function Virtualization (NFV) — VNF Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkt[Incoming Packet] --> vFW[Virtual Firewall VNF]
    vFW --> vNAT[Virtual NAT VNF]
    vNAT --> vLB[Virtual Load Balancer VNF]
    vLB --> vMon[Virtual Monitoring VNF]
    vMon --> out[Egress to Service]
    subgraph HW[Commodity x86 Server]
        vFW
        vNAT
        vLB
        vMon
    end
```

> **NFV insight:** Four functions that *used* to be four separate hardware boxes (a hardware firewall, a hardware NAT device, a hardware load balancer, and a hardware probe) are now **four software VNFs** chained on a single x86 server. ETSI's **NFV MANO** (Management and Orchestration) framework automates their lifecycle.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define OS-level virtualization. List any four key features that differentiate it from hypervisor-based virtualization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**OS-level virtualization** is a server virtualization technique in which the **kernel of the host operating system** is shared by multiple isolated user-space instances called **containers**, without the need for a separate guest OS or hypervisor.

*Key differentiating features (any 4):*
1. **Single shared kernel** — no guest OS per instance (1/2 mark)
2. **Lightweight** — boot time in milliseconds; memory overhead in MBs (1/2 mark)
3. **High density** — supports 100+ containers per host (1/2 mark)
4. **Native performance** — ~95–99% of bare-metal throughput (1/2 mark)
5. Uses **namespaces + cgroups** for isolation instead of hardware-assisted VT-x/AMD-V (1/2 mark)
6. Examples: **Docker, LXC, Solaris Zones, FreeBSD Jails** (1/2 mark)

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 features @ 0.5 each)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a VLAN? State its purpose and the maximum number of VLANs supported by IEEE 802.1Q.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Virtual LAN (VLAN)** is a logical grouping of switch ports (or MAC addresses) that behave as if they are on the *same physical LAN segment*, even though they may be physically separated.

- **Purpose:** Broadcast domain segmentation, security isolation, traffic management, and reduced broadcast storm impact. (1.5 marks)
- **IEEE 802.1Q** supports a 12-bit VLAN ID (VID), giving a maximum of $2^{12} - 2 = \mathbf{4094}$ usable VLANs (IDs 0 and 4095 are reserved). (1.5 marks)

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — *[Module 2, Choice 1]*

**[KTU University Exam — Model QP 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **OS-level virtualization** with a neat diagram. Compare it with hypervisor-based virtualization across **any five** technical parameters. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **network namespaces** are used to provide isolated network stacks to containers. Describe the role of a **virtual bridge (veth pair)** in enabling inter-container communication. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**1. Architecture Explanation (4 marks):**
The OS-level virtualization stack consists of:
- **Layer 1 — Hardware:** Physical CPU, RAM, storage, NIC (no special virtualization extensions required).
- **Layer 2 — Host OS Kernel:** A *single* Linux/UNIX kernel that services all containers via system calls.
- **Layer 3 — Container Engine:** Software such as Docker that translates user commands into kernel API calls (`clone()`, `unshare()`).
- **Layer 4 — Isolation Primitives:**
   - **Namespaces** → Provide *visibility* isolation (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER).
   - **cgroups** → Provide *resource* isolation (CPU shares, memory limits, blkio weights, net_cls).
- **Layer 5 — Union Filesystem:** Layered image stack (OverlayFS/AUFS) where base layers are shared read-only and each container gets a thin writable top layer.
- **Layer 6 — Container Image & Application:** The user payload (code + libs + config).

*(Award 0.5 mark per layer, capped at 4 marks; diagram carries the remaining credit.)*

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks — 0.6 each for 5 parameters):**

| Parameter | OS-Level Virtualization | Hypervisor-Based Virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| Guest OS | **Not required** | Required (one per VM) |
| Kernel instances | **1 (shared)** | N (one per VM) |
| Boot time | **< 1 second** | 10–60 seconds |
| Memory overhead | **MB** (lightweight) | **GB** (heavy) |
| Isolation strength | Weaker (process-level) | Stronger (hardware-level) |
| Performance vs. native | ~95–99% | ~80–95% |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**1. Network Namespaces (3.5 marks):**
A **network namespace** is a logical copy of the entire network stack — interfaces, routing tables, firewall rules, port numbers — that a process can see. When the container engine creates a container, it invokes `unshare(CLONE_NEWNET)` to give the new container a *private* network namespace.

*Example:* Suppose Container A runs an Nginx web server on port 80. Container B *also* runs Nginx on port 80. In a normal (non-namespaced) system, the second Nginx would fail to bind. With network namespaces, **each container sees its own "port 80"** because they have *different* copies of the socket table. There is no conflict.

**2. Virtual Bridge & veth Pair (3.5 marks):**
To allow *communication* between containers (or between a container and the host), the engine creates a **virtual bridge** (e.g., `docker0`, a Linux bridge) and a pair of **virtual Ethernet (veth)** interfaces for each container. One end of the veth pair lives inside the container's namespace (e.g., `eth0`), and the other end is attached to the bridge on the host. Packets sent by the container traverse the veth pair → reach the bridge → are forwarded to the host NIC (for external traffic via NAT) or to another container's veth (for intra-host traffic).

**Diagram (textual):**
```
[Container 1] eth0 ---veth1--- [Bridge docker0] ---veth2--- eth0 [Container 2]
                                  |
                                  | (host side)
                                  v
                              [Host NIC eth0] --> Internet (via MASQUERADE iptables)
```
*(Award 1 mark for the diagram + 1.25 marks for the veth explanation + 1.25 marks for the namespace explanation.)*

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *[Module 2, Choice 2]*

**[KTU University Exam — Model QP 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)**. With a block diagram, describe its three-plane architecture and the role of the **OpenFlow** protocol. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)**. Explain the **ETSI NFV reference architecture** and state **four** advantages of NFV over traditional hardware-based network functions. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**1. SDN Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** is a network architecture that **decouples the control plane** (which decides *where* traffic should go) **from the data plane** (which *forwards* the traffic). The control logic is centralized in a software-based **SDN controller**, which programs the forwarding behavior of underlying switches/routers using standardized protocols such as **OpenFlow**.

**2. Three-Plane Architecture (4 marks — see diagram in §4.3):**
- **Application Plane:** Business applications (load balancers, firewalls, orchestrators) that express network requirements via northbound APIs (e.g., REST).
- **Control Plane:** The **SDN Controller** (e.g., OpenDaylight, ONOS, Floodlight, Ryu) maintains a global view of the network topology, computes paths, and installs flow rules.
- **Data Plane:** Commodity switches (hardware or software, e.g., **Open vSwitch**) that simply forward packets based on **flow tables** installed by the controller.

**3. Role of OpenFlow (1.5 marks):**
**OpenFlow** is the *southbound* protocol between the controller and the switches. When a packet arrives at a switch and does not match any flow entry, the switch sends a **Packet-In** message to the controller. The controller computes a path and replies with a **Flow-Mod** message that installs a new rule in the switch's flow table. Subsequent packets of the same flow are forwarded at line rate without further controller involvement — a hybrid *reactive + proactive* model.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**1. NFV Definition (1 mark):**
**Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** is the principle of replacing dedicated, proprietary hardware middleboxes (firewalls, load balancers, NAT, DPI) with **virtualized software functions (VNFs)** running on commodity x86 servers, orchestrated by a standardized **MANO (Management and Orchestration)** framework defined by **ETSI** (European Telecommunications Standards Institute).

**2. ETSI NFV Reference Architecture (3 marks):**
The architecture has three main components:
- **VNF (Virtual Network Function):** The software implementation of a network function (e.g., `vRouter`, `vFW`).
- **NFVI (NFV Infrastructure):** The physical and virtual resources — compute (x86 servers), storage, and networking — on which VNFs run. Hypervisors and the host OS form the virtualization layer.
- **MANO (Management & Orchestration):** Three sub-blocks:
   - **VIM** (Virtualized Infrastructure Manager) — e.g., OpenStack — manages NFVI resources.
   - **VNFM** (VNF Manager) — handles VNF lifecycle (instantiation, scaling, healing, termination).
   - **NFVO** (NFV Orchestrator) — orchestrates end-to-end network services across multiple VNFs.

**3. Four Advantages of NFV (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **CapEx Reduction** — Commodity hardware replaces expensive proprietary boxes.
2. **OpEx Reduction** — Centralized, automated management reduces manual configuration.
3. **Elastic Scalability** — VNFs can be scaled out/in in minutes via software; hardware boxes need physical procurement.
4. **Faster Time-to-Market** — New network services are deployed as software templates, not hardware installations.
*(Optional 5th: Multi-tenancy and energy efficiency.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in OS-Level & Network Virtualization Answers:**
> 1. **Don't confuse *virtualization* with *emulation*.** Virtualization uses the *same architecture* (x86-on-x86); emulation translates between architectures (e.g., QEMU emulating ARM on x86).
> 2. **Don't say "containers are as secure as VMs."** Isolation is *weaker* in containers because they share the kernel. A kernel exploit compromises *all* containers.
> 3. **VLAN ID count** — students often write **4096** instead of **4094** (forgetting that VID 0 and 4095 are reserved). This is a guaranteed **0.5 mark** loss.
> 4. **VXLAN VNI is 24-bit, not 16-bit.** Writing "$2^{16}$ segments" is a common mistake; the correct figure is $2^{24} = 16\,777\,216$.
> 5. **SDN ≠ NFV.** SDN is about *control plane separation*; NFV is about *virtualizing network functions*. They are *complementary*, not synonyms.
> 6. **OpenFlow is the *southbound* API, not northbound.** The controller exposes *northbound* REST APIs to applications.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### OS-Level Virtualization — Key Takeaways
- ✅ A *single* host kernel serves all containers — no guest OS.
- ✅ Isolation = **Namespaces** (visibility) + **cgroups** (resource limits).
- ✅ Containers boot in **< 1 second**; VMs take **10–60 seconds**.
- ✅ Examples: **Docker, LXC, Podman, containerd, runC**.
- ✅ Image layers are *shared* read-only across containers (OverlayFS/AUFS).
- ✅ ~**72% memory savings** over hypervisor-based for typical web-server workloads.
- ✅ Cannot run mixed-OS workloads (all containers share the host kernel's family).

### Network Virtualization — Key Takeaways
- ✅ **VLAN (802.1Q):** 12-bit VID → **4094** logical LANs.
- ✅ **VXLAN (RFC 7348):** 24-bit VNI → **16.77 million** logical networks; UDP port **4789**; ~50 B overhead.
- ✅ **SDN:** Decouples control plane from data plane. Controller = brain; switches = muscle. Protocol = **OpenFlow**.
- ✅ **NFV:** Replaces hardware middleboxes with VNFs on commodity servers. **ETSI MANO** = orchestration framework.
- ✅ Overlay networks = logical topologies on a physical underlay (tunneling = VXLAN/GRE/GENEVE).
- ✅ **SDN ≠ NFV** — complementary paradigms.

### KTU High-Yield "One-Liners" to Memorize
- *"OS-level virtualization uses **namespaces + cgroups**; hypervisor-based uses **VT-x/AMD-V**."*
- *"VLAN = **4094** segments; VXLAN = **16 M** segments."*
- *"SDN separates **control plane** from **data plane**; OpenFlow is the **southbound** protocol."*
- *"NFV virtualizes **network functions**; ETSI defines the **MANO** framework."*
- *"Containers share the **kernel**; VMs each have their **own kernel**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Virtualization and Desktop Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage Virtualization and Desktop Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — STORAGE VIRTUALIZATION & DESKTOP VIRTUALIZATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Storage Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Virtualization** is the process of abstracting the physical storage resources (HDDs, SSDs, SAN, NAS arrays) from their logical representation, such that they appear as a single, unified, manageable pool of storage to the host operating systems, applications, and users — independent of the underlying hardware manufacturer, physical location, or interface protocol.

The **Storage Networking Industry Association (SNIA)** defines it as:
> *"The application of virtualization concepts to the creation and management of logical storage units, decoupled from physical storage hardware."*

**Key Boundaries (KTU High-Yield Highlights):**
- Abstracts **LUNs (Logical Unit Numbers)**, **RAID groups**, and **JBODs** into a single virtual pool.
- Enables **heterogeneous** storage management (mixing vendors like NetApp, EMC, IBM in one fabric).
- Standard metric: **IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second)** — measured in operations/sec.
- Standard metric: **Latency** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**, typically < **10 ms** for enterprise SAN.

---

### 1.2 Storage Virtualization — Intuitive Analogy 🍕

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Pizza Buffet Model:**
> Imagine you walk into a large pizza restaurant. You do not care which oven baked your slice, which chef rolled the dough, or whether the oven runs on gas or electricity. You simply approach the **buffet counter** and pick a slice.
> 
> - The **physical ovens (HDDs/SSDs)** are hidden in the kitchen.
> - The **buffet counter** = the **Virtual Storage Pool**.
> - The **waiter (hypervisor/storage controller)** abstracts which oven supplied which slice.
> - You (the host/server) only see slices — never the ovens.

This is exactly what storage virtualization does: the **host sees a logical volume**, not the physical disks underneath.

---

### 1.3 Desktop Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Desktop Virtualization** is the technology that separates the **desktop environment** (operating system, applications, user data) from the **physical client device** (PC, laptop, thin client) by running the desktop inside a virtual machine hosted on a centralized data center server. The user accesses this remote desktop via a network using a display protocol.

The host where the desktop VM runs is called the **Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)** server.

**Core Components:**
- **Hypervisor** (Type-1 / Type-2) — runs the VMs.
- **Connection Broker** — authenticates and routes users to their desktops (e.g., VMware Horizon Connection Server, Citrix Broker).
- **Display Protocol** — RDP, PCoIP, HDX, Blast Extreme.
- **Client Endpoint** — thin client, zero client, or HTML5 browser.

---

### 1.4 Desktop Virtualization — Intuitive Analogy 🏨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Hotel Chain Model:**
> A frequent business traveler (user) books a room in any hotel of a chain (e.g., Taj, Marriott). The room is identical — same bed layout, same toiletries, same TV channel lineup — **regardless of which city or which physical hotel** they check into.
> 
> - The **physical hotel building** = the data center server.
> - The **standardized room** = the virtual desktop OS.
> - The **loyalty card / booking system** = the Connection Broker.
> - The **guest (user)** carries only a small keycard (thin client / browser).

This is the essence of desktop virtualization: **user experience is decoupled from physical hardware**.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Industry Terms (Bold Callouts)

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **IOPS** | I/O Operations Per Second | SSD: ~100,000 ; HDD: ~150 |
| **Latency** | Time to complete single I/O | SSD: < 1 ms ; HDD: 5–10 ms |
| **Throughput** | Data transferred per second | SSD: 500 MB/s+ ; HDD: 120 MB/s |
| **Capacity** | Total raw storage | TB / PB scale |
| **SLA Uptime** | Service availability guarantee | **99.9% (Three-Nines)** |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | HDDs: ~1.2 M hours |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Storage Virtualization — Architectural Layers

Storage virtualization operates at **three architectural levels**, each critical for KTU theory questions.

### 📌 Level 1: Host-Based Virtualization
- Virtualization layer sits **inside the host OS** (e.g., Windows Storage Spaces, Linux LVM).
- No specialized hardware needed.
- **Pros:** Cheap, simple.
- **Cons:** Consumes host CPU/RAM; no shared access across hosts.

### 📌 Level 2: Array-Based Virtualization
- Each storage array (e.g., EMC VMAX, NetApp FAS) manages its own internal virtualization.
- Vendor-locked; cannot pool across vendors.
- Used inside enterprise SAN cabinets.

### 📌 Level 3: Network-Based Virtualization
- A dedicated **virtualization appliance/engine** sits in the SAN fabric between hosts and storage arrays.
- Examples: **IBM SVC (SAN Volume Controller)**, **EMC VPLEX**, **NetApp V-Series**.
- **This is the highest KTU-priority level** because it enables heterogeneous pooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** Be ready to draw and explain the **3-tier SAN architecture with a virtualization engine (in-band vs out-of-band)** — this is a classic 7-mark question.

---

## 2.2 Storage Virtualization — Block-Level vs File-Level

| Type | Abstraction Layer | Example Technologies | Use Case |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|----------|
| **Block-Level** | LUNs / Virtual Disks | SAN, iSCSI, Fibre Channel | Databases (Oracle, SQL) |
| **File-Level** | Files & Folders | NAS (NFS, CIFS/SMB) | User shares, home directories |
| **Object-Level** | Objects with metadata | AWS S3, Azure Blob | Cloud archives, big data |

---

## 2.3 Desktop Virtualization — The Four Major Models

### 🖥️ Model 1: Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)
- Each user gets a **dedicated VM** running an OS.
- Persistent (stateful) or non-persistent.
- Heavy resource usage; high isolation.

### 🖥️ Model 2: Remote Desktop Services (RDS) — aka Terminal Services
- A single Windows Server hosts **multiple sessions**.
- All users share the same OS kernel; their sessions are isolated at the session layer.
- **Cheaper than VDI**, but lower customization.

### 🖥️ Model 3: OS Streaming / Application Virtualization
- OS or application is **streamed from a server** to a local device on demand.
- Examples: Citrix Provisioning Services (PVS), Microsoft App-V.

### 🖥️ Model 4: Desktop-as-a-Service (DaaS)
- Cloud-hosted variant of VDI (AWS WorkSpaces, Azure Virtual Desktop).
- Provider manages hypervisor, broker, and storage.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU High-Yield. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{usable}} = (n \times D) - (P \times D)$ | Usable capacity after RAID parity | GB / TB |
| 2 | $n_{\text{disks,RAID6}} = 2P + \text{mirrors}$ | Min disks for RAID-6 (double parity) | count |
| 3 | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IOPS}_{i}$ | Total IOPS = sum across disk spindles | ops/sec |
| 4 | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{\text{Reads} + \text{Writes} \times W_f}{\text{Latency}_{\text{cap}}}$ | IOPS required by workload | ops/sec |
| 5 | $W_f$ (Write Penalty) | RAID-0=1, RAID-1=2, RAID-5=4, RAID-6=6 | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{\text{throughput}} = \text{IOPS} \times \text{BS}$ | Throughput = IOPS × Block Size | MB/s |
| 7 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability ratio | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $\text{Cost/GB} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Usable Capacity}}$ | Cost efficiency | $/GB |
| 9 | $N_{\text{desktops}} = \dfrac{\text{CPU}_{\text{server}}}{C_{\text{per-VM}}}$ | VM density (CPU bound) | count |
| 10 | $\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{desktops}} \times \text{RAM}_{\text{per-VM}} + \text{Hypervisor}_{\text{overhead}}$ | Total RAM provisioning | GB |

### 💡 Engineering Utility
- **Formulas 1–4** are used daily by **Storage Architects** designing enterprise SANs.
- **Formulas 7–8** drive **cloud SLA contracts** and **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** models.
- **Formulas 9–10** are used by **VDI capacity planners** to size VMware Horizon farms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Usable Capacity in RAID-5

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A RAID-5 array has **6 disks**, each of size **2 TB**. Calculate the usable capacity. Given $D = 2\ \text{TB}$, $n = 6$, parity drives $P = 1$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{usable}} &= (n \times D) - (P \times D) \\
&= (6 \times 2\ \text{TB}) - (1 \times 2\ \text{TB}) \\
&= 12\ \text{TB} - 2\ \text{TB} \\
C_{\text{usable}} &= 10\ \text{TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** RAID-5 distributes parity across all drives but logically dedicates **one drive's worth** of space to parity information. Hence, with 6 physical disks of 2 TB each, we lose 2 TB to parity and get 10 TB of usable storage.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — IOPS Calculation with Write Penalty

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A database workload generates **800 reads/sec** and **200 writes/sec**. The array is configured as **RAID-10 (Write Penalty = 2)**. Calculate the total IOPS demand on the storage backend.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{backend}} &= \text{Reads} \times 1 + \text{Writes} \times W_f \\
&= (800 \times 1) + (200 \times 2) \\
&= 800 + 400 \\
\text{IOPS}_{\text{backend}} &= 1200\ \text{ops/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** A read operation requires only **one** backend I/O. A write in RAID-10 requires **two** backend I/Os (one to write data, one to write the mirrored copy). Hence the multiplication by $W_f = 2$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Throughput Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** If the above array sustains 1200 IOPS at a block size of **16 KB**, derive the throughput.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{throughput}} &= \text{IOPS} \times \text{BS} \\
&= 1200\ \text{ops/sec} \times 16\ \text{KB} \\
&= 19{,}200\ \text{KB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to MB/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{throughput}} &= \dfrac{19{,}200}{1024}\ \text{MB/s} \\
&\approx 18.75\ \text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — VDI Sizing (Desktop Virtualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company wants to deploy **200 virtual desktops**. Each VM requires **2 vCPU** and **4 GB RAM**. The server has **2× Intel Xeon Gold 6248 (20 cores each = 40 cores total)** and **256 GB RAM**. Hypervisor overhead = **10%**. Verify if the server can host the 200 desktops.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**CPU Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{desktops,CPU}} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Cores}}{C_{\text{per-VM}}} = \dfrac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ (naive, fails)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This naive ratio fails because hypervisors overcommit CPU at 4:1 to 6:1 ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{desktops,practical}} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Cores} \times \text{Overcommit ratio}}{C_{\text{per-VM}}} \\
&= \dfrac{40 \times 4}{2} = 80\ \text{desktops per server}
\end{aligned}
$$

So one server hosts **80 VMs**; we need **3 such servers** for 200 desktops.

**RAM Check (Strict — no overcommit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{desktops}} \times \text{RAM}_{\text{per-VM}} + \text{Overhead} \\
&= 80 \times 4\ \text{GB} + 25.6\ \text{GB} \\
&= 320 + 25.6\ \text{GB} \ (\text{exceeds 256 GB, fails})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** One server with 256 GB RAM cannot host 80 desktops. We need either:
- Servers with **384 GB RAM**, or
- **4 servers** at 256 GB with **~50 VMs each**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Storage Virtualization — Network-Based Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOSTS["Host Servers"]
        H1["App Server 1"]
        H2["App Server 2"]
        H3["App Server 3"]
    end

    subgraph FABRIC["SAN Fabric FC Switches"]
        SW1["FC Switch Zone A"]
        SW2["FC Switch Zone B"]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["Virtualization Engine In-Band Appliance"]
        VIRT["Metadata + Cache Controller"]
        LOGV["Logical Volume Manager"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["Heterogeneous Storage Arrays"]
        A1["NetApp FAS9000"]
        A2["EMC VMAX 250F"]
        A3["IBM FlashSystem"]
    end

    H1 --> SW1
    H2 --> SW1
    H3 --> SW2
    SW1 --> VIRT
    SW2 --> VIRT
    VIRT --> LOGV
    LOGV --> A1
    LOGV --> A2
    LOGV --> A3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Hosts send I/O via FC switches into the **virtualization engine**.
- The engine's **metadata controller** translates logical LUN IDs to physical disk locations.
- The host sees a single virtual LUN; the engine manages the heterogeneous backend arrays.

---

## 4.2 Desktop Virtualization — VDI Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENTS["End User Devices"]
        C1["Thin Client"]
        C2["Laptop with Browser"]
        C3["Zero Client"]
    end

    subgraph ACCESS["Access Layer"]
        BR1["Connection Broker Auth and Routing"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["Compute Layer Hypervisor Farm"]
        HV1["ESXi Host 1 VMs"]
        HV2["ESXi Host 2 VMs"]
        HV3["Hyper-V Host VMs"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE2["Storage Layer"]
        SANV["SAN Unified Storage Pool"]
        NASV["NAS User Profile Store"]
    end

    subgraph MGMT["Management Layer"]
        ACTV["Active Directory"]
        MONV["vRealize Operations Monitor"]
    end

    C1 --> BR1
    C2 --> BR1
    C3 --> BR1
    BR1 --> HV1
    BR1 --> HV2
    BR1 --> HV3
    HV1 --> SANV
    HV2 --> SANV
    HV3 --> NASV
    ACTV -.-> BR1
    MONV -.-> HV1
    MONV -.-> HV2
    MONV -.-> HV3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Clients** (any device) connect via the **Connection Broker** (authentication + desktop assignment).
- The broker hands them a session on one of the **hypervisor hosts**.
- All VMs read/write to a **centralized SAN/NAS**.
- **Active Directory** validates users; **Monitoring tools** track performance.

---

## 4.3 In-Band vs Out-of-Band Virtualization Engines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INBAND["In-Band Symmetric Engine"]
        H1A["Host"] --> EA["Virtualization Engine All Data Path"]
        EA --> AR1["Array 1"]
        EA --> AR2["Array 2"]
    end

    subgraph OUTBAND["Out-of-Band Asymmetric Engine"]
        H1B["Host"] --> SWB["FC Switch"]
        SWB --> ARX1["Array 1"]
        SWB --> ARX2["Array 2"]
        EB["Metadata Engine Control Path Only"]
        EB -.-> SWB
    end
```

**Key Difference (High KTU Value):**
- **In-band:** All I/O flows through the engine — risk of **single point of failure** and **latency bottleneck**.
- **Out-of-band:** Engine only handles metadata; data flows directly host ↔ array — **better performance**, more complex failover.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Storage Virtualization. List its three levels of implementation.**

**Model Answer:**

Storage Virtualization is the abstraction of physical storage from its logical representation, presenting multiple heterogeneous storage devices as a single unified pool to hosts.

**Three Levels:**
1. **Host-Based** — implemented inside the host OS (e.g., Windows LVM).
2. **Array-Based** — implemented within each storage array.
3. **Network-Based** — implemented using a dedicated appliance in the SAN fabric (e.g., IBM SVC).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between VDI and RDS in Desktop Virtualization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | VDI | RDS |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Architecture | One VM per user | Shared OS session |
| Isolation | Full VM-level | Session-level |
| Customization | High (own OS image) | Limited (same OS) |
| Resource Cost | High (RAM + Storage per VM) | Lower (shared kernel) |
| Use Case | Power users, developers | Task workers, call centers |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Explain the architecture of network-based storage virtualization with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between in-band and out-of-band virtualization. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Network-based storage virtualization places a dedicated **virtualization appliance** (e.g., IBM SVC, EMC VPLEX) in the SAN fabric between the hosts and the storage arrays. The appliance maintains a **metadata mapping table** that translates logical volume requests from hosts to physical LUN locations across heterogeneous arrays.

**Architecture Steps:**
1. Hosts send SCSI/FC I/O requests addressed to a **virtual LUN**.
2. The virtualization engine intercepts these requests.
3. The engine's **metadata service** maps the virtual LUN to the correct physical disk(s).
4. The I/O is forwarded to the target array.
5. Read responses are routed back to the host; writes are committed and acknowledged.

**In-Band vs Out-of-Band:**

| Aspect | In-Band | Out-of-Band |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Data Path | Flows through engine | Direct host-to-array |
| Metadata Path | Through engine | Engine out-of-loop |
| Latency | Higher | Lower |
| Failure Impact | Engine failure = data loss | Engine failure = read-only |
| Example | IBM SVC (in-band mode) | EMC VPLEX (Metro mode) |

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 4.1.

---

**b) A RAID-6 array is built with 8 disks of 4 TB each. Compute (i) usable capacity, (ii) number of parity drives, and (iii) write penalty if the workload is 1500 reads/sec and 300 writes/sec. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Usable Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{usable}} &= (n \times D) - (P \times D) \\
&= (8 \times 4\ \text{TB}) - (2 \times 4\ \text{TB}) \\
&= 32\ \text{TB} - 8\ \text{TB} \\
C_{\text{usable}} &= 24\ \text{TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of Parity Drives:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2 \text{ (fixed for RAID-6, double parity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) IOPS on Backend:**

RAID-6 Write Penalty $W_f = 6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{backend}} &= (1500 \times 1) + (300 \times 6) \\
&= 1500 + 1800 \\
&= 3300\ \text{IOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample Paper]**

**a) Explain the four major models of Desktop Virtualization with suitable use cases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Model 1 — VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure):**
Each user gets a dedicated VM hosted on a server. Persistent or non-persistent variants exist. **Use case:** Software developers needing custom OS configurations, healthcare (HIPAA) compliance with strict isolation.

**Model 2 — RDS (Remote Desktop Services):**
Multiple users share a single Windows Server through separate sessions. **Use case:** Call centers with task workers using standardized applications like CRM.

**Model 3 — OS Streaming:**
A base OS image is streamed to local devices at boot. **Use case:** Schools deploying standardized labs cheaply.

**Model 4 — DaaS (Desktop-as-a-Service):**
Cloud-hosted desktops managed by third-party providers (AWS WorkSpaces, Azure Virtual Desktop). **Use case:** SMBs without capital expenditure for on-premise infrastructure.

---

**b) A company must deploy 500 VDI desktops. Each VM needs 2 vCPU and 8 GB RAM. Servers have 2× Xeon 8280 (28 cores each = 56 cores), 512 GB RAM, and hypervisor overhead of 15%. Assuming a 4:1 CPU overcommit ratio, calculate how many servers are needed. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — CPU capacity per server:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{desktops,CPU}} &= \dfrac{56 \times 4}{2} = \dfrac{224}{2} = 112\ \text{desktops per server}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — RAM capacity per server (no overcommit on RAM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAM}_{\text{usable}} &= 512\ \text{GB} \times (1 - 0.15) = 435.2\ \text{GB} \\
N_{\text{desktops,RAM}} &= \dfrac{435.2}{8} = 54.4 \approx 54\ \text{desktops per server}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck identification:**

RAM is the limiting factor → **54 desktops per server**.

**Step 4 — Number of servers required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Servers} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{500}{54} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.26 \rceil = 10\ \text{servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** **10 servers** are required to host 500 VDI desktops under the given constraints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hypervisor overhead %** — RAM calculations without subtracting 10–15% overhead will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Confusing CPU overcommit ratios** — RAM is **never overcommitted** in production VDI; only CPU is.
> 3. **Wrong RAID write penalty** — Students often use $W_f = 2$ for RAID-5; the correct value is **4**. RAID-6 = **6**.
> 4. **Mixing up VDI vs RDS** — VDI = VM-per-user; RDS = session-per-user. Examiners **specifically test this distinction**.
> 5. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in server count — always round **up**, never down, because partial servers don't exist.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Storage Virtualization** = abstract physical storage into a logical pool; three levels: **host, array, network**.
- 🔑 **Network-based** is the **most powerful and most asked** level — uses appliances like IBM SVC.
- 🔑 **RAID write penalties**: RAID-0=1, RAID-1=2, RAID-5=4, **RAID-6=6**, RAID-10=2.
- 🔑 **Desktop Virtualization** = separating desktop OS from physical hardware; 4 models: **VDI, RDS, OS Streaming, DaaS**.
- 🔑 **VDI = VM per user** (heavy); **RDS = session per user** (lightweight).
- 🔑 **VDI sizing** is governed by **RAM** (not CPU); always subtract hypervisor overhead.
- 🔑 **Connection Broker** is the heart of any VDI deployment — handles auth, routing, and load balancing.
- 🔑 **IOPS = Reads × 1 + Writes × Write Penalty**; **Throughput = IOPS × Block Size**.
- 🔑 **In-band** engines have single point of failure; **Out-of-band** engines are resilient but complex.
- 🔑 Cloud evolution: **On-premise VDI → DaaS (AWS WorkSpaces, Azure Virtual Desktop)**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Storage Virtualization & Desktop Virtualization** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Resource Provisioning in Cloud. Open Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud: OpenStack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Resource Provisioning in Cloud. Open Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud: OpenStack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resource Provisioning in Cloud Computing

### Formal Definition
**Resource Provisioning** is the process of allocating, configuring, managing, and releasing computing resources (CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth) to cloud workloads either in advance (static) or on-demand (dynamic), based on Service Level Agreements (SLA), Quality of Service (QoS) parameters, and workload characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** Resource provisioning strategies are foundational to cloud orchestration. The distinction between **static** and **dynamic** provisioning directly maps to the elasticity property of cloud computing.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Electricity Grid
Imagine a city power grid:
- **Static provisioning** is like the municipality permanently installing 100 MW of capacity for a residential colony, regardless of whether the residents use 30 MW or 100 MW. The infrastructure is **fixed at design time**.
- **Dynamic provisioning** is like a smart grid that automatically brings additional generators online when the colony's consumption rises above a threshold, and shuts them down when demand falls. The infrastructure **scales elastically in real time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:** In cloud context, *workload elasticity* and *cost optimization* are achieved primarily through **dynamic provisioning**, while **static provisioning** offers predictability and lower management complexity at the cost of poor utilization.

---

## 1.2 Static Resource Provisioning

### Formal Definition
**Static Resource Provisioning** (also called *rigid* or *fixed* provisioning) is a deployment model where the **exact number and type of virtual resources (VMs, containers, storage volumes, network interfaces)** are pre-determined by the cloud administrator at deployment time and remain **constant throughout the application lifecycle**, unless manually reconfigured.

**Standard Metrics (KTU Board Definition):**
- **Peak Load Capacity ($L_{peak}$)** — Maximum workload the static cluster can handle, expressed in **requests/second**.
- **Over-provisioning Ratio ($\rho_{over}$)** — Ratio of allocated peak capacity to average load.
- **Resource Wastage ($W_r$)** — Idle resources as a percentage of total allocated resources.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Wedding Hall
A banquet hall books **exactly 500 chairs** for a wedding, even though only 300 guests may arrive. The chair count is **locked at booking time**. This is static provisioning. If 700 guests show up, the hall cannot seat them; if 100 show up, 400 chairs remain empty.

### Characteristics of Static Provisioning
- **Deterministic capacity** — known in advance.
- **Low runtime overhead** — no auto-scaling engines.
- **Poor elasticity** — cannot react to traffic spikes.
- **High cost of over-provisioning** — bills paid for idle resources.
- **High risk of under-provisioning** — SLA violations during traffic bursts.

---

## 1.3 Dynamic Resource Provisioning

### Formal Definition
**Dynamic Resource Provisioning** is a self-adaptive allocation model where cloud resources are **automatically scaled up (scale-out) or scaled down (scale-in)** in real time, in response to fluctuating workload conditions, using pre-defined policies, predictive algorithms, or reactive monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):** The triggering mechanism is the differentiator:
> - **Reactive provisioning** — based on monitored metrics (CPU > 80% → add VM).
> - **Proactive / Predictive provisioning** — based on historical load patterns, time-series forecasting (ARIMA, LSTM), or scheduled scaling windows.

### Intuitive Analogy — Uber Surge Pricing Model
Uber's app does not own a fixed number of cars. When demand spikes, the platform **dynamically attracts more drivers** (scales out) using incentives; when demand drops, drivers log off (scales in). The fleet size **fluctuates with the demand curve** — this is dynamic provisioning.

### Core Sub-Categories (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping)
1. **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up / Scale-Down)** — Increase CPU/RAM of an *existing* VM.
2. **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out / Scale-In)** — Add or remove *additional* VM instances.
3. **Auto-Scaling** — Automated horizontal scaling governed by threshold rules.
4. **Live Migration** — Moving a running VM between physical hosts without downtime.

---

## 1.4 Open Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud

### Formal Definition
**Open Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud** are community-developed, license-free software stacks that an enterprise can deploy within its own data center to deliver **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** services — providing the agility of public cloud while preserving data sovereignty, security, and compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A *Private Cloud* is a cloud infrastructure provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization, which may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization, a third party, or a combination thereof (per NIST SP 800-145).

### Major Open Source Private Cloud Platforms
| Platform | Primary Role | First Release | License |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OpenStack** | IaaS Orchestrator | 2010 (NASA + Rackspace) | Apache 2.0 |
| **Eucalyptus** | IaaS (AWS-compatible API) | 2008 | GPL |
| **CloudStack** | IaaS Orchestrator | 2010 | Apache 2.0 |
| **OpenNebula** | IaaS / Hybrid | 2005 | Apache 2.0 |
| **Kubernetes (with KubeVirt)** | Container + VM orchestration | 2014 | Apache 2.0 |

### Intuitive Analogy — Building Your Own Power Plant
A private cloud is like a corporation building its own solar power plant instead of buying electricity from the state grid. The corporation gets **energy on demand**, but retains **full control over the infrastructure**.

---

## 1.5 OpenStack — Introduction

### Formal Definition
**OpenStack** is an open-source, massively scalable, and highly available **IaaS cloud operating system** that pools compute, storage, and networking resources across a data center and allows on-demand provisioning of virtual resources through a RESTful API and a web dashboard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Tagline (Board Exam Favorite):** *"OpenStack is the Linux of the Cloud"* — its modular, kernel-like architecture orchestrates heterogeneous hardware into a unified programmable cloud fabric.

### Core Capabilities of OpenStack
- Provisioning of **virtual machines** and **bare-metal servers**.
- **Object storage** and **block storage** services.
- **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** with VLANs, routers, firewalls.
- **Identity, image, telemetry, and orchestration** services.
- Compatible with **AWS EC2/S3 APIs** (via compatible drivers).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Theoretical Analysis: Static vs Dynamic Provisioning

### 2.1.1 Static Provisioning — Operational Logic

The static provisioning workflow is **linear and non-reactive**:

1. **Capacity Planning Phase** — Administrator analyzes historical peak load data.
2. **Resource Reservation Phase** — Fixed quota of $N$ VMs is reserved on the cluster.
3. **Deployment Phase** — Application is deployed to the static pool.
4. **Steady-State Execution** — Pool size remains constant; no runtime reallocation.
5. **Manual Re-provisioning** — Administrator manually adjusts pool size based on long-term trends.

**The Why (Engineering Rationale):**
- Suitable for **predictable batch workloads** (scientific simulations, payroll processing).
- Eliminates **provisioning latency** ($t_{boot} \approx 30\text{–}120$ s) since resources are pre-allocated.
- Simpler to **audit and certify** for compliance (e.g., healthcare, defense).

**The How (Technical Mechanism):**
- Uses **VM pinning** or **affinity rules** to lock workloads to specific hosts.
- No autoscaler daemon (e.g., no `heat` or `senlin` services running).
- Capacity is reserved through OpenStack's `--max-count` and flavor quotas.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Provisioning — Operational Logic

The dynamic provisioning workflow is **closed-loop and feedback-driven**:

1. **Monitoring Phase** — Telemetry service (Ceilometer / Gnocchi / Aodh) samples metrics every $\Delta t$ seconds.
2. **Threshold Evaluation Phase** — Current metric $M(t)$ compared to scale-out threshold $T_{out}$ and scale-in threshold $T_{in}$.
3. **Decision Phase** — Policy engine (Heat Orchestration Template, Senlin, or Watcher) decides scaling action.
4. **Execution Phase** — Nova spawns/terminates VMs, Cinder attaches volumes, Neutron configures networking.
5. **Cooldown Phase** — System waits for cooldown interval $T_{cd}$ to prevent flapping.

**The Why (Engineering Rationale):**
- Optimizes **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** through right-sizing.
- Maintains **SLA compliance** during traffic spikes.
- Enables **just-in-time capacity** delivery aligned with demand.

**The How (Technical Mechanism):**
- Driven by **policy-based** (threshold), **scheduled** (cron), or **predictive** (ML) triggers.
- Implemented via **Heat AutoScalingGroup**, **Senlin clustering**, or **Watcher optimization**.
- Uses **placement schedulers** (FilterScheduler, CacheScheduler) for intelligent VM distribution.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU board-exam tested equations. Memorize the variables, units, and boundary conditions.**

### Table 2.1 — Provisioning Metrics and Equations

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{L(t)}{C} \, dt$ | $L(t)$ = instantaneous load, $C$ = capacity, $T$ = window | dimensionless (0–1) | Average utilization over period |
| 2 | $\rho_{over} = \frac{C_{peak}}{L_{avg}}$ | Over-provisioning ratio | dimensionless (≥ 1) | Measures idle capacity penalty |
| 3 | $W_r = 1 - U_{avg}$ | Resource wastage fraction | dimensionless (0–1) | Cost of idle resources |
| 4 | $N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak} \cdot S_{req}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil$ | $N_{vm}$ = VMs needed, $S_{req}$ = per-request CPU share, $C_{vm}$ = VM capacity | integer count | Static capacity sizing |
| 5 | $t_{boot} = t_{hyper} + t_{image} + t_{network}$ | $t_{boot}$ = VM boot latency | seconds | Reactive scaling delay |
| 6 | $N_{active}(t) = N_{base} + \sum_{i} \Delta N_i \cdot H(t - t_i)$ | $H(\cdot)$ = Heaviside step, $\Delta N_i$ = scale event delta | integer count | Dynamic provisioning trajectory |
| 7 | $T_{SLA} = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}$ | $T_{SLA}$ = SLA availability | fraction (0.99, 0.999) | Compliance metric |
| 8 | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{vm}} (c_{cpu} \cdot P_i + c_{mem} \cdot M_i + c_{sto} \cdot S_i)$ | $c_{cpu}, c_{mem}, c_{sto}$ = unit costs | currency | TCO calculation |
| 9 | $\lambda_{opt} = \arg\min_{\lambda} \left( C_{total}(\lambda) + \alpha \cdot P_{SLA}(\lambda) \right)$ | $\lambda$ = provisioning parameter, $\alpha$ = SLA penalty | cost | Optimization objective |
| 10 | $R_{scale} = \frac{dN_{vm}}{dt} \bigg\vert_{out}$ | Scale-out rate | VMs/sec | Burst capacity ramp-up |

### Boundary Conditions and Constraints (Board Exam Essentials)

- **Utilization Constraint:** $0 \leq U_{avg} \leq 1$. Saturation at $U_{avg} = 1$ triggers SLA violation.
- **Over-provisioning Constraint:** $\rho_{over} \geq 1$. Equality $\rho_{over} = 1$ implies perfect just-in-time provisioning.
- **Cooldown Constraint:** $T_{cd} \geq t_{boot}$ to prevent oscillation (flapping).
- **Hysteresis Constraint:** $T_{in} < T_{out}$ (scale-in threshold must be strictly less than scale-out threshold).
- **Integer Constraint:** $N_{vm} \in \mathbb{Z}^+$. Continuous relaxation requires rounding via $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ or $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$.

## 2.3 OpenStack — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 OpenStack Service Taxonomy

OpenStack is composed of **modular services** that interact via RESTful APIs and a message bus (RabbitMQ). Each service has a specific role:

### Table 2.2 — Core OpenStack Services (Mandatory Memorization)

| Service | Code Name | Function | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute | **Nova** | VM lifecycle management, scheduling | Factory assembly line |
| Networking | **Neutron** | SDN, L2/L3 services, routers, LBaaS | City road network |
| Object Storage | **Swift** | Petabyte-scale redundant storage | Distributed warehouse |
| Block Storage | **Cinder** | Persistent block volumes for VMs | Hard disk plugged into VM |
| Identity | **Keystone** | Authentication, authorization, tokens | Security gate / ID card |
| Image Service | **Glance** | VM image catalog & registration | Photo library / template shop |
| Dashboard | **Horizon** | Web-based GUI for all services | Control room monitor |
| Orchestration | **Heat** | Template-driven stack deployment | Blueprint architect |
| Telemetry | **Ceilometer / Gnocchi / Aodh** | Metrics, time-series DB, alarms | Sensor network & alert system |
| Clustering | **Senlin** | Auto-scaling and grouping | HR / group manager |
| Workflow | **Mistral** | Task orchestration, DAG execution | Project management office |
| Bare Metal | **Ironic** | PXE-based physical server provisioning | Hardware delivery service |
| DNS | **Designate** | RESTful DNS-as-a-Service | Phone directory |
| Key Manager | **Barbican** | Secret, key, certificate management | Vault / safe deposit |

### 2.3.2 Architecture Layers

OpenStack architecture is structured into **four logical layers**:

1. **Application Layer (Horizon, CLI, SDKs)** — User-facing interfaces.
2. **Orchestration Layer (Heat, Senlin, Mistral)** — Policy and workflow engines.
3. **Resource Layer (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Swift, Glance)** — Core IaaS services.
4. **Shared Services Layer (Keystone, Ceilometer, RabbitMQ, MariaDB)** — Cross-cutting concerns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When asked "Which service does X?", remember the **K-N-C-S-G-H** mnemonic: **K**eystone (auth), **N**ova (compute), **C**inder (block storage), **S**wift (object storage), **G**lance (images), **H**orizon (UI).

### 2.3.3 Logical Architecture Diagram (Conceptual)

```
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|  HORIZON (Dashboard)  |  CLI  |  REST API Clients             |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ORCHESTRATION: Heat  |  Senlin (Clustering)  |  Mistral       |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|  NOVA      | NEUTRON    | CINDER    | SWIFT     | GLANCE        |
|  (Compute) | (Network)  | (Block)   | (Object)  | (Image)       |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
                          |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|  KEYSTONE (Identity)  |  CEILOMETER/AODH (Telemetry)           |
|  RABBITMQ (Message Bus)  |  MARIADB (Database)                 |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### 2.3.4 Provisioning Workflow in OpenStack (Sequential Phases)

1. **Authentication:** Client sends credentials to **Keystone** → receives a **token**.
2. **Image Acquisition:** Client queries **Glance** for the desired VM image UUID.
3. **Network Preparation:** Client requests a **Neutron** network, subnet, and router.
4. **Volume Attachment (optional):** Client attaches a **Cinder** volume.
5. **Compute Launch:** Client invokes **Nova** `POST /servers` with flavor, image, network.
6. **Scheduling:** Nova scheduler filters and weighs candidate compute nodes.
7. **Hypervisor Invocation:** KVM/LXD/Xen hypervisor on selected host spawns the VM.
8. **Telemetry Initiation:** **Ceilometer** begins polling VM metrics.
9. **Status Reporting:** Nova returns VM IP, status (`ACTIVE`), and metadata to client.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Capacity Sizing Equation

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** A web application requires $S_{req} = 0.05$ CPU cores per concurrent user. Each VM has $C_{vm} = 8$ CPU cores. Peak load is $L_{peak} = 2{,}000$ concurrent users. Calculate the minimum number of VMs required under **static provisioning**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** (from Section 2.1 Formula 4):
$$N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak} \cdot S_{req}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numeric values:**
$$N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{2000 \cdot 0.05}{8} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**
$$2000 \cdot 0.05 = 100$$

**Step 4 — Divide by VM capacity:**
$$\frac{100}{8} = 12.5$$

**Step 5 — Apply ceiling operator** (since partial VMs are not deployable):
$$N_{vm} = \lceil 12.5 \rceil = 13$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{N_{vm}^{static} = 13 \text{ VMs}}$$

**Step 7 — Resource utilization at peak load:**
$$U_{peak} = \frac{100}{13 \cdot 8} = \frac{100}{104} \approx 0.9615$$

**Interpretation:** At peak load, the static cluster runs at **96.15%** utilization, leaving only 4 CPU cores as safety margin — risky for traffic bursts above 2,000 users.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Over-Provisioning Ratio for Static Cluster

**Problem Statement:** A static cluster has $C_{peak} = 130$ CPU cores, but the historical average load is $L_{avg} = 70$ cores. Compute the over-provisioning ratio.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** (Formula 2):
$$\rho_{over} = \frac{C_{peak}}{L_{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$\rho_{over} = \frac{130}{70}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**
$$\rho_{over} \approx 1.857$$

**Step 4 — Compute resource wastage** (Formula 3):
$$W_r = 1 - U_{avg} = 1 - \frac{70}{130} \approx 0.4615 = 46.15\%$$

**Step 5 — Annual cost penalty (assume $c_{cpu} = \$50/\text{core-month}$):**
$$C_{waste} = W_r \cdot C_{peak} \cdot 12 \cdot c_{cpu} = 0.4615 \cdot 130 \cdot 12 \cdot 50 = \$35{,}997$$

**Interpretation:** The organization wastes **~\$36,000/year** on idle cores. This is the economic motivation for **dynamic provisioning**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic Auto-Scaling Threshold Logic

**Problem Statement (Board Exam Classic):** A web application triggers **scale-out** when CPU utilization exceeds $T_{out} = 80\%$ and **scale-in** when CPU drops below $T_{in} = 30\%$. The cooldown period is $T_{cd} = 300$ seconds. Current state: 5 VMs running at 85% CPU. Show the auto-scaling decision.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Compare current metric to thresholds:**
$$M(t) = 85\%,\quad T_{out} = 80\%,\quad T_{in} = 30\%$$

**Step 2 — Decision evaluation:**
$$\text{Since } M(t) = 85\% > T_{out} = 80\% \implies \text{Trigger SCALE-OUT}$$

**Step 3 — Compute scale-out increment** (default policy: $\Delta N = \lceil N_{current} \cdot \beta \rceil$ with $\beta = 0.5$):
$$\Delta N = \lceil 5 \cdot 0.5 \rceil = \lceil 2.5 \rceil = 3 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 4 — New VM count after provisioning:**
$$N_{new} = N_{current} + \Delta N = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 5 — Boot latency consideration:**
$$t_{boot} = 90 \text{ seconds} < T_{cd} = 300 \text{ seconds}$$
$$\therefore \text{The system is in safe state — no flapping.}$$

**Step 6 — Hysteresis verification:**
$$T_{in} = 30\% < T_{out} = 80\% \quad \checkmark$$
$$\text{No additional scale-out triggered for 300 seconds (cooldown).}$$

**Step 7 — Final State:**
$$\boxed{N_{active} = 8 \text{ VMs, next evaluation after 300 s}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: OpenStack Flavor-based Resource Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An OpenStack `m1.large` flavor is defined as: **vCPUs = 4, RAM = 8 GB, Disk = 80 GB**. A tenant launches 3 such VMs. Compute the **total allocated resources**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Per-VM resource vector:**
$$R_{vm} = (4 \text{ vCPU},\; 8 \text{ GB RAM},\; 80 \text{ GB disk})$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate for $N = 3$ VMs:**
$$R_{total} = N \cdot R_{vm} = 3 \cdot (4, 8, 80)$$

**Step 3 — Component-wise computation:**
$$R_{total}^{cpu} = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 \text{ vCPUs}$$
$$R_{total}^{ram} = 3 \cdot 8 = 24 \text{ GB}$$
$$R_{total}^{disk} = 3 \cdot 80 = 240 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 4 — Cost calculation** (assume pricing: $\$0.04/\text{vCPU-h}, \$0.005/\text{GB-RAM-h}, \$0.0001/\text{GB-disk-h}$):
$$C_{hourly} = (12 \cdot 0.04) + (24 \cdot 0.005) + (240 \cdot 0.0001)$$
$$C_{hourly} = 0.48 + 0.12 + 0.024 = \$0.624/\text{hour}$$

**Step 5 — Monthly projection (730 hours):**
$$C_{monthly} = 0.624 \cdot 730 = \$455.52$$

**Step 6 — Final Summary:**
$$\boxed{R_{total} = (12 \text{ vCPUs},\; 24 \text{ GB RAM},\; 240 \text{ GB disk}),\quad C_{monthly} \approx \$455.52}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Static vs Dynamic Provisioning

### Table 3.1 — Comprehensive Trade-off Analysis (Board Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Static Provisioning | Dynamic Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Provisioning Time** | Once at design ($t = 0$) | Continuous ($t > 0$, every $\Delta t$) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Low ($\rho_{over} > 1.5$ typical) | High ($\rho_{over} \to 1$ achievable) |
| **Elasticity** | None | High (horizontal + vertical) |
| **SLA Compliance** | Poor under spikes | Excellent via auto-scaling |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | High (requires monitoring, policies) |
| **Boot Latency Impact** | None (pre-allocated) | Yes (cold start $t_{boot} \approx 30$–$120$ s) |
| **Predictability** | High (deterministic) | Moderate (depends on policy) |
| **Compliance/Audit** | Easy | Requires policy audit |
| **Use Case** | Batch, scientific, payroll | Web, e-commerce, streaming |
| **OpenStack Implementation** | Fixed `--max-count` quota | Heat AutoScalingGroup, Senlin |
| **Failure Recovery** | Manual | Semi-automated (Watcher) |
| **Overhead Cost** | Idle resources ($W_r > 0.3$) | Telemetry + scheduler overhead |
| **Workload Type** | Stable, predictable | Variable, bursty |

```mermaid
        CN5[Cinder Scheduler]
        CN6[Swift Proxy]
        CN7[Heat Engine]
        CN8[Horizon Dashboard]
        CN9[Ceilometer Collector]
    end
    subgraph NetworkNode["Network Node"]
        NN1[Neutron L3 Agent]
        NN2[Neutron DHCP Agent]
        NN3[Neutron OVS Agent]
    end
    subgraph CompNode1["Compute Node 1"]
        CN1A[Nova Compute]
        CN1B[KVM Hypervisor]
        CN1C[Neutron OVS Agent]
    end
    subgraph CompNode2["Compute Node 2"]
        CN2A[Nova Compute]
        CN2B[KVM Hypervisor]
        CN2C[Neutron OVS Agent]
    end
    subgraph StorageNode["Storage Node"]
        SN1[Cinder Volume]
        SN2[Swift Storage]
        SN3[Glance Image Backend]
    end
    UserA[Client] --> CN8
    CN8 --> CN3
    CN3 --> CN1A
    CN3 --> CN2A
    CN1A --> CN1B
    CN2A --> CN2B
    CN1B --> CN1C
    CN2B --> CN2C
    CN1C --> NN1
    CN2C --> NN1
    CN4 --> NN1
    CN5 --> SN1
    CN6 --> SN2
    CN2 --> SN3
    CN3 --> CN9
```
