# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **static** and **dynamic resource provisioning** in cloud computing. List any two advantages of dynamic provisioning.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Breakdown):**

| Point | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition – Static** | Pre-allocated, fixed resource pool determined at deployment; pool size remains constant during runtime. | 0.5 |
| **Definition – Dynamic** | Resources are scaled in/out automatically based on workload metrics, SLA, or predictive policies. | 0.5 |
| **Difference – Elasticity** | Static = No elasticity; Dynamic = High elasticity. | 0.5 |
| **Difference – Cost** | Static = High idle cost; Dynamic = Optimized TCO. | 0.5 |
| **Difference – SLA** | Static = Vulnerable to spikes; Dynamic = SLA-aware. | 0.5 |
| **Advantage 1** | Improved resource utilization (reduces wastage $W_r$). | 0.25 |
| **Advantage 2** | Better SLA compliance through automated scale-out. | 0.25 |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is **OpenStack**? List any **four core services** of OpenStack with their primary functions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Breakdown):**

| Point | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **OpenStack Definition** | Open-source IaaS cloud operating system for pooling compute, storage, and networking resources. | 1.0 |
| **Nova** | Compute service — VM lifecycle and scheduling. | 0.5 |
| **Neutron** | Networking service — SDN, routers, subnets, LBaaS. | 0.5 |
| **Cinder** | Block storage service — persistent volumes for VMs. | 0.5 |
| **Keystone** | Identity service — authentication, tokens, RBAC. | 0.5 |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With neat diagrams, explain the **architecture of OpenStack**. Discuss the role of any **six core services** in detail. Also describe the **step-by-step workflow** of provisioning a virtual machine.

#### Part (a) — OpenStack Architecture and Six Core Services (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

OpenStack follows a **modular, service-oriented architecture** with four logical layers (see Section 2.3.2 and Section 4.1).

**Six Core Services (7 Marks Breakdown):**

1. **Nova – Compute Service (1.5 Marks)**
   - Manages VM lifecycle: spawn, pause, suspend, terminate.
   - Components: nova-api, nova-scheduler, nova-compute, nova-conductor.
   - Supports multiple hypervisors: KVM, QEMU, Xen, LXD, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V.
   - Uses FilterScheduler for intelligent host selection based on filters (CPUFilter, RamFilter, DiskFilter) and weighers.

2. **Neutron – Networking Service (1.5 Marks)**
   - Provides Software-Defined Networking (SDN) with pluggable backends.
   - Supports networks, subnets, routers, floating IPs, security groups, load balancers (LBaaS), firewalls (FWaaS).
   - Plugins: ML2 (Modular Layer 2), Open vSwitch, Linux Bridge, OVN.
   - **Use case:** Multi-tenant network isolation for cloud workloads.

3. **Cinder – Block Storage Service (1 Mark)**
   - Provides persistent block devices attached to VMs via iSCSI, FC, NFS, RBD.
   - Supports volume snapshots, clones, and encryption.
   - Backend drivers: LVM, Ceph RBD, NetApp, SolidFire.

4. **Keystone – Identity Service (1 Mark)**
   - Centralized authentication and authorization.
   - Issues **tokens** (UUID, Fernet, PKI, PKIZ) post-authentication.
   - Manages **tenants (projects)**, users, roles, and service catalogs.
   - Implements RBAC (Role-Based Access Control).

5. **Glance – Image Service (1 Mark)**
   - Stores and retrieves VM disk images (RAW, QCOW2, VHD, VMDK, ISO).
   - Supports **metadata** (OS type, kernel, RAM disk).
   - Backends: filesystem, Swift, Ceph, HTTP, S3.

6. **Swift – Object Storage Service (1 Mark)**
   - Highly available, distributed object store with RESTful HTTP API.
   - Data is replicated across zones/regions (configurable replication factor, typically $\times 3$).
   - **Use case:** Storing backups, images, archives, large media files.

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step VM Provisioning Workflow (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (7 Marks Breakdown):**

**Step 1 — Authentication (1 Mark)**
- User submits credentials to **Keystone** via API or Horizon.
- Keystone validates against identity backend (LDAP, SQL, Active Directory).
- A scoped **token** is returned to the client.

**Step 2 — Image Lookup (0.5 Mark)**
- Client queries Glance: `GET /v2/images` with token.
- Glance returns image metadata (ID, name, disk format, size, checksum).

**Step 3 — Flavor Selection (0.5 Mark)**
- Client chooses a **flavor** (e.g., `m1.medium: 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM, 40 GB disk`).
- Flavor defines hardware template of the VM.

**Step 4 — Network and Security Configuration (1 Mark)**
- Client requests a **Neutron** network, subnet, and router.
- Defines a **security group** (firewall rules: ingress/egress).
- Requests a **floating IP** for external access.

**Step 5 — Volume Attachment (0.5 Mark)**
- If persistent storage is needed, a **Cinder volume** is created.
- Volume is attached to the VM on boot (`--block-device-mapping`).

**Step 6 — Nova Boot Request (1.5 Marks)**
- Client: `POST /servers {flavor, image, network, security_group}` to Nova API.
- Nova scheduler applies **filters** (CPU, RAM, affinity, availability zone) and **weighers**.
- Selected compute node's nova-compute receives the message via RabbitMQ.

**Step 7 — Hypervisor Invocation (1 Mark)**
- The compute node invokes the **hypervisor** (e.g., KVM via libvirt).
- VM image is copied from Glance to local storage (or streamed).
- Virtual NIC is connected to the Open vSwitch bridge.
- VM enters **BUILDING → ACTIVE** state.

**Step 8 — Telemetry and Reporting (1 Mark)**
- Ceilometer begins polling VM metrics (CPU, RAM, network, disk I/O).
- VM IP, hostname, and metadata returned to client.
- Horizon dashboard displays the running VM.

**Final Answer Summary:**
$$\boxed{\text{VM Provisioning} = \text{Keystone} \to \text{Glance} \to \text{Neutron} \to \text{Cinder} \to \text{Nova} \to \text{Ceilometer}}$$

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare **static and dynamic resource provisioning** in cloud computing with suitable examples. Derive the formula for the **number of virtual machines required** for a peak load scenario, and explain the **auto-scaling threshold model** with a state diagram.

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis with Examples (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Static Provisioning (3.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Resources are pre-allocated and remain constant.
- **Example:** A university library reservation system that must support exactly 500 concurrent users on exam day. The cloud admin provisions 10 VMs (`m1.medium`) and never scales.
- **Characteristics:** Deterministic, low complexity, high over-provisioning penalty.
- **Cost formula:** $C_{static} = N_{vm} \cdot c_{vm} \cdot T_{duration}$ (paid regardless of usage).

**Dynamic Provisioning (3.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Resources are automatically scaled based on monitored metrics.
- **Example:** A flash-sale e-commerce site where traffic rises from 100 to 10,000 users in 5 minutes. AWS Auto Scaling Group (or OpenStack Heat AutoScalingGroup) spawns additional VMs.
- **Characteristics:** Elastic, SLA-aware, cost-optimized.
- **Cost formula:** $C_{dynamic} = \int_{0}^{T} N_{vm}(t) \cdot c_{vm} \, dt$ (paid per actual usage).

**Comparative Summary Table (Embedded in answer):**

| Criterion | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Elasticity | None | High |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Cost Efficiency | Poor at low load | Excellent |
| SLA Risk | High under burst | Low |
| Boot Latency | $0$ (pre-allocated) | $t_{boot} \approx 30$–$120$ s |

#### Part (b) — Derivation of VM Count and Auto-Scaling Threshold Model (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the Sizing Equation (1.5 Marks):**

$$N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak} \cdot S_{req}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Worked Example (1.5 Marks):**
Given $L_{peak} = 5{,}000$ users, $S_{req} = 0.04$ cores/user, $C_{vm} = 8$ cores:
$$N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{5000 \cdot 0.04}{8} \right\rceil = \lceil 25 \rceil = 25 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 3 — Auto-Scaling Threshold Model (2 Marks):**

Let $M(t)$ be the monitored metric (e.g., average CPU utilization across VMs).

- **Scale-Out Trigger:** If $M(t) > T_{out}$ → add $\Delta N$ VMs.
- **Scale-In Trigger:** If $M(t) < T_{in}$ → remove $\Delta N$ VMs.
- **Steady State:** $T_{in} \leq M(t) \leq T_{out}$ → no action.
- **Cooldown:** Wait $T_{cd}$ seconds after each action.

**State Transition Equation:**

$$N_{active}(t + \Delta t) = \begin{cases} N_{active}(t) + \Delta N & \text{if } M(t) > T_{out} \\ N_{active}(t) - \Delta N & \text{if } M(t) < T_{in} \\ N_{active}(t) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Condition (1 Mark):**
$$T_{in} < T_{out} \quad \text{(e.g., } T_{in} = 30\%, T_{out} = 80\%\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — State Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
        M(t) > T_out
  +---------+----------+
  |                    |
  v                    |
[STEADY]   M(t) < T_in
  |                    |
  +---------+----------+
        v
   [SCALE-OUT]   [SCALE-IN]
        |              |
        +-- cooldown --+
```

**Final Formula Box:**
$$\boxed{N_{vm} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak} \cdot S_{req}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil \quad \text{with threshold hysteresis } T_{in} < M(t) < T_{out}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - ❌ **Forgetting the ceiling operator** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — partial VMs are not deployable. **(-1 mark)**
> - ❌ **Confusing Cinder (block) with Swift (object)** — block is mounted as a disk; object is accessed via REST API. **(-1 mark)**
> - ❌ **Stating "OpenStack is a hypervisor"** — it is an *orchestrator*, not a hypervisor. The hypervisor is KVM/Xen. **(-2 marks)**
> - ❌ **Mixing up Keystone roles** — Keystone provides *Identity*, not *Networking* (Neutron) or *Storage* (Cinder). **(-1 mark)**
> - ❌ **Forgetting units in the sizing equation** — always state the units (VMs, users, cores). **(-0.5 mark)**
> - ❌ **Not mentioning cooldown** in auto-scaling answers — flapping protection is a board-exam expectation. **(-1 mark)**
> - ❌ **Writing "OpenStack = AWS"** — OpenStack is open-source and self-hosted; AWS is a public cloud service. **(-1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points — Read before every KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Static provisioning** = fixed, pre-allocated, deterministic, low complexity, high wastage penalty.
- ✅ **Dynamic provisioning** = elastic, policy-driven, SLA-aware, optimized TCO.
- ✅ **Two flavors of dynamic scaling:** **Vertical (scale-up/down)** and **Horizontal (scale-out/in)**.
- ✅ **Two flavors of dynamic triggers:** **Reactive (threshold-based)** and **Proactive (predictive/ML-based)**.
- ✅ **Sizing equation:** $N_{vm} = \lceil (L_{peak} \cdot S_{req}) / C_{vm} \rceil$.
- ✅ **Over-provisioning ratio:** $\rho_{over} = C_{peak} / L_{avg} \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Hysteresis condition:** $T_{in} < T_{out}$ (mandatory for stable auto-scaling).
- ✅ **Cooldown rule:** $T_{cd} \geq t_{boot}$ (prevents flapping).
- ✅ **OpenStack = IaaS Orchestrator** (NOT a hypervisor, NOT a public cloud).
- ✅ **Core OpenStack services (must memorize):** **Nova** (compute), **Neutron** (network), **Cinder** (block storage), **Swift** (object storage), **Glance** (images), **Keystone** (identity), **Horizon** (dashboard), **Heat** (orchestration), **Ceilometer** (telemetry), **Aodh** (alarming), **Senlin** (clustering).
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **K-N-C-S-G-H** for Keystone-Nova-Cinder-Swift-Glance-Horizon.
- ✅ **VM provisioning order:** Keystone → Glance → Neutron → Cinder → Nova → Ceilometer.
- ✅ **OpenStack messaging backbone:** RabbitMQ (AMQP).
- ✅ **OpenStack metadata service:** MariaDB / MySQL.
- ✅ **OpenStack is Apache 2.0 licensed** and launched in **2010** by NASA + Rackspace.
- ✅ **Private cloud** = single-tenant, on-premise, full control; **Public cloud** = multi-tenant, third-party owned.
- ✅ **Auto-scaling is implemented in OpenStack via Heat AutoScalingGroup or Senlin clustering service.**
- ✅ **Ceilometer collects metrics, Gnocchi stores time-series, Aodh raises alarms — three-tier telemetry pipeline.**
- ✅ **Static is best for:** batch, scientific, payroll. **Dynamic is best for:** web, e-commerce, streaming, SaaS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eucalyptus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eucalyptus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Eucalyptus — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eucalyptus (Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems)** is an open-source cloud computing platform used to build private and hybrid cloud computing environments. It is designed to deliver Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) functionality by replicating the **Amazon Web Services (AWS) APIs** (specifically EC2, S3, and IAM) on-premises within an organization's own data center infrastructure.

In simpler KTU-2024 Scheme academic terminology:
- It is a **Linux-based software framework** that turns a cluster of commodity servers into a private cloud.
- It supports **WS-Security** (not the standard AWS public key authentication), making it compatible with enterprise identity systems.
- It was originally developed at the **University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB)** and later acquired by **Hewlett-Packard (HP)** in 2014.
- Current stable lineage: **Eucalyptus 4.x** (the 4.x series introduced compatibility with AWS IAM, S3, and EC2 APIs at near 100% parity).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Private Amazon Clone"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "Think of Eucalyptus as a Local Power Grid"**
>
> Imagine your city (Amazon AWS) has a massive power grid that provides electricity (computing) to any house (virtual machine) that plugs into it. Now, suppose you live in a remote village (your university/enterprise data center) and you want the *same* electrical infrastructure without depending on the city's grid. You hire an engineer (Eucalyptus) to build a **local, on-site mini-grid that uses the exact same plugs and switches** as the city's grid. This way, every appliance (application, VM, bucket) built for the city works seamlessly on your village grid. **That is Eucalyptus** — it is an API-compatible private cloud that mimics AWS, so existing AWS tools and SDKs work out-of-the-box.

## 1.3 Why Eucalyptus Matters in KTU Curriculum

- It is the **classic textbook example** of a *private cloud orchestration platform* (revisit Modules 1 & 2 of PEEET637 for context on cloud deployment models).
- It demonstrates the **SOA (Service-Oriented Architecture)** pattern of cloud design.
- It introduces students to **multi-tier distributed system architecture** before they study OpenStack (the more modern equivalent).
- Frequently appears as a **2-mark or 14-mark question** in University Examinations under "Open Source Cloud Platforms" or "Private Cloud Toolkits."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under Module 3 of PEEET637, students are expected to understand cloud platforms including Eucalyptus, OpenStack, and OpenNebula. The emphasis is on **architecture, components, communication protocols, and AWS-API compatibility.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Eucalyptus — System Architecture (Layered View)

Eucalyptus follows a **strictly modular, hierarchical, and loosely-coupled architecture**. Each tier is an independent web service exposing its own SOAP/REST interface. Communication between tiers uses **WS-Security** (enterprise-grade) rather than AWS's public key signature.

### 2.1.1 Five High-Level Components (Tier Model)

| Tier # | Component Name | Standard Acronym | Role in Cloud |
|--------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Cloud Controller | **CLC** | Top-level entry point; presents the EC2-compatible API to clients; handles user authentication, resource accounting, virtual instance management, and global decisions. |
| 2 | Walrus | **Walrus** (W) | Implements the S3-compatible object storage service (bucket-based PUT/GET); users can store VM images and data. |
| 3 | Cluster Controller (Storage Controller may co-reside) | **CC** + **SC** | Manages a single cluster of machines; handles VM scheduling, instance-to-node assignment, and block storage (EBS-equivalent via SC). |
| 4 | Node Controller | **NC** | The hypervisor-level agent running on every physical compute node; executes the actual VMs and reports resource state. |
| 5 | Optional: VMware Broker / HTTP Front-End | **Broker** | Provides access to VMware infrastructure (vSphere/ESX) when nodes are virtualized via VMware rather than Xen/KVM. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam Tip):** Remember the order from top-to-bottom of the user request path — **C → W → C → N** (Cloud → Walrus → Cluster → Node). Each arrow represents a SOAP-based authenticated web service call.

### 2.1.2 Communication Protocols and Interfaces

- **Client ↔ CLC:** AWS-compatible Query/REST API (SOAP under the hood for Eucalyptus-internal calls).
- **CLC ↔ CC:** Internal SOAP using WS-Security.
- **CC ↔ NC:** Internal SOAP using WS-Security.
- **CLC ↔ Walrus:** Internal SOAP.
- **Inter-node messaging:** None (no peer-to-peer); strictly hierarchical — every component only talks to its immediate parent/child.

## 2.2 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Term | Definition / Value | KTU Exam Significance |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Eucalyptus** | Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems | Full-form frequently asked in 2-mark questions |
| **API Compatibility** | 100% EC2 + S3 + IAM compatible | Core value proposition |
| **Default Hypervisors** | Xen (initial), KVM, VMware ESX/ESXi | Multi-hypervisor support |
| **Network Modes** | SYSTEM (default), STATIC, MANAGED (VLAN-based), MANAGED-NOVLAN | Determines IP allocation strategy |
| **Security Standard** | WS-Security with X.509 certificates | Differs from AWS public key auth |
| **Metadata Service** | `http://169.254.169.254/` (within VM) | Same IP as AWS — enabling portability |
| **Image Format** | Bundled/Registered AMI, root filesystem + kernel + ramdisk | Similar to EC2 AMI lifecycle |
| **Instance Addressing** | Private IPs internally; elastic IPs (optional) | Same address-pool semantics as EC2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU 2024 Board Exams:** The IP address **169.254.169.254** is the *metadata service IP* that Eucalyptus inherits from EC2 — any 14-mark question on Eucalyptus often includes this as a sub-question.

## 2.3 Why "Why?" — The Real Engineering Rationale

- **Why API compatibility with AWS?** → Lets enterprises reuse existing AWS-compatible tooling, training, and scripts without rewriting — dramatically reduces migration cost.
- **Why a hierarchical, not flat architecture?** → Scalability and fault isolation; each component can fail independently without bringing down the entire cloud.
- **Why Walrus instead of integrating with external S3?** → To make the cloud *self-contained* and avoid outbound dependencies, critical for isolated data centers (e.g., defense, banking).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through: User Request Lifecycle in Eucalyptus

The following exhaustive step-by-step analysis traces what happens when a user issues the command:

```
euca-run-instances emi-ABC12345
```

### Step 1 — Client Authentication
The client tool (euca2ools) signs the request using the user's private key (X.509 certificate). This is the standard AWS Signature Version 2/3 protocol.

### Step 2 — Request Routed to CLC
The CLC receives the `RunInstances` SOAP message. It performs:
- **Identity verification** via its internal user database (or LDAP/Active Directory bridge).
- **Quota check** — has the user exceeded their allotted instance count, CPU, memory, or storage?
- **Image metadata lookup** — does the requested EMI (Eucalyptus Machine Image) exist? In which Walrus bucket?

### Step 3 — CLC Selects a Cluster
The CLC maintains a top-level map of all available Clusters. It chooses one based on:
- Round-robin or load-balancing algorithm (configurable).
- Cluster availability and capacity metrics reported by CC heartbeat messages.

### Step 4 — CLC Forwards to Selected CC
A SOAP message is dispatched to the chosen CC containing:
- EMI ID
- Instance type (m1.small, c1.medium, etc.)
- User/group ID
- Key pair name (for SSH access)
- Network configuration

### Step 5 — CC Performs Node Selection
The CC queries its NCs for resource availability (free cores, RAM, disk). It selects the NC with the most free resources (greedy heuristic) and forwards the launch request.

### Step 6 — NC Instantiates the VM
The NC invokes the local hypervisor (KVM/Xen/VMware) to:
- Download the EMI from Walrus to local disk cache (or iSCSI mount).
- Inject the user's SSH public key.
- Boot the VM.
- Assign IP address from the configured network mode pool.

### Step 7 — NC Reports Back Up the Chain
The NC returns the instance ID and IP address to CC → CLC → Client.

### Step 8 — Metadata Service Configuration
Inside the running VM, the IP **169.254.169.254** is routed to the CC, which serves metadata (instance ID, user-data, SSH keys) — identical to EC2 behavior.

## 3.2 Mathematical Model of Cluster Capacity

Although Eucalyptus does not use explicit load-balancing formulas, the resource-selection logic at the CC can be formally expressed as:

Let:
- $N$ = total number of Node Controllers in a cluster
- $R_i$ = remaining free RAM on node $i$
- $C_i$ = remaining free CPU cores on node $i$
- $D_i$ = remaining free disk (in GB) on node $i$

The CC computes a **fitness score** for each node:

$$
S_i = w_1 R_i + w_2 C_i + w_3 D_i
$$

Where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are configurable weight coefficients. The CC picks:

$$
i^* = \arg\max_{i \in \{1, \dots, N\}} S_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to } R_i \geq r_{\text{req}}, \quad C_i \geq c_{\text{req}}, \quad D_i \geq d_{\text{req}}
$$

Where $r_{\text{req}}, c_{\text{req}}, d_{\text{req}}$ are the resource demands of the requested instance type.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a user requests a **m1.small** instance with requirements:

$$
r_{\text{req}} = 512 \text{ MB}, \quad c_{\text{req}} = 1 \text{ core}, \quad d_{\text{req}} = 5 \text{ GB}
$$

Three NCs report the following free resources, and the CC uses equal weights $w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = 1$:

| Node ID | Free RAM (MB) | Free CPU (cores) | Free Disk (GB) |
|---------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| NC-1    | 1024          | 4                | 20              |
| NC-2    | 2048          | 2                | 10              |
| NC-3    | 512           | 1                | 5               |

**Step 1 — Feasibility Check:**

$$
\text{NC-3: } R_3 = 512 \geq 512 \;\checkmark,\; C_3 = 1 \geq 1 \;\checkmark,\; D_3 = 5 \geq 5 \;\checkmark
$$

All three nodes satisfy the constraints.

**Step 2 — Compute Fitness Scores:**

$$
S_1 = 1024 + 4 + 20 = 1048
$$

$$
S_2 = 2048 + 2 + 10 = 2060
$$

$$
S_3 = 512 + 1 + 5 = 518
$$

**Step 3 — Select Maximum:**

$$
i^* = \arg\max(1048, 2060, 518) = 2
$$

**Result:** The instance will be placed on **NC-2**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Responsibility Breakdown

| Component | Listens On (Default Port) | Data Store Backend | Failure Impact |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| CLC | TCP 8773 | PostgreSQL / MySQL | Entire cloud unreachable |
| Walrus | TCP 8773 (HTTP), 8443 | Local filesystem (MogileFS) | Cannot register/launch new images |
| CC | TCP 8774 | Local SQLite (4.x) | One cluster goes offline; rest of cloud unaffected |
| SC | TCP 8773 | iSCSI / AoE backend | Block storage broken |
| NC | TCP 8775 | Local filesystem | Just that one node's VMs die |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Eucalyptus

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user["USER CLIENT euca2ools SDK"]
    c1["CLOUD CONTROLLER CLC"]
    walrus["WALRUS S3 STORAGE"]
    cc1["CLUSTER CONTROLLER CC Zone A"]
    cc2["CLUSTER CONTROLLER CC Zone B"]
    nc1["NODE CONTROLLER NC1"]
    nc2["NODE CONTROLLER NC2"]
    nc3["NODE CONTROLLER NC3"]
    nc4["NODE CONTROLLER NC4"]
    hyper1["HYPERVISOR KVM Xen"]
    hyper2["HYPERVISOR KVM Xen"]

    user -->|"EC2 REST Query signed request"| c1
    c1 -->|"SOAP WS Security auth + quota check"| walrus
    c1 -->|"RunInstances dispatch"| cc1
    c1 -->|"RunInstances dispatch"| cc2
    cc1 -->|"launch VM on best node"| nc1
    cc1 -->|"launch VM on best node"| nc2
    cc2 -->|"launch VM on best node"| nc3
    cc2 -->|"launch VM on best node"| nc4
    nc1 --> hyper1
    nc2 --> hyper1
    nc3 --> hyper2
    nc4 --> hyper2
    walrus -.->|"EMI image download"| nc1
    walrus -.->|"EMI image download"| nc2
    walrus -.->|"EMI image download"| nc3
    walrus -.->|"EMI image download"| nc4
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — Virtual Machine Lifecycle in Eucalyptus

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending: euca-run-instances
    Pending --> Running: NC launches VM
    Running --> Running: user reboot
    Running --> Stopped: user stop
    Stopped --> Running: user start
    Running --> Terminated: user terminate
    Running --> Terminated: NC crash detection
    Stopped --> Terminated: user terminate
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant CLC as Cloud Controller
    participant W as Walrus
    participant CC as Cluster Controller
    participant NC as Node Controller
    participant VM as Virtual Machine

    U->>CLC: RunInstances EMI ID
    CLC->>W: Verify EMI exists
    W-->>CLC: EMI metadata OK
    CLC->>CC: Allocate instance to cluster
    CC->>NC: Select best node
    NC->>W: Download EMI image
    NC->>VM: Boot via hypervisor
    VM-->>NC: Running state
    NC-->>CC: Instance started
    CC-->>CLC: Success
    CLC-->>U: Return instance ID and IP
```

## 4.4 Network Modes Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SYSTEM MODE host bridge simple DHCP"]
    B["STATIC MODE admin assigns IPs from pool"]
    C["MANAGED MODE VLAN + DHCP daemon"]
    D["MANAGED NO VLAN flat network"]
    E["VM Instance receives IP"]
    A --> E
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Eucalyptus and list any two of its key features.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Eucalyptus (Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems) is an open-source software framework that enables the construction of private and hybrid IaaS clouds. It is API-compatible with Amazon Web Services (EC2, S3, IAM).

**Key Features (any two):**
1. **AWS API Compatibility** — Allows reuse of existing AWS tools, scripts, and SDKs.
2. **Modular Hierarchical Architecture** — Composed of independent components (CLC, CC, NC, Walrus) enabling scalability and fault isolation.
3. **Multi-Hypervisor Support** — Supports Xen, KVM, and VMware ESXi.
4. **WS-Security Based Authentication** — Enterprise-grade security using X.509 certificates instead of public-key signatures.

---

### Q2. What is the role of the Walrus component in Eucalyptus?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Walrus is the storage controller component of Eucalyptus that implements the **S3-compatible object storage service**. It allows users to store and retrieve data using a bucket-based PUT/GET model. Functionally, it is used for:
1. Storing **Eucalyptus Machine Images (EMIs)** — the VM templates used to launch instances.
2. Storing **persistent user data** — similar to AWS S3.
3. Acting as the backend for the metadata of registered images.

Walrus ensures the cloud is **self-contained** and does not require external dependencies for image or data storage.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Eucalyptus with a neat diagram. List the responsibilities of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step process of launching a virtual instance in Eucalyptus. What role does the metadata service (169.254.169.254) play? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Eucalyptus architecture is **hierarchical and modular**, comprising five primary components:

**1. Cloud Controller (CLC)** — The top-tier component.
- Presents the EC2-compatible API to end users.
- Performs user authentication and authorization.
- Maintains the global system state in a database (PostgreSQL/MySQL).
- Performs instance scheduling across clusters.
- Handles quota, accounting, and reporting.

**2. Walrus** — The object storage component.
- Implements the S3-compatible API.
- Stores EMIs, snapshots, and user data.
- Supports bucket-based access semantics.

**3. Cluster Controller (CC)** — The mid-tier component.
- Manages a single cluster of compute nodes.
- Receives instance launch requests from CLC.
- Performs node selection based on resource availability.
- Manages the instance network (IP allocation, security groups).
- Optionally, the Storage Controller (SC) co-resides here for block storage (EBS-equivalent).

**4. Node Controller (NC)** — The lowest-tier component.
- Runs on each physical compute host.
- Executes VMs using the local hypervisor (Xen/KVM/VMware).
- Reports resource availability to CC.
- Handles VM lifecycle at the host level.

**5. Optional VMware Broker** — Provides bridging between Eucalyptus and VMware vSphere.

The diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The step-by-step instance launch process:

1. **Step 1 — Client Request:** User issues `euca-run-instances emi-XXXX` with their X.509-signed credentials.
2. **Step 2 — CLC Validation:** CLC verifies credentials, checks quotas, and confirms the EMI exists in Walrus.
3. **Step 3 — Cluster Selection:** CLC picks a target CC using load-balancing heuristics.
4. **Step 4 — CC Node Selection:** CC queries all NCs, computes fitness scores, and selects the best node using the formula:
$$S_i = w_1 R_i + w_2 C_i + w_3 D_i$$
5. **Step 5 — VM Boot:** NC downloads EMI from Walrus, injects the SSH key, and boots the VM through the local hypervisor.
6. **Step 6 — IP Allocation:** An IP is assigned from the configured network mode pool (SYSTEM/STATIC/MANAGED).
7. **Step 7 — Reporting:** The instance ID and IP are returned up the chain to the user.

**Role of 169.254.169.254 (Metadata Service):**
- This IP address, when queried from inside a running VM, returns critical metadata such as:
  - Instance ID
  - User-data (passed via `euca-run-instances -d <user-data>`)
  - SSH public key
  - Security group info
- This mirrors EC2's metadata service, enabling **AWS-portable workloads** to run unmodified on Eucalyptus.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare Eucalyptus and OpenStack across at least five parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four network modes available in Eucalyptus. Which mode is most commonly deployed and why? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Eucalyptus | OpenStack |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| **Origin** | UCSB research project (2008), later acquired by HP | NASA + Rackspace joint project (2010) |
| **AWS Compatibility** | Native and near 100% EC2/S3/IAM API compatibility | API-compatible through separate projects (e.g., Neutron); not the core focus |
| **Architecture** | Strictly hierarchical (CLC → CC → NC) | Distributed and decentralized; loosely-coupled services |
| **Components** | CLC, Walrus, CC, SC, NC | Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Keystone, Glance, Horizon, Swift |
| **Hypervisor Support** | Xen, KVM, VMware | KVM, Xen, VMware, Hyper-V, LXC, QEMU, Bare Metal (Ironic) |
| **Web Interface** | Eucalyptus User Console (EUCALYPTUS simple) | Horizon Dashboard (rich, feature-rich) |
| **Identity Backend** | WS-Security + X.509; local DB | Keystone (supports LDAP, SAML, OpenID, OAuth) |
| **Current Status** | Maintenance mode (HP Helion Eucalyptus was discontinued in 2017) | Active open-source development under OpenInfra Foundation |
| **Modularity** | Lower (tighter integration) | Higher (each service is independent) |
| **Community** | Smaller (commercial focus) | Massive global community |

#### Model Solution for (b):

Eucalyptus supports four network modes, configurable at the CC level:

**1. SYSTEM Mode (Default):**
- The simplest mode.
- The instance is attached to the host's existing network bridge.
- The host's DHCP server provides the IP.
- **Use Case:** Testing, lab environments.

**2. STATIC Mode:**
- Administrator pre-defines a pool of IP addresses.
- Eucalyptus assigns IPs from this pool to VMs.
- No dynamic allocation.
- **Use Case:** Environments where IPs must be predictable.

**3. MANAGED Mode:**
- Most feature-rich mode.
- Uses a dedicated VLAN (Ethernet bridge with tagged VLAN ID).
- Eucalyptus runs its own **eucalyptus-dhcpd** daemon to manage leases.
- Provides isolation between security groups.
- **Use Case:** Production multi-tenant cloud.

**4. MANAGED-NOVLAN Mode:**
- Similar to MANAGED but without VLAN tagging.
- Uses a flat bridge across nodes.
- Provides DHCP-managed IPs without VLAN overhead.
- **Use Case:** When switches do not support 802.1Q VLAN tagging.

**Most Commonly Deployed:** **MANAGED Mode** — because it provides proper tenant isolation through VLANs, supports dynamic IP allocation, and scales to multi-tenant production environments while still being standards-compliant.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CLC and CC** — Many students write "Cloud Controller" when they mean "Cluster Controller." Remember: CLC = cloud-wide; CC = per-cluster.
> 2. **Forgetting the API compatibility aspect** — Eucalyptus without its AWS API compatibility is just another hypervisor manager; the *API parity* is its defining feature.
> 3. **Wrong port numbers or wrong default hypervisor** — Default hypervisor is Xen for older versions and KVM for newer versions. Eucalyptus 4.x defaults to KVM.
> 4. **Missing the metadata IP** — `169.254.169.254` is a high-yield trivia question.
> 5. **Ignoring Walrus** — Many answers omit Walrus as if it were optional; it is mandatory for image storage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Eucalyptus** = Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems.
- It is an **open-source private/hybrid IaaS** platform.
- **Key differentiator:** 100% AWS EC2 + S3 + IAM API compatibility.
- **Architecture:** Hierarchical — **CLC → CC → NC**; with **Walrus** for storage and **SC** for block storage.
- **Security:** Uses **WS-Security with X.509 certificates** (not AWS-style public key signatures).
- **Hypervisors supported:** Xen, KVM, VMware ESXi.
- **Metadata IP:** `169.254.169.254` (EC2-compatible).
- **Network modes:** SYSTEM, STATIC, MANAGED, MANAGED-NOVLAN.
- **Image format:** EMI (Eucalyptus Machine Image) — uploaded via `euca-bundle-image`.
- **Default CLC data store:** PostgreSQL or MySQL.
- **Default Walrus storage:** Local filesystem via MogileFS (in older versions) or direct filesystem (4.x).
- **Origin:** UC Santa Barbara (Eucalyptus Systems, Inc., 2008); acquired by HP in 2014.
- **Current status:** Largely in maintenance mode — students should be aware that **OpenStack succeeded Eucalyptus** in the open-source IaaS arena, but Eucalyptus remains academically significant.
- **Cross-reference:** Compare with **OpenStack** and **OpenNebula** (both KTU Module 3 topics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When answering any 14-mark Eucalyptus question in the KTU University Exam, always include a **labelled block diagram** showing CLC, Walrus, CC, NC, and the user's client. A diagram is worth at least 2–3 marks by itself, even if textual content is average.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Nebula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Nebula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OpenNebula** is an open-source, industry-grade **cloud computing platform** designed to manage virtualized data centers. It enables the dynamic provisioning, scheduling, and management of virtual machines (VMs) and virtual infrastructure (compute, storage, and network) across distributed heterogeneous resources, supporting both private and hybrid cloud deployments.

OpenNebula is engineered to provide a flexible, robust, and scalable virtualization management layer that interfaces with underlying **hypervisors** (KVM, VMware, Xen) to deliver Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud capabilities. It follows a centralized management architecture that allows administrators to consolidate, monitor, and orchestrate workloads efficiently.

### Key Characteristics (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

- **Open-Source Foundation**: Licensed under Apache 2.0, ensuring transparency, extensibility, and community-driven development.
- **Multi-Hypervisor Support**: Compatible with KVM, VMware vCenter, Xen, and Microsoft Hyper-V.
- **Hybrid Cloud Enablement**: Integration with public cloud providers (AWS, Azure) for workload bursting.
- **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**: Composed of decoupled, independently scalable services.
- **Standards-Compliant**: Implements cloud APIs (OCCI) and supports EC2 and vCloud interfaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Building Manager**

Imagine a large commercial office complex with multiple floors, rooms, lighting, and climate control systems. Instead of managing each device individually, the building manager has a **centralized control room** with dashboards and switches to:
- Allocate rooms to tenants dynamically
- Monitor electricity and water usage
- Schedule maintenance for individual systems
- Expand the building by adding new floors

**OpenNebula plays the exact same role for a data center.** It is the "smart manager" that:
- Allocates **Virtual Machines (rooms)** to users (tenants) on demand
- Monitors CPU, memory, storage, and network (utilities)
- Schedules VM lifecycles (maintenance, scaling, decommissioning)
- Seamlessly extends the data center to public clouds (expanding the building)

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** OpenNebula is to a data center what a **Resource Scheduler** is to a multi-tenant office building. It abstracts physical complexity and exposes a unified management interface.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Hypervisor** | Software that creates and runs virtual machines (e.g., KVM) |
| **Front-end** | The OpenNebula server that orchestrates operations |
| **Host (Node)** | Physical server that runs the virtual machines |
| **Cluster** | A group of physical hosts grouped for load balancing |
| **Virtual Datacenter (VDC)** | Logical grouping of resources assigned to a tenant/group |
| **Driver** | Component interfacing with underlying infrastructure (VM, Storage, Network, etc.) |
| **oned** | The core OpenNebula daemon that processes user requests |
| **Sunstone** | The web-based graphical user interface for OpenNebula |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OpenNebula Operational Metrics** — These standard metrics are critical for KTU exam answers:
> - **Front-end Capacity**: Single management server typically handles up to **500 hosts** and **20,000 VMs**
> - **Default API Port**: HTTP **:2633** for XML-RPC API communication
> - **Database**: Default SQLite; production deployments use **MySQL** or **PostgreSQL**
> - **Image Repository**: Supports raw, qcow2, vmdk, and ISO formats

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OpenNebula Architecture (Three-Layer Model)

OpenNebula follows a classic **three-tier architecture** consisting of:

### Layer 1: Front-end (Management Layer)
- Acts as the centralized control plane.
- Hosts the **oned** daemon, **scheduler**, and **Sunstone GUI**.
- Manages authentication, authorization, and quota enforcement.
- Communicates with hosts via SSH (default) or specific drivers.

### Layer 2: Core (Middleware Layer)
- Manages the **OpenNebula database** (datastore, image, VM templates, users, groups, ACLs).
- Implements the **scheduler logic** for VM placement.
- Coordinates drivers for resource provisioning.

### Layer 3: Hosts (Physical/Virtual Resources)
- Physical servers running supported hypervisors.
- Execute the actual VMs.
- Communicate with the front-end via driver scripts.

## 2.2 Core Components (Detailed Breakdown)

### A. **oned (OpenNebula Daemon)**
The master service that:
- Receives and validates all user API requests.
- Maintains persistent state in the database.
- Triggers driver operations.
- Manages the lifecycle: `pending → hold → prolog → running → migrate → done → failed`.

### B. **Scheduler (MATCH MAKER)**
Determines the optimal host for VM placement using a **rank-based matchmaking algorithm**:
- **RANK** = $W_1 \cdot \text{CPU} + W_2 \cdot \text{Memory} + W_3 \cdot \text{Network} - W_4 \cdot \text{Load}$

Where each $W_i$ is a configurable weight.

### C. **Drivers (4 Types)**

> [!NOTE]
> OpenNebula uses a modular driver framework for flexibility:

1. **Virtualization Driver** — Interfaces with hypervisors (KVM, VMware, Xen)
2. **Storage Driver** — Manages datastores (NFS, LVM, Ceph, GlusterFS)
3. **Network Driver** — Configures virtual networks (VLAN, Open vSwitch, VXLAN)
4. **Monitoring Driver** — Collects host and VM metrics (KVM, Xen, ganglia, IM)

### D. **Sunstone (Web UI)**
- Built using **Sinatra (Ruby)** framework.
- Provides self-service portal, admin panel, and cloud views.
- Supports multi-tenant dashboards.

### E. **CLI Tools**
- `onevm`, `onehost`, `oneimage`, `onetemplate`, `oneuser`, `onevnet`, `onecluster` — modular command-line utilities.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Use Case / Notes |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| **VM Scheduling Rank** | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot M_i$ | Determines best host for placement |
| **Overcommit Ratio** | $\rho = \frac{V_{\text{allocated}}}{V_{\text{physical}}}$ | Default $\rho = 1.0$; tunable per host |
| **Datastore Capacity** | $C_{\text{free}} = C_{\text{total}} - \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i$ | Disk tracking per datastore |
| **Image Cloning (COW)** | $S_{\text{derived}} = S_{\text{base}} + \Delta_{\text{writes}}$ | Copy-on-Write efficiency |
| **Network Isolation** | VLAN ID = $\vert 2^{12} - 1 \vert$ per tenant | Up to 4094 VLANs |
| **API Endpoint** | `http://<frontend>:2633/RPC2` | Default XML-RPC port |
| **HA Heartbeat** | Period $T_{\text{HB}} = 10 \text{ seconds}$ | Default for leader election |
| **Federation SLA** | $L_{\text{max}} \le 4 \text{ seconds}$ for inter-zone latency | For multi-zone hybrid clouds |

## 2.4 Why OpenNebula? (Engineering Justification)

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in engineering contexts:**
> - **Heterogeneity Management**: Enterprises use mixed hardware/hypervisors; OpenNebula unifies them.
> - **Cost Efficiency**: Avoids vendor lock-in (no VMware vSphere license).
> - **Edge-Cloud Federation**: Lightweight enough to deploy at network edge nodes.
> - **Research Reproducibility**: Used in academic testbeds (e.g., Grid'5000, BonFIRE) for reproducible cloud experiments.

## 2.5 OpenNebula vs Other Cloud Platforms

| Feature | OpenNebula | OpenStack | Eucalyptus |
|---------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **Origin** | Research (2005) | NASA+Rackspace (2010) | Research (UCSB) |
| **Architecture** | Centralized | Distributed (microservices) | Hybrid |
| **Complexity** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Hypervisors** | KVM, Xen, VMware | KVM, Xen, Hyper-V, VMware | KVM, Xen, VMware |
| **Best For** | Private/Hybrid Cloud | Large-scale Public Cloud | AWS-compatible Private |
| **API** | OCCI, EC2, vCloud | REST (Nova) | EC2 |
| **Database** | SQLite/MySQL | MySQL/PostgreSQL | MySQL |
| **Learning Curve** | Gentle | Steep | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: When comparing platforms, always highlight OpenNebula's **lightweight, single-binary installation** and **multi-tenant zone federation** as distinguishing features.

## 2.6 Operational Workflow (VM Deployment)

The complete flow from user request to running VM:

1. **User Authentication** → via Sunstone/CLI → `oneacctd` validates credentials
2. **Template Selection** → User picks VM template (CPU, RAM, disk, network)
3. **API Request** → `onevm create` sent to `oned` daemon
4. **Scheduler Invocation** → Match-making algorithm ranks all hosts
5. **Driver Dispatch** → Best host selected; driver scripts executed via SSH
6. **Resource Provisioning** → Storage driver prepares image; network driver sets up NIC
7. **VM Boot** → Hypervisor launches VM
8. **State Monitoring** → `mm_sched` updates VM state to `RUNNING`

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Host Ranking Algorithm (Match-Making)

OpenNebula uses a weighted sum scoring system to select the best host for VM placement. Let's derive the ranking formula step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Host Metrics

For a host $h_i$, define the following normalized metrics:
- $C_i$ = Available CPU cores (normalized in $[0, 1]$)
- $M_i$ = Free memory fraction
- $N_i$ = Network bandwidth availability
- $L_i$ = Current load (CPU usage)

### Step 2: Establish Default Weights

The OpenNebula scheduler uses the following default weights:

$$W_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1}{200}, \quad W_{\text{MEM}} = \frac{1}{40}, \quad W_{\text{NET}} = \frac{1}{50}, \quad W_{\text{LOAD}} = \frac{1}{100}$$

### Step 3: Construct the Rank Expression

The rank of host $h_i$ is given by:

$$
R_i = W_{\text{CPU}} \cdot C_i + W_{\text{MEM}} \cdot M_i + W_{\text{NET}} \cdot N_i - W_{\text{LOAD}} \cdot L_i
$$

**Explanation**: 
- $W_{\text{CPU}} \cdot C_i$ rewards hosts with free CPU cores.
- $W_{\text{MEM}} \cdot M_i$ rewards hosts with abundant memory.
- $W_{\text{NET}} \cdot N_i$ rewards hosts with high network capacity.
- $W_{\text{LOAD}} \cdot L_i$ penalizes hosts with high current load.

### Step 4: Selection Rule

The scheduler selects the host with the **maximum** rank:

$$
h^* = \underset{i \in H}{\mathrm{argmax}} \; R_i
$$

Where $H$ is the set of all hosts that satisfy the VM's hard requirements (RAM $\ge$ requested, etc.).

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose we have 3 hosts with metrics:

| Host | $C_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i$ | $L_i$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| h1   | 0.80  | 0.60  | 0.50  | 0.20  |
| h2   | 0.50  | 0.80  | 0.70  | 0.10  |
| h3   | 0.40  | 0.40  | 0.90  | 0.30  |

**Calculate Rank for h1:**

$$
R_1 = \left(\frac{1}{200}\right)(0.80) + \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)(0.60) + \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)(0.50) - \left(\frac{1}{100}\right)(0.20)
$$

$$
R_1 = 0.004 + 0.015 + 0.010 - 0.002 = 0.027
$$

**Calculate Rank for h2:**

$$
R_2 = \left(\frac{1}{200}\right)(0.50) + \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)(0.80) + \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)(0.70) - \left(\frac{1}{100}\right)(0.10)
$$

$$
R_2 = 0.0025 + 0.0200 + 0.0140 - 0.0010 = 0.0355
$$

**Calculate Rank for h3:**

$$
R_3 = \left(\frac{1}{200}\right)(0.40) + \left(\frac{1}{40}\right)(0.40) + \left(\frac{1}{50}\right)(0.90) - \left(\frac{1}{100}\right)(0.30)
$$

$$
R_3 = 0.0020 + 0.0100 + 0.0180 - 0.0030 = 0.0270
$$

**Result**: $R_2 > R_1 > R_3$, so $h^* = h_2$ is selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: Even though h1 has more free CPU, h2 wins because it has the highest combination of memory, network, and low load — the weighting system is holistic, not greedy.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overcommit Ratio & Capacity Planning

### Step 1: Define Total Physical Capacity

For a host with $N_{\text{CPU}}$ cores and $M_{\text{RAM}}$ GB memory, total physical capacity:

$$
C_{\text{phys}} = (N_{\text{CPU}}, M_{\text{RAM}})
$$

### Step 2: Define Allocation Vector

Let $\vec{V} = (v_1^{CPU}, v_1^{RAM}, v_2^{CPU}, v_2^{RAM}, \ldots, v_k^{CPU}, v_k^{RAM})$ represent all currently allocated resources for $k$ VMs.

### Step 3: Overcommit Ratio

$$
\rho_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i^{CPU}}{N_{\text{CPU}}}, \quad \rho_{\text{RAM}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i^{RAM}}{M_{\text{RAM}}}
$$

### Step 4: Admission Control Constraint

A new VM request $(v^{CPU}, v^{RAM})$ is admitted only if:

$$
\rho_{\text{CPU}}^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i^{CPU} + v^{CPU}}{N_{\text{CPU}}} \le \rho_{\text{max}}^{CPU}
$$

$$
\rho_{\text{RAM}}^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i^{RAM} + v^{RAM}}{M_{\text{RAM}}} \le \rho_{\text{max}}^{RAM}
$$

Where $\rho_{\text{max}}$ is the configured overcommit threshold.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: VM Lifecycle States

| State | Code | Description | Allowed Transitions |
|-------|------|-------------|---------------------|
| PENDING | `pend` | Submitted, awaiting scheduling | → HOLD, → PROLOG |
| HOLD | `hold` | Stopped by user, not scheduled | → PENDING |
| PROLOG | `prol` | Resources being prepared (image copy, network) | → RUNNING, → FAILED |
| RUNNING | `runn` | Active VM | → MIGRATE, → POWEROFF, → SUSPEND |
| MIGRATE | `migr` | Live migration in progress | → RUNNING, → POWEROFF |
| POWEROFF | `poff` | VM halted, resources intact | → RUNNING (resume) |
| DONE | `done` | VM terminated, cleanup pending | → (terminal) |
| FAILED | `fail` | Error state | → (terminal) |
| SUSPENDED | `susp` | State saved to disk | → RUNNING (resume) |

## 3.4 Federation Zone Derivation (Hybrid Cloud)

For multi-zone deployments, OpenNebula uses a **Hub-and-Spoke federation model**:

- **Local Zone**: Independent OpenNebula instance
- **Master Zone**: Coordinates multiple local zones
- **SLA-driven Workload Offloading**: 

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Local} & \text{if } R_{\text{local}} \ge R_{\text{req}} \text{ and } C_{\text{local}} \ge C_{\text{req}} \\ \text{Offload} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{local}}$: Resources available locally
- $R_{\text{req}}$: Resources required
- $C_{\text{local}}$: Local compliance/security check
- $C_{\text{req}}$: Required compliance level

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OpenNebula High-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[End User] --> sunstoneUI[Sunstone Web UI]
    userB[Admin User] --> cliTool[CLI Tools onevm etc]
    apiClient[API Client OCCI EC2] --> onedCore[oned Daemon Core]
    sunstoneUI --> onedCore
    cliTool --> onedCore
    onedCore --> scheduler[Scheduler Match Maker]
    onedCore --> database[(OpenNebula DB MySQL SQLite)]
    scheduler --> driverInterface[Driver Interface Layer]
    driverInterface --> vmDriver[Virtualization Driver]
    driverInterface --> storageDriver[Storage Driver]
    driverInterface --> networkDriver[Network Driver]
    driverInterface --> monitorDriver[Monitoring Driver]
    vmDriver --> kvmHost[Host KVM]
    vmDriver --> vmwareHost[Host VMware]
    vmDriver --> xenHost[Host Xen]
    storageDriver --> nfsStore[Datastore NFS LVM Ceph]
    networkDriver --> vlanNet[Network VLAN OVS VXLAN]
    monitorDriver --> metricsDB[(Metrics DB)]
    kvmHost --> monitorDriver
    vmwareHost --> monitorDriver
    xenHost --> monitorDriver
    onedCore --> haManager[HA Manager Heartbeat]
    haManager --> onedCore
```

## 4.2 VM Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PENDING : User submits VM
    PENDING --> HOLD : User holds VM
    PENDING --> PROLOG : Scheduler assigns host
    HOLD --> PENDING : User releases
    PROLOG --> RUNNING : VM booted successfully
    PROLOG --> FAILED : Resource error
    RUNNING --> MIGRATE : Live migration
    MIGRATE --> RUNNING : Migration complete
    MIGRATE --> POWEROFF : Migration failed
    RUNNING --> POWEROFF : User poweroff
    RUNNING --> SUSPENDED : User suspend
    POWEROFF --> RUNNING : User resume
    SUSPENDED --> RUNNING : User resume
    RUNNING --> DONE : User terminate
    POWEROFF --> DONE : User terminate
    SUSPENDED --> DONE : User terminate
    DONE --> [*]
    FAILED --> [*]
```

## 4.3 OpenNebula Federation Architecture (Hybrid Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LocalZoneA[Local Zone A Enterprise DC]
        onedA[oned Master A]
        hostA1[Host 1]
        hostA2[Host 2]
    end
    subgraph LocalZoneB[Local Zone B Branch Office]
        onedB[oned Master B]
        hostB1[Host 1]
    end
    subgraph MasterZone[Master Zone Cloud Broker]
        masterOned[oned Master Hub]
    end
    subgraph PublicCloud[Public Cloud Bursting]
        awsInst[AWS EC2]
        azureInst[Azure VM]
    end
    onedA --> masterOned
    onedB --> masterOned
    masterOned --> awsInst
    masterOned --> azureInst
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Sequence (VM Creation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Sunstone/CLI
    participant D as oned Daemon
    participant DB as Database
    participant SC as Scheduler
    participant DR as Driver
    participant H as Host
    U->>S: onevm create template
    S->>D: XML-RPC API call
    D->>DB: Persist VM record
    D->>SC: Trigger scheduling
    SC->>SC: Rank hosts
    SC-->>D: Selected host
    D->>DR: Deploy VM via SSH
    DR->>H: Copy image + boot VM
    H-->>DR: VM started
    DR-->>D: Status RUNNING
    D->>DB: Update state
    D-->>S: Success response
    S-->>U: VM ready
```

## 4.5 Storage Datastore Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    systemDS[System Datastore] --> imageRepo[Image Repository]
    systemDS --> imageRepo
    imageRepo --> img1[Base Image Linux]
    imageRepo --> img2[Base Image Windows]
    imageRepo --> img3[ISO Image]
    fileDS[File Datastore] --> vmDisk1[VM Disk 1]
    fileDS --> vmDisk2[VM Disk 2]
    img1 --> vmDisk1
    img2 --> vmDisk2
    img1 -.Copy on Write.-> vmDisk1
    img2 -.Copy on Write.-> vmDisk2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
