# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define OpenNebula and list its key features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **OpenNebula** is an open-source cloud computing platform that enables the management of virtualized data centers, providing Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) capabilities for private, public, and hybrid cloud deployments.

**Key Features (any 3 for 3 marks):**
1. **Open-source** under Apache 2.0 license
2. Supports **multiple hypervisors** (KVM, VMware, Xen, Hyper-V)
3. **Hybrid cloud** federation with public providers
4. Lightweight, single-binary installation
5. Standards-compliant APIs (OCCI, EC2, vCloud)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the OpenNebula scheduler (Match-Making).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The OpenNebula scheduler, also called the **Match-Making scheduler**, is responsible for **selecting the most appropriate physical host** to deploy a virtual machine based on resource availability, current load, and predefined policies.

**Key Functions:**
- Evaluates all candidate hosts using a **weighted ranking algorithm** (1 mark)
- Considers CPU, memory, network, and load metrics (1 mark)
- Implements **scheduling policies** such as packing, striping, load-aware, and priority-based (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a) Describe the three-tier architecture of OpenNebula with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The OpenNebula architecture is organized into three distinct tiers, each with specific responsibilities:

**Tier 1: Front-end (Management Layer)** (2 marks)
- Acts as the centralized control plane of the entire cloud.
- Hosts the `oned` daemon, the scheduler, and the Sunstone GUI.
- Manages authentication, authorization, and quota policies.
- Receives user requests via CLI, Sunstone, or API.

**Tier 2: Core Services (Middleware Layer)** (2 marks)
- Maintains the OpenNebula database (VM templates, user accounts, datastores).
- Coordinates the four driver types: Virtualization, Storage, Network, Monitoring.
- Implements the scheduling logic and resource allocation.

**Tier 3: Hosts (Physical Resources)** (3 marks)
- Physical servers running supported hypervisors (KVM, VMware, Xen).
- Execute VMs based on commands from the front-end.
- Communicate via SSH and driver scripts.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram.

---

#### **(b) Explain the various VM lifecycle states in OpenNebula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

OpenNebula manages VMs through a well-defined state machine consisting of the following states (each state 1 mark; transitions 1 mark):

1. **PENDING**: VM is submitted and waiting for scheduler to assign a host.
2. **HOLD**: VM is on hold (admin action); not scheduled.
3. **PROLOG**: Resources are being prepared — image copy, network setup.
4. **RUNNING**: VM is active and operational on the host.
5. **MIGRATE**: Live migration to another host is in progress.
6. **POWEROFF**: VM is halted but its resources are retained.
7. **SUSPENDED**: VM state saved to disk; can be resumed later.
8. **DONE**: VM has been terminated; awaiting cleanup.
9. **FAILED**: VM encountered an error during operation.

**State Transitions:** Refer to Section 4.2 state machine diagram. Key transitions include: PENDING → PROLOG → RUNNING (normal path), RUNNING → MIGRATE → RUNNING (live migration), and any active state → DONE (termination).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

#### **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a) Compare OpenNebula, OpenStack, and Eucalyptus as cloud platforms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | OpenNebula | OpenStack | Eucalyptus |
|-----------|------------|-----------|------------|
| **Architecture** | Centralized | Distributed (microservices) | Hybrid |
| **Origin** | Research (2005, UCM) | NASA + Rackspace (2010) | UCSB Research |
| **Complexity** | Low | High | Medium |
| **API Standards** | OCCI, EC2, vCloud | REST (Nova API) | EC2-compatible |
| **Hypervisor Support** | KVM, Xen, VMware, Hyper-V | KVM, Xen, Hyper-V, VMware | KVM, Xen, VMware |
| **Database** | SQLite/MySQL | MySQL/PostgreSQL | MySQL |
| **Best Suited For** | Private + Hybrid Cloud | Large-scale Public Cloud | AWS-compatible Private Cloud |
| **Installation** | Single binary, lightweight | Multi-node, complex | Moderate setup |
| **Community** | C12G Labs + community | OpenStack Foundation | Eucalyptus Systems |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** OpenNebula is best for organizations seeking a lightweight, simple-to-deploy, hybrid cloud solution, while OpenStack is ideal for large-scale clouds and Eucalyptus for AWS-compatible environments.

---

#### **(b) Explain OpenNebula's hybrid cloud federation model with its components. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
OpenNebula's federation model enables multiple independent OpenNebula instances (zones) to be coordinated centrally to build a multi-cloud or hybrid cloud environment. A **Master Zone** acts as a cloud broker, orchestrating workloads across multiple **Local Zones** and optionally extending to public clouds.

**Components (4 marks):**
1. **Master Zone**: Central hub that coordinates all local zones; routes user requests.
2. **Local Zones**: Independent OpenNebula deployments with their own resources.
3. **Federation User**: A unified identity that works across all zones.
4. **SLA-driven Offloading**: Workloads are offloaded to remote zones/public clouds based on resource availability, cost, and policy.

**Workflow (1 mark):**
When a user submits a VM request to the Master Zone:
- If local resources suffice → deployed in local zone.
- If not → Master Zone offloads to another local zone or public cloud (AWS/Azure).

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 federation architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing OpenNebula's "front-end" with a hypervisor. The front-end is a **management server**, NOT where VMs run.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to mention the **Apache 2.0 license** in definitions; KTU examiners specifically check for this.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Writing only "scheduling" for the Match-Maker; you must explain the **ranking algorithm** with at least one formula.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Skipping VM lifecycle states — KTU 2024 papers have direct 7-mark questions on this.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Comparing OpenNebula with OpenStack but mixing up "centralized vs distributed" architecture — these are KEY differentiators.
> - **Common Mistake 6**: Not drawing diagrams — in 7-mark questions, a labeled diagram is **MANDATORY** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OpenNebula (Module 3)**

### **Definition & Identity**
- ☐ OpenNebula is an **open-source (Apache 2.0)** IaaS cloud platform
- ☐ Manages **virtualized data centers** (private + hybrid)
- ☐ Origin: Research project, currently maintained by **C12G Labs**

### **Architecture**
- ☐ **Three-tier**: Front-end, Core (Middleware), Hosts
- ☐ `oned` daemon = core service; **Sunstone** = web UI
- ☐ Default API port: **2633** (XML-RPC)

### **Key Components**
- ☐ **Scheduler (Match-Making)** uses weighted rank formula
- ☐ **Four Driver Types**: Virtualization, Storage, Network, Monitoring
- ☐ **Sunstone**: Sinatra-based web GUI

### **Hypervisors & Storage**
- ☐ Supports **KVM, VMware vCenter, Xen, Hyper-V**
- ☐ Storage backends: **NFS, LVM, Ceph, GlusterFS**
- ☐ Image formats: raw, qcow2, vmdk, ISO

### **VM Lifecycle States (MEMORIZE)**
- ☐ `pend → hold → prol → runn → migr/poff/susp → done/fail`

### **Federation**
- ☐ Master Zone + Local Zones model
- ☐ Enables **hybrid cloud bursting** to AWS/Azure

### **Comparison Triggers**
- ☐ OpenNebula vs OpenStack: **Centralized vs Distributed**
- ☐ OpenNebula vs Eucalyptus: **Hybrid vs AWS-focused**

### **KTU-Standard Keywords to Use**
- ☐ "IaaS", "hypervisor abstraction", "multi-tenancy", "federation", "match-making scheduler", "Apache 2.0", "Sunstone", "oned daemon", "driver framework"

### **High-Yield Formulas**
- ☐ Rank: $R = W_{\text{CPU}} \cdot C + W_{\text{MEM}} \cdot M - W_{\text{LOAD}} \cdot L$
- ☐ Overcommit: $\rho = V_{\text{allocated}} / V_{\text{physical}}$

### **Common Exam Traps to Avoid**
- ☐ Don't confuse **datastore** (storage pool) with **image** (VM disk)
- ☐ Don't say OpenNebula "creates" VMs — it **orchestrates** them
- ☐ Always mention the **scheduler ranking algorithm** in scheduling questions

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nimbus. Popular Public Cloud Platforms: AWS - AWS Ecosystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nimbus. Popular Public Cloud Platforms: AWS - AWS Ecosystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nimbus — Open-Source Cloud Computing Toolkit

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)
> **Nimbus** is an open-source **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** cloud computing toolkit originally developed at the **University of Chicago** (under the Scientific Cloud Computing effort) and later donated to the Apache Software Foundation ecosystem. It enables users to deploy and manage virtualized compute, storage, and networking resources on commodity hardware, serving as both a production-grade cloud platform and a research testbed for cloud computing experimentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Nimbus is studied as a representative **open-source IaaS alternative** to commercial platforms like AWS. It is the spiritual successor to the early cloud experiments (Globus/Nimbus project) and supports the **EC2-compatible WSRF (Web Service Resource Framework)** and **EC2 front-end** interfaces.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Open Hardware Rental Shop"
Think of Nimbus as a **community-owned, transparent hardware rental shop**:
- The **shopkeeper** (Nimbus Service) lets you rent out physical machines.
- You do **not** own the machines — you provision them as **virtual machines (VMs)** on demand.
- The shopkeeper keeps a **ledger (Service Registry)** of all available hardware.
- Unlike a commercial shop (AWS), **the blueprints (source code) of the shop are public**, so any university, research lab, or company can build their own identical shop.

This is exactly the philosophy of Nimbus: **democratizing cloud infrastructure by open-sourcing the entire cloud stack**.

---

## 1.2 AWS (Amazon Web Services) — The AWS Ecosystem

### Formal Definition
> **AWS (Amazon Web Services)** is a comprehensive, evolving, and secure **public cloud computing platform** offered by Amazon.com. It provides a mixture of **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** offerings across more than **200 fully featured services** from data centers globally. AWS services span compute, storage, databases, networking, analytics, machine learning, IoT, security, and developer tools — collectively referred to as the **AWS Ecosystem**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "City of Utilities"
Imagine a vast, modern city:
- **Electricity** (EC2 Compute) — flick a switch, get power on demand.
- **Water reservoir** (S3 Storage) — unlimited tank, pay per litre.
- **Post office** (SQS Messaging) — guaranteed delivery of messages.
- **City planning department** (VPC Networking) — design your own private lanes and streets.
- **Police force** (IAM Security) — guards, identity cards, and access rules.
- **City map & GPS** (Route 53, CloudFront) — find the fastest path globally.
- **Mayor’s office** (AWS Management Console) — one dashboard to rule the city.

You **never build a power plant** — you just plug in. That is the essence of AWS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core AWS Characteristics to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **On-demand provisioning** (minutes to deploy)
> - **Pay-as-you-go pricing** (no upfront capital expenditure)
> - **Elastic scalability** (scale up/down automatically)
> - **Global infrastructure** (Regions, Availability Zones, Edge Locations)
> - **Shared Responsibility Model** for security
> - **Multi-tenancy** with isolated virtual resources

### Nimbus vs. AWS — Quick Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Nimbus | AWS |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Model** | Private / Hybrid (self-hosted) | Public (managed by Amazon) |
| **License** | Open-source (Apache) | Proprietary (Commercial) |
| **Target Audience** | Researchers, Academia, Private cloud builders | Enterprises, Startups, Public users |
| **Hardware** | Commodity on-premise servers | Amazon-owned hyperscale data centers |
| **Interface Compatibility** | EC2-compatible (AWS API emulation) | Native AWS APIs |
| **Global Reach** | Limited to operator’s sites | 30+ Regions, 100+ Availability Zones |
| **Pricing Model** | Free (software) + hardware cost | Pay-per-use (compute hours, GB stored, requests) |
| **SLA Guarantee** | Operator-defined | 99.9% to 99.99% depending on service |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nimbus — Architectural Deep Dive

The Nimbus ecosystem is composed of several loosely-coupled components. Understanding these is **high-yield** for KTU theory questions.

### 2.1.1 Nimbus Core Components

**1. Workspace Service (a.k.a. Workspace Control)**
- The **heart of Nimbus** — accepts user requests to deploy VMs.
- Supports both **EC2-style elastic VMs** and **workspace (persistent) VMs**.
- Exposes both **EC2 Query API** and **WS-Resource Framework** endpoints.

**2. Workspace Control Client (`workspace-client.sh`)**
- The **command-line tool** users invoke to:
  - Create / launch a workspace
  - Destroy / shutdown a workspace
  - List available deployments
- Mirrors AWS CLI behavior.

**3. Service Registration / Context Broker**
- Maintains the **catalogue of available VM images** and node configurations.
- Acts as a **metadata service** similar to AWS EC2 metadata service (`169.254.169.254`).
- Provides **hostname → IP resolution** within the cloud.

**4. Cloud Client (EC2 Front-end)**
- Allows Nimbus to **emulate the EC2 API surface** — meaning existing AWS tools (boto, ec2-api-tools) can be used against a Nimbus cloud.

**5. Hypervisor Backends**
- Supports **Xen** (historically) and **KVM** (modern default).
- Manages physical-to-virtual resource mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nimbus matters for KTU:**
> It demonstrates that an IaaS cloud is **not magic** — it is a combination of a **service front-end**, a **resource registry**, a **scheduler**, and a **hypervisor**. This is the same pattern AWS follows internally (just at a vastly larger scale).

---

## 2.2 AWS Ecosystem — Service Categories

AWS organizes its 200+ services into logical **categories**. KTU questions frequently test these groupings.

### 2.2.1 The Seven Pillars of the AWS Ecosystem

**Pillar 1 — Compute**
- **EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud):** Resizable virtual servers in the cloud.
- **Lambda:** Serverless, event-driven function execution.
- **ECS / EKS / Fargate:** Container orchestration (Docker / Kubernetes / serverless containers).
- **Elastic Beanstalk:** PaaS for quick web app deployment.
- **Lightsail:** Simplified VPS for small workloads.

**Pillar 2 — Storage**
- **S3 (Simple Storage Service):** Object storage — virtually unlimited buckets of files.
- **EBS (Elastic Block Store):** Block-level volumes attached to EC2.
- **EFS (Elastic File System):** Shared NFS-style file system.
- **FSx:** High-performance Lustre / Windows file systems.
- **Glacier / Glacier Deep Archive:** Cold archival storage (pennies per GB/month).

**Pillar 3 — Database**
- **RDS (Relational Database Service):** Managed MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB, Aurora.
- **DynamoDB:** Managed NoSQL key-value / document database.
- **Redshift:** Petabyte-scale data warehouse.
- **Aurora:** Cloud-native relational engine (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible).
- **Neptune:** Graph database.
- **DocumentDB:** MongoDB-compatible document DB.
- **ElastiCache:** In-memory caching (Redis, Memcached).

**Pillar 4 — Networking & Content Delivery**
- **VPC (Virtual Private Cloud):** Isolated private network inside AWS.
- **Route 53:** Scalable DNS and domain registration.
- **CloudFront:** Global CDN (Content Delivery Network).
- **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB):** Distributes traffic across multiple instances.
- **Direct Connect:** Dedicated network link from on-premise to AWS.
- **API Gateway:** Front-door for REST/WebSocket APIs.

**Pillar 5 — Security, Identity & Compliance**
- **IAM (Identity and Access Management):** Users, groups, roles, policies.
- **KMS (Key Management Service):** Managed encryption keys.
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall):** Filter malicious HTTP traffic.
- **Shield:** DDoS protection.
- **Cognito:** User authentication for mobile/web apps.
- **Secrets Manager:** Secure storage of API keys, passwords.

**Pillar 6 — Management & Governance**
- **CloudWatch:** Metrics, logs, alarms, dashboards.
- **CloudFormation:** Infrastructure-as-Code (JSON/YAML templates).
- **AWS Auto Scaling:** Automatically adjust compute capacity.
- **Trusted Advisor:** Cost, performance, security recommendations.
- **CloudTrail:** API audit logging.
- **Config:** Resource configuration tracking.

**Pillar 7 — Analytics, ML, IoT, DevOps (Specialized Domains)**
- **Athena:** Serverless SQL queries on S3.
- **EMR:** Managed Hadoop/Spark big-data framework.
- **SageMaker:** Managed machine learning.
- **Rekognition:** Image/video analysis (computer vision).
- **IoT Core:** Bidirectional communication between IoT devices and cloud.
- **CodePipeline / CodeBuild / CodeDeploy:** CI/CD tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy** (must memorize for KTU):
> **Region → Availability Zone (AZ) → Edge Location / Local Zone**
> - A **Region** is a physical geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` = Mumbai).
> - An **Availability Zone** is one or more discrete data centers within a Region, with redundant power and networking.
> - **Edge Locations** are CDN endpoints used by CloudFront for low-latency content delivery.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all tables below, the vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is rendered using **\vert** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.3.1 Cloud Pricing Formulas (Direct Cost Models)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand EC2 Cost** | $C_{od} = R_h \times T_{used}$ | USD | $R_h$ = hourly rate, $T_{used}$ = hours of usage |
| **Reserved Instance Cost** | $C_{ri} = U + (R_h^{ri} \times T_{used})$ | USD | $U$ = upfront fee, $R_h^{ri}$ = discounted hourly rate |
| **Spot Instance Cost** | $C_{spot} = R_{spot} \times T_{used}$ | USD | $R_{spot}$ = current spot price (varies) |
| **S3 Storage Cost** | $C_{S3} = S \times P_{GB}$ | USD/month | $S$ = stored GB, $P_{GB}$ = per-GB monthly price |
| **Data Transfer Out Cost** | $C_{dt} = D_{out} \times P_{dt}$ | USD | $D_{out}$ = GB transferred out, $P_{dt}$ = per-GB price |
| **Total Monthly TCO** | $TCO = \sum (C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{dt} + C_{other})$ | USD/month | Sum of all cost components |

### 2.3.2 Availability & SLA Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Availability (%)** | $A = \dfrac{U}{U + D} \times 100$ | $U$ = uptime, $D$ = downtime (same period) |
| **Downtime from SLA** | $D = T \times \left(1 - \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$ | $T$ = total time period (e.g., 1 month = 43,200 min) |
| **Composite SLA** | $A_{composite} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | For services in **series**; product of individual SLAs |
| **Parallel Redundancy** | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$ | For redundant services in **parallel** |

### 2.3.3 Scalability & Performance Metrics

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Scalability Ratio** | $S_{ratio} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{base}}$ | $P_{max}$ = peak performance, $P_{base}$ = baseline |
| **Cost per User** | $C_{user} = \dfrac{TCO}{N_{users}}$ | USD per active user |
| **Storage Utilization** | $U_{storage} = \dfrac{S_{used}}{S_{provisioned}} \times 100$ | Percentage of provisioned storage used |
| **Elastic Response Time** | $T_{elastic} = T_{detect} + T_{provision}$ | Time to detect load + time to provision new instances |

### 2.3.4 Nimbus Specific Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Workspace Creation Time** | $T_{ws} = T_{schedule} + T_{boot} + T_{register}$ | Sum of scheduling, OS boot, and registry update |
| **Resource Utilization** | $U_{node} = \dfrac{\sum VM_{allocated}}{P_{node}} \times 100$ | % of physical node resources allocated to VMs |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Startups** | AWS enables a 2-person startup to launch globally scalable apps without buying a single server (e.g., Airbnb started on AWS). |
| **Scientific Research** | Nimbus is used in bioinformatics, particle physics (e.g., Nimbus on FutureGrid), and genomics — researchers get elastic compute without depending on AWS budgets. |
| **Enterprise Migration** | Companies like Netflix, Capital One, and NASA migrated entire IT estates to AWS. |
| **Disaster Recovery** | AWS regions act as geographically separated DR sites — using parallel redundancy formulas, 99.99% availability becomes practically achievable. |
| **IoT & Edge** | AWS IoT + Greengrass brings cloud intelligence to factory floors and remote sensors. |
| **Education** | Universities deploy private Nimbus clouds to teach virtualization, scheduling, and IaaS concepts hands-on. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Monthly EC2 On-Demand Cost Calculation

### Problem Statement
A KTU final-year project team uses an `t3.medium` EC2 instance running 24/7 for a month (30 days).
- Hourly On-Demand rate: $0.0416$ USD/hour
- Data transfer out: **500 GB/month** at $0.09$ USD/GB
- EBS storage: **100 GB** at $0.10$ USD/GB/month

**Find the total monthly bill.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute hours in the month.**

The instance runs 24 hours a day for 30 days.

$$
T_{used} = 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 720 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EC2 compute cost.**

Using the On-Demand formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
C_{od} = R_h \times T_{used}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{od} = 0.0416 \text{ USD/hour} \times 720 \text{ hours}
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
C_{od} = 29.952 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EBS storage cost.**

Using the S3/EBS storage cost formula:

$$
C_{EBS} = S \times P_{GB}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{EBS} = 100 \text{ GB} \times 0.10 \text{ USD/GB}
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
C_{EBS} = 10.00 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute data transfer cost.**

$$
C_{dt} = D_{out} \times P_{dt}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{dt} = 500 \text{ GB} \times 0.09 \text{ USD/GB}
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
C_{dt} = 45.00 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total TCO.**

$$
TCO = C_{od} + C_{EBS} + C_{dt}
$$

$$
TCO = 29.952 + 10.00 + 45.00
$$

$$
\boxed{TCO = 84.952 \text{ USD/month}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show units. Examiners award partial marks for **unit consistency** even if the final number is slightly off.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SLA Composite Availability (Series Composition)

### Problem Statement
A web application architecture has the following components in a request path:
- **ELB (Elastic Load Balancer)**: SLA = $99.99\%$
- **EC2 Instance**: SLA = $99.95\%$
- **RDS Database**: SLA = $99.95\%$
- **S3 Bucket**: SLA = $99.9\%$

**Find the composite availability. Comment on whether this meets a $99.9\%$ target.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify series vs. parallel.**

In a request path, every component must succeed → **series composition**. Use the product rule:

$$
A_{composite} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
A_{composite} = 0.9999 \times 0.9995 \times 0.9995 \times 0.999
$$

**Step 3 — Perform multiplication step by step.**

First, compute $0.9999 \times 0.9995$:

$$
0.9999 \times 0.9995 = 0.99940005
$$

Next, multiply by the second $0.9995$:

$$
0.99940005 \times 0.9995 = 0.99890040
$$

Finally, multiply by $0.999$:

$$
0.99890040 \times 0.999 = 0.99790150
$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage.**

$$
A_{composite} \approx 99.790\% \approx 99.79\%
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to downtime per month.**

Using the downtime formula:

$$
D = T \times \left(1 - \dfrac{A}{100}\right)
$$

For one month ($T = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43,200$ minutes):

$$
D = 43200 \times (1 - 0.9979)
$$

$$
D = 43200 \times 0.0021
$$

$$
D = 90.72 \text{ minutes/month}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict.**

$$
\boxed{A_{composite} = 99.79\%, \quad D \approx 90.7 \text{ min/month}}
$$

**Comment:** The composite falls **below** the $99.9\%$ target → we must introduce **redundancy** (multi-AZ deployment) to compensate.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Parallel Redundancy (Multi-AZ Failover)

### Problem Statement
The team deploys **two EC2 instances in parallel** (active-active in two AZs), each with $99.95\%$ availability. The system is considered "down" only if **both** fail simultaneously.

**Find the new parallel composite availability.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the parallel redundancy formula.**

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)
$$

For $n = 2$ instances, $A_1 = A_2 = 0.9995$:

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - [(1 - 0.9995) \times (1 - 0.9995)]
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the inner product.**

$$
(1 - 0.9995) = 0.0005
$$

$$
0.0005 \times 0.0005 = 0.00000025 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute final availability.**

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - 0.00000025
$$

$$
A_{parallel} = 0.99999975
$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage.**

$$
\boxed{A_{parallel} = 99.999975\% \quad (\text{commonly called "four nines plus"})}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute new downtime per year.**

$$
T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525,600 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
D_{year} = 525600 \times (1 - 0.99999975) = 525600 \times 0.00000025 = 0.1314 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
D_{year} \approx 7.88 \text{ seconds/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why AWS recommends **multi-AZ deployments** — you trade 2× the compute cost for **astronomically better** availability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Choosing AWS Service

| Scenario | Recommended AWS Service | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Static website hosting with global low-latency | S3 + CloudFront | S3 stores objects; CloudFront caches at edge locations. |
| Stateful database with ACID transactions | RDS (Aurora) | Managed RDBMS with automatic failover and backups. |
| Unstructured key-value lookups at millions of TPS | DynamoDB | Single-digit-millisecond latency, fully serverless. |
| Running custom Linux processes on demand | EC2 | Full OS control, any software stack. |
| Event-driven function execution (image resize on upload) | Lambda | Pay per invocation, no server management. |
| Containerized microservices | ECS / EKS | Native Docker / Kubernetes orchestration. |
| Long-term compliance logs (7-year retention) | S3 Glacier Deep Archive | $0.00099/GB/month — cheapest storage tier. |
| Private isolated network inside AWS | VPC | Custom IP ranges, subnets, route tables, NAT. |
| Centralized user authentication | Cognito / IAM | Handles millions of users with OAuth / SAML / OpenID. |
| Infrastructure defined as code | CloudFormation | Reproducible, version-controlled stacks. |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nimbus Reference Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **high-level Nimbus cloud architecture**, showing how a user request flows through the various components to finally instantiate a virtual machine on a physical node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User / Researcher] -->|SSH / CLI| cliA[Workspace Client]
    cliA -->|API Request| wsA[Workspace Service]
    wsA -->|Lookup Image Metadata| ctxA[Context Broker]
    ctxA -->|Return Image ID| wsA
    wsA -->|Schedule VM| schedA[Scheduler]
    schedA -->|Select Node| hypA[Hypervisor Backend XEN KVM]
    hypA -->|Boot VM| vmA1[VM Instance 1]
    hypA -->|Boot VM| vmA2[VM Instance 2]
    hypA -->|Boot VM| vmA3[VM Instance 3]
    vmA1 -->|Heartbeat| monA[Resource Monitor]
    vmA2 -->|Heartbeat| monA
    vmA3 -->|Heartbeat| monA
    monA -->|Update Status| ctxA
    schedA -.->|Poll Resources| monA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The user issues a command via the **Workspace Client**.
- The request hits the **Workspace Service** (front-door).
- The **Context Broker** provides the image metadata and tracks running VMs.
- The **Scheduler** picks a physical node and instructs the **Hypervisor** to boot a VM.
- VMs report health back to the **Resource Monitor**, which updates the Context Broker.

---

## 4.2 AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    globeA[AWS Global Cloud] --> r1A[Region US East 1]
    globeA --> r2A[Region EU West 1]
    globeA --> r3A[Region AP South 1 Mumbai]
    globeA --> r4A[Region AP Southeast 1 Singapore]

    r3A --> az1A[Availability Zone AZ1 Mumbai]
    r3A --> az2A[Availability Zone AZ2 Mumbai]
    r3A --> az3A[Availability Zone AZ3 Mumbai]

    az1A --> dc1A[Data Center 1]
    az1A --> dc2A[Data Center 2]
    az2A --> dc3A[Data Center 3]
    az2A --> dc4A[Data Center 4]

    globeA --> edgeA1[Edge Location Chennai]
    globeA --> edgeA2[Edge Location Bangalore]
    globeA --> edgeA3[Edge Location Delhi]

    dc1A -.->|Serves Cached Content| edgeA1
    dc3A -.->|Serves Cached Content| edgeA2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Globe** represents the entire AWS cloud.
- Each **Region** is an independent geographic area (e.g., Mumbai).
- Each Region contains **multiple Availability Zones** (physically separate data centers).
- Each AZ contains **one or more data centers**.
- **Edge Locations** are CDN endpoints scattered across cities, caching content from the data centers for low-latency delivery.

---

## 4.3 AWS Service Ecosystem — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEC_AWS_COMPUTE[Compute Layer]
        cmpA[EC2]
        cmpB[Lambda]
        cmpC[ECS EKS Fargate]
        cmpD[Elastic Beanstalk]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS_STORAGE[Storage Layer]
        stA[S3 Object Storage]
        stB[EBS Block Storage]
        stC[Glacier Cold Archive]
        stD[EFS Shared File System]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS_DATABASE[Database Layer]
        dbA[RDS Aurora]
        dbB[DynamoDB]
        dbC[Redshift Data Warehouse]
        dbD[ElastiCache Redis]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS_NETWORK[Network Layer]
        ntA[VPC]
        ntB[Route 53 DNS]
        ntC[CloudFront CDN]
        ntD[API Gateway]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS_SECURITY[Security Layer]
        scA[IAM Identity]
        scB[KMS Encryption Keys]
        scC[WAF Web Firewall]
        scD[Shield DDoS Protection]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS_MGMT[Management Layer]
        mgA[CloudWatch Monitoring]
        mgB[CloudFormation IaC]
        mgC[Auto Scaling]
        mgD[CloudTrail Audit]
    end

    cmpA --- ntA
    cmpB --- ntA
    stA --- dbA
    dbA --- ntA
    scA --- cmpA
    mgA --- cmpA
    mgA --- dbA
    mgB --- cmpA
    mgB --- stA
    mgB --- dbA
    ntC --- stA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- AWS services are organized into **six functional layers** (Compute, Storage, Database, Network, Security, Management).
- Arrows show typical service interactions: e.g., EC2 sits inside a VPC, CloudWatch monitors all layers, CloudFormation provisions all layers.
- A real production workload usually **touches services from 4–5 layers simultaneously**.

---

## 4.4 AWS Shared Responsibility Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEC_CUSTOMER[Customer Responsibility]
        c1A[OS Patching]
        c2A[Application Security]
        c3A[IAM User Policies]
        c4A[Data Encryption Keys]
        c5A[Network ACL Configuration]
    end

    subgraph SEC_AWS[AWS Responsibility]
        a1A[Physical Data Center Security]
        a2A[Hardware Maintenance]
        a3A[Hypervisor Patching]
        a4A[Network Infrastructure]
        a5A[Storage Disposal]
    end

    customerA[Customer] --> c1A
    customerA --> c2A
    customerA --> c3A
    customerA --> c4A
    customerA --> c5A

    amazonA[Amazon AWS] --> a1A
    amazonA --> a2A
    amazonA --> a3A
    amazonA --> a4A
    amazonA --> a5A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud"** — physical infrastructure, hardware, hypervisor.
- **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud"** — OS, app code, identity policies, data encryption, network configuration.
- The boundary lies **above the hypervisor and below the OS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Statement:** *"AWS secures the infrastructure; the customer secures what they put in it."* — This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU Module 3.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define Nimbus. List any two key components of the Nimbus toolkit.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Nimbus** is an open-source **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** cloud computing toolkit developed originally at the University of Chicago and used to set up private or research cloud environments on commodity hardware.
>
> **Two key components:**
> 1. **Workspace Service** — accepts user requests to deploy/manage virtual machines and exposes EC2-compatible and WSRF interfaces.
> 2. **Context Broker** — maintains the registry of available VM images, node configurations, and active workspaces (acts as a metadata service).
>
> *(Other acceptable components: Workspace Client, Cloud Client, Hypervisor Backend, Service Registration.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the AWS Shared Responsibility Model? Briefly explain the responsibilities of AWS and the customer.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **AWS Shared Responsibility Model** defines the split of security duties between Amazon (the cloud provider) and the customer (the cloud user).
>
> **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud":**
> - Physical security of data centers
> - Hardware and network infrastructure maintenance
> - Hypervisor patching
> - Storage device disposal
>
> **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud":**
> - Operating system patching on EC2
> - Application code and configuration
> - IAM user and role policies
> - Data encryption (in-transit and at-rest)
> - Network ACL and security group rules
>
> **Boundary:** The responsibility line lies between the **hypervisor layer** (AWS-owned) and the **guest OS layer** (customer-owned). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Part B Module-3 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically worth 7 marks. Students must attempt **one full question** (either A or B).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain the **AWS Global Infrastructure** in detail. Describe Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Compute the **composite availability** of a system that uses ELB ($99.99\%$), one EC2 instance ($99.95\%$), and one RDS database ($99.95\%$) in series. If the team now deploys **two EC2 instances in parallel** (each $99.95\%$) in place of the single instance, find the **new composite availability** and comment on the improvement. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**AWS Global Infrastructure** is the physical and logical foundation on which all AWS services run. It is organized in three layers:

**1. Regions (7 Marks breakdown begins)**

A **Region** is a **physical geographic location** in the world where AWS clusters data centers. Examples:
- `ap-south-1` → Mumbai, India
- `us-east-1` → Northern Virginia, USA
- `eu-west-1` → Ireland

Each Region is **completely independent** — resources in one Region do not automatically replicate to another. Regions are chosen to:
- Bring compute closer to end users (reduce latency)
- Meet data residency / compliance requirements
- Provide disaster recovery across geographies

**2. Availability Zones (AZs)**

Each Region contains **2 or more AZs**. An AZ is **one or more discrete data centers** with:
- Redundant power (independent grids)
- Redundant networking (fiber paths)
- Independent cooling and physical security

AZs within a Region are connected via **low-latency private fiber links** (typically < 1 ms RTT). A customer can deploy resources across multiple AZs to achieve **high availability** — if one AZ fails, the workload continues in the other.

**3. Edge Locations**

Edge locations are **CDN endpoints** used primarily by **Amazon CloudFront** to cache content close to end users. There are **400+ edge locations** worldwide (more than Regions). They are used for:
- Static and streaming content delivery
- Route 53 DNS queries
- AWS Shield DDoS mitigation at the edge

**Diagram** (already provided in Section 4.2) showing the three-tier hierarchy should accompany the answer. **[Total: 7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the original series composite availability.**

The components are: ELB, EC2, RDS — all in **series** (the request must pass through all three).

Using the formula:

$$
A_{composite} = A_{ELB} \times A_{EC2} \times A_{RDS}
$$

$$
A_{composite} = 0.9999 \times 0.9995 \times 0.9995
$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$
0.9999 \times 0.9995 = 0.99940005
$$

$$
0.99940005 \times 0.9995 = 0.99890040
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{composite} = 99.89\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute new availability with two parallel EC2 instances.**

The new architecture is: **ELB in series with [2 parallel EC2s] in series with RDS**.

First, compute the parallel EC2 availability:

$$
A_{EC2,parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.9995)^2
$$

$$
A_{EC2,parallel} = 1 - (0.0005)^2
$$

$$
A_{EC2,parallel} = 1 - 0.00000025
$$

$$
A_{EC2,parallel} = 0.99999975
$$

Now compute the new series composite with ELB and RDS:

$$
A_{new} = 0.9999 \times 0.99999975 \times 0.9995
$$

$$
A_{new} = 0.9999 \times 0.9995 = 0.99940005
$$

$$
A_{new} = 0.99940005 \times 0.99999975 = 0.99939980
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{new} = 99.94\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the improvement.**

- Original: $99.89\%$ → ~$94$ min downtime/month
- Improved: $99.94\%$ → ~$26$ min downtime/month
- **Improvement:** ~$68$ minutes less downtime per month, achieved by adding redundancy (one extra EC2 instance).

> **Engineering conclusion:** Parallel redundancy gives **super-linear** availability improvement at the cost of **linear** resource cost. This is the cornerstone of cloud-native HA design. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Compare **Nimbus and AWS** as cloud computing platforms. Discuss their licensing, target audience, and architectural approach. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A startup uses **S3 to store 5 TB of data** at $0.023$ USD/GB/month, with **20,000 GET requests/day** at $0.0004$ USD per 1,000 requests, and **1 TB/month of data transfer out** at $0.09$ USD/GB. Calculate the **total monthly bill** and the **cost per GB stored per user**, assuming the data serves **10,000 active users**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Parameter | Nimbus | AWS |
|---|---|---|
| **License** | Open-source (Apache-style) | Proprietary, commercial |
| **Origin** | University of Chicago research project | Amazon.com (commercial) |
| **Target Audience** | Academics, researchers, private cloud operators | Enterprises, startups, government, public users |
| **Deployment** | Self-hosted on on-premise hardware | Fully managed by Amazon in global data centers |
| **API Compatibility** | EC2-compatible front-end (mimics AWS APIs) | Native AWS APIs (de facto industry standard) |
| **Service Breadth** | Limited to IaaS (compute + storage) | 200+ services across IaaS, PaaS, SaaS |
| **SLA** | Operator-defined (no commercial guarantee) | 99.9% – 99.99% backed by service credits |
| **Pricing** | Free software; pays for hardware/electricity | Pay-as-you-go (compute hours, GB stored, requests) |
| **Innovation Speed** | Slow (community-driven) | Very fast (continuous new service launches) |
| **Educational Value** | High — exposes internal cloud mechanics | Low — abstracts internal mechanisms |
| **Global Reach** | Limited to operator’s sites | 30+ Regions, 100+ AZs, 400+ edge locations |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Low (open standards) | High (proprietary services) |

**Architectural Approach:**
- **Nimbus** uses a **modular, research-friendly** architecture: separate Workspace Service, Context Broker, and Hypervisor backend. Each component is replaceable and customizable.
- **AWS** uses a **monolithic, hyper-optimized, multi-tenant** architecture: services are deeply integrated, with proprietary hardware (Nitro System) and custom networking (SRD, EFA).

**Conclusion:** Nimbus is best for **learning and private deployments**; AWS is best for **production-scale public cloud workloads**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert TB to GB (1 TB = 1,024 GB).**

$$
S_{GB} = 5 \text{ TB} = 5 \times 1024 = 5120 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute S3 storage cost.**

$$
C_{storage} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB} = 5120 \times 0.023
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{storage} = 117.76 \text{ USD/month}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute GET request cost.**

Daily requests = $20,000$. Monthly requests (assuming 30 days):

$$
R_{monthly} = 20,000 \times 30 = 600,000 \text{ requests}
$$

Convert to units of 1,000:

$$
R_{units} = 600{,}000 / 1000 = 600 \text{ (units of 1,000 requests)}
$$

$$
C_{requests} = 600 \times 0.0004 = 0.24 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute data transfer cost.**

$$
D_{out, GB} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
C_{dt} = 1024 \times 0.09 = 92.16 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total monthly bill.**

$$
TCO = C_{storage} + C_{requests} + C_{dt}
$$

$$
TCO = 117.76 + 0.24 + 92.16
$$

$$
\boxed{TCO = 210.16 \text{ USD/month}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute cost per GB stored per user.**

Total storage in GB = $5120$. Number of users = $10,000$.

$$
C_{perGBperUser} = \dfrac{C_{storage}}{S_{GB} \times N_{users}}
$$

$$
C_{perGBperUser} = \dfrac{117.76}{5120 \times 10000} = \dfrac{117.76}{51{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{perGBperUser} \approx 2.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ USD/GB/user}}
$$

**Alternative interpretation — cost per user per month (total bill amortized):**

$$
C_{perUser} = \dfrac{TCO}{N_{users}} = \dfrac{210.16}{10000} = 0.021 \text{ USD/user/month}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** At $10,000$ users, the **per-user cost is just 2 cents/month** — this is why cloud storage makes consumer apps economically viable. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the series/parallel distinction** in SLA questions: Always state explicitly whether components are in *series* (use product) or *parallel* (use $1 - \prod(1-A_i)$). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ambiguity.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors in cost questions:** Always convert TB → GB using $1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$ (decimal conversion is $1000$, but cloud billing typically uses $1024$ for storage tiers). Show the conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up EBS (block) and S3 (object) storage:** EBS is attached to one EC2; S3 is accessed over HTTP — examiners test this distinction in Part A.
> 4. **Not drawing the Region/AZ/Edge diagram in Part B answers:** Even a hand-drawn block diagram earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. **Confusing the Shared Responsibility Model:** Students often list “AWS does security” generically — examiners want the *exact split* (security OF vs. IN the cloud).
> 6. **Forgetting to show formula substitution:** Writing the final answer without the intermediate algebraic substitution loses 1 mark per sub-step in 7-mark problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Nimbus & AWS Ecosystem**

### Nimbus — Must Remember
- ⭐ **Definition:** Open-source IaaS toolkit from the University of Chicago.
- ⭐ **Key Components:** Workspace Service, Workspace Client, Context Broker, Cloud Client, Hypervisor Backend (Xen/KVM).
- ⭐ **API Compatibility:** EC2-compatible front-end + WSRF.
- ⭐ **Use Case:** Research clouds, academic private clouds, IaaS learning.
- ⭐ **License:** Open-source (Apache).
- ⭐ **Hardware:** Runs on commodity x86 servers.

### AWS Ecosystem — Must Remember
- ⭐ **Hierarchy:** Region → Availability Zone → Data Center; separate Edge Locations for CDN.
- ⭐ **Three Tiers of Global Infra:** Regions (geographic), AZs (data center clusters), Edge Locations (CDN caches).
- ⭐ **Compute:** EC2, Lambda (serverless), ECS/EKS (containers), Beanstalk (PaaS).
- ⭐ **Storage:** S3 (object), EBS (block), EFS (file), Glacier (archive).
- ⭐ **Database:** RDS (relational), DynamoDB (NoSQL), Redshift (warehouse), Aurora (cloud-native RDBMS).
- ⭐ **Network:** VPC (private network), Route 53 (DNS), CloudFront (CDN), API Gateway.
- ⭐ **Security:** IAM (identity), KMS (keys), WAF (firewall), Shield (DDoS), Cognito (users).
- ⭐ **Management:** CloudWatch (monitoring), CloudFormation (IaC), Auto Scaling, CloudTrail (audit).

### Formulas — Must Memorize
- ⭐ On-Demand Cost: $C_{od} = R_h \times T_{used}$
- ⭐ Series Availability: $A_{series} = \prod A_i$
- ⭐ Parallel Availability: $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$
- ⭐ Monthly Downtime: $D = T \times (1 - A/100)$ where $T = 43{,}200$ min for 30 days
- ⭐ Storage Cost: $C = S_{GB} \times P_{GB}$
- ⭐ Data Transfer: $C = D_{out} \times P_{dt}$

### Conceptual Points — Must Memorize
- ⭐ **Shared Responsibility:** AWS = security OF the cloud; Customer = security IN the cloud.
- ⭐ **Pay-as-you-go:** No upfront CAPEX; pay only for resources consumed.
- ⭐ **Elasticity:** Scale horizontally (more instances) and vertically (bigger instances).
- ⭐ **Multi-tenancy:** Many customers share the same physical hardware, isolated via virtualization.
- ⭐ **On-demand vs. Reserved vs. Spot:** On-Demand = highest cost, flexible; Reserved = discount for 1–3 yr commit; Spot = cheapest, can be terminated by AWS.

### Common Exam Traps
- ⚠️ Do **not** confuse **Regions** (geographic) with **Availability Zones** (data center clusters inside a region).
- ⚠️ Do **not** claim S3 is a block storage — it is **object storage** with HTTP-based key access.
- ⚠️ Do **not** say Lambda runs containers — it runs **stateless functions** in managed runtimes.
- ⚠️ Do **not** forget that **data transfer IN to AWS is free**, but data transfer OUT is charged.
- ⚠️ Do **not** confuse **EBS** (one EC2 at a time, like a USB stick) with **EFS** (shared, like a network drive).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services: EC2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compute Services: EC2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2)** is a web service provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that delivers **secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud**. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier for developers by providing virtual servers known as **Instances** that can be provisioned, configured, scaled, and terminated on-demand through a simple web service interface.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing syllabus (PEEET637 – Module 3)**, EC2 is classified as the flagship **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** offering of AWS, providing:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EC2 = Elastic + Compute + Cloud**
> - **Elastic** → The compute capacity can be scaled up or down dynamically based on demand.
> - **Compute** → Provides raw processing power (CPU, RAM, Storage, Networking) as a service.
> - **Cloud** → Hosted on AWS's global infrastructure across multiple geographical **Regions** and **Availability Zones (AZs)**.

Formally, EC2 can be defined as:

$$
EC2 = \{ \text{VM Instances} \mid \text{Instance Types}, \text{AMIs}, \text{Regions/AZs}, \text{Pricing Models} \}
$$

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🍕

To make EC2 crystal clear, consider the **Pizza Shop Analogy**:

| Real World | EC2 Equivalent | Purpose |
|------------|---------------|---------|
| Owning a brick-and-mortar restaurant | Buying a physical server | High upfront CapEx, fixed capacity, wasted when idle |
| Renting a shop **only when you need it** | Launching an EC2 instance | Pay only for what you use (OpEx model) |
| Choosing shop size (small, medium, large) | Selecting an **Instance Type** (t2.micro, m5.large, etc.) | Match compute to workload |
| Modular shop interior (counters, ovens) | **AMI (Amazon Machine Image)** | Pre-configured software template |
| Shop front door lock with security guard | **Security Group** | Virtual firewall controlling traffic |
| Renting shop on hourly basis | **On-Demand Pricing** | Pay per second/hour of usage |
| Pre-booking a venue for a year | **Reserved Instance** | Commitment discount (up to 72%) |
| Renting a shop only during a festival | **Spot Instance** | Use spare capacity at huge discounts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Elastic"?** Just as a rubber band stretches and contracts based on force, your EC2 fleet can **auto-scale** from 2 instances to 200 instances in minutes when traffic spikes (e.g., during a flash sale), and scale back down when demand drops — you pay only for what was actually used.

---

## 1.3 Why EC2 is a Pillar of IaaS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> "Compute Services: EC2 — Instance types, AMIs, Security Groups, Key Pairs, Pricing Models, and Elastic Load Balancing integration."

EC2 is the **backbone** of most cloud-native applications because it offers:

1. **Complete Control** → Root access via SSH, customizable OS, software stack.
2. **Scalability** → Horizontal (add more instances) and Vertical (resize instance).
3. **Reliability** → 99.99% SLA, multi-AZ deployment, EBS-backed storage.
4. **Security** → VPC isolation, Security Groups, IAM roles, Key Pairs.
5. **Flexibility** → 400+ instance types, multiple OS, container support, bare-metal.
6. **Cost Efficiency** → 4 pricing models, Spot market for up to 90% discount.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Expansion | One-line meaning |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **AMI** | Amazon Machine Image | Template containing OS + software + configuration |
| **Instance** | Virtual Server | A running copy of an AMI in the cloud |
| **EBS** | Elastic Block Store | Persistent block-level storage volume attached to instances |
| **Instance Type** | Hardware Config | Defines CPU, RAM, Network, Storage capacity |
| **Region** | Geographical Location | A physical AWS location worldwide (e.g., Mumbai `ap-south-1`) |
| **AZ** | Availability Zone | One or more discrete data centers within a Region |
| **Security Group** | Virtual Firewall | Stateful firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic |
| **Key Pair** | SSH Credential | Public-key cryptography for secure login |
| **EIP** | Elastic IP | Static public IPv4 address for dynamic cloud workloads |
| **ENI** | Elastic Network Interface | Virtual network card attached to an instance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EC2 Architecture — The Big Picture

EC2 is built on a **multi-tenant, virtualized infrastructure**. When you launch an EC2 instance, AWS performs the following logical sequence:

1. **Select a Region & AZ** → Determines the physical data center location.
2. **Choose an AMI** → Pre-baked OS + application template.
3. **Select an Instance Type** → Hardware profile (CPU, RAM, etc.).
4. **Configure Network** → VPC, Subnet, Public/Private IP, Security Group.
5. **Attach Storage** → EBS volume (root + additional).
6. **Authenticate** → Key Pair for SSH, IAM Role for API access.
7. **Launch** → Hypervisor creates a KVM/Xen/Nitro VM in milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> AWS uses a **Nitro System** (custom hardware + hypervisor) for modern instance families (e.g., M5, C5, R5 onwards). Nitro offloads networking, storage, and security to dedicated hardware, resulting in **near-bare-metal performance**.

---

## 2.2 EC2 Instance Type Families (The "Alphabet Soup")

Instance types follow a naming convention: `Family.Generation.Size`

**Example:** `m5.large` → Family = m (General purpose), Generation = 5, Size = large

| Family | Code | Optimized For | Use Case Examples |
|--------|------|---------------|-------------------|
| General Purpose | **t3, t4g, m5, m6i** | Balanced CPU + Memory | Web servers, small DBs, dev environments |
| Compute Optimized | **c5, c6i, c7g** | High-performance processors | Batch processing, gaming servers, HPC |
| Memory Optimized | **r5, r6i, x1, z1d** | Large memory footprint | In-memory caches (Redis), real-time analytics |
| Storage Optimized | **i3, d2, h1** | High IOPS / sequential disk throughput | NoSQL DBs (Cassandra), data warehousing |
| Accelerated Computing | **p4, g5, f1, inf1** | GPUs / FPGAs / ASICs | ML training, video transcoding, scientific simulation |
| Burstable Performance | **t3, t4g** | Baseline + CPU credits | Low-traffic websites, microservices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The `t-family` instances (t3, t4g) use **CPU Credits**. When idle, they accumulate credits; when bursting, they consume credits. This is a frequently asked question in Part A.

---

## 2.3 Pricing Models — The Cost Equation

EC2 offers **four pricing models**, and selecting the right one is a major exam topic.

### 2.3.1 On-Demand Instances
- Pay by the **second** (Linux) or **hour** (Windows).
- No commitment, no upfront cost.
- Use when: unpredictable workloads, short-term testing.

### 2.3.2 Reserved Instances (RI)
- **1-year or 3-year** term commitment.
- Up to **72% discount** vs On-Demand.
- Use when: steady-state production workloads.
- Three payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront.

### 2.3.3 Spot Instances
- Bid for **unused AWS capacity** at up to **90% discount**.
- Can be **terminated by AWS with 2-minute notice**.
- Use when: fault-tolerant, flexible batch jobs (e.g., video rendering, ML training).

### 2.3.4 Savings Plans
- Commit to a fixed $/hour spend for 1 or 3 years.
- Up to **66% discount**, flexible across instance families & regions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for all EC2 cost, performance, and scaling calculations that may appear in your KTU End Semester Exam (ESE).

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|------|-------|
| 1 | **vCPU Mapping** | $1 \text{ vCPU} = 2 \text{ threads} = 1 \text{ physical core (approx.)}$ | vCPU | Most general-purpose families |
| 2 | **EBS IOPS Provisioned (gp3)** | $3{,}000 \text{ baseline} + 500 \text{ per provisioned MiB/s}$ | IOPS | Free baseline up to 3000 IOPS |
| 3 | **EBS Throughput (gp3)** | $125 \text{ MiB/s baseline} + \text{provisioned}$ | MiB/s | Up to 1000 MiB/s |
| 4 | **Cost (On-Demand)** | $C = P_{\text{per-hr}} \times T_{\text{hrs}}$ | $ | $P$ = hourly price |
| 5 | **Cost (Reserved)** | $C = \frac{A + (P_{\text{per-hr}} \times T_{\text{committed}})}{T_{\text{used}}}$ | $ | $A$ = upfront amount |
| 6 | **Cost (Spot)** | $C = \text{SpotPrice} \times T_{\text{used}}$ | $ | Bid $\geq$ SpotPrice to retain |
| 7 | **CPU Credit Earning (t-family)** | $\text{Credits}_{\text{earned/hr}} = \frac{\text{vCPU} \times 100}{60}$ | credits | When at baseline |
| 8 | **Horizontal Scaling Trigger** | $\text{ScaleOut} \iff \text{CPU}_{avg} > T_{\text{up}}$ | % | CloudWatch Alarm |
| 9 | **Instance Limit (Default)** | $\text{Max Instances per Region} = 20 \text{ (soft limit)}$ | count | Can be raised via AWS Support |
| 10 | **SLA Uptime** | $\text{Uptime} = 99.99\% \implies \text{Downtime} \approx 52.6 \text{ min/year}$ | min | Per Region, multi-AZ EC2 |

---

## 2.5 Amazon Machine Image (AMI) Deep-Dive

An **AMI** is a packaged template containing:
- **Operating System** (Linux, Windows, Ubuntu, Amazon Linux 2)
- **Application Server / Middleware** (Apache, Nginx, Node.js)
- **Pre-installed Software** (WordPress, Docker, monitoring agents)
- **Launch Permissions** (who can launch this AMI)
- **Root Volume Snapshot** (EBS snapshot backing the OS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMI Sources (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Quick Start AMIs** → AWS-published, pre-built (Amazon Linux 2, Ubuntu, Windows).
> 2. **My AMIs** → AMIs you created from existing instances.
> 3. **AWS Marketplace AMIs** → Third-party (e.g., pre-configured WordPress, Palo Alto firewall).
> 4. **Community AMIs** → Shared by other AWS users (use with caution).

---

## 2.6 Security Groups vs Network ACLs (Frequently Confused!)

| Feature | Security Group (SG) | Network ACL (NACL) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Layer** | Instance ENI (Instance level) | Subnet level |
| **State** | **Stateful** (return traffic auto-allowed) | **Stateless** (return traffic must be explicit) |
| **Rules** | Allow rules only | Allow + Deny rules |
| **Scope** | Up to 5 SGs per ENI | 1 NACL per subnet |
| **Default** | Denies all inbound, allows all outbound | Allows all by default |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often say "Security Groups can Deny traffic." **WRONG** — Security Groups have only **ALLOW** rules. To block traffic, you simply omit the rule (default deny).

---

## 2.7 EC2 Lifecycle States

```
pending → running → stopping → stopped → terminated
                ↘ shutting-down ↗
                ↘ rebooting ↗
```

- **Stopped** → Instance is off, EBS volume preserved. Can be restarted.
- **Terminated** → Instance and root EBS volume are **permanently deleted**. Cannot be recovered.
- **Hibernate** → RAM state saved to EBS; resumes in seconds (supported on C, M, R families).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EC2 Cost Calculation for a 1-Month Workload

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern):**
> A startup runs a `m5.large` On-Demand instance in `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) for **720 hours/month** at **$0.096/hr**. They migrate to a **1-year No-Upfront Reserved Instance** priced at **$0.06/hr equivalent** (savings plan: 37% off). Calculate:
> 1. Monthly cost before migration.
> 2. Monthly cost after migration.
> 3. Monthly savings and annual savings.
> 4. Break-even time vs. one-time migration cost of $50.

### Step 1: Identify Given Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{OD}} &= \$0.096 \text{ per hour} \\
P_{\text{RI}} &= \$0.060 \text{ per hour} \\
T_{\text{monthly}} &= 720 \text{ hours (24 hr/day} \times 30 \text{ days)} \\
C_{\text{migration}} &= \$50 \text{ (one-time engineer cost)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** $P_{\text{OD}}$ is the published on-demand hourly rate; $P_{\text{RI}}$ is the effective hourly rate after applying the 1-year reserved discount. $T_{\text{monthly}}$ is computed as 24 hours × 30 days = 720 hours. $C_{\text{migration}}$ is a fixed one-time engineering overhead.

### Step 2: Compute Monthly On-Demand Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{OD, monthly}} &= P_{\text{OD}} \times T_{\text{monthly}} \\
&= 0.096 \times 720 \\
&= 69.12 \text{ USD/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Multiply hourly rate by total hours used. This is the baseline cost the startup currently pays.

### Step 3: Compute Monthly Reserved Instance Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{RI, monthly}} &= P_{\text{RI}} \times T_{\text{monthly}} \\
&= 0.060 \times 720 \\
&= 43.20 \text{ USD/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Same formula, but with the reserved rate. Notice we assume the instance runs 24/7.

### Step 4: Compute Monthly Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{monthly}} &= C_{\text{OD}} - C_{\text{RI}} \\
&= 69.12 - 43.20 \\
&= 25.92 \text{ USD/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Savings = difference in cost. $25.92 is the cash flow benefit per month.

### Step 5: Compute Annual Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= S_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 \\
&= 25.92 \times 12 \\
&= 311.04 \text{ USD/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Annualize the monthly savings. This is the headline ROI figure.

### Step 6: Compute Break-Even Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{breakeven}} &= \frac{C_{\text{migration}}}{S_{\text{monthly}}} \\
&= \frac{50}{25.92} \\
&\approx 1.93 \text{ months} \approx 58 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The migration pays for itself in under 2 months. After that, every dollar saved is pure profit.

### ✅ Final Answer Box

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Monthly On-Demand Cost | **$69.12** |
| Monthly Reserved Cost | **$43.20** |
| Monthly Savings | **$25.92** |
| Annual Savings | **$311.04** |
| Break-Even Time | **~1.93 months** |
| 5-Year Net Savings | **$1,505.20** |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Instance Type

The following decision matrix helps map **workload characteristics → instance family**. This is a common KTU "compare and contrast" question.

| Workload Pattern | CPU % | RAM Ratio | I/O Pattern | Recommended Family | Reason |
|------------------|-------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|--------|
| Static Website | < 25% | Low | Low | **t3.micro** | Burstable, cheapest |
| E-commerce App | 40–60% | Medium | Medium | **m5.large** | Balanced general purpose |
| In-Memory Cache | 20% | **High** | Low | **r5.large** | Memory optimized (RAM:CPU = 8:1) |
| Video Encoding | **90–100%** | Medium | High | **c5.2xlarge** | Compute optimized, high GHz |
| Hadoop HDFS | 50% | Medium | **Very High Seq I/O** | **d2.xlarge** | Storage optimized (HDD) |
| ML Training | **100%** | High | Medium | **p4d.24xlarge** | GPU accelerated (8× A100) |
| NoSQL DB (Cassandra) | 40% | Medium | **High Random I/O** | **i3.2xlarge** | NVMe SSD, high IOPS |
| Real-Time Analytics | 70% | **Very High** | Medium | **x1.16xlarge** | Memory optimized (1.9 TB RAM) |

---

## 3.3 Auto-Scaling Math (Horizontal Scaling)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** "An application runs on 2 m5.large instances. CPU > 70% triggers +1 instance; CPU < 30% removes 1 instance. After 3 hours, average traffic required 4 instances. Calculate total instance-hours."

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \times t_i \\
&= (2 \times 0.5) + (3 \times 1.0) + (4 \times 1.5) \\
&= 1.0 + 3.0 + 6.0 \\
&= 10.0 \text{ instance-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Sum across time windows. $N_i$ is the number of instances running during window $i$, and $t_i$ is the duration of that window in hours. Total billing is then:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{total}} \times P_{\text{per-hr}} = 10.0 \times 0.096 = \$0.96
$$

---

## 3.4 EBS Volume Sizing Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** "A database needs 5000 IOPS and 250 MiB/s throughput. Compare gp2 vs gp3."

For **gp2**, IOPS is tied to volume size:

$$
\text{IOPS}_{gp2} = 3 \times \text{Size (GiB)} \quad \text{(capped at 16000 IOPS at 5334 GiB)}
$$

For **5000 IOPS**: Size = 5000/3 ≈ 1667 GiB → Wasteful (you don't need that much disk space).

For **gp3**, IOPS and throughput are **independent** of size:

$$
\text{IOPS}_{gp3} = 3000 \text{ (free)} + 500 \text{ per MiB/s provisioned}
$$

For **5000 IOPS and 250 MiB/s**: Provision 4 MiB/s extra (since baseline 125 MiB/s) and 2000 extra IOPS.

**Conclusion:** gp3 is **cheaper and more efficient** for predictable high-IOPS workloads — a key cost-optimization insight tested in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EC2 Instance Launch Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Launch Request] --> B[AWS Console / CLI / SDK]
    B --> C[EC2 Service Endpoint]
    C --> D{Region and AZ Selected}
    D --> E[Subnet in VPC]
    E --> F[Select AMI from Catalog]
    F --> G[Choose Instance Type Family]
    G --> H[Attach Security Group Rules]
    H --> I[Attach Key Pair for SSH Access]
    I --> J[Provision EBS Root Volume]
    J --> K[Allocate Elastic IP if Requested]
    K --> L[Nitro Hypervisor Allocates VM]
    L --> M[Instance State Pending]
    M --> N[OS Boot and User Data Execution]
    N --> O[Instance State Running]
    O --> P[CloudWatch Metrics Activated]
    P --> Q[Application Ready for Traffic]
    Q --> R[Auto Scaling Group Monitors CPU]
    R --> S{Traffic Threshold}
    S -->|Above Limit| T[Launch Additional Instance]
    S -->|Below Limit| U[Terminate Excess Instance]
    T --> R
    U --> R
```

**Textual Walkthrough of Diagram:**

1. **User** → Submits launch request via Console, CLI, or SDK.
2. **EC2 Service Endpoint** → Authenticates the request using IAM credentials.
3. **Region/AZ Selection** → Determines physical data center location.
4. **AMI Catalog** → Provides OS + application template.
5. **Instance Type** → Defines hardware (vCPU, RAM, Network).
6. **Security Group** → Acts as a stateful firewall.
7. **Key Pair** → Provides secure SSH access.
8. **EBS Volume** → Persistent block storage attached to the instance.
9. **Nitro Hypervisor** → Allocates physical hardware to a virtual machine.
10. **CloudWatch** → Collects metrics (CPU, Network I/O) for monitoring.
11. **Auto Scaling** → Dynamically adds/removes instances based on traffic.

---

## 4.2 EC2 Security Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Internet["Internet / External Traffic"]
        EXT[Client Browser]
    end

    subgraph Region["AWS Region"]
        subgraph VPC["VPC 10.0.0.0/16"]
            subgraph PublicSubnet["Public Subnet 10.0.1.0/24"]
                WEB[EC2 Web Server]
                SG1[Security Group Web Tier]
            end
            subgraph PrivateSubnet["Private Subnet 10.0.2.0/24"]
                DB[EC2 Database Server]
                SG2[Security Group DB Tier]
            end
        end
        NACL1[Network ACL Public]
        NACL2[Network ACL Private]
    end

    EXT -->|HTTPS 443| SG1
    SG1 -->|Allow 443| WEB
    WEB -->|MySQL 3306| SG2
    SG2 -->|Allow 3306| DB
    NACL1 -.->|Stateless Layer| PublicSubnet
    NACL2 -.->|Stateless Layer| PrivateSubnet
```

**Key Insights from the Diagram:**

- The **Internet** can only reach the **Web Server** via Security Group rule (port 443).
- The **Web Server** can talk to the **Database Server** on port 3306 (allowed by DB's SG).
- The **Database Server** has **no public IP** and cannot be reached from the internet directly.
- **NACLs** operate at the subnet boundary as an additional stateless layer.
- This implements the **defense-in-depth** principle — a KTU favorite.

---

## 4.3 EC2 Pricing Model Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New EC2 Workload] --> B{Is Workload Predictable and Long-Term?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Is Fault-Tolerance Required?}
    B -->|No| D[Use On-Demand Instances]
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Spot Instances up to 90% Discount]
    C -->|No| F{Commit 1 or 3 Years?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Reserved Instances or Savings Plans up to 72% Discount]
    F -->|No| H[Use On-Demand and Reassess Quarterly]
```

**Reading the Tree:**

- **On-Demand** → Default for unpredictable, short-lived workloads.
- **Reserved** → Best for steady-state, multi-year production.
- **Spot** → Best for fault-tolerant, flexible batch jobs.
- **Savings Plans** → Hybrid flexibility + commitment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Amazon EC2. List any FOUR instance type families with their primary use case.**

**Model Answer:**

Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is a web service that provides **secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud** in the form of virtual servers called **instances**. It is the flagship IaaS offering of AWS.

**Four Instance Type Families:**

| Family | Optimization | Use Case |
|--------|--------------|----------|
| **General Purpose (m5)** | Balanced CPU + RAM | Web servers, dev environments |
| **Compute Optimized (c5)** | High GHz processors | Batch processing, gaming |
| **Memory Optimized (r5)** | High RAM-to-CPU ratio | In-memory databases, caching |
| **Storage Optimized (i3)** | High IOPS NVMe SSD | NoSQL DBs, data warehousing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 4 families with uses (2 marks — 0.5 each).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between On-Demand, Reserved, and Spot Instances in EC2.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | On-Demand | Reserved | Spot |
|---------|-----------|----------|------|
| **Commitment** | None | 1 or 3 years | None |
| **Discount** | None | Up to 72% | Up to 90% |
| **Interruption** | None | None | **Yes (2-min notice)** |
| **Billing** | Per second/hour | Upfront + hourly | Per hour of usage |
| **Best For** | Short-term, unpredictable | Steady-state production | Fault-tolerant batch jobs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark each for the three models, with a clear distinguishing feature (interruption, commitment, discount).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Module Choice (Internal Choice Provided)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The student must answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PEEET637]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **architecture of Amazon EC2**, including the role of **AMI, Instance Type, Security Group, Key Pair, EBS, and Region/AZ**. Draw a labeled diagram showing the relationship between these components during instance launch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Security Groups and Network ACLs** in AWS. Explain with an example why a 3-tier web application architecture (Web, App, DB) requires multiple security groups.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (a)

**EC2 Architecture — Step by Step Explanation:**

1. **Region & Availability Zone (AZ):** AWS hosts data centers globally grouped into Regions, each with multiple AZs. A user selects the closest Region (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai) and a specific AZ for low latency and high availability.

2. **VPC and Subnet:** The instance is launched inside a logically isolated **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** and a **Subnet** (public or private). Public subnets route to an **Internet Gateway**; private subnets do not.

3. **AMI (Amazon Machine Image):** The instance is launched from an **AMI**, which contains the OS, application server, software packages, and a root volume snapshot. AMIs can be AWS-published, custom-built, or marketplace-based.

4. **Instance Type:** Defines the hardware configuration — number of **vCPUs, RAM size, network bandwidth, EBS throughput**. Examples: `t3.micro` (1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM) for testing; `m5.4xlarge` (16 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM) for production.

5. **Key Pair:** AWS generates a **public–private key pair** for SSH access. The public key is stored in EC2; the private key is downloaded by the user. The user SSHs using `ssh -i mykey.pem ec2-user@<public-ip>`.

6. **Security Group:** A **stateful virtual firewall** attached to the instance's **Elastic Network Interface (ENI)**. It allows only specified inbound ports (e.g., 22 for SSH, 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS).

7. **EBS Volume:** An **Elastic Block Store** volume is attached as the root disk. It persists independently of the instance lifecycle, enabling snapshots and re-attachment.

8. **Elastic IP (Optional):** A **static public IPv4 address** that can be remapped across instances for fault tolerance.

9. **User Data:** A bootstrap script executed at first launch for automated configuration (e.g., `#!/bin/bash; yum install -y httpd; systemctl start httpd`).

10. **CloudWatch & Monitoring:** Once running, the instance emits **CPU, Network, Disk, and Status Check metrics** to CloudWatch every minute (or 1 second with detailed monitoring).

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** above (EC2 Launch Architecture).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Region/AZ (1) + AMI (1) + Instance Type (1) + Key Pair (1) + Security Group (1) + EBS (1) + Diagram (1).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Security Groups vs Network ACLs — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Security Group (SG) | Network ACL (NACL) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Operates at | Instance ENI | Subnet |
| Stateful? | **Yes** (return traffic automatic) | **No** (return rules needed) |
| Rules | Allow only | Allow + Deny |
| Default | Deny inbound, allow outbound | Allow all |
| Use case | Instance-level protection | Subnet-level coarse filtering |

**3-Tier Web Application — Multiple Security Groups Example:**

- **Web Tier Security Group:** Allows inbound **port 80/443 from `0.0.0.0/0`** (public internet). Allows outbound **all**.
- **App Tier Security Group:** Allows inbound **port 8080 from Web SG only** (not the world). Allows outbound **all**.
- **DB Tier Security Group:** Allows inbound **port 3306 from App SG only**. No outbound to internet.

**Why multiple SGs?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Defense in Depth:** Each tier has its own micro-firewall. If the Web tier is compromised, the attacker still **cannot** reach the database — because the DB SG only allows traffic from the App SG, not the Web SG. This implements the **principle of least privilege**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write "Security Groups can deny traffic" or "NACLs are stateful." Both are **wrong** — these are the most common ways students lose 2–3 marks on this question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PEEET637]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **four EC2 pricing models** (On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, Savings Plans) with their discount percentages, commitment periods, and ideal use cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company runs a `c5.xlarge` instance ($0.204/hr on-demand) 24/7 for one year. Calculate:
1. The total annual on-demand cost.
2. The cost after migrating to a 1-year No-Upfront Reserved Instance at $0.124/hr.
3. The total savings and the discount percentage achieved.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Four EC2 Pricing Models:**

| Pricing Model | Commitment | Discount | Ideal Use Case |
|---------------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **On-Demand** | None | 0% (baseline) | Short-term, unpredictable workloads, dev/test |
| **Reserved Instances** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **72%** | Steady-state, predictable production workloads |
| **Spot Instances** | None | Up to **90%** | Fault-tolerant, flexible jobs (ML training, batch, rendering) |
| **Savings Plans** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **66%** | Flexible commitment across instance families and regions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Each model = 1.5 marks (description + discount + use case). Add an example like "For 1-year RI No-Upfront on m5.large, ~37% discount."

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{OD}} &= \$0.204 \text{ per hour} \\
P_{\text{RI}} &= \$0.124 \text{ per hour} \\
T_{\text{annual}} &= 24 \times 365 = 8760 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Annual On-Demand Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{OD}} &= P_{\text{OD}} \times T_{\text{annual}} \\
&= 0.204 \times 8760 \\
&= 1787.04 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual Reserved Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{RI}} &= P_{\text{RI}} \times T_{\text{annual}} \\
&= 0.124 \times 8760 \\
&= 1086.24 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Annual Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= C_{\text{OD}} - C_{\text{RI}} \\
&= 1787.04 - 1086.24 \\
&= 700.80 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Discount Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\%} &= \frac{S}{C_{\text{OD}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{700.80}{1787.04} \times 100 \\
&\approx 39.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Annual On-Demand Cost | **$1,787.04** |
| Annual Reserved Cost | **$1,086.24** |
| Annual Savings | **$700.80** |
| Discount Achieved | **~39.22%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common error is computing $T_{\text{annual}}$ as $24 \times 30 = 720$ (using months) instead of $24 \times 365 = 8760$ (using days). Always read the question carefully — "**1 year**" means 365 days, not 30 days.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist for EC2:**

- **EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud** → IaaS, on-demand virtual servers (instances).
- **Instance = Running VM** launched from an **AMI** (template).
- **Instance Type naming** → `family.generation.size` (e.g., `m5.large`).
- **5 Major Families** → General (m), Compute (c), Memory (r), Storage (i/d), GPU (p/g).
- **T-family is Burstable** → Uses **CPU credits** (earn at baseline, spend during burst).
- **4 Pricing Models** → On-Demand (no commit), Reserved (1/3 yr, 72% off), Spot (90% off, interruptible), Savings Plans (66% off, flexible).
- **AMI Sources** → Quick Start, My AMIs, AWS Marketplace, Community.
- **Security Group** → **Stateful**, instance-level, **Allow rules only**, default deny inbound.
- **NACL** → **Stateless**, subnet-level, Allow + Deny rules.
- **Key Pair** → Public key on AWS, private key with user → SSH access.
- **EBS** → Persistent block storage, survives instance stop, **deleted on terminate** (if root).
- **Elastic IP** → Static public IPv4, can be remapped.
- **Regions contain AZs** → AZ = 1+ data centers, deploy across AZs for HA.
- **Instance Lifecycle** → pending → running → stopping → stopped → terminated.
- **Hibernate** → RAM saved to EBS, resumes in seconds (C/M/R families only).
- **SLA** → 99.99% uptime for EC2 in a single Region.
- **Nitro System** → Custom hardware hypervisor, near-bare-metal performance.
- **Auto Scaling** → Horizontal scaling based on CloudWatch alarms (CPU, Network, custom).
- **Vertical Scaling** → Change instance type (requires stop + start, state preserved).
- **Horizontal Scaling** → Add more instances (use ALB + Auto Scaling Group).
- **User Data** → Bootstrap script run at first launch for automation.
- **Termination Protection** → `DisableApiTermination` flag prevents accidental deletion.
- **Cost Optimization Tip** → gp3 EBS is cheaper than gp2 for predictable IOPS workloads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, you are most likely to get a question on (1) EC2 instance types & families, (2) Pricing models & cost calculation, (3) Security Groups vs NACLs, or (4) AMI & lifecycle. Master these four areas and you are exam-ready! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Compute Services
topics.content:
