> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Advanced Compute Services ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Advanced Compute Services** refer to the highly elastic, programmable, and on-demand compute resources offered by hyperscale cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) that go beyond basic Virtual Machines. They encompass **auto-scaling fleets, serverless functions, managed container orchestration, bare-metal HPC, and edge compute primitives** — all delivered via declarative APIs and Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) manifests. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET637 – Cloud Computing, Module 3)**, *Advanced Compute Services* specifically covers the architectural and operational models that allow a workload to **dynamically acquire CPU/GPU cycles, scale horizontally or vertically, balance traffic intelligently, and execute ephemeral code** without the user provisioning the underlying hypervisor, rack, or power infrastructure. The core service families include: | Service Family | Example (AWS Reference) | Abstract Role | |---|---|---| | Elastic Compute | EC2, Spot, Reserved | Programmable VMs | | Container Compute | ECS, EKS, Fargate | Orchestrated micro-VMs | | Serverless | AWS Lambda | Event-driven ephemeral execution | | HPC / Bare-Metal | EC2 Bare Metal, ParallelCluster | Tightly-coupled supercomputing | | Edge / Hybrid | Outposts, Wavelength, Local Zones | Compute outside the region | | Batch | AWS Batch | Job-scheduled multi-step workloads | > [!NOTE] > **Module-3 Anchor Statement (KTU 2024):** *"Advanced Compute Services abstract the hypervisor, the scheduler, and the cluster manager behind a managed control plane so the developer consumes CPU, memory, and GPU as a metered utility."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine a restaurant where customers order food at unpredictable times. - **Plain VMs** = A fixed number of chefs standing in the kitchen. If 1,000 customers arrive, only those chefs can cook. The rest wait. Food gets cold. - **Auto Scaling** = A manager at the door who, when the queue length exceeds a threshold (say 10 people), shouts "we need 5 more chefs from the agency!" They arrive, cook, and leave when the queue is empty. You pay them *only by the hour they worked*. - **Load Balancer** = A host at the entrance who distributes the arriving customers evenly across the available tables so no single chef is overworked. - **Serverless (Lambda)** = You don't hire any chefs at all. You send a recipe to a vending machine. The moment the order is placed, the machine instantly cooks exactly that one dish and bills you per dish, not per hour. - **Containers (ECS/EKS)** = Pre-cooked, standardized meal kits that any chef can finish in 3 minutes — so bringing in a new "chef" is nearly instant. - **HPC / Bare-Metal** = A specialized molecular-gastronomy lab built into the kitchen, used only for dishes that need a microscope. The *"Advanced Compute Services"* syllabus is the engineering blueprint of that restaurant manager, vending machine, and meal-kit factory. ### 1.3 Why this Module Matters in KTU 2024 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Topic:** Questions on Auto Scaling policies, Load Balancing algorithms, Lambda cold-start latency, and container vs. VM trade-offs appear regularly in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)**. The examiner expects you to compare compute primitives in a *decision matrix* form, not just list them. ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (must memorise) - **vCPU** = 1 hyper-thread of an Intel Xeon or AMD EPYC core (≈ **2.4 GHz baseline clock** on Graviton 2). - **ECU (EC2 Compute Unit)** = 1 vCPU ≈ **1.0–1.2 GHz 2007 Opteron**; deprecated, replaced by **vCPU + architecture class**. - **RPS (Requests Per Second)** — load balancer throughput unit. - **Cold-start Latency** = Time from invocation to first byte; **typical Lambda cold-start ≈ 100 ms–3 s**; provisioned concurrency brings this to **< 50 ms**. - **P99 Latency** = 99th percentile response time — the SLO target cloud architects design for. - **Spot Interruption Rate** = Approx. **< 5%** of total instance-hours for stable pools, but can spike during AWS reclaims. - **Concurrency Limit (Lambda)** = **1,000 concurrent executions per region** (soft default, can be raised). --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Compute Service Continuum Advanced compute services are not a list of disconnected products. They form a **continuum of abstraction**, ranging from *most control* to *most abstraction*: | Tier | Primitive | User Manages | Cloud Manages | Billing Granularity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bare-Metal / HPC | OS, scheduler, MPI | Physical host | Per-hour reservation | | 2 | EC2 (VM) | OS, middleware, app | Hypervisor, host | Per-second (min 60 s) | | 3 | Container (ECS/EKS on EC2) | App, Dockerfile | K8s control plane, host* | Per-second (cluster) | | 4 | Container (Fargate) | App, container image | Everything except app | Per-vCPU-second + GB-second | | 5 | Serverless (Lambda) | Function code | Runtime, OS, scaling, HA | Per-GB-second + per-request | | 6 | Edge Lambda / Wavelength | Function code | Everything + cell tower | Per-GB-second | > *ECS-managed control plane; EKS-managed Kubernetes API server; on EC2 the user still manages the kubelet.* ### 2.2 Auto Scaling — The Three Policy Models **Auto Scaling Groups (ASG)** maintain a desired count of healthy instances by reacting to load. 1. **Reactive (Target Tracking)** - Cloud provider's controller continuously adjusts capacity so that a chosen metric (e.g., average CPU) stays at a target value. - Example: AWS `TargetTrackingScaling` with `PredefinedMetricType=ASGAverageCPUUtilization` and `TargetValue=60%`. - Uses **control-theory PI-loop** internally. 2. **Predictive (Scheduled / Forecast)** - Capacity is pre-warmed for known load patterns. - `ScheduledAction` (cron-style) for static events (e.g., Black Friday). - **AWS Auto Scaling Forecast** uses **Prophet / DeepAR+** ML models trained on 14-day history to predict load 2 days ahead. 3. **Step (Manual / Cooldown-based)** - Discrete rules: "If CPU > 70% for 5 min, add 2 instances." Each rule is a *step* in a *step-adjustment function*. - **Cooldown period** (default **300 s**) prevents thrashing. > [!NOTE] > **Step-Scaling vs. Simple-Scaling (KTU Favourite):** > - **Simple Scaling**: Adds capacity, then *waits* the full cooldown before re-evaluating. > - **Step Scaling**: Adds **N** instances in proportion to the magnitude of breach (e.g., 40–50% breach ⇒ +1; 50–60% breach ⇒ +2). Does **not** wait for cooldown between steps. ### 2.3 Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) Algorithms A load balancer (LB) sits between clients and a *target group* of compute instances. The KTU syllabus expects you to know **at least four algorithms**: | Algorithm | Description | Use Case | Failure Mode | |---|---|---|---| | **Round Robin** | Sequential dispatch | Homogeneous backends | Slow node gets equal traffic | | **Least Connections** | Picks node with fewest open TCP connections | Sticky workloads (DB, WS) | Initial burst unfairness | | **Least Outstanding Requests (LOR)** | Tracks in-flight HTTP/2 streams | Microservices, gRPC | Needs HTTP/2 support | | **Weighted Round Robin** | Honors static capacity weights | Mixed instance types (c5 + c6g) | Static weights miss runtime CPU | | **IP Hash** | Hash(client IP) % N | Session stickiness | Uneven distribution | | **Random with Two Choices (Power of Two)** | Pick 2 random nodes, send to lower load | Massive scale (e.g., AWS ALB at 1 M+ targets) | Slightly suboptimal vs. true LOR | > **ALB (Application Load Balancer)** operates at **OSI Layer 7** and supports **content-based routing** (path, host, header, query string, method). It can route to **Lambda** directly — a unique KTU-exam-friendly fact. ### 2.4 Serverless Compute — The Lambda Model **AWS Lambda** runs functions in response to **event sources** (S3, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge, Kinesis, SNS, S3, Cognito, etc.). Key concepts: - **Execution Environment** = a microVM (**Firecracker**) ≈ **128 MB–10,240 MB** RAM in **1 MB** increments. - **vCPU allocation** is **non-linear** with RAM: - 1,769 MB ⇒ 1 vCPU - 3,538 MB ⇒ 2 vCPU - 5,307 MB ⇒ 3 vCPU - > 10,240 MB ⇒ full core - **Cold start** = first invocation on a new sandbox ≈ **100 ms–3 s**. - **Warm start** = subsequent calls ≈ **< 5 ms**. - **Provisioned Concurrency** = keeps N environments warm continuously (priced at GB-hour + vCPU-hour). - **Maximum execution time** = **15 minutes** per invocation (raised from 5 min in 2018). - **Deployment package limit** = **50 MB (zipped) / 250 MB (unzipped)**; container image up to **10 GB**. ### 2.5 Container Orchestration — ECS vs. EKS vs. Fargate | Feature | ECS | EKS | Fargate | |---|---|---|---| | Orchestrator | AWS proprietary | Upstream **Kubernetes** | Serverless engine for either | | Control plane | Free, managed | **$0.10/hour/cluster** | Per-task billing | | Workload spec | JSON Task Definition | Kubernetes Pod/Deployment | Pod or Task | | Networking mode | awsvpc, bridge, host | VPC-CNI (pod = ENI) | awsvpc only | | Use case | AWS-native, simple | Multi-cloud, K8s ecosystem | No node management | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fargate vCPU pricing rule (KTU 2024):** Billed in **1-vCPU increments at 1-minute granularity** for vCPU, and **GB-hour** for memory. Minimum billing is **1 minute**. This is a frequent calculation question. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every numerical formula the examiner can test. Memorise the **units** in bold. | # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{\text{bill}} = \lceil t_{\text{sec}} / 60 \rceil$ for EC2 | $t_{\text{sec}}$ = instance seconds | **Per-second billing, min 60 s** | | 2 | $C_{\text{λ}} = N \cdot t \cdot R \cdot P_R + N \cdot P_I$ | $N$ = invocations, $t$ = ms, $R$ = GB RAM, $P_R$ = $0.0000166666/GB·s$, $P_I$ = $0.20/M$ | **Lambda cost** | | 3 | $C_{\text{Fargate}} = v \cdot T \cdot P_v + m \cdot T \cdot P_m$ | $v$ = vCPU, $m$ = GB, $T$ = hours | **vCPU-hour + GB-hour** | | 4 | $N_{\text{desired}} = \lceil (M_{\text{current}} \cdot L_{\text{current}}) / (M_{\text{target}} \cdot C_{\text{instance}}) \rceil$ | $M$ = metric, $L$ = load, $C$ = per-instance capacity | **Target tracking ASG** | | 5 | $\text{SLA}_{\text{monthly}} = (1 - D_{\text{down}}/T_{\text{month}}) \cdot 100\%$ | $D_{\text{down}}$ = downtime min | **99.99% = 4.38 min/month** | | 6 | $\text{vCPU}_\text{λ}(R) = \lfloor R/1769 \rfloor + (R \mod 1769 > 0 \text{ ? } 1 : 0)$ | $R$ = MB RAM | Capped at 6 vCPU | | 7 | $C_{\text{RI savings}} = (P_{\text{on}} - P_{\text{RI}}) \cdot T \cdot (1 - U)$ | $U$ = utilization | RI = **up to 75% off** on-demand | | 8 | $\text{Cold start}_P = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot T_{\text{idle}}}$ | $\lambda$ = invocation rate, $T_{\text{idle}}$ = idle seconds | **Probabilistic warm-pool** | | 9 | $L_{\text{concurrent}} = \text{RPS} \cdot T_{\text{latency}}$ | $T_{\text{latency}}$ in **seconds** | **Little's Law** — KTU classic | | 10 | $P_{\text{spot\_max}} = P_{\text{on-demand}} \cdot 0.90$ (typical ceiling) | Spot price fluctuates | **Cap is on-demand** | > **Critical LaTeX note:** All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Auto Scaling** is the *load-shedding* mechanism behind Netflix, Flipkart Big-Billion-Days, and IPL live-stream peaks. - **ALB → Lambda** powers serverless APIs (e.g., Amazon Prime Video's image-processing pipeline). - **EKS + Fargate** is the standard for *AI/ML inference* where pods spin up per request and die after 5 s. - **AWS Batch** is the workhorse for **genomics (DNAstack), pharma simulation, financial risk Monte-Carlo** — jobs that take 1 s to 7 days. - **Outposts** are used by **air-gapped banks and defense** to keep PII on-prem while consuming AWS APIs. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Lambda Cost from First Principles Lambda bills in **GB-seconds** of compute time and **number of requests**. Derive the total cost for a workload. **Given:** - Invocations per month, $N = 50{,}000{,}000$ (50 M) - Average execution time, $t = 200$ ms $= 0.2$ s - Allocated RAM, $R = 512$ MB $= 0.5$ GB - Region: US-East-1 (free tier ignored) **Standard published prices (US-East-1 reference):** - $P_R = \$0.0000166666$ per **GB-second** - $P_I = \$0.20$ per **1 million requests** **Step 1: Compute billable duration in GB-seconds** $$ T_{\text{GB·s}} = N \cdot t \cdot R $$ $$ T_{\text{GB·s}} = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.5 $$ $$ T_{\text{GB·s}} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ GB-seconds} $$ *Explanation:* Each of the 50 M invocations consumes 0.2 s × 0.5 GB = 0.1 GB-s. Multiplying by 50 M gives the total work-done equivalent. **Step 2: Compute request cost** $$ C_{\text{req}} = N \cdot \frac{P_I}{10^6} $$ $$ C_{\text{req}} = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot \frac{0.20}{10^6} $$ $$ C_{\text{req}} = 50 \cdot 0.20 $$ $$ C_{\text{req}} = \$10.00 $$ *Explanation:* 50 M requests × \$0.20 per million = 50 × \$0.20 = \$10. **Step 3: Compute compute-time cost** $$ C_{\text{comp}} = T_{\text{GB·s}} \cdot P_R $$ $$ C_{\text{comp}} = 5 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.0000166666 $$ $$ C_{\text{comp}} = \$83.33 $$ *Explanation:* 5 M GB-s × the per-GB-s rate. **Step 4: Total** $$ C_{\text{λ}} = C_{\text{comp}} + C_{\text{req}} $$ $$ C_{\text{λ}} = 83.33 + 10.00 $$ $$ C_{\text{λ}} = \$93.33 \text{ per month} $$ > **Insight:** Even at 50 M requests, the cost is **< \$100/month**, illustrating the *serverless value proposition* — for spiky low-duty-cycle workloads, Lambda is **2–4× cheaper** than an always-on EC2 fleet sized for peak. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Auto Scaling — Desired Capacity from Target Tracking **Problem:** An ASG has 4 running instances. Average CPU is 85%. The target tracking policy aims for 60% CPU at 1-minute granularity. Per-instance CPU capacity is 100% of one vCPU. Calculate the new desired count. **Step 1: Identify the gap** $$ \Delta = M_{\text{current}} - M_{\text{target}} = 85\% - 60\% = 25\% $$ **Step 2: Estimate load relative to current capacity** $$ L = N_{\text{current}} \cdot M_{\text{current}} = 4 \cdot 85\% = 3.4 \text{ vCPU-equivalents of load} $$ *Explanation:* Treat each percent of CPU as 0.01 vCPU of demand. 4 instances × 85% = 3.4 units of "load". **Step 3: Solve for capacity needed at target** $$ C_{\text{needed}} = \frac{L}{M_{\text{target}}} = \frac{3.4}{0.60} $$ $$ C_{\text{needed}} = 5.667 $$ **Step 4: Round up to integer (ceiling)** $$ N_{\text{desired}} = \lceil 5.667 \rceil = 6 $$ *Explanation:* You cannot run 5.667 instances. The ceiling function ensures the target *is met or beaten*. **Step 5: Instances to add** $$ \Delta N = 6 - 4 = +2 \text{ instances} $$ > **Insight:** AWS's actual internal algorithm uses a *PI controller* (Proportional-Integral) to dampen oscillation, but this closed-form approximation is the KTU-expected method. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Concurrency Requirement (Little's Law) **Problem:** An e-commerce site expects 5,000 RPS at peak. Average request latency is 80 ms. How many concurrent Lambda executions are needed (assuming Lambda is the backend)? **Given:** - $\text{RPS} = 5000$ - $T_{\text{latency}} = 80$ ms $= 0.08$ s **Step 1: Apply Little's Law** $$ L = \lambda \cdot W $$ $$ L = 5000 \cdot 0.08 $$ $$ L = 400 \text{ concurrent executions} $$ *Explanation:* 5,000 requests per second, each occupying a Lambda sandbox for 80 ms, implies an average of 400 in-flight sandboxes. This must be **< 1,000** (default account concurrency) **or** a quota increase must be requested. > **Edge case:** If latency is 250 ms instead, $L = 1{,}250$ — exceeding the default limit. The KTU examiner loves this trap. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Compute Service? | Decision Criterion | EC2 | ECS (EC2) | EKS (Fargate) | Lambda | |---|---|---|---|---| | Predictable steady load, 24×7 | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | | Spiky / event-driven | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Long-running (> 15 min) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ✗ | | Sub-100 ms cold start | ★★★★★ (warm) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ (cold) | | Custom kernel / GPU pass-through | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ✗ | | No OS / runtime management | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Cheapest at < 10% duty cycle | ✗ | ✗ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | | Vendor lock-in tolerance | Low | Med | Low | High | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb KTU examiners check:** "Lambda is **NOT** always cheaper. For steady high-CPU workloads > 15 min, EC2 Reserved Instances win." --- ### 3.5 Derivation: SLA-to-Downtime Conversion **Problem:** A 99.95% SLA. Convert to monthly downtime allowance. **Step 1: Convert 1 month to minutes** $$ T_{\text{month}} = 30 \text{ d} \cdot 24 \text{ h} \cdot 60 \text{ min} = 43{,}200 \text{ min} $$ **Step 2: Allowed downtime fraction** $$ D_{\text{frac}} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005 $$ **Step 3: Compute allowed downtime** $$ D_{\text{down}} = 43{,}200 \cdot 0.0005 $$ $$ D_{\text{down}} = 21.6 \text{ min/month} $$ > **Reference table (must memorise for KTU viva):** | SLA | Monthly Downtime | |---|---| | 99% | 432 min (7.2 h) | | 99.9% | 43.2 min | | 99.95% | 21.6 min | | 99.99% | 4.32 min | | 99.999% ("five-nines") | 25.9 s | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Auto Scaling Group — Reactive Scaling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB userTraffic["USER TRAFFIC
INCOMING REQUESTS"] --> cloudWatch["CLOUDWATCH
METRIC STREAM"] cloudWatch --> alarmEval{"ALARM
THRESHOLD
CHECK"} alarmEval -- breach --> scalingPolicy["SCALING POLICY
STEP OR TARGET TRACKING"] alarmEval -- no breach --> idleWait["WAIT 60 SECONDS"] scalingPolicy --> asgCtrl["AUTOSCALING GROUP
CONTROL PLANE"] asgCtrl --> launchTpl["LAUNCH TEMPLATE
AMI + USER DATA"] launchTpl --> ec2a["EC2 INSTANCE A"] launchTpl --> ec2b["EC2 INSTANCE B"] launchTpl --> ec2c["EC2 INSTANCE C NEW"] ec2a --> healthCheck{"HEALTH CHECK
ELB PING"} ec2b --> healthCheck ec2c --> healthCheck healthCheck -- healthy --> lb["APPLICATION LOAD BALANCER"] healthCheck -- unhealthy --> terminate["TERMINATE INSTANCE"] lb --> userTraffic ``` **Reading guide:** - `cloudWatch` emits 1-minute metric streams. - `alarmEval` is the decision diamond; if breached, the policy engine is invoked. - `asgCtrl` is the ASG's master controller — it talks to EC2 Fleet APIs. - `healthCheck` is the ELB ping (HTTP 200 on `/health`). - `terminate` removes bad nodes; `launchTpl` creates replacements. --- ### 4.2 Load Balancer Decision Flow (Application Load Balancer) ```mermaid flowchart LR client["CLIENT BROWSER"] --> dns["ROUTE 53 DNS"] dns --> alb["APPLICATION LOAD BALANCER
L7 PROXY"] alb --> ruleCheck{"LISTENER RULE
MATCH?"} ruleCheck -- "/api/* path" --> tgApi["TARGET GROUP API
ECS SERVICE"] ruleCheck -- "/images/* path" --> tgLambda["TARGET GROUP LAMBDA
SERVERLESS FUNCTION"] ruleCheck -- default --> tgWeb["TARGET GROUP WEB
EC2 FLEET"] tgApi --> svcA["ECS TASK A"] tgApi --> svcB["ECS TASK B"] tgLambda --> fn["LAMBDA FUNCTION"] tgWeb --> ec2web1["EC2 WEB 1"] tgWeb --> ec2web2["EC2 WEB 2"] ec2web1 --> health1["HEALTH CHECK
HTTP 200"] ec2web2 --> health2["HEALTH CHECK
HTTP 200"] svcA --> health3["HEALTH CHECK
HTTP 200"] svcB --> health4["HEALTH CHECK
HTTP 200"] fn --> health5["HEALTH CHECK
REPORTBACK"] ``` **Reading guide:** - `ruleCheck` represents ALB's **content-based routing** — header, host, path, query string, HTTP method. - A single ALB can route to **Lambda, ECS, EC2, and IP addresses** simultaneously — a *hybrid* compute fan-out. - Each `health*` block is a separate HealthCheck per target group. --- ### 4.3 Serverless Application — Lambda Behind API Gateway ```mermaid flowchart TB mobileApp["MOBILE APP"] --> apiGw["API GATEWAY
REST OR HTTP API"] webApp["WEB APP"] --> apiGw apiGw --> auth{"COGNITO
AUTHORIZER"} auth -- valid --> lambdaFn["LAMBDA FUNCTION
HANDLER CODE"] auth -- invalid --> reject["403 FORBIDDEN"] lambdaFn --> dynamo["DYNAMODB
NOSQL STORE"] lambdaFn --> s3["S3 BUCKET
OBJECT STORE"] lambdaFn --> sns["SNS TOPIC
ASYNC NOTIFY"] sns --> emailUser["EMAIL VIA SES"] sns --> sqsQueue["SQS QUEUE
DECOUPLED WORKER"] sqsQueue --> workerLambda["LAMBDA WORKER
HEAVY PROCESSING"] workerLambda --> s3 lambdaFn --> cwLogs["CLOUDWATCH LOGS"] workerLambda --> cwLogs lambdaFn --> xray["X-RAY TRACING"] ``` **Reading guide:** - `apiGw` is the **front door** — handles throttling (10,000 RPS default), WAF integration, custom domains. - `auth` is the **Cognito Authorizer** or **Lambda Authorizer** — KTU loves asking about this. - `sns → sqsQueue → workerLambda` is the canonical *fan-out / decoupling* pattern. - `xray` enables distributed tracing for cold-start diagnosis. --- ### 4.4 Container Orchestration Comparison — ECS vs EKS vs Fargate ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph userLayer["DEVELOPER"] docker["DOCKERFILE
CONTAINER IMAGE"] helm["HELM CHART
KUBERNETES MANIFEST"] taskDef["TASK DEFINITION
ECS JSON"] end subgraph ecs["ECS PATH"] ecsCtrl["ECS CONTROL PLANE
AWS MANAGED FREE"] ecsNode1["EC2 NODE 1"] ecsNode2["EC2 NODE 2"] end subgraph eks["EKS PATH"] eksCtrl["EKS CONTROL PLANE
KUBERNETES API $0.10/HR"] eksNodeGroup["MANAGED NODE GROUP
EC2 INSTANCES"] eksFargate["FARGATE PROFILE
SERVERLESS"] end docker --> taskDef docker --> helm taskDef --> ecsCtrl ecsCtrl --> ecsNode1 ecsCtrl --> ecsNode2 helm --> eksCtrl eksCtrl --> eksNodeGroup eksCtrl --> eksFargate ecsNode1 --> podA["POD A"] ecsNode2 --> podB["POD B"] eksNodeGroup --> podC["POD C"] eksFargate --> podD["POD D"] eksFargate --> podE["POD E"] ``` **Reading guide:** - The same `docker` image can be deployed via *any* of the three paths. - `ecsCtrl` is free; `eksCtrl` is **\$0.10/hour** per cluster (~ **\$73/month**). - `eksFargate` bills per-pod; `eksNodeGroup` is EC2-backed. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q:** Differentiate between **Vertical Scaling** and **Horizontal Scaling** in the context of cloud compute services. Give one AWS example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Vertical Scaling | Horizontal Scaling | |---|---|---| | Definition | Increase size of a **single** instance (more vCPU, RAM) | Increase **count** of instances | | Limit | Hard cap of largest instance type (e.g., **u-24tb1.metal = 24 TB RAM**) | Theoretically unlimited | | Downtime | Usually requires reboot / stop-start | Add new node live, no downtime | | AWS Example | **EC2 T3.nano → M5.4xlarge** resize | **Auto Scaling Group** adding a 5th c6g.large | > **Marker Expectation (1 mark each):** definition, comparison point, AWS example. --- > **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q:** What is a **cold start** in AWS Lambda? List two techniques to mitigate it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A *cold start* occurs when AWS Lambda must **initialise a new execution environment** (Firecracker microVM + language runtime + handler) for an incoming invocation because no warm sandbox is available. (1 mark) - Typical latency: **100 ms – 3 s** depending on runtime (Java is slowest, Node.js/Python fastest). (1 mark) - **Mitigations:** (1 mark for any two) 1. **Provisioned Concurrency** — keeps N sandboxes pre-initialised. 2. **Smaller deployment package** — reduces init time. 3. **Choose a faster runtime** (e.g., `provided.al2` over JVM). 4. **Scheduled keep-warm pings** (anti-pattern in production but exam-valid). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` > **(a)** Explain the **three Auto Scaling policy types** in AWS. How does target tracking differ from step scaling? (7 marks) > **(b)** An e-commerce application receives **3,000 RPS** at peak with an average response time of **120 ms**. Compute the required Lambda concurrency and the **monthly cost** if the function is allocated **1 GB RAM**, runs **50 M invocations/month**, each lasting **150 ms**. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B-A(a): 7 marks** **Three Auto Scaling Policies (5 marks):** 1. **Target Tracking Scaling (2 marks)** - You specify a *target value* for a CloudWatch metric (e.g., 60% average CPU). - The ASG controller uses a PI feedback loop to **continuously adjust** capacity so the metric stays at the target. - Cool-down: integrated warm-up time per instance. - Example: keep ASG-average CPU at **60%**. 2. **Step Scaling (2 marks)** - You define **discrete alarm thresholds** with associated step adjustments. - E.g., `CPU > 70%` → add 1; `CPU > 85%` → add 4; `CPU < 30%` → remove 2. - Allows *graduated* responses proportional to breach magnitude. - Does **not** wait for full cooldown between steps. 3. **Simple Scaling (1 mark)** - One alarm → one fixed adjustment (e.g., add 2). - Waits for full **cooldown period (300 s default)** before re-evaluating. - Mostly legacy; rarely used in modern designs. **Comparison (2 marks):** | Property | Target Tracking | Step Scaling | |---|---|---| | Granularity | Continuous, smooth | Discrete steps | | Configuration | Single metric + target | Multiple alarms + steps | | Cooldown | Per-instance warm-up | Per-alarm cooldown | | Use case | Standard web workloads | Bursty / spikey traffic | --- #### **Model Solution for Question B-A(b): 7 marks** **Part 1: Concurrency (3 marks)** By **Little's Law**: $$ L = \lambda \cdot W = 3000 \cdot 0.120 = 360 \text{ concurrent executions} $$ This is within the **default 1,000 account concurrency**, so no quota increase is needed. (½ mark for interpretation) **Part 2: Monthly cost (4 marks)** - $N = 50 \times 10^6$ invocations - $t = 150$ ms $= 0.15$ s - $R = 1$ GB - $P_R = \$0.0000166666$ per GB-s - $P_I = \$0.20$ per 1 M requests **Compute-seconds:** $$ T_{\text{GB·s}} = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 1 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ GB-s} $$ **Compute cost:** $$ C_{\text{comp}} = 7.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.0000166666 = \$125.00 $$ **Request cost:** $$ C_{\text{req}} = 50 \cdot 0.20 = \$10.00 $$ **Total:** $$ C_{\text{λ}} = 125.00 + 10.00 = \boxed{\$135.00 \text{ per month}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert ms → s** in $t$. Students often write `0.15 * 1` as if it were a dimensionless multiplier. Always show units in the derivation. > 2. **Mis-applying Little's Law**: $\lambda$ must be in **requests per SECOND** and $W$ in **seconds**, not minutes. > 3. **Ignoring the 1,000-concurrency default** — you do not get a quota increase automatically. --- > **Question B-B (Alternative Choice for Module 3)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of AWS Elastic Load Balancing (ELB)**. Compare **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** and **Network Load Balancer (NLB)** on **at least four** parameters. (7 marks) > **(b)** What is **AWS Fargate**? A team runs **20 Fargate tasks**, each needing **0.5 vCPU** and **1 GB RAM**, for **720 hours/month**. Compute the monthly bill. (7 marks) *(Standard US-East-1 reference rates: vCPU-hour = \$0.04048, GB-hour = \$0.004445. These are KTU-acceptable exam defaults.)* #### **Model Solution for Question B-B(b): 7 marks** **Definition (2 marks):** AWS Fargate is a **serverless compute engine for containers** that works with both ECS and EKS. The user supplies only a **container image, vCPU, and memory**; AWS provisions, scales, and patches the underlying host. **Billing rule (1 mark):** $$ C_{\text{Fargate}} = (v \cdot P_v + m \cdot P_m) \cdot T \cdot N $$ **Per-task per-month (2 marks):** - $v \cdot P_v = 0.5 \cdot 0.04048 = \$0.02024$/hour - $m \cdot P_m = 1 \cdot 0.004445 = \$0.004445$/hour - Sum = \$0.024685/hour - 720 h × 0.024685 = **\$17.77 per task per month** **Total for 20 tasks (2 marks):** $$ C_{\text{total}} = 20 \cdot 17.77 = \boxed{\$355.46 \text{ per month}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 720 hours** — students often quote only the per-hour rate. > 2. **Conflating ECS-on-EC2 vs Fargate** — Fargate includes host cost; ECS-on-EC2 does not. > 3. **Ignoring GB-hour pricing for Fargate** — many students compute vCPU only and lose **½ mark**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print before exam):** - [ ] **Auto Scaling has THREE policy types:** Target Tracking, Step, Simple. Target Tracking is the **default recommended**. - [ ] **Cooldown** = 300 s default; **Warm-up** = per-instance grace period (different concept!). - [ ] **ALB = Layer 7** (HTTP/HTTPS, content-based routing). **NLB = Layer 4** (TCP/UDP, ultra-low latency, static IPs). - [ ] **Lambda max execution time = 15 min** (hard cap). **Max memory = 10,240 MB**. - [ ] **Lambda vCPU scales non-linearly with RAM:** 1 vCPU at 1,769 MB; +1 vCPU per additional 1,769 MB. - [ ] **Lambda default concurrency = 1,000/region**. Use **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ to size it. - [ ] **EC2 billing = per-second, min 60 s**. **Lambda billing = per-GB-s + per-request, no minimum**. - [ ] **Fargate pricing = vCPU-hour + GB-hour, 1-min minimum**. - [ ] **EKS control plane = \$0.10/hour/cluster** (≈ \$73/month). **ECS control plane = free**. - [ ] **Spot Instances = up to 90% off on-demand**, can be reclaimed with 2-min warning. - [ ] **Reserved Instances = up to 75% off** on-demand, 1- or 3-year commit. - [ ] **SLA ladder:** 99% (7.2 h) → 99.9% (43.2 min) → 99.99% (4.32 min) → 99.999% (26 s) per month. - [ ] **Firecracker microVM** powers both Lambda and Fargate for sub-second cold start. - [ ] **ALB can route directly to Lambda** — a unique hybrid compute primitive. - [ ] **Provisioned Concurrency** eliminates cold starts but bills **GB-hour + vCPU-hour** continuously. - [ ] **Outposts = on-prem AWS hardware**; **Wavelength = 5G edge**; **Local Zones = metro extension**. ---

topics.title: Storage Services: Amazon S3
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage Services: Amazon S3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Storage Services: Amazon S3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3)** is an **object-based, highly scalable, durable, and secure cloud storage service** provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS). It allows users to store and retrieve any amount of data, at any time, from anywhere on the web, through a simple web-services interface (REST/SOAP APIs). S3 guarantees **99.999999999% (11 nines) of object durability** and **99.99% availability** over a given year.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Warehouse" Model

Imagine you are running a massive global e-commerce business (say, Flipkart or Amazon itself). You cannot store all your product images, invoices, user-generated videos, and backup logs on a single hard disk or even a single server. You need an **infinite warehouse** that:

- Has **endless shelf space** 📦
- Lets you **drop off any parcel** without worrying about shelf number
- Gives you a **unique tracking ID (key)** for every parcel
- Lets you **retrieve any parcel instantly** from anywhere in the world
- **Automatically replicates** parcels across multiple geographic locations so a flood in one city won't lose your goods

**Amazon S3 is exactly this warehouse — but digital.**

| Warehouse Analogy | Amazon S3 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The Warehouse Building | **Bucket** (a logical container) |
| A Parcel / Box | **Object** (file + metadata) |
| Tracking ID / Barcode | **Key** (unique object name) |
| Warehouse Manager | **AWS S3 Service Backend** |
| Insurance Policy | **Versioning + Replication** |
| Different Storage Floors (Cold, Hot) | **Storage Classes** (Standard, Glacier, etc.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** S3 is an *object storage* system — **NOT** a block storage (like EBS) and **NOT** a file system (like EFS). It stores data as discrete units (objects) inside flat namespaces (buckets) rather than in hierarchical folders at the OS level.

### Core Terminology Every KTU Student Must Memorize

- **Bucket** — A top-level container for objects in S3. Bucket names are **globally unique** across all AWS accounts.
- **Object** — The fundamental entity stored in S3. Consists of **data (file)**, **key (name)**, **metadata**, and a **version ID**.
- **Key** — The unique identifier (path-style string) for an object within a bucket, e.g., `images/2024/profile.jpg`.
- **Region** — The AWS geographical location where the bucket resides (e.g., `ap-south-1` for Mumbai).
- **Storage Class** — Cost-vs-availability trade-off category chosen per object.
- **Versioning** — A feature to keep multiple variants of an object in the same bucket.
- **Lifecycle Policy** — Automated rules to transition objects between storage classes or expire (delete) them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 S3 Architecture — How It Actually Works

Amazon S3 is built on a **distributed, decentralized architecture** with the following operational layers:

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow (KTU Board Favourite ⭐)

1. **User Request Initiation** → Client application invokes the S3 REST API (`PUT`, `GET`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `LIST`) over **HTTPS (port 443)**.
2. **Request Routing via DNS** → The S3 service uses **Amazon's DNS infrastructure** to route the request to the nearest AWS edge location / regional endpoint.
3. **Authentication & Authorization** → AWS validates the request signature (**Signature Version 4 — SigV4**) using the user's **Access Key ID** + **Secret Access Key**, then checks **IAM policies**, **bucket policies**, and **ACLs**.
4. **Request Processing at Front-End Server** → The front-end server (stateless) authenticates and routes to the appropriate **storage node**.
5. **Storage Node Interaction** → The storage node performs the actual read/write against the object's location using an **object lookup index**.
6. **Data Persistence & Replication** → Data is stored across **multiple devices in multiple Availability Zones (AZs)** within the chosen region.
7. **Response Returned** → HTTP status code (e.g., `200 OK`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 NoSuchKey`) is returned to the client.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "11 Nines" of Durability?** S3 achieves this by **synchronously replicating data across at least 3 Availability Zones** within a region. The probability of losing an object is calculated as $(1 - D)^{N}$, where $D$ is single-device durability and $N$ is the redundancy count.

## 2.2 S3 Data Consistency Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> S3 provides **strong read-after-write consistency** for **ALL** operations (PUT, GET, LIST, DELETE) — this was updated in **December 2020**. Earlier, S3 offered only *eventual consistency* for overwrite PUTs and DELETEs.

| Operation Type | Consistency Guarantee |
|---|---|
| PUT (new object) | **Strong Read-After-Write** |
| PUT (overwrite existing) | **Strong Read-After-Write** ✅ |
| DELETE | **Strong Read-After-Write** ✅ |
| LIST | **Strong Consistency** ✅ |
| Tagging operations | **Strong Consistency** ✅ |

## 2.3 S3 Storage Classes — Cost vs. Access Trade-Off

> [!NOTE]
> **S3 Storage Classes are a HIGH-YIELD topic for KTU exams** — memorize the trade-off triangle of **Cost ↓, Availability ↓, Retrieval Time ↑** as data goes colder.

| Storage Class | Use Case | Availability | Durability | Min Storage Duration | Retrieval Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S3 Standard** | Frequently accessed data | 99.99% | 11 nines | None | Free |
| **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | Unknown/changing access patterns | 99.9% | 11 nines | None | Monitoring fee per object |
| **S3 Standard-IA** | Infrequent access, rapid retrieval | 99.9% | 11 nines | 30 days | Per-GB retrieval fee |
| **S3 One Zone-IA** | Infrequent, non-critical, reproducible | 99.5% | 11 nines (1 AZ) | 30 days | Per-GB retrieval fee |
| **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** | Archive, millisecond retrieval | 99.9% | 11 nines | 90 days | Per-GB retrieval fee |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | Archive, minutes-to-hours retrieval | 99.99% | 11 nines | 90 days | Cheaper retrieval |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term archive, 12–48 hours | 99.99% | 11 nines | 180 days | Cheapest storage |

## 2.4 S3 Pricing Model — Key Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Numerical/calculation-based questions on S3 pricing are frequently asked (4–5 marks).

### Core Cost Components

The total monthly S3 bill for a region is the sum of:

$$C_{total} = C_{storage} + C_{requests} + C_{dataTransfer} + C_{management}$$

Where each component is computed as follows.

**1. Storage Cost ($C_{storage}$):**

$$C_{storage} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( S_i \times P_i \times \frac{D_i}{30} \right)$$

- $S_i$ = size of data in storage class $i$ (in GB)
- $P_i$ = price per GB-month for class $i$ (in USD)
- $D_i$ = number of days data was stored in class $i$ in that month
- $n$ = number of distinct storage classes used

**2. Request Cost ($C_{requests}$):**

$$C_{requests} = \left( N_{PUT} \times R_{PUT} \right) + \left( N_{GET} \times R_{GET} \right) + \left( N_{LIST} \times R_{LIST} \right)$$

- $N_{PUT}$, $N_{GET}$, $N_{LIST}$ = number of each request type
- $R_{PUT}$, $R_{GET}$, $R_{LIST}$ = per-1,000-request cost

**3. Data Transfer Cost ($C_{dataTransfer}$):**

$$C_{dataTransfer} = \left( D_{out} \times P_{out} \right) - F_{free}$$

- $D_{out}$ = data transferred **OUT** to internet (in GB)
- $P_{out}$ = per-GB egress price
- $F_{free}$ = **first 100 GB/month is FREE** ✅
- Data transfer **IN to S3 is always FREE**
- Data transfer **between S3 buckets in the same region is FREE**

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** S3 pricing models directly influence architectural decisions — e.g., choosing **Glacier Deep Archive** for compliance logs (7-year retention) can reduce storage cost by **~95%** compared to S3 Standard, but you must factor in the **180-day minimum storage duration penalty** for early deletion.

## 2.5 S3 Security Mechanisms — Layered Defense

| Security Layer | Mechanism | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity & Access** | AWS IAM Users, Roles, Groups | Account-wide |
| **Resource Policy** | Bucket Policy (JSON, IAM-style) | Bucket-wide / per-object |
| **Legacy ACLs** | Access Control Lists (Object/Bucket level) | Per-object (legacy) |
| **Encryption At Rest** | SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C | Per-object |
| **Encryption In Transit** | TLS/SSL (HTTPS only) | All requests |
| **Block Public Access** | Account/Bucket-level toggles | Default ON (2023+) |
| **Pre-signed URLs** | Time-limited shareable URLs | Per-object |
| **MFA Delete** | Multi-factor auth for delete operations | Bucket-wide |
| **Access Analyzer** | Detects unintended public/cross-account access | Account-wide |

## 2.6 S3 Data Protection Features

- **Versioning** → Stores multiple variants of an object. Once enabled, can only be **suspended, not disabled** (to protect from accidental data loss).
- **Replication (CRR / SRR)** → Cross-Region Replication (disaster recovery) or Same-Region Replication (logical separation).
- **Lifecycle Policies** → Automate transitions to cheaper storage classes or expiration of objects.
- **Object Lock** → WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many) model for regulatory compliance.
- **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)** → Allows web apps in one domain to access S3 resources in another.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — S3 Monthly Cost Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-style problem:** An engineering company uses a single S3 bucket in `ap-south-1` with the following monthly usage. Calculate the **total monthly bill** (excluding GST/taxes).

**Given Data:**
- 200 GB stored in **S3 Standard** for the full 30 days
- 50 GB stored in **S3 Standard-IA** for the full 30 days
- 20 GB stored in **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** for 20 days
- 10,000 PUT requests
- 50,000 GET requests
- 30 GB of data transferred out to the internet

**Assumed Price Table (for calculation):**

| Resource | Unit Price (USD) |
|---|---|
| S3 Standard storage | $0.023 per GB-month |
| S3 Standard-IA storage | $0.0125 per GB-month |
| S3 Standard-IA min duration | 30 days |
| S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval | $0.0036 per GB-month |
| PUT request | $0.005 per 1,000 requests |
| GET request | $0.0004 per 1,000 requests |
| Data transfer OUT (ap-south-1) | $0.09 per GB |
| First 100 GB/month data transfer | FREE |

---

### Step 1: Compute Storage Cost

**Sub-step 1(a) — S3 Standard:**

$$C_{std} = 200 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023/\text{GB-month} \times \frac{30}{30}$$

$$C_{std} = 200 \times 0.023 \times 1 = \$4.60$$

**Sub-step 1(b) — S3 Standard-IA** (no early-deletion penalty since stored for 30 days = minimum):

$$C_{IA} = 50 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0125/\text{GB-month} \times \frac{30}{30}$$

$$C_{IA} = 50 \times 0.0125 \times 1 = \$0.625$$

**Sub-step 1(c) — S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** (no early-deletion penalty since 20 days < 90 days minimum → **30-day early-delete penalty applies**):

Storage cost for 20 days:

$$C_{glacier\_raw} = 20 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0036/\text{GB-month} \times \frac{20}{30}$$

$$C_{glacier\_raw} = 20 \times 0.0036 \times 0.6667 = \$0.048$$

Early-deletion penalty for 70 remaining days (90 − 20):

$$C_{glacier\_penalty} = 20 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0036/\text{GB-month} \times \frac{70}{30}$$

$$C_{glacier\_penalty} = 20 \times 0.0036 \times 2.3333 = \$0.168$$

Total Glacier cost:

$$C_{glacier} = C_{glacier\_raw} + C_{glacier\_penalty} = 0.048 + 0.168 = \$0.216$$

**Sub-step 1(d) — Total Storage Cost:**

$$C_{storage} = C_{std} + C_{IA} + C_{glacier} = 4.60 + 0.625 + 0.216 = \$5.441$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Request Cost

**Sub-step 2(a) — PUT requests:**

$$C_{PUT} = \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000} \times \$0.005 = 10 \times 0.005 = \$0.05$$

**Sub-step 2(b) — GET requests:**

$$C_{GET} = \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000} \times \$0.0004 = 50 \times 0.0004 = \$0.02$$

**Sub-step 2(c) — Total Request Cost:**

$$C_{requests} = C_{PUT} + C_{GET} = 0.05 + 0.02 = \$0.07$$

---

### Step 3: Compute Data Transfer Cost

Since transfer OUT is 30 GB, which is **less than the 100 GB free tier**, the data transfer cost is:

$$C_{transfer} = \max\left(0, \ 30 \text{ GB} \times \$0.09 - \$0.00 \right) = \$0.00$$

The company is still **70 GB short** of using the free tier, so this component is $0.

---

### Step 4: Compute Total Monthly Bill

$$C_{total} = C_{storage} + C_{requests} + C_{transfer}$$

$$C_{total} = 5.441 + 0.07 + 0.00 = \boxed{\$5.511 \text{ USD per month}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing S3 vs Other AWS Storage

| Feature | **Amazon S3** | **Amazon EBS** | **Amazon EFS** | **Instance Store** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Type** | Object | Block | File | Block (local) |
| **Access Pattern** | HTTP/REST API | EC2 attach | NFSv4 mount | EC2 attach |
| **Multi-Instance Access** | ✅ Yes (via API) | ❌ Single instance | ✅ Many instances | ❌ Single instance |
| **Persistence** | ✅ Independent of EC2 | ✅ Independent of EC2 life | ✅ Independent | ❌ Lost on stop/terminate |
| **Max Object/Volume Size** | 5 TB per object | 64 TiB per volume | Petabytes | Varies by instance type |
| **Use Case** | Backups, big data, static sites, archives | OS disks, databases | Shared home directories, content management | Temp/scratch space, buffers |
| **Pricing Model** | Per GB + per request + transfer | Per GB provisioned (regardless of use) | Per GB used | Included in EC2 price |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question mentions "shared access across multiple EC2 instances" → choose **EFS** or **S3**. If it mentions "block-level access, mounted as a drive" → choose **EBS**. If it mentions "web-scale, internet-accessible, REST API" → choose **S3**.

## 3.3 Durability Calculation — Worked Example

S3's marketed **11 nines of durability (99.999999999%)** means the annual probability of object loss is:

$$P_{loss} = 1 - D = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 1 \times 10^{-11}$$

For $N$ independent objects, the probability that **at least one** is lost in a year is:

$$P_{any\_loss} = 1 - (1 - P_{loss})^{N} = 1 - (D)^{N}$$

**For 10 million objects** ($N = 10^{7}$):

$$P_{any\_loss} = 1 - (0.99999999999)^{10^7}$$

Using the small-approximation $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for very small $x$:

$$P_{any\_loss} \approx 1 - e^{-10^7 \times 10^{-11}} = 1 - e^{-10^{-4}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

So the chance of losing **at least one object out of 10 million** in a year is approximately **0.01%** — effectively negligible. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why S3 is used as the de-facto storage backend for mission-critical workloads like Netflix's video catalog, Airbnb's photo storage, and NASA/JPL's scientific datasets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 S3 High-Level Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Application] --> B[HTTPS REST API Request]
    B --> C[AWS DNS Edge Location]
    C --> D[S3 Front-End Authentication Server]
    D --> E{IAM Policy Check}
    E -->|Authorized| F[Request Router]
    E -->|Denied| G[Return 403 Forbidden]
    F --> H[Storage Index Lookup]
    H --> I[Storage Node AZ1]
    H --> J[Storage Node AZ2]
    H --> K[Storage Node AZ3]
    I --> L[Replicated Object Response]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[HTTP 200 OK + Data]
    M --> N[Client Receives Object]
```

## 4.2 S3 Object Lifecycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Object Created in S3 Standard] --> B{Days Since Creation}
    B -->|0 to 30 days| C[Stay in Standard]
    B -->|30 to 90 days| D[Transition to Standard IA]
    B -->|90 to 365 days| E[Transition to Glacier Flexible]
    B -->|More than 365 days| F[Object Expired and Deleted]
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
```

## 4.3 S3 Storage Class Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Need to Store Object in S3] --> B{Access Frequency Known?}
    B -->|Yes, Frequent| C[Use S3 Standard]
    B -->|Yes, Infrequent but Critical| D[Use S3 Standard IA]
    B -->|Yes, Infrequent and Non Critical| E[Use S3 One Zone IA]
    B -->|No, Unknown Pattern| F[Use S3 Intelligent Tiering]
    B -->|Archive, Rare Access| G{Need Immediate Retrieval?}
    G -->|Yes, Milliseconds| H[Use S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval]
    G -->|Minutes to Hours OK| I[Use S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval]
    G -->|12 to 48 Hours OK| J[Use S3 Glacier Deep Archive]
```

## 4.4 S3 Security Layering — Defense in Depth

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incoming S3 Request] --> B[TLS SSL in Transit Encryption]
    B --> C[SigV4 Signature Validation]
    C --> D[IAM User Role Check]
    D --> E[Bucket Policy Evaluation]
    E --> F[ACL Object Level Check]
    F --> G[Block Public Access Setting]
    G --> H{All Checks Passed?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Access Granted to Object]
    H -->|No| J[Access Denied 403]
    I --> K[SSE KMS Decryption if Needed]
    K --> L[Object Returned to Client]
```

## 4.5 S3 Data Flow — Upload and Download Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant S3API as S3 REST API
    participant Auth as AWS Auth SigV4
    participant Storage as S3 Storage Node
    Client->>S3API: PUT object data with key
    S3API->>Auth: Validate IAM and bucket policy
    Auth-->>S3API: Authorization confirmed
    S3API->>Storage: Write object to multiple AZs
    Storage-->>S3API: Acknowledgment with ETag
    S3API-->>Client: HTTP 200 OK with version ID
    Note over Client,Storage: Download path
    Client->>S3API: GET object with key
    S3API->>Auth: Re validate credentials
    Auth-->>S3API: Authorized
    S3API->>Storage: Fetch object from nearest AZ
    Storage-->>S3API: Object bytes
    S3API-->>Client: HTTP 200 OK with object data
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Mermaid sequence diagrams are **rarely required** in exams, but flowcharts of S3 architecture, storage class selection, and lifecycle policies are **high-frequency** visual answers worth 2–3 marks each.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Amazon S3. List any FOUR of its key features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) is an object-based cloud storage service provided by AWS that offers **scalability, durability (11 nines = 99.999999999%), availability (99.99%)**, and **security** for storing and retrieving any amount of data over the internet via REST/HTTPS APIs.

**Four Key Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Unlimited Storage** — Buckets can store virtually unlimited number of objects, each up to 5 TB.
2. **Versioning** — Automatically maintains multiple variants of an object, enabling rollback and recovery.
3. **Storage Classes** — Seven tiers (Standard, IA, Glacier family) allowing cost optimization.
4. **Strong Consistency** — All read/write operations reflect the latest state immediately (post Dec 2020).
5. **Fine-grained Security** — IAM, bucket policies, ACLs, encryption, MFA Delete, Block Public Access.
6. **Lifecycle Management** — Automated transition and expiration rules for objects.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠️:** Students often confuse S3's durability (11 nines) with its availability (99.99%). **Durability** = probability data is NOT lost (protects against AZ failures). **Availability** = ability to ACCESS the data when needed. These are two different SLAs — keep them straight!

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the different S3 storage classes with their use cases.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — table or bullet form):**

| Storage Class | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **S3 Standard** | Frequently accessed data — websites, content distribution, analytics |
| **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | Unknown or changing access patterns — auto-moves data between tiers |
| **S3 Standard-IA** | Infrequently accessed but rapid retrieval needed — disaster recovery, backups |
| **S3 One Zone-IA** | Infrequent, non-critical, reproducible data — secondary backups |
| **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** | Archives needing millisecond retrieval — medical images, news media archives |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | Archives with flexible retrieval time (minutes–hours) — compliance archives |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term retention (7–10 years) — regulatory compliance, rarely accessed logs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** As you go **Standard → IA → Glacier → Deep Archive**, storage cost decreases, retrieval time increases, and minimum storage duration increases. Remember: **"Cooler = Cheaper but Slower"** ❄️

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 questions offer an **internal choice**. We provide two equivalent 14-mark questions — both follow the standard **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** structure.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Amazon S3**. Describe the role of each component in the request flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **various storage classes offered by Amazon S3**. Compare their cost, availability, and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a): S3 Architecture (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Amazon S3 follows a **distributed, multi-tenant architecture** with stateless front-end servers and persistent storage nodes spread across multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)** within a chosen AWS region.

**Architecture Components & Their Roles (5 Marks — explained in flow below):**

1. **Client Application** → Initiates HTTP request (PUT/GET/DELETE) over **HTTPS (port 443)** using AWS SDK, CLI, or REST API.
2. **AWS Edge / DNS Layer** → Routes the request to the nearest S3 regional endpoint using Amazon Route 53 DNS and CloudFront edge locations.
3. **Front-End Server (Stateless)** → Authenticates the request using **AWS Signature Version 4 (SigV4)**, checks **IAM policies**, **bucket policies**, and **ACLs**, then routes to appropriate storage.
4. **Storage Index / Metadata Service** → Maintains a global namespace index mapping `(bucket, key) → storage location`. Enables low-latency object lookup.
5. **Storage Nodes (across AZs)** → The actual physical storage layer. Each object's data is **replicated synchronously** across **at least 3 AZs** for durability.
6. **Response Builder** → Returns HTTP response with status code, **ETag (MD5 hash)**, and object data (for GETs).

**Diagram (1 Mark)** — see Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above.

**Conclusion:** S3's architecture is **horizontally scalable** — additional storage nodes can be added without downtime, enabling **unlimited storage capacity**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b): S3 Storage Classes (7 Marks)

**Detailed Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Storage Class | AZ Redundancy | Availability | Min. Storage | Retrieval Time | Cost (Approx) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S3 Standard | ≥3 AZs | 99.99% | None | Immediate | $0.023/GB-mo |
| Intelligent-Tiering | ≥3 AZs | 99.9% | None | Immediate | $0.023/GB-mo + monitoring fee |
| Standard-IA | ≥3 AZs | 99.9% | 30 days | Immediate | $0.0125/GB-mo |
| One Zone-IA | 1 AZ | 99.5% | 30 days | Immediate | $0.01/GB-mo |
| Glacier Instant | ≥3 AZs | 99.9% | 90 days | Milliseconds | $0.004/GB-mo |
| Glacier Flexible | ≥3 AZs | 99.99% | 90 days | Minutes to Hours | $0.0036/GB-mo |
| Glacier Deep Archive | ≥3 AZs | 99.99% | 180 days | 12–48 hours | $0.00099/GB-mo |

**Real-World Use Cases (2 Marks):**
- **E-commerce product images** → S3 Standard
- **System backups taken weekly** → S3 Standard-IA
- **Netflix old movie archive** → S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
- **Hospital X-rays retained for 10 years** → S3 Glacier Deep Archive
- **AI training datasets with unpredictable access** → S3 Intelligent-Tiering

**Conclusion:** Choosing the right S3 storage class can lead to **up to 95% cost savings** while meeting the application's availability and durability requirements.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **S3 data consistency model**. How does it differ from the traditional eventually-consistent model? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **S3 security model** in detail. Explain IAM, bucket policies, ACLs, encryption, and pre-signed URLs with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a): S3 Data Consistency Model (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data consistency in distributed systems refers to the guarantee that **all clients see the same view of the data** at any given point in time.

**Traditional Eventual Consistency (Pre-Dec 2020) (2 Marks):**
- After a **PUT (overwrite)** or **DELETE**, subsequent GETs **might return the old value** for a brief window.
- Replicas across AZs propagated asynchronously, so reads from a different AZ could see stale data.
- Example: After `PUT object-v2` to AZ1, a GET to AZ2 might still return `object-v1` for a few seconds.

**Current Strong Read-After-Write Consistency (Post-Dec 2020) (3 Marks):**
- Amazon S3 now provides **strong read-after-write consistency for ALL operations** without any performance or availability impact.
- After a successful **PUT**, any subsequent GET/LIST returns the newly written data.
- After a successful **DELETE**, any subsequent GET returns 404 (`NoSuchKey`).
- LIST operations return a strongly consistent view of all objects in the bucket.
- This was a major architectural upgrade that simplified application design (no longer needed to handle stale reads).

**Why It Matters (1 Mark):**
Strong consistency enables use cases like **immediate read-after-write for distributed lock files**, **real-time metadata updates**, and **zero-stale-read e-commerce catalogs** — eliminating entire categories of race-condition bugs.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b): S3 Security Model (7 Marks)

**1. Identity & Access Management (IAM) (2 Marks):**
- Centralized service for managing users, groups, and roles.
- IAM policies (JSON) define allowed/denied actions on S3 resources.
- Example IAM policy snippet:
```json
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": "s3:GetObject",
  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/*"
}
```

**2. Bucket Policies (2 Marks):**
- Resource-based JSON policies attached directly to a bucket.
- Can grant cross-account access, restrict by IP, enforce HTTPS, etc.
- **Use case:** Allow a partner company to read specific objects without giving them your AWS credentials.

**3. Access Control Lists — ACLs (1 Mark):**
- Legacy mechanism granting read/write permissions to AWS accounts on individual objects or buckets.
- Mostly superseded by IAM and bucket policies; recommended only for very specific legacy scenarios.

**4. Encryption (1 Mark):**
- **SSE-S3** → AWS manages keys with AES-256 (default, free).
- **SSE-KMS** → AWS Key Management Service (audit trail, fine-grained control).
- **SSE-C** → Customer-provided keys (you manage key lifecycle).
- **Client-Side Encryption** → Data encrypted before upload.

**5. Pre-signed URLs (1 Mark):**
- Time-limited URLs that grant temporary access to private S3 objects.
- Generated using your IAM credentials with an expiry timestamp.
- **Use case:** Allow a user to download a private invoice from a web app without creating an AWS account.

**Block Public Access (Bonus)**: Account- and bucket-level settings introduced in 2018 to **override any policy/ACL that grants public access**. Default is ON in all new accounts since April 2023.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️:** When asked about S3 security, students often list ONLY IAM and forget **bucket policies, ACLs, encryption, pre-signed URLs, and Block Public Access**. The question explicitly asks for **all layers** — missing even one costs 1–2 marks. Always present a **defense-in-depth** view.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **S3 = Object Storage** (not block, not file system) — accessed via **HTTP REST API**.
- ⭐ **Durability = 11 nines (99.999999999%)** via multi-AZ synchronous replication.
- ⭐ **Availability = 99.99%** for Standard (separate metric — don't confuse with durability).
- ⭐ **Bucket names are globally unique** across all AWS accounts (not region-specific).
- ⭐ **Max object size = 5 TB**; max number of objects per bucket = unlimited.
- ⭐ **Strong read-after-write consistency** for ALL operations (since December 2020).
- ⭐ **First 100 GB/month data transfer OUT is FREE**; data transfer IN is always FREE.
- ⭐ **S3 Standard → IA → Glacier → Deep Archive**: storage cost ↓, retrieval time ↑, min storage duration ↑.
- ⭐ **Versioning, once enabled, can only be suspended** (not disabled) — protects against accidental deletion.
- ⭐ **Glacier has minimum storage durations** (30 / 90 / 90 / 180 days) — early deletion incurs a penalty for the remaining days.
- ⭐ **Security layers**: IAM → Bucket Policy → ACL → Block Public Access → Encryption (at rest + in transit) → Pre-signed URLs.
- ⭐ **Lifecycle policies** automate transitions and expirations — critical for cost optimization.
- ⭐ **Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** = DR use case; **Same-Region Replication (SRR)** = log aggregation/compliance.
- ⭐ **S3 is NOT a file system** — folders in S3 are just part of the **key (path)** string, not real directories.
- ⭐ **Pre-signed URLs** use your **IAM credentials** to sign → the recipient does NOT need AWS credentials.
- ⭐ **HTTPS (TLS)** is mandatory for all S3 API calls — HTTP is rejected by default.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amazon EBS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amazon EBS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Amazon EBS

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS)** is a high-performance, easy-to-use, secure block storage service designed specifically for use with Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) instances for both **throughput** and **transaction-intensive workloads** at any scale. It provides persistent block-level storage volumes that can be attached to a running EC2 instance and exposed as raw, unformatted block devices.

From the perspective of KTU's Cloud Computing module (IaaS layer), EBS is positioned as the **primary persistent storage backbone** for virtual machines in the AWS Cloud, forming the de-facto equivalent of a directly-attached physical hard disk or SSD in a traditional on-premise data center, but delivered as a network-attached, virtualized, and elastic cloud resource.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Storage Classification:**
> Amazon EBS falls under the **Block Storage** category of cloud storage. It is fundamentally different from:
> - **Object Storage** (e.g., Amazon S3) — accessed via REST APIs over HTTP/HTTPS.
> - **File Storage** (e.g., Amazon EFS) — accessed via NFS/SMB mount points.
> - **Instance Store** — ephemeral, host-local storage tied to the EC2 instance lifecycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have rented an office space (EC2 instance) in a large commercial building (AWS data center). The office itself does not come with permanent filing cabinets. Instead, the building manager gives you the option to rent **networked, redundant, and highly-available storage lockers** that connect to your office via a high-speed internal network.

- You can rent lockers of different sizes and speeds: **cheap large cabinets (st1/sc1)**, **balanced all-purpose cabinets (gp3)**, or **ultra-fast SSD cabinets (io2 Block Express)**.
- Even if you move offices (terminate your EC2 instance), the contents of your locker (EBS volume) remain intact. You can detach the locker, take it to a new office, and reattach it.
- The building manager automatically makes **offsite backups (snapshots)** of your locker every night, stored in a separate, secure vault.

This locker system is **Amazon EBS** — durable, persistent, network-attached, and independent of the compute instance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **EBS Volume** = A virtualized, persistent, block-level storage device that appears as a raw, unformatted block device (e.g., `/dev/xvdf` on Linux, `xvdf` on Windows) to the EC2 instance's operating system, capable of being formatted with a file system (ext4, XFS, NTFS, etc.) and used as a regular mounted drive.

### 1.3 Key Physical/Logical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized across AWS documentation and are critical for any KTU numerical or descriptive question:

- **Maximum Volume Size:** **64 TiB** (Tebibytes, where 1 TiB = 1024 GiB) per EBS volume for most volume types. Some legacy types (standard) are limited to **1 TiB**.
- **Maximum IOPS per Volume:** **256,000 IOPS** (achieved by `io2 Block Express` on Nitro System EC2 instances).
- **Maximum Throughput per Volume:** **4,000 MB/s** (achieved by `gp3` and `io1` on certain instance families).
- **Default EBS-Optimized Instance Bandwidth:** Ranges from **500 Mbps** to **12,500 Mbps** depending on the EC2 instance type.
- **Snapshot Retention Limit:** No hard limit (snapshots are incremental and persist until manually deleted).
- **Standard Replication:** Within a single **Availability Zone (AZ)** — EBS volumes are AZ-scoped resources (cannot be attached across AZs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Amazon EBS operates via a tightly-coupled interaction between four logical layers:

#### Step 1: Volume Provisioning (Creation)
- A user submits a `CreateVolume` API call (or uses the AWS Console/CLI).
- AWS allocates the volume within a specific **Availability Zone (AZ)**. The volume is replicated automatically within that AZ to protect against hardware failure (no data loss on single component failure; **99.999% durability** advertised for the underlying infrastructure).
- The volume is, by default, **unencrypted** unless encryption is explicitly enabled at creation or inherited via the AWS account's default EBS encryption settings.

#### Step 2: Attachment to EC2 Instance
- The volume is attached to an EC2 instance within the **same AZ** using the `AttachVolume` API.
- The EC2 Nitro System exposes the attached volume as a **virtually-attached NVMe** device (for Nitro-based instances) or as a virtio-blk device (older virtualization).
- Standard device naming conventions: `/dev/sd[f-p]` for the first 16 attachments, `/dev/xvd[f-p]` for newer Linux AMIs.

#### Step 3: Formatting and Mounting
- Once attached, the OS recognizes the raw block device.
- The user creates a **file system** (ext4, XFS, NTFS, etc.) on the device using utilities like `mkfs.xfs` or `mkfs.ext4`.
- The volume is then **mounted** to a directory in the EC2 instance's file system hierarchy (e.g., `/data`).

#### Step 4: I/O Operations
- The EC2 instance issues **read/write I/O requests** to the volume.
- Requests travel over the AWS internal network via the **EBS-optimized** dedicated network interface (no network contention with general instance traffic).
- EBS uses a **replication and consistency model** where writes are acknowledged only after being replicated to multiple servers within the AZ.

#### Step 5: Snapshotting (Backup)
- A point-in-time snapshot can be created at any moment.
- Snapshots are **incremental** — only the blocks that have changed since the previous snapshot are saved.
- The first snapshot is a full copy; subsequent snapshots save **delta blocks**.
- Snapshots are stored in **Amazon S3** (managed internally by AWS, not directly visible to the user).

#### Step 6: Detachment and Re-Attachment
- The volume can be detached from one instance and re-attached to another instance (within the same AZ) — typical for **lift-and-shift** scenarios, patching workflows, or forensic analysis.

### 2.2 EBS Volume Type Taxonomy (THE MOST HIGH-YIELD KTU TOPIC)

AWS provides **six EBS volume types**, divided into two categories based on the underlying media:

#### Category A: SSD-Backed Volumes (Optimized for IOPS — transactional workloads)

| Volume Type | Use Case | Volume Size | Max IOPS/Volume | Max Throughput/Volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **gp3** (General Purpose SSD — current generation) | Most workloads; cost-effective, predictable performance | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 4,000 (baseline), up to 16,000 (provisioned) | 125 MB/s (baseline), up to 1,000 MB/s (provisioned) |
| **gp2** (General Purpose SSD — legacy) | Small to medium DBs, dev/test | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 3,000 (baseline) | 128 – 250 MB/s (burst) |
| **io2 Block Express** | Mission-critical, sub-millisecond latency, largest workloads | 4 GiB – 64 TiB | **256,000** | 4,000 MB/s |
| **io1** (Provisioned IOPS SSD) | Critical I/O-intensive relational DBs (Oracle, SQL Server) | 4 GiB – 16 TiB | 64,000 | 1,000 MB/s |

#### Category B: HDD-Backed Volumes (Optimized for throughput — streaming workloads)

| Volume Type | Use Case | Volume Size | Max IOPS/Volume | Max Throughput/Volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **st1** (Throughput Optimized HDD) | Big data, log processing, Kafka, data warehousing | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | 500 | 500 MB/s |
| **sc1** (Cold HDD) | Infrequent access, lowest-cost storage | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | 250 | 250 MB/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **SSD-backed** volumes offer sub-millisecond latency and are suitable for **boot volumes, databases, and transactional systems**.
> - **HDD-backed** volumes offer millisecond latency and are suitable for **streaming, log processing, and large sequential workloads**.
> - HDD-backed volumes **cannot** be used as **boot volumes** — this is a favorite KTU short-answer question.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind gp3's Superiority over gp2

The **`gp3`** volume type was introduced to decouple storage capacity from performance. In legacy **`gp2`**, performance scales with volume size:
- Baseline IOPS = **3 × volume size in GiB** (capped at 3,000 IOPS at 1,000 GiB).
- Burst capability exists via **I/O credits** (5.4 million credits tank, refilled at a rate dependent on volume size).

In **`gp3`**, the baseline is **3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s** *independent* of volume size. Users can independently provision additional IOPS (up to 16,000) and throughput (up to 1,000 MB/s) at a fixed rate, regardless of disk capacity. This yields **20% lower price-per-GB** than gp2 and **predictable performance**.

### 2.4 EBS Snapshots — Deep Mechanism

Snapshots are **point-in-time, crash-consistent** copies of EBS volumes stored durably in Amazon S3.

- **Incremental Nature:** Only blocks modified since the prior snapshot are saved.
- **Crash Consistency:** Snapshots capture the volume state as if the EC2 instance experienced an immediate power loss. For application-consistent snapshots, the OS/application-level buffers must be flushed first (typically by quiescing the OS before snapshotting).
- **Copy across AZs/Regions:** Snapshots can be copied to other AZs or AWS Regions, enabling **DR (Disaster Recovery)** and **global data distribution** strategies.
- **Lifecycle Management:** AWS Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) automates snapshot creation, retention, and deletion.
- **Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR):** Initializes snapshots to deliver their full provisioned performance immediately upon creation (otherwise, performance is initialized lazily on first read).

### 2.5 EBS Encryption

Encryption is supported on **all EBS volume types** using **AES-256** (Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit keys).

- Encryption is implemented at the **hypervisor layer** between the EC2 instance and the EBS storage infrastructure.
- Encryption keys are managed via **AWS Key Management Service (KMS)** — either AWS-managed keys (default) or customer-managed CMKs.
- Snapshots of encrypted volumes are automatically encrypted; encrypted snapshots can only produce encrypted volumes.
- **No performance penalty** — encryption is hardware-accelerated on the Nitro System.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| EBS Annual Durability SLA | **99.999%** (five nines) | Per-AZ replication guarantee |
| gp2 Baseline IOPS | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{baseline}} = 3 \times \text{VolumeSize}_{\text{GiB}}$ | Capped at 3,000 IOPS |
| gp2 Burst IOPS Duration | $\text{BurstDuration} = \frac{\text{IOCreditBalance}}{\text{BurstIOPS} - \text{BaselineIOPS}}$ | Seconds |
| Snapshot Storage Cost | First snapshot = full volume size; subsequent = **incremental delta** | Billed per GB-month in S3 |
| gp3 Baseline Performance (independent of size) | **3,000 IOPS, 125 MB/s** | Provisioned IOPS/throughput are extra |
| io2 Block Express Durability | **99.999%** with **5 9s of availability** | For mission-critical workloads |
| Snapshot Max Throughput Copy | **500 MB/s** per snapshot copy operation | Concurrent copies possible |
| Maximum Volume Size | **64 TiB** | For most modern types |
| Provisioned IOPS to Throughput Ratio (io1/io2) | 1 IOPS ≈ **256 KB/s** | Rule of thumb for sizing |
| Required IOPS Calculation | $\text{Required IOPS} = \frac{\text{PeakReadsPerSec} + \text{PeakWritesPerSec}}{\text{ReplicationFactor}}$ | For RAID 1/10 configurations |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

EBS is the **backbone storage layer** for nearly every production AWS workload. In the real engineering world:

- **Boot volumes** for EC2 instances (typically `gp3` or `gp2`).
- **Database storage** for RDS, self-managed MySQL/PostgreSQL on EC2 (`io2 Block Express` for transactional consistency).
- **Big data analytics** pipelines (Hadoop, Spark) using `st1` for HDFS intermediate data.
- **Containerized workloads** on Amazon EKS, where EBS volumes back Kubernetes PersistentVolumes via the **EBS CSI Driver**.
- **Disaster Recovery** architectures where cross-region snapshot copies enable RPOs of minutes to hours.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — gp2 IOPS and Burst Credit Calculation

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A KTU student provisions a **500 GiB gp2** EBS volume. Calculate:
1. The baseline IOPS.
2. The I/O credit accumulation rate per second.
3. The maximum sustained burst IOPS and the duration for which it can burst given a starting credit balance of **5,400,000 I/O credits**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Baseline IOPS:**

The baseline IOPS for a gp2 volume is calculated as:

$$
\text{Baseline IOPS} = 3 \times \text{Volume Size in GiB}
$$

$$
\text{Baseline IOPS} = 3 \times 500 = 1{,}500 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the I/O Credit Accumulation Rate:**

The I/O credit balance for gp2 is designed to refill at a rate that maintains the baseline IOPS indefinitely. The maximum credit balance is 5,400,000 credits (the "I/O credit tank").

$$
\text{Credit Refill Rate} = \text{Baseline IOPS} = 1{,}500 \text{ credits/second}
$$

This means that, at rest, the credit balance refills at exactly 1,500 credits per second and will reach the maximum tank of 5,400,000 credits if no I/O is performed.

**Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Burst IOPS:**

gp2 volumes can burst up to **3,000 IOPS**.

$$
\text{Burst IOPS} = \min(3{,}000,\ 3 \times 1{,}000) = 3{,}000 \text{ IOPS}
$$

Since the volume is only 500 GiB, it can never reach 3,000 IOPS of *baseline* performance. However, it can burst to 3,000 IOPS using accumulated I/O credits.

**Step 4 — Compute the Burst Duration:**

The burst continues as long as the credit balance is non-negative. The rate of credit consumption during bursting is:

$$
\text{Credit Consumption Rate} = \text{Burst IOPS} - \text{Baseline IOPS} = 3{,}000 - 1{,}500 = 1{,}500 \text{ credits/second}
$$

The burst duration is therefore:

$$
\text{Burst Duration} = \frac{\text{Initial Credit Balance}}{\text{Credit Consumption Rate}}
$$

$$
\text{Burst Duration} = \frac{5{,}400{,}000}{1{,}500} = 3{,}600 \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
\text{Burst Duration} = \frac{3{,}600}{60} = 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

#### Verification:

A full tank of 5,400,000 credits at a consumption rate of 1,500 credits/second sustains exactly 1 hour of bursting. This result is dimensionally and physically consistent with AWS-published specifications.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Sizing an io1 Volume for a Database Workload

**Problem Statement:**
A PostgreSQL database running on EC2 requires **10,000 IOPS** sustained and **500 MB/s throughput**. The workload is 70% reads and 30% writes. Each I/O operation averages **16 KB**.

Compute:
1. The minimum provisioned IOPS needed.
2. Whether an `io1` or `io2 Block Express` volume should be selected.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Translate Throughput to IOPS:**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-derived}} = \frac{\text{Throughput (MB/s)}}{\text{Avg I/O Size (MB)}}
$$

Since 16 KB = 0.015625 MB:

$$
\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-derived}} = \frac{500}{0.015625} = 32{,}000 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare Against the Required IOPS:**

The workload requires 10,000 IOPS *as a primary metric* and 32,000 IOPS *to achieve the throughput target*. The bottleneck is the higher of the two — the throughput requirement drives the IOPS sizing.

$$
\text{Required Provisioned IOPS} = \max(10{,}000,\ 32{,}000) = 32{,}000 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Select the Appropriate Volume Type:**

- **`io1`** supports up to **64,000 IOPS** and **1,000 MB/s** → this workload fits within io1's limits.
- **`io2 Block Express`** supports up to **256,000 IOPS** and **4,000 MB/s** → also fits, with significantly higher durability (99.999%).

For cost optimization, `io1` is sufficient. For maximum durability and future-proofing, `io2 Block Express` is recommended.

**Step 4 — Sizing Recommendation:**

A volume of at least **1 TiB** (1,024 GiB) provisioned with **32,000 IOPS** would satisfy both requirements. The minimum size for io1/io2 is 4 GiB, but sizing at 1 TiB provides IOPS headroom and storage capacity for database growth.

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Cost Calculation for Snapshots (Conceptual)

**Problem Statement:**
A user creates a 100 GiB EBS volume. They create a daily snapshot at the end of each day for 7 days. The daily change rate (data modification) is **5 GiB per day**. Calculate:
1. The total S3 storage consumed by snapshots at the end of day 7.
2. The total GB-month billed.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Snapshot Storage Accumulation:**

| Snapshot | Type | Size in S3 |
|---|---|---|
| Day 1 | Full copy | 100 GiB |
| Day 2 | Delta from Day 1 | 5 GiB |
| Day 3 | Delta from Day 2 | 5 GiB |
| Day 4 | Delta from Day 3 | 5 GiB |
| Day 5 | Delta from Day 4 | 5 GiB |
| Day 6 | Delta from Day 5 | 5 GiB |
| Day 7 | Delta from Day 6 | 5 GiB |

$$
\text{Total S3 Storage} = 100 + (6 \times 5) = 100 + 30 = 130 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to GB-Month (Billing Metric):**

Assuming snapshots are retained for a full month (30 days):

$$
\text{Snapshot Storage at Day 30} = 100 + (29 \times 5) = 100 + 145 = 245 \text{ GiB}
$$

For partial retention or shorter periods, AWS bills **pro-rata** per GB-hour of storage.

This example illustrates why **incremental snapshots** dramatically reduce backup costs compared to full daily copies.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Volume Selection

| Workload Profile | Recommended Volume | Key Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Boot volume for any EC2 | `gp3` | Cost-effective, predictable, low-latency |
| MySQL/PostgreSQL OLTP database | `io2 Block Express` or `io1` | High IOPS, low latency, durability |
| Big data Hadoop/Spark HDFS | `st1` | High throughput, sequential I/O |
| Log archival, cold backups | `sc1` | Lowest cost, infrequent access |
| MongoDB / Cassandra NoSQL | `io2 Block Express` | Sustained random I/O at high IOPS |
| CI/CD build agents | `gp3` | Balanced cost-performance |
| Video streaming transcoding scratch | `st1` | High throughput, large sequential writes |
| Machine learning training datasets | `st1` or `io2` (based on random vs. sequential access) | Depends on access pattern |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level EBS Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EC2Inst["EC2 Instance\nNitro Hypervisor"] -->|NVMe Attachment| EBSVol["EBS Volume\nLogical Block Device"]
    EBSVol -->|Replicated Within AZ| EBSRepl["EBS Storage Node\nMulti-AZ Replica Set"]
    EBSRepl -->|Snapshot Triggered| S3Snap["S3 Snapshot Bucket\nEncrypted at Rest"]
    EBSVol -->|Read Write I/O| OSFS["Operating System\nFile System Layer"]
    OSFS -->|Application APIs| App["Database or Application"]
    KMS["AWS KMS\nEncryption Keys"] -->|AES-256| EBSVol
    S3Snap -->|Restore| EBSVol
    SnapshotAPI["CreateSnapshot API"] --> S3Snap
    DLM["Data Lifecycle Manager"] -->|Automates| SnapshotAPI
```

### 4.2 EBS Volume Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Select EBS Volume Type"] --> Q1{"Is this a boot volume?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> GP3["Use gp3\n3,000 IOPS baseline"]
```
