```mermaid
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Workload is transactional\ndatabases OLTP?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{"Mission critical with\nsub-ms latency?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> IO2["Use io2 Block Express\nUp to 256,000 IOPS"]
    Q3 -- No --> IO1["Use io1\nUp to 64,000 IOPS"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{"Workload is streaming\nor sequential?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{"Accessed frequently?"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> ST1["Use st1\nThroughput Optimized HDD"]
    Q5 -- No --> SC1["Use sc1\nCold HDD Lowest Cost"]
    Q4 -- No --> GP3ALT["Use gp3\nGeneral Purpose SSD"]
```

### 4.3 EBS Snapshot Lifecycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SourceVol["Source EBS Volume\nin Availability Zone A"] -->|CreateSnapshot| Snap1["Snapshot 1\nFull Copy Stored in S3"]
    Snap1 -->|Incremental Update| Snap2["Snapshot 2\nDelta Blocks Only"]
    Snap2 -->|Incremental Update| Snap3["Snapshot 3\nDelta Blocks Only"]
    Snap1 -->|Copy Operation| SnapCopyAZB["Snapshot Copy\nin Availability Zone B"]
    Snap1 -->|Copy Operation| SnapCopyReg["Snapshot Copy\nin Different Region"]
    SnapCopyAZB -->|CreateVolume| NewVolB["Restored Volume in AZ B"]
    SnapCopyReg -->|CreateVolume| NewVolReg["Restored Volume in Other Region"]
    DLM2["Data Lifecycle Manager DLM"] -->|Schedules and Retains| Snap1
    DLM2 -->|Schedules and Retains| Snap2
    DLM2 -->|Schedules and Retains| Snap3
```

### 4.4 EBS vs Instance Store Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EC2A["EC2 Instance with EBS"]
        EBS1["EBS Volume\nNetwork Attached\nPersistent Across Stop Start"]
        Inst1["Instance Lifecycle\nBound to Volume\nAZ Scoped"]
    end
    subgraph EC2B["EC2 Instance with Instance Store"]
        IS1["Instance Store\nPhysically Attached\nHost Local Disk"]
        Inst2["Instance Lifecycle\nData Lost on Stop or Termination"]
    end
    EBS1 -->|Detach and Reattach| OtherInst["Another EC2 Instance\nSame AZ"]
    IS1 -.->|Cannot Reattach| X["Data Lost on Instance Stop"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EBS I/O Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App["Application Issues I/O Request"] --> SysCall["Operating System Syscall\nread write open"]
    SysCall --> FSLayer["File System Layer\next4 XFS NTFS ZFS"]
    FSLayer --> BlockLayer["Linux Block Layer\nIO Scheduler"]
    BlockLayer --> NVMeDrv["NVMe Driver\nEBS Nitro Module"]
    NVMeDrv --> EBSOpt["EBS Optimized Network Interface\nDedicated Bandwidth"]
    EBSOpt --> EBSFabric["AWS EBS Storage Fabric\nAZ Internal Network"]
    EBSFabric --> Replica1["EBS Replica Node 1\nPrimary"]
    EBSFabric --> Replica2["EBS Replica Node 2\nSecondary"]
    EBSFabric --> Replica3["EBS Replica Node 3\nTertiary"]
    Replica1 -->|Acknowledge Write| NVMeDrv
    Replica2 -->|Synchronous Replication| Replica1
    Replica3 -->|Asynchronous Optional| Replica1
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Amazon EBS. Mention any two key differences between EBS and Instance Store.

**Model Answer:**

Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) is a high-performance block storage service provided by AWS that offers persistent, network-attached storage volumes for use with Amazon EC2 instances. EBS volumes behave like raw, unformatted block devices that can be attached, formatted with a file system, and mounted like a physical disk.

**Key Differences between EBS and Instance Store:**

| Feature | Amazon EBS | Instance Store |
|---|---|---|
| **Persistence** | Data persists beyond instance lifecycle | Data is lost when instance stops, terminates, or fails |
| **Attachment Scope** | Can be detached and reattached to another instance | Physically tied to the host machine, cannot be reattached |
| **Storage Type** | Network-attached block storage | Local physical disk on the host server |
| **Use Case** | Databases, boot volumes, persistent workloads | Temporary caches, buffers, scratch data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always explicitly state that EBS is **AZ-scoped** and **persistent**, while Instance Store is **host-local** and **ephemeral**. Students often lose marks by mentioning only one feature.

---

#### Question 2: List any three EBS volume types and specify one appropriate use case for each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **gp3 (General Purpose SSD):** Used as boot volumes for EC2 instances and for general workloads such as small-to-medium databases and development environments.

2. **io2 Block Express (Provisioned IOPS SSD):** Used for mission-critical, latency-sensitive relational databases like Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, and high-performance NoSQL databases requiring up to 256,000 IOPS per volume.

3. **st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD):** Used for big data workloads, log processing, and streaming workloads such as Kafka, MapReduce, and large sequential data pipelines.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the different categories of Amazon EBS volume types. Compare SSD-backed and HDD-backed volume types in terms of performance characteristics and suitable workloads. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture of Amazon EBS including the role of Availability Zones, snapshots, encryption, and the EBS-optimized instance connection. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Amazon EBS volume types are classified into two major categories based on the underlying storage media:

**Category 1: SSD-Backed Volumes**
SSD-backed volumes are optimized for **transactional, IOPS-intensive workloads**. They provide **sub-millisecond latency** and are suitable for use as boot volumes and databases.

- **gp3 (General Purpose SSD — Current Generation):**
  - Size: 1 GiB to 16 TiB.
  - Baseline: 3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s, independent of volume size.
  - Provisioned up to 16,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s for an additional fee.
  - 20% cheaper per GB than gp2.
- **gp2 (General Purpose SSD — Legacy):**
  - Baseline IOPS scales as $3 \times \text{size in GiB}$, capped at 3,000 IOPS at 1,000 GiB.
  - Burstable up to 3,000 IOPS using I/O credits.
- **io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD):**
  - For critical I/O-intensive relational databases.
  - Up to 64,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s.
- **io2 Block Express:**
  - Highest performance, up to 256,000 IOPS, 4,000 MB/s, and 99.999% durability.
  - Requires Nitro System EC2 instances.

**Category 2: HDD-Backed Volumes**
HDD-backed volumes are optimized for **streaming and throughput-intensive workloads**. They provide **millisecond-range latency** and cannot be used as boot volumes.

- **st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD):**
  - Size: 125 GiB to 16 TiB.
  - Up to 500 MB/s throughput, 500 IOPS.
  - Suited for Hadoop, Kafka, log processing.
- **sc1 (Cold HDD):**
  - Lowest-cost EBS option.
  - Up to 250 MB/s and 250 IOPS.
  - For infrequent access workloads such as archival storage.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Parameter | SSD-Backed | HDD-Backed |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | Sub-millisecond | Millisecond range |
| Optimization | IOPS (random I/O) | Throughput (sequential I/O) |
| Boot Volume Support | Yes | No |
| Typical Cost | Higher per GB | Lower per GB |

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

The architecture of Amazon EBS is illustrated by the following structured description:

**1. Availability Zone (AZ) Scoping:**
EBS volumes are created within a specific AZ. They are automatically replicated within the AZ to provide high durability (99.999%) and availability. EBS volumes cannot be attached to instances in a different AZ; they are isolated resources bound to a single AZ.

**2. EC2 Instance Attachment:**
An EBS volume is attached to an EC2 instance over the AWS internal network via an EBS-optimized connection. The Nitro System exposes the volume as an NVMe device, which the guest OS recognizes as a block device.

**3. Encryption:**
All data on EBS volumes can be encrypted using AES-256, with keys managed via AWS Key Management Service (KMS). Encryption occurs transparently at the hypervisor layer, with no performance penalty on Nitro-based instances.

**4. Snapshots:**
Snapshots are point-in-time copies of volumes, stored durably in Amazon S3. They are incremental — only changed blocks are saved after the first full snapshot. Snapshots can be copied across AZs or Regions for disaster recovery.

**5. Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM):**
DLM automates the creation, retention, and deletion of snapshots, ensuring compliance with backup policies.

**Block Diagram (Refer to SECTION_4.1 above):**
The architecture consists of: EC2 Instance → EBS Volume → EBS Replica Set (within AZ) → Snapshots stored in S3, with KMS handling encryption keys.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is an EBS snapshot? Explain how incremental snapshots reduce storage costs. Compute the total S3 storage consumed by a 200 GiB volume with daily 10 GiB change rate for 10 days. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain EBS encryption. Discuss the role of AWS KMS in EBS encryption and describe the process of encrypting a volume and its snapshots. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

An EBS snapshot is a **point-in-time, crash-consistent copy** of an EBS volume, stored durably in Amazon S3. Snapshots serve as the primary backup and disaster-recovery mechanism for EBS volumes.

**Incremental Snapshot Mechanism:**

The first snapshot of a volume is a **full copy** of all the volume's data. Subsequent snapshots are **incremental** — only the blocks that have changed since the prior snapshot are saved. AWS tracks the differences at the **block level** (each block is 512 KB).

**Cost Reduction Example:**

| Snapshot Day | Snapshot Type | Storage Added |
|---|---|---|
| Day 1 | Full | 200 GiB |
| Day 2 | Delta | 10 GiB |
| Day 3 | Delta | 10 GiB |
| ... | ... | ... |
| Day 10 | Delta | 10 GiB |

$$
\text{Total S3 Storage} = 200 + (9 \times 10) = 200 + 90 = 290 \text{ GiB}
$$

Without incremental snapshots (full daily copies), the storage would be $10 \times 200 = 2{,}000$ GiB, making incremental snapshots dramatically more cost-efficient.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

EBS encryption provides **data-at-rest protection** by automatically encrypting data on the volume, data moving between the volume and the instance, and all snapshots created from the volume. AWS uses the **AES-256 algorithm**, a symmetric block cipher widely adopted as an industry standard.

**Role of AWS KMS:**

AWS Key Management Service (KMS) is the centralized key management service used by EBS to generate, manage, and protect cryptographic keys. KMS integrates seamlessly with EBS to:

- Generate and store **Customer Master Keys (CMKs)**.
- Define key access policies (IAM-based).
- Maintain an **audit trail** via AWS CloudTrail for every key usage event.
- Enable **automatic key rotation** (typically annual).

**Encryption Process:**

1. **User Action:** A user creates an EBS volume with encryption enabled (or relies on the account's default EBS encryption setting).
2. **KMS Key Selection:** AWS KMS supplies a CMK — either AWS-managed (default) or customer-managed.
3. **Volume Encryption:** A unique **data encryption key (DEK)** is generated for the volume. The DEK is encrypted by the CMK and stored with the volume metadata. The DEK is used to encrypt all blocks written to the volume.
4. **In-Transit Encryption:** Data moving between EC2 and EBS is encrypted at the Nitro hardware level.
5. **Snapshot Encryption:** When a snapshot is created from an encrypted volume, the snapshot is automatically encrypted using the same CMK.
6. **Restored Volume:** A volume created from an encrypted snapshot is automatically encrypted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse encryption scope:** Students often wrongly state that EBS encryption protects data only on the volume. Correctly mention that encryption covers (a) data at rest on the volume, (b) data in transit between instance and volume, and (c) all snapshots.
> 2. **Snapshot copy pitfall:** When copying a snapshot across regions, the destination snapshot may be encrypted with a different key. Specify explicitly that you can re-encrypt during the copy operation.
> 3. **HDD-backed boot volume:** Stating that "any volume can be a boot volume" is a common mistake. HDD-backed volumes (`st1`, `sc1`) **cannot** be used as boot volumes. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this.
> 4. **IOPS formula for gp2:** Many students forget to cap the baseline IOPS at 3,000 for volumes larger than 1 TiB. Always include the cap.
> 5. **Snapshot data consistency:** Failing to mention that snapshots are **crash-consistent** (not application-consistent by default) loses marks. Application-consistency requires pre-snapshot OS quiescing.
> 6. **AZ-scoping:** Some students incorrectly claim EBS volumes can be attached across AZs. They cannot — AZ-scoped attachment is a hard architectural constraint.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EBS = Block-level, persistent, network-attached storage** for EC2 instances.
- **EBS volumes are AZ-scoped** — they cannot be attached across AZs without first creating a snapshot and restoring it in the target AZ.
- **Six volume types:** gp3, gp2, io1, io2 Block Express, st1, sc1.
- **gp3 is the current default** for general-purpose workloads; **gp2 is legacy**.
- **SSD-backed** = transactional, sub-millisecond latency, boot volumes supported.
- **HDD-backed** = streaming, millisecond latency, **cannot be boot volumes**.
- **Maximum IOPS** = 256,000 (io2 Block Express); **Maximum Throughput** = 4,000 MB/s; **Maximum Volume Size** = 64 TiB.
- **Snapshots are incremental** — first is full, subsequent save only changed blocks; stored in S3 internally.
- **Snapshots are crash-consistent**, not application-consistent by default.
- **EBS encryption** uses AES-256 with AWS KMS; covers data at rest, in transit, and in snapshots.
- **Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR)** eliminates the lazy initialization penalty.
- **EBS-Optimized instances** provide dedicated bandwidth between EC2 and EBS, separate from general network traffic.
- **Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM)** automates snapshot policies.
- **gp2 baseline IOPS** = $3 \times \text{size in GiB}$, capped at 3,000; bursting uses I/O credits (max tank = 5.4 million).
- **gp3 decouples performance from size** — baseline 3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s at any size, provisioned IOPS/throughput available for additional fee.
- **Instance Store vs EBS:** Instance Store is ephemeral and host-local; EBS is persistent and network-attached.
- **99.999% durability** is the standard SLA advertised for EBS volumes within an AZ.
- **RAID configurations** on EBS require careful consideration of the **I/O lottery** (random distribution of I/O across underlying disks) for predictable performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Database Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Database Services in Cloud Computing** refer to the on-demand provisioning, scaling, and management of database systems over the internet, delivered as a managed, utility-based service. The most prevalent paradigm is **Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS)**, where the cloud provider handles infrastructure provisioning, software patching, backup, replication, performance tuning, and high-availability operations, while the consumer focuses purely on schema design, queries, and business logic.

In the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus, *Database Services* sit within the **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** layer, where developers can deploy databases without managing the underlying OS, storage fabric, or replication topology.

### Classification of Cloud Database Services

| **Service Model** | **Provider Responsibility** | **User Responsibility** |
|---|---|---|
| **DBaaS (Managed)** | Hardware, OS, Installation, Patching, Backups, Failover | Schema, Queries, Indexing, Optimization |
| **RDS-style Relational** | MySQL/PostgreSQL/Oracle engines managed | Schema, Stored Procedures, Tuning |
| **NoSQL Managed (e.g., DynamoDB)** | Partitioning, Replication, Scaling | Data modeling, Partition keys |
| **Self-managed on IaaS** | Hardware, Networking, Storage | Entire DB stack including OS & engine |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 💡

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Electricity Grid vs. Personal Generator**
> 
> Imagine a city where every house used to own its **own diesel generator** for electricity (this is the *traditional on-premise database* model). You had to fuel it, maintain it, fix breakdowns at 2 AM, and replace parts. **Database Services in the cloud** are like connecting to the **centralized electricity grid** — you just plug in, pay for what you consume, and the power company handles generation, transmission, backups, and load balancing. You focus only on what you *do* with the electricity (run your appliances = run your applications), not on *how* it is produced.
> 
> **Another simpler analogy**: Think of cloud database services like **renting a fully-furnished serviced apartment** vs. **buying a house and buying all the furniture**. The cloud gives you a "move-in ready" database with all maintenance, security, and utilities bundled into a monthly bill.

## 1.3 Why Database Services Matter in Cloud

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Value Proposition of Cloud Database Services**
> 
> 1. **Elasticity** — Auto-scale storage and compute to handle workload spikes (e.g., Black Friday traffic).
> 2. **High Availability** — Built-in replication across multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)** with SLA guarantees of **99.95%** or higher.
> 3. **Cost Efficiency** — Pay-per-use model eliminates CapEx and converts to OpEx.
> 4. **Reduced Operational Burden** — The provider's SRE teams manage patching, monitoring, and DR drills.
> 5. **Global Distribution** — Data can be replicated across **geographic regions** with single-digit-millisecond read latency.

## 1.4 Popular Real-World Database Services

- **Amazon RDS** (Relational Database Service) — MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server
- **Amazon DynamoDB** — Serverless NoSQL key-value & document store
- **Amazon Aurora** — Cloud-native distributed relational engine
- **Google Cloud SQL / Cloud Spanner / Bigtable / Firestore**
- **Azure SQL Database / Cosmos DB**
- **MongoDB Atlas** — Managed document database

---

**Key Terminology Introduced**:
- **DBaaS** — Database-as-a-Service
- **OLTP** — Online Transaction Processing
- **OLAP** — Online Analytical Processing
- **SLA** — Service Level Agreement
- **AZ** — Availability Zone
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Cloud Database Services

Cloud database services can be classified along three orthogonal axes: **data model**, **deployment model**, and **consistency model**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Data Model

| **Type** | **Examples** | **Best For** |
|---|---|---|
| **Relational (SQL)** | Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure SQL | Structured data, ACID transactions, complex joins |
| **Key-Value** | Amazon DynamoDB, Redis, Aerospike | Session storage, caching, leaderboards |
| **Document** | MongoDB Atlas, Couchbase, Azure Cosmos DB | Semi-structured JSON-like data, content management |
| **Wide-Column (Columnar)** | Google Bigtable, Apache Cassandra, HBase | Time-series, IoT, write-heavy workloads |
| **Graph** | Neo4j Aura, Amazon Neptune | Social networks, fraud detection, recommendation engines |
| **In-Memory** | Redis, Memcached, SAP HANA Cloud | Sub-millisecond latency, real-time analytics |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Deployment Topology

- **Single-Region, Multi-AZ** — Replicated within one geographic region.
- **Multi-Region, Active-Passive** — Primary in one region, standby in another.
- **Multi-Region, Active-Active** — Reads/writes served from multiple regions (e.g., DynamoDB Global Tables, Cosmos DB).
- **Hybrid** — On-premise + cloud (e.g., AWS Outposts, Azure Arc).

## 2.2 CAP Theorem — The Fundamental Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000)**
> 
> In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two out of three** of the following properties:
> 
> 1. **C — Consistency** — Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
> 2. **A — Availability** — Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes are down.
> 3. **P — Partition Tolerance** — The system continues operating despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.
> 
> Since network partitions (P) are **inevitable in real distributed systems**, the real choice is between **CP** and **AP**.

### CAP Variants for Cloud Databases

| **Combination** | **Real-World Example** | **Trade-off** |
|---|---|---|
| **CA** | Traditional RDBMS (single-node Oracle) | Cannot survive partitions |
| **CP** | HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis Cluster, Google Spanner (with TrueTime) | Sacrifices availability during partition |
| **AP** | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak | Sacrifices strong consistency for availability |

## 2.3 ACID vs. BASE — The Consistency Spectrum

> [!NOTE]
> **ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** — The traditional RDBMS guarantee.
> **BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent)** — The distributed NoSQL paradigm.

| **Property** | **ACID** | **BASE** |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | Strong (immediate) | Eventual (delayed) |
| **Transaction** | Strict, isolated | Loosely defined, optimistic |
| **Availability** | Sacrificed for safety | Prioritized |
| **Performance** | Lower under scale | Higher throughput |
| **Use Case** | Banking, ERP, inventory | Social media, IoT, analytics |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for KTU examination numerical problems on cloud database scaling.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Replication Factor** | $RF = \frac{Total\ Copies\ of\ Data}{Original\ Copies}$ | $RF$ — replication factor | Dimensionless integer (e.g., $RF=3$) |
| **Storage Overhead** | $S_{total} = S_{logical} \times RF$ | $S_{logical}$ — logical dataset size, $S_{total}$ — physical storage | Bytes, GB, TB |
| **Sharding Distribution** | $N_{shards} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{C_{shard}} \right\rceil$ | $T_{total}$ — total records, $C_{shard}$ — capacity per shard | Integer |
| **Read Latency (Multi-Region)** | $L_{read} = L_{propagation} + L_{query}$ | $L_{propagation}$ — cross-region delay, $L_{query}$ — local query time | Milliseconds |
| **Eventual Consistency Window** | $T_{converge} \approx RTT \times Replica\ Hops$ | $RTT$ — round-trip time | Seconds |
| **Throughput Estimate** | $Throughput = \frac{N_{shards} \times IOPS_{per\ shard}}{Contention\ Factor}$ | $IOPS$ — I/O operations per second | Operations per second |
| **Availability (Series)** | $A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $A_i$ — availability of component $i$ | Decimal (e.g., $0.999$) |
| **Availability (Parallel Redundant)** | $A_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | $A_i$ — availability of redundant component $i$ | Decimal |
| **Horizontal Scaling (Amdahl's)** | $Speedup = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}}$ | $p$ — parallel fraction, $n$ — number of nodes | Dimensionless ratio |

> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note**: All absolute values and divisions in the table above use `\frac`, `\times`, and `\lceil` / `\rceil` instead of pipe characters `\|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Sharding Strategies

Sharding (horizontal partitioning) splits a dataset across multiple nodes.

1. **Range-based Sharding** — Rows with contiguous key ranges go to the same shard. Risk: hotspots.
2. **Hash-based Sharding** — Hash function $h(key) \mod N$ determines shard. Better load distribution.
3. **Directory-based Sharding** — Lookup service maps keys to shards. Most flexible, but extra lookup hop.
4. **Geo-sharding** — Data partitioned by geographic region (good for data residency laws).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce (Amazon)**: DynamoDB handles cart, session, and product catalog with single-digit-ms latency.
- **Banking (HSBC, JPMorgan)**: Cloud Spanner or Aurora provides globally distributed ACID transactions.
- **IoT (Tesla, Siemens)**: Wide-column stores like Bigtable ingest millions of telemetry events per second.
- **Social Media (Twitter/X)**: Manhattan + Manhattan-like key-value stores serve timelines at massive scale.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Overhead with Replication

**Problem Statement**: A social media platform stores **2 TB** of user-generated photos. The cloud DBA configures a replication factor of **$RF = 3$** across three Availability Zones for high availability. Calculate the **total physical storage** consumed, and the **annual storage cost** if the provider charges **\$0.10 per GB-month**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert logical size to GB**
$$S_{logical} = 2\ TB \times 1024\ \frac{GB}{TB} = 2048\ GB$$

**Step 2: Apply the replication formula**
$$S_{total} = S_{logical} \times RF$$

$$S_{total} = 2048\ GB \times 3 = 6144\ GB$$

**Step 3: Convert to TB for readability**
$$S_{total} = \frac{6144}{1024} = 6\ TB$$

**Step 4: Calculate monthly cost**
$$Cost_{monthly} = S_{total} \times Rate = 6144\ GB \times \$0.10\ /\ GB$$

$$Cost_{monthly} = \$614.40\ /\ month$$

**Step 5: Calculate annual cost**
$$Cost_{annual} = Cost_{monthly} \times 12 = \$614.40 \times 12$$

$$Cost_{annual} = \$7372.80\ /\ year$$

> **Engineering Insight**: The **300% storage overhead** is the price of high availability. Compare this with the cost of a single-AZ outage potentially losing 100% of user data — a tradeoff that is almost always favorable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Shards Required

**Problem Statement**: A startup's user table has **120 million rows**. They choose a sharded NoSQL DB where each shard can handle at most **15 million rows** for optimal performance. How many shards are required?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the sharding formula**
$$N_{shards} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{C_{shard}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**
$$N_{shards} = \left\lceil \frac{120{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000{,}000} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3: Compute the division**
$$N_{shards} = \lceil 8.0 \rceil = 8$$

**Step 4: Add headroom for future growth (engineering best practice)**

If we want a **20% safety buffer**:
$$N_{shards,buffered} = \lceil 8 \times 1.20 \rceil = \lceil 9.6 \rceil = 10\ shards$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 10 instead of 8?** Allocating 2 extra shards provides headroom for hot-shard rebalancing, future traffic spikes, and re-sharding operations without downtime.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: System Availability with Redundancy

**Problem Statement**: A cloud database has **3 redundant replicas**, each with an individual availability of **$A_i = 0.99$ (99%)**. The replicas are configured in **parallel redundancy** (any one serving requests suffices). Compute the system availability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the parallel redundancy formula**
$$A_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$$

**Step 2: Substitute $n = 3$ and $A_i = 0.99$**
$$A_{system} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^3$$

**Step 3: Compute the unavailability term**
$$(1 - 0.99)^3 = (0.01)^3 = 0.000001$$

**Step 4: Subtract from 1**
$$A_{system} = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999$$

**Step 5: Convert to "five-nines"**
$$A_{system} = 99.9999\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This corresponds to roughly **31.5 seconds of downtime per year**, which is the gold standard for mission-critical cloud database SLAs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Database Service Selection Guide

| **Workload Characteristic** | **Recommended Service** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Complex joins, financial transactions | Amazon Aurora / Cloud SQL | Strong ACID, SQL compatibility |
| Millions of reads/writes per second, simple lookups | DynamoDB / Cosmos DB | Auto-scaling, single-digit-ms latency |
| IoT telemetry, time-series sensor data | Bigtable / Cassandra / InfluxDB Cloud | Wide-column optimized for write throughput |
| Social graph, recommendation engine | Neptune / Neo4j Aura | Native graph traversal, joins on relationships |
| Full-text search, log analytics | Elasticsearch Service / OpenSearch | Inverted index, fast fuzzy matching |
| Globally distributed transactions | Google Spanner / CockroachDB | Multi-region strong consistency via TrueTime / Hybrid Logical Clocks |
| Caching, session store | Redis / Memcached | In-memory, sub-millisecond latency |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Database Scaling

**Problem Statement**: A query workload in a database cluster has **$p = 0.85$ (85%)** of its execution time that is **parallelizable** across shards, and **$1 - p = 0.15$** that is **serial** (e.g., aggregation, coordination). If we scale from 1 node to **$n = 16$ nodes**, what is the theoretical speedup?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply Amdahl's Law**
$$Speedup = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**
$$Speedup = \frac{1}{0.15 + \frac{0.85}{16}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the parallel fraction term**
$$\frac{0.85}{16} = 0.053125$$

**Step 4: Add serial + parallel denominator**
$$0.15 + 0.053125 = 0.203125$$

**Step 5: Compute the speedup**
$$Speedup = \frac{1}{0.203125} \approx 4.92\times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Amdahl's Law**: The serial **15% bottleneck** caps the maximum achievable speedup at $1 / 0.15 \approx 6.67\times$ even with **infinite** nodes. This is why removing coordination overhead (e.g., using consensus-free designs) is critical in cloud databases.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical: Hash-Based Sharding Distribution

**Problem Statement**: A key-value store uses **hash-based sharding** with 4 shards. The hash function is $h(key)\ \bmod\ 4$. For keys "user1" through "user7" with $h$ values $\{5, 2, 7, 0, 4, 6, 3\}$, determine the distribution.

### Step-by-Step Solution

| **Key** | **$h(key)$** | **$h(key) \mod 4$** | **Shard ID** |
|---|---|---|---|
| user1 | 5 | 1 | Shard 1 |
| user2 | 2 | 2 | Shard 2 |
| user3 | 7 | 3 | Shard 3 |
| user4 | 0 | 0 | Shard 0 |
| user5 | 4 | 0 | Shard 0 |
| user6 | 6 | 2 | Shard 2 |
| user7 | 3 | 3 | Shard 3 |

**Final Distribution**:
- **Shard 0**: 2 keys (user4, user5)
- **Shard 1**: 1 key (user1)
- **Shard 2**: 2 keys (user2, user6)
- **Shard 3**: 2 keys (user3, user7)

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation**: Modulo-based hashing creates an uneven distribution when adding/removing shards (e.g., going from 4 to 5 shards remaps ~80% of keys). This is why production systems use **consistent hashing** instead.

## 4.1 High-Level Cloud Database Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT LAYER"]
        app1[Web Application]
        app2[Mobile App]
        app3[Analytics Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph APILayer["API GATEWAY LAYER"]
        apigw[API Gateway with TLS and Auth]
    end

    subgraph DBaaS["DBaaS CONTROL PLANE"]
        ctrl1[Provisioning Engine]
        ctrl2[Auto Scaling Manager]
        ctrl3[Backup and Snapshot Service]
        ctrl4[Patching and Upgrade Daemon]
        ctrl5[Monitoring and Metrics]
    end

    subgraph DataPlane["DATA PLANE"]
        primary[Primary DB Node]
        replica1[Standby Replica AZ1]
        replica2[Standby Replica AZ2]
        shardA[Shard A]
        shardB[Shard B]
        shardC[Shard C]
    end

    subgraph Storage["UNDERLYING STORAGE"]
        ssd1[EBS Volume 1]
        ssd2[EBS Volume 2]
        ssd3[EBS Volume 3]
    end

    app1 --> apigw
    app2 --> apigw
    app3 --> apigw
    apigw --> primary
    primary -.replicate.-> replica1
    primary -.replicate.-> replica2
    primary --> shardA
    primary --> shardB
    primary --> shardC
    shardA --> ssd1
    shardB --> ssd2
    shardC --> ssd3
    ctrl1 -.manages.-> primary
    ctrl2 -.scales.-> DataPlane
    ctrl3 -.backs up.-> Storage
    ctrl4 -.patches.-> DataPlane
    ctrl5 -.monitors.-> DataPlane
```

> **Architecture Notes for KTU Exam**: Notice the **separation of control plane (management) and data plane (actual data serving)** — a defining characteristic of mature cloud DBaaS offerings like Amazon RDS, Azure SQL, and Cloud SQL.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Consistency]
    A[Availability]

    C --- CA

    C --- CP
```

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS). List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer**:
> **DBaaS** is a cloud computing service model in which a third-party provider hosts and manages a database on behalf of the customer, providing access to the database over the internet (or a private network) with a pay-per-use billing model.
> 
> **Advantages**:
> 1. **Elastic Scalability** — Compute and storage can be scaled up/down automatically based on workload.
> 2. **Reduced Operational Overhead** — Provider handles patching, backups, replication, and DR.
> 3. **Cost Efficiency** — Converts CapEx to OpEx; no upfront hardware investment.
> 4. **High Availability** — Built-in multi-AZ replication with strong SLAs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between ACID and BASE properties in cloud databases.**

**Model Answer**:

| **Parameter** | **ACID** | **BASE** |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong, immediate | Eventual (converges over time) |
| **Transactions** | Strict, isolated, can be rolled back | Loosely defined, optimistic |
| **Availability vs Consistency** | Favors consistency | Favors availability |
| **Typical Systems** | RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL) | NoSQL (Cassandra, DynamoDB) |
| **Use Cases** | Banking, ERP, financial systems | Social media, IoT, content feeds |

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions on Module 3 typically include an internal choice between two questions, each subdivided into two 7-mark parts.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CAP Theorem** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss how different categories of cloud databases (relational, NoSQL key-value, and NewSQL) position themselves within the CAP trade-off.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a typical cloud-managed database service (DBaaS)**. Explain the roles of the control plane and data plane.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition**:
The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer in 2000** and proven by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, states that in any distributed data store, you can simultaneously provide **at most two** of the following three guarantees:
- **C — Consistency**: Every read sees the most recent committed write.
- **A — Availability**: Every request receives a non-error response (without guaranteeing it is the latest data).
- **P — Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network failures (partitions) between nodes.

**Why partitions are non-negotiable**:
In any real-world distributed system spanning multiple data centers, switches, and routers, network partitions are **inevitable**. Therefore, the practical choice is between **CP** and **AP**.

**Positioning of cloud databases in CAP**:

1. **CA Systems** — Single-node RDBMS (traditional MySQL, PostgreSQL) that do not distribute data; cannot survive partitions. Not truly "distributed".

2. **CP Systems** — Sacrifices availability during partitions to maintain strong consistency.
   - **HBase**: Uses Zookeeper for coordination; rejects writes if quorum lost.
   - **MongoDB (default config)**: Primary node required for writes.
   - **Google Spanner**: Uses atomic clocks (TrueTime API) for global consistency.

3. **AP Systems** — Sacrifices strong consistency for availability.
   - **Cassandra**: Tunable consistency; writes always accepted, reads may return stale data.
   - **Amazon DynamoDB**: Uses vector clocks and "last-write-wins" semantics.
   - **CouchDB**: Eventual consistency with offline-first synchronization.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

A cloud-managed database service has two distinct planes:

**1. Control Plane** (Management Layer):
- **Provisioning Engine** — Allocates compute, storage, and network resources.
- **Auto-Scaling Manager** — Adjusts instance class and replica count based on load.
- **Backup & Snapshot Service** — Creates automated backups and point-in-time recovery.
- **Patching Daemon** — Applies security patches and minor version upgrades with zero downtime.
- **Monitoring & Alerting** — Streams metrics (CPU, IOPS, latency) to CloudWatch/Azure Monitor.

**2. Data Plane** (Data Serving Layer):
- **Primary Node** — Handles all writes; replicates to standbys synchronously.
- **Standby Replicas** — Located in different AZs; ready for failover in <60 seconds.
- **Read Replicas** (optional) — Handle read-heavy traffic and analytics queries.
- **Underlying Storage** — Typically network-attached block storage (EBS, Persistent Disk, Managed Disks).

**Workflow**:
1. Application sends query via API Gateway (TLS-encrypted).
2. Connection pooler routes to primary or appropriate replica.
3. Primary commits transaction to WAL (Write-Ahead Log).
4. Synchronous replication to standby in another AZ.
5. Async replication to read replicas or cross-region standby.
6. Backup service snapshots storage periodically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Many students **confuse the control plane with the data plane** or omit the role of the connection pooler. Always label both planes explicitly in your block diagram and state at least 2 responsibilities for each.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **horizontal partitioning (sharding)** and **vertical partitioning** in cloud databases. Illustrate with examples and discuss the challenges of re-sharding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **Amazon DynamoDB** as a cloud-native NoSQL database service. Explain its data model, partition key design, and consistency model.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Vertical Partitioning**:
- Splits a table **column-wise** into multiple tables, each containing a subset of columns.
- Example: Splitting a `Users` table into `UserCredentials (id, password_hash)` and `UserProfiles (id, name, email, address)`.
- Use case: Optimizing I/O by reading only the columns you need (columnar databases).
- Limitation: Limited scalability — cannot exceed single-server capacity.

**Horizontal Partitioning (Sharding)**:
- Splits a table **row-wise** into multiple chunks (shards), each holding a subset of rows.
- Example: A `Users` table with 1 billion rows split into 10 shards of 100M rows each, using $user\_id \mod 10$.
- Use case: Massive scale-out, parallel query execution.
- Strategies: Range-based, hash-based, directory-based, geo-sharding.

**Comparison Table**:

| **Aspect** | **Vertical Partitioning** | **Horizontal Sharding** |
|---|---|---|
| **Split Axis** | Columns | Rows |
| **Goal** | I/O efficiency, security | Scalability, parallelism |
| **Limit** | Single node | Distributed across nodes |
| **Joins** | Often required (re-assemble row) | Rare within single shard |
| **Re-sharding** | Rare | Frequent at scale |

**Re-sharding Challenges**:
1. **Data Migration** — Moving TBs of data with minimal downtime.
2. **Hash Function Change** — Modulo-based hashing remaps ~80% of keys when shard count changes. Solution: **Consistent Hashing**.
3. **Hot Shards** — One shard receives disproportionate load. Solution: Virtual nodes, split hot keys.
4. **Cross-Shard Queries** — JOINs across shards are expensive. Solution: Denormalization, materialized views.
5. **Referential Integrity** — Foreign keys across shards are hard to enforce.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Amazon DynamoDB Overview**:
- Fully managed, serverless **NoSQL key-value and document** database.
- Single-digit-millisecond latency at any scale.
- Auto-scales throughput and storage transparently.

**Data Model**:
- **Tables** → collections of items.
- **Items** → analogous to rows (max 400 KB).
- **Attributes** → key-value pairs (flexible schema).
- **Primary Key**:
  - **Simple**: `PartitionKey` only.
  - **Composite**: `PartitionKey` + `SortKey`.

**Partition Key Design (Critical)**:
- DynamoDB internally hashes the partition key and uses **consistent hashing** to map to physical partitions.
- Goal: **Uniform distribution** of reads/writes AND **avoid hot partitions**.
- Best practices:
  - High-cardinality keys (e.g., `user_id` not `country`).
  - Distribute load across partitions using **write sharding** (e.g., appending random suffix).
  - For time-series: Use composite sort key with timestamp.

**Consistency Model**:
- **Eventually Consistent Reads** (default) — reflects all writes within ~1 second.
- **Strongly Consistent Reads** (opt-in) — reflects all successful prior writes; consumes double the read capacity.
- **Transactions** — ACID transactions supported via `TransactGetItem` and `TransactWriteItem`.
- **Global Tables** — Multi-region, multi-active replication with last-writer-wins conflict resolution.

**Pricing**:
- **On-Demand**: Pay per request.
- **Provisioned**: Pay for `Read Capacity Units (RCU)` and `Write Capacity Units (WCU)`.
- $1 WCU = 1\ write/sec\ of\ items\ up\ to\ 1\ KB$.
- $1 RCU = 1\ strongly\ consistent\ read/sec\ of\ items\ up\ to\ 4\ KB$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Pitfall on DynamoDB**:
> 1. Students often forget that **sort keys must be combined with partition key** in the primary key (composite key).
> 2. Confusion between **eventual** and **strong** consistency — only **strongly consistent reads** return the latest data; the default is **eventual**.
> 3. Neglecting **partition size limits (10 GB per partition)** — exceeding this requires redesigning partition keys.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **DBaaS** — managed database service with provider handling infrastructure.
- **CAP Theorem** — choose **2 of 3**: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.
- **ACID** — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability (RDBMS).
- **BASE** — Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent (NoSQL).
- **Polyglot Persistence** — using multiple DB types in one application.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
$$S_{total} = S_{logical} \times RF$$
$$N_{shards} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{C_{shard}} \right\rceil$$
$$A_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$$
$$Speedup = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}}$$

### ✅ Database Service Examples
- **Relational**: Amazon RDS, Aurora, Google Cloud SQL, Azure SQL
- **NoSQL Key-Value**: DynamoDB, Redis
- **Document**: MongoDB Atlas, Cosmos DB
- **Wide-Column**: Bigtable, Cassandra
- **Graph**: Neptune, Neo4j Aura
- **In-Memory**: Redis, Memcached

### ✅ CAP Positioning (Most Asked)
- **CP**: MongoDB, HBase, Spanner
- **AP**: DynamoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB
- **CA**: Single-node RDBMS

### ✅ Sharding Strategies
- Range-based (risk: hotspots)
- Hash-based (better balance)
- Directory-based (flexible)
- Geo-sharding (compliance)

### ✅ Key Numbers for KTU
- DynamoDB item size limit: **400 KB**
- Aurora replica lag: **<100 ms**
- Multi-AZ failover time: **<60 seconds**
- DynamoDB partition size: **<10 GB**
- Strongly consistent read capacity: **2x** eventual read

### ✅ Common KTU Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing **sharding** (rows) with **partitioning** (columns).
- ❌ Forgetting that **CAP's P is mandatory** → real choice is CP vs AP.
- ❌ Mixing up **eventual** and **strong** consistency in DynamoDB.
- ❌ Saying DynamoDB is "relational" — it is **NoSQL**.
- ❌ Missing the **control plane vs. data plane** distinction in DBaaS architecture.

> **Best of luck for your KTU exams!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Major Services. Google Cloud: IaaS Offerings - Compute Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Major Services. Google Cloud: IaaS Offerings - Compute Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Google Compute Engine (GCE)** is the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** component of Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that delivers **scalable, high-performance virtual machine (VM) instances** running in Google's global data centers. It provides configurable compute, storage, and networking resources on a pay-as-you-go basis, supporting both Linux and Windows workloads.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Compute Engine is classified under the **IaaS delivery model** of the NIST cloud computing reference architecture. It exposes raw, programmable infrastructure primitives — **vCPUs, memory, block storage, virtual networks, and load balancers** — to the consumer, who retains full control over the operating system, middleware, runtime, and application layers.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics of Compute Engine (as a Service)

- **Elastic compute capacity** with live migration and automatic restart.
- **Per-second billing** after a one-minute minimum charge.
- **Global footprint** across 40+ regions and 120+ zones.
- **Custom machine types** allowing fractional vCPU and memory configurations.
- **Live migration** of running VMs during host maintenance events with **zero downtime**.
- **Predefined machine families** optimized for specific workload profiles.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Renting a Computer Across the Street"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Compute Engine like renting a fully-furnished office space in a building owned by Google.** You choose the size of the room (machine type), the furniture (OS + software), the electricity budget (CPU/RAM), and the lock on the door (firewall rules). Google takes care of the building's plumbing, cooling, security guards, and 24/7 power backup. You only pay for the *time and space* you occupy — down to the second.

This analogy captures the **three pillars of IaaS**:
1. **You manage**: OS, runtime, application, data, and middleware.
2. **Google manages**: Host hardware, hypervisor, physical network, and data center infrastructure.
3. **Shared responsibility**: Network configuration, IAM policies, and instance metadata.

### 1.4 Position of GCE in the GCP Service Hierarchy

| Layer | GCP Service | Cloud Model |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| Application Services | App Engine, Cloud Run | PaaS / Serverless |
| Container Orchestration | GKE, Cloud Run | CaaS |
| **Virtual Infrastructure** | **Compute Engine** | **IaaS** |
| Serverless Functions | Cloud Functions, Cloud Run Jobs | FaaS |
| Storage Primitives | Persistent Disk, Cloud Storage | IaaS Storage |

### 1.5 Core Service Components (High-Level Inventory)

> [!NOTE]
> A GCE deployment is composed of **seven core abstractions**:
> 1. **Instance** — the virtual machine (vCPU + RAM + boot disk).
> 2. **Image** — the bootable OS template (public, custom, or marketplace).
> 3. **Machine Type** — the hardware profile (vCPUs, RAM, optional GPUs).
> 4. **Persistent Disk / Local SSD** — block storage volumes.
> 5. **Network Interface** — VPC attachment, subnets, and external IP.
> 6. **Firewall Rules** — stateful packet filtering at the VPC layer.
> 7. **Metadata & Startup Scripts** — instance configuration automation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Compute Engine Resource Hierarchy

GCE follows a strict parent-child resource topology, which is **frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 6-mark questions**:

```
Organization (Resource Root)
    └── Folder (optional, billing scope)
            └── Project (billing & IAM boundary)
                    ├── VPC Network
                    │       ├── Subnet
                    │       │       ├── Instance (VM)
                    │       │       │       ├── Attached Disks
                    │       │       │       └── Network Interfaces
                    │       └── Firewall Rules
                    ├── Instance Templates
                    ├── Managed Instance Groups (MIGs)
                    └── Images / Snapshots
```

**Why the hierarchy matters (KTU focus):**
- **Project** is the *hard isolation boundary* — billing, quota, and IAM are scoped here.
- **VPC Networks** are *global objects*, but subnets are *regional*.
- **Instance Templates** are immutable blueprints used by Managed Instance Groups (MIGs).
- **Snapshots** are *global*; **Persistent Disks** are *zonal* by default and *regional* when configured.

### 2.2 Machine Type Families — The "Catalogue of Servers"

Google groups predefined machine types into **four specialized families**:

| Family | Prefix | Optimized For | Example |
|--------|--------|---------------|---------|
| General Purpose | `e2`, `n2`, `n2d`, `t2d` | Balanced workloads, web servers, small DBs | n2-standard-4 |
| Compute Optimized | `c2`, `c2d` | High-CPU tasks, gaming, batch analytics | c2-standard-30 |
| Memory Optimized | `m2`, `m3` | In-memory DBs, SAP HANA, large caches | m2-ultramem-208 |
| Accelerator Optimized | `a2` | ML training, HPC, CUDA workloads | a2-highgpu-1g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Custom Machine Types**: For non-standard workloads, GCE permits specifying vCPUs (even-numbered) and memory (in 256 MB increments) independently. This is **highly testable** — expect a question like *"A startup needs 6 vCPUs and 22 GB RAM. Can they achieve this on GCE? Justify."*

### 2.3 Storage Options — Three Block-Storage Tiers

| Storage Type | Scope | Performance | Persistence |
|--------------|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Zonal Persistent Disk (HDD)** | Zonal | Sequential I/O, cost-optimized | Independent of VM lifecycle |
| **Zonal Persistent Disk (SSD / Balanced)** | Zonal | Low-latency random I/O | Independent of VM lifecycle |
| **Regional Persistent Disk** | Multi-zone (synchronous replication) | HA, lower latency variance | Survives zone failures |
| **Local SSD** | Physically attached to host | Highest IOPS, ephemeral | **Lost on VM stop/termination** |
| **Cloud Storage (buckets)** | Global, object-based | Cold/archival tier available | Independent, 11 nines durability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Local SSD is ephemeral** — it is physically attached to the host hardware, not the VM. KTU exam trap: *"A student assumes their data is safe on Local SSD after a controlled shutdown."* The correct answer: data is lost; use Persistent Disk for durability.

### 2.4 Pricing Models — The "Five Tiers of Cost Optimization"

> [!NOTE]
> GCE offers **five distinct pricing models** that must be understood for both Part A and Part B questions.

1. **On-Demand Pricing** — Standard rate, billed per second, no commitment.
2. **Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs)** — Automatic discounts applied when a VM runs for a significant portion of a month (up to ~20% for N1, automatic for N2/C2).
3. **Preemptible VMs** — Short-lived (max 24 hours), up to 80% cheaper, can be preempted with 30 seconds notice.
4. **Spot VMs** — Replacement for preemptible; no 24-hour cap, can be preempted based on capacity/price.
5. **Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)** — 1-year or 3-year commitments yielding **up to 57% discount** for N2/C2 and 70% for memory-optimized.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All numerical pricing questions reduce to these formulas.** The rates below are **illustrative averages for educational purposes** — actual GCP rates vary by region.

| Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|------------------|------|-------|
| **Total vCPU Cost** | $C_{cpu} = R_{cpu} \times N_{cpu} \times T_{hrs}$ | USD | $R_{cpu}$ = on-demand rate per vCPU-hr |
| **Total Memory Cost** | $C_{mem} = R_{mem} \times M_{GB} \times T_{hrs}$ | USD | $R_{mem}$ = on-demand rate per GB-hr |
| **On-Demand Instance Cost** | $C_{od} = (R_{cpu} \times N_{cpu} + R_{mem} \times M_{GB}) \times T_{hrs}$ | USD | Billed per second after 1 min |
| **Sustained Use Discount (N1)** | $C_{sud} = C_{od} \times (1 - d)$ | USD | $d$ = discount factor (0 → 0.20) |
| **Committed Use Discount** | $C_{cud} = C_{od} \times (1 - D)$ | USD | $D$ = 0.25 (1-yr) to 0.57 (3-yr) |
| **Preemptible / Spot Cost** | $C_{spot} = C_{od} \times (1 - 0.80)$ | USD | Up to 80% off |
| **Total Monthly Compute Cost** | $C_{month} = C_{base} \times 720$ | USD | Assumes 30 days × 24 hrs = 720 hrs |
| **Snapshots Storage Cost** | $C_{snap} = V_{GB} \times R_{snap}$ | USD/month | $R_{snap} \approx \$0.026$/GB-month |
| **PD Storage Cost (SSD)** | $C_{pd} = V_{GB} \times R_{pd}$ | USD/month | $R_{pd} \approx \$0.17$/GB-month |
| **Network Egress Cost** | $C_{egress} = V_{GB} \times R_{egress}$ | USD | Tiered by destination region |

### 2.6 Instance Lifecycle States — The State Machine

A GCE instance transitions through **six explicit states**:

```
PROVISIONING  →  STAGING  →  RUNNING  →  STOPPING  →  STOPPED  →  TERMINATED
     ↑                                              ↑
     └────────── (re-start) ────────────────────────┘
```

| State | Description | Billing Implication |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **PROVISIONING** | Resources being allocated | Not billed |
| **STAGING** | Image loading, network setup | Not billed |
| **RUNNING** | Active and accessible | **Fully billed** |
| **STOPPING** | Graceful shutdown in progress | Billed (briefly) |
| **STOPPED** | VM off, disks persist | **Disks only** |
| **TERMINATED** | VM deleted, can be recreated from snapshot | Nothing billed |

### 2.7 Networking Components (IaaS Layer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VPC matters in KTU exams**: Networking is the *connective tissue* of IaaS. Expect a question linking VPC, subnet, firewall, and external/internal IP.

- **VPC Network**: Global, isolated, software-defined.
- **Subnet**: Regional, defines IP range.
- **External IP**: Publicly routable, billed while attached.
- **Internal IP**: Subnet-scoped, free.
- **Firewall Rules**: Stateful, evaluated by tag or service account.
- **Cloud Load Balancing**: Global, anycast, integrates with MIGs.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Compute Engine powers **engineering workloads** such as:
- **CAD/CAE simulation** (ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL) on Compute-Optimized `c2` instances.
- **Geophysical seismic processing** on `c2d-highcpu` and `a2` GPU nodes.
- **EDA chip verification** using Spot VMs for cost-efficient regression runs.
- **ML model training** on A2 accelerators with NVLink.
- **CI/CD build farms** using Preemptible VMs — failures are tolerable, cost savings are massive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1 — On-Demand Cost Computation

> **Scenario (KTU 2-mark modification):** A startup provisions a **`n2-standard-4`** instance (4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM) in `us-central1` and runs it **continuously for 30 days (720 hours)**.
>
> Given illustrative rates:
> $$R_{cpu} = \$0.021174 \text{ per vCPU-hr}$$
> $$R_{mem} = \$0.002845 \text{ per GB-hr}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
N_{cpu} = 4, \quad M_{GB} = 16, \quad T_{hrs} = 720
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the vCPU sub-cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{cpu} &= R_{cpu} \times N_{cpu} \times T_{hrs} \\
&= \$0.021174 \times 4 \times 720 \\
&= \$0.021174 \times 2880 \\
&= \$60.98
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the memory sub-cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{mem} &= R_{mem} \times M_{GB} \times T_{hrs} \\
&= \$0.002845 \times 16 \times 720 \\
&= \$0.002845 \times 11520 \\
&= \$32.77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain the total on-demand monthly cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{od} &= C_{cpu} + C_{mem} \\
&= \$60.98 + \$32.77 \\
&= \$93.75
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer**: The total monthly on-demand compute cost is approximately **$93.75** (excluding disks and network).

---

### 3.2 Problem 2 — Sustained Use Discount (SUD) Application

> **Continuation**: The same instance runs the full 720 hours. N2 machine types **automatically receive a SUD of up to 20%** when running for the full month.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum SUD factor.**

For N2, the maximum automatic SUD for full-month usage is:

$$
d_{max} = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the discount formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sud} &= C_{od} \times (1 - d_{max}) \\
&= \$93.75 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= \$93.75 \times 0.80 \\
&= \$75.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer**: With automatic SUD, the cost reduces to **$75.00/month** — a saving of **$18.75**.

---

### 3.3 Problem 3 — 1-Year Committed Use Discount (CUD) Comparison

> **Scenario**: The startup signs a **1-year CUD**, receiving **37% off** the on-demand rate.

**Step 1 — Identify the CUD factor.**

$$
D_{1yr} = 0.37
$$

**Step 2 — Compute discounted cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{cud} &= C_{od} \times (1 - D_{1yr}) \\
&= \$93.75 \times (1 - 0.37) \\
&= \$93.75 \times 0.63 \\
&= \$59.06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare the three pricing models.**

| Model | Monthly Cost | Annual Cost | Savings vs. On-Demand |
|-------|--------------|-------------|-----------------------|
| On-Demand | $93.75 | $1,125.00 | — |
| Sustained Use (auto) | $75.00 | $900.00 | $225.00 (20%) |
| 1-Year CUD | $59.06 | $708.75 | $416.25 (37%) |

> **Engineering Insight**: For **predictable, baseline workloads**, CUDs are unbeatable. For **bursty or experimental workloads**, On-Demand + SUD protects flexibility.

---

### 3.4 Problem 4 — Spot/Preemptible Cost for Batch Workload

> **Scenario**: A research lab runs **fault-tolerant simulations** on the same instance for 720 hours/month using **Spot VMs**, which offer **80% discount**.

**Step 1 — Compute the spot cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{spot} &= C_{od} \times (1 - 0.80) \\
&= \$93.75 \times 0.20 \\
&= \$18.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with all models.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank (low → high)}: \quad C_{spot} < C_{cud} < C_{sud} < C_{od}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Trade-off**: Spot VMs can be **preempted at any time** (typically with a 30-second notice). They are **unsuitable for stateful services** but ideal for **batch processing, CI builds, and Monte Carlo simulations**.

---

### 3.5 Problem 5 — Persistent Disk Storage Cost

> **Scenario**: Attach a **500 GB Balanced Persistent Disk** (illustrative rate: **$0.10/GB-month**) for 30 days.

**Step 1 — Apply the storage formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pd} &= V_{GB} \times R_{pd} \\
&= 500 \times \$0.10 \\
&= \$50.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total cost of ownership (TCO) with VM + disk.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TCO} &= C_{od} + C_{pd} \\
&= \$93.75 + \$50.00 \\
&= \$143.75 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Real-world tip**: Snapshot storage is **cheaper than PD** (~$0.026/GB-month), so archiving old snapshots to **Coldline Storage** further reduces long-term TCO.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Machine Type to Choose?

| Workload Profile | Recommended Family | Reason (with Justification) |
|------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Web server, microservices | `e2-medium`, `n2-standard` | Balanced CPU/RAM ratio |
| In-memory database (Redis, Memcached) | `m2-ultramem` | High memory-to-vCPU ratio |
| Video transcoding | `c2-standard` + Local SSD | CPU-bound, needs high clock speed |
| ML training (TensorFlow) | `a2-highgpu-1g` | GPU acceleration with NVLink |
| CI/CD ephemeral build | Spot `n2-standard` | Cost-optimized, fault-tolerant |
| SAP HANA | `m3-ultramem` | Certified, certified RAM sizes |
| HPC fluid dynamics | `c2d-highcpu-112` | High core count, low latency |

### 3.7 Comparative Pricing Cheat Sheet (For Exam Memorization)

| Discount Type | Max Savings | Trigger Condition | Reversible? |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Sustained Use** | 20–30% (N1/N2) | Auto, runtime > threshold | Yes (instance-level) |
| **Committed Use (1-yr)** | 25–37% | Resource-based commit | Yes (after term) |
| **Committed Use (3-yr)** | 52–57% | Resource-based commit | No (lock-in) |
| **Spot / Preemptible** | 60–80% | No commitment, capacity-based | Yes (anytime) |
| **Sole-Tenant Node CUD** | Up to 70% | Per-host commitment | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory-optimized CUDs offer the deepest discounts (up to 70%)** — a frequently tested KTU fact.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GCE Resource Hierarchy & Request Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    org[Organization Node]
    folder[Folder - Optional Billing Scope]
    project[Project - IAM and Billing Boundary]
    vpc[VPC Network - Global Object]
    subnet[Subnet - Regional IP Range]
    fw[Firewall Rules - VPC Level]
    inst[Instance - Virtual Machine]
    disk[Persistent Disk - Zonal]
    lssd[Local SSD - Ephemeral]
    img[Image - Bootable OS Template]
    tmpl[Instance Template - Immutable]
    mig[Managed Instance Group MIG]
    lb[Cloud Load Balancer - Global Anycast]

    org --> folder
    folder --> project
    project --> vpc
    project --> img
    project --> tmpl
    vpc --> subnet
    vpc --> fw
    subnet --> inst
    tmpl --> mig
    mig --> inst
    inst --> disk
    inst --> lssd
    inst --> lb
    img -.boot.-> inst
```

### 4.2 GCE Instance Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PROVISIONING
    PROVISIONING --> STAGING : Resources allocated
    STAGING --> RUNNING : OS loaded network ready
    RUNNING --> STOPPING : User API call or auto event
    STOPPING --> STOPPED : Graceful shutdown complete
    STOPPED --> RUNNING : Start API call
    STOPPING --> RUNNING : Restart API call
    RUNNING --> [*] : Delete API TERMINATED
    STOPPED --> [*] : Delete API TERMINATED
    RUNNING --> PREEMPTED : Spot capacity reclaimed
    PREEMPTED --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GCE Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Layer]
        u[Cloud Console gcloud CLI API Client]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control Plane - Google Managed]
        apiserver[Compute Engine API Server]
        scheduler[Scheduler]
        hypermgr[Hypervisor and Host Manager]
    end

    subgraph DATA[Data Plane - Tenant Visible]
        vm1[Instance A vCPU Memory]
        vm2[Instance B vCPU Memory]
        vm3[Instance C Preemptible]
        pd1[Persistent Disk SSD]
        pd2[Persistent Disk HDD Regional]
    end

    subgraph PHYS[Physical Infrastructure - Google Managed]
        host1[Server Host 1]
        host2[Server Host 2]
        rack[Rack and Power and Cooling]
    end

    USER --> apiserver
    apiserver --> scheduler
    scheduler --> hypermgr
    hypermgr --> vm1
    hypermgr --> vm2
    hypermgr --> vm3
    vm1 --- pd1
    vm2 --- pd2
    vm1 -.lives on.-> host1
    vm2 -.lives on.-> host2
    host1 --- rack
    host2 --- rack
```

### 4.4 Pricing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Need Compute on GCE]
    q1{Is workload predictable for 1 to 3 years?}
    q2{Is the workload fault tolerant and batch like?}
    q3{Is the workload long running full month?}
    od[Use On-Demand Pricing]
    sud[Benefit from automatic SUD]
    cud[Purchase Committed Use Discount]
    spot[Use Spot or Preemptible VMs]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| cud
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| spot
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| sud
    q3 -->|No| od
```

### 4.5 Storage Tier Selection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    need[Need persistent block storage]
    q1{Need highest IOPS and ephemeral?}
    q2{Need multi zone HA?}
    q3{Is workload sequential and cost sensitive?}
    lssd[Local SSD - Ephemeral and ultra fast]
    rpds[Regional Persistent Disk - Synchronous replication]
    ssd[Zonal SSD Persistent Disk - Low latency]
    hdd[Zonal HDD Persistent Disk - Low cost]

    need --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| lssd
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| rpds
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| hdd
    q3 -->|No| ssd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Google Compute Engine and list any four of its salient features.**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Google Compute Engine (GCE) is the Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) offering of Google Cloud that provides scalable virtual machine instances running in Google's data centers. (1 mark)

**Salient features (any four — ½ mark each):**
1. Per-second billing with a one-minute minimum charge.
2. Live migration of running VMs during host maintenance with **zero downtime**.
3. Custom machine types with fractional vCPU and memory configuration.
4. Multiple pricing models — On-Demand, SUD, CUD, Preemptible, and Spot.
5. Global VPC networking with regional subnets.
6. Tight integration with Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) for autoscaling.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Preemptible VMs and Spot VMs in Google Compute Engine.**
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Preemptible VM | Spot VM |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Maximum runtime | 24 hours | **No fixed cap** |
| Preemption notice | 30 seconds | 30 seconds |
| Pricing | Fixed (up to 80% off) | **Market-driven, can be lower or higher** |
| Availability | Legacy model (deprecated for new workloads) | **Current recommendation** |
| Use case | Short batch jobs, fault-tolerant workloads | Distributed ML, large-scale HPC |

Spot VMs are the **modern replacement** for Preemptible VMs and are recommended for all new fault-tolerant workloads. (1 mark for the distinction + 1 mark for the use case + 1 mark for tabular clarity)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Note**: As per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, answer **ONE** full question from each module. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the different pricing models offered by Google Compute Engine. Compare them using a numerical example for a 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM instance running 720 hours per month.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Google Compute Engine offers **five pricing models** to balance cost and flexibility:

1. **On-Demand Pricing** — Pay per second after a 1-minute minimum. No commitment. Highest unit cost. Ideal for unpredictable or short-lived workloads.
2. **Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs)** — Automatic discounts when a VM runs for a significant portion of a month. For N2 instances, the maximum discount is **20%** for full-month usage.
3. **Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)** — Resource-based commitments for 1-year or 3-year terms. Discounts range from **25% to 57%** for compute-optimized and **up to 70%** for memory-optimized families.
4. **Preemptible VMs** — Fixed up to **80% discount** with a hard 24-hour lifetime cap. Suitable for fault-tolerant batch jobs.
5. **Spot VMs** — Replacement for Preemptible. **No runtime cap**, market-driven pricing, can be preempted based on capacity.

**Numerical Comparison** (illustrative rates, n2-standard-4, 720 hrs):

- On-Demand cost: $(0.021174 \times 4 + 0.002845 \times 16) \times 720 = \$93.75$ **(2 marks for derivation)**
- With 20% SUD: $93.75 \times 0.80 = \$75.00$ **(1 mark)**
- With 1-yr CUD (37% off): $93.75 \times 0.63 = \$59.06$ **(1 mark)**
- With Spot/Preemptible (80% off): $93.75 \times 0.20 = \$18.75$ **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion**: For predictable, long-running production workloads, CUDs yield the best TCO. For batch/fault-tolerant jobs, Spot VMs offer maximum savings at the cost of availability guarantees. (1 mark for synthesis)

---

**(b) Describe the instance lifecycle states of a GCE VM with a neat diagram. What happens to billing in each state?** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A GCE instance transitions through the following **six states** (3 marks for the description + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for billing):

| State | Description | Billing |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **PROVISIONING** | Google is allocating host resources | Free |
| **STAGING** | Loading image, configuring network | Free |
| **RUNNING** | VM is active and accessible | Full compute charges apply |
| **STOPPING** | Graceful shutdown in progress | Brief billing continues |
| **STOPPED** | VM is off, but disks persist | **Disks only** (no compute charges) |
| **TERMINATED** | VM is deleted; can be recreated from snapshots | Nothing billed |

**Diagram (expected):**
```
PROVISIONING → STAGING → RUNNING ⇄ STOPPING → STOPPED
                  ↑          ↓                       ↓
                  └── (Restart) ─── TERMINATED ←──────┘
```

**Key Point**: Stopping an instance does **not** delete attached Persistent Disks. Only **deleting** (terminate) the instance removes it, and **Local SSD data is lost on stop**. (2 marks for the key point and billing nuance)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the different machine type families in Google Compute Engine. Recommend an appropriate family for: (i) a Redis cache, (ii) a video transcoding pipeline, and (iii) a TensorFlow training job.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

GCE organizes predefined machine types into **four specialized families** (2 marks for the overview table):

| Family | Prefix | CPU:RAM Ratio | Best For |
|--------|--------|---------------|----------|
| General Purpose | e2, n2, n2d, t2d | 1:4 | Web servers, microservices |
| Compute Optimized | c2, c2d | 1:2 (high clock) | Batch, gaming, transcoding |
| Memory Optimized | m2, m3 | 1:8 or higher | In-memory DBs, SAP HANA |
| Accelerator Optimized | a2 | 1:4 + GPU | ML training, HPC |

**Recommendations** (1.5 marks each):

1. **Redis Cache (i)** → **`m2-ultramem` or `m3-ultramem`** family. Reason: Redis is an in-memory key-value store that benefits from a **high memory-to-vCPU ratio** (8:1 or 16:1). Memory-optimized families provide certified RAM sizes for Redis Enterprise. (1.5 marks)

2. **Video Transcoding Pipeline (ii)** → **`c2-standard` + Local SSD**. Reason: Transcoding is **CPU-bound** (single-thread performance matters for FFmpeg). Compute-optimized `c2` instances offer the highest per-core clock speed, and Local SSD provides the high-throughput scratch storage needed for intermediate frames. (1.5 marks)

3. **TensorFlow Training Job (iii)** → **`a2-highgpu-1g` (A100 GPUs)**. Reason: Deep learning training is **GPU-accelerated**; the A2 family offers NVIDIA A100 GPUs with NVLink for multi-GPU scaling and 96 vCPUs + 1.3 TB RAM to feed the GPU pipeline. (1.5 marks)

---

**(b) Explain the different block storage options in GCE. Why is Local SSD considered ephemeral, and when should it be used?** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

GCE provides **four primary block-storage options** (3 marks for the comparison):

| Type | Scope | Use Case | Durability |
|------|-------|----------|------------|
| **Zonal Persistent Disk (HDD)** | Single zone | Cold data, boot disks, log volumes | High (independent of VM) |
| **Zonal Persistent Disk (SSD / Balanced)** | Single zone | Databases, low-latency apps | High |
| **Regional Persistent Disk** | Two zones (synchronous) | HA databases, zone-failure tolerant apps | Higher (multi-zone) |
| **Local SSD** | Physically attached to host | Scratch, cache, swap, high-IOPS temp data | **Ephemeral — lost on stop/terminate** |

**Why Local SSD is ephemeral (2 marks):**
Local SSD is a **physically attached NVMe device** on the host server, not a network-attached block device. When a VM is **stopped, terminated, or live-migrated to another host**, the local NVMe drive does not follow it. Data is **permanently lost**.

**Appropriate use cases for Local SSD (2 marks):**
- **Scratch space** for batch processing (e.g., Hadoop shuffle, FFmpeg intermediates).
- **Swap / tempDB** for SQL Server on high-RAM instances.
- **Cache layer** in front of Persistent Disk (e.g., Redis persistence).
- **Read replicas** where the source of truth is a regional PD.

> **Anti-pattern (KTU exam trap)**: Storing **databases, logs, or user uploads** on Local SSD is a critical design flaw. Always pair Local SSD with a durable backing store (Cloud Storage bucket or Regional PD).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GCE Questions (Compiled from KTU 2022–2024 Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Spot with Preemptible pricing** — Spot is **market-driven**; Preemptible is **fixed 80% off**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 2. **Saying "Local SSD persists data"** — Always state it is **ephemeral** and lost on stop/migration. Costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting that custom machine types require even-numbered vCPUs** — A 6-vCPU custom machine is valid; a 5-vCPU is **not** (must round up to 6).
> 4. **Billing state confusion** — A stopped VM still bills for **disks**, not compute. Examiners check this distinction.
> 5. **CUD vs. SUD mix-up** — SUDs are **automatic and per-instance**; CUDs are **purchased commitments** for the whole project. A 1-mark trap in nearly every KTU paper.
> 6. **Missing the project boundary** — All GCE resources live inside a project; **quotas, IAM, and billing** are project-scoped. Examiners expect this hierarchy.
> 7. **No diagram for state machine** — A 7-mark lifecycle question without a state diagram loses at least 1–2 marks under KTU 2024 OBE rubric.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **GCE = IaaS** of GCP; provides VMs, block storage, VPC, and load balancers.
- [x] **Billing is per-second** after a 1-minute minimum.
- [x] **Four machine families**: General (`e2/n2`), Compute (`c2`), Memory (`m2/m3`), Accelerator (`a2`).
- [x] **Custom machine types** require **even-numbered vCPUs** and 256 MB memory increments.
- [x] **Five pricing models**: On-Demand, SUD, CUD (1-yr/3-yr), Preemptible, Spot.
- [x] **Spot VMs replace Preemptible VMs** — no 24-hour cap, market-driven pricing.
- [x] **CUDs** offer up to **57% off compute** and **70% off memory-optimized** resources.
- [x] **Local SSD is ephemeral** — lost on stop/migrate; use PD for durability.
- [x] **Regional PD** survives zone failures; **Zonal PD** does not.
- [x] **Instance lifecycle**: PROVISIONING → STAGING → RUNNING → STOPPING → STOPPED → TERMINATED.
- [x] **Stopped VM** still bills for **disks**, not compute.
- [x] **VPC is global**; **subnets are regional**; **firewalls are stateful**.
- [x] **MIGs + Instance Templates** enable autoscaling and rolling updates.
- [x] **Snapshots are global**; **PDs are zonal by default**.
- [x] **Live migration** = zero-downtime host maintenance.
- [x] **Hierarchy**: Organization → Folder → Project → VPC → Subnet → Instance → Disk.
- [x] **Use case mapping**: Redis → Memory-optimized; Transcoding → Compute-optimized + Local SSD; ML training → A2 with GPUs; CI builds → Spot/Preemptible.

> **One-Line Exam Mantra**:
> *"GCE = IaaS VMs billed per second; pick the right family, the right pricing model, and never trust Local SSD with state."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage PaaS Offerings - GAE. SaaS Offerings. Microsoft Azure: Azure Platform Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage PaaS Offerings - GAE. SaaS Offerings. Microsoft Azure: Azure Platform Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Storage PaaS Offerings — Google App Engine (GAE)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Google App Engine (GAE) is a **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** offering from Google Cloud that provides developers with a managed runtime environment for building and hosting scalable web applications. Its *Storage PaaS Offerings* refer to the family of managed, auto-scaling, eventually-consistent or strongly-consistent data services exposed to applications running on the GAE runtime, abstracting away physical disks, replication, sharding, and backup concerns from the developer.

The four primary GAE storage services are:

| Service | Type | Consistency | Intended Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Datastore** | NoSQL Document/Entity store | Strong (post-2.0) | Web-app structured data, user profiles |
| **Cloud SQL** | Managed RDBMS (MySQL/PostgreSQL) | Strong (ACID) | Relational OLTP workloads |
| **Cloud Storage** | Object store (buckets/objects) | Strong | Blobs: images, video, logs, backups |
| **Memcache** | In-memory key-value cache | Eventual | Latency-critical read-through caching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** GAE storage is fundamentally *schemaless* (Datastore/Cloud Storage) vs *schema-bound* (Cloud SQL). The Datastore is built on top of **Bigtable** and **Megastore**, providing a hierarchical key-value model with transactions limited to *entity groups*.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners):**
Think of GAE storage like a **smart rental warehouse system**:

- **Datastore** is like a giant, infinitely expandable filing cabinet where you drop labelled folders (entities) — Google handles adding new drawers, splitting folders across warehouses, and finding them in milliseconds.
- **Cloud SQL** is a fully-managed traditional bank locker room — structured tables, strict rules, but the bank staff handle vault security, backups, and electricity.
- **Cloud Storage** is an enormous self-storage unit where you dump cardboard boxes (objects) and only remember the box label (key) — perfect for videos and backups.
- **Memcache** is the reception desk's sticky-note cache — blazing fast, but if the receptionist forgets a note, the main office still has the answer.

---

### 1.2 SaaS Offerings

**Formal Definition:**
**Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** is a cloud delivery model in which a provider hosts, maintains, and remotely delivers a complete, ready-to-use application to end-users over the internet, typically via a thin client (browser) or API, on a **subscription or pay-per-use** basis. The consumer does **not** manage the underlying infrastructure, network, servers, operating systems, storage, or even application capabilities — only limited user-specific configuration settings.

**NIST Essential Characteristics of SaaS:**
1. Provider-managed infrastructure, platform, and application layer.
2. Single-instance multi-tenancy with resource pooling.
3. Elastic scaling transparent to users.
4. Metered billing and SLAs.
5. Browser-based or thin-client access.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
SaaS is like **ordering a meal at a restaurant** instead of cooking at home. You didn't grow the vegetables, butcher the chicken, light the stove, or wash the dishes — you just chose from a menu and ate. If more friends arrive, the kitchen expands its operation without you noticing; if they leave, the kitchen scales back. Gmail, Salesforce, Dropbox, Microsoft 365, and Slack are quintessential SaaS examples.

> [!NOTE]
