> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always distinguish **SaaS** (consumer uses finished app) from **PaaS** (developer builds their own app on a managed platform) and **IaaS** (consumer provisions raw VMs/networks). A common valuation pitfall is treating Gmail as PaaS — it is **SaaS**.

---

### 1.3 Microsoft Azure — Azure Platform Architecture

**Formal Definition:**
The **Azure Platform Architecture** is the layered, service-fabric-based design of Microsoft's public cloud, comprising physical infrastructure (datacenters, regions, geographies), a unified **Fabric Controller** layer, a logical **Compute / Storage / App Fabric** abstraction exposed to consumers, and a developer-facing management plane (Azure Portal, REST APIs, PowerShell, CLI, SDKs).

The **five logical layers** from bottom-up are:
1. **Physical Infrastructure Layer** — Datacenters, hardware, network fabric, regions, geographies.
2. **Fabric Layer** — Fabric Controller, Failover Cluster, Hypervisor (Hyper-V).
3. **Compute Layer** — Web Role, Worker Role, VM Role (Classic); today: App Service, Azure VMs, Azure Functions, AKS.
4. **Storage Layer** — Blob, Table, Queue, File, Disk (Azure SQL lives in the data layer above compute).
5. **App Fabric Layer** — Service Bus, Access Control, Caching, Workflow — providing middleware services for distributed applications.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Azure's architecture is like a **multi-storey smart hotel**:
- **Foundation (Physical Layer)** = the land, concrete, steel girders.
- **Mechanical systems (Fabric Layer)** = plumbing, electricity, HVAC, fire safety — these keep the building alive and automatically respond to faults.
- **Rooms (Compute Layer)** = guest rooms (VMs), banquet halls (Web Roles), kitchen staff (Worker Roles).
- **Storage rooms (Storage Layer)** = luggage rooms, refrigerators, filing cabinets for the rooms to use.
- **Concierge & amenities (App Fabric Layer)** = the lobby services that connect guests, route requests, and grant access.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 GAE Datastore — Internal Mechanics (Why & How)

- **Entities** are schemaless objects stored under a **Kind** (similar to a table) with named **Properties** (columns). Each entity has a **Key** uniquely identifying it.
- Keys form a **hierarchical path**: `ParentKind:ParentID / ChildKind:ChildID`. Operations on the same **entity group** can be performed in a single **ACID transaction**.
- Datastore uses **optimistic concurrency control** and provides two read consistency modes:
  - **Strong (default since GAE 1.5.0):** Reads reflect the latest committed write.
  - **Eventual (legacy):** Reads may return slightly stale data but are faster/cheaper.
- Underlying storage is built on **Bigtable** (sparse, distributed, sorted map) plus **Megastore** (synchronous replication + entity-group transactions).
- **Indexes** are auto-generated for simple queries; **composite indexes** must be defined manually in `index.yaml` for multi-property queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why entity groups matter:** Cross-group (XG) transactions exist, but they are slower, have higher failure probability, and are limited in throughput. Designing your data model around entity-group locality is a high-yield exam point.

### 2.2 GAE Storage Decision Heuristic

Use the following decision flow:

1. Is the data relational with complex joins? → **Cloud SQL**.
2. Is the data >10 MB binary (video, image)? → **Cloud Storage**.
3. Is the workload read-heavy and latency-critical (<10 ms)? → **Memcache** in front of Datastore.
4. Otherwise (structured, scalable, app-tier OLTP)? → **Datastore**.

### 2.3 SaaS — Deployment & Tenancy Models

| Model | Description | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-instance, multi-tenant** | One app instance serves all tenants | Cheap, but isolation is weak |
| **Multi-instance, single-tenant** | Each tenant gets a dedicated stack | Strong isolation, expensive |
| **Hybrid / Vertical SaaS** | Vertical-industry specific (e.g., healthcare SaaS) | Compliance-friendly, niche market |

> [!NOTE]
> SaaS maturity is graded by the **SaaS Maturity Model** (Microsoft-influenced): *Level 1 — Ad-Hoc/Custom* → *Level 2 — Configurable* → *Level 3 — Configurable + Multi-Tenant Efficient* → *Level 4 — Scalable + Configurable + Multi-Tenant*. KTU frequently asks "levels of SaaS maturity."

### 2.4 Azure Platform — Fabric Controller in Depth

The **Fabric Controller (FC)** is the brain of Azure. It:
- Owns and orchestrates all server resources across the datacenter.
- Monitors **Failover Clusters** and decides **live migration** of VMs away from failing hosts.
- Allocates compute, storage, and IP addresses to role instances.
- Communicates with each host agent (the **Host Agent**) via a **kernel-mode driver** for hypervisor-level control.
- Provides a **Service Registry** for internal DNS of role instances.

The **Failover Cluster** is a set of ~1000 racks whose failures are isolated to a **Cluster Partition** — if one rack fails, the FC re-instantiates VMs elsewhere within seconds, with **SLA of 99.95 %** for VM role instances.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance & Cost Equations

| Equation / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Cost-per-request (SaaS metering) | $C = N_q \cdot c_q + N_s \cdot c_s$ | $N_q$ = queries, $c_q$ = $/query; $N_s$ = storage-GB, $c_s$ = $/GB-month |
| Datastore write throughput ceiling | $T_w = \dfrac{B}{S_w \cdot (1 + r)}$ | $B$ = Bigtable bandwidth, $S_w$ = write size, $r$ = replication factor (typically 3) |
| Datastore entity-group transaction rate | $R_{eg} \le 1$ write/sec per entity group | Hard limit imposed by Megastore |
| Azure VM SLA uptime | $\text{SLA} = 1 - \dfrac{t_{\text{down}}}{t_{\text{month}}}$ | $t_{\text{month}} = 43\,200$ min; for 99.95 %: $t_{\text{down}} \le 21.6$ min |
| Cache hit ratio impact | $t_{avg} = h \cdot t_{cache} + (1-h) \cdot t_{db}$ | $h \in [0,1]$; $t_{cache} \approx 1$ ms, $t_{db} \approx 50$ ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Datastore entity groups enforce a hard ceiling of **1 write/sec** for strongly-consistent transactions. This is a guaranteed exam line item under Bloom's Level *Apply / Analyze*.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GAE Datastore** powers Google Search index metadata, Maps POIs, and YouTube user state at petabyte scale.
- **SaaS** underpins enterprise IT (Microsoft 365, Salesforce) replacing on-premise licensed software — global capex savings for SMBs.
- **Azure Fabric Controller** architecture inspires fault-tolerance patterns in OpenStack and AWS EC2 Control Plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Cache Hit Ratio for Target Latency

Suppose a web application must achieve a target average response time $t_{target}$. Given cache latency $t_{cache}$ and database latency $t_{db}$, derive the minimum cache hit ratio $h_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Define the average latency model.**

$$
t_{avg} = h \cdot t_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot t_{db}
$$

This is a convex linear combination: a fraction $h$ of requests are served from cache, the rest from the database.

**Step 2 — Substitute the known constants and rearrange for $h$.**

$$
t_{avg} = t_{db} - h \cdot (t_{db} - t_{cache})
$$

**Step 3 — Impose the SLA constraint $t_{avg} \le t_{target}$.**

$$
t_{db} - h \cdot (t_{db} - t_{cache}) \le t_{target}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $h$.**

$$
h \cdot (t_{db} - t_{cache}) \ge t_{db} - t_{target}
$$

$$
h \ge \dfrac{t_{db} - t_{target}}{t_{db} - t_{cache}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values to verify.**

Let $t_{cache} = 1$ ms, $t_{db} = 50$ ms, $t_{target} = 10$ ms.

$$
h_{min} = \dfrac{50 - 10}{50 - 1} = \dfrac{40}{49} \approx 0.816
$$

Therefore, the cache must absorb **at least ~81.6 % of reads** to meet a 10 ms latency target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **Memcache** is a non-optional dependency in production GAE apps — without it, the Datastore tail latency violates any meaningful UX SLA.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — GAE Storage Trade-offs

| Criterion | Datastore | Cloud SQL | Cloud Storage | Memcache |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data model | Document/Entity | Relational | Blob/Object | Key-Value (in-memory) |
| Max single entity / row | **1 MB** | ~10 MB (BLOB offload recommended) | **5 TB per object** | **1 MB per item** |
| Consistency | Strong (default) | Strong (ACID) | Strong | Eventual |
| Query language | GQL | Standard SQL | REST listing | None (get/set only) |
| Transactional? | Yes (per entity group) | Yes (full ACID) | No | No |
| Cost model | $/read, /write, /lookup | $/hour + storage | $/GB-month + ops | $/GB-hour |
| Typical use | User profiles, sessions | E-commerce, OLTP | Media, backups | Session, hot keys |

---

### 3.3 Azure Role-Instance Lifecycle Derivation

Azure Compute roles transition through states during deployment:

$$
\text{Idle} \rightarrow \text{Busy} \rightarrow \text{Stopping} \rightarrow \text{Stopped} \rightarrow \text{Deleted}
$$

Each role instance is allocated an **input endpoint** (public IP + port) and is registered in the **Service Registry** by the Fabric Controller. The role host process (e.g., `WaWorkerHost.exe` for Worker Roles) communicates via a TCP channel to the **FIP (Fabric Identity Provider)** which issues an **X.509 certificate** for inter-role authentication.

> [!NOTE]
> The complete SLA arithmetic for an Azure deployment with $n$ role instances spread across $k$ upgrade domains and $f$ fault domains is:
>
> $$
> P(\text{uptime}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - P_i)
> $$
>
> where $P_i$ is the per-upgrade-domain unavailability. For typical $k = 5$ and $P_i = 0.001$, the combined SLA approaches $99.995\,\%$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GAE Storage Stack — Service Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userApp["User GAE Application Code"]
    ds["Datastore NoSQL API"]
    csql["Cloud SQL Managed RDBMS"]
    cstr["Cloud Storage Object API"]
    mc["Memcache In Memory KV"]
    bt["Bigtable Distributed Sorted Map"]
    ms["Megastore Layer Entity Groups ACID"]
    gcs["Google Colossus File System"]

    userApp --> ds
    userApp --> csql
    userApp --> cstr
    userApp --> mc
    ds --> ms
    ms --> bt
    cstr --> gcs
    mc -.cache miss.-> ds
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The Memcache layer is drawn with a dashed edge to indicate it is a **read-through / read-aside** dependency, not a primary store. A cache miss falls through to Datastore, not the other way around.

---

### 4.2 Azure Platform Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Layer 5 App Fabric"]
        sb["Service Bus"]
        acs["Access Control"]
        cache["Azure Caching"]
        wf["Workflow Engine"]
    end

    subgraph B["Layer 4 Storage Layer"]
        blob["Blob Storage"]
        tab["Table Storage"]
        que["Queue Storage"]
        fil["File Storage"]
    end

    subgraph C["Layer 3 Compute Layer"]
        web["Web Role"]
        wrk["Worker Role"]
        vm["VM Role"]
    end

    subgraph D["Layer 2 Fabric Layer"]
        fc["Fabric Controller"]
        ha["Host Agent"]
        hv["Hyper V Hypervisor"]
        foc["Failover Cluster"]
    end

    subgraph E["Layer 1 Physical Infrastructure"]
        dc["Datacenters"]
        rg["Regions"]
        geo["Geographies"]
        nw["Network Fabric"]
    end

    fc --> ha
    ha --> hv
    foc --> fc
    web --> blob
    web --> tab
    web --> que
    wrk --> que
    vm --> fil
    web -.uses.-> sb
    wrk -.uses.-> acs
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    C --> A
```

---

### 4.3 SaaS Multi-Tenancy Models — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Model A Single Instance Multi Tenant"]
        i1a["Tenant A"]
        i1b["Tenant B"]
        i1c["Tenant C"]
        appA["Single App Instance"]
        dbA["Shared Database"]
        i1a --> appA
        i1b --> appA
        i1c --> appA
        appA --> dbA
    end

    subgraph M2["Model B Multi Instance Single Tenant"]
        i2a["Tenant A"]
        appB["App A"]
        dbB["Database A"]
        i2a --> appB --> dbB
    end

    subgraph M3["Model C Multi Instance Multi Tenant"]
        i3a["Tenant A"]
        i3b["Tenant B"]
        appC1["App Instance 1"]
        appC2["App Instance 2"]
        dbC1["DB Shard 1"]
        dbC2["DB Shard 2"]
        i3a --> appC1 --> dbC1
        i3b --> appC2 --> dbC2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows above model **request flow**, not ownership. In **Model C**, Azure-style, App Instances and DB Shards are dynamically provisioned by the fabric controller in proportion to tenant load.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[Q.A1] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Differentiate between Datastore and Cloud SQL as GAE storage options. When would you choose one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Datastore | Cloud SQL |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Schemaless, NoSQL | Relational, schema-bound |
| Consistency | Strong, eventually-consistent read opt-in | Full ACID |
| Scalability | Auto-sharded, horizontally scalable | Vertical scale + read replicas |
| Transaction | Per entity group only | Full SQL transactions |
| Best for | Web-app structured data, variable schema | Complex joins, OLTP, reporting |

**Decision heuristic (1 mark):** Choose **Datastore** for schemaless, high-write-scale web data (user profiles, game state). Choose **Cloud SQL** for relational OLTP, joins, BI reporting, or when ACID across multiple tables is mandatory.

---

**[Q.A2] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*List the essential characteristics of SaaS as per NIST cloud model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure (network, servers, OS, storage) — only limited user-specific application settings.
2. The application is accessed via **thin client** (browser) or **API** over the network.
3. **Multi-tenancy** with resource pooling — a single application instance serves multiple tenants with logical isolation.
4. **Elasticity and scalability** — capacity scales transparently with demand.
5. **Metered / pay-per-use** billing with **SLAs** (e.g., 99.9 % uptime).
6. Provider manages application-level updates, patches, and security.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

**[Q.B-A] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3] — Question Choice A**

**(a)** With a neat architectural diagram, explain the **Microsoft Azure Platform Architecture**. Describe the role of the **Fabric Controller** and **Failover Cluster** in achieving high availability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Azure's platform is structured into **five logical layers**, each abstracting complexity from the layer above:

1. **Physical Infrastructure Layer** — Datacenters organized into **Regions** (geographic clusters of datacenters) and **Geographies** (data-residency boundaries). Network fabric interconnects racks via high-speed optical links.

2. **Fabric Layer** — The **Fabric Controller (FC)** is a distributed state machine running on a reserved set of machines in each cluster. It:
   - Allocates compute, storage, IP, and bandwidth resources to role instances.
   - Tracks **health** of every host, VM, and service.
   - Decides **when and where** to live-migrate VMs (a host failure triggers re-instantiation within seconds).
   - Maintains a **Service Registry** (internal DNS) mapping role instance names to private IPs.
   
   The **Failover Cluster** is the rack-level grouping (~1000 racks) where the FC enforces fault isolation. Within a cluster, host failures are independent and bounded; VM live-migration completes within **~30 seconds**, giving a **VM SLA of 99.95 %**.

3. **Compute Layer** — Three classical roles:
   - **Web Role**: Hosts IIS + ASP.NET, automatically load-balanced and exposed via HTTP/HTTPS input endpoints.
   - **Worker Role**: Long-running background process; no HTTP endpoint, consumes **Queue** messages.
   - **VM Role**: Customer-provided Windows Server image; lowest abstraction.
   
   Modern equivalents: **App Service**, **Azure Functions** (serverless), **Azure VMs**, **AKS**.

4. **Storage Layer** — Four primitives:
   - **Blob**: Binary large objects (3 tiers: Hot, Cool, Archive).
   - **Table**: NoSQL key-value store.
   - **Queue**: Asynchronous messaging for Worker Roles.
   - **File**: SMB-mounted shared file systems.

5. **App Fabric Layer** — Middleware services:
   - **Service Bus**: Topic/queue-based pub-sub with brokered messaging.
   - **Access Control Service (ACS)**: Identity federation (claims-based).
   - **Caching**: Distributed in-memory cache.
   - **Workflow**: Long-running orchestrated business processes.

**High Availability Mechanism (3 marks breakdown):**
- FC continuously polls **Host Agents** (one per physical server) at ~30-second intervals.
- On host failure, FC reassigns its VMs to a healthy host within the same cluster partition.
- VMs are stamped across **upgrade domains** (rolling deploys) and **fault domains** (rack-level fault isolation).
- Storage data is **triply replicated** within the datacenter; **geo-replication** to a paired region is opt-in.

**(b)** Compare **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)**, **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)**, and **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** with one real-world example for each. Explain which layer Azure Web Roles occupy and justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Consumer Controls | Provider Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | Hardware, network, storage, virtualization | AWS EC2, Azure VMs |
| **PaaS** | App code, data | OS, middleware, runtime, scaling | GAE, Azure Web/Worker Roles |
| **SaaS** | Only user-level configuration | Everything including the app itself | Gmail, Salesforce, Office 365 |

**Azure Web Role = PaaS (justification, 4 marks):**
- Developer writes **only** application code (e.g., ASP.NET pages).
- Azure automatically provisions the VM (Hyper-V + Windows Server + IIS + ASP.NET runtime), configures load balancer input endpoint, monitors health, applies OS patches, and scales out/in based on CPU or queue depth.
- Developer has **no OS-level RDP access** by default (limited access possible).
- Therefore, the Web Role sits squarely in the PaaS category — between IaaS (where you manage the OS) and SaaS (where you only consume a finished app).

---

**[Q.B-B] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3] — Question Choice B (Alternative)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Storage PaaS Offerings of Google App Engine (GAE)**. With diagrams, explain how **Datastore**, **Cloud SQL**, **Cloud Storage**, and **Memcache** differ in terms of data model, consistency, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

GAE provides four storage tiers, each optimized for a distinct access pattern.

1. **Datastore (NoSQL Document Store)**
   - Data model: schemaless **entities** of a **Kind** with named **properties**; identified by **Keys** forming a hierarchical path.
   - Consistency: **Strongly consistent** by default (since GAE 1.5.0); older **Eventual Consistency** mode available for high-throughput reads.
   - Transactions: limited to **single entity group** (~1 write/sec ceiling); **Cross-Group (XG)** transactions exist but are slower.
   - Underlying: **Bigtable** (sparse, sorted, distributed map) + **Megastore** (entity-group ACID over Bigtable).
   - Typical use: user profiles, game state, sessions, product catalog.

2. **Cloud SQL (Managed RDBMS)**
   - Data model: relational schema with tables, FKs, indexes; supports MySQL and PostgreSQL.
   - Consistency: full **ACID** transactions with strong isolation.
   - Limits: single-row size, recommended offload of BLOBs to Cloud Storage.
   - Typical use: OLTP workloads needing complex joins, BI front-end, e-commerce order management.

3. **Cloud Storage (Object Store)**
   - Data model: **buckets** containing **objects** addressed by key; metadata optional.
   - Consistency: **strong read-after-write** for all operations.
   - Tiers: Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive — each with $/GB-month and retrieval-cost trade-offs.
   - Typical use: media files, backups, logs, static asset hosting.

4. **Memcache (In-Memory Cache)**
   - Data model: simple key → blob.
   - Consistency: **eventual**, no durability guarantee — items can be evicted at any time.
   - Limits: ~1 MB per item, ~250 MB total per instance in shared mode.
   - Typical use: read-through cache for Datastore, session cache, hot-key acceleration.

**Decision flow (2 marks):** Binary/Blob → Cloud Storage; relational joins → Cloud SQL; structured scalable app data → Datastore; latency-critical reads → Memcache fronting Datastore.

**(b)** Explain the **SaaS deployment models** and the **SaaS Maturity Model levels**. What are the key challenges of multi-tenancy in SaaS? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Deployment Models (3 marks):**
1. **Public SaaS** — Provider-owned infrastructure, multi-tenant, internet-delivered (e.g., Salesforce).
2. **Private SaaS** — Single-tenant deployment, often on customer premises or in a private cloud (e.g., dedicated Office 365 GCC High).
3. **Hybrid SaaS** — Combines public SaaS with on-premise integration via connectors (e.g., ServiceNow + on-prem SAP).
4. **Vertical SaaS** — Industry-specific (healthcare SaaS: Epic on Azure; legal SaaS: Clio).

**SaaS Maturity Model (Microsoft-derived, 2 marks):**
| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **L1 — Ad-Hoc / Custom** | Per-customer builds, no multi-tenancy |
| **L2 — Configurable** | Single instance, per-tenant configuration |
| **L3 — Configurable + Multi-Tenant Efficient** | Single instance, pooled resources, shared services |
| **L4 — Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant** | Auto-scaling across servers with elastic capacity |

**Multi-Tenancy Challenges (2 marks):**
- **Data Isolation** — preventing cross-tenant leakage via row-level security, schema-per-tenant, or DB-per-tenant trade-offs.
- **Performance Isolation** — noisy-neighbor effect; one tenant's heavy query cannot starve another's.
- **Customization** — balancing per-tenant configuration vs. core-code drift.
- **Compliance & Auditing** — GDPR, HIPAA demand tenant-scoped audit logs and right-to-be-forgotten semantics.
- **Metering & Billing** — fine-grained usage telemetry without violating isolation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Datastore with Cloud Storage.** Datastore stores *structured entities*; Cloud Storage stores *binary blobs*. Students lose 2–3 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Web Role ≠ VM Role.** Web Role is PaaS (provider-managed IIS); VM Role is closer to IaaS (user-supplied image). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Always state "1 write/sec per entity group"** when discussing Datastore transactions. Omitting this limit is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 4. **SaaS Maturity Model** — listing only 2 levels out of 4 fetches partial credit. Memorize all four.
> 5. **Fabric Controller ≠ Hypervisor.** Fabric Controller is the orchestrator (control plane); Hyper-V is the actual hypervisor (data plane). Examiners deduct for conflating them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GAE Storage Tiers:** Datastore (NoSQL + entity groups + 1 write/sec/group), Cloud SQL (managed MySQL/PostgreSQL), Cloud Storage (objects/blobs up to 5 TB), Memcache (in-memory KV, no durability).
- **Datastore Transactions:** Limited to a single **entity group**; cross-group transactions exist but are slower and prone to failure.
- **Cloud Storage Tiers:** Standard / Nearline / Coldline / Archive — each trade-off between $/GB-month and retrieval latency/cost.
- **Memcache:** Read-through cache in front of Datastore; ~1 MB per item; essential for meeting sub-10 ms latency SLAs.
- **SaaS = consumed app, not built.** Consumer configures settings, never touches OS/middleware.
- **SaaS Maturity Model Levels:** L1 Ad-Hoc → L2 Configurable → L3 Multi-Tenant Efficient → L4 Scalable + Configurable + Multi-Tenant.
- **Azure 5-Layer Stack (bottom-up):** Physical → Fabric → Compute → Storage → App Fabric.
- **Fabric Controller** orchestrates resources; **Failover Cluster** isolates failures; live-migration of VMs achieves **99.95 % SLA**.
- **Compute Role Taxonomy:** Web Role (PaaS, IIS+ASP.NET), Worker Role (background), VM Role (IaaS-ish).
- **Storage Primitives:** Blob, Table, Queue, File — each suited to a different access pattern (binary / sparse / messaging / SMB share).
- **App Fabric Services:** Service Bus, Access Control, Caching, Workflow — middleware for distributed apps.
- **High Availability Math:** SLA = $1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - P_i)$ across $k$ upgrade domains.
- **Cache SLA Formula:** $h_{min} = \dfrac{t_{db} - t_{target}}{t_{db} - t_{cache}}$ — quantifies minimum cache hit ratio for a latency target.
- **Decision Heuristic to Memorize:** Blob → Cloud Storage; Joins → Cloud SQL; Scalable structured → Datastore; Latency → Memcache.
- **Terminology Pitfalls:** Datastore ≠ Cloud Storage; Fabric Controller ≠ Hypervisor; Web Role ≠ VM Role; SaaS ≠ PaaS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyper-V
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hyper-V

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hyper-V** is Microsoft's native **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** that creates and manages virtualized computing environments, known as *virtual machines (VMs)*, by abstracting the physical hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage, and networking) of a host machine and distributing them among multiple isolated execution environments.

Introduced initially as a server role in **Windows Server 2008**, Hyper-V was later extended to client operating systems (Windows 8/10/11 Pro, Enterprise, and Education editions) as **Windows Hyper-V Platform (Client Hyper-V)**. It forms the foundational virtualization layer for **Microsoft Azure** and is integral to private cloud and hybrid cloud deployments in enterprise data centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Hyper-V falls under the *Server Virtualization & Hypervisors* section of Module 3. Students must focus on its **Type-1 architecture**, **parent/child partition model**, **virtual networking (vSwitch)**, and **high-availability features** such as **Live Migration** and **Hyper-V Replica**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large industrial building (your **physical server**) with one main electrical panel. A **hypervisor** is like the **building's energy management controller** — instead of giving all the electricity to a single tenant, it intelligently partitions power, cooling, space, and network cables so that *many independent offices* (**virtual machines**) can operate in parallel, each believing it has its own dedicated building.

- The **Hyper-V hypervisor** = the master controller sitting directly on the bare metal.
- Each **Virtual Machine (VM)** = an isolated office running its own operating system (Windows, Linux, etc.).
- The **Virtual Switch (vSwitch)** = a programmable patch panel that connects these offices to physical or virtual networks.

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **VM** | Virtual Machine — isolated guest OS environment |
| **VHD / VHDX** | Virtual Hard Disk — file-based virtual storage (VHDX max 64 TB) |
| **vCPU** | Virtual Central Processing Unit allocated to a VM |
| **NUMA** | Non-Uniform Memory Access — topology preserved for VMs |
| **vSwitch** | Virtual Switch — Layer-2 software network switch |
| **SLAT** | Second Level Address Translation (EPT/RVI hardware assist) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLAT (Second Level Address Translation)** is mandatory for Hyper-V. It is implemented as **Intel EPT (Extended Page Tables)** or **AMD RVI (Rapid Virtualization Indexing)**. Without SLAT, Hyper-V cannot be installed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 Hyper-V Architecture: The Partition Model

Hyper-V uses a **partition-based architecture** in which each running virtual machine is a logical unit called a *partition*.

### 2.1.1 Parent Partition (Root Partition)
- It is the **first partition created** when the Hyper-V role is installed.
- Runs the **host operating system** (Windows Server / Windows 10/11).
- Hosts the **Virtualization Service Provider (VSP)** which delivers device services to child partitions.
- **Owns and controls** physical hardware resources, except those explicitly delegated to child partitions.
- Manages VM lifecycle operations (create, start, stop, snapshot, migrate).

### 2.1.2 Child Partitions
- Each child partition hosts **one or more guest operating systems**.
- Does **not** have direct access to physical hardware.
- Uses a **Virtualization Service Client (VSC)** that consumes resources via **VMBus**.
- Believes it owns real hardware; Hyper-V transparently emulates or paravirtualizes the I/O.

### 2.1.3 The VMBus — The Backbone Communication Channel
- A high-speed, logical, inter-partition communication bus.
- Uses **shared memory (VSP/VSC ring buffers)** for extremely low-latency I/O.
- All paravirtualized I/O (storage, network) traverses the VMBus.

## 2.2 Core Components of Hyper-V

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Hyper-V Hypervisor** | Bare-metal microkernel hypervisor (runs in Ring -1) |
| **VM Worker Process (vmwp.exe)** | Hosts the execution of a single VM |
| **VM Service (vmservice.exe)** | Manages virtual machine state in the management OS |
| **WMI Provider (vmms.exe)** | Windows Management Instrumentation scripting interface |
| **VHD/VHDX Files** | Encapsulated virtual hard disk formats (VHDX = resilient, large) |
| **Virtual Switch (vSwitch)** | Software-defined Layer-2 switch for VM networking |
| **Hyper-V Manager** | MMC snap-in GUI for local and remote management |
| **Failover Cluster Manager** | Provides high availability and Live Migration coordination |

## 2.3 Generation 1 vs Generation 2 Virtual Machines

| Feature | Generation 1 | Generation 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Firmware | Legacy BIOS | Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (UEFI) |
| Boot Disk | IDE only | SCSI / virtualized boot from SCSI |
| Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported |
| Network Boot | Legacy NIC | Synthetic NIC |
| Maximum VHDX | 64 TB (VHDX) | 64 TB (VHDX) |
| Recommended Use | Legacy OS (Windows XP, older Linux) | Modern OS (Windows 8+, Linux 3.x+) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024, always use **Generation 2 VMs** unless the question explicitly specifies a legacy guest OS. Generation 2 is the default and the most commonly tested variant.

## 2.4 Hyper-V Networking — The Virtual Switch

The **Hyper-V Virtual Switch (vSwitch)** is a software-based, Layer-2, extensible network switch available in three operating modes:

1. **External Switch** — Binds to a physical NIC; provides VMs access to the external network.
2. **Internal Switch** — Allows communication between VMs and the host, but no external network connectivity.
3. **Private Switch** — Communication only between VMs on the same switch; host cannot interact.

**Advanced features include:**
- VLAN tagging (802.1Q)
- Bandwidth management (minimum/maximum)
- Port mirroring
- SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization) for near-native network throughput
- Network isolation and protected networks

## 2.5 Storage Architecture

| Storage Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fixed-size VHD/VHDX** | Entire disk space pre-allocated; highest performance |
| **Dynamically expanding VHD/VHDX** | Grows on demand; lower initial footprint |
| **Differencing disk** | Child VHDX referencing a parent read-only VHDX |
| **Pass-through disk** | Direct access to a physical LUN (no file format) |
| **SMB 3.0 Share** | VM files stored on a network file share |
| **Storage Spaces / Cluster Shared Volumes (CSV)** | Used in clustered Hyper-V deployments |

## 2.6 High-Availability & Disaster Recovery Features

- **Live Migration**: Move a running VM between Hyper-V hosts with **zero downtime** and **zero packet loss**. Utilizes Kerberos authentication or constrained delegation.
- **Storage Live Migration**: Migrates only the VM's storage while the VM continues running.
- **Hyper-V Replica**: Asynchronous replication of VMs to a secondary site for disaster recovery. Recovery Point Objective (RPO) typically **5 minutes (or 30 seconds for 15 minutes in newer builds)**.
- **Failover Clustering**: Multiple Hyper-V hosts grouped to provide automatic restart of VMs in case of host failure.
- **Nested Virtualization**: Running Hyper-V *inside* a Hyper-V VM (useful for labs and containers).
- **Shielded VMs**: Generation 2 VMs with BitLocker encryption, secure boot, and remote attestation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Live Migration** uses the **SMB (Server Message Block) Multichannel** protocol by default, allowing simultaneous transfer over multiple network adapters for faster migration.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hyper-V underpins:
- **Microsoft Azure** — Azure's compute fabric originated from Hyper-V technology.
- **Windows Server Containers & Hyper-V Containers** — Two isolation levels for containerized workloads.
- **Software-Defined Datacenter (SDDC)** — Combined with System Center Virtual Machine Manager (SCVMM) and Azure Pack.
- **DevOps & CI/CD Labs** — Rapid provisioning of Windows/Linux test environments.
- **VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure)** — Remote desktop delivery using **Remote Desktop Services** on Hyper-V.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Configuration Walkthrough

This section consolidates **operational logic, deployment steps, and decision matrices** that are frequently evaluated in KTU's analytical questions.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: VM Creation Workflow in Hyper-V

The complete lifecycle of provisioning a VM in Hyper-V proceeds as follows:

**Step 1: Hardware Prerequisite Verification**
- Processor must support **SLAT** (Intel EPT or AMD RVI).
- **Virtualization Technology (VT-x / AMD-V)** must be enabled in the UEFI/BIOS.
- **Data Execution Prevention (DEP)** must be enabled.

**Step 2: Role Installation (on Windows Server)**
- Open **Server Manager → Add Roles and Features → Hyper-V**.
- Select the physical network adapter to bind to the **Virtual Switch**.

**Step 3: Virtual Switch Configuration**
- Choose **External**, **Internal**, or **Private** mode depending on connectivity requirements.

**Step 4: Create Virtual Machine**
- Open **Hyper-V Manager → New → Virtual Machine**.
- Assign a name, choose **Generation 1 or 2**, set startup memory, configure networking, and create a virtual hard disk (VHDX).

**Step 5: Install Guest Operating System**
- Mount an ISO file or boot from network (PXE).
- Install **Hyper-V Integration Services** (auto-installed in modern Windows guests).

**Step 6: Configure VM Settings**
- Adjust vCPUs, memory (static or **Dynamic Memory**), checkpoints, and automatic start actions.

**Step 7: Validate and Operate**
- Use the **Validate Cluster** wizard for clustered environments.

## 3.2 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which VM Storage Type

| Storage Type | Best For | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed VHDX | Production servers, high I/O workloads | Higher disk consumption |
| Dynamic VHDX | Dev/test environments | Slight fragmentation overhead |
| Differencing Disk | Lab setups, multiple VMs sharing one base image | Parent corruption affects all children |
| Pass-through Disk | High-performance SAN-backed VMs | No snapshot/live migration convenience |
| SMB 3.0 Share | Scale-out file server storage | Network latency dependent |

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Hyper-V vs VMware ESXi vs Xen

| Attribute | Microsoft Hyper-V | VMware ESXi | Citrix XenServer (Xen) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hypervisor Type | Type 1 (Bare-metal) | Type 1 (Bare-metal) | Type 1 (Bare-metal) |
| Parent OS Required | Yes (Windows Server) or Hyper-V Server (free) | No (runs on bare metal) | No (runs on bare metal) |
| Partition Model | Parent + Child partitions | VMkernel + VMs | Dom0 + DomU |
| Live Migration | Yes (Shared Nothing Live Migration supported) | Yes (vMotion) | Yes (XenMotion) |
| Replica/DR | Hyper-V Replica | vSphere Replication | Not built-in |
| Cloud Integration | Native Azure integration | VMware Cloud on AWS | Citrix Cloud |
| Cost | Bundled with Windows Server (license required) | Free vSphere Hypervisor; paid for advanced features | Open source core |

## 3.4 Dynamic Memory: Mathematical Resource Adjustment Model

Hyper-V supports **Dynamic Memory**, which automatically adjusts the amount of memory assigned to a VM based on current demand. The conceptual evaluation criteria are:

- **Startup RAM**: Initial memory allocated at VM boot.
- **Minimum RAM**: Floor memory the VM can shrink to.
- **Maximum RAM**: Ceiling memory the VM can grow up to.
- **Memory Buffer**: Percentage of available memory to reserve as a buffer.
- **Memory Weight**: Relative priority for memory distribution when there is contention.

The Hyper-V balancer continuously monitors:
- **Current memory demand** from the guest.
- **Available host memory**.
- **Configured buffer and weight**.

The effective memory is dynamically adjusted to lie within the *\[Minimum RAM, Maximum RAM\]* window, and the **memory pressure** is computed as the ratio of *requested memory to available memory*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Memory** is available only for **Generation 1 and Generation 2 VMs** running guest operating systems that support it (Windows Server 2008 R2 SP1+, Windows 7+). Linux guests require **KVM Hyper-V Enlightenment** or newer kernels with the hyperv balloon driver.

## 3.5 Live Migration: Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The **Live Migration** process executes the following sequence to achieve zero-downtime VM movement:

1. **Pre-Migration Setup** — Validate source and destination hosts are in the same Failover Cluster (or use Shared Nothing Live Migration with SMB).
2. **Authentication** — Kerberos or CredSSP-based constrained delegation.
3. **VM State Copy** — Memory pages of the running VM are transmitted iteratively to the destination host.
4. **Iterative Bitmap Tracking** — Tracks which memory pages have been modified during transfer.
5. **Freeze-and-Copy Phase** — A brief pause occurs (typically **less than 1 second on 1 Gbps**; under **200 ms on 10 Gbps**) during which final modified pages are copied.
6. **VM Resumes at Destination** — The destination VM is activated and continues execution. The source VM is then deleted.
7. **Network Redirection** — Because the destination MAC address is the same, only a gratuitous ARP update is sent to refresh switch MAC tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **Shared Nothing Live Migration**, only SMB 3.0 file shares are required; no shared storage is needed. This enables cost-effective DR scenarios in branch offices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hyper-V Layered Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GuestVMs["GUEST VIRTUAL MACHINES"]
        VM1["VM 1: Windows Server 2022"]
        VM2["VM 2: Ubuntu Linux 22.04"]
        VM3["VM 3: Windows 11 Desktop"]
    end

    subgraph ChildPartitions["CHILD PARTITIONS (Ring 0/3)"]
        VSC1["VSC - Storage"]
        VSC2["VSC - Network"]
        VMBus["VMBus High-Speed Channel"]
    end

    subgraph ParentPartition["PARENT PARTITION (Host OS)"]
        VSP1["VSP - Storage Provider"]
        VSP2["VSP - Network Provider"]
        VMMS["VMM Service and WMI Provider"]
        HYPERVMGR["Hyper-V Manager GUI"]
    end

    subgraph HypervisorLayer["HYPER-V HYPERVISOR (Ring -1)"]
        SCHED["CPU Scheduler"]
        MEMMGR["Memory Manager"]
        PARTITIONCTRL["Partition Controller"]
    end

    subgraph HardwareLayer["PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
        CPU["CPU SLAT EPT RVI"]
        RAM["DDR Memory"]
        NIC["Network Adapters"]
        STORAGE["SSDs HDDs SAN"]
    end

    VM1 --> VSC1
    VM2 --> VSC1
    VM3 --> VSC1
    VSC1 --> VMBus
    VSC2 --> VMBus
    VMBus --> VSP1
    VMBus --> VSP2
    VSP1 --> STORAGE
    VSP2 --> NIC
    HYPERVMGR --> VMMS
    VMMS --> PARTITIONCTRL
    SCHED --> CPU
    MEMMGR --> RAM
    PARTITIONCTRL --> SCHED
    PARTITIONCTRL --> MEMMGR
```

## 4.2 Hyper-V Networking — Virtual Switch Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External["EXTERNAL VSWITCH"]
        EXTNIC["Bound to Physical NIC"]
    end

    subgraph Internal["INTERNAL VSWITCH"]
        INTNET["Host to VM only"]
    end

    subgraph Private["PRIVATE VSWITCH"]
        PVTONLY["VM to VM only"]
    end

    INTERNET["External Network"] -->|Uplink| EXTNIC
    EXTNIC --> VMEXT["VM with External Access"]
    INTNET --> HOSTOS["Host OS"]
    INTNET --> VMINT["Internal VMs"]
    VMINT <--> VMINT2["Internal VM 2"]
    VMPRV1["Private VM 1"] <--> PVTONLY
    VMPRV2["Private VM 2"] <--> PVTONLY
```

## 4.3 Live Migration Workflow (Sequential Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Step 1: Authenticate Source and Destination"] --> B["Step 2: Open Kerberos or CredSSP Session"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Transfer Memory Pages Iteratively"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Track Modified Pages via Bitmap"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Pause VM at Source under 1 second"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Copy Final Modified Pages"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Resume VM at Destination"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Issue Gratuitous ARP Update"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Decommission Source VM"]
```

## 4.4 Generation 1 vs Generation 2 VM Boot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph G1["GENERATION 1 VM"]
        G1BIOS["Legacy BIOS Firmware"]
        G1IDE["IDE Emulated Controller"]
        G1NIC["Legacy Emulated NIC"]
        G1OS["Guest OS"]
        G1BIOS --> G1IDE --> G1OS
        G1BIOS --> G1NIC
    end

    subgraph G2["GENERATION 2 VM"]
        G2UEFI["UEFI Firmware with Secure Boot"]
        G2SCSI["Synthetic SCSI Controller"]
        G2SYN["Synthetic VMBus NIC"]
        G2OS["Guest OS"]
        G2UEFI --> G2SCSI --> G2OS
        G2UEFI --> G2SYN
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All four schematics above emphasize the layered, microkernel-based architecture of Hyper-V. The parent–child partition model and VMBus-based I/O are recurring themes that examiners expect students to draw and label precisely.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Hyper-V. Why is it classified as a Type-1 hypervisor?**
*`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 3 Marks]`*

**Model Answer:**

Hyper-V is a **native bare-metal hypervisor** developed by Microsoft that enables multiple isolated virtual machines to run concurrently on a single physical host by virtualizing CPU, memory, storage, and network resources.

Hyper-V is classified as a **Type-1 hypervisor** because it installs and runs **directly on the physical hardware**, without the requirement of a host operating system in the case of **Hyper-V Server** (the standalone free edition). The hypervisor microkernel interacts with hardware at **Ring -1 (also called Root Mode)**, and the management operating system is itself a partitioned component above the hypervisor. This bare-metal placement delivers higher performance, stability, and isolation compared to Type-2 hypervisors such as VMware Workstation or Oracle VirtualBox, which depend on a host OS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly state that Hyper-V is a Type-2 hypervisor simply because it can be installed as a Windows role. The correct interpretation is: **when installed as a Windows role**, the management OS becomes the *parent partition*; the *hypervisor itself still executes at Ring -1*, preserving its Type-1 identity.

---

**Q2. What is the role of the VMBus in Hyper-V? List two advantages of VMBus-based communication.**
*`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 Marks]`*

**Model Answer:**

The **VMBus** is a logical, high-speed, in-memory communication channel that connects the **Virtualization Service Clients (VSCs)** running inside child partitions to the **Virtualization Service Providers (VSPs)** running in the parent partition. It enables paravirtualized I/O, allowing storage, network, and input device traffic to flow without expensive emulation.

**Two key advantages of VMBus-based communication are:**

1. **Low-latency I/O:** Because VMBus uses **shared memory ring buffers** rather than trapping into the hypervisor for every I/O operation, the latency is comparable to native I/O.
2. **Reduced hypervisor overhead:** The hypervisor is not involved in every data transfer, freeing CPU cycles for actual VM workloads and improving overall consolidation density.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do *not* confuse VMBus with the Virtual Switch (vSwitch). VMBus is an inter-partition communication bus; the vSwitch is a Layer-2 networking construct for VM traffic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-end Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. The student must answer *either* the full OR alternative question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**a) Explain the architecture of Hyper-V with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the parent partition and child partitions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Hyper-V adopts a **partition-based architecture**. A *partition* is a logical isolated unit in which an operating system executes. There are two kinds of partitions: the **parent (root) partition** and one or more **child partitions**.

**Parent Partition:**
- Created **first** during Hyper-V initialization.
- Runs the **management operating system** (Windows Server or Hyper-V Server).
- Owns and controls all physical hardware resources.
- Hosts the **VSPs (Virtualization Service Providers)** that expose storage, network, and input services to child partitions.
- Runs the **VMM service, WMI provider, and Hyper-V Manager** console.

**Child Partitions:**
- Each runs a **guest operating system** (Windows, Linux, FreeBSD, etc.).
- Does not directly access physical hardware; instead, it consumes virtualized resources.
- Uses **VSCs (Virtualization Service Clients)** that connect to the parent’s VSPs through the **VMBus**.
- Believes it is running on real hardware, but all privileged operations are intercepted by the hypervisor.

**The Hyper-V Hypervisor (Microkernel):**
- Sits **below the parent partition** at **Ring -1**.
- Controls **CPU scheduling, memory partitioning, and interrupt routing**.
- Provides **isolation** between partitions and enforces security boundaries.

**Diagram Reference:** See **SECTION 4.1** for the layered architecture.

**b) Describe the three types of Hyper-V Virtual Switches. In which scenario would you use each? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| vSwitch Type | Connectivity | Typical Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **External** | Bound to a **physical NIC**; allows VMs to access the physical LAN/WAN | Production servers needing internet and corporate network access |
| **Internal** | VMs can communicate with the **host OS** and other VMs on the same switch; **no external network** | Isolated test labs, secure management network, host-to-VM service testing |
| **Private** | VMs can communicate **only with each other**; host cannot interact | Simulating multi-tier internal applications with no external dependency |

**Real-World Scenario Mapping:**
- A **web server VM** that must serve public users → **External vSwitch**.
- A **database VM** that only the application server should reach → **Internal vSwitch**.
- A **cluster of test VMs** replicating a multi-tier app → **Private vSwitch**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Internal** with **Private**. The defining difference is that the **host can communicate with Internal-vSwitch VMs**, but **cannot** with Private-vSwitch VMs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**a) Explain Generation 1 and Generation 2 Virtual Machines in Hyper-V. Compare them in at least five points. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Hyper-V supports two VM formats, introduced to support evolving firmware standards:

**Generation 1 (Legacy):**
- Uses **legacy BIOS** firmware.
- Boot devices connect through an **emulated IDE controller**.
- Supports **legacy network adapters** for PXE boot.
- Maximum 4 IDE devices per VM.
- Required for older operating systems (e.g., Windows XP, Windows Server 2003).

**Generation 2 (Modern):**
- Uses **UEFI** firmware natively.
- Supports **Secure Boot** for integrity verification.
- Boot disks connect through a **synthetic SCSI controller** with much higher performance.
- Uses **synthetic NICs** for network boot.
- Supports **Shielded VMs** for tamper-proof workloads.
- Recommended for all modern OS workloads.

**Comparison Table:**

| Attribute | Generation 1 | Generation 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Firmware | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
| Boot Controller | IDE (emulated) | SCSI (synthetic) |
| Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported |
| Boot from SCSI | No | Yes |
| Network Boot | Legacy NIC | Synthetic NIC |
| Maximum VHDX Size | 64 TB | 64 TB |
| Shielded VM Support | No | Yes |
| Default in Modern Hyper-V | Optional | Default |

**b) Discuss the Live Migration process in Hyper-V. What is Shared Nothing Live Migration? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Live Migration** is a Hyper-V feature that moves a running virtual machine from one Hyper-V host to another **without downtime** and **without dropped network connections**. The migration maintains VM memory state and network identity.

**Step-by-Step Process (see SECTION 3.5):**
1. **Authentication** using Kerberos or constrained delegation.
2. **Iterative memory copy** — pages are transmitted to the destination host while the VM continues running.
3. **Bitmap tracking** of pages modified during transfer.
4. **Freeze-and-copy phase** — VM pauses for **sub-second duration** while final modified pages transfer.
5. **Resumption at destination** — VM continues execution on the target host.
6. **Network identity preservation** — destination VM uses the **same MAC and IP addresses**; only a gratuitous ARP refreshes switch tables.
7. **Source VM decommission** — the original VM is removed.

**Shared Nothing Live Migration (SNLM):**
- Allows live migration **without shared storage** between source and destination hosts.
- The source host transmits the **entire VM configuration and VHDX files** over the network to the destination using **SMB 3.0**.
- Once synchronized, the VM is moved.
- **Use Case:** Branch office disaster recovery, cost-effective HA without a SAN.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often claim that Live Migration requires shared storage. This is incorrect for **Shared Nothing Live Migration** — only **SMB 3.0** connectivity is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hyper-V (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Hyper-V is Microsoft's **Type-1 bare-metal hypervisor**, executing at **Ring -1**, supporting parent and child partitions.
- **Mandatory Hardware:** Processor must support **SLAT (EPT / RVI)**, **VT-x / AMD-V**, and **DEP**.
- **Parent Partition** runs the management OS, VSPs, and WMI provider; **Child Partitions** run guest OSes and use VSCs.
- **VMBus** is the in-memory logical bus connecting VSPs and VSCs; it enables low-latency paravirtualized I/O.
- **Virtual Switch (vSwitch):** Three modes — **External, Internal, Private**; remember the host can reach **Internal** but not **Private** VMs.
- **Storage formats:** **VHD** (older, max 2 TB) and **VHDX** (modern, max 64 TB, resilient).
- **Generation 1** = legacy BIOS + IDE; **Generation 2** = UEFI + SCSI + Secure Boot. Use Generation 2 by default.
- **Live Migration** uses SMB Multichannel; downtime is typically **< 1 second**. **Shared Nothing Live Migration** removes the shared storage requirement.
- **Hyper-V Replica** provides asynchronous DR with an RPO of 5 minutes (or 15 seconds in newer builds).
- **Failover Clustering** uses **CSV (Cluster Shared Volumes)** for concurrent VM access from multiple hosts.
- **Nested Virtualization** is supported only on Generation 2 VMs with specific processor requirements.
- **Microsoft Azure** is built on a customized version of Hyper-V — understanding Hyper-V is foundational for Azure IaaS.
- **Integration Services / Hyper-V Enlightenment** drivers must be installed in Linux guests for VMBus acceleration.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PIV-SNG":**
> **P**artitions (parent/child) → **I**ntegration Services → **V**MBus → **S**torage (VHDX) → **N**etworking (vSwitch) → **G**eneration (1 vs 2).
> Memorize this order to structure any descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azure VM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Azure VM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🖥️ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Azure Virtual Machine (VM):** An **on-demand, scalable computing resource** provisioned over Microsoft's Azure cloud platform that provides Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) by virtualizing physical server hardware into multiple isolated virtual environments. Each Azure VM runs its own operating system, offers dedicated vCPUs, memory, storage, and networking, and is billed on a per-second consumption basis.

From the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing (PEEET637)** perspective, Azure VMs are the canonical implementation of the **IaaS service model** defined by NIST SP 800-145, where the consumer can provision processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources to deploy arbitrary software (OS + applications).

### 1.2 Key Technical Components of an Azure VM

| Component | Description | Typical Range |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **vCPU** | Virtual Central Processing Unit | 1 – 128 vCPUs |
| **RAM** | Memory allocated to the VM | 0.5 GiB – 6 TiB |
| **Temp Storage** | Local SSD-backed scratch disk | 4 GiB – 32 TiB |
| **OS Disk** | Managed disk for OS | 30 GiB – 2 TiB |
| **Data Disks** | Persistent managed disks | Up to 64 data disks |
| **Accelerators** | GPUs / FPGAs for HPC / AI workloads | Up to 8 GPUs |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Hotel" Model 🏨

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of Azure as a massive **electronic hotel chain** spread across the globe.
> - **The Hotel Building** = An Azure **Region** (e.g., East US, Central India).
> - **The Individual Room** = A **Virtual Machine**.
> - **The Room Size** = The **VM Size** (B1s = small single-room, M-series = large suite, H-series = AI super-suite).
> - **The Hotel Reception (Front Desk)** = The **Azure Portal / Azure Resource Manager (ARM) API**.
> - **Your Check-in Time** = **Provisioning Time** (typically 1–5 minutes for an Azure VM).
> - **The Mini-bar Billing** = **Per-second billing** (you pay only for what you use, even by the second).
> - **The Key Card** = **SSH Key / RDP credentials / Managed Identity**.
> - **The Hotel Safe** = **Azure Disk Encryption** (your data at rest is encrypted).

Just as you can book a room for one night, an entire conference, or a permanent suite, you can spin up a VM for a few minutes to run a script, or run it 24/7 for production. Azure handles the **"building maintenance"** — power, cooling, physical security, hypervisor patching — while you focus on your **"stay"** — installing your OS, running your applications, and managing your data.

## 1.4 Classification of Azure VMs (Series Families)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus emphasizes understanding the **VM SKU selection criteria**. Azure classifies VMs into multiple series, each optimized for a distinct workload class:

- **B-Series (Burstable):** Economical VMs for low-traffic workloads; accumulate CPU credits when idle and burst above baseline. Examples: `B1s`, `B2ms`, `B20ms`.
- **D-Series (General Purpose):** Balanced CPU-to-memory ratio; default choice for most production apps. Examples: `D2s_v5`, `D8s_v5`.
- **E-Series (Memory Optimized):** High memory-to-CPU ratio for in-memory databases (SAP HANA, Redis, SQL). Examples: `E4s_v5`.
- **F-Series (Compute Optimized):** High CPU-to-memory ratio for batch processing, gaming, web servers. Examples: `F4s_v2`.
- **H-Series (High Performance Compute):** For HPC, scientific simulations, large-scale parallel jobs.
- **N-Series (GPU-enabled):** Equipped with NVIDIA GPUs for deep learning, video rendering, CAD. Examples: `NC6s_v3`, `ND96asr_v4`.
- **L-Series (Storage Optimized):** High disk throughput and IOPS for NoSQL databases, data warehousing.
- **M-Series (Massive Memory):** Up to **6 TiB of RAM** for SAP HANA, large in-memory analytics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Azure Virtual Machine

An Azure VM is **not** a bare-metal server. It is a logical construct layered on top of Microsoft's hypervisor (based on **Hyper-V** technology) running on physical hosts inside an Azure datacenter. The architecture stack, from top to bottom, is:

### Layered Architecture (Conceptual Stack)

- **Layer 6 — Application Workload:** Customer code (web app, ML model, database engine).
- **Layer 5 — Guest OS:** Windows Server, Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS, Debian, SUSE.
- **Layer 4 — Azure VM Agent (WaAppAgent.exe / walinuxagent):** Heartbeats health, provisions extensions.
- **Layer 3 — Virtual Hardware (Synthetic Devices):** Synthetic NIC, vDisk controller, vKeyboard.
- **Layer 2 — Azure Hyper-V Hypervisor:** Type-1 hypervisor providing isolation, live migration, and vNUMA.
- **Layer 1 — Physical Host Hardware:** Intel Xeon / AMD EPYC CPUs, DDR4/DDR5 RAM, NVMe SSDs, network fabric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" matters:** Each VM is hardware-isolated via the hypervisor. The hypervisor enforces **CPU time-slicing**, **memory partitioning using Extended Page Tables (EPT)**, and **I/O virtualization using SR-IOV** to ensure one noisy neighbor cannot degrade another tenant's performance.

## 2.2 Core Concept: The "Why" and "How" of Azure VM Internals

### 2.2.1 Provisioning Lifecycle (How a VM is created)
1. **Request Initiation:** User submits a deployment via Azure Portal, CLI (`az vm create`), PowerShell, ARM template, or Terraform.
2. **Resource Manager Validation:** ARM checks the subscription, region quotas, RBAC permissions.
3. **Resource Provider Coordination:** Compute Resource Provider (CRP) and Network Resource Provider (NRP) work in parallel.
4. **Host Allocation:** Azure's **placement engine** selects a physical host satisfying vCPU, memory, accelerated networking, and proximity-placement-group constraints.
5. **Hypervisor Instantiation:** VM is created on the selected host; vCPUs, RAM, and synthetic devices are mapped.
6. **OS Disk Attach:** VHD/managed disk is attached; the guest OS boots.
7. **Extensions and Agent Initialization:** Custom Script Extension, DSC, monitoring agents boot.
8. **Public IP & Load Balancer Binding (optional):** Network resources are attached.
9. **Health Probe:** The VM reports "Ready" to the Fabric Controller.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice
- **Why Managed Disks?** They eliminate storage-account limits, provide 99.999% availability, and support zone-redundant storage (ZRS).
- **Why Availability Sets / Zones?** To meet the **99.99% SLA** — VMs in the same Availability Set are placed on separate physical racks, power, and network.
- **Why Accelerated Networking?** SR-IOV bypasses the host's virtual switch, reducing latency from ~3 µs to <1 µs.
- **Why Spot VMs?** They use **spare Azure capacity at up to 90% discount**, with eviction when capacity is reclaimed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Azure VM Capacity & Cost Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU 2024 numerical / derivation questions** in Module 3.

### 2.3.1 CPU Credit Mathematics (B-Series Burstable VMs)

| Variable | Meaning | Unit |
|----------|---------|------|
| $B$ | Baseline CPU performance (per vCPU) | % |
| $C_b$ | CPU credits accumulated per hour (per vCPU) | credits |
| $C_b^{(ref)}$ | Reference credit rate | **100 credits / hour per vCPU** |
| $C_{max}$ | Maximum accumulated credits (per vCPU) | credits |
| $C_{used}$ | Credits consumed during burst | credits |

$$
C_b = \frac{B}{100} \times C_b^{(ref)}
$$

$$
C_{used} = \text{vCPUs} \times (P_{burst} - B) \times \frac{t}{60}
$$

Where $P_{burst}$ is the burst percentage and $t$ is the burst duration in minutes.

### 2.3.2 Azure VM Cost Calculation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $P_{hour}$ | Per-hour price of the VM SKU (USD) |
| $t$ | Total usage time |
| $C_{total}$ | Total cost (USD) |
| $D_{disk}$ | Disk cost (USD/month) |
| $T_{bandwidth}$ | Outbound bandwidth (GB) |

$$
C_{total} = P_{hour} \times \frac{t}{3600} + D_{disk} \times \frac{d}{30} + T_{bandwidth} \times P_{GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Azure bills **per second** with a minimum of **60 seconds** for Linux and **1 minute** for Windows. The first **100 GB/month of outbound bandwidth is free**.

### 2.3.3 Availability SLA Formula

$$
\text{Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N} (1 - A_i)
$$

Where $A_i$ is the availability of the $i$-th VM. For **2 VMs in Availability Zones**: $A = 1 - (1-0.9999)(1-0.9999) \approx 0.99999999$ (≈ **99.99%**).

### 2.3.4 VM Sizing Sanity Check

$$
\text{vCPU : RAM Ratio} = \frac{\text{vCPUs} \times 100}{\text{Memory (GiB)}}
$$

> Ideal ratios:
> - General purpose: **1:4** (e.g., D2s_v5 = 2 vCPU, 8 GiB).
> - Compute optimized: **1:2**.
> - Memory optimized: **1:8** (e.g., E2s_v5 = 2 vCPU, 16 GiB).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dev/Test Environments:** Engineers spin up B1s VMs costing ~$3–8/month for testing.
- **Enterprise Web Hosting:** Auto-Scaling VM Scale Sets behind Azure Load Balancer host millions of users.
- **AI/ML Training:** ND-series VMs with H100 GPUs accelerate deep-learning model training by 10x–100x over CPU.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Azure Site Recovery replicates on-prem VMs to Azure for failover.
- **Hybrid Cloud:** Azure Arc extends VM management to on-prem Kubernetes clusters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **exhaustive, KTU-board-style step-by-step numerical derivations** most commonly asked in Cloud Computing (PEEET637) Module-3 ESE questions. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — B-Series Burstable VM Credit Computation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> A B2s Azure VM has **2 vCPUs**, baseline performance of **20%**, and maximum credit accumulation of **120 credits per vCPU**. It has accumulated the full **120 credits per vCPU**. The VM is then required to burst at **100% CPU for 15 minutes**. Calculate: (a) credits consumed, (b) remaining credits, and (c) whether the burst is fully sustainable.

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

$$
\text{vCPUs} = 2, \quad B = 20\%, \quad P_{burst} = 100\%, \quad t = 15 \text{ min}, \quad C_{max}^{vCPU} = 120
$$

### Step 2 — Total Maximum Credits Available

$$
C_{max}^{total} = 2 \times 120 = 240 \text{ credits}
$$

### Step 3 — Credits Consumed During Burst

Using the formula from §2.3.1:

$$
C_{used} = \text{vCPUs} \times (P_{burst} - B) \times \frac{t}{60}
$$

$$
C_{used} = 2 \times (100 - 20) \times \frac{15}{60}
$$

$$
C_{used} = 2 \times 80 \times 0.25 = 40 \text{ credits}
$$

### Step 4 — Remaining Credits

$$
C_{remaining} = C_{max}^{total} - C_{used} = 240 - 40 = 200 \text{ credits}
$$

### Step 5 — Sustainability Check

Since $C_{used} = 40 \le C_{max}^{total} = 240$, the **burst is fully sustainable**. The VM will operate at 100% CPU for the full 15 minutes using only **16.7%** of its credit bank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This is why B-series VMs are **cost-effective** for bursty workloads like CI/CD build servers, small dev VMs, and low-traffic websites — the VM can sit idle (consuming minimal credits) and burst during peak compile/load times.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Azure VM Monthly Cost Estimation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> An organization provisions a **D4s_v5** VM (4 vCPUs, 16 GiB RAM) running Windows in **East US**. Pricing data:
> - VM hourly rate: **$0.192/hr**
> - Premium SSD P30 (1024 GiB): **$135.17/month**
> - Outbound data transfer: **50 GB** at $0.087/GB (beyond free 100 GB)
> - Duration: **730 hours** (full month)
>
> Calculate the total monthly cost.

### Step 1 — Compute Cost of VM Compute

$$
C_{compute} = P_{hour} \times t = 0.192 \times 730 = 140.16 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Cost of Managed Disk

$$
C_{disk} = D_{disk} \times \frac{d}{30} = 135.17 \times \frac{30}{30} = 135.17 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Cost of Bandwidth

$$
C_{bw} = T_{bandwidth} \times P_{GB} = 50 \times 0.087 = 4.35 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum All Cost Components

$$
C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{disk} + C_{bw}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 140.16 + 135.17 + 4.35 = 279.68 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 5 — Add Reserved Instance Savings (1-Year Reserved)

If the organization commits to a **1-year reserved instance**, the VM cost drops by ~40%:

$$
C_{compute}^{RI} = 140.16 \times 0.6 = 84.10 \text{ USD}
$$

$$
C_{total}^{RI} = 84.10 + 135.17 + 4.35 = 223.62 \text{ USD}
$$

**Savings:** $279.68 - 223.62 = 56.06$ **USD/month ≈ 20%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** KTU 2024 examiners often ask students to compare **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) vs Reserved Instances vs Spot VMs** as a 7-mark part-(a) question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SLA Availability of a Multi-Tier Architecture

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> A web application uses **2 VMs in an Availability Set** for the web tier and **1 standalone VM** for the database. Compute the overall SLA.

### Step 1 — Availability Set SLA

For 2 VMs in an Availability Set, Azure provides SLA of **99.95%** per VM (which is the per-VM SLA when at least 2 instances are deployed). For the **virtual machine cloud service** SLA with 2 instances:

$$
A_{web} = 1 - (1 - 0.9995) \times (1 - 0.9995) = 1 - 0.000005^2
$$

$$
A_{web} = 1 - 0.000000025 = 0.999999975 \approx 99.9975\%
$$

### Step 2 — Standalone Database VM SLA

A single standalone VM with Premium SSD has an SLA of **99.9%**.

$$
A_{db} = 0.999
$$

### Step 3 — Composite System SLA

The system is up only if **all tiers are up**:

$$
A_{system} = A_{web} \times A_{db}
$$

$$
A_{system} = 0.999975 \times 0.999 = 0.998975
$$

$$
A_{system} \approx 99.8975\% \approx 99.9\%
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Bottleneck

The single database VM is the **weakest link**. To raise the system SLA, the student should recommend:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** Deploy the database using **Azure SQL PaaS (99.99% SLA)** OR replicate the database VM into an **Availability Zone** to lift $A_{db}$ to 99.99%, raising the system SLA to **99.987%**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — VM Series Selection Decision Tree

| Workload Type | Recommended Series | vCPU:RAM Ratio | Example SKU | Approx. Cost (USD/hr) |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Small dev/test | B-series (burstable) | 1:4 | B2s | 0.0416 |
| General web | D-series | 1:4 | D4s_v5 | 0.192 |
| In-memory DB | E-series | 1:8 | E8s_v5 | 0.504 |
| Batch / API | F-series | 1:2 | F4s_v2 | 0.169 |
| SAP HANA | M-series | 1:32 | M64s | 12.39 |
| Deep Learning | N-series (GPU) | 1:7 | ND96asr_v4 | 27.20 |
| HPC simulations | H-series | 1:7 | H8 | 0.985 |
| Big Data / NoSQL | L-series | 1:8 | L8s_v2 | 0.545 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students often recommend a "bigger VM" to solve performance issues. The correct answer is usually **horizontal scaling** (more VMs in a VM Scale Set) or **right-sizing using Azure Advisor recommendations**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Azure VM High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph USER["USER LAYER"]
        userA[End User 1]
        userB[End User 2]
        userC[Administrator]
    end

    subgraph MGMT["MANAGEMENT PLANE"]
        portal[Azure Portal]
        cli[Azure CLI / PowerShell]
        arm[Azure Resource Manager]
        rbac[RBAC and Identity]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL PLANE - Azure Fabric"]
        fc[Fabric Controller]
        crp[Compute Resource Provider]
        nrp[Network Resource Provider]
        srp[Storage Resource Provider]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA PLANE - Physical Datacenter"]
        subgraph regionA["Azure Region - East US"]
            subgraph host1["Host Server 1 - Hyper-V Hypervisor"]
                vm1[VM 1 - Web Frontend]
                vm2[VM 2 - API Backend]
            end
            subgraph host2["Host Server 2 - Hyper-V Hypervisor"]
                vm3[VM 3 - Database]
                vm4[VM 4 - ML Inference]
            end
        end
        storageA[Premium SSD Managed Disks]
        netA[VNet and NSG and Load Balancer]
    end

    userA --> portal
    userB --> portal
    userC --> cli
    portal --> arm
    cli --> arm
    arm --> rbac
    arm --> fc
    fc --> crp
    fc --> nrp
    fc --> srp
    crp --> vm1
    crp --> vm2
    crp --> vm3
    crp --> vm4
    nrp --> netA
    srp --> storageA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Azure VM Provisioning Workflow (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RequestReceived : User submits ARM template
    RequestReceived --> Validating : ARM checks subscription
    Validating --> QuotaCheck : Region and quota limits
    QuotaCheck --> PlacementEngine : Find suitable host
    PlacementEngine --> HypervisorAlloc : Reserve physical resources
    HypervisorAlloc --> DiskAttach : Mount OS managed disk
    DiskAttach --> GuestBoot : Guest OS loads
    GuestBoot --> AgentInit : WaAppAgent heartbeats
    AgentInit --> ExtensionsRun : Custom Script and Monitoring
    ExtensionsRun --> HealthProbe : Fabric Controller pings
    HealthProbe --> Running : VM Ready
    Running --> Stopped : User stops deallocates
    Stopped --> Running : User starts
    Running --> Deleted : User deletes resource
    Deleted --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Azure VM SKU Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Choose Azure VM SKU] --> q1{Workload type?}
    q1 -->|Low traffic| q2{Bursty pattern?}
    q1 -->|Web or API| q3{Compute heavy?}
    q1 -->|Database| q4{Memory intensive?}
    q1 -->|AI or ML| q5{Need GPU?}
    q1 -->|HPC| q6{Need InfiniBand?}

    q2 -->|Yes| pickB[B-Series: B1s, B2ms]
    q2 -->|No| pickD[D-Series: D2s_v5]

    q3 -->|Yes| pickF[F-Series: F4s_v2]
    q3 -->|No| pickD

    q4 -->|Yes| pickE[E-Series: E4s_v5]
    q4 -->|No| pickD

    q5 -->|Yes| pickN[N-Series: NC6s_v3 or ND96asr_v4]
    q5 -->|No| pickD

    q6 -->|Yes| pickH[H-Series: H8 or HB120]
    q6 -->|No| pickN
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Azure VM Internals

> [!NOTE]
> The following block schematic maps the **functional interaction between a guest VM and the Azure fabric** — a frequent diagram-based question in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Guest Applications** | User code (.NET, Java, Python) |
| **Guest OS** | Windows Server / Linux distro kernel |
| **Azure VM Agent** | Reports health, runs extensions, processes metadata |
| **Hyper-V Synthetic Devices** | Virtual NIC, vDisk, vKeyboard, vFramebuffer |
| **VMBus** | High-speed inter-partition communication channel |
| **Hyper-V Hypervisor (Root Partition)** | Enforces isolation, schedules vCPUs |
| **Host OS / Root Partition** | Runs Azure fabric agent, manages host resources |
| **Azure Fabric Controller (FC)** | Master control plane service orchestrating hosts, networks, storage |
| **Hardware (Intel / AMD CPUs, NVMe, NICs)** | Physical server resources |

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                Guest Applications                   |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|              Guest OS (Win/Linux)                   |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|        Azure VM Agent (WaAppAgent/walinuxagent)     |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|       Hyper-V Synthetic Devices (vNIC, vDisk)       |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|               VMBus (Partition Channel)             |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|        Hyper-V Hypervisor (Type-1, Hardware-Assisted)|
+----------------------------------------------------+
|              Root Partition (Host OS)               |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|       Azure Fabric Controller (FC) - Global         |
+----------------------------------------------------+
|   Physical Hardware: Xeon/EPYC, DDR5, NVMe, NIC     |
+----------------------------------------------------+
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question tests a **single Course Outcome (CO)** and requires a concise but technically precise answer (~50–80 words).

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Define an Azure Virtual Machine. List any two of its characteristics that distinguish it from Azure App Service.**

**Model Answer:**
> An Azure VM is an **IaaS compute resource** that emulates a physical server, running a customer-selected OS in an isolated virtual environment on shared physical hardware managed by Microsoft's hypervisor.
>
> **Distinguishing characteristics:**
> 1. **Full OS Control:** The user has administrator/root access and can install any software, unlike App Service which restricts to a managed runtime.
> 2. **Bring-Your-Own-License (BYOL):** Customers can upload custom VHDs with pre-installed enterprise software, while App Service forbids OS-level modifications.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is the difference between a B-series and D-series Azure VM? When would you choose one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | B-Series (Burstable) | D-Series (General Purpose) |
> |--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
> | **CPU Pattern** | Accumulates credits when idle; bursts above baseline | Sustained, fixed CPU performance |
> | **Cost** | Very low ($3–8/month) | Moderate |
> | **Use Case** | Dev/test, low-traffic websites, CI/CD | Production web apps, mid-tier databases |
> | **Workload** | Spiky / intermittent | Continuous / steady-state |
>
> **Choice Rule:** Use **B-series** when CPU usage is < 50% on average with periodic spikes. Use **D-series** when the application requires consistent baseline CPU performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module-3 Part-B questions are **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an Azure Virtual Machine**, listing the role of the hypervisor, the Azure VM Agent, and the Fabric Controller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A financial-services startup deploys a **3-tier application** in Azure:
- **Web tier:** 2 VMs in an Availability Set
- **API tier:** 3 VMs in a VM Scale Set
- **Database tier:** 1 standalone VM
>
> Compute the **overall system SLA** and identify the bottleneck. Recommend **two architectural changes** to raise the SLA to at least 99.99%. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks)

**Diagram (Block Schematic):**

```
[Guest Apps] → [Guest OS] → [VM Agent] → [Synthetic Devices] → [VMBus] → [Hyper-V Hypervisor] → [Root Partition] → [Azure Fabric Controller] → [Physical Hardware]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Hypervisor (Hyper-V):** A **Type-1, hardware-assisted** hypervisor running directly on bare metal. It enforces **strict isolation** between tenants using **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** for memory and **SR-IOV** for I/O. The hypervisor schedules vCPUs, allocates RAM, and provides synthetic device abstractions to the guest.

2. **Azure VM Agent (`WaAppAgent.exe` / `walinuxagent`):** A daemon inside the guest OS that:
   - Reports **heartbeat health** to the Fabric Controller every 30 seconds.
   - Installs and runs **VM Extensions** (Custom Script, DSC, Monitoring).
   - Provisions the **metadata service (IMDS)** at `169.254.169.254` for the guest to query Azure.

3. **Azure Fabric Controller (FC):** The **central brain** of Azure's data plane. It:
   - Manages **placement, healing, and live migration** of VMs across hosts.
   - Maintains the **desired-state inventory** of all VMs in a region.
   - Triggers **automatic recovery** when a host fails (re-provisions the VM on a healthy host within minutes).

#### Part (b) — SLA Calculation (4 marks) + Recommendations (3 marks)

**Step 1 — Per-tier SLAs (Azure documentation):**

| Tier | Configuration | SLA |
|------|---------------|-----|
| Web | 2 VMs in Availability Set | 99.95% |
| API | 3 VMs in VM Scale Set | 99.99% |
| DB | 1 standalone VM (Premium SSD) | 99.9% |

**Step 2 — Composite System SLA:**

$$
A_{system} = A_{web} \times A_{api} \times A_{db} = 0.9995 \times 0.9999 \times 0.999
$$

$$
A_{system} = 0.9984 = 99.84\%
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Bottleneck:**

The **database tier (99.9%)** is the weakest link, dragging the system SLA to **99.84%**.

**Step 4 — Two Recommendations:**

1. **Replace the standalone DB VM with Azure SQL Managed Instance (PaaS):** Azure SQL offers a **99.99% SLA** built-in, eliminating the customer's need to manage the VM, patches, and backups. New system SLA: $0.9995 \times 0.9999 \times 0.9999 = 0.9993$ ≈ **99.93%**.

2. **Migrate the database VM to Availability Zones (ZRS) and add a read replica:** Deploying the DB across two zones with automatic failover raises the DB SLA to **99.99%**, and the overall system SLA to **99.97%**. Adding an Azure SQL PaaS as a managed alternative achieves the **≥99.99%** target.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Azure VM pricing models** — Pay-As-You-Go, Reserved Instances, and Spot VMs. Compare them on cost, commitment, and suitability for production workloads using a **suitable mathematical example**. **(7 marks)**
