**(b)** Your team is running a **machine learning training job** that must complete within 6 hours but is fault-tolerant and can be interrupted. Recommend a **VM SKU and pricing strategy** that minimizes cost, and justify your choice with a numerical cost comparison. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Pricing Models Comparison (4 marks table + 3 marks example)

| Model | Commitment | Discount | Suitable For | Eviction Risk |
|-------|-----------|----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)** | None | 0% (baseline) | Short-term, unpredictable workloads | None |
| **Reserved Instances (RI)** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **72%** (3-year) | Steady-state production | None |
| **Savings Plans** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **65%** | Flexible commitment across regions | None |
| **Spot VMs** | None | Up to **90%** | Fault-tolerant, batch, dev/test | High (30-sec notice) |

**Numerical Example (D2s_v5 in East US, 730 hours/month):**

- **PAYG:** $0.096/hr × 730 = **$70.08/month**
- **1-Year RI:** $0.096 × 0.62 = $0.0595/hr × 730 = **$43.46/month** (savings: $26.62)
- **3-Year RI:** $0.096 × 0.40 = $0.0384/hr × 730 = **$28.03/month** (savings: $42.05)
- **Spot:** $0.096 × 0.20 = $0.0192/hr × 730 = **$14.02/month** (savings: $56.06, with eviction risk)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** RIs lock in capacity and discount but require upfront commitment. Spot VMs are the cheapest but **not suitable for production** due to eviction when Azure needs capacity.

#### Part (b) — Cost-Optimized ML Training Strategy (4 marks analysis + 3 marks calculation)

**Recommendation:** Use an **ND-series Spot VM** with **Azure ML low-priority training job**.

**Justification:**

- ML training is **fault-tolerant** because Azure ML automatically checkpoints weights and resumes from the last checkpoint after eviction.
- Spot VMs offer up to **90% discount** vs PAYG.
- An ND96asr_v4 with H100 GPUs accelerates training by 10–100x, making the 6-hour SLA achievable.

**Numerical Cost Comparison (6-hour job):**

| Pricing Strategy | Hourly Rate | 6-Hour Cost |
|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| PAYG (ND96asr_v4) | $27.20 | **$163.20** |
| Spot (90% discount) | $2.72 | **$16.32** |

**Savings:** $163.20 - $16.32 = **$146.88 (90% saved)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Justification points (must appear in the answer):**
> 1. ML training is **embarrassingly parallel** and **restartable** — eviction causes a re-launch, not data loss, because Azure ML persists checkpoints in Azure Blob Storage.
> 2. Spot VMs use **Azure's spare capacity**, providing the same hardware as PAYG at a fraction of the cost.
> 3. The 30-second eviction notice allows Azure ML to checkpoint and gracefully shut down.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Azure VM Questions (Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Confusing IaaS with PaaS:** Writing about Azure App Service or Azure Functions when the question is about **Azure VMs**. Always clarify: *Azure VM = IaaS, App Service = PaaS, Functions = Serverless.*
> 2. **Ignoring the Hypervisor:** Many students describe Azure VMs as "virtual servers" but forget to mention the **Type-1 Hyper-V hypervisor** and its role in isolation. This costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Miscalculating SLAs:** A common error is using $A = 1 - A_1 \times A_2$ instead of the correct $A = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$. Memorize the **complementary probability formula**.
> 4. **Forgetting Disk and Bandwidth Costs:** In cost-calculation questions, students often compute only the VM hourly rate and forget **managed disks** and **outbound bandwidth**, losing **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Not Justifying SKU Selection:** Writing "use M-series" without explaining the vCPU:RAM ratio loses marks. Always tie the SKU choice to a **specific workload characteristic**.
> 6. **Mixing Up Region and Availability Zone:** A **Region** is a geographic area (e.g., East US), and an **Availability Zone** is a physically separate datacenter within a region. Do not interchange these terms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Azure VM (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Azure VM = IaaS compute resource virtualized on shared physical hardware via the **Hyper-V hypervisor**.
- ☐ **Service Model:** Pure **IaaS** — user manages OS, runtime, middleware, app, and data; Azure manages hardware, networking, and storage.
- ☐ **VM Series Families:** B (burstable), D (general), E (memory), F (compute), M (massive memory), N (GPU), H (HPC), L (storage).
- ☐ **Hypervisor:** Type-1, **Hyper-V** based, hardware-assisted, uses **EPT** and **SR-IOV** for isolation.
- ☐ **VM Agent:** `WaAppAgent` (Windows) / `walinuxagent` (Linux) — heartbeats health and runs extensions.
- ☐ **Metadata Service:** IMDS at `169.254.169.254` for retrieving instance metadata.
- ☐ **Storage:** **Managed Disks** (Standard HDD, Standard SSD, Premium SSD, Premium SSD v2, Ultra Disk) replace storage account blobs.
- ☐ **Availability Options:** **Availability Set** (fault/update domains, 99.95% SLA), **Availability Zone** (99.99% SLA), **VM Scale Set** (auto-scaling).
- ☐ **Networking:** Virtual Network (VNet), Subnets, NSG, Public IP, Private IP, Accelerated Networking (SR-IOV), Azure Load Balancer, Application Gateway.
- ☐ **Pricing Models:** PAYG (per-second), **1/3-Year Reserved Instances** (up to 72% off), **Spot VMs** (up to 90% off, evictable), **Savings Plans** (up to 65% off).
- ☐ **SLA Formula:** $A_{system} = \prod A_i$ for the composite architecture; $A_{tier} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$ for redundant tiers.
- ☐ **Cost Formula:** $C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{disk} + C_{bandwidth}$.
- ☐ **Burstable Credit Formula:** $C_b = \frac{B}{100} \times 100$ credits/hr per vCPU; $C_{max}^{vCPU} = 120$ for B2s.
- ☐ **First 100 GB outbound bandwidth per month is free**.
- ☐ **Minimum Billing:** Linux = 60 seconds; Windows = 1 minute.
- ☐ **Stop vs Deallocate:** "Stop" keeps the VM allocated (still billed for compute); "Stop (Deallocate)" releases hardware (no compute charge).
- ☐ **Hybrid:** **Azure Arc** extends Azure management to on-prem VMs and Kubernetes clusters.
- ☐ **Migration:** **Azure Migrate** assesses and migrates on-prem VMs to Azure with minimal downtime.

> 🎓 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "explain Azure VM," always cover: **Definition → Architecture (Hypervisor + Agent + FC) → SKU Families → Provisioning → Pricing → SLA**. This 6-point structure fetches full marks in any 7-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compute Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Cloud Compute Services?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Cloud Compute Services** are on-demand, scalable, and metered computing resources delivered over the internet, encompassing raw virtualized hardware (IaaS), managed runtime platforms (PaaS), containerized execution environments (CaaS), and event-driven function execution (FaaS / Serverless). They abstract the underlying physical servers, storage, and networking so that consumers can deploy and run applications without managing the physical infrastructure.

In simple terms, **Compute** in cloud computing is the *brain* and *muscle* of every cloud application. It refers to the processing power, memory, and runtime environment required to execute code, host operating systems, run containers, or trigger short-lived functions. Everything you do in the cloud — running a website, training an AI model, processing a transaction — consumes compute resources.

---

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Cloud Compute

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept: The Compute Service Stack**
> Every cloud compute offering can be classified into one of four abstraction layers. Each layer moves *further away* from the hardware and *closer* to the application code, trading control for convenience.

| Layer | Full Form | Who Manages Hardware? | Who Manages OS & Runtime? | User Manages | Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | Cloud Provider | User | Apps, Data, OS, Middleware | Renting an empty flat — bring your own furniture. |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | Apps & Data only | Renting a fully furnished flat with electricity. |
| **CaaS** | Container as a Service | Cloud Provider | Shared (User controls containers) | Apps, Containers, Configs | Renting a modular apartment unit with shared utilities. |
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | Individual functions & triggers | Pay-per-use electric scooter — no parking hassle. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine you want to serve food to customers:

- **IaaS (Raw Cloud Server)**: You rent the *kitchen building* — empty rooms, raw gas lines, and freezers. You buy your own ovens, hire chefs, and design the menu. *(Maximum control, maximum effort.)*
- **PaaS (Managed Platform)**: You rent a *kitchen that already has ovens, gas, and prep stations*. You just bring the recipe and the chef. *(Focus on the food, not the kitchen.)*
- **CaaS (Container Service)**: Your food is pre-cooked, sealed in standardized containers, and placed in a shared industrial kitchen. You just heat and serve. *(Portability, consistency.)*
- **FaaS (Serverless Functions)**: A vending machine. You press a button (trigger) and get a specific snack (output). The machine is built, stocked, and maintained by someone else. You only pay per snack. *(Zero infrastructure thinking.)*

---

## 1.4 Why Compute Services Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 3 of **PEEET637 (Cloud Computing)** specifically focuses on the *execution* layer of the cloud stack. KTU expects students to:
> 1. Differentiate between VM-based, container-based, and serverless compute.
> 2. Explain the working of major compute offerings (EC2, Lambda, ECS, AKS, EKS).
> 3. Discuss auto-scaling, load balancing, and pricing models.
> 4. Evaluate trade-offs between cold starts, latency, cost, and control.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Compute Paradigm

### 2.1.1 Virtual Machines (IaaS — The Foundation Layer)

- **What it is:** A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software emulation of a physical computer, created by a *hypervisor* that partitions a physical server into multiple isolated guest operating systems.
- **How it works (the 'Why' and 'How'):**
    - The **hypervisor** (Type-1: bare-metal like VMware ESXi; Type-2: hosted like VirtualBox) allocates CPU cores, RAM, disk, and network NICs to each VM.
    - Each VM runs its **own guest OS kernel** (e.g., Linux, Windows Server), making it a *heavyweight* abstraction.
    - Boot time: **30 seconds to several minutes** depending on OS payload.
- **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Used for legacy enterprise application migration ("lift-and-shift"), running heterogeneous OS environments on one physical host, and creating isolated dev/test sandboxes.

### 2.1.2 Containers (CaaS — The Lightweight Layer)

- **What it is:** A **container** packages an application with *only* its dependencies, libraries, and configuration into a standardized unit that runs consistently on any infrastructure.
- **How it works:**
    - Uses **OS-level virtualization** via the host kernel's `cgroups` (control groups) and `namespaces` features.
    - Shares the host OS kernel — *no guest OS* is booted. Image size is typically in **MB** vs GB for VMs.
    - Boot time: **milliseconds to a few seconds**.
    - **Docker** is the de-facto container image format; **Kubernetes (K8s)** is the de-facto orchestrator.
- **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Microservices architecture, CI/CD pipelines, hybrid cloud portability, edge computing deployments.

### 2.1.3 Serverless / FaaS (The Event-Driven Layer)

- **What it is:** **Function as a Service (FaaS)** lets you run individual functions in response to events without provisioning or managing servers. You supply code; the cloud runs and scales it.
- **How it works:**
    - Functions are triggered by **events** (HTTP request, queue message, file upload, schedule).
    - The provider automatically scales from **zero to thousands** of parallel invocations.
    - Billing is per **100 milliseconds** of execution time + number of invocations.
    - **Cold start** penalty: the first invocation after idle may take **100 ms to several seconds** as the runtime is initialized.
- **Real-World Engineering Utility:** REST API backends, image/video processing pipelines, IoT telemetry ingestion, chatbots, scheduled cron jobs.

### 2.1.4 Platform as a Service (PaaS)

- **What it is:** A managed environment where you deploy application code (without worrying about OS, runtime, or scaling rules).
- **Examples:** AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Azure App Service, Heroku.
- **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Web app hosting, API services, rapid prototyping, startups that want to ship code without DevOps overhead.

---

## 2.2 Auto-Scaling — The Elastic Heart of Compute

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition**
> **Auto-scaling** is the automatic adjustment of compute resource count (horizontal) or capacity (vertical) based on real-time demand metrics such as CPU utilization, request count, queue depth, or custom CloudWatch metrics.

### 2.2.1 Scaling Strategies

- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out / In):** Add or remove *instances* of the resource. Used for stateless workloads.
- **Vertical Scaling (Scale Up / Down):** Increase or decrease the *capacity* (CPU, RAM) of a single instance. Used for stateful or monolithic databases.
- **Predictive Scaling:** Uses machine learning on historical traffic to pre-provision resources before demand spikes.
- **Reactive Scaling:** Uses threshold-based rules (e.g., add 2 instances when CPU > 70% for 5 minutes).

### 2.2.2 Load Balancing

A **load balancer** distributes incoming traffic across multiple compute instances to ensure:
1. **High availability** — no single point of failure.
2. **Scalability** — traffic spread evenly.
3. **SSL termination** — centralizing encryption handling.

Types: **Application Load Balancer (ALB — Layer 7)**, **Network Load Balancer (NLB — Layer 4)**, **Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Compute Economics & Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Numerical Questions**
> Cloud compute problems in KTU often involve cost calculation, utilization, and scaling trade-offs. Master the equations below.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Compute Cost (On-Demand)** | $C = P_{hr} \times T_{hr} \times N$ | $P_{hr}$ = price per hour (USD), $T_{hr}$ = total hours, $N$ = number of instances | Straight-line billing |
| **Reserved Instance Savings** | $S_{\%} = \frac{P_{od} - P_{ri}}{P_{od}} \times 100$ | $P_{od}$ = on-demand price, $P_{ri}$ = reserved price | Typical 1-yr RI: **30–40%**; 3-yr RI: **60%+** |
| **Spot Price Ceiling** | $C_{max} = P_{spot} \times T$ | $P_{spot}$ = your maximum bid | Bid rejected if spot price > $P_{spot}$ |
| **Serverless Cost** | $C = (N_{inv} \times C_{inv}) + (T_{ms} \times C_{gb\text{-}s})$ | $N_{inv}$ = number of invocations, $C_{inv}$ = per-invoke cost, $T_{ms}$ = total duration in ms, $C_{gb\text{-}s}$ = GB-second cost | Free tier: 1M invocations/month (typical) |
| **Scaling Decision** | $N_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{total}}{L_{inst}} \right\rceil$ | $L_{total}$ = total load, $L_{inst}$ = per-instance load capacity | Round up to next integer |
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{\%} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | $T_{busy}$ = active time, $T_{total}$ = wall time | Auto-scale trigger threshold usually 60–75% |
| **Total Cost of Ownership** | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{opex,t} - S_{residual}$ | $C_{capex}$ = capital expenditure, $C_{opex}$ = operational cost per year, $S$ = salvage | Used in cloud migration decisions |
| **Storage IOPS Needed** | $IOPS = \frac{R_{req/s}}{L_{depth}}$ | $R$ = request rate, $L$ = queue depth (default 1) | Compute-bound vs I/O-bound sizing |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU problems where students are given traffic loads, instance costs, and asked to recommend the most cost-effective compute strategy (e.g., "compare on-demand vs reserved vs spot for a workload running 24×7 for 3 years").

---

## 2.4 Major Provider Compute Offerings — The KTUs Recognized Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyper-Scaler Compute Service Mapping (Memorize for ESE)**

| Provider | IaaS (VM) | CaaS (Container) | PaaS | FaaS (Serverless) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | EC2 | ECS, EKS, Fargate | Elastic Beanstalk | AWS Lambda |
| **Microsoft Azure** | Azure Virtual Machines | ACI, AKS | App Service | Azure Functions |
| **Google Cloud (GCP)** | Compute Engine | GKE, Cloud Run | App Engine | Cloud Functions |
| **IBM Cloud** | IBM Virtual Servers | IBM Cloud Kubernetes | IBM Code Engine | IBM Cloud Functions |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Instances for Given Load

This is a classic KTU numerical problem. Given a target load, instance capacity, and a Service Level Agreement (SLA), derive the minimum number of instances required.

### Problem Setup
- A web application must handle **$L_{total}$ = 50,000 requests per minute**.
- Each VM instance can handle **$L_{inst}$ = 8,000 requests per minute**.
- For high availability, a **redundancy factor** $f$ of 1.25 (i.e., 25% extra capacity) must be provisioned.
- An **Application Load Balancer** rounds up partial instances.

### Step 1 — Raw Instance Count
The naive count of instances needed is total load divided by per-instance capacity.

$$
N_{raw} = \frac{L_{total}}{L_{inst}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values
Insert the problem values into the formula.

$$
N_{raw} = \frac{50{,}000}{8{,}000}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Division
Perform the numerical evaluation.

$$
N_{raw} = 6.25 \text{ instances}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Ceiling Function for Whole Instances
Since you cannot deploy a fractional VM, round *up* to the next whole integer.

$$
N_{req} = \lceil N_{raw} \rceil = \lceil 6.25 \rceil = 7 \text{ instances}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Redundancy Factor
Multiply by the redundancy factor $f$ to meet the SLA.

$$
N_{final} = N_{req} \times f
$$

$$
N_{final} = 7 \times 1.25
$$

### Step 6 — Final Result with Rounding
Once again, round up to the next whole instance since partial VMs are not deployable.

$$
N_{final} = 8.75 \implies \lceil 8.75 \rceil = 9 \text{ instances}
$$

### Step 7 — Conclusion
**You need 9 VM instances** to serve 50,000 requests/minute with a 25% redundancy buffer, assuming each VM handles 8,000 req/min.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**
> The 2 extra instances beyond the bare minimum (7) are *insurance* against instance failure and traffic spikes. KTU expects this kind of "real-world engineering thinking" in full-mark answers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Cost Comparison — On-Demand vs Reserved vs Spot

This derivation teaches you to evaluate compute cost trade-offs — a common 7-mark KTU question.

### Given Data
- Workload: a batch-processing job running **8 hours/day, 5 days/week, 52 weeks/year**.
- On-demand price: **$P_{od}$ = $0.10/hour per vCPU**.
- 1-year reserved price: **$P_{ri}$ = $0.06/hour per vCPU**.
- Spot price (average): **$P_{spot}$ = $0.04/hour per vCPU**, but with a **20% interruption probability** that requires checkpointing overhead equivalent to **2 extra hours of compute per week**.
- Required vCPUs: **$N = 4$ vCPUs**.

### Step 1 — Total Operating Hours per Year
Compute the annual hours the workload actually runs.

$$
T_{hr} = 8 \times 5 \times 52 = 2{,}080 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 2 — On-Demand Annual Cost
Multiply the hours, hourly price, and number of vCPUs.

$$
C_{od} = P_{od} \times T_{hr} \times N
$$

$$
C_{od} = 0.10 \times 2{,}080 \times 4
$$

$$
C_{od} = \$832 \text{ per year}
$$

### Step 3 — Reserved Instance Annual Cost
Same formula, with reserved price.

$$
C_{ri} = 0.06 \times 2{,}080 \times 4 = \$499.20 \text{ per year}
$$

### Step 4 — Spot Instance Effective Cost
Add the interruption overhead (2 extra hours/week × 52 weeks = 104 extra hours).

$$
T_{spot} = 2{,}080 + 104 = 2{,}184 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
C_{spot} = 0.04 \times 2{,}184 \times 4 = \$349.44 \text{ per year}
$$

### Step 5 — Savings Comparison
Compute the percentage savings of Reserved vs On-Demand.

$$
S_{ri} = \frac{C_{od} - C_{ri}}{C_{od}} \times 100 = \frac{832 - 499.20}{832} \times 100
$$

$$
S_{ri} = \frac{332.80}{832} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

### Step 6 — Spot Savings Comparison

$$
S_{spot} = \frac{832 - 349.44}{832} \times 100 = 58\%
$$

### Step 7 — Final Recommendation
| Model | Annual Cost | Savings vs On-Demand | Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| On-Demand | $832.00 | 0% | None |
| Reserved (1-yr) | $499.20 | 40% | Low — capacity guaranteed |
| Spot (with overhead) | $349.44 | 58% | High — can be terminated anytime |

> **Conclusion:** For a *fault-tolerant* batch job, **Spot is cheapest**. For a *production* workload needing guaranteed uptime, **Reserved offers the best balance**. KTU looks for this kind of multi-criteria reasoning in Part B answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Compute Service

| Decision Criterion | Choose **VM (IaaS)** | Choose **Container (CaaS)** | Choose **Serverless (FaaS)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Workload Duration** | Long-running, persistent | Long-running, portable | Short bursts, event-driven |
| **Startup Latency Tolerance** | Minutes acceptable | Seconds acceptable | Milliseconds preferred (cold starts matter) |
| **Stateful Application?** | Yes (databases, legacy) | Possible with volumes | Not ideal (stateless preferred) |
| **Control over OS & Runtime** | Full | Moderate (container image) | None |
| **Cost Pattern** | Pay per second/minute/hour | Pay per second + cluster fee | Pay per invocation + GB-sec |
| **Scaling Granularity** | Manual or auto-scaling groups | Pod-level, K8s replicas | Per-invocation automatic |
| **Example Use Case** | SAP, Oracle DB, Windows apps | Microservices, REST APIs | Image resize, webhook handlers |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Cloud Compute Service Stack

This diagram illustrates how the four compute paradigms stack on top of physical hardware, showing the management boundary between user and provider.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph phyLayer ["PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER"]
        serverA["Server Rack A"]
        serverB["Server Rack B"]
        netSw["Network Switch"]
        storageA["SAN Storage Array"]
    end

    subgraph hypLayer ["HYPERVISOR LAYER"]
        hv1["VMware ESXi Type1"]
        hv2["KVM Type1"]
    end

    subgraph iaasLayer ["IAAS LAYER"]
        vm1["EC2 t3 medium VM Linux"]
        vm2["Azure VM Windows Server"]
        vm3["GCE n2 standard VM"]
    end

    subgraph caasLayer ["CAAS LAYER"]
        ctr1["Docker Container App A"]
        ctr2["Docker Container App B"]
        k8s["Kubernetes Pod Cluster"]
    end

    subgraph paasLayer ["PAAS LAYER"]
        paas1["AWS Elastic Beanstalk"]
        paas2["Azure App Service"]
        paas3["Google App Engine"]
    end

    subgraph faasLayer ["FAAS SERVERLESS LAYER"]
        fn1["AWS Lambda Function"]
        fn2["Azure Function Event Trigger"]
        fn3["Google Cloud Function HTTP"]
    end

    serverA --> hv1
    serverB --> hv2
    storageA --> hv1
    netSw --> hv1
    netSw --> hv2

    hv1 --> vm1
    hv1 --> vm2
    hv2 --> vm3

    vm1 --> k8s
    vm2 --> k8s
    k8s --> ctr1
    k8s --> ctr2

    vm1 --> paas1
    vm2 --> paas2
    vm3 --> paas3

    paas1 --> fn1
    paas2 --> fn2
    paas3 --> fn3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The bottom-up flow shows how raw hardware is virtualized into VMs, which then host either container clusters or PaaS runtimes, which finally expose individual functions to the user. Each upward step trades user control for provider convenience.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Auto-Scaling Decision Flow

This block diagram shows how an auto-scaling group reacts to changing load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userReq["Incoming User Request"] --> lb["Application Load Balancer"]
    lb --> asgCheck{"Check ASG Health Metrics"}

    asgCheck -->|"CPU greater than 70 percent"| scaleOut["Scale Out Action Add 2 Instances"]
    asgCheck -->|"CPU less than 30 percent"| scaleIn["Scale In Action Remove 2 Instances"]
    asgCheck -->|"CPU between 30 and 70 percent"| noAction["Maintain Current Count"]

    scaleOut --> provision["Provision New VM via API"]
    provision --> healthCheck["Wait for Health Check Pass"]
    healthCheck --> register["Register with Load Balancer"]
    register --> lb

    scaleIn --> deregister["Deregister from Load Balancer"]
    deregister --> terminate["Terminate Instance Gracefully"]

    noAction --> serveTraffic["Serve Traffic from Pool"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A continuous monitoring loop evaluates CPU utilization. When thresholds are crossed, scaling actions add or remove VMs from the load balancer's pool, ensuring elastic capacity.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Serverless Function Execution Lifecycle

This diagram captures the cold-start vs warm-start behavior critical to FaaS performance.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    event["Trigger Event HTTP API S3 Upload Queue Message"] --> idleCheck{"Is Function Warm in Memory?"}

    idleCheck -->|"Yes Warm Path"| invokeWarm["Invoke Function Directly"]
    invokeWarm --> execWarm["Execute Code in Existing Container"]
    execWarm --> responseWarm["Return Response to Caller"]
    responseWarm --> billWarm["Bill only execution time"]

    idleCheck -->|"No Cold Start Path"| initRuntime["Initialize Runtime Container"]
    initRuntime --> loadCode["Load Function Code Package"]
    loadCode --> initHandler["Execute Initialization Code"]
    initHandler --> execCold["Execute Handler Function"]
    execCold --> responseCold["Return Response to Caller"]
    responseCold --> keepWarm["Keep Container Warm for Next Invocation"]
    keepWarm --> billCold["Bill init time plus execution time"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The cold-start path is significantly longer because the runtime environment must be initialized. KTU questions often ask students to *minimize* cold starts through techniques like provisioned concurrency, keep-alive pings, or smaller deployment packages.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Serverless Computing and Explain its Key Characteristics.
**Model Answer:**
Serverless computing is a cloud execution model in which the cloud provider dynamically allocates compute resources on-demand, and the consumer is billed only for the actual execution time of their code, not for idle capacity. Key characteristics are:
1. **No server management** — the provider handles all infrastructure provisioning, patching, and scaling.
2. **Flexible scaling** — the application scales automatically from zero to thousands of concurrent invocations.
3. **Pay-per-use billing** — cost is calculated per request and per GB-second of execution time.
4. **Built-in high availability** — fault tolerance and availability are inherent in the platform.
5. **Event-driven invocation** — functions are triggered by events such as HTTP calls, database changes, or queue messages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention *pay-per-use* and *auto-scaling* — they are the two most frequently awarded keywords.

### Question 2: Differentiate Between Vertical Scaling and Horizontal Scaling with Examples.
**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Vertical Scaling (Scale Up) | Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Increasing CPU, RAM, or storage of a single machine | Adding more instances/machines to the resource pool |
| **Downtime** | Often requires reboot | No downtime — instances added live |
| **Limit** | Bounded by hardware ceiling | Theoretically unlimited |
| **Example** | Upgrading EC2 instance from t3.medium to t3.2xlarge | Increasing the desired count in an Auto Scaling Group from 3 to 8 |
| **Use Case** | Databases, legacy monolithic apps | Stateless web servers, microservices |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Compute Services — A Comparative Study

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of Virtual Machine-based IaaS. Describe the role of Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors in resource allocation.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of VM-based IaaS:**

The IaaS model is built on three core components stacked vertically:

1. **Physical Hardware Layer:** Comprises the actual servers, networking equipment, and storage arrays in the data center. Each physical host typically has multi-core CPUs (often 32–128 cores), hundreds of GBs of RAM, and terabytes of local or SAN-attached storage.

2. **Hypervisor Layer (Virtualization Layer):** Software that sits directly on the hardware (Type-1) or on a host OS (Type-2) and partitions the physical resources into multiple isolated virtual environments. The hypervisor creates and runs Virtual Machines (VMs), each with its own virtualized CPU (vCPU), memory, disk, and network interface.

3. **Virtual Machine Layer:** Each VM is a complete software emulation of a computer, running its own guest OS (Linux, Windows, etc.) and applications. The VM is encapsulated in a disk image file and is portable across hypervisors.

**Hypervisor Types and Their Role:**

- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor:** Runs directly on the host's hardware with no intervening OS. Examples include VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, and Citrix XenServer. They offer **near-native performance**, superior isolation, and are used in production cloud data centers (e.g., AWS uses a customized Xen/KVM hypervisor).
- **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor:** Runs as an application on top of a conventional OS. Examples include Oracle VirtualBox and VMware Workstation. They are primarily used for **development, testing, and learning** because of the overhead introduced by the host OS.

**Resource Allocation Mechanism:**
The hypervisor uses a **scheduling algorithm** to share physical CPU cycles (time-slicing), uses **memory ballooning** and **page sharing** to optimize RAM, and uses **thin-provisioned virtual disks** to allocate storage on demand.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Compare and contrast Containers and Virtual Machines. Discuss in which scenarios containers are preferred over VMs in cloud-native application development.

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OS Dependency** | Each VM runs its own guest OS kernel | Containers share the host OS kernel |
| **Size** | Gigabytes (GB) | Megabytes (MB) |
| **Boot Time** | 30 seconds to minutes | Milliseconds to a few seconds |
| **Isolation** | Strong (hardware-level via hypervisor) | Process-level (weaker, namespace-based) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (~95–98% of bare metal) | Native (no virtualization overhead) |
| **Resource Overhead** | High (each VM needs OS memory & CPU) | Low (only process footprint) |
| **Portability** | Less portable (hypervisor-dependent) | Highly portable across any Docker/K8s host |
| **Security** | Stronger boundary | Weaker boundary; needs additional tooling |

**Scenarios Where Containers Are Preferred:**

1. **Microservices Architecture:** Each microservice can be packaged as a lightweight container, allowing independent deployment and scaling. Example: 50 microservices cannot fit efficiently as 50 separate VMs.
2. **CI/CD Pipelines:** Containers start in seconds, enabling fast integration and deployment cycles.
3. **Hybrid Cloud Portability:** A container image runs identically on AWS, Azure, GCP, or on-premises, eliminating "works on my machine" issues.
4. **Dev/Test Parity:** Developers ship the exact same image to production, reducing environment drift.
5. **Auto-Scaling at High Frequency:** Spinning up thousands of containers per minute is economically feasible, while doing the same with VMs would be cost-prohibitive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse FaaS with PaaS.** PaaS keeps your app always running; FaaS is event-triggered and may scale to zero.
> - **Do NOT say containers are "more secure than VMs"** without context. Containers are *less* isolated by default.
> - **Always justify your choice** with a real-world scenario in Part B answers, not just a textbook definition.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Auto-Scaling, Load Balancing, and Cost Models

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain the working of an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) in AWS with a block diagram. Describe the various scaling policies used to trigger scale-out and scale-in actions.

**Model Answer:**

**Working of an Auto Scaling Group:**

An Auto Scaling Group is a logical collection of EC2 instances that are treated as a single unit for the purposes of scaling and management. The ASG continuously monitors the health and load of its instances and adjusts capacity according to defined policies.

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **CloudWatch Alarms** — Metrics (CPU, network, custom) are published to CloudWatch at 1-minute granularity.
2. **Alarm State Evaluation** — When a metric crosses a defined threshold (e.g., CPU > 70% for 5 minutes), the alarm state changes to ALARM.
3. **Scaling Policy Trigger** — The alarm invokes a scaling policy attached to the ASG.
4. **Capacity Adjustment** — The ASG either launches new instances (scale-out) or terminates existing ones (scale-in) via the EC2 API.
5. **Health Check & Registration** — New instances pass the ELB health check before receiving traffic; unhealthy instances are automatically replaced.

**Scaling Policies:**

1. **Target Tracking Scaling:** Maintains a specific metric value (e.g., keep average CPU at 50%). The ASG automatically adds/removes instances to stay near the target. Most common and recommended.
2. **Step Scaling:** Adds/removes a specific number of instances based on the *magnitude* of the alarm breach. E.g., CPU 70–80% → add 1 instance; CPU > 80% → add 3 instances.
3. **Simple Scaling:** Adds/removes a fixed number of instances, then waits for a *cooldown period* (typically 300 seconds) before evaluating again.
4. **Scheduled Scaling:** Pre-provisions capacity for known traffic patterns (e.g., add 5 instances every weekday at 9 AM).
5. **Predictive Scaling:** Uses machine learning to forecast traffic and pre-warm capacity before spikes.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A company runs a stateless web application on AWS EC2. The application experiences 3 peak hours per day (10 AM–1 PM) and runs at low load otherwise. Compare the cost-effectiveness of On-Demand, Reserved Instances, and Spot Instances for this workload. Recommend the best strategy with justification.

**Model Answer:**

**Workload Profile:**
- Peak hours: 3 hours/day × 7 days = **21 hours/week** of high demand.
- Off-peak: 24 − 3 = 21 hours/day of low demand.
- Assume the baseline is 2 instances always running, with 4 additional instances needed during peak (total 6 during peak, 2 during off-peak).

**Cost Calculation (per week, assuming $0.10/hr per instance):**

- **On-Demand Cost:**
    - Peak: 6 instances × 3 hr × 7 days = 126 instance-hours
    - Off-peak: 2 instances × 21 hr × 7 days = 294 instance-hours
    - Total = 420 instance-hours × $0.10 = **$42.00/week**

- **Reserved Instances (1-year, ~40% discount → $0.06/hr):**
    - Reserve 2 baseline instances full-time: 2 × 168 hr × $0.06 = $20.16
    - On-demand burst for 4 extra during peak: 4 × 3 × 7 = 84 hr × $0.10 = $8.40
    - Total = **$28.56/week** → 32% savings

- **Spot Instances (for peak capacity, ~60% discount → $0.04/hr):**
    - Spot for 4 extra during peak: 4 × 21 hr × $0.04 = $3.36
    - Reserved 2 baseline: $20.16
    - Total = **$23.52/week** → 44% savings (but with interruption risk)

**Recommendation:**

A **hybrid strategy** is best:
- **Reserved Instances** for the 2 baseline instances → guaranteed capacity.
- **Spot Instances** for the 4 burst-capacity instances during peak → massive cost savings, with interruption risk acceptable for stateless web app (auto-scales back via ASG).
- **On-Demand** as fallback if Spot capacity is unavailable.

This gives the best of all three worlds: cost savings, reliability, and elasticity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls**
> - **Numerical questions demand units and intermediate steps.** A bare final answer without showing the math loses 3–4 marks.
> - **Don't forget to mention cooldown periods** when discussing ASG — it's a favorite KTU follow-up.
> - **Always relate theory to a real provider** (AWS, Azure, GCP) — generic answers score lower.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3: Compute Services**

- ☐ **Four layers of compute**: IaaS (VMs), PaaS (managed platforms), CaaS (containers), FaaS (serverless functions). Remember the management responsibility map.
- ☐ **Hypervisor types**: Type-1 (bare-metal, production-grade) vs Type-2 (hosted, dev/test). Examples: ESXi, KVM, Xen, VirtualBox.
- ☐ **Container vs VM**: Containers share the host kernel (lightweight, fast); VMs have their own kernel (heavyweight, isolated). Boot time: ms vs minutes.
- ☐ **Kubernetes (K8s)**: The de-facto container orchestrator. Manages pods, deployments, services, and scaling. AWS EKS, Azure AKS, GCP GKE are managed K8s services.
- ☐ **Serverless / FaaS**: Pay per invocation + GB-second. Auto-scales from zero. Suffers from cold starts. AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.
- ☐ **Auto-scaling policies**: Target tracking, step scaling, simple scaling, scheduled scaling, predictive scaling. Each has a specific use case.
- ☐ **Load balancers**: ALB (Layer 7, HTTP), NLB (Layer 4, TCP), GWLB (third-party appliances). They distribute traffic and provide health checks.
- ☐ **Pricing models**: On-Demand (flexible, expensive), Reserved (1/3-yr commitment, 30–60% cheaper), Spot (up to 90% off, interruptible), Savings Plans (commit to $/hr).
- ☐ **Cold start problem**: First invocation after idle is slow. Mitigations: provisioned concurrency, smaller packages, keep-alive pings, initialized runtimes.
- ☐ **Key formulas to memorize**:
    - $N_{req} = \lceil L_{total} / L_{inst} \rceil$ (instance count)
    - $C = P_{hr} \times T \times N$ (compute cost)
    - $S_{\%} = (C_{od} - C_{ri}) / C_{od} \times 100$ (savings %)
- ☐ **Provider mapping** (must memorize): AWS → EC2/Lambda/ECS/EKS; Azure → VM/AKS/Functions; GCP → Compute Engine/GKE/Cloud Functions.
- ☐ **Stateless vs Stateful**: Stateless apps (web frontends) are ideal for horizontal scaling. Stateful apps (databases) prefer vertical scaling or specialized services (RDS, DynamoDB).
- ☐ **Common ESE trap questions**: "Is serverless really server-less?" (No, servers exist; you just don't manage them.) "Can containers run Windows apps?" (Yes, with Windows containers, but Linux is more common.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage Services (STaaS – Storage as a Service)** is a cloud computing service model in which data is stored, managed, backed up, and made remotely accessible to users over a public or private network (typically the Internet) on virtualized infrastructure maintained and managed by a Cloud Service Provider (CSP). The user consumes storage capacity, performance tiers, and data services on a **pay-per-use** or subscription basis, abstracting away the underlying physical hardware complexity.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET637 – Cloud Computing, Module 3)** syllabus, *Storage Services* encompasses the architectural patterns, abstraction layers, and operational mechanisms that allow applications to persist, retrieve, replicate, and protect data in a distributed, elastic, and fault-tolerant manner.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Infinite Warehouse"**

Imagine you run a global e-commerce company. Buying your own warehouses in every city is expensive, slow to scale, and a maintenance nightmare. Instead, you rent shelf space from a logistics giant (the Cloud Provider). You call a number or use an app, and immediately a new shelf appears, a barcode is issued, and your package is delivered to the nearest customer hub.

- **You** = the application / developer.
- **The Logistics Giant** = AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud.
- **The Shelves** = virtual disks, buckets, or file shares.
- **The Barcode System** = the storage API (REST/S3, etc.).
- **The Warehouses in Many Cities** = the globally distributed data centers.

You only pay for the **shelf space** you actually occupy (**per GB-month**) and the number of times you open the door to put in or take out a box (**per I/O request**). If your inventory doubles before a festive sale, you simply rent more shelves — no construction, no procurement.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Storage Abstractions

| Abstraction | What it Stores | Access Method | Typical Latency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Object Storage** | Unstructured blobs (images, videos, backups, logs) | HTTP/REST API (PUT, GET, DELETE) | Tens of ms | Static assets, big data, archives |
| **Block Storage** | Raw volumes of fixed-size blocks | Mounted as a virtual disk (iSCSI, NVMe) | Sub-millisecond | Databases, OS boot drives |
| **File Storage** | Hierarchical folders and files | NFS / SMB mount | Few ms | Shared file systems, content mgmt |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 3 specifically stresses that modern cloud platforms expose storage through *abstractions* — students must understand the *data model*, *access protocol*, and *consistency guarantees* of each abstraction.

## 1.4 Key Cloud Storage Industry Metrics to Remember

- **Durability:** `99.999999999%` (eleven 9s) for AWS S3 Standard — meaning statistically you lose 1 object every **10,000 years** for every 10 million objects.
- **Availability SLA:** typically `99.9%` to `99.99%` depending on tier.
- **Storage Unit Base:** `1 GiB = 2^{30}` bytes; pricing is in **GB-month**.
- **IOPS:** Input/Output Operations Per Second — the **throughput** of block storage.
- **Throughput:** measured in **MB/s** or **MiB/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Cloud Storage Services

Cloud storage is built as a **layered, distributed system**. The KTU 2024 syllabus asks students to identify each layer:

### Layered Stack
1. **Access Layer** — REST APIs (HTTP/S), S3 SDK, CLI, web consoles.
2. **Interface Layer** — Authentication (IAM, OAuth), Authorization (Bucket Policies, ACLs), Request Signing.
3. **Metadata Layer** — Catalog of objects, locations, replicas, version IDs.
4. **Storage Abstraction Layer** — Object/File/Block virtualization.
5. **Data Plane / Storage Nodes** — Physical disks (HDDs, SSDs) arranged in **Erasure-Coded** or **Replicated** clusters.
6. **Geographic Layer** — Regions, Availability Zones (AZs), Edge Locations.

## 2.2 Why Cloud Storage? — The Engineering Drivers

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Motivations**
> - **Elasticity:** Capacity scales horizontally with workload.
> - **Durability via Redundancy:** Data is copied across multiple failure domains.
> - **Cost Efficiency:** No upfront CapEx; OpEx model.
> - **Global Accessibility:** APIs work from anywhere with low latency via **CDNs**.
> - **Managed Operations:** Patching, backups, snapshots, encryption-at-rest are handled by the CSP.

## 2.3 Replication vs. Erasure Coding — The Trade-off

| Scheme | Formula | Storage Overhead | Failure Tolerance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **3-way Replication** | `Data = D1, D2, D3` | **3x** | 2 node failures | Hot data, low latency |
| **Erasure Coding (k, m)** | Split D into `k` data + `m` parity | `(k+m)/k` | Any `m` failures | Cold/archival data |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Replication Factor Storage** | $S_{total} = N_r \times S_{object}$ | GB | Total physical storage with $N_r$ replicas |
| 2 | **Erasure Coding Storage** | $S_{total} = \dfrac{k+m}{k} \times S_{object}$ | GB | Storage efficiency of EC |
| 3 | **Storage Cost** | $C = S_{GB} \times P_{GB\text{-}month} \times T_{months}$ | Currency | Pay-as-you-go storage cost |
| 4 | **Egress Cost** | $C_{egress} = D_{out} \times P_{per\,GB}$ | Currency | Cost of data leaving the cloud |
| 5 | **Effective Durability (Replicated)** | $D = 1 - (p_f)^{N_r}$ | Probability | Probability all replicas fail |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\Delta t}$ | MB/s | Bandwidth between user and storage |
| 7 | **IOPS from Latency** | $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{L_{avg}}$ | ops/s | For single-thread sequential I/O |
| 8 | **Mean Time To Data Loss (MTTDL)** | $MTTDL = \dfrac{MTBF}{N^2 \times AFR}$ | Hours | With $N$ disks and AFR (Annual Failure Rate) |
| 9 | **Storage Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{useful}}{S_{allocated}} \times 100\%$ | % | Ratio of logical to physical used storage |
| 10 | **CDN Cache Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}}$ | % | Fraction of requests served from edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Throughout this document, the vertical bar in absolute values and probability notation is written as `\vert` or `\mid` inside math mode to prevent it from breaking markdown tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Lakes for AI/ML:** Object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) forms the substrate for **Lakehouse architectures** (Databricks, Snowflake).
- **Disaster Recovery as a Service (DRaaS):** Block storage snapshots replicated cross-region.
- **Streaming & IoT Ingestion:** High-throughput object buckets (e.g., AWS S3 with **PUT request rates of 3,500 req/s/prefix**).
- **Regulatory Archives:** Cold tiers (Glacier, Azure Archive) for **WORM** (Write Once Read Many) compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Durability of an Object with `N_r` Replicas

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A cloud object store places `N_r = 3` replicas of an object across three independent failure domains. Each domain has an independent annual failure probability `p_f = 0.001` (i.e., 0.1% per year). Compute the **annual durability** of the object.

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Probability that a single replica survives a year.**
Since each domain is independent, the probability a given replica is intact at year-end is:

$$
P_{survive} = 1 - p_f
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that all `N_r` replicas fail simultaneously (data loss).**
Because replicas are placed in **independent** domains, their failures are independent events. The probability that **every** replica is lost in the same year is:

$$
P_{loss} = (p_f)^{N_r}
$$

**Step 3 — Durability = Probability that the object is NOT lost.**

$$
D = 1 - (p_f)^{N_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the given numbers.**

$$
D = 1 - (0.001)^{3}
$$

$$
D = 1 - 1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
D = 1 - 0.000000001
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 0.999999999}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation in 'Nines'.**
This is famously called **"Eleven Nines"** of durability — the design target of AWS S3 Standard. With 10 million objects, the expected loss is roughly:

$$
E[loss] = 10^{7} \times 10^{-9} = 0.01 \text{ objects / year}
$$

Which translates to **1 object every 100 years** for that scale — an order of magnitude better than the marketing slogan suggests, but still the gold standard.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Erasure Coding Storage Overhead

**Problem Setup:**
A photo storage service uses Reed–Solomon erasure coding with `k = 10` data shards and `m = 4` parity shards. Compute the storage efficiency and overhead for a **1 TiB** logical dataset.

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Total number of shards.**

$$
N_{shards} = k + m = 10 + 4 = 14
$$

**Step 2 — Storage expansion factor.**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{k+m}{k} = \dfrac{14}{10} = 1.4
$$

**Step 3 — Total physical storage for 1 TiB logical data.**

$$
S_{phys} = 1 \text{ TiB} \times 1.4 = 1.4 \text{ TiB}
$$

**Step 4 — Storage efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{1.4} = 0.7142 \approx 71.42\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with 3-way replication.**
3-way replication of the same 1 TiB would require `3 × 1 = 3 TiB`, so erasure coding saves:

$$
S_{saved} = 3 - 1.4 = 1.6 \text{ TiB}
$$

A **53.3%** reduction in physical storage footprint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Storage Cost Calculation (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Setup:**
A startup stores `S = 5,000 GB` of application logs in S3 Standard for `T = 6` months. The pricing is:
- Storage: `$0.023` per GB-month
- PUT requests: `$0.005` per 1,000 requests
- GET requests: `$0.0004` per 1,000 requests

If the application makes `N_{put} = 2,000,000` PUTs and `N_{get} = 8,000,000` GETs, compute the **total bill**.

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Storage cost.**

$$
C_{storage} = S \times P_{GB\text{-}month} \times T
$$

$$
C_{storage} = 5{,}000 \times 0.023 \times 6
$$

$$
C_{storage} = 115{,}000 \times 0.023
$$

$$
C_{storage} = \$2{,}645.00
$$

**Step 2 — PUT request cost.**

$$
C_{put} = \dfrac{N_{put}}{1{,}000} \times 0.005
$$

$$
C_{put} = 2{,}000 \times 0.005 = \$10.00
$$

**Step 3 — GET request cost.**

$$
C_{get} = \dfrac{N_{get}}{1{,}000} \times 0.0004
$$

$$
C_{get} = 8{,}000 \times 0.0004 = \$3.20
$$

**Step 4 — Total cost.**

$$
C_{total} = C_{storage} + C_{put} + C_{get}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 2{,}645.00 + 10.00 + 3.20
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = \$2{,}658.20}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering takeaway.**
Storage cost dominates (99.5%) over API request costs in this workload. Optimizations should focus on **lifecycle policies** (move to S3-IA or Glacier) and **data compression**, not on reducing request counts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CDN Cache Hit Ratio and Origin Offload

**Problem Setup:**
A video streaming site uses a CDN. In one hour, the CDN receives `N_{req} = 10,000,000` requests. The cache hit ratio is `H = 92%`. The origin (cloud storage) charges `$0.05` per 10,000 egress requests. Compute origin savings compared to a no-CDN scenario.

### Step-by-step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Requests served by CDN edge cache.**

$$
N_{hit} = H \times N_{req} = 0.92 \times 10^{7} = 9{,}200{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Requests that go to origin.**

$$
N_{miss} = (1 - H) \times N_{req} = 0.08 \times 10^{7} = 800{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Cost without CDN (all 10M from origin).**

$$
C_{noCDN} = \dfrac{10^{7}}{10{,}000} \times 0.05 = 1{,}000 \times 0.05 = \$50.00
$$

**Step 4 — Cost with CDN (only misses reach origin).**

$$
C_{CDN} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{5}}{10{,}000} \times 0.05 = 80 \times 0.05 = \$4.00
$$

**Step 5 — Savings.**

$$
\Delta C = 50.00 - 4.00 = \$46.00
$$

The CDN reduces origin egress costs by **92%** — exactly equal to the hit ratio, a fundamental property of CDN economics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud Storage Service Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client App A] -->|REST API| apiGW
    clientB[Client App B] -->|SDK| apiGW
    clientC[Mobile App] -->|HTTPS| apiGW

    apiGW[API Gateway and Auth Layer IAM and OAuth] --> meta

    subgraph ControlPlane[Control Plane]
        meta[Metadata Service Object Index and Versioning]
        policy[Lifecycle and Policy Engine]
        acl[ACL and Bucket Policy]
    end

    meta --> placement

    subgraph DataPlane[Data Plane Distributed Storage Cluster]
        placement[Placement Engine Replication or EC]
        nodeA[Storage Node AZ1 HDD plus SSD]
        nodeB[Storage Node AZ2 HDD plus SSD]
        nodeC[Storage Node AZ3 HDD plus SSD]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer[Edge and CDN Layer]
        edge1[Edge POP North America]
        edge2[Edge POP Europe]
        edge3[Edge POP Asia]
    end

    clientA -.->|Cache Hit| edge1
    clientB -.->|Cache Miss| edge2
    clientC -.->|Cache Hit| edge3

    edge1 -.->|Origin Fetch| placement
    edge2 -.->|Origin Fetch| placement
    edge3 -.->|Origin Fetch| placement

    policy -.->|Transition Objects| placement
    acl -.->|Enforce Access| apiGW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *Control Plane* decides **where** and **how** data is stored (metadata, policies, ACLs). The *Data Plane* is the actual physical storage cluster spread across failure domains (AZs). The *Edge Layer* sits in front to reduce latency for end users. A request from a client first hits the Edge/CDN; if not cached, it traverses the API Gateway, is authorized via IAM, indexed via metadata, and finally lands on a storage node.

## 4.2 Storage Class Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S3_Standard : PUT object
    S3_Standard : Hot Tier Frequent access
    S3_IA : Infrequent Access Lower cost
    S3_Glacier : Cold Archive Lowest cost
    S3_Delete : Object removed after retention

    S3_Standard --> S3_IA : Lifecycle rule 30 days no access
    S3_IA --> S3_Glacier : Lifecycle rule 90 days no access
    S3_Glacier --> S3_Delete : Lifecycle rule expiry
    S3_Standard --> S3_Delete : Manual DELETE or policy
    S3_IA --> S3_Standard : READ request (transition back)
    S3_Glacier --> S3_IA : Restore initiated
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In KTU exams, you may be asked to draw or describe a **storage tiering state diagram**. Always include the **transitions** (lifecycle rules) and the **conditions** (e.g., 30 days no access) — these are the marks-bearing details.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Distributed Object Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ingest[Ingestion Layer HTTP PUT] --> chunker[Chunker Splits into 64MB chunks]
    chunker --> encoder{Erasure Encoder}
    encoder -->|Data shards k=6| raft1[Raft Group 1 SSDs]
    encoder -->|Parity shards m=3| raft2[Raft Group 2 HDDs]
    raft1 --> journal[Write Ahead Log WAL]
    raft2 --> journal
    journal --> scrubber[Background Scrubber Checksum Verify]
    scrubber --> index[Distributed Hash Index DHT]
    index -->|Lookup on GET| reader[Reader Reassembles Chunks]
    reader --> egress[Egress Layer HTTP GET]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage in cloud environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Object Storage | Block Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Data unit | Whole object/blob (key–value) | Fixed-size blocks (e.g., 16 KiB) |
| Access | HTTP/REST API | Mounted as raw disk (iSCSI, NVMe) |
| Metadata | Rich, custom tags | Limited (LBA) |
| Scalability | Virtually unlimited | Limited per volume |
| Latency | Tens of ms | Sub-ms |
| Use case | Images, backups, logs | Databases, OS disks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only *one* difference. KTU valuation expects at least **three** distinct points (data model, access pattern, use case) for full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is a Content Delivery Network (CDN) and how does it improve cloud storage performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **CDN** is a geographically distributed network of proxy servers (edge locations) that cache copies of static and dynamic content close to end users. It improves cloud storage performance in three ways:
1. **Reduced Latency** — content is served from the nearest edge, not the origin data center.
2. **Reduced Origin Load** — cache hits prevent repeated fetches from cloud storage, lowering egress costs.
3. **Higher Availability** — if the origin is down, the CDN can still serve cached copies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of cloud storage services with suitable examples. Compare their access methods, performance, and typical use cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A healthcare application stores **10 TB** of patient records. The cloud provider offers:
- Hot tier: `$0.021` per GB-month
- Cold tier: `$0.004` per GB-month
- Transition cost: `$0.01` per GB

If a lifecycle policy moves data to the cold tier after **60 days of no access** and **80%** of data becomes cold, compute the **monthly cost saving** versus keeping everything in the hot tier. Assume the data is constant in size for simplicity.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Cloud storage is delivered in three primary abstractions:

**1. Object Storage (7 marks subdivided):**
Stores data as **opaque objects** identified by a unique key inside a flat namespace called a **bucket** (S3) or **container** (Azure Blob). Each object bundles the data, a globally unique ID, and rich **custom metadata**.
- *Access:* HTTP/REST (PUT, GET, HEAD, DELETE).
- *Performance:* Tens of ms; throughput scales with parallelism.
- *Examples:* AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage.
- *Use Cases:* Static websites, media, backups, data lakes, log archives.

**2. Block Storage:**
Exposes raw **volumes** divided into equally sized blocks addressed by Logical Block Addresses (LBAs). Volumes are attached to a single VM at a time (typically) and appear as a physical disk.
- *Access:* iSCSI, NVMe-oF, or direct hypervisor attachment.
- *Performance:* Sub-millisecond latency; tens of thousands of **IOPS**.
- *Examples:* AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks, Google Persistent Disk.
- *Use Cases:* Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle), boot disks, transactional workloads.

**3. File Storage:**
Provides a **hierarchical file system** (folders and files) mountable across multiple VMs over standard network protocols.
- *Access:* NFS v4.1, SMB 3.0.
- *Performance:* Few ms; medium IOPS; high throughput.
- *Examples:* AWS EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore.
- *Use Cases:* Lift-and-shift legacy apps, content management, shared home directories, HPC scratch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus mapping (CO3 – Module 3):** This answer addresses the CO *"Compare and contrast the various cloud storage models and their suitability for different workloads."*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical Cost Optimization

**Step 1 — Total dataset size in GB.**

$$
S = 10 \text{ TB} = 10 \times 1{,}024 \text{ GB} = 10{,}240 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Baseline cost (all hot).**

$$
C_{hot} = 10{,}240 \times 0.021 = \$215.04 / \text{month}
$$

**Step 3 — Cold-eligible data volume.**

$$
S_{cold} = 0.80 \times 10{,}240 = 8{,}192 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Hot-remaining data.**

$$
S_{hot} = 0.20 \times 10{,}240 = 2{,}048 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Optimized hot-tier cost.**

$$
C'_{hot} = 2{,}048 \times 0.021 = \$43.008
$$

**Step 6 — Optimized cold-tier cost.**

$$
C_{cold} = 8{,}192 \times 0.004 = \$32.768
$$

**Step 7 — One-time transition cost for the 80% chunk.**

$$
C_{trans} = 8{,}192 \times 0.01 = \$81.92
$$

> *For a steady-state month (after the first transition), the one-time cost is amortized to zero, but for the first month we include it.*

**Step 8 — Total optimized cost (first month).**

$$
C_{total} = C'_{hot} + C_{cold} + C_{trans} = 43.008 + 32.768 + 81.92 = \$157.696
$$

**Step 9 — Total optimized cost (subsequent steady-state months).**

$$
C_{steady} = 43.008 + 32.768 = \$75.776
$$

**Step 10 — Savings (steady state).**

$$
\Delta C = 215.04 - 75.776 = \$139.264 / \text{month}
$$

$$
\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{139.264}{215.04} \times 100\% \approx 64.76\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The lifecycle policy saves approximately **\$139.26 per month (≈ 64.8% reduction)** in steady state. **Total final monthly cost = \$75.78** after the policy is in effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to subtract the **hot-remaining 20%** and end up with a saving figure that is **too high**. Also, always convert **TB → GB** correctly (using `1 TB = 1024 GB` in cloud billing unless explicitly stated as decimal TB).

---

### Question B — Alternative 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical cloud object storage service. Identify the roles of the **control plane** and **data plane**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An object is stored with `k = 6` data shards and `m = 3` parity shards using Reed–Solomon erasure coding. Calculate:
1. The storage overhead percentage.
2. The maximum number of concurrent shard failures the system can tolerate.
3. The minimum number of shards required to reconstruct the original object.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Diagram (textual, since exam is pen-and-paper):**

> **Cloud Object Storage Architecture:**
> 1. **Client Layer** — Web/mobile apps, CLI tools, SDKs.
> 2. **API Gateway** — Receives HTTP requests; routes to metadata or data services; enforces authentication via IAM, OAuth, or signature (e.g., AWS Signature V4).
> 3. **Control Plane** — *Stateless* services that decide **where** data goes:
>    - **Metadata Service** — maintains object index, version IDs, ACLs, ETag.
>    - **Placement Engine** — chooses storage nodes (hash ring).
>    - **Lifecycle / Policy Engine** — applies transition and expiration rules.
> 4. **Data Plane** — *Stateful* storage nodes that actually hold bytes; organized into failure domains (AZs); use replication or erasure coding.
> 5. **Monitoring Plane** — collects metrics, triggers self-healing.

**Role of Control Plane vs. Data Plane:**

| Plane | Stateful? | Handles | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Plane** | Stateless (mostly) | Decisions, metadata, policies, auth | Metadata DB, IAM, Scheduler |
| **Data Plane** | Stateful | Actual byte storage and retrieval | Storage nodes with disks |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Storage Overhead Percentage.**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{k+m}{k} = \dfrac{6+3}{6} = 1.5
$$

$$
\text{Overhead \%} = (\rho - 1) \times 100\% = 0.5 \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**2. Maximum Concurrent Shard Failures Tolerated.**

$$
F_{max} = m = 3 \text{ shards}
$$

**3. Minimum Shards to Reconstruct the Object.**

$$
R_{min} = k = 6 \text{ shards (any 6 of the 9 are sufficient)}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Overhead: **50%**
- Tolerable failures: **3**
- Reconstruction requires: **any 6 shards**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students confuse `m` (parity) with `k` (data). Remember the mnemonic: **"k for Keep (data you need), m for Missing (parity)."** Reconstruction requires **k** shards, but tolerates up to **m** losses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Storage Services**

- **Three Storage Abstractions:** Object (REST/flat), Block (LBA/raw disk), File (NFS/SMB/hierarchical).
- **STaaS (Storage as a Service):** Pay-per-GB-month, pay-per-request, pay-per-egress.
- **Durability Eleven 9s:** Achieved via cross-AZ replication; design target of S3 Standard.
- **Replication:** Simple but **3x** storage cost; use for hot data with low latency needs.
- **Erasure Coding:** Storage efficient `(k+m)/k`; higher rebuild cost; use for warm/cold data.
- **Storage Tiers:** Hot (Standard) → Infrequent Access (IA) → Cold (Glacier) → Archive. Use **lifecycle policies** based on access frequency.
- **CDN:** Edge caching reduces latency and origin load; cache hit ratio drives cost savings.
- **Block Storage Performance:** Measured in **IOPS** and **throughput (MB/s)**; sub-ms latency.
- **Object Storage Limits:** Single object up to **5 TiB** (S3), PUT rate ~3,500 req/s per prefix.
- **Encryption:** At-rest (AES-256), in-transit (TLS 1.2+); managed keys (KMS) vs. customer-managed (CMK).
- **Replication Strategies:** Synchronous (strong consistency, higher latency) vs. Asynchronous (eventual consistency, lower latency).
- **Consistency Model:** Most object stores now offer **strong read-after-write consistency** (since S3 update in 2020).
- **Formula to Memorize:**
  - Replication durability: $D = 1 - (p_f)^{N_r}$
  - Erasure coding overhead: $S_{phys} = S_{logic} \times (k+m)/k$
  - Cost: $C = S_{GB} \times P_{GB\text{-}month} \times T$
- **Common Exam Traps:** Mixing TB/GB; forgetting transition costs; confusing control vs. data plane; omitting lifecycle triggers; not mentioning **availability zones** as the unit of failure isolation.
- **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily serves **CO3** — *"Design and evaluate cloud-based storage, database, and application services for given requirements."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Month Revision Tip:** Before the KTU exam, redraw the **state diagram of storage tier transitions** from memory and solve at least **one numerical** on erasure coding overhead. These two items carry the most marks in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Computing and Programming Paradigms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Computing and Programming Paradigms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Parallel Computing and Programming Paradigms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Computing** is the simultaneous use of multiple compute resources (cores, processors, or machines) to solve a problem by dividing the workload into discrete sub-tasks that are executed concurrently, reducing overall execution time and enabling the processing of larger datasets.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Computing** is defined as a computing paradigm in which multiple processors (or compute nodes) cooperate by communicating and coordinating through shared memory, message passing, or distributed file systems to execute a single computational job faster than a sequential system could. It is the **theoretical and architectural backbone** upon which modern Cloud Computing, Big Data analytics, and HPC (High-Performance Computing) systems are built.

A **Programming Paradigm** in this context refers to the model or abstraction used by the developer to express parallel execution — including thread-based models (OpenMP), message-passing models (MPI), data-parallel models (CUDA/OpenCL), and dataflow/batch models (MapReduce, Spark).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

Imagine a single chef (one CPU core) trying to cook **100 pizzas** in a kitchen. The chef must do every step — knead, sauce, bake, deliver — for each pizza, one after another. This is **sequential computing**.

Now imagine hiring **10 chefs** and dividing the kitchen into 10 stations. Each chef handles 10 pizzas in parallel. They coordinate by sharing the oven (a shared resource) and pass trays to each other (message passing). Total time drops dramatically. This is **parallel computing**.

The **programming paradigm** is the "kitchen rulebook" that dictates how the chefs coordinate:
- **Shared-memory rulebook** (OpenMP): all chefs look at the same whiteboard.
- **Message-passing rulebook** (MPI): chefs shout to each other across stations.
- **Data-parallel rulebook** (CUDA): each chef handles one slice of every pizza.
- **Batch-processing rulebook** (MapReduce): pizzas go through a "sauce station" and a "bake station" in waves.

### Key High-Yield Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Speedup (S)** | Ratio of sequential execution time to parallel execution time |
| **Efficiency (E)** | Speedup divided by the number of processors |
| **Scalability** | Ability of a system to maintain performance as processors/nodes are added |
| **Granularity** | Size of the sub-tasks (fine vs coarse) |
| **Amdahl's Law** | Theoretical upper bound on speedup due to serial fraction |
| **Gustafson's Law** | Scaled-speedup law assuming problem size grows with resources |

> [!TIP]
> The standard unit for **FLOPS** (Floating-point Operations Per Second) is the key performance metric for HPC systems. Modern cloud data centers operate at **PetaFLOPS** (10¹⁵) and **ExaFLOPS** (10¹⁸) scale.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flynn's Taxonomy of Parallel Architectures

Flynn's classification is the **canonical KTU-recommended framework** for categorizing parallel hardware.

| Class | Full Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SISD** | Single Instruction, Single Data | Traditional sequential computer | Legacy desktop PC |
| **SIMD** | Single Instruction, Multiple Data | One instruction operates on many data items simultaneously | GPU shaders, SSE/AVX CPU instructions |
| **MISD** | Multiple Instruction, Single Data | Rare; fault-tolerant systems | Space Shuttle flight control (historically cited) |
| **MIMD** | Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data | Most general and common; each processor runs its own program | Multicore CPUs, Cloud clusters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Cloud Computing context**, the dominant model is **MIMD**, with sub-classes:
> - **Shared-Memory MIMD (SMP)** → multi-core VMs
> - **Distributed-Memory MIMD** → Hadoop/Spark clusters

## 2.2 Memory Architecture Models

### A) Shared Memory Model
- All processors access **one common address space**.
- Communication happens via **reads/writes** to shared variables.
- Pros: easy programming, low latency.
- Cons: scalability bottleneck (memory contention), coherence overhead.
- **Programming APIs:** OpenMP, Pthreads, Java threads.

### B) Distributed Memory Model
- Each processor has its **own private memory**.
- Communication happens via **explicit message passing** over a network.
- Pros: highly scalable (used in Cloud).
- Cons: programmer must handle communication explicitly.
- **Programming APIs:** MPI (Message Passing Interface).

### C) Hybrid Model
- Combines both: distributed nodes with shared memory within each node.
- Most modern supercomputers and cloud clusters use this.

## 2.3 Parallel Programming Paradigms (Detailed)

### 2.3.1 OpenMP (Open Multi-Processing)
- **API** for shared-memory multiprocessing in C/C++/Fortran.
- Uses **compiler directives** (`#pragma omp parallel`) to parallelize loops.
- **Master/Slave (Fork-Join) Model:** a master thread forks worker threads at parallel regions, which then join back.
- **Best for:** multicore CPUs, single-node parallelism.

### 2.3.2 MPI (Message Passing Interface)
- **Standard** for distributed-memory communication.
- Functions: `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`, `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`.
- Each process has a unique **rank** in a **communicator**.
- **Best for:** HPC clusters, inter-node cloud communication.

### 2.3.3 MapReduce
- **Data-parallel batch processing** model introduced by Google (2004).
- Two phases:
  - **Map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)**: transform input records.
  - **Reduce(k2, list(v2)) → list(v3)**: aggregate values per key.
- **Framework handles** parallelism, fault tolerance, and data distribution.
- **Best for:** log analytics, ETL, indexing at Petabyte scale.

### 2.3.4 CUDA / OpenCL (GPU Computing)
- **SIMD-style** massively parallel programming on GPUs.
- Thousands of lightweight threads grouped into **warps/wavefronts**.
- **Best for:** deep learning, scientific simulation.

### 2.3.5 Apache Spark
- **In-memory** distributed dataflow engine.
- Uses **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** abstraction.
- Supports transformations (lazy) and actions.
- **Best for:** iterative ML, streaming, interactive analytics on cloud.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup (S)** | $S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Ratio of sequential time to parallel time |
| 2 | **Efficiency (E)** | $E(N) = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p}$ | Per-processor utilization |
| 3 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S(N) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{N}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction; $N$ = processors |
| 4 | **Amdahl's Bound (N→∞)** | $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Maximum possible speedup |
| 5 | **Gustafson's Law** | $S(N) = N - f(N-1)$ | Scaled speedup with growing problem size |
| 6 | **Karp-Flatt Metric** | $e = \frac{\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{N}}{1 - \frac{1}{N}}$ | Experimentally determines serial fraction |
| 7 | **Cost** | $C = N \cdot T_p$ | Product of processors × parallel time |
| 8 | **Communication Overhead** | $T_{total} = T_{comp} + T_{comm}$ | Total time = compute + communication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Avoidance Note:** In markdown tables, absolute value is rendered as $\vert x \vert$ (not `|x|`) to preserve table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Cloud Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Data Centers** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) expose MIMD distributed clusters → MPI/MapReduce/Spark are first-class citizens.
- **GPU Cloud Instances** (AWS p4, Azure ND) enable CUDA-based AI training at scale.
- **Serverless Parallelism** (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) abstracts the paradigm entirely — the cloud provider schedules MapReduce-style work across containers.
- **Database Parallelism** (Snowflake, BigQuery) uses Massively Parallel Processing (MPP) — a hybrid shared-nothing + shared-disk model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speedup from First Principles

**Given:**
- $T_s$ = time to execute the program on a single processor (sequential time).
- $T_p(N)$ = time to execute the program on $N$ processors (parallel time).

**Step 1 — Define the Speedup ratio.**

By definition, speedup is the factor by which parallel execution is faster:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p(N)}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Parallel Time in terms of ideal parallelism.**

Assume the work $W$ is perfectly divisible across $N$ processors with no overhead:

$$
T_p^{ideal}(N) = \frac{T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the serial fraction $f$.**

In any real program, a fraction $f$ (where $0 \le f \le 1$) is inherently serial and cannot be parallelized. The remaining $(1-f)$ is perfectly parallelizable:

$$
T_p(N) = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Speedup definition.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{T_s}{f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $T_s$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{N}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the canonical form of **Amdahl's Law**. It appears in nearly every KTU Part B 14-mark question on parallel computing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Bound (Limit as N → ∞)

**Step 1 — Start with the Amdahl formula.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

As $N$ becomes very large, the term $\frac{1-f}{N} \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1-f}{N} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

If only 5% of the program is serial ($f = 0.05$):

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

No matter how many processors you add, the program can never run more than 20× faster.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)

**Premise:** Amdahl's Law assumes a **fixed problem size**. Gustafson argued that in practice, we use more processors to solve **bigger problems** in the same time.

**Step 1 — Define the scaled runtime.**

With $N$ processors, the parallel portion scales up:

$$
T_p(N) = f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot N \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 2 — The sequential equivalent time would be:**

$$
T_s^{scaled} = f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot N \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup under Gustafson:**

$$
S_{Gustafson}(N) = \frac{T_s^{scaled}}{T_p(N)} = \frac{f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot N \cdot T_s}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
S_{Gustafson}(N) = f + (1-f) \cdot N = N - f(N-1)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight for KTU:** Amdahl = fixed problem → bounded speedup. Gustafson = scaled problem → linear speedup. This contrast is a **favourite KTU Part A question**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A program takes $T_s = 100$ seconds on a single processor. The serial fraction is $f = 0.10$. Find:
1. The speedup on 8 processors.
2. The maximum achievable speedup.
3. The efficiency on 8 processors.

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law for N = 8.**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{1-0.10}{8}}
$$

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{8}}
$$

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125}
$$

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum speedup (N → ∞).**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency on 8 processors.**

$$
E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{N} = \frac{4.706}{8} \approx 0.588 \; (58.8\%)
$$

**Verification using alternate formula:**

$$
E(8) = \frac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p} = \frac{100}{8 \cdot 21.25} \approx 0.588 \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Programming Paradigms

| Property | OpenMP | MPI | MapReduce | CUDA | Spark |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory Model** | Shared | Distributed | Distributed (HDFS) | GPU shared | Distributed (RDD) |
| **Granularity** | Fine/Thread | Coarse/Process | Coarse/Job | Fine/Warp | Medium/Task |
| **Communication** | Implicit (shared vars) | Explicit messages | Implicit (framework) | Shared GPU mem | Implicit (shuffle) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | None | None | High (replication) | None | Medium (lineage) |
| **Best Scale** | Single node | 10⁵ cores | 10⁴ nodes | 10⁴ GPU cores | 10³ nodes |
| **Cloud Fit** | Moderate | High | Very High | High (AI/ML) | Very High |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | High | Medium | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
