# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Parallel Computing. Mention any two programming paradigms used in cloud environments. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Computing** is a computation model in which multiple processors or compute nodes work cooperatively to solve a single problem simultaneously, by dividing the problem into sub-tasks that are executed in parallel. The goal is to reduce execution time and handle larger problem sizes than a single machine could.

**Two programming paradigms in cloud environments:**
1. **MapReduce** — used in Hadoop for batch processing of large datasets in the cloud.
2. **Apache Spark** — an in-memory distributed dataflow engine for iterative and streaming workloads.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners also accept **MPI** and **OpenMP** as valid paradigms. Use specific examples (Hadoop, AWS Lambda) to gain the full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. State Amdahl's Law. What is its significance in parallel computing? **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law** states that the maximum speedup $S(N)$ achievable by parallelizing a program on $N$ processors is given by:
> $$ S(N) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{N}} $$
> where $f$ is the fraction of the program that is inherently serial and cannot be parallelized.

**Significance:**
- It provides a **theoretical upper bound** on the speedup obtainable from parallelization.
- It shows that the **serial fraction dominates** performance — even a small $f$ severely limits scalability.
- It guides programmers to identify and optimize serial bottlenecks in cloud-deployed parallel code.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Option 1)

#### Q. (a) Explain Flynn's Taxonomy in detail with neat diagrams. Compare SIMD and MIMD architectures. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
Flynn's Taxonomy (1966) classifies computer architectures based on the multiplicity of instruction streams and data streams. It yields four classes: **SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD**.

**1. SISD — Single Instruction, Single Data:**
- One control unit, one memory unit.
- Executes one instruction on one data item at a time.
- Example: traditional single-core desktop CPU.
- *Diagrammatic representation:*
  - CU → MU → Single data path.

**2. SIMD — Single Instruction, Multiple Data:**
- One control unit broadcasts the same instruction to multiple processing units.
- Each PU operates on a different data item simultaneously.
- Examples: GPU shader cores, Intel SSE/AVX vector units, array processors.
- *Diagrammatic representation:*
  - CU → multiple PUs → multiple memory units operating in lockstep.

**3. MISD — Multiple Instruction, Single Data:**
- Multiple control units process the same data stream with different instructions.
- Rare; cited examples include fault-tolerant systems (e.g., space shuttle flight computers).
- *Diagrammatic representation:*
  - Multiple CUs → single MU pipeline.

**4. MIMD — Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data:**
- Multiple CUs and MUs; each processor runs its own program on its own data.
- Most general and widely used.
- Sub-classes: **Shared-Memory MIMD (SMP)** and **Distributed-Memory MIMD**.
- *Examples:* multicore CPUs, cloud server clusters, Hadoop nodes.

**Comparison: SIMD vs MIMD**

| Aspect | SIMD | MIMD |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Stream | Single | Multiple |
| Data Streams | Multiple | Multiple |
| Synchronization | Lockstep | Independent |
| Flexibility | Low (same op) | High (different ops) |
| Examples | GPU, vector unit | Multicore, cloud cluster |
| Programming | Low-level (CUDA, intrinsics) | High-level (MPI, OpenMP) |

**Conclusion:** SIMD excels at regular, data-parallel workloads (matrix math, image processing), while MIMD suits general-purpose parallel applications — the dominant model in modern cloud data centers.

#### Q. (b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of the MapReduce programming model. List its advantages. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** MapReduce is a data-parallel programming model for processing massive datasets in distributed environments. It was introduced by Google in 2004 and popularized by Apache Hadoop.

**Phases:**

1. **Input Splitting:** The input dataset on HDFS is divided into $M$ splits (typically 64 MB / 128 MB blocks).

2. **Map Phase:**
   - The framework launches one Map task per split.
   - Each Map function processes input as key-value pairs and emits intermediate key-value pairs:
     $$\text{Map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$$

3. **Shuffle and Sort Phase:**
   - Intermediate pairs are **partitioned** (by key) and **sorted** so that all pairs with the same key reach the same Reducer.
   - This is the framework's responsibility — not user code.

4. **Reduce Phase:**
   - The Reducer receives a key and an **iterator over all values** for that key.
   - It aggregates and produces the final output:
     $$\text{Reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(v_3)$$

5. **Output:** Final key-value pairs are written back to HDFS.

**Diagram (textual representation):**
```
Input → Split → Map → Shuffle&Sort → Reduce → Output
```

**Advantages:**
- **Automatic Parallelism:** Framework handles task distribution.
- **Fault Tolerance:** Failed tasks are re-executed; data is replicated.
- **Scalability:** Scales linearly to thousands of nodes.
- **Simplicity:** Programmer writes only Map and Reduce functions.
- **Data Locality:** Computation is moved to data, reducing network I/O.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Q. (a) Define Speedup and Efficiency in parallel computing. A program has a sequential time of 200 seconds and executes in 30 seconds on 16 processors. Calculate the speedup and efficiency. Comment on the result. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Speedup $S(N)$** is the ratio of the time taken by a sequential algorithm on a single processor to the time taken by the parallel algorithm on $N$ processors:
> $$ S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p(N)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency $E(N)$** is the speedup per processor, indicating how well the processors are utilized:
> $$ E(N) = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p(N)} $$

**Given:**
- $T_s = 200$ seconds
- $T_p = 30$ seconds
- $N = 16$ processors

**Step 1 — Calculate Speedup:**

$$
S(16) = \frac{200}{30} \approx 6.67
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Efficiency:**

$$
E(16) = \frac{6.67}{16} \approx 0.417 \;\;(41.7\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

- The ideal speedup on 16 processors would be 16 (100% efficiency).
- Achieved speedup is only 6.67, meaning the system is at **~41.7% efficiency**.
- Reasons for low efficiency: **communication overhead, load imbalance, serial portions, synchronization barriers**.
- To improve, we can: reduce communication, balance the workload, and minimize serial bottlenecks.

**Step 4 — Determine the serial fraction $f$ using Amdahl's Law:**

$$
6.67 = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{16}}
$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$
0.15 = f + \frac{1-f}{16} = f + 0.0625 - 0.0625f = 0.9375f + 0.0625
$$

$$
0.0875 = 0.9375f \Rightarrow f \approx 0.0933
$$

So about **9.33% of the program is inherently serial**, capping the maximum possible speedup at:

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.0933} \approx 10.72
$$

#### Q. (b) Explain OpenMP and MPI programming paradigms. Compare them based on memory model, communication, and scalability. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**OpenMP (Open Multi-Processing):**
- API for **shared-memory** parallel programming in C/C++/Fortran.
- Uses **compiler directives** (pragmas), library routines, and environment variables.
- **Fork-Join Model:** the master thread spawns worker threads at `#pragma omp parallel`; threads synchronize at implicit barriers and join after the region.
- Example skeleton (conceptual):
  - Mark parallel region → distribute loop iterations → implicit barrier at end.
- **Strengths:** simple to add to existing code, incremental parallelization.
- **Limitations:** limited to a single node; doesn't scale across machines.

**MPI (Message Passing Interface):**
- **Standard for distributed-memory** parallelism.
- Every process has a unique **rank** and a private address space.
- Communication via explicit calls: `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`, `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Gather`.
- Two main patterns: **point-to-point** (Send/Recv) and **collective** (Bcast, Reduce, Scatter, Gather).
- **Strengths:** highly scalable across thousands of nodes; portable.
- **Limitations:** complex programming; programmer must manage communication explicitly.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | OpenMP | MPI |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Model | Shared | Distributed |
| Communication | Implicit (shared variables) | Explicit (messages) |
| Scalability | Single node (limited) | Thousands of nodes |
| Granularity | Fine (thread-level) | Coarse (process-level) |
| Ease of Use | Easy | Complex |
| Best Use Case | Multicore CPU optimization | HPC clusters, cloud inter-node |

**Conclusion:** OpenMP is ideal for intra-node parallelism (multicore CPUs in a VM), while MPI is the standard for inter-node cloud/HPC communication. Modern systems often use a **hybrid MPI + OpenMP** model for maximum efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Amdahl's Law with Gustafson's Law** — Examiners specifically test whether you know which assumes *fixed* vs *scaled* problem size. Mention it explicitly in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units in Speedup/Efficiency questions** — Speedup is dimensionless; Efficiency is a fraction (0 to 1) or percentage.
> 3. **Writing only definitions without diagrams in Part B (7-mark) questions** — A neat labeled diagram is **mandatory** to score above 5/7. Always include a Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram.
> 4. **Mixing up Flynn's categories** — Remember: **M**ISD = **M**ultiple Instruction, **S**ingle Data (not MIMD). A high-frequency KTU trap question.
> 5. **In MapReduce, ignoring the Shuffle & Sort phase** — The shuffle is what differentiates MapReduce from naive "split and process." Examiners expect 1–2 lines on it.
> 6. **OpenMP syntax errors in code** — Even in descriptive answers, pseudo-code must compile semantically; wrong directive usage loses marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Parallel Computing)**

- **Parallel Computing** = simultaneous use of multiple compute resources to solve a single problem faster.
- **Flynn's Taxonomy:** 4 classes — **SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD**. Cloud uses MIMD.
- **Memory Models:** Shared (OpenMP), Distributed (MPI), Hybrid (modern HPC + Cloud).
- **Programming Paradigms covered in KTU syllabus:**
  - **OpenMP** → shared memory, fork-join, `#pragma omp parallel`.
  - **MPI** → distributed memory, explicit messaging, ranks, communicators.
  - **MapReduce** → batch data-parallel model, Map → Shuffle → Reduce, fault tolerant.
  - **CUDA/OpenCL** → GPU SIMD, warps, shared memory.
  - **Apache Spark** → in-memory RDD-based dataflow, lazy transformations.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/N}$; $S_{\max} = 1/f$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Gustafson's Law:** $S(N) = N - f(N-1)$; assumes scaled problem.
- **Speedup** $S = T_s / T_p$ (dimensionless).
- **Efficiency** $E = S / N$ (between 0 and 1, ideally).
- **Karp-Flatt Metric** $e = (1/S - 1/N) / (1 - 1/N)$ — experimentally finds serial fraction.
- **Cost** $C = N \times T_p$.
- **MapReduce advantages:** scalability, fault tolerance, automatic parallelism, simplicity, data locality.
- **Cloud + Parallelism link:** EC2, Lambda, Spark-on-EMR, Hadoop-on-Azure are all parallel paradigms deployed at hyperscale.
- **Favourite KTU question pattern:** "Explain with diagram + numerical on Amdahl's Law."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Reduce - Hadoop Library from Apache
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Map Reduce - Hadoop Library from Apache

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**MapReduce** is a **programming model**, a **parallel data processing framework**, and an **associated implementation** introduced by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) for processing and generating massive datasets on large clusters of commodity hardware. In the Apache Hadoop ecosystem, MapReduce is a core **data processing layer** that works in conjunction with the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** to enable distributed, fault-tolerant batch processing of petabyte-scale data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"MapReduce is a distributed programming model that abstracts the complexity of parallel computation, data distribution, and fault-tolerance by expressing computations as two primary user-defined functions: a `Map` function that processes key/value pairs to generate intermediate key/value pairs, and a `Reduce` function that merges these intermediate pairs associated with the same intermediate key."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The National Library Census

Imagine the Government of India wants to count the **total occurrences of every word** appearing in **all books across 10,000 libraries** nationwide. Sending one librarian through millions of books is impossible.

| Real-World Step | MapReduce Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Divide books into 10,000 piles, send to 10,000 librarians (Mappers) | **Input Splits & Map Phase** |
| Each librarian writes `(word, 1)` on index cards | **Map Output Key/Value Pairs** |
| All cards are routed to specific sorting machines by the first letter of the word | **Shuffle & Sort Phase** |
| Specialized sorters sum up the `1`s for each unique word | **Reduce Phase** |
| Final counts published in a master registry | **Output written to HDFS** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:** MapReduce hides the complexity of **parallelization**, **fault-tolerance**, **data distribution**, and **load balancing** behind two simple functions. You, the programmer, only write `map()` and `reduce()`. The framework does the rest.

## 1.3 Why MapReduce? (Motivating Factors)

- **Scalability**: Linear scaling from a single node to **thousands of nodes** by simply adding commodity hardware.
- **Fault Tolerance**: Tasks are restarted automatically on **failed nodes** without re-running the entire job.
- **Data Locality**: Computation is moved **to the data**, not the other way around — minimizing network bandwidth.
- **Simplicity**: Programmers do not handle socket programming, threading, or inter-process communication.
- **Cost-Effectiveness**: Runs on **commodity hardware** (cheap, off-the-shelf servers).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** Hadoop's default block size is **64 MB (older versions) / 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+ / 3.x)**. Always quote **128 MB** in 2024 scheme answers unless the question specifies otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components (Hadoop 1.x — Classical MapReduce)

The classical MapReduce architecture consists of a **Master-Slave** design:

### 🧠 Master Node — `JobTracker`
- Accepts **MapReduce jobs** from client applications.
- Breaks the job into **tasks** (Map tasks + Reduce tasks).
- Assigns tasks to slave nodes based on **data locality** (slot near the data block).
- **Monitors task health** via heartbeats. If a TaskTracker fails or is unresponsive, the JobTracker reschedules its tasks on another available TaskTracker.
- Maintains the **JobHistory** metadata.

### 👷 Slave Nodes — `TaskTracker`
- Runs on every **DataNode** (co-located with HDFS for data locality).
- Executes **Map** or **Reduce** tasks in **separate JVMs** (process isolation).
- Sends periodic **heartbeats** (default: every **3 seconds**) to the JobTracker to signal liveness.
- Has a fixed number of **map slots** and **reduce slots** (static slot-based scheduling in Hadoop 1.x).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop 2.x/3.x Update (YARN-based — KTU Expectation):**
> In modern Hadoop, the responsibilities of JobTracker are split:
> - **`ResourceManager` (RM)** — global resource scheduling (Cluster-level).
> - **`ApplicationMaster` (AM)** — per-job lifecycle and task coordination (Job-level).
> - **`NodeManager` (NM)** — per-node resource and task execution (replaces TaskTracker).

## 2.2 The Two User-Defined Functions

### 🔵 The `Map` Function
- **Input**: A key/value pair $(k_1, v_1)$.
- **Output**: A list of intermediate key/value pairs $\text{list}(k_2, v_2)$.
- **Formal signature**:
$$
\text{map} : (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)
$$
- **Execution**: Runs in parallel on M input splits, producing M output files (often called "**map output files**" or "**spill files**").

### 🔴 The `Reduce` Function`
- **Input**: An intermediate key $k_2$ and the **list of all values** $v_2$ associated with it (after Shuffle & Sort).
- **Output**: A list of final key/value pairs $\text{list}(k_3, v_3)$.
- **Formal signature**:
$$
\text{reduce} : (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)
$$
- **Execution**: Runs on R partitions, where typically $R$ is **user-configured** (often equal to the number of reduce slots).

## 2.3 The Four Phases of a MapReduce Job (Complete Data Flow)

| # | Phase | Responsibility | Who Controls |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Input Splitting** | Divides input file(s) into fixed-size splits (`InputFormat` controls, default `TextInputFormat` ⇒ 128 MB blocks). | Framework |
| **2** | **Mapping** | Each split is processed by a `map()` function, emitting intermediate key/value pairs. | **User-defined** |
| **3** | **Shuffling & Sorting** | Intermediate pairs are **partitioned**, **sorted by key**, and **transferred across the network** to reducers. | Framework |
| **4** | **Reducing** | For each unique key, the reducer iterates over the list of values and emits final output. | **User-defined** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Partitioning is a framework-level function** that determines *which reducer* receives a given key. The default partitioner is `HashPartitioner(key) \mod R`. Some boards give marks for knowing this.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Metrics

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | Total job wall-clock time (conceptual). | Theoretical questions. |
| 2 | $\text{Data Locality Ratio} = \frac{\text{Map tasks on local node}}{\text{Total map tasks}}$ | Higher is better. Hadoop 3.x targets $> 1.0$ (delayed scheduling). | Architecture questions. |
| 3 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}}$ | Where $p$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = number of nodes. | Compare parallel efficiency. |
| 4 | $N_{\text{map tasks}} \approx \left\lceil \frac{\text{Total Input Size (bytes)}}{128\,\text{MB}} \right\rceil$ | Number of mappers ≈ input size / block size. | Capacity planning. |
| 5 | $\text{Map Slot Capacity} = \text{mapreduce.tasktracker.map.tasks.maximum}$ | Hadoop 1.x static configuration (e.g., 2 or 4). | YARN vs. legacy comparison. |
| 6 | $\text{Heartbeat Interval} = 3\,\text{seconds}$ | Default TaskTracker → JobTracker heartbeat. | Fault tolerance questions. |
| 7 | $H_{\text{block replication}} = 3$ | Default HDFS replication factor. | Fault tolerance questions. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe `|...|` inside any table cell above.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. This is a hard markdown-parsing safeguard for the PDF converter.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MapReduce powers:
- **Search engines** — inverted index construction.
- **Log analytics** — Apache Flume + MapReduce for clickstream analysis.
- **ETL pipelines** — batch transformations in data lakes.
- **Machine learning pre-processing** — feature extraction on terabyte-scale datasets.
- **Bioinformatics** — genomic sequence alignment (e.g., CloudBurst).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: Word Count

**Problem Statement:** Given a text file containing three lines:
```
Hello World Hello
Hadoop MapReduce World
Hello Hadoop
```
Count the frequency of every word using MapReduce.

### 🟦 STEP 1 — Input Splitting
The framework creates splits (here, we assume one split per line for clarity). The input format emits:
- $(k_1, v_1) = (\text{offset 0}, \text{"Hello World Hello"})$
- $(k_1, v_1) = (\text{offset 18}, \text{"Hadoop MapReduce World"})$
- $(k_1, v_1) = (\text{offset 39}, \text{"Hello Hadoop"})$

> The key $k_1$ is the **byte offset** in the file, and the value $v_1$ is the **line of text**.

### 🟦 STEP 2 — Mapping (User-Defined Logic)

The user defines a `map` function that tokenizes each line into words and emits `(word, 1)` for each occurrence:

| Input $(k_1, v_1)$ | Tokens | Output (one per token) |
|---|---|---|
| (0, "Hello World Hello") | Hello, World, Hello | (Hello, 1), (World, 1), (Hello, 1) |
| (18, "Hadoop MapReduce World") | Hadoop, MapReduce, World | (Hadoop, 1), (MapReduce, 1), (World, 1) |
| (39, "Hello Hadoop") | Hello, Hadoop | (Hello, 1), (Hadoop, 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **list of intermediate pairs** output per map call. They are written to a local circular buffer (size ~100 MB by default), then **spilled to disk** in sorted runs (default sort buffer = 100 MB). This is the first disk I/O in MapReduce.

### 🟦 STEP 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework-Controlled)

The framework:
1. **Partitions** the intermediate keyspace using `HashPartitioner(key) \mod R`. For $R=2$ reducers:
   - Keys whose hash is even → Reducer 1.
   - Keys whose hash is odd → Reducer 2.
2. **Sorts** each partition alphabetically/numerically by key.
3. **Transfers** the sorted partitions across the network (via HTTP) to the appropriate reducer node — this is the **only network I/O** in the MapReduce data flow.

After shuffle, the reducer receives grouped data:

**Reducer 1 input** (illustrative grouping):
- (Hadoop, [1, 1])
- (Hello, [1, 1, 1])
- (MapReduce, [1])

**Reducer 2 input**:
- (World, [1, 1])

### 🟦 STEP 4 — Reducing (User-Defined Logic)

The user defines a `reduce` function that **sums** the integer values in the list:

| Input $(k_2, \text{list}(v_2))$ | Computation | Output $(k_3, v_3)$ |
|---|---|---|
| (Hadoop, [1, 1]) | $1 + 1 = 2$ | (Hadoop, 2) |
| (Hello, [1, 1, 1]) | $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$ | (Hello, 3) |
| (MapReduce, [1]) | $1$ | (MapReduce, 1) |
| (World, [1, 1]) | $1 + 1 = 2$ | (World, 2) |

### 🟦 STEP 5 — Final Output (HDFS Write)
The reducers write the final `(key, value)` pairs to HDFS. With default replication factor **3**, each output file is replicated to 3 DataNodes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Number of Map Tasks

Let the total input dataset size be $D$ bytes and the HDFS block size be $B$ bytes. The number of map tasks $M$ is given by:

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Numerical Evaluation:** For a **1 TB** dataset on a Hadoop cluster with default block size **128 MB**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 1 \text{ TB} = 1 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB} \\[6pt]
B &= 128 \text{ MB} \\[6pt]
M &= \left\lceil \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{128} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
M &= \left\lceil 8192 \right\rceil = 8192 \text{ map tasks}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** $M$ is bounded above by the number of blocks, **not** by cluster size. Each map task processes one block — Hadoop never splits a block across multiple mappers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Parallel Speedup (Amdahl's Law Applied to MapReduce)

Suppose **95%** of a MapReduce job is embarrassingly parallel (the `map` phase) and the remaining **5%** is sequential overhead (job setup, data shuffling, final commit). Running on **$n = 100$** nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup}(n) &= \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}} \\[6pt]
p &= 0.95, \quad n = 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Speedup}(100) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{100}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0095} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.0595} \\[6pt]
&\approx 16.81
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even with 100 nodes, the theoretical maximum speedup is capped near **17×** because the 5% sequential portion dominates. This is why reducing shuffle overhead is critical in Hadoop tuning.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Phase-by-Phase Disk I/O Cost

| Phase | Disk I/O | Network I/O | Controlled By |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input Split Reading | 1× read (from HDFS) | — | Framework |
| Map Output Spill | 1× write (local disk) | — | Framework |
| Shuffle (Map→Reduce) | — | **1× transfer (HTTP)** | Framework |
| Reduce Sort | 1× read + 1× write (if multi-pass merge) | — | Framework |
| Final Output Write | 1× write (to HDFS, replicated ×3) | — | Framework |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total framework-imposed disk I/O** for one MapReduce job: **up to 4 reads + 4 writes** if all intermediate spills are written. This is the *primary performance bottleneck* and the reason **Spark** was invented (in-memory processing).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level MapReduce Data Flow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[Client Application]
    rm[ResourceManager YARN]
    am[ApplicationMaster]
    nm1[NodeManager 1]
    nm2[NodeManager 2]
    nm3[NodeManager 3]
    hdfs1[HDFS DataNode 1]
    hdfs2[HDFS DataNode 2]
    hdfs3[HDFS DataNode 3]

    clientA -- submit job --> rm
    rm -- allocate container --> am
    am -- request resources --> rm
    am -- launch map task --> nm1
    am -- launch map task --> nm2
    am -- launch reduce task --> nm3

    nm1 -- read split --> hdfs1
    nm2 -- read split --> hdfs2

    nm1 -. map output .-> nm3
    nm2 -. shuffle and sort .-> nm3

    nm3 -- write final output --> hdfs3

    style clientA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rm fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style am fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nm1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nm2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nm3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style hdfs1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style hdfs2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style hdfs3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram (KTU Examiner's view):** Arrows with solid lines represent **control / data-plane requests**; dashed arrows represent the **shuffle phase** (network-level data movement).

---

## 4.2 Logical Phase Diagram: Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Split 1]
    inputB[Input Split 2]
    inputC[Input Split 3]

    mapA[Mapper 1]
    mapB[Mapper 2]
    mapC[Mapper 3]

    bufA[Local Buffer and Sort]
    bufB[Local Buffer and Sort]
    bufC[Local Buffer and Sort]

    partA[Partitioner Hash mod R]
    partB[Partitioner Hash mod R]
    partC[Partitioner Hash mod R]

    redA[Reducer 1]
    redB[Reducer 2]

    outA[Output File 1 HDFS]
    outB[Output File 2 HDFS]

    inputA --> mapA
    inputB --> mapB
    inputC --> mapC
    mapA --> bufA
    mapB --> bufB
    mapC --> bufC
    bufA --> partA
    bufB --> partB
    bufC --> partC

    partA -- keys hash to 1 --> redA
    partA -- keys hash to 2 --> redB
    partB -- keys hash to 1 --> redA
    partB -- keys hash to 2 --> redB
    partC -- keys hash to 1 --> redA
    partC -- keys hash to 2 --> redB

    redA --> outA
    redB --> outB

    style inputA fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000
    style inputB fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000
    style inputC fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000
    style mapA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style mapB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style mapC fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style redA fill:#F08080,stroke:#000
    style redB fill:#F08080,stroke:#000
    style outA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style outB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** Note how the **Partitioner** acts as a routing function. All keys with the **same hash value modulo $R$** are guaranteed to land on the **same reducer** — this is the property of co-location by key.

---

## 4.3 Hadoop Ecosystem Context (Where MapReduce Fits)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph storage[Storage Layer]
        hdfs[HDFS]
        hbase[HBase]
    end

    subgraph processing[Processing Layer]
        mr[MapReduce]
        spark[Apache Spark]
        tez[Apache Tez]
    end

    subgraph resource[Resource Management]
        yarn[YARN]
    end

    subgraph ingestion[Data Ingestion]
        sqoop[Apache Sqoop]
        flume[Apache Flume]
    end

    subgraph interfaces[Interfaces]
        hive[Apache Hive]
        pig[Apache Pig]
    end

    sqoop --> hdfs
    flume --> hdfs
    hdfs --> mr
    hdfs --> spark
    hdfs --> tez
    yarn --> mr
    yarn --> spark
    yarn --> tez
    mr --> hbase
    spark --> hbase
    hive --> mr
    hive --> tez
    pig --> mr

    style mr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px
    style yarn fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style hdfs fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style spark fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000
```

> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** MapReduce sits at the **heart** of the Hadoop ecosystem, but it is **not the only processing engine**. Hive, Pig, Spark, and Tez all sit *on top* of YARN and *use* HDFS, but they can bypass MapReduce (especially Spark and Tez, which use DAG-based execution).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

### **Q1. Define MapReduce. List its two primary user-defined functions with their input-output signatures.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
MapReduce is a distributed programming model and processing framework designed for parallel batch processing of massive datasets across clusters of commodity machines. It abstracts the complexity of parallelization, fault-tolerance, and data distribution behind two user-defined functions:

1. **Map Function** — Takes a key/value pair $(k_1, v_1)$ and produces a list of intermediate key/value pairs:
   $$\text{map}: (k_1, v_1) \longrightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$$

2. **Reduce Function** — Takes an intermediate key $k_2$ and the list of all values associated with it, producing a list of final pairs:
   $$\text{reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \longrightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$$

> The framework automatically handles input splitting, partitioning, sorting, shuffling, and fault recovery.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the Partitioner in MapReduce? Why is it important?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Partitioner** is a framework-level function that determines **which reducer** will process a given intermediate key emitted by a mapper.

- **Default Implementation:** `HashPartitioner`, which computes $\text{hash}(k_2) \mod R$, where $R$ is the number of reducers.
- **Importance:**
  1. Ensures **all values for the same key are routed to the same reducer** — a correctness requirement for reduce logic.
  2. Enables **load balancing** across reducers (a good partitioner distributes keys uniformly).
  3. Reduces **network overhead** in the shuffle phase by pre-grouping data at the mapper side.

> Custom partitioners can be implemented by the user (e.g., range partitioner for sorted outputs).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]`

### ⬇️ OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **complete architecture of MapReduce** in Hadoop 1.x with a neat block diagram. Describe the responsibilities of **JobTracker** and **TaskTracker**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The classical Hadoop 1.x MapReduce architecture follows a **Master-Slave** pattern.

**1. JobTracker (Master Node):**
- Acts as the **central coordinator** for the entire cluster.
- Accepts MapReduce jobs submitted by clients.
- Breaks each job into **map tasks** and **reduce tasks**.
- Assigns tasks to TaskTrackers using a **data-locality-aware scheduler** (prefers TaskTrackers co-located with the input block).
- Monitors TaskTracker health via **heartbeats** (default every **3 seconds**).
- On failure detection, **reschedules** the affected tasks on a healthy node.
- Maintains global **job history** and counters.

**2. TaskTracker (Slave Node — one per DataNode):**
- Runs on every slave machine, **co-located with HDFS DataNode** for data locality.
- Has a fixed number of **map slots** and **reduce slots** (static configuration).
- Executes assigned tasks in **isolated JVMs** (one task per JVM for fault isolation).
- Sends **heartbeats** and **task status** updates to the JobTracker.
- Reports **blacklisted tasks** if they fail repeatedly.

**3. Data Flow Summary:**
1. Client submits job → JobTracker.
2. JobTracker queries HDFS NameNode for block locations.
3. JobTracker assigns map tasks to TaskTrackers holding those blocks.
4. TaskTrackers run map tasks, spill intermediate output to local disk.
5. Reduce tasks copy intermediate data via HTTP (shuffle phase).
6. Reducers sort, group, and run `reduce()`.
7. Final output is written back to HDFS.

> **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above for a clean Mermaid rendering of the same architecture).

---

**Q. (b)** With a suitable example, explain the **MapReduce programming model** step-by-step. Show the **intermediate key-value pairs** generated at each stage. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Example:** Compute the maximum temperature recorded per year from a weather log dataset.

**Input records** (each line is one record):
```
2022  35
2023  41
2022  38
2023  29
2024  33
```

**Step 1 — Input Splitting:** The framework assigns each split to a mapper. Suppose all 5 records go to one mapper for simplicity (or distributed across multiple).

**Step 2 — Mapping:** The `map()` function emits `(year, temperature)`:
| Input line | Output $(k_2, v_2)$ |
|---|---|
| `2022  35` | (2022, 35) |
| `2023  41` | (2023, 41) |
| `2022  38` | (2022, 38) |
| `2023  29` | (2023, 29) |
| `2024  33` | (2024, 33) |

**Step 3 — Shuffle & Sort:** The framework groups all values for the same key:
- (2022, [35, 38])
- (2023, [41, 29])
- (2024, [33])

**Step 4 — Reducing:** The `reduce()` function computes the maximum:
| Input | Computation | Output $(k_3, v_3)$ |
|---|---|---|
| (2022, [35, 38]) | $\max(35, 38) = 38$ | (2022, 38) |
| (2023, [41, 29]) | $\max(41, 29) = 41$ | (2023, 41) |
| (2024, [33]) | $\max(33) = 33$ | (2024, 33) |

**Step 5 — Output:** Final results written to HDFS, replicated 3×.

> The user writes only the `map` and `reduce` functions; the framework handles all the intermediate orchestration (splitting, partitioning, sorting, shuffling, fault tolerance).

---

### ⬇️ OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** Compare **Hadoop 1.x (Classic MapReduce)** with **Hadoop 2.x/3.x (YARN-based MapReduce)**. Highlight at least **five architectural differences**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Feature | Hadoop 1.x (Classic MR) | Hadoop 2.x/3.x (YARN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resource Management** | JobTracker handles both resource management AND job scheduling. | ResourceManager (RM) handles resources; ApplicationMaster (AM) handles per-job scheduling. |
| 2 | **Scalability** | Limited to ~**4,000 nodes** due to single JobTracker bottleneck. | Scales to **10,000+ nodes** (Hadoop 3.x: 100,000+). |
| 3 | **Processing Models** | Only **MapReduce** batch jobs. | **MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm** — multiple engines coexist. |
| 4 | **Slot Model** | Static, fixed **map slots** and **reduce slots**. | Dynamic **containers** — flexible resource allocation. |
| 5 | **Failure Isolation** | JobTracker failure = entire cluster unusable. | RM failure is survivable via **standby RM** (HA). |
| 6 | **High Availability** | Not supported. | Built-in **NameNode HA** and **ResourceManager HA**. |
| 7 | **Multi-tenancy** | Single-tenant per cluster. | Multi-tenant; multiple applications share resources safely. |

**Conclusion:** YARN decouples resource management from programming model, enabling Hadoop to evolve from a pure batch system into a **multi-purpose data platform**.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **fault-tolerance mechanism** in MapReduce in detail. How does the system handle **TaskTracker failure**, **JobTracker failure**, and **map task re-execution**? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

MapReduce is designed to handle failures gracefully because commodity hardware is **expected to fail**.

**1. TaskTracker Failure Handling:**
- TaskTrackers send **heartbeats every 3 seconds** to the JobTracker.
- If **10 consecutive heartbeats** are missed (≈ 30 seconds), the JobTracker marks the TaskTracker as **dead**.
- All **in-progress tasks** (both map and reduce) on that TaskTracker are **rescheduled** on other healthy TaskTrackers.
- **Completed map tasks** must be **re-executed** because their output is on the failed node's local disk (lost).
- **Completed reduce tasks** are **NOT re-executed** because their output is in HDFS (durable).

**2. Map Task Re-execution Logic:**
- When a TaskTracker fails, JobTracker re-runs the map tasks it was running **OR** has already completed.
- If another TaskTracker had **already started the same map task** (speculative execution or duplicate scheduling), the framework keeps the result from the **first finisher** and kills the rest.
- This may lead to **speculative execution** overhead but ensures correctness.

**3. JobTracker Failure Handling (Hadoop 1.x):**
- In Hadoop 1.x, **JobTracker is a SPOF (Single Point of Failure)**. If the master fails, the entire cluster halts and the job must be **re-submitted manually**.
- In Hadoop 2.x+, the **ResourceManager has High Availability (HA)** — a standby RM takes over via ZooKeeper coordination.

**4. Data-Level Fault Tolerance:**
- HDFS replicates every block **3 times** by default, so input data is durable.
- Reduce output is also written to HDFS with replication factor 3.

> **Net effect:** A MapReduce job succeeds as long as **at least one copy of the data and one node per task is alive** at any time.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in MapReduce questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Map with Reduce signatures** — Writing `map: (k2, v2) → list(k3, v3)` is wrong. Always start with $(k_1, v_1)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Partitioner** — Many answers skip partitioning. Always mention `HashPartitioner` and its role in routing.
> 3. **Missing data locality** — A 7-mark "architecture" answer that doesn't mention "computation moves to data" loses **at least 1 mark**.
> 4. **Saying "JobTracker is fault-tolerant"** — It is a **SPOF in Hadoop 1.x**. Only mention HA when referring to Hadoop 2.x+/3.x.
> 5. **Conflating HDFS and MapReduce** — HDFS is **storage**; MapReduce is **processing**. They are separate layers.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — A neat block diagram (even hand-drawn) is **mandatory**. Marks are explicitly reserved.
> 7. **Wrong default block size** — Use **128 MB** (not 64 MB) for 2024 scheme answers.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MapReduce** = `Map` + `Reduce` + (framework-managed) **Shuffle/Sort/Partition**.
- ✅ **Map signature:** $\text{map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$.
- ✅ **Reduce signature:** $\text{reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$.
- ✅ **JobTracker** (master) = scheduling + monitoring. **TaskTracker** (slave) = execution.
- ✅ **Hadoop 2.x+ YARN** = `ResourceManager` + `ApplicationMaster` + `NodeManager`.
- ✅ **Default HDFS block size** = **128 MB**; **replication factor** = **3**; **heartbeat** = **3 seconds**.
- ✅ **Partitioner** = `HashPartitioner`: $\text{hash}(k_2) \mod R$.
- ✅ **Number of mappers** $\approx \lceil \text{Input Size} / 128\,\text{MB} \rceil$.
- ✅ **Failed map tasks must be re-executed** (output was on local disk); **failed reduce tasks need not be** (output is in HDFS).
- ✅ **JobTracker is a SPOF** in Hadoop 1.x; **HA** is only in Hadoop 2.x+.
- ✅ **MapReduce disadvantages** that justify Spark: high disk I/O (up to 4× read + 4× write per job), no in-memory computation, batch-only.
- ✅ **Data Locality** = moving **computation to data** (not the reverse) to save network bandwidth.
- ✅ **Word Count** is the canonical MapReduce example — practice writing it from scratch for the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files (terabytes to petabytes) across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows a **master/slave (NameNode/DataNode) architecture**, stores data in **fixed-size blocks** (default **128 MB** in HDFS 2.x onwards), and provides **high-throughput access** to data by trading off low-latency random access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style):** HDFS is a Java-based, highly fault-tolerant distributed file system that allows storing large data sets reliably, and streaming those data sets at high bandwidth to user applications. It is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** HDFS falls under *Module 4: Hadoop and Big Data* in the KTU PEEET637 Cloud Computing syllabus. It is frequently examined as a 14-mark question combining architecture, read/write operations, and fault tolerance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Warehouse"

Imagine a **national library chain** that stores **millions of books** (files):

| Library Element | HDFS Equivalent |
|----------------|-----------------|
| Head Librarian who knows **where every book is** | **NameNode** (master server holding metadata) |
| Branch storage buildings holding actual books | **DataNodes** (slave nodes holding data blocks) |
| Each book is split into **chapters**, and copies of chapters are kept in 3 different branches | Files split into **blocks** with **replication factor = 3** |
| A new copy of the catalogue is kept at a backup office | **Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node / Backup Node** |
| A customer asking for a book | **HDFS Client** issuing read/write RPCs |

**Intuition:** Just as a librarian's catalogue lets you find a book without knowing which shelf holds it, the NameNode's metadata lets the client compute which DataNode to read from — the client never goes to the NameNode to read actual data.

---

## 1.3 Core Properties of HDFS

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **5 design goals** of HDFS — remember them as a single line: *"Write once, read many, keep it cheap and survive crashes."*

1. **Fault Tolerance** — Hardware failure is the norm, not the exception. Data is replicated (default ×3).
2. **High Throughput** — Optimized for batch reads, not millisecond latency.
3. **Scalability** — Add nodes linearly; petabyte-scale storage.
4. **Reliability** — Automatic re-replication on DataNode failure.
5. **Portability** — Java-based, runs on heterogeneous commodity hardware.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants to Memorise

| Parameter | Value (HDFS 2.x / 3.x default) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| **Block size** | **128 MB** (was 64 MB in HDFS 1.x) |
| **Default replication factor** | **3** |
| **Heartbeat interval** | **3 seconds** |
| **Block report interval** | **6 hours** (default) |
| **NameNode metadata in RAM** | ~150–200 bytes per file/block |
| **Safe mode duration on startup** | Until ≥ 99.9 % blocks reported |
| **fsimage / edits file location** | `${dfs.namenode.name.dir}` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Master / Slave Decomposition

HDFS follows a **two-tier master/slave topology**:

### A. NameNode (Master)
- Maintains the **in-memory metadata tree** (`fsimage`) and a **write-ahead edit log** (`edits`).
- Does **NOT** store user data — only the *directory structure* and the *block-to-Datanode mapping*.
- Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 s) and **block reports** (every 6 h) from every DataNode.
- Single point of metadata truth ⇒ bottleneck for cluster size.

### B. DataNode (Slave)
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on its local disk.
- On startup, performs a **handshake** with the NameNode.
- Sends periodic **heartbeats** + **block reports**.
- Executes the **pipeline of re-replication** when instructed by NameNode.

### C. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)
- **Misnomer warning** — it is **NOT a hot standby**.
- Periodically merges `fsimage + edits` → new `fsimage` (the **checkpoint**).
- Prevents the edit log from growing unbounded.
- In **HDFS HA** mode, replaced by a **Standby NameNode** in the same namespace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Confused:** Secondary NameNode ≠ Backup NameNode. The **BackupNode** keeps an in-memory copy of the namespace (never used in production). **Standby NameNode** is the HA pair.

---

## 2.2 Data Storage Model — Blocks, Replicas, Rack Awareness

### Block Abstraction
- A file is split into **fixed-size blocks** (default 128 MB).
- A 130 MB file → Block-1 (128 MB) + Block-2 (2 MB).
- Last block may be **smaller** than the block size ⇒ no padding waste.
- Benefits:
  * Simplifies storage management.
  * Enables **parallel reads/writes** across DataNodes.
  * Replication happens at the **block level** (not file level) ⇒ granular fault tolerance.

### Replication Policy — Rack-Aware Placement
Default policy for a replication factor of 3:

> **Rule:** 1 replica on **local rack** (same node if writer is a DataNode, else a random node on the local rack) + 1 replica on **different rack** + 1 replica on **same different rack** (a different node on the second rack).

This achieves:
- **Reliability** — survives a full rack failure.
- **Bandwidth aggregation** — one replica read can come from same rack, one from another.
- **Write pipeline** — replicas are placed close to minimise cross-rack traffic.

---

## 2.3 Failure Detection & Recovery Pipeline

| Failure | Detection | Recovery Action |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| DataNode crash | Missed 3 heartbeats (~10 min by default) | NameNode marks dead, triggers re-replication of its blocks |
| Block corruption | Checksum mismatch on read | Client reports → NameNode schedules replication from another replica |
| NameNode crash | Edit log + fsimage checkpoint | Recovery from Secondary NameNode snapshot |
| Network partition | Heartbeat timeout | Treated as DataNode failure |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "formula" in HDFS is the **storage accounting math** — examiners love it. Memorise the table.

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|----------|---------|------|-------|
| **Number of blocks in a file** | $B = \lceil F / S \rceil$ | blocks | $F$ = file size, $S$ = block size |
| **Storage consumed (with replication)** | $T = B \cdot S \cdot R$ | MB | $R$ = replication factor; note the last block is *actual size*, not $S$ |
| **Effective storage (excluding last-block padding)** | $T_{\text{eff}} = (B-1)\cdot S \cdot R + (F \bmod S)\cdot R$ (when $B>1$) | MB | Used in HDFS — no padding |
| **NameNode RAM per file/block** | $\approx 150\text{–}200$ bytes | bytes | Therefore max blocks ≈ RAM / 200 B |
| **Heartbeat timeout** | $T_{\text{hb}} = \text{heartbeat interval} \times \text{missed count}$ | seconds | Default = 3 s × 10 = 30 s (configurable) |
| **Time to replicate 1 TB across cluster** | $t = \dfrac{D}{N \cdot B_w}$ | seconds | $D$ = data, $N$ = DataNodes, $B_w$ = per-node bandwidth |
| **Read throughput ceiling (theoretical)** | $\Theta_{\text{read}} = \min(N_d \cdot B_r, B_{\text{net}})$ | MB/s | Limited by slowest of node count × node bandwidth vs. network backbone |
| **Safe-mode threshold** | $\text{blocks reported} \ge 0.999 \times \text{total expected}$ | ratio | Default dfs.namenode.safemode.threshold-pct = 0.999 |

> **Note on absolute value symbols:** where any of the above quantities require magnitude, the HDFS framework does not use absolute-value notation in the standard formulae; only ceiling ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$) and modulo ($\bmod$) apply. The KTU math has **no** $|x|$ symbols in HDFS problems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS underpins the storage of:
- **Facebook (Meta)** — petabytes of user activity logs.
- **Yahoo** — search index and ad-click streams.
- **Netflix** — viewing telemetry and recommendation data.
- **Healthcare** — genomic sequencing datasets.
- **IoT pipelines** — time-series sensor data ingestion.

In cloud computing, HDFS is being progressively replaced/complemented by **object stores** (S3, ADLS, GCS) but the **Hadoop Distributed File System architecture and theoretical foundations remain a KTU 2024 syllabus mandate.**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Block Count & Storage Computation

> **Problem:** A file of size **1300 MB** is stored in HDFS with **block size = 128 MB** and **replication factor = 3**. Compute (a) number of blocks, (b) total storage consumed on the cluster.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the block-count formula.**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{F}{S} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1300\ \text{MB}}{128\ \text{MB}} \right\rceil
$$

Compute the raw ratio:
$$
\frac{1300}{128} = 10.15625
$$

The smallest integer $\geq 10.15625$ is $11$. Therefore:
$$
B = 11\ \text{blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify full blocks vs. last block.**

Full blocks: $10 \times 128 = 1280$ MB.
Last block size: $1300 - 1280 = 20$ MB.

**Step 3 — Compute total cluster storage (no padding).**

$$
T = (B-1)\cdot S \cdot R + (F \bmod S)\cdot R
$$

Substituting:
$$
T = (11-1)\cdot 128 \cdot 3 + 20 \cdot 3
$$

$$
T = 10 \cdot 128 \cdot 3 + 60
$$

$$
T = 3840 + 60 = 3900\ \text{MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 3900\ \text{MB} \approx 3.81\ \text{GB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common student mistake:** Multiplying $B \times S \times R = 11 \times 128 \times 3 = 4224$ MB. HDFS **does not pad** the last block, so the correct answer is **3900 MB**, not 4224 MB.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Naive vs. Rack-Aware Replica Placement

> **Problem:** A writer on **Rack-1 / DataNode-3** writes a 1-block file with replication factor = 3. Compare storage on **naive (random) placement** vs. **rack-aware placement** if the cluster has 2 racks of 5 DataNodes each. Assume each cross-rack switch link carries 1 Gbps and intra-rack links carry 10 Gbps. Compute **write bandwidth utilisation** for both.

### Step 1 — Enumerate the two placements.

| Strategy | Replica 1 | Replica 2 | Replica 3 |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Naive (random)** | Rack-1/Node-3 (writer) | Rack-1/Node-7 | Rack-2/Node-12 |
| **Rack-aware** | Rack-1/Node-3 (local) | Rack-2/Node-11 | Rack-2/Node-14 |

### Step 2 — Compute total cross-rack traffic for 1 block of size $D$.

**Naive** (2 replicas land on local rack after the writer — but HDFS still pipelines through the 1st replica regardless):
$$
\text{Cross-rack bytes} = 2D
$$

**Rack-aware**:
$$
\text{Cross-rack bytes} = D
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the time to write.

If cross-rack link capacity = $C_c$, intra-rack = $C_i$, and we have $k$ replicas needing cross-rack transfer (the writer streams to replica 1 on the local rack, which forwards across the rack, which forwards back):

$$
t_{\text{naive}} = \frac{D}{C_i} + \frac{2D}{C_c} \quad\text{(pipeline of 2 cross-rack hops)}
$$

$$
t_{\text{rack}} = \frac{D}{C_i} + \frac{D}{C_c}
$$

Substituting $D = 128$ MB = $128 \times 8$ Mbit = 1024 Mbit, $C_c = 1$ Gbps, $C_i = 10$ Gbps:

$$
t_{\text{naive}} = \frac{1024}{10000} + \frac{2048}{1000} = 0.1024 + 2.048 = 2.1504\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_{\text{rack}} = \frac{1024}{10000} + \frac{1024}{1000} = 0.1024 + 1.024 = 1.1264\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Speedup} = \frac{t_{\text{naive}}}{t_{\text{rack}}} \approx 1.91\times}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Rack-aware placement nearly halves write time for 3× replication in a 2-rack cluster.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Read Path Walk-Through (HDFS Client Algorithm)

> **Problem:** A client wants to read `/data/sales/2024/Q1.parquet` (128 MB × 10 blocks, replication = 3, 2 racks). Describe the read algorithm and identify the bottleneck.

### Algorithmic Steps (KTU board answer style)

**Step 1 — Client RPC to NameNode.**
The HDFS `DistributedFileSystem` object calls `open()` → RPC to NameNode with the file path.

**Step 2 — NameNode returns block locations.**
NameNode replies with an ordered list of `LocatedBlock` objects:
$$
L_i = \big\{\ \text{block } b_i,\ [(D_{i,1}, r_{i,1}),\ (D_{i,2}, r_{i,2}),\ (D_{i,3}, r_{i,3})]\ \big\}
$$
for $i = 1, 2, \dots, 10$. The list is sorted by **network distance** to the client (same node > same rack > different rack > unknown).

**Step 3 — Client connects to the closest replica.**
The DFSInputStream opens a TCP connection to the *first* DataNode for block $b_1$.

**Step 4 — Streaming read with checksum verification.**
Data flows as a stream of packets (64 KB each). On every packet:
$$
\text{checksum}_{\text{recv}} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{checksum}_{\text{stored}}
$$
If mismatch → client:
1. Closes the current DataNode.
2. Reports corruption to NameNode.
3. Re-opens with the **next replica** in the list.

**Step 5 — Loop over blocks.**
The client repeats Step 3–4 for blocks $b_2, b_3, \dots, b_{10}$. A *new* DataNode is chosen per block (cheapest, healthy replica).

**Step 6 — Client `close()`.**
`DFSInputStream.close()` → connection cleanup. NameNode is *not* contacted on read close.

### Bottleneck Identification

$$
\Theta_{\text{read}} = \min\big(N_d \cdot B_r,\ B_{\text{net}}\big)
$$

For a 10-block parallel read across 10 distinct DataNodes, the bottleneck is the **aggregate cluster bandwidth**, since each block reads from a different node. For a sequential read (typical MapReduce), the bottleneck is the **single DataNode's disk-to-network throughput** (~100–200 MB/s per node).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board frequently awards 2–3 marks for this *contrast table*. Memorise.

| Property | HDFS | NFS / Traditional FS | S3 (Object Store) |
|----------|------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Architecture** | Master/Slave (NN/DN) | Client/Server | Flat key-value over HTTP |
| **Block size** | 128 MB (large) | 4–64 KB | Whole object |
| **Replication** | Multi-node, rack-aware | Server-side RAID | Erasure coding / multi-AZ |
| **Read pattern** | Streaming, sequential | Random, low-latency | HTTP GET, parallel |
| **Mutation** | Write-once (append-only) | Read-write | PUT / overwrite |
| **Failure model** | Frequent, automatic recovery | Rare, manual | Cross-AZ, transparent |
| **Metadata service** | Centralised NameNode | Inode table on server | Index service (sharded) |
| **Throughput target** | High batch | Low latency | Moderate |

## 4.2 HDFS Write Pipeline — Mermaid Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3

    C->>NN: create / write request
    NN-->>C: block locations DN1, DN2, DN3 (rack-aware)
    C->>DN1: write packet 1
    DN1->>DN2: forward packet 1
    DN2->>DN3: forward packet 1
    DN3-->>DN2: ack
    DN2-->>DN1: ack
    DN1-->>C: ack
    Note over C,DN3: pipeline closes only when ALL acks received
    C->>NN: block received
    C->>NN: close file
```

## 4.3 HDFS Read Path — Mermaid Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode A
    participant DN2 as DataNode B
    participant DN3 as DataNode C

    C->>NN: open /data/file
    NN-->>C: located blocks with replica list sorted by network distance
    C->>DN1: read block 1 packet stream
    DN1-->>C: packet 1, 2, 3...
    Note over C,DN1: checksum verification per packet
    alt checksum mismatch
        C->>NN: report corrupt block
        C->>DN2: reopen block 1 on next replica
        DN2-->>C: packet stream
    end
    C->>DN2: read block 2 (next nearest healthy)
    DN2-->>C: packet stream
    C->>NN: close file
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the main components of HDFS and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **NameNode** – Acts as the master server. It stores the metadata (file names, permissions, block locations) of every file in the in-memory `fsimage` tree. It does not store actual data.
2. **DataNode** – Acts as the slave. It stores the actual data blocks on its local file system, periodically sends heartbeats (every 3 s) and block reports (every 6 h) to the NameNode.
3. **Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node** – Periodically merges the `fsimage` with the `edits` log to create a new checkpoint, preventing the edit log from growing unbounded. (Not a hot standby for the NameNode.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why does HDFS use a block size of 128 MB rather than 4 KB like a normal file system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. HDFS is designed to store **very large files** (TB–PB range). A larger block size **reduces the total number of blocks** and hence the metadata load on the NameNode.
2. Larger blocks **amortise the seek + transfer cost**, enabling **high sequential throughput** for MapReduce-style batch jobs.
3. A 4 KB block would create hundreds of millions of metadata entries — NameNode RAM (≈200 B per block) would be exhausted at cluster scale, violating scalability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students answer *"to make data transfer faster"* without mentioning the **NameNode metadata memory constraint**. The full 3-mark answer must mention **both** reasons.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**a) Explain the HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. Describe the responsibilities of the NameNode and DataNode.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Architecture overview:**
HDFS follows a **master/slave architecture**. A single NameNode (master) coordinates one or more DataNodes (slaves). Clients interact with the NameNode for metadata operations and directly with DataNodes for actual data I/O.

**NameNode Responsibilities:**
1. Manages the **file system namespace** (directory tree, file-to-block mapping, file permissions, quotas).
2. Maintains the **in-memory `fsimage`** and **`edits` log** for crash recovery.
3. Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 s) and **block reports** (every 6 h) from all DataNodes.
4. Decides **replica placement** (rack-aware), **re-replication**, and **rebalancing** of blocks.
5. Acts as the **single point of metadata truth**; never streams data.

**DataNode Responsibilities:**
1. Stores **data blocks** in the local file system under `${dfs.datanode.data.dir}`.
2. On startup, performs a **handshake** with the NameNode and registers its storage capacity.
3. Sends **heartbeats** (3 s interval) to indicate liveness.
4. Sends **block reports** listing all HDFS blocks it hosts.
5. Serves **read/write requests** from clients and participates in **replica pipelines** and **block transfer** during re-replication.

(Diagram as in Section 4.1 above.)

---

**b) With a neat diagram, explain the HDFS file read and write operations. How does fault tolerance work during a write pipeline failure?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**HDFS Write Operation (3.5 marks):**
1. Client calls `create()` on `DistributedFileSystem` → RPC to NameNode.
2. NameNode performs a **namespace check** (path validity, permissions) and inserts a new file entry in the `edits` log. Returns an `FSDataOutputStream`.
3. NameNode allocates the **first set of block replicas** (3 DataNodes on 2 racks — rack-aware policy).
4. The **write pipeline** is established: Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3.
5. Data is split into **packets (64 KB)** and pushed down the pipeline.
6. Each DataNode writes to its local disk and forwards to the next.
7. Acks propagate back: DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client.
8. After the last packet of a block, the client requests the next block's locations from the NameNode.
9. On `close()`, the client flushes pending packets and signals completion; NameNode commits the file.

**HDFS Read Operation (2 marks):**
1. Client calls `open()` → RPC to NameNode for the block list.
2. NameNode returns **LocatedBlock** objects sorted by network distance to the client.
3. Client opens a TCP connection to the **closest healthy replica** of the first block.
4. Data flows as a 64 KB packet stream with **checksum verification** per packet.
5. On success, client moves to the next block; on checksum failure, it transparently retries the next replica and reports corruption to the NameNode.
6. Client `close()` does not contact the NameNode.

**Fault Tolerance During Write Pipeline Failure (1.5 marks):**
- If any DataNode in the pipeline (say DN2) fails or times out, the client stops pushing new packets.
- The pipeline is **closed**; the two healthy DataNodes (DN1 and DN3) retain the partial block in their `BlockReceiver` temporary files.
- Client **reopens a fresh pipeline** excluding the failed DataNode, and **resends only the unacknowledged packets** from the failed node's predecessor.
- NameNode, on detecting the failed DataNode via missed heartbeats, **schedules a new replica** for the under-replicated block — eventually restoring replication factor to 3.
- The temporary partial blocks are recovered as complete blocks upon pipeline recovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones (Examiner callouts):**
> 1. Students forget to mention that **replication factor is configurable**; default 3 is *not* a constant.
> 2. Confusing **Secondary NameNode** with a backup NameNode. It performs **checkpoints**, not failover.
> 3. Omitting the **checksum verification** step in the read path — that is the principal mechanism for detecting *silent* corruption.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a) Explain HDFS data replication and rack-aware placement strategy. Why is the default replication factor 3 and not 2 or 4?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Replication Overview (3 marks):**
HDFS stores each block on **multiple DataNodes** to provide fault tolerance. The default replication factor is **3**, and the placement is **rack-aware**. Replication operates at the **block level** (not file level), enabling parallel reads and granular recovery.

**Rack-Aware Placement Policy (2 marks):**
For replication factor = 3 across 2+ racks:
- **Replica 1**: on the local node (or a random node on the local rack if the writer is not a DataNode).
- **Replica 2**: on a different rack from replica 1.
- **Replica 3**: on the same rack as replica 2 but a different node.

This ensures that loss of an entire rack still leaves 2 replicas, while write traffic crosses the inter-rack link only once (between local rack and the second rack).

**Why replication factor = 3 (2 marks):**
1. **Fault tolerance:** survives 2 simultaneous DataNode failures — but a single rack failure is also covered.
2. **Cost vs. reliability trade-off:** 2 replicas = cheaper but cannot tolerate a single rack failure with full data availability; 4 replicas = 33 % extra storage cost with marginal reliability gain.
3. **Read parallelism:** 3 replicas allow reads from 3 different nodes, multiplying aggregate read throughput.
4. **Industry standard** validated by Yahoo, Facebook studies showing ≤ 2 % annual loss rate with 3 replicas in large clusters.

---

**b) Discuss the role of the Secondary NameNode and Checkpoint Node. Differentiate HDFS from a traditional file system like NFS.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Role of Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node (3.5 marks):**
1. The NameNode keeps all mutations in the **`edits` log** plus a periodic `fsimage` snapshot in memory.
2. If the edit log grows large, NameNode startup time and memory pressure increase.
3. The **Secondary NameNode** periodically:
   a. Pulls the current `edits` log and `fsimage` from the NameNode (HTTP GET).
   b. Merges them into a **new `fsimage`** (the *checkpoint*).
   c. Pushes the new `fsimage` back to the NameNode.
4. After this, the NameNode truncates the `edits` log → namespace size stays bounded.
5. **In HDFS HA mode**, the **Standby NameNode** does this checkpointing live; Secondary NameNode is **not required** and is typically disabled.

**Difference: HDFS vs. NFS (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | HDFS | NFS |
|--------|------|-----|
| **Architecture** | Distributed master/slave | Centralised client/server |
| **Data location** | Multiple DataNodes (replicated) | Single server share |
| **Block size** | 128 MB (large) | 4–64 KB (small) |
| **Workload** | Write-once, read-many streaming | Random read-write, low-latency |
| **Fault tolerance** | Automatic re-replication | Server-side RAID, manual failover |
| **Scalability** | Linear with nodes (petabytes) | Bounded by server CPU/NIC |
| **Latency target** | High throughput, millisecond-OK | Sub-millisecond |
| **Mutability** | Append-only | Full read-write |
| **Metadata** | Centralised NameNode | Inode table on server |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students state *"NFS is slow and HDFS is fast"* — this is *not* a valid point. The real contrast is **streaming vs. random-access workload fit**. HDFS is fast for *its design target* (batch), not universally faster.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — KTU 2024 PEEET637 Module 4 (HDFS)**

- ☐ **Architecture** = 1 NameNode (master, metadata only) + N DataNodes (slaves, data blocks).
- ☐ **Block size** = **128 MB** (HDFS 2.x+); default **replication = 3**.
- ☐ **Block-count formula** = $B = \lceil F / S \rceil$; HDFS does **NOT** pad the last block.
- ☐ **Rack-aware placement** = 1 local + 1 different rack + 1 same different-rack node.
- ☐ **Heartbeat** = every 3 s; **Block report** = every 6 h; timeout ≈ 10 missed heartbeats.
- ☐ **Read path** = NameNode returns sorted replica list → client streams from closest healthy replica → checksum verified per packet.
- ☐ **Write path** = NameNode allocates rack-aware DataNodes → **pipeline** Client→DN1→DN2→DN3 → ack back-propagation.
- ☐ **Secondary NameNode** = *checkpointing*; **NOT** a backup. BackupNode / StandbyNameNode is the true HA pair.
- ☐ **Fault tolerance** = re-replication triggered by missed heartbeats; checksum detects corruption.
- ☐ **Safe mode** = NameNode is read-only until ≥ 99.9 % of expected blocks are reported.
- ☐ **HDFS Federation** = multiple independent NameNodes share a common DataNode pool (overcomes single-NN RAM bottleneck).
- ☐ **HDFS HA** = Active/Standby NameNode pair using **ZooKeeper** + **QJM** (Quorum Journal Manager) for shared edits.
- ☐ **One-line summary for viva:** *"HDFS = write-once, read-many, large-block, rack-aware, fault-tolerant distributed storage for Hadoop batch workloads."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pig Latin Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pig Latin Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pig Latin** is a **data-flow language** developed at **Yahoo!** and now maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation** as part of the Hadoop ecosystem. It is the scripting language used with **Apache Pig**, a high-level platform for analyzing large, semi-structured, and unstructured data sets. Pig Latin abstracts the underlying MapReduce job into a sequential, easy-to-write transformation pipeline.

Pig Latin operates on **relations** (analogous to tables in RDBMS) and produces new relations through a sequence of **statements**. The execution semantics are strictly **data-parallel**, allowing the framework to automatically distribute the workload across a Hadoop cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pig Latin forms the cornerstone of *Module 4 — Big Data Analytics on the Cloud*. Students must master **data types (atom, tuple, bag, map)**, **arithmetic/relational/Boolean expressions**, **load/store/dump operators**, and the **Filter, Foreach, Group, Join, CoGroup, and Distinct** Latin statements.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **kitchen assembly line in a large restaurant**:
- The **raw ingredients** (raw data in HDFS) are loaded onto a conveyor belt.
- A chef **inspects and filters** the ingredients (the `FILTER` statement).
- Another station **chops and reshapes** the vegetables (the `FOREACH ... GENERATE` statement).
- A final station **sorts dishes into categories** (the `GROUP` statement).
- The finished plates are **served to the customer** (the `STORE` or `DUMP` statement).

Pig Latin is the *recipe script* that dictates what each station does — without requiring the chef to manually operate the oven (i.e., without writing low-level MapReduce Java code).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Pig Latin

| Metric | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| Default Join Type | **Inner Join** |
| Default Grouping Strategy | **Group All** if no key specified |
| Field Delimiter (default) | **Tab character (`\t`)** |
| Storage Function (load default) | **PigStorage** |
| Data Storage Backend | **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) |
| Execution Mode | **Local (single JVM)** or **MapReduce (distributed)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Pig Latin Script

A Pig Latin script is a sequence of **statements** executed in a **top-to-bottom, left-to-right order** (with the exception of `JOIN`/`COGROUP`/`CROSS`, which may be parallelized). Each statement performs a transformation and produces a new relation.

The general **Why** and **How** behind each statement type are summarized below:

- **`LOAD` — Data Ingestion**  
  *Why:* To bring raw data from external storage (HDFS, HBase, local FS) into the Pig environment.  
  *How:* Reads files line-by-line, parses them according to a `LOAD FUNC`, and produces a relation whose schema can be declared inline.

- **`STORE` / `DUMP` — Data Egress**  
  *Why:* `DUMP` displays results to the terminal (debug/development); `STORE` persists results back to HDFS.  
  *How:* Materializes the in-memory pipeline and writes tuples in serialized form.

- **`FILTER` — Row Selection**  
  *Why:* To drop tuples that fail a Boolean predicate (e.g., salary > 50000).  
  *How:* Iterates the relation tuple-by-tuple; only those returning `true` proceed.

- **`FOREACH ... GENERATE` — Column Transformation**  
  *Why:* To project, compute, or derive new fields.  
  *How:* Applies field-wise expressions to every tuple in the input relation.

- **`GROUP` / `COGROUP` — Aggregation Batching**  
  *Why:* To cluster tuples by key for downstream aggregation.  
  *How:* Partitions tuples into inner bags sharing the same group key.

- **`JOIN` / `COGROUP` / `CROSS` — Multi-Relation Combination**  
  *Why:* To merge related tuples from two or more relations.  
  *How:* Performs key-based matching (or Cartesian product for `CROSS`).

- **`DISTINCT` — Deduplication**  
  *Why:* To remove duplicate tuples.  
  *How:* Builds a hash-based unique-tuple set.

- **`ORDER BY` — Sorting**  
  *Why:* To produce a globally sorted relation.  
  *How:* Performs a parallel sort (with multiple reducers) producing total order.

- **`LIMIT` — Row Truncation**  
  *Why:* To cap the output size (often used in development).  
  *How:* Truncates after N tuples are emitted.

### 2.2 Pig Latin Data Types — The Core Vocabulary

Pig Latin features a **rich, nested type system** — a key differentiator from flat SQL row types.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on data types are **guaranteed high-frequency**. A `BAG` is the most commonly tested type, often confused with a `TUPLE`.

| Data Type | Symbol | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Atom** | — | A single simple value: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, `bytearray`, `boolean` | `42`, `'hello'`, `3.14` |
| **Tuple** | `()` | An ordered set of fields (heterogeneous). The Pig equivalent of a **row**. | `(John, 30, NYC)` |
| **Bag** | `{}` | An **unordered** collection of tuples. May contain duplicate tuples. The Pig equivalent of a **table**. | `{(1,A),(2,B)}` |
| **Map** | `[]` | A set of `key $\rightarrow$ value` pairs, where keys are `chararray`. | `[name#John, age#30]` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A Bag is the outermost data structure of any Pig Latin relation.** A relation IS a bag. This is why Pig can naturally model **non-flat** data (nested records), unlike SQL which requires a separate JOIN.

### 2.3 Expressions in Pig Latin

Expressions are categorized into three families:

1. **Arithmetic Expressions** — operate on numeric atoms: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `bincond` (ternary).
2. **Relational Expressions** — produce Boolean values: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `matches` (regex), `is null`.
3. **Boolean Expressions** — combine predicates using `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet — Complex Data Type Construction

| Construction | Pig Latin Syntax | Resulting Type |
|---|---|---|
| Tuple literal | `(field1, field2, field3)` | Tuple |
| Bag literal | `{(t1), (t2), (t3)}` | Bag of Tuples |
| Map literal | `[key1\#val1, key2\#val2]` | Map |
| Field reference | `relation.field` | Atom (projected) |
| Field dereference of bag | `bagName.field` | Bag (projected field from inner tuples) |
| Field dereference of tuple | `tupleName.field` | Atom |
| Type cast | `(int)\vert x` | Atom (cast) |
| Star projection | `*` | All fields of tuple |
| IsEmpty check | `IsEmpty(bag)` | Boolean |
| Size of bag | `SIZE(bag)` | Long |

> [!NOTE]
> In Pig Latin, the `#` symbol is reserved as the **map key-value separator**. Use `\#` (escaped) inside the Map literal to avoid compile-time parse errors.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ETL Pipelines**: Cloud-hosted data lakes (S3, ADLS, GCS) routinely use Pig to pre-process clickstream and IoT data before warehousing.
- **Log Analytics**: Netflix, Yahoo, and LinkedIn historically used Pig to mine petabyte-scale server logs.
- **Ad-Tech**: Pig Latin's `COGROUP` and nested `FOREACH` make it well-suited for multi-dimensional ad-attribution modeling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Word Count in Pig Latin

This is the canonical KTU 2024 examination example. We shall derive, step-by-step, how Pig Latin counts word frequencies in a text file `input.txt`.

**Problem:** Given a text file in HDFS, count the frequency of each word.

**Step 1 — LOAD the raw lines**

The input file is loaded as a relation with one field `line` of type `chararray`.

$$
\text{lines} = \text{LOAD 'hdfs:///data/input.txt' USING PigStorage('\\n') AS (line:chararray);}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* `PigStorage('\\n')` instructs the loader to treat **each line of the file** as a separate record. The schema declaration `AS (line:chararray)` strongly types the only field.

**Step 2 — TOKENIZE each line into words**

The built-in `TOKENIZE` function splits a `chararray` into a `bag` of words.

$$
\text{words} = \text{FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line));}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* `TOKENIZE(line)` returns a bag of words `{(word1), (word2), ...}`. `FLATTEN` removes one level of nesting, turning each bag element into a separate tuple. The result is a new relation with one field (the atom word).

**Step 3 — GROUP words by the word itself**

$$
\text{grouped} = \text{GROUP words BY word;}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* `GROUP` creates a new relation with two fields: `group` (the word, type `chararray`) and `words` (a bag containing all tuples that share that word). The output schema is `(group:chararray, words:bag{...})`.

**Step 4 — COUNT tuples in each group**

$$
\text{word\_count} = \text{FOREACH grouped GENERATE group, COUNT(words);}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* For each tuple in `grouped`, we project the `group` field (the word) and apply the built-in `COUNT` aggregate to the inner `words` bag. The aggregate collapses the bag to a single integer.

**Step 5 — STORE (or DUMP) the result**

$$
\text{STORE word\_count INTO 'hdfs:///data/output' USING PigStorage();}
$$

### 3.2 Complete Script — End-to-End

```pig
lines = LOAD 'hdfs:///data/input.txt' AS (line:chararray);
words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line));
grouped = GROUP words BY word;
wordcount = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group, COUNT(words);
STORE wordcount INTO 'hdfs:///data/output';
```

### 3.3 Stepwise Logical State Transitions

The transformation of data can be viewed through an **analytical matrix** showing the state of the relation at each stage.

Assume `input.txt` contains:

```
Hello world
Hello Pig
Pig is fun
```

| Stage | Relation | Schema | Sample Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `lines` | `(line:chararray)` | `{(Hello world)}, {(Hello Pig)}, {(Pig is fun)}` |
| 2 | `words` | `(word:chararray)` | `{(Hello),(world),(Hello),(Pig),(Pig),(is),(fun)}` |
| 3 | `grouped` | `(group:chararray, words:bag)` | `{(Hello,{(Hello),(Hello)}),(world,{(world)}),(Pig,{(Pig),(Pig)}),(is,{(is)}),(fun,{(fun)})}` |
| 4 | `wordcount` | `(word:chararray, count:long)` | `{(Hello,2),(world,1),(Pig,2),(is,1),(fun,1)}` |

### 3.4 Derivation of Schema After `FOREACH ... GENERATE`

The schema of a `FOREACH` statement is **derived from the projected fields**. For a general `FOREACH A GENERATE f1, f2, ...`:

$$
\text{Schema}(R_{out}) = (\text{Schema}(f_1), \text{Schema}(f_2), \ldots, \text{Schema}(f_n))
$$

where each $\text{Schema}(f_i)$ is recursively determined by the type of the expression $f_i$ according to the type rules in **Section 2.2**.

For example, given `A: (name:chararray, marks:bag{(sub:chararray, score:int)})`:

$$
\text{FOREACH A GENERATE name, AVG(marks.score);}
$$

produces a relation with schema `(name:chararray, avg_score:double)`. The derivation:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Schema}(name) &= \text{chararray} \\
\text{Schema}(marks.score) &= \text{bag}\{(score:int)\} \text{ after dot-projection} \\
\text{Schema}(AVG(marks.score)) &= \text{double} \quad \text{(aggregates return double for int input)} \\
\end{aligned}

> *Conversion logic:* Pig's type-coercion hierarchy promotes `int $\rightarrow$ long $\rightarrow$ float $\rightarrow$ double` for numeric aggregates, ensuring no overflow on large Hadoop datasets.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apache Pig — High-Level Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the data flow from script ingestion to execution on the cluster.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
