```mermaid
    A[Pig Latin Script .pig] --> B[Pig Grunt Shell]
    B --> C[Parser]
    C --> D{Logical Plan}
    D --> E[Logical Optimizer]
    E --> F[Physical Plan]
    F --> G{Execution Mode}
    G -->|Local| H[Single JVM]
    G -->|MapReduce| I[Hadoop Cluster YARN]
    H --> J[Result Output DUMP or STORE]
    I --> J
    J --> K[HDFS or Local FS]
```

### 4.2 Pig Latin Data Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atom] --> A1[int]
    A --> A2[long]
    A --> A3[float]
    A --> A4[double]
    A --> A5[chararray]
    A --> A6[bytearray]
    A --> A7[boolean]
    T[Tuple] --> T1[(f1:atom, f2:atom, ...)]
    T --> T2[Ordered, fixed schema]
    B[Bag] --> B1[Inner Bag]
    B --> B2[Outer Bag IS a relation]
    B --> B3[Unordered, may have duplicates]
    M[Map] --> M1[key:chararray to value:any]
    M --> M2[Unordered, lookup by key]
    T --> B
    A --> T
    A --> M
```

### 4.3 End-to-End Pig Latin Execution Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 LOAD] --> S2[Step 2 TRANSFORM FOREACH FILTER]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 AGGREGATE GROUP COGROUP]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 COMBINE JOIN CROSS]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 DEDUP DISTINCT]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 ORDER ORDER BY]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 EGRESS DUMP or STORE]
    subgraph Parallel_Execution
        S1
        S2
        S3
        S4
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler Insight:** Pig Latin's `FOREACH ... GENERATE` is a **logical projection** — it does NOT force a data shuffle. The actual data movement happens only at `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `COGROUP`, `CROSS`, `ORDER BY`, and `DISTINCT` (the "shuffle-bound" operators).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Define Pig Latin. List its four complex data types with one-line examples.

**Model Answer:**

> **Pig Latin** is a high-level, data-flow scripting language used in **Apache Pig** to write data analysis programs on top of **Hadoop** without writing low-level MapReduce code.
>
> **Four Complex Data Types:**
>
> 1. **Atom** — A single simple value, e.g., `42` or `'Kerala'`.
> 2. **Tuple** — An ordered set of fields, e.g., `(Riya, 22, CSE)`.
> 3. **Bag** — An unordered collection of tuples, e.g., `{(Riya,22),(Arun,21)}`.
> 4. **Map** — A set of `key\#value` pairs, e.g., `[dept\#CSE, year\#3]`.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for types & examples)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Differentiate between `DUMP` and `STORE` operators in Pig Latin.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `DUMP` | `STORE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Displays output to the terminal | Persists output to file system |
| **Storage** | Not stored (volatile) | Stored in HDFS / local FS |
| **Use case** | Development, debugging | Production, downstream consumers |
| **Statement dependency** | Always terminal in the script | Often the final statement |
| **Overhead** | Low (just prints) | High (writes to disk) |

> **Key Point:** A relation is **materialized** only when `DUMP` or `STORE` is invoked; otherwise, Pig Latin is *lazy*.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistakes students make in Pig Latin derivations are:
> 1. **Forgetting to FLATTEN** a bag after `TOKENIZE`, resulting in a nested bag-of-bags that breaks the `GROUP BY`.
> 2. **Confusing `Bag` and `Tuple`** — projecting a field from a bag returns a bag, not an atom. Use `FLATTEN` or an aggregate.
> 3. **Mis-using `\#` in Maps** — forgetting the backslash escape causes a parse-time failure.
> 4. **Assuming `FOREACH` triggers a shuffle** — it does not; only `GROUP`/`JOIN`/`ORDER BY` do. Drawing a wrong architecture diagram costs **2 marks** instantly.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Explain the various data types available in Pig Latin with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Pig Latin supports **two categories** of data types — **simple (scalar) types** and **complex (collection) types**.

**(i) Simple Data Types (Atoms):**
- `int`, `long` — 32-bit and 64-bit signed integers.
- `float`, `double` — Single- and double-precision floating-point.
- `chararray` — UTF-8 string.
- `bytearray` — Binary blob (default untyped value).
- `boolean` — `true` or `false`.
- **Example:** `42`, `'Kerala'`, `true`.

**(ii) Complex Data Types:**

1. **Tuple** — An *ordered*, *fixed-length* set of fields of possibly different types. Wrapped in parentheses.
   - Example: `(Ramesh, 22, CSE)` — three fields: name, age, department.
   - Schema declaration: `AS (name:chararray, age:int, dept:chararray)`.

2. **Bag** — An *unordered* collection of tuples. May contain duplicates. Wrapped in curly braces. *A bag is the outermost data structure of every Pig relation.*
   - Example: `{(1,Apple),(2,Mango),(3,Banana)}`.
   - **Inner Bag:** A bag nested inside a tuple, e.g., `(Riya, {(Math,90),(Physics,85)})`.

3. **Map** — A set of `key\#value` pairs, where keys are always `chararray`. Wrapped in square brackets.
   - Example: `[name\#Ramesh, age\#22]`.

> **Engineering Note:** The ability to nest bags inside tuples makes Pig Latin inherently suited for **denormalized, hierarchical data** (e.g., JSON, Avro, Parquet with nested schemas) — a strong advantage over flat SQL tables.

*(7 marks — 2 for simple types, 5 for complex types with examples)*

---

**Q3(b).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Write a Pig Latin script to count the number of occurrences of each word in a given text file `books.txt` stored in HDFS. Show the relation schema at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pig Latin Script:**

```pig
-- Step 1: Load each line of the text file
lines = LOAD 'hdfs:///data/books.txt' AS (line:chararray);

-- Step 2: Tokenize and flatten
words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line));

-- Step 3: Group by word
grouped = GROUP words BY word;

-- Step 4: Count occurrences
wordcount = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group AS word, COUNT(words) AS count;

-- Step 5: Store the result
STORE wordcount INTO 'hdfs:///data/wordcount_output';
```

**Schema Table at Each Stage:**

| Stage | Relation | Schema | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `lines` | `(line:chararray)` | One tuple per line in the file |
| 2 | `words` | `(word:chararray)` | One tuple per word, after `FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(...))` |
| 3 | `grouped` | `(group:chararray, words:bag{...})` | All words clustered by their value |
| 4 | `wordcount` | `(word:chararray, count:long)` | Final frequency table |
| 5 | Output | Same as `wordcount` | Persisted to HDFS |

> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The `FLATTEN` operator in Step 2 is *essential* — without it, `words` would be a relation with a single field of type `bag`, and `GROUP` would group entire bags, not individual words. This is a **classic 1-mark trap** in valuation.

*(7 marks — 3 for script, 2 for explanation, 2 for schema table)*

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q4(a).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Explain the architecture of Apache Pig with a neat diagram. Compare the **Local** and **MapReduce** execution modes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apache Pig Architecture** consists of the following major components:

1. **Parser** — Lexically and syntactically analyzes the Pig Latin script. Produces a parse tree.
2. **Optimizer** — Applies logical optimizations (e.g., predicate pushdown, projection pruning) to the logical plan.
3. **Compiler** — Converts the optimized logical plan into a **physical plan** (a sequence of MapReduce jobs in distributed mode).
4. **Execution Engine** — Submits the physical plan to the chosen execution engine (Local JVM or YARN/Hadoop).
5. **Grunt Shell** — Interactive command-line interface for executing Pig commands.
6. **Pig Latin Statements** — The user-written script.

*(Block diagram already shown in Section 4.1.)*

**Comparison of Execution Modes:**

| Feature | Local Mode | MapReduce Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Single JVM, single node | Distributed cluster via YARN |
| **Data size** | Small-to-medium (debugging) | Petabyte-scale (production) |
| **Parallelism** | Thread-level | Cluster-level |
| **HDFS access** | Optional (can use local FS) | Mandatory |
| **Command flag** | `pig -x local` | `pig` (default) or `pig -x mapreduce` |
| **Speed (small data)** | Faster (no job submission overhead) | Slower (job startup dominates) |
| **Failure tolerance** | None | Built-in via YARN |

> **Conclusion:** Local mode is purely for development; MapReduce mode is the production target for cloud-scale analytics.

*(7 marks — 3 for architecture, 2 for diagram reference, 2 for mode comparison)*

---

**Q4(b).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Given two input relations —

$$
A = \{(1, \text{John}), (2, \text{Riya}), (3, \text{Arun})\}, \quad
B = \{(1, \text{CSE}), (2, \text{ECE}), (4, \text{MECH})\}
$$

write a Pig Latin script to perform an **INNER JOIN** and a **LEFT OUTER JOIN** on the common field `id`. Show the output relations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pig Latin Script:**

```pig
-- Load A and B with explicit schema
A = LOAD 'hdfs:///data/A' AS (id:int, name:chararray);
B = LOAD 'hdfs:///data/B' AS (id:int, dept:chararray);

-- Inner Join
inner_result = JOIN A BY id, B BY id;
DUMP inner_result;

-- Left Outer Join
left_result = JOIN A BY id LEFT OUTER, B BY id;
DUMP left_result;
```

**Output Relations:**

**INNER JOIN `inner_result`:**

$$
\text{inner\_result} = \{(1, \text{John}, 1, \text{CSE}), (2, \text{Riya}, 2, \text{ECE})\}
$$

> *Tuple 3 of A (Arun) and tuple 3 of B (MECH) are dropped because they have no matching `id` on the other side.*

**LEFT OUTER JOIN `left_result`:**

$$
\text{left\_result} = \{(1, \text{John}, 1, \text{CSE}), (2, \text{Riya}, 2, \text{ECE}), (3, \text{Arun}, \text{null}, \text{null})\}
$$

> *Tuple 3 of A is preserved; the right-side fields are filled with `null` because no match exists in B.*

**Schema Derivation:**

The `JOIN` produces a relation whose schema is the **concatenation** of the input schemas:

$$
\text{Schema}(\text{join}) = (\text{Schema}(A), \text{Schema}(B))
$$

Formally, after `JOIN A BY id, B BY id`:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Schema}(\text{join}) &= (\text{id:int}, \text{name:chararray}, \text{id:int}, \text{dept:chararray}) \\
\end{aligned}

> **Naming Tip:** Pig Latin allows renaming via `A::id` and `B::id` to disambiguate common field names — a frequent examination point.

*(7 marks — 3 for script, 2 for inner output, 2 for left outer output)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Pig Latin is *lazy*** — the data pipeline executes only at `DUMP` or `STORE`.
- ✅ **A relation is a bag** — the outermost container is always a `bag` of tuples.
- ✅ **Four complex types:** Atom, Tuple, Bag, Map. **Master the order in nesting: Atom $\subset$ Tuple $\subset$ Bag.**
- ✅ **`FLATTEN` removes exactly one level of bag nesting** — essential after `TOKENIZE` or any `GROUP` projection.
- ✅ **`FOREACH` is a logical projection** — it does **not** trigger a shuffle. Only `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `COGROUP`, `CROSS`, `ORDER BY`, and `DISTINCT` cause data redistribution.
- ✅ **Default load function:** `PigStorage`. **Default field delimiter:** tab.
- ✅ **Default join:** inner join. Use `LEFT`, `RIGHT`, `FULL OUTER` keywords explicitly for non-inner joins.
- ✅ **Aggregate functions** (`SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`) **always** operate on a bag and return a scalar.
- ✅ **Map separator:** use `\#` (escaped hash) inside Map literals.
- ✅ **Two execution modes:** `Local` (single JVM, debug) and `MapReduce` (cluster, production).
- ✅ **Pig Latin scripts** are saved with the `.pig` extension.
- ✅ **Schema is optional** in Pig — relations can be schema-less (type inferred at use-site) or schema-ful (declared via `AS (...)`).
- ✅ **`DUMP` ≠ `STORE`** — `DUMP` prints, `STORE` persists. Do not interchange them in board answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apache Spark. Fundamentals of Cloud Security: Basic Terms & Concepts in Security - Threat Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Apache Spark. Fundamentals of Cloud Security: Basic Terms & Concepts in Security - Threat Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Apache Spark — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory data processing engine** designed for **fast, large-scale data analytics, machine learning, and stream processing** across clustered computing environments. It extends the **MapReduce** paradigm by supporting **in-memory computation**, **lazy evaluation**, and a **unified engine** for batch, interactive, iterative, and streaming workloads.

Spark is built around the abstraction of the **Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD)** — an immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel. It provides higher-level APIs (DataFrames, Datasets, Spark SQL) while preserving the fault-tolerance guarantees of distributed systems.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **Apache Spark** as a **giant factory assembly line** for data.
> - The **raw materials** = the massive datasets stored across many machines (HDFS, S3, etc.).
> - The **factory floor** = the **Cluster** (Driver + Executors).
> - The **factory manager** = the **Driver Program / SparkContext**, which takes your "recipe" (code) and breaks the job into smaller tasks.
> - The **workers on the line** = the **Executors**, each processing a portion of the data in parallel.
> - The **production logbook** = the **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** scheduler, which optimizes the order of operations.
> - **In-memory storage** = keeping partially built products *on the workbench* rather than sending them back to the warehouse after every step — this is what makes Spark **100x faster** than traditional MapReduce for iterative jobs like **ML training**.

---

## 1.2 Fundamentals of Cloud Security — Basic Terms & Concepts

### Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat**: Any potential event or action that can exploit a vulnerability in a system and cause harm (data loss, unauthorized access, denial of service, etc.).
> **Vulnerability**: A weakness or flaw in a system (software bug, misconfiguration, unpatched OS) that can be exploited.
> **Attack**: An actual attempt to exploit a vulnerability and cause harm.
> **Asset**: Anything of value to an organization — data, hardware, software, services, reputation.
> **Risk**: The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact of the resulting damage.
> **Countermeasure / Control**: A safeguard (technical, administrative, or physical) deployed to reduce risk.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Securing a House"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine your **cloud environment** is a **house**:
> - **Assets** = your furniture, jewelry, important documents.
> - **Vulnerabilities** = unlocked windows, weak locks, open garage door.
> - **Threats** = possible events like "a burglar may try to enter," "a storm may damage the roof."
> - **Threat Agents** = the *actual* burglars, storms, or even a careless housekeeper — the *entities* that may carry out the threat.
> - **Controls** = alarm systems, strong locks, CCTV cameras, insurance.
> - **Risk** = the *likelihood* of a burglar actually breaking in *combined with* the value of what's lost.

---

## 1.3 Threat Agents — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Threat Agent** (in the **NIST SP 800-30** and **ENISA** threat-modeling frameworks) is any **entity, circumstance, or actor** that has the **capability, intent, and opportunity** to adversely affect an information system — i.e., to **initiate, cause, or contribute to an attack** on cloud assets.
> Threat agents are the **"who"** behind a threat. Without a threat agent, a threat remains a theoretical possibility.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Villain Behind the Heist"

> [!NOTE]
> While a **threat** is the *idea* of a bank robbery, a **threat agent** is the **actual person or group** who plans and executes it. In cybersecurity:
> - The **threat** is "data exfiltration will occur."
> - The **threat agent** is the **hacker, insider employee, ransomware gang, or nation-state actor** that performs it.
> - A **vulnerability** (e.g., an unpatched API) is the *open window* the threat agent exploits.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Threat Agents in Cloud Computing

| # | Threat Agent | Description | Typical Cloud Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Insider (Trusted)** | Employees, contractors, or partners with legitimate access. | Customer data, source code, admin panels. |
| 2 | **Insider (Untrusted / Malicious)** | Privileged user who misuses access for personal gain or sabotage. | Data deletion, privilege escalation. |
| 3 | **Outsider / Anonymous Attacker** | External hackers exploiting internet-facing services. | Public APIs, web apps, VMs. |
| 4 | **Organized Crime Groups** | Financially motivated, use advanced persistent threats (APTs). | Banking data, credentials, ransomware. |
| 5 | **Nation-State Actors** | State-sponsored, well-funded, target critical infrastructure. | Defense, healthcare, government clouds. |
| 6 | **Competitors / Corporate Espionage** | Industrial spies seeking trade secrets. | Proprietary algorithms, customer lists. |
| 7 | **Hacktivists** | Ideologically motivated attackers. | Defacing public-facing sites, DDoS. |
| 8 | **Script Kiddies** | Unskilled attackers using pre-built tools. | Misconfigured S3 buckets, default passwords. |
| 9 | **Malware / Botnets** | Automated software agents. | Credential brute force, DDoS. |
| 10 | **Natural / Environmental Agents** | Earthquakes, floods, fires, power outages. | Datacenter physical infrastructure. |
| 11 | **Cloud Provider Insider** | Employee of the IaaS/PaaS/SaaS vendor. | Multi-tenant isolation, hypervisor. |
| 12 | **Supply Chain / Third-Party** | Vendors with weak security upstream. | Backdoored libraries, compromised updates. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake**: Students often confuse **"Threat"** with **"Threat Agent"**. In your exam answers, always clarify that the *threat* is the *event*, while the *threat agent* is the *actor who causes* the event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Spark — Architecture Deep Dive

Spark follows a **master–worker (driver–executor) architecture**. The breakdown is as follows:

### 2.1.1 Spark Cluster Architecture — Layered View

- **Layer 1 — Cluster Manager**: Allocates cluster resources among competing applications. Examples: **Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes**.
- **Layer 2 — Driver Program**: The process running the user's `main()` function. It contains the **SparkContext / SparkSession**, which is the entry point to Spark functionality.
  - Creates the **DAG** of stages and tasks.
  - Schedules tasks on executors via the Cluster Manager.
  - Coordinates fault recovery by re-submitting lost tasks.
- **Layer 3 — Executors**: Worker processes launched on cluster nodes. Each executor:
  - Runs **tasks** assigned by the driver.
  - Stores data in **memory (cache)** and **on disk** for the duration of the application.
  - Reports back results and metrics to the driver.
- **Layer 4 — Task**: The smallest unit of work — applied to a single **partition** of an RDD/DataFrame.

### 2.1.2 The Spark Engine Stack

> [!NOTE]
> Spark is a **unified analytics engine** — a single platform powers multiple workloads through distinct libraries:

| Library | Purpose | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Spark Core** | Base engine — RDDs, scheduling, memory mgmt. | Foundation of all components. |
| **Spark SQL** | Structured data via DataFrames / Datasets + SQL. | ETL, BI dashboards. |
| **Spark Streaming (DStreams)** | Micro-batch stream processing. | Real-time log analytics. |
| **Structured Streaming** | Stream processing using DataFrame API. | Event-driven pipelines. |
| **MLlib** | Distributed machine learning. | Classification, clustering, regression at scale. |
| **GraphX** | Graph-parallel computation. | Social network analysis. |

### 2.1.3 Transformations vs Actions — Lazy Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Spark uses **lazy evaluation**: transformations are *not* executed until an *action* triggers the DAG.

- **Transformations** (lazy): `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `join`, `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `union`, `distinct`. They return a **new RDD/DataFrame**.
- **Actions** (eager): `count`, `collect`, `take(n)`, `reduce`, `saveAsTextFile`, `first`, `foreach`. They trigger execution and return values to the driver or write to external storage.

> **Why Lazy Evaluation?**
> 1. **Optimization** — Spark can fuse multiple operations into a single pass (pipelining).
> 2. **Efficiency** — Avoids unnecessary computation on intermediate data.
> 3. **Fault Tolerance** — Lineage (the DAG) is preserved and can be replayed if a partition is lost.

### 2.1.4 RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset

| Feature | RDD | DataFrame | Dataset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type Safety | Compile-time | Not type-safe (rows) | Compile-time |
| Structured | No | Yes (schema) | Yes (schema) |
| Optimization | Manual | Catalyst + Tungsten | Catalyst + Tungsten |
| API Style | Functional | SQL-like | Object-oriented + functional |
| Best For | Low-level control | Structured data / analytics | Typed, object-oriented ML |

---

## 2.2 Fundamentals of Cloud Security — Threat Agent Classification

### 2.2.1 The CIA Triad (Foundation of Cloud Security)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone of all information-security frameworks:
> - **Confidentiality** — Only authorized users can read data (encryption, access control).
> - **Integrity** — Data is not modified or corrupted in transit/storage (hashing, MACs).
> - **Availability** — Services are accessible when needed (redundancy, DDoS protection).

### 2.2.2 Threat Agent Properties — The "3 I's"

A threat agent must possess all three to be considered a real-world risk:

1. **Capability** — Has the technical tools, knowledge, and resources to mount the attack.
2. **Intent** — Has the motive (financial, political, revenge, curiosity).
3. **Opportunity** — Can reach the target (network access, insider position, supply chain foothold).

> **Without any one of these, the threat agent is largely neutralized.**

### 2.2.3 The Attack Flow — How a Threat Agent Operates

> [!NOTE]
> A typical cloud attack follows a **kill chain** (modeled on **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain**):

- **Step 1 — Reconnaissance**: Threat agent scans public cloud assets, S3 buckets, DNS records, employee LinkedIn profiles.
- **Step 2 — Weaponization**: Crafts exploit, malware, or phishing payload.
- **Step 3 — Delivery**: Sends phishing email, exploits misconfigured API, or injects malicious code.
- **Step 4 — Exploitation**: Exploits vulnerability (e.g., SQL injection, unpatched CVE).
- **Step 5 — Installation**: Deploys backdoor, rootkit, or persistent cloud credential.
- **Step 6 — Command & Control (C2)**: Establishes remote control channel to exfiltrate data or expand access.
- **Step 7 — Actions on Objectives**: Data theft, ransomware, sabotage.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Apache Spark computations and performance questions, KTU commonly tests these formulas:**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of partitions in output | $P_{out} = P_{in}$ for `map`/`filter` | Wide ops like `groupByKey` cause **shuffle**. |
| Cost of shuffle | $C_{shuffle} = \alpha \cdot N_{partitions} \cdot S_{data}$ | Disk + network I/O dominates. |
| Stage boundary | A stage ends at a **shuffle dependency**. | Tasks within a stage are **pipelined**. |
| Executor memory split | $M_{total} = M_{execution} + M_{storage} + M_{overhead}$ | $M_{overhead} \approx 384$ MB default. |
| Recommended partition size | $64\,\text{MB} \le S_{part} \le 256\,\text{MB}$ | Smaller = more parallelism, more overhead. |
| Ideal parallelism | $N_{tasks} \approx 2 \cdot \text{Cores}_{\text{cluster}}$ | Avoid idle cores. |
| Speedup (Amdahl) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores. |
| Risk equation | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$=threat, $V$=vulnerability, $I$=impact. |
| Annualized Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$ = single loss expectancy, $ARO$ = annual rate of occurrence. |
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $AV$ = asset value, $EF$ = exposure factor. |

> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are used in **capacity planning** for Spark clusters (sizing executor memory, tuning partitions) and in **risk management** frameworks like **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO 27005** for cloud deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Apache Spark — Worked Performance Problem (KTU-style)

> **Problem** *(adapted from KTU 2019/2024 patterns)*: A Spark cluster has **4 worker nodes**, each with **8 CPU cores** and **16 GB RAM**. You process a **200 GB dataset** using `groupByKey` with default `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200`. Compute (a) total cluster cores, (b) recommended partition count, and (c) approximate shuffle data volume.

### Given Data
- Cluster nodes $N_{nodes} = 4$
- Cores per node $C_{node} = 8$
- RAM per node $R_{node} = 16$ GB
- Dataset size $D = 200$ GB
- Default shuffle partitions $P_{default} = 200$

### Step (a) — Total Cluster Cores

$$
C_{total} = N_{nodes} \times C_{node}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ cores}
$$

> **Interpretation**: With **32 cores**, you have 32 slots of parallel task execution.

### Step (b) — Recommended Number of Partitions

The rule of thumb is to target **2–3 tasks per core**:

$$
P_{ideal} = 2 \times C_{total} = 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ partitions}
$$

> **Alternative method** (size-based):
> 
> $$P_{size} = \left\lceil \dfrac{D}{S_{target}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{200\,\text{GB}}{128\,\text{MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1600 \right\rceil = 1600 \text{ partitions}$$
> 
> Pick the larger of the two: **1600 partitions** is recommended to keep each task manageable.

### Step (c) — Approximate Shuffle Data Volume

For `groupByKey`, every key must be shuffled to a single partition. Assume on average 50% of the data participates in the shuffle:

$$
V_{shuffle} = D \times f_{shuffle}
$$

$$
V_{shuffle} = 200\,\text{GB} \times 0.50 = 100\,\text{GB}
$$

> **Result**: ~**100 GB** must traverse the network during the shuffle phase — a key bottleneck for cluster I/O.

### Final Engineering Recommendation

| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` | $\mathbf{1600}$ |
| Executor memory | $\approx 8$ GB (50% of node RAM) |
| Executor cores | $\approx 4$ (to run 2 per node) |

---

## 3.2 Cloud Security — Risk Computation Example

> **Problem**: A cloud-hosted web application has an **asset value** of **₹50,00,000**. The probability of a successful SQL-injection attack occurring in a year is **0.4 (ARO = 0.4)**, and the exposure factor (data destroyed/exposed) is **60%**. Compute the **SLE** and **ALE**.

### Step 1 — Compute SLE

$$
SLE = AV \times EF
$$

$$
SLE = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 30{,}00{,}000
$$

> **SLE = ₹30,00,000** (loss per single incident).

### Step 2 — Compute ALE

$$
ALE = SLE \times ARO
$$

$$
ALE = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 12{,}00{,}000
$$

> **ALE = ₹12,00,000 per year**.

### Step 3 — Interpret for the Cloud Security Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the cost of deploying a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** and **input validation controls** is **less than ₹12,00,000/year**, the **control is cost-justified** — a direct application of the **NIST 800-30** risk-management methodology.

---

## 3.3 Threat Agent — Capability × Intent × Opportunity Matrix

> The total risk posed by a threat agent is the **product** of three factors. Below is a worked scoring example for a cloud SaaS platform.

| Threat Agent | Capability (1–5) | Intent (1–5) | Opportunity (1–5) | Risk Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anonymous Script Kiddie | 1 | 2 | 4 | $1 \times 2 \times 4 = \mathbf{8}$ |
| Disgruntled Insider | 4 | 5 | 5 | $4 \times 5 \times 5 = \mathbf{100}$ |
| Organized Crime Group | 5 | 5 | 3 | $5 \times 5 \times 3 = \mathbf{75}$ |
| Nation-State APT | 5 | 4 | 2 | $5 \times 4 \times 2 = \mathbf{40}$ |
| Cloud Provider Insider | 3 | 2 | 5 | $3 \times 2 \times 5 = \mathbf{30}$ |

### Final Ranking (Highest Risk First)
1. **Disgruntled Insider** (100) — highest priority for mitigation.
2. **Organized Crime Group** (75).
3. **Nation-State APT** (40).
4. **Cloud Provider Insider** (30).
5. **Anonymous Script Kiddie** (8) — still relevant but lower priority.

> **KTU Insight**: The **insider** is consistently the **highest-risk threat agent** in cloud environments because opportunity is naturally high. Mitigations: **least privilege**, **MFA**, **audit logging**, **separation of duties**, **zero-trust architecture**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apache Spark — High-Level Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT["User Application"]
        App1["Driver Program - SparkSession"]
    end

    subgraph CM["Cluster Manager Layer"]
        cm1["Standalone"]
        cm2["YARN"]
        cm3["Mesos"]
        cm4["Kubernetes"]
    end

    subgraph WORKER_NODES["Worker Nodes - Cluster"]
        subgraph W1["Worker Node 1"]
            E1["Executor 1"]
            C1["Cache Block 1"]
        end
        subgraph W2["Worker Node 2"]
            E2["Executor 2"]
            C2["Cache Block 2"]
        end
        subgraph W3["Worker Node N"]
            E3["Executor N"]
            C3["Cache Block N"]
        end
    end

    App1 -->|"Request Resources"| cm1
    App1 -->|"Submit DAG"| E1
    App1 -->|"Schedule Tasks"| E2
    App1 -->|"Monitor"| E3
    E1 -->|"Read Partition"| DS1["HDFS or S3 Storage"]
    E2 -->|"Read Partition"| DS1
    E3 -->|"Read Partition"| DS1
    E1 -->|"Return Results"| App1
    E2 -->|"Return Results"| App1
    E3 -->|"Return Results"| App1
```

**Caption**: A **driver program** (SparkSession) requests resources from a **Cluster Manager** (YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes, or Standalone), which then launches **Executors** on worker nodes. Each executor reads data partitions from distributed storage (HDFS, S3, etc.) and runs tasks in parallel. Results return to the driver.

---

## 4.2 Spark Execution Flow — DAG to Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User Code - RDD Operations"] --> B["DAG Scheduler"]
    B --> C["Stage 1 - Pipelined Transformations"]
    B --> D["Stage 2 - After Shuffle"]
    B --> E["Stage 3 - Final Transformations"]
    C --> F["Task 1.1"]
    C --> G["Task 1.2"]
    D --> H["Task 2.1"]
    D --> I["Task 2.2"]
    E --> J["Task 3.1"]
    F --> K["Action - count or collect"]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

**Caption**: Spark's **DAG Scheduler** breaks the lineage of transformations into **stages**, separated by **shuffle boundaries**. Each stage is then divided into **tasks** — one per partition — which are dispatched to executors in **pipelined** fashion.

---

## 4.3 Spark Library Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SPARK["Apache Spark Unified Engine"]
    SPARK --> CORE["Spark Core - RDD API"]
    SPARK --> SQL["Spark SQL - DataFrames"]
    SPARK --> SS["Structured Streaming"]
    SPARK --> ML["MLlib - Machine Learning"]
    SPARK --> GX["GraphX - Graph Processing"]
    CORE --> RUNTIME["Cluster Manager - YARN, Mesos, K8s"]
    SQL --> RUNTIME
    SS --> RUNTIME
    ML --> RUNTIME
    GX --> RUNTIME
```

**Caption**: The **unified Spark engine** powers batch, interactive, iterative, and streaming workloads through dedicated libraries, all sharing the same **Spark Core** and **Cluster Manager** runtime.

---

## 4.4 Cloud Security — Threat Agent Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TA["Threat Agent Categories"]
    TA --> INS["Insider Agents"]
    TA --> OUT["Outsider Agents"]
    TA --> NAT["Natural or Environmental Agents"]
    TA --> SUP["Supply Chain Agents"]

    INS --> INS1["Trusted Insider - Employee"]
    INS --> INS2["Malicious Insider - Privileged Abuse"]
    INS --> INS3["Cloud Provider Insider - Vendor Staff"]

    OUT --> OUT1["Anonymous Hacker"]
    OUT --> OUT2["Organized Crime Group"]
    OUT --> OUT3["Nation State APT"]
    OUT --> OUT4["Hacktivist"]
    OUT --> OUT5["Script Kiddie"]
    OUT --> OUT6["Malware or Botnet"]

    NAT --> NAT1["Earthquake"]
    NAT --> NAT2["Flood or Fire"]
    NAT --> NAT3["Power Outage"]

    SUP --> SUP1["Third Party Vendor Breach"]
    SUP --> SUP2["Compromised Software Update"]
    SUP --> SUP3["Backdoored Library"]

    INS1 --> ACT["Attack on Cloud Asset"]
    INS2 --> ACT
    INS3 --> ACT
    OUT1 --> ACT
    OUT2 --> ACT
    OUT3 --> ACT
    OUT4 --> ACT
    OUT5 --> ACT
    OUT6 --> ACT
    NAT1 --> ACT
    NAT2 --> ACT
    NAT3 --> ACT
    SUP1 --> ACT
    SUP2 --> ACT
    SUP3 --> ACT
```

**Caption**: Threat agents in cloud computing are categorized into **insider**, **outsider**, **natural/environmental**, and **supply chain** categories. Each category contains multiple sub-types, all of which converge on the same end goal: compromising cloud **confidentiality, integrity, or availability**.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Flow — From Threat to Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Identify Assets"] --> B["Identify Threat Agents"]
    B --> C["Identify Vulnerabilities"]
    C --> D["Compute Risk - R = T x V x I"]
    D --> E{"Risk Acceptable?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Document and Monitor"]
    E -->|No| G["Apply Controls - Encryption, MFA, IDS"]
    G --> H["Re-assess Residual Risk"]
    H --> E
```

**Caption**: This flow mirrors the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)** — assets → threat agents → vulnerabilities → risk computation → decision (mitigate, accept, transfer, avoid) → re-evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **mapped to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Bloom's Taxonomy** levels: *Remember* (L1), *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), *Analyze* (L4), *Evaluate* (L5), *Create* (L6). Marks are allocated as per the **ESE (End Semester Exam)** pattern of the 2024 regulation.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define Apache Spark. List any four of its built-in libraries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Apache Spark** *(1 mark)* is an open-source, distributed, in-memory data processing engine designed for fast, large-scale analytics, machine learning, and stream processing.
>
> **Four libraries** *(2 marks)*:
> 1. **Spark Core** — base engine, RDD API.
> 2. **Spark SQL** — DataFrames and SQL queries.
> 3. **Spark Streaming / Structured Streaming** — real-time data processing.
> 4. **MLlib** — distributed machine learning.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between a *Threat* and a *Threat Agent* with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - A **Threat** *(1 mark)* is a *potential event* that can cause harm — e.g., "unauthorized data disclosure."
> - A **Threat Agent** *(1 mark)* is the *actor or entity* that initiates the threat — e.g., an external hacker or a malicious insider.
> - **Example** *(1 mark)*: The threat is "credit-card data will be exfiltrated from a public S3 bucket"; the threat agent is the **external attacker** who exploits the misconfigured bucket policy.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024)**: For every 14-mark question, the paper-setter provides two alternatives (Q-A or Q-B). You answer **either one** fully.

---

### **Q-A. [KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14 Marks, L4 / L5 Bloom]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cluster architecture of Apache Spark**. Describe the role of the **Driver Program, Cluster Manager, and Executors** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **lazy evaluation, transformations, and actions** in Spark. How do they contribute to the efficiency of distributed processing? *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Q-A

### Part (a) — Spark Architecture *(7 marks)*

- **Block Diagram** *(2 marks)* — Draw the layered architecture:
  - **Driver Program** at the top — contains `SparkSession`, `SparkContext`, DAG Scheduler, Task Scheduler.
  - **Cluster Manager** in the middle — YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes, or Standalone — responsible for resource allocation.
  - **Executors** at the bottom — worker processes on cluster nodes running **tasks** and storing **cached data**.
  - **External Storage** (HDFS, S3) on the side — source of input data.

- **Role of Driver** *(2 marks)*: The Driver runs the user's `main()` function. It builds the **DAG** of operations, requests resources from the Cluster Manager, schedules tasks on executors, and tracks their progress. It holds the **SparkContext** — the entry point to all Spark functionality.

- **Role of Cluster Manager** *(1.5 marks)*: Allocates CPU and memory to executors. Manages multiple Spark applications running concurrently.

- **Role of Executors** *(1.5 marks)*: Each executor is a JVM process launched on a worker node. It runs individual **tasks** (one per partition), stores RDD partitions in memory or on disk, and reports status back to the driver.

### Part (b) — Lazy Evaluation, Transformations, Actions *(7 marks)*

- **Transformations** *(2 marks)*: Lazy operations that return a *new* RDD/DataFrame without computing immediately. Examples: `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `join`, `groupByKey`. They build the **lineage** (DAG) of the computation.

- **Actions** *(2 marks)*: Eager operations that *trigger* the DAG and return a value to the driver or write to storage. Examples: `count`, `collect`, `take`, `reduce`, `saveAsTextFile`.

- **Lazy Evaluation** *(1.5 marks)*: Transformations are queued and *not executed* until an action is called. The DAG scheduler then optimizes and fuses operations.

- **Contribution to Efficiency** *(1.5 marks)*:
  1. **Pipelining** — multiple narrow transformations run in a single pass.
  2. **Optimization** — Catalyst optimizer rewrites the query plan.
  3. **Reduced I/O** — avoids materializing intermediate results.
  4. **Fault tolerance** — lineage graph can replay lost partitions.

---

### **Q-B. [KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14 Marks, L4 / L5 Bloom]**

**(a)** Define **Threat Agent** in the context of cloud security. Classify and explain the **major categories of threat agents** with at least two examples for each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **CIA Triad**. Discuss how **insider threat agents** exploit weaknesses in each of the three security goals, with cloud-specific examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Q-B

### Part (a) — Threat Agent Definition & Classification *(7 marks)*

- **Definition** *(2 marks)*: A **Threat Agent** is any entity — human, software, or environmental — that has the **capability, intent, and opportunity** to cause harm to an information system. They are the *actors* behind threats (per **NIST SP 800-30**).

- **Major Categories** *(5 marks)*:

  1. **Insider Agents** *(1.5 marks)*:
     - *Trusted insider*: employee misusing access unintentionally (e.g., accidentally exposing S3 bucket).
     - *Malicious insider*: contractor stealing source code.

  2. **Outsider Agents** *(1.5 marks)*:
     - *Anonymous hacker*: exploiting open SSH port on a VM.
     - *Organized crime group*: deploying ransomware against a SaaS provider.

  3. **Natural / Environmental Agents** *(1 mark)*:
     - Earthquake damaging a cloud datacenter region.
     - Power outage disrupting availability.

  4. **Supply Chain / Third-Party Agents** *(1 mark)*:
     - Compromised software update injecting a backdoor.
     - Vulnerable open-source library used in the application.

### Part (b) — CIA Triad & Insider Threat *(7 marks)*

- **CIA Triad Definition** *(2 marks)*: The three foundational security goals:
  - **C**onfidentiality — preventing unauthorized disclosure.
  - **I**ntegrity — preventing unauthorized modification.
  - **A**vailability — ensuring timely access.

- **Insider Threat Against Confidentiality** *(1.5 marks)*: A privileged cloud admin **reads customer data** they should not have access to — violating *confidentiality*. *Example*: AWS admin exfiltrating customer records.

- **Insider Threat Against Integrity** *(1.5 marks)*: A developer **modifies production code** to plant a backdoor — violating *integrity*. *Example*: injecting malicious update into a CI/CD pipeline.

- **Insider Threat Against Availability** *(1.5 marks)*: A disgruntled engineer **deletes production databases** — violating *availability*. *Example*: revoking encryption keys to lock out the company.

- **Mitigations** *(0.5 mark — bonus)*: Principle of least privilege, MFA, audit logging, separation of duties, zero-trust.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Mixing up *Threat* and *Threat Agent*** — these are *not* the same. Threats are *events*; threat agents are *actors*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Listing Spark libraries without explaining their purpose** — merely naming MLlib/Spark SQL is *not enough*; you must state what each does.
> 3. **Forgetting the Cluster Manager role** — a frequent omission; the CM is the *resource negotiator*, not a mere *scheduler*.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — every 7-mark sub-part in Part B should have a **neat labeled diagram** to score full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring lazy evaluation** — when asked about Spark efficiency, *always* explain **pipelining** and **DAG optimization**.
> 6. **No real-world examples** — for threat agent questions, generic answers score low. Tie every answer to a **cloud-specific scenario** (S3, IAM, hypervisor, multi-tenancy).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Apache Spark — Rapid Revision Checklist
- ✅ Spark is an **in-memory distributed processing engine** — **100x faster** than MapReduce for iterative jobs.
- ✅ Architecture: **Driver → Cluster Manager → Executors → Tasks**.
- ✅ Supports **YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes, and Standalone** as cluster managers.
- ✅ **RDD = Resilient Distributed Dataset** — immutable, partitioned, lineage-tracked.
- ✅ **DataFrames** (schema) and **Datasets** (typed) build on RDDs with **Catalyst optimizer** and **Tungsten execution engine**.
- ✅ Spark stack = **Core, SQL, Streaming, Structured Streaming, MLlib, GraphX**.
- ✅ **Transformations are lazy**; **actions trigger** execution.
- ✅ **Stages** are separated by **shuffle boundaries**; tasks within a stage are **pipelined**.
- ✅ Recommended partition size: **64 MB – 256 MB**; target **2 tasks per core**.
- ✅ Speedup follows **Amdahl's Law**: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$.

### 🔐 Cloud Security — Threat Agents Rapid Revision Checklist
- ✅ **Threat** = possible harmful event; **Threat Agent** = actor behind it.
- ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **Risk equation**: $R = T \times V \times I$.
- ✅ **ALE** = SLE × ARO; **SLE** = AV × EF.
- ✅ Threat agent categories: **Insider, Outsider, Natural/Environmental, Supply Chain**.
- ✅ Threat agents need **Capability + Intent + Opportunity**.
- ✅ Insiders are typically the **highest-risk** agents due to inherent opportunity.
- ✅ Countermeasures: **encryption, MFA, IDS, zero-trust, least privilege, audit logging**.
- ✅ Follow the **NIST RMF** / **ISO 27005** risk-management lifecycle.
- ✅ Always tie threat agent examples to **cloud-specific assets** (S3, IAM, VM, hypervisor, API).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Security Threat/Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Security Threat/Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cloud Security Threats/Risks** are defined as the set of vulnerabilities, malicious events, attack vectors, and operational hazards that compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of data, applications, and services hosted within a cloud computing environment. According to the **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** and the **NIST SP 500-292 Reference Architecture**, these threats span across service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community), and virtualization layers (Hypervisor, VM, Application).

A **Threat** is a potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system or organization, while a **Risk** is the likelihood of that threat being exploited multiplied by its potential impact.

$$Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student must be able to identify, classify, and propose mitigation strategies for at least **seven major cloud security threats** as documented by CSA, along with understanding the architectural foundation of **Trusted Cloud Computing (TCC)** and its **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Shared Apartment Building

Imagine cloud computing as a **giant high-rise apartment building**:

- **The Building** = The Cloud Data Center
- **Apartments** = Virtual Machines (VMs) rented by different tenants
- **The Building Manager** = The Cloud Service Provider (CSP) — e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP
- **The Building's Lock System** = Identity & Access Management (IAM)
- **The Building's CCTV & Security Guards** = Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Security Operations
- **The Shared Plumbing/Electricity** = Shared hypervisor, shared network, shared storage
- **You (the tenant)** = The cloud customer / organization using the service

### Where Threats Hide in This Analogy:

| Apartment Building Element | Cloud Computing Equivalent | Associated Threat |
|---|---|---|
| Master key copied illegally | Stolen admin credentials | **Account Hijacking / Service Traffic Hijacking** |
| Tenant peeks into neighbor's window | Hypervisor escape | **VM Escape / Multi-tenancy breach** |
| Plumbing burst affects all floors | Shared infrastructure failure | **Shared Technology Vulnerabilities** |
| Disgruntled building manager | Rogue CSP employee | **Malicious Insider** |
| Building gets flooded | DDoS attack | **Denial of Service** |
| Theft from apartment while tenant is away | Insecure APIs | **Insecure API & Interfaces** |
| Mailbox tampering | Data in transit interception | **Data Breach / Data Loss** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** When you move from owning your own house (on-premise servers) to renting an apartment (cloud), you give up some *physical control* but gain *shared responsibility*. The CSP secures the building's structure; **YOU** are still responsible for locking your own door, vetting your guests, and protecting your valuables.

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Cloud Security

Every cloud threat ultimately attacks one of these three pillars:

1. **Confidentiality** → Data should only be accessible to authorized entities. *(Threat: Data Breach)*
2. **Integrity** → Data should not be altered or tampered with in an unauthorized manner. *(Threat: Data Loss/Corruption)*
3. **Availability** → Services must be accessible when needed. *(Threat: DoS/DDoS)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary Security Properties** (often asked in KTU exams as 2-mark questions): **Authenticity, Non-repudiation, Accountability, and Privacy.** Always remember these as the *extended CIA+* properties of modern cloud security.

## 1.4 The Shared Responsibility Model

A critical KTU concept: security in the cloud is **NOT solely the CSP's job**. The **Shared Responsibility Model** divides duties:

| Layer | AWS | Azure | GCP | Customer Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Data Center | CSP | CSP | CSP | None |
| Network Infrastructure | CSP | CSP | CSP | None |
| Hypervisor | CSP | CSP | CSP | None |
| Operating System | CSP* | CSP* | CSP* | Customer (for IaaS) |
| Application | Customer | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| Data & Access | Customer | Customer | Customer | Customer |

*Note: For PaaS and SaaS, the CSP handles more layers; for IaaS, the customer takes on OS-level responsibility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CSA Top Cloud Security Threats (KTU High-Priority — Erasiadne-E9 List)

The **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** publishes the industry-standard "**Treacherous 12**" / "**Top Threats to Cloud Computing**." KTU examiners frequently test these directly.

### Threat 1: Data Breaches
A **data breach** is an incident where confidential, sensitive, or protected information is accessed or disclosed without authorization. It can be the *most damaging* type of threat because of the **irreversibility** — once data is leaked, you cannot "un-leak" it.

**Common Causes:**
- Weak credentials, misconfigured databases (e.g., open S3 buckets)
- Insider threats, application vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS)
- Lost devices, phishing attacks

**Impact:** Financial loss, regulatory fines (GDPR, HIPAA), reputational damage.

### Threat 2: Data Loss
Permanent loss of data due to:
- Accidental deletion by CSP or customer
- Malicious deletion (ransomware)
- Natural disasters destroying data center
- Loss of encryption keys

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Data loss is *different* from data breach. In breach, data is *copied*; in loss, data is *destroyed/unavailable*.

### Threat 3: Account Hijacking (Service Traffic Hijacking)
Attackers steal **credentials** (username/password, API keys, SSH keys) to:
- Impersonate legitimate users
- Manipulate data
- Redirect clients to malicious sites
- Inject malicious code into cloud services

**Common Methods:** Phishing, password reuse attacks, credential stuffing, exploiting session management flaws.

### Threat 4: Insecure APIs & Interfaces
Cloud services expose **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)** for customer interaction. If these APIs are insecure, attackers can:
- Bypass authentication
- Manipulate data
- Cause service disruption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2019 University Exam Favorite:** "Explain why APIs are the weakest link in cloud security." — Always mention: *APIs are public-facing, externally accessible, and often used by third-party developers with minimal security awareness.*

### Threat 5: Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed DoS (DDoS)
Attackers overwhelm cloud resources (CPU, memory, bandwidth) making services unavailable to legitimate users.

**Types:**
- **Volumetric attacks** (UDP flood, ICMP flood)
- **Protocol attacks** (SYN flood, Ping of Death)
- **Application-layer attacks** (HTTP flood, Slowloris)

**Cloud-specific concern:** Multi-tenancy means a DoS in one tenant's VM can affect others (the "**Noisy Neighbor**" problem).

### Threat 6: Malicious Insider
A current or former employee, contractor, or business partner with authorized access who intentionally misuses that access. The **2019 CSA report** named this as the #1 threat for some years.

**Why is it dangerous in cloud?**
- Insider has legitimate access (bypasses perimeter defenses)
- Cloud environments have admin-level access for many employees
- Difficult to detect with traditional security tools

### Threat 7: Abuse of Cloud Services
Attackers use legitimate cloud resources for malicious purposes:
- **Cryptojacking** (mining crypto using stolen cloud accounts)
- **Spam/phishing hosting**
- **DDoS-for-hire** botnets
- **Brute-force cracking**

### Threat 8: Insufficient Due Diligence
Organizations move to cloud without fully understanding:
- CSP's security posture
- Legal/compliance requirements
- Architectural differences from on-premise
- SLAs (Service Level Agreements)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse "Due Diligence" with "Due Care." **Due Diligence = pre-migration assessment**. **Due Care = ongoing security practices after migration.**

### Threat 9: Shared Technology Vulnerabilities
Cloud providers share infrastructure (hypervisor, shared storage, shared network). A vulnerability in *one shared component* compromises *all tenants*.

**Example:** Hypervisor escape — VM breaks out of its sandbox and accesses the host or other VMs.

### Threat 10-12 (Additional CSA Threats)
- **Weak Identity, Credential and Access Management**
- **Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)**
- **System and Application Vulnerabilities**

## 2.2 Trusted Cloud Computing (TCC) — KTU 2024 Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Weightage Topic:** Trusted Cloud Computing is the **architectural framework** proposed to mitigate cloud security threats. Expect 7-mark questions.

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Trusted Cloud Computing (TCC)** is a security paradigm that combines the principles of **Trusted Computing (TC)** with cloud architectures to provide verifiable, end-to-end security guarantees across all cloud layers (hardware, hypervisor, OS, application, and data).

### 2.2.2 Trusted Computing Base (TCB)

The **TCB** is the set of all hardware, software, and firmware components that are *critical* to the security of a system. If any component in the TCB is compromised, the entire system's security is compromised.

**TCB Components in a Cloud Environment:**

| Layer | TCB Element | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | **TPM (Trusted Platform Module)** | Hardware root of trust, key storage |
| Hardware | **Secure Enclave (Intel SGX)** | Isolated execution environment |
| Virtualization | **Hypervisor / VMM** | VM isolation, resource allocation |
| OS | **Secured Guest OS** | OS-level enforcement |
| Application | **Trusted Application** | Application-level security |
| Network | **TLS/SSL, VPN** | Secure communication |
| Data | **Encryption, DLP** | Data protection |

### 2.2.3 The TCG (Trusted Computing Group) Chain of Trust

A core TCC concept is the **Chain of Trust**, a sequence of verified boot processes:

$$Power\ On \rightarrow BIOS\ (Verified) \rightarrow Bootloader\ (Verified) \rightarrow OS\ Kernel\ (Verified) \rightarrow Hypervisor\ (Verified) \rightarrow VMs\ (Verified) \rightarrow Applications\ (Verified)$$

Each stage **cryptographically hashes** the next stage and verifies it against a known-good value stored in the **TPM**. If any verification fails, the system **halts the boot process** (secure boot).

### 2.2.4 Reference Model for Trusted Cloud Computing

The standard TCC reference model (proposed in academic literature, often referenced in KTU) has the following components:

1. **Cloud Customer (Tenant)**
2. **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)**
3. **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** — Auditor/Certifier
4. **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** — Inside the CSP infrastructure
5. **Trusted Software Stack (TSS)** — Interfaces with TPM
6. **Remote Attestation Service** — Verifies trust claims

## 2.3 Identity and Access Management (IAM)

IAM is the *first line of defense* against most cloud threats. It includes:

- **Authentication** (Who are you?) — MFA, biometrics, certificates
- **Authorization** (What can you do?) — RBAC, ABAC, least privilege
- **Accounting** (What did you do?) — Audit logs, monitoring
- **Identity Federation** (SAML, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect)
- **Single Sign-On (SSO)**

## 2.4 Encryption in Cloud

| Encryption Type | Use Case | Key Managed By |
|---|---|---|
| **Data-at-Rest Encryption** | Stored data (databases, storage) | CSP or Customer |
| **Data-in-Transit Encryption** | Network transmission (TLS/SSL) | Application |
| **Data-in-Use Encryption** | Active processing (Homomorphic, SGX) | Hardware |
| **Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)** | Customer controls keys | Customer |
| **Hold Your Own Key (HYOK)** | Customer fully controls keys | Customer |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Qualitative or quantitative |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annual Rate of Occurrence |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Monetary value |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \frac{(ALE \times Mitigation\ \%) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Percentage or ratio |
| **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** | $MTTF = \frac{\sum Time\ of\ Operation}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Hours |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum Downtime}{Number\ of\ Repairs}$ | Hours |
| **Availability** | $Availability = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\ \%$ | Percentage (e.g., 99.99% = "four nines") |
| **Hash Function Property** | $H(M) \rightarrow fixed\ length\ digest$ | SHA-256 = 256-bit output |
| **DDoS Mitigation** | $Capacity_{required} \geq Peak_{legitimate} + Attack_{volume}$ | Bandwidth/Throughput |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The formulas $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ and $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ are asked almost every year in 3-mark questions. Memorize them with the variable definitions.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud security risk analysis is used in:
- **Banking & Finance:** PCI-DSS compliance, transaction integrity
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA compliance, EHR protection
- **Government:** FedRAMP, data sovereignty
- **E-commerce:** Payment data security (data breaches cost millions)
- **IoT/Edge:** Securing massive device fleets
- **DevSecOps:** Integrating security into CI/CD pipelines

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Threat vs. Mitigation Strategy

The following exhaustive matrix maps each major cloud threat to its **attack vector**, **impact**, and **mitigation strategy**. This is a KTU high-yield tabular answer worth up to 7 marks.

| # | Threat | Attack Vector | CIA Pillar Affected | Impact | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Breach** | SQLi, misconfig, stolen creds | Confidentiality | Data exposure, GDPR fines, reputational loss | Encryption (AES-256), DLP, IAM, network segmentation, encryption at rest & in transit |
| 2 | **Data Loss** | Accidental deletion, ransomware, hardware failure | Availability + Integrity | Permanent data unavailability, business halt | Backup (3-2-1 rule), disaster recovery, multi-region replication, snapshotting |
| 3 | **Account Hijacking** | Phishing, credential stuffing, session theft | Confidentiality + Integrity + Authenticity | Full account takeover, data manipulation | MFA, RBAC, short-lived tokens, session management, credential rotation |
| 4 | **Insecure APIs** | Poor auth, missing rate limiting, broken object-level auth | All three | Full system compromise | OAuth 2.0, API gateways, rate limiting, input validation, OWASP API Top 10 |
| 5 | **DDoS/DoS** | Flood attacks, slowloris, protocol attacks | Availability | Service downtime, revenue loss | CDN (Cloudflare, Akamai), auto-scaling, WAF, rate limiting, scrubbing centers |
| 6 | **Malicious Insider** | Authorized access misuse | All three | Data theft, sabotage, fraud | Background checks, least privilege, audit logging, separation of duties, UEBA |
| 7 | **Abuse of Cloud** | Crypto mining, spam hosting, botnets | Integrity + Availability | Resource exhaustion, cost spike, account suspension | Anomaly detection, behavioral analytics, CSP abuse reporting, billing alerts |
| 8 | **Insufficient Due Diligence** | Lack of assessment pre-migration | All three | Regulatory non-compliance, data leakage | Thorough CSP evaluation, SLA review, compliance mapping (ISO 27001, SOC 2) |
| 9 | **Shared Technology Vuln** | Hypervisor escape, container breakout | All three (multi-tenant impact) | Cross-tenant compromise | Patch management, hypervisor hardening, container isolation, micro-segmentation |
| 10 | **Weak IAM** | Weak passwords, no MFA, privilege creep | Confidentiality | Unauthorized access | MFA, RBAC, ABAC, just-in-time access, privileged access management (PAM) |
| 11 | **APTs** | Long-term, multi-stage attacks | All three | Espionage, intellectual property theft | Zero Trust Architecture, EDR, threat intelligence, network segmentation |
| 12 | **System Vulnerabilities** | Unpatched OS, zero-days | All three | System compromise | Patch management, vulnerability scanning, intrusion prevention systems (IPS) |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Risk Quantification for Cloud Assets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Numerical risk calculation problems appear in Part B (14 marks). Below is a complete, exhaustive solution methodology.

### Problem Setup (Sample KTU Pattern):
> *"An organization is considering migrating its customer database to the cloud. The database has an asset value of ₹50,00,000. The exposure factor is estimated at 60%. The threat is expected to occur 0.5 times per year. Calculate SLE, ALE, and recommend a control costing ₹1,50,000 per year that mitigates the risk by 80%. Is the investment justified?"*

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**

$$Asset\ Value\ (AV) = \text{₹}50,00,000$$

$$Exposure\ Factor\ (EF) = 60\% = 0.60$$

$$Annual\ Rate\ of\ Occurrence\ (ARO) = 0.5\ per\ year$$

$$Cost\ of\ Control\ (CoC) = \text{₹}1,50,000\ per\ year$$

$$Mitigation\ Percentage\ (MP) = 80\% = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

The SLE represents the monetary loss expected from a *single occurrence* of the threat.

$$SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$$

$$SLE = 50,00,000 \times 0.60$$

$$SLE = 30,00,000$$

> Therefore, if the threat occurs once, the expected monetary loss is **₹30,00,000**.

**Step 3: Calculate the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

The ALE represents the *annualized* expected loss, accounting for the frequency of the threat.

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE = 30,00,000 \times 0.5$$

$$ALE = 15,00,000$$

> Therefore, without any control, the organization expects to lose **₹15,00,000 per year** to this threat.

**Step 4: Calculate the mitigated ALE after applying the control.**

$$ALE_{mitigated} = ALE \times (1 - MP)$$

$$ALE_{mitigated} = 15,00,000 \times (1 - 0.80)$$

$$ALE_{mitigated} = 15,00,000 \times 0.20$$

$$ALE_{mitigated} = 3,00,000$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Annual Savings from the control.**

$$Savings = ALE - ALE_{mitigated}$$

$$Savings = 15,00,000 - 3,00,000$$

$$Savings = 12,00,000$$

**Step 6: Calculate the Net Benefit of the control.**

$$Net\ Benefit = Savings - Cost\ of\ Control$$

$$Net\ Benefit = 12,00,000 - 1,50,000$$

$$Net\ Benefit = 10,50,000$$

**Step 7: Calculate the Return on Security Investment (ROSI).**

$$ROSI = \frac{(ALE \times MP) - CoC}{CoC}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{(15,00,000 \times 0.80) - 1,50,000}{1,50,000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{12,00,000 - 1,50,000}{1,50,000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{10,50,000}{1,50,000}$$

$$ROSI = 7.0 = 700\%$$

**Step 8: Decision and Interpretation.**

Since $Net\ Benefit > 0$ (specifically ₹10,50,000) and $ROSI = 700\%$, the security control is **highly justified**. For every ₹1 invested, the organization saves ₹7 in expected losses. The investment is strongly recommended.

### 3.3 Derivation: Availability Calculation for Cloud SLAs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Question:** "An AWS EC2 instance has an MTTF of 8760 hours and MTTR of 4 hours. Calculate the availability percentage and downtime per year."

**Step 1: Recall the availability formula.**

$$Availability = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$Availability = \frac{8760}{8760 + 4}$$

$$Availability = \frac{8760}{8764}$$

$$Availability = 0.9995435$$

$$Availability \approx 99.954\%$$

**Step 3: Convert to "nines" notation for SLA comparison.**

$$99.954\% \approx 99.95\% = "Three\ and\ a\ half\ nines"$$

> *Note: This is below the typical cloud SLA of 99.99% ("four nines").*

**Step 4: Calculate the annual downtime.**

$$Downtime\ per\ year = (1 - Availability) \times Hours\ in\ a\ year$$

$$Downtime = (1 - 0.9995435) \times 8760$$

$$Downtime = 0.0004565 \times 8760$$

$$Downtime \approx 4.0\ hours\ per\ year$$

> This matches the MTTR (as expected for a single-failure system). For systems with $n$ redundant components, MTTF increases proportionally.

### 3.4 Derivation: Chain of Trust Boot Verification

The Chain of Trust uses **hash chaining**. Let the system boot through $n$ stages. Each stage $i$ verifies stage $i+1$.

**Step 1:** TPM stores a known-good hash of the BIOS: $H_{expected}(BIOS)$.

**Step 2:** On boot, compute actual hash: $H_{actual}(BIOS) = SHA256(BIOS\ code)$.

**Step 3:** Verification:
$$If\ H_{actual}(BIOS) = H_{expected}(BIOS) \Rightarrow BIOS\ is\ trusted, \Rightarrow continue\ to\ next\ stage$$
$$If\ H_{actual}(BIOS) \neq H_{expected}(BIOS) \Rightarrow halt\ boot, \Rightarrow raise\ alert$$

**Step 4:** The chain continues:

$$BIOS \rightarrow Bootloader \rightarrow OS\ Kernel \rightarrow Hypervisor \rightarrow Guest\ VM$$

Each link in the chain is verified cryptographically using a **digital signature** (typically RSA-2048 or ECDSA).

**Why is this secure?**
- The **TPM hardware** stores the root of trust — it cannot be tampered with by software alone.
- **Attestation** allows a remote party to verify the trust state of the system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Cloud Security Threat Classification (Hierarchical Mind Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Cloud Security Threats and Risks]
    
    rootA --> threatB1[Data Related Threats]
    rootA --> threatB2[Identity and Access Threats]
    rootA --> threatB3[Infrastructure Threats]
    rootA --> threatB4[Service and Operational Threats]
    rootA --> threatB5[Human and Organizational Threats]
    
    threatB1 --> leafC1[Data Breach]
    threatB1 --> leafC2[Data Loss]
    threatB1 --> leafC3[Data Leakage]
    threatB1 --> leafC4[Insecure Data Deletion]
    
    threatB2 --> leafC5[Account Hijacking]
    threatB2 --> leafC6[Weak IAM Policies]
    threatB2 --> leafC7[Credential Theft]
    threatB2 --> leafC8[Insufficient Authentication]
    
    threatB3 --> leafC9[Hypervisor Escape]
    threatB3 --> leafC10[Shared Technology Vulns]
    threatB3 --> leafC11[VM Hopping]
    threatB3 --> leafC12[Noisy Neighbor]
    
    threatB4 --> leafC13[DoS and DDoS]
    threatB4 --> leafC14[Insecure APIs]
    threatB4 --> leafC15[Service Manipulation]
    threatB4 --> leafC16[Advanced Persistent Threats]
    
    threatB5 --> leafC17[Malicious Insider]
    threatB5 --> leafC18[Abuse of Cloud Services]
    threatB5 --> leafC19[Insufficient Due Diligence]
    threatB5 --> leafC20[Social Engineering]
```
