- **Confidential Computing Insight:** Even with full Management OS compromise, **SEV-SNP and TDX** prevent the attacker from reading customer memory because the encryption keys are bound to the hardware, not the host OS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrastructure Security - Network Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Infrastructure Security - Network Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Infrastructure Security at the Network Level** refers to the systematic deployment of hardware- and software-based controls that protect the data path between cloud tenants, virtual machines, hypervisors, and the public Internet. It operates primarily at **OSI Layers 2–4** (Data Link, Network, and Transport) and encompasses firewall rule sets, intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS), Virtual Private Clouds (VPC), network access control lists (NACLs), Virtual Private Networks (VPNs), Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) mitigation, and encrypted transport channels (TLS/IPsec).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, network-level security is the **first defensive perimeter** of the Cloud Shared Responsibility Model — the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) secures the *underlying* physical and virtual network, while the tenant secures *what runs on top* (applications, OS, identity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEET637 – Module 4):** Network-level security is the foundational layer of the **Cloud Security Stack**. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE) questions, as questions on firewalls, IDS/IPS, and VPCs appear almost every semester.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Guarded Gated Community"

Imagine your cloud infrastructure as a **large residential gated community**:

| Cloud Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| VPC / Subnet | A gated community with internal streets |
| Security Group | Door-lock of an individual house (instance-level) |
| NACL | The guardhouse gate at the street entrance (subnet-level) |
| Firewall | A checkpoint that inspects every car (packet) entering |
| IDS/IPS | CCTV cameras + armed guards patrolling the streets |
| VPN | An encrypted underground tunnel from your home to the community |
| DDoS Protection | A traffic police system that reroutes floods of suspicious vehicles |
| TLS/SSL | A sealed, tamper-proof envelope around every letter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Just as a gated community has *multiple* layers of defense (compound wall, gate, door lock, CCTV), cloud networks must use **defense-in-depth** — no single control is sufficient.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards (Bolded for Recall)

- **Default MTU for cloud Ethernet:** **1500 bytes**
- **Standard TLS version in production:** **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446)**
- **IPsec tunnel mode vs. transport mode:** Tunnel = encrypts entire IP packet; Transport = encrypts payload only
- **Three-Authentication Factor model:** **Something you know, have, are**
- **NIST SP 800-53 control families used:** **SC (System and Communications Protection), AC (Access Control)**
- **Common attack surface metric — CVSS:** **0.0 – 10.0 severity score**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Network-Level Cloud Security

### Pillar 1 — Perimeter Defense (Firewalls & NACLs)
A firewall filters traffic based on a **5-tuple**: `⟨Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol⟩`. In cloud platforms, this manifests as:

- **Security Groups (SGs):** Stateful, instance-level virtual firewalls (e.g., AWS SG, Azure NSG rules at NIC level).
- **Network Access Control Lists (NACLs):** Stateless, subnet-level rule lists evaluated in numerical order.

**Stateful vs. Stateless — The Key Distinction:**
- *Stateful* = remembers the connection (return traffic is auto-allowed).
- *Stateless* = each packet evaluated independently (return traffic needs explicit rules).

### Pillar 2 — Intrusion Detection & Prevention (IDS/IPS)
- **IDS (Intrusion Detection System):** Passive monitor; raises alerts on suspicious patterns. Placed *inline-out-of-band*.
- **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System):** Active inline device; drops or blocks malicious traffic in real-time.

Detection methodologies:
1. **Signature-based (Misuse Detection):** Compares packet payload against a database of known attack signatures. *Low false-positive, high false-negative on zero-days.*
2. **Anomaly-based:** Builds a baseline of "normal" traffic; flags statistical deviations. *High false-positive, catches zero-days.*
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., TCP three-way handshake).

### Pillar 3 — Encryption in Transit (VPN, TLS, IPsec)
- **IPsec** = Layer 3 (Network) encryption; uses **AH** (Authentication Header) and **ESP** (Encapsulating Security Payload).
- **TLS** = Layer 4–7 (Transport) encryption; runs over TCP port 443 in HTTPS.
- **VPN Types:**
  - *Site-to-Site VPN* — Connects on-premise datacenter to cloud VPC.
  - *Remote Access VPN* — Individual user → cloud.
  - *SSL/TLS VPN* — Browser-based, no client software.

### Pillar 4 — DDoS Mitigation
**DDoS attack types** map to OSI layers:
- **Volumetric (L3/L4):** UDP flood, ICMP flood — measured in **Gbps**.
- **Protocol (L4):** SYN flood, Ping of Death — measured in **Mpps**.
- **Application (L7):** HTTP GET/POST flood — measured in **RPS**.

Cloud DDoS services use **anycast routing**, **rate limiting**, and **scrubbing centers** to absorb attacks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary Conditions | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Firewall rule evaluation | First-match wins; rule order = priority | Stateless NACLs require both ingress + egress rules | Tuple count = 5 |
| TCP 3-way handshake (SYN flood defense) | $SYN \to SYN\text{-}ACK \to ACK$ | Half-open connections cap = backlog queue size | Bytes ≈ 0 (control only) |
| IPsec ESP overhead | $Payload_{wire} = Payload_{app} + 50$ to $70$ bytes | Excludes outer IP header in tunnel mode | Bytes |
| TLS record overhead | $Ciphertext = Plaintext + 16 + MAC$ (TLS 1.2) | TLS 1.3 reduces to ≈ $Plaintext + 16$ | Bytes |
| Shannon entropy (anomaly detection) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $0 \le H(X) \le \log_2 n$ | Bits/symbol |
| DDoS attack intensity | $I_{attack} = \frac{Packets_{received}}{Packets_{legit}}$ | Threshold $I > 1.0$ triggers scrubbing | Dimensionless ratio |
| Subnet mask to hosts | $Hosts = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = host bits; subtract 2 for network + broadcast | Count |
| Bandwidth-delay product | $BDP = Bandwidth \times RTT$ | Determines optimal TCP window | Bits |
| CVSS severity range | $Score = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Score range: **0.0 – 10.0** | Numeric |
| TLS 1.3 cipher suite | $AEAD$ mandatory (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) | RC4, MD5, SHA-1 forbidden | Bits = 128/256 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Network-level security controls are the *only* mechanisms that scale to handle **multi-Tbps** attack volumes because they operate at line-rate in hardware ASICs (e.g., AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection). Application-level controls cannot match this throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Selecting Network Controls

The following exhaustive decision tree is what KTU examiners expect when a question asks *"Choose the appropriate network-level security control for scenario X"*:

**Step 1 — Identify the threat actor:**
- External attacker → requires perimeter defense (Firewall, WAF, DDoS).
- Insider threat → requires segmentation (VPC, NACLs, Zero Trust).
- Compromised workload → requires East-West traffic inspection (micro-segmentation).

**Step 2 — Identify the OSI layer of the threat:**
- Layer 3/4 flood → DDoS scrubbing + rate limiting.
- Layer 7 manipulation → WAF + TLS termination.
- Lateral movement → Network segmentation + IDS/IPS.

**Step 3 — Map to control:**
$$Control_{selected} = f(Threat_{type}, Layer_{OSI}, Tenant_{responsibility})$$

**Step 4 — Verify with defense-in-depth principle:**
$$\text{Score}_{depth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i$$
where $C_i$ = control present (1/0), $w_i$ = weight of control $i$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Shannon Entropy for Anomaly Detection

We derive the threshold for flagging a network traffic stream as anomalous based on Shannon entropy. This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that frequently appears in ESE Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** Anomaly-based IDS engines (e.g., those using entropy to detect DDoS) operate on the principle that legitimate traffic has *high, predictable entropy* in payload distribution, whereas attack traffic (e.g., UDP flood with constant payloads) has *low entropy*.

**Step 1 — Define the probability distribution of observed byte values in a packet stream window.**

Let $X$ be a discrete random variable representing byte values $x_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, 255\}$ in a sliding window of $N$ packets. The probability of byte value $x_i$ is:
$$p(x_i) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i)}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shannon entropy formula:**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

*Conversion logic:* We sum the negative log-probability of each byte value, weighted by its probability. Bytes that appear frequently contribute little to entropy; rare bytes contribute more.

**Step 3 — Establish the boundary conditions:**

The maximum possible entropy occurs when all 256 byte values are equally likely:
$$H_{max} = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \log_2 \frac{1}{256} = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits}$$

The minimum is when only one byte value appears:
$$H_{min} = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 - \dots = 0 \text{ bits}$$

Therefore:
$$0 \le H(X) \le 8$$

**Step 4 — Define the anomaly threshold.**

For legitimate HTTPS traffic, empirical studies show $H(X) \in [5.5, 7.5]$. A DDoS flood with constant payload has $H(X) < 1.0$. Define threshold:
$$\theta = 4.0 \text{ bits (typical cloud IDS setting)}$$

Anomaly flag:
$$A = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } H(X) < \theta \quad (\text{flag as attack}) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Combine with packet rate for robust detection.**

$$S_{anomaly} = w_1 \cdot \frac{H(X) - \theta}{H_{max} - \theta} + w_2 \cdot \frac{R_{observed} - R_{baseline}}{R_{baseline}}$$

where $R$ = packet rate, $w_1 + w_2 = 1$.

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example.**

Consider a 1000-packet window. 950 packets contain the byte value `0x00`; the remaining 50 are uniformly distributed across other values.

$$p(0x00) = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95$$

For each of the other 255 byte values:
$$p(x_i) = \frac{50/255}{1000} \approx 0.000196$$

Compute entropy:
$$H(X) = -0.95 \log_2 0.95 - \sum_{i=1}^{255} 0.000196 \log_2 0.000196$$

$$H(X) = -0.95 \cdot (-0.074) - 255 \cdot 0.000196 \cdot (-12.32)$$

$$H(X) = 0.0703 + 0.6157 \approx 0.686 \text{ bits}$$

Since $0.686 < \theta = 4.0$, the IDS flags this as **anomalous**. This is consistent with a SYN flood or UDP flood attack.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stateful vs. Stateless Comparison

| Parameter | Stateful Firewall | Stateless Firewall (NACL) |
|---|---|---|
| Rule set size | Typically smaller (auto return) | Larger (explicit return rules) |
| Performance | Lower throughput (~10–20% overhead) | Higher throughput |
| OSI layer focus | L3, L4, L7 (with DPI) | L3, L4 only |
| Connection tracking | Yes — uses state table | No |
| Cloud example | AWS Security Group, Azure NSG | AWS NACL |
| Return traffic handling | Automatic | Must be explicitly allowed |
| Use case | Instance-level protection | Subnet-level coarse filtering |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Network-Level Security Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Internet / External User]:::src
    B[DDoS Protection Layer / Anycast Scrubbing]:::perim
    C[Edge Firewall / WAF Layer 7]:::perim
    D[TLS Termination / IPsec VPN Gateway]:::encrypt
    E[VPC Router / Virtual Private Cloud Boundary]:::vpc
    F[Subnet NACL Stateless Filter]:::nacl
    G[Security Group Stateful Instance Filter]:::sg
    H[Virtual Machine Workload]:::vm
    I[IDS Sensor Tap / East West Traffic]:::ids
    J[IPS Inline Mitigation Engine]:::ips
    K[Centralized SIEM / Log Aggregator]:::siem
    L[Key Management Service KMS]:::kms

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H <--> I
    I --> J
    J --> H
    H --> K
    D -.uses.-> L
    E -.isolates.-> H
    I -.feeds.-> K

    classDef src fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef perim fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef encrypt fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef vpc fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    classDef nacl fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef sg fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,color:#000
    classDef vm fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef ids fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef ips fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef siem fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    classDef kms fill:#cffafe,stroke:#0e7490,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds top-down — Internet traffic first hits the **DDoS scrubbing center**, then the **WAF/edge firewall**, is **encrypted in transit** via TLS/IPsec, enters the **VPC boundary**, traverses the **stateless NACL**, passes the **stateful Security Group**, and reaches the **VM workload**. East-West traffic is mirrored to an **IDS sensor**; if an attack is detected, the **IPS** blocks inline. All events are forwarded to a centralized **SIEM** for correlation. **KMS** services keys to the VPN/TLS layer.

## 4.2 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Threat source?}:::q
    A1[External]:::ans
    A2[Internal Insider]:::ans
    A3[Compromised Workload]:::ans
    Q2{OSI Layer?}:::q
    L3[Layer 3/4 Flood]:::layer
    L7[Layer 7 Application]:::layer
    Q3{Goal?}:::q
    G1[Detect only]:::goal
    G2[Block inline]:::goal
    G3[Encrypt channel]:::goal

    Q1 -->|External| A1
    Q1 -->|Insider| A2
    Q1 -->|Compromised| A3
    A1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|L3/L4| L3
    Q2 -->|L7| L7
    L3 --> DDoS[DDoS Scrubbing]:::control
    L7 --> WAF[WAF]:::control
    A2 --> SEG[VPC Segmentation NACL]:::control
    A3 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Detect| G1
    Q3 -->|Block| G2
    Q3 -->|Encrypt| G3
    G1 --> IDSsol[IDS]:::control
    G2 --> IPSsol[IPS]:::control
    G3 --> VPNsol[VPN or TLS]:::control

    classDef q fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef layer fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#cffafe,stroke:#155e75,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between a Stateful firewall and a Stateless firewall. Give one cloud example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stateful Firewall | Stateless Firewall |
|---|---|---|
| Connection tracking | Maintains a state table for every connection | No state tracking |
| Return traffic | Automatically allowed (matches established connection) | Must be explicitly allowed in rules |
| Inspection depth | Can inspect L3, L4, and L7 (with DPI) | L3 and L4 only |
| Performance | Slightly lower due to state table lookup | Higher throughput, line-rate |
| **Cloud example** | **AWS Security Group**, Azure NSG (when stateful) | **AWS NACL** |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the difference highlighted, 1 mark for cloud examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**List and briefly explain the three categories of DDoS attacks with the OSI layer they target.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Volumetric Attacks — OSI Layer 3/4:** Flood the network with massive traffic volumes (e.g., UDP flood, ICMP flood). Measured in **Gbps**. Mitigation: anycast routing, scrubbing centers.
2. **Protocol Attacks — OSI Layer 4:** Exploit protocol weaknesses (e.g., SYN flood, Ping of Death). Measured in **packets per second (Mpps)**. Mitigation: SYN cookies, rate limiting.
3. **Application-Layer Attacks — OSI Layer 7:** Mimic legitimate requests to exhaust server resources (e.g., HTTP GET flood). Measured in **RPS**. Mitigation: WAF, CAPTCHA, rate limiting.

*(1 mark per category with OSI layer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means you answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B) in full.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Choose this OR Question B)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QP Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** and describe how **Security Groups** and **Network ACLs** provide defense-in-depth at the network level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **IPsec** and **TLS** as encryption-in-transit mechanisms. Explain their mode of operation, OSI layer, and typical use cases in a cloud environment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Architecture of VPC (4 marks):**
A **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** is a logically isolated virtual network in the cloud where the tenant has full control over IP address ranges, subnets, route tables, and gateways. Components include:

- **Subnets** — Divisions of the VPC CIDR block; can be *public* (with route to Internet Gateway) or *private* (no direct Internet route).
- **Route Tables** — Define traffic forwarding rules.
- **Internet Gateway (IGW)** — Allows public subnet resources to access the Internet.
- **NAT Gateway** — Allows private subnet resources to initiate outbound traffic without inbound access.
- **VPC Peering / Transit Gateway** — Connects multiple VPCs.

**Security Groups vs. NACLs (3 marks):**

| Feature | Security Group | NACL |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Instance ENI (Elastic Network Interface) | Subnet |
| State | Stateful | Stateless |
| Rules | Allow rules only | Allow + Deny rules |
| Default | Deny all inbound, allow all outbound | Allow all by default |

**Defense-in-Depth Argument:** SG operates at the *instance* (innermost) layer; NACL operates at the *subnet* (middle) layer. An attacker must bypass **both** to reach the workload — therefore, the security posture is multiplicative:

$$P_{compromise} = P_{bypass\_NACL} \times P_{bypass\_SG}$$

Lower joint probability means stronger defense.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | IPsec | TLS |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4–7 (Transport / Application) |
| Modes | Tunnel mode, Transport mode | Record protocol, Handshake protocol |
| Authentication | X.509 certificates, PSK | X.509 certificates (asymmetric) |
| Key exchange | IKEv2 (Diffie-Hellman) | (EC)DHE in TLS 1.3 |
| Encryption | AES-CBC, AES-GCM | AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 (mandatory in 1.3) |
| Use case | Site-to-site VPN, host-to-host | HTTPS, API calls, browser traffic |
| Complexity | High (kernel-level) | Low (user-space library) |

**Typical Cloud Use Cases (3 marks):**
- **IPsec** is used in **AWS Site-to-Site VPN** to connect on-premise datacenters to a VPC. Entire IP packets are encapsulated, encrypted, and tunneled.
- **TLS 1.3** is used for all **HTTPS API calls** to S3, EC2 metadata, Azure Storage — providing confidentiality and integrity of individual application sessions.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the working of an **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** and an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**. Compare **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **DDoS attack**? Describe the three categories of DDoS attacks and explain how cloud-based DDoS protection services (e.g., AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection) mitigate them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

**IDS vs. IPS (3 marks):**
- **IDS:** Sits *off the critical path*; copies traffic via a tap/SPAN port; **alerts** on suspicious activity but does not block. Examples: Snort (open source), Suricata.
- **IPS:** Sits *inline* on the data path; **drops, resets, or rate-limits** malicious traffic in real-time. Examples: Cisco Firepower, AWS Network Firewall with IPS mode.

**Signature-Based Detection (2 marks):**
- Maintains a database of known attack *signatures* (packet patterns, byte sequences, regex).
- Example: Snort rule `alert tcp any any → any 80 (content:"/etc/passwd"; msg:"Passwd file access")`.
- **Advantage:** Low false-positive, fast. **Disadvantage:** Cannot detect zero-day attacks.

**Anomaly-Based Detection (2 marks):**
- Builds a statistical baseline of normal traffic (packet size, entropy, protocol distribution, timing).
- Flags traffic whose features deviate by more than a threshold (e.g., $\theta = 2\sigma$).
- **Advantage:** Detects zero-day attacks. **Disadvantage:** Higher false-positive rate (e.g., legitimate traffic spikes may be flagged).

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**DDoS Definition (1 mark):** A Distributed Denial-of-Service attack uses *multiple compromised systems* (botnet) to flood a target with traffic, exhausting bandwidth, processing, or application resources.

**Three Categories (3 marks):**
1. **Volumetric (L3/L4):** Saturate the link. Example: **DNS amplification** — small query → large response; attacker's spoofed source IP is the victim.
2. **Protocol (L4):** Exhaust server state. Example: **SYN flood** — attacker sends many SYNs, never completes handshake, fills the SYN backlog.
3. **Application (L7):** Mimic legit users, exhaust application resources. Example: **HTTP GET/POST flood** on `/login`.

**Cloud Mitigation (3 marks):**
- **Anycast routing:** Distributes attack across many global POPs so that no single link is saturated.
- **Scrubbing centers:** Analyze incoming packets, separate malicious from legitimate using signatures + ML, forward only clean traffic.
- **Rate limiting & Web Application Firewall (WAF):** Block application-layer floods.
- Examples: **AWS Shield Standard (free, L3/L4)**, **AWS Shield Advanced (paid, L7 + cost protection)**, **Azure DDoS Protection**, **Cloudflare**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Security Groups with NACLs:** SGs are *stateful* and *allow-only*; NACLs are *stateless* and *allow + deny*. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for stating "NACL is stateful."
> 2. **Wrong OSI layer for TLS:** TLS is **NOT Layer 4 only** — it spans **Layers 4–7**. Stating "TLS is Layer 4" loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting return-traffic rules for NACLs:** NACLs are stateless — a rule for inbound port 80 **does not** auto-allow the ephemeral return port. Always state both rules. **-1 mark** if missed.
> 4. **Conflating IDS and IPS:** IDS = detect + alert; IPS = detect + **block inline**. Examiners are strict here. **-2 marks** if you say "IDS blocks traffic."
> 5. **No cloud example:** For a Cloud Computing paper, every network security concept *must* include a cloud example (AWS SG, Azure NSG, VPC, etc.). **-1 mark** if absent.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram:** For 7-mark questions, a labelled block diagram is **mandatory**. KTU rubric reserves **1–2 marks** for diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Network Level**

- **Network-level security = Layers 2–4 of the OSI model** (primarily).
- **Defense-in-Depth:** Perimeter → VPC → Subnet (NACL) → Instance (SG) → Application (WAF). Each layer reduces joint compromise probability multiplicatively.
- **Stateful firewall** tracks connections; **Stateless (NACL)** evaluates every packet independently.
- **Security Groups = instance-level, stateful, allow-only.** **NACLs = subnet-level, stateless, allow + deny.**
- **IDS = detect + alert (passive tap).** **IPS = detect + block (inline).**
- **Signature-based IDS:** Low false-positive, blind to zero-days. **Anomaly-based IDS:** Detects zero-days, higher false-positive.
- **DDoS categories:** Volumetric (L3/L4, Gbps) → Protocol (L4, Mpps) → Application (L7, RPS).
- **IPsec = Layer 3** encryption (site-to-site VPN). **TLS = Layer 4–7** (HTTPS, APIs). TLS 1.3 is the current production standard.
- **Anomaly detection metric — Shannon Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$; range $0$ to $8$ bits for byte distribution.
- **Cloud DDoS services** (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS, Cloudflare) use anycast + scrubbing + WAF.
- **VPN types:** Site-to-Site, Remote Access, SSL/TLS (browser-based).
- **Encryption-in-transit overhead:** IPsec adds ~50–70 bytes; TLS 1.3 adds ~16 bytes per record.
- **Always include a labelled diagram** for any 7-mark KTU question on this topic.
- **Default ports to remember:** HTTPS = **443**, SSH = **22**, RDP = **3389**, IPsec IKE = **500/4500**.
- **Zero Trust model** (NIST SP 800-207) assumes *no implicit trust* — verify every packet regardless of source network — this is the modern KTU-expected answer for "best practice."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Host Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Host Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Host-Level Virtualization** (also termed *Hardware-Level Virtualization* or *Server Virtualization*) is a foundational cloud computing paradigm wherein a single physical host machine — referred to as the **Host** or **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** — is partitioned into multiple isolated, concurrently executing **Virtual Machines (VMs)**, each emulating a complete hardware stack and capable of running an independent guest operating system alongside its application workload.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this sits at the **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** substrate layer and constitutes the bedrock upon which elastic compute, multi-tenancy, and resource pooling services are constructed.

$$ \text{Host} = \{\text{CPU}_{\text{phy}}, \text{Mem}_{\text{phy}}, \text{NIC}_{\text{phy}}, \text{Disk}_{\text{phy}}\} \xrightarrow{\text{VMM/Hypervisor}} \{\text{VM}_1, \text{VM}_2, \dots, \text{VM}_n\} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> - **Host Machine** = The bare-metal physical server providing hardware resources.
> - **Guest Machine** = The virtual instance running on top of the host.
> - **Hypervisor / VMM** = The software layer that creates, runs, and manages guest VMs.
> - **Virtualization** = The act of decoupling the operating system from the underlying physical hardware.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine a large plot of land (your **physical host** with its CPU, RAM, and disk). Instead of letting one family occupy the entire land, you build an **apartment building** (the **hypervisor/VMM**). Each apartment (a **Virtual Machine**) is fully self-contained — it has its own plumbing, electricity meter, walls, and lock. Tenants (guest OS + applications) can independently decorate, install utilities, and live without disturbing neighbors.

- The **building's foundation** = the physical CPU/memory bus.
- The **landlord** = the hypervisor.
- The **apartment walls** = isolation boundaries enforced by the VMM.
- The **shared electricity grid** = virtualized I/O resources.

This analogy directly mirrors how host-level virtualization enables **multi-tenancy, isolation, and resource pooling** — three pillars of cloud computing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Host-Level Virtualization matters in Cloud:**
> 1. **Server Consolidation** — A 64-core server running 1 OS wastes ~70% of its capacity. With VMs, the same hardware serves 10+ tenants at ~80% utilization.
> 2. **Elasticity** — VMs can be cloned, migrated (live migration), or terminated in seconds.
> 3. **Heterogeneous OS support** — Run Windows, Linux, and BSD on the same physical host.
> 4. **Fault Isolation** — A crash in VM₁ does not affect VM₂.

### 1.3 The Two Architectural Realms

> [!NOTE]
> **Host-Level vs. Bare-Metal Provisioning Distinction:**
> Host-level virtualization requires the cloud tenant to **lease an entire VM** (with its own kernel, libraries, and OS). This is fundamentally different from **container-level virtualization** (Docker/containers) which shares the host kernel. This module focuses exclusively on **VM-based** host-level isolation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Host-Level Virtualization Stack

The system can be decomposed into **four logical strata** from bottom to top:

1. **Physical Hardware Layer** — CPU (with virtualization extensions like **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V**), RAM, NIC, Storage, BIOS/UEFI.
2. **Host Operating System / Bare-Metal Firmware** — Either a thin privileged OS or no OS at all (Type-1 case).
3. **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) / Hypervisor** — The *control program* that intercepts privileged operations, schedules vCPUs, and maps virtual resources to physical resources.
4. **Guest Virtual Machines** — Each with its own *virtual BIOS, vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC*, guest OS, and applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The VMM is **THE most critical software component** in host-level virtualization. Popek and Goldberg (1974) formalized three properties any VMM must satisfy:
> - **Equivalence** — A program running under VMM behaves identically to one running natively.
> - **Resource Control (Efficiency)** — The VMM must retain complete control over physical resources.
> - **Fidelity** — Privileged instructions must be trapped and emulated, not executed directly.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Hypervisors (High-Yield for KTU)

| Hypervisor Type | Also Known As | Position | Examples | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | Native / Native VMM | Installed directly on hardware | **Xen, KVM, VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V Server, Citrix XenServer** | High performance, low overhead, production-grade | Driver compatibility, hardware lock-in |
| **Type-2 (Hosted)** | Hosted VMM | Runs as application on a host OS | **VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop, QEMU** | Easy install, rich GUI, good for dev/test | Double-scheduling overhead, slower I/O |

### 2.3 CPU Virtualization Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Strategy | Guest-OS Awareness | Overhead | Example Technology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Virtualization (Binary Translation)** | Hypervisor translates guest privileged instructions at runtime via BT | None (guest thinks it owns hardware) | **Moderate (~10-20%)** | VMware ESXi (early versions) |
| **Paravirtualization** | Guest OS is *modified* to make hypercalls to VMM | Required (hypercall API) | **Low (~2-5%)** | Xen (classic), early Xen domU kernels |
| **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** | CPU hardware traps privileged ops (Ring -1 / VMX) | None | **Very Low (~1-3%)** | **Intel VT-x, AMD-V, ARM Virtualization Extensions** |
| **Hybrid (Hybrid Virtualization)** | Combines hardware assist with selective paravirtualized drivers | Partial (only drivers) | **Low (~3-5%)** | Xen HVM, KVM, VMware (modern) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **popek-goldberg theorem** established that a machine can be virtualized **IF and ONLY IF** its set of sensitive instructions is a *subset* of its privileged instructions. x86 originally failed this (had 17 non-virtualizable instructions), which is why **binary translation** and later **VT-x/AMD-V** were developed.

### 2.4 Memory Virtualization — The Nested Paging Problem

Modern host-level virtualization uses **Two-Dimensional Paging (Shadow Paging → EPT/NTP)**:

$$ \text{Guest Virtual Address (GVA)} \xrightarrow{\text{guest page table}} \text{Guest Physical Address (GPA)} \xrightarrow{\text{EPT/NTP}} \text{Host Physical Address (HPA)} $$

- **Shadow Page Tables**: VMM maintains a shadow mapping GVA → HPA; every guest page-table update triggers a VMM intervention. (Slow.)
- **Extended Page Tables (EPT) on Intel / Nested Page Tables (NPT) on AMD**: Hardware walks a two-level page walk natively. (Fast; modern standard.)

> [!WARNING]
> **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) Miss Penalty** in nested paging is the single biggest memory performance bottleneck in VM workloads. EPT reduces this by caching combined translations.

### 2.5 I/O Virtualization

| Technique | Description | Latency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Emulation (I/O through VMM)** | Every I/O trapped and emulated | **Very High** | Early QEMU, dev only |
| **Paravirtualized I/O (virtio)** | Guest uses shared ring buffers and hypercalls | **Low** | Xen + Linux domU, KVM + virtio |
| **Direct Device Assignment (PCI Passthrough / IOMMU/VT-d)** | Physical NIC/HBA bound to single guest | **Near-native** | High-perf databases, NFV |
| **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** | One physical NIC exposes multiple Virtual Functions (VFs) | **Near-native, line-rate** | Carrier-grade NFV, 5G core |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are *frequently* tested in KTU 14-mark problems. Memorize the **bolded** terms.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **VM Density** | $\text{VM Density} = \dfrac{\text{Total vCPUs allocated}}{\text{Physical CPU cores}}$ | **Recommended ≤ 4 : 1** for general workloads |
| **Server Consolidation Ratio** | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{\text{Physical servers pre-virt}}{\text{Physical servers post-virt}}$ | Typical cloud: **10 : 1** to **15 : 1** |
| **VM Overhead (Memory)** | $\text{Mem}_{\text{usable}} = \text{Mem}_{\text{phy}} - \text{Mem}_{\text{VMM}} - \sum \text{Mem}_{\text{VM}_{\text{reserved}}}$ | VMM typically reserves **64 MB – 2 GB** |
| **CPU Entitlement Ratio** | $\text{CPU}_{\text{share}_{\text{VM}_i}} = \dfrac{\text{vCPU}_{\text{VM}_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{vCPU}_{\text{VM}_j}}$ | Used in proportional-share schedulers |
| **Hypervisor Call Cost** | $T_{\text{hypercall}} \approx 100\text{–}500 \text{ cycles}$ vs. $T_{\text{syscall}} \approx 50\text{–}200 \text{ cycles}$ | Paravirt reduces this |
| **Nested Paging TLB Miss Penalty** | $T_{\text{walk}}^{\text{nested}} = T_{\text{walk}}^{\text{L1}} + T_{\text{walk}}^{\text{L2}}$ (approx **2× native** without EPT) | Hardware EPT brings this back to **1.1–1.3×** |
| **Live Migration Downtime** | $T_{\text{down}} \approx \dfrac{\text{Dirty Page Rate} \times RTT_{\text{network}}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | Goal: **< 1 second** |
| **Consolidation Benefit** | $\text{Savings} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Power}_{\text{post}}}{\text{Power}_{\text{pre}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Often **60–80%** power reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used directly in **Data Center Capacity Planning**, **SLA management**, **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) calculation**, and **green cloud** initiatives (PUE/CUE metrics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: VM Memory Capacity Calculation

> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> *A host has 64 GB of physical RAM. The VMM reserves 2 GB for its own footprint. Four VMs (VM₁, VM₂, VM₃, VM₄) are configured with 12 GB, 16 GB, 8 GB, and 10 GB respectively. (a) Compute the available memory after VMM reservation. (b) Verify if all VMs can be admitted. (c) If the cloud's target oversubscription ratio is 1.5 : 1, what is the maximum *configured* memory the cloud orchestrator can promise tenants?*

#### Part (a) — Usable Physical Memory After VMM Reservation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mem}_{\text{usable}} &= \text{Mem}_{\text{phy}} - \text{Mem}_{\text{VMM}} \\
&= 64 \text{ GB} - 2 \text{ GB} \\
&= 62 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: Subtract the hypervisor's resident set from total physical RAM. The VMM (e.g., ESXi) needs RAM for its own kernel, drivers, and control structures.

#### Part (b) — VM Admission Control

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mem}_{\text{requested}} &= \text{Mem}_{\text{VM}_1} + \text{Mem}_{\text{VM}_2} + \text{Mem}_{\text{VM}_3} + \text{Mem}_{\text{VM}_4} \\
&= 12 + 16 + 8 + 10 \\
&= 46 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Admission Check: } 46 \text{ GB} &\leq 62 \text{ GB} \;\;\checkmark \\
\text{Remaining Headroom} &= 62 - 46 = 16 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: The sum of VM reservations must not exceed the usable pool. The remaining 16 GB forms the **headroom** for ballooning, page sharing, and host-level caching.

#### Part (c) — Maximum Configured Memory Under Oversubscription

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowed}_{\text{config}} &= \text{Mem}_{\text{usable}} \times \text{Oversubscription Ratio} \\
&= 62 \text{ GB} \times 1.5 \\
&= 93 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: An oversubscription ratio of 1.5 : 1 means the orchestrator may sell 1.5× the physical capacity, *relying* on the fact that not all tenants will use 100% of their allocated RAM simultaneously. (Statistical multiplexing gain.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: Oversubscription works because average VM memory utilization in real workloads is **20–40%** of allocation, not 100%. This is the **Jevons Paradox** in action — more efficient resource sharing encourages *more* provisioning, not less.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Full vs. Paravirtualization

| Parameter | Full Virtualization (BT) | Paravirtualization | Hardware-Assisted |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Guest OS modification** | None | Required (hypercall patches) | None |
| **Privilege handling** | Binary translation at runtime | Hypercalls (explicit traps) | Hardware VM exits |
| **Performance (CPU-bound)** | ~80–90% of native | ~95–98% of native | ~97–99% of native |
| **Performance (I/O-bound)** | Poor (emulated) | Excellent (virtio) | Excellent (virtio + VT-d) |
| **Security** | Strong isolation | Weaker (guest knows VMM) | Strong isolation |
| **Portability** | Any x86 OS | Only modified guest OS | Any x86 OS |
| **Complexity of VMM** | High (BT engine) | Medium | Low (offloaded to HW) |
| **Example Stack** | VMware ESXi (classic) | Xen (PV mode) | **KVM, modern ESXi, Hyper-V** |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: CPU Scheduling in a Host with 8 Cores and 3 VMs

> **Problem:** A host has **8 physical cores**. Three VMs are configured with vCPU counts of 4, 4, and 2 respectively. Using **proportional-share scheduling**, determine each VM's CPU entitlement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total vCPUs requested} &= 4 + 4 + 2 = 10 \\
\text{VM}_1 \text{ share} &= \frac{4}{10} = 40\% \\
\text{VM}_2 \text{ share} &= \frac{4}{10} = 40\% \\
\text{VM}_3 \text{ share} &= \frac{2}{10} = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: Proportional-share (a.k.a. fair-share) schedulers like **Credit Scheduler (Xen)** and **Completely Fair Scheduler (Linux/KVM)** allocate pCPU time slices in proportion to the configured vCPU weight. The total may *exceed* the number of physical cores (here 10 > 8) — this is **vCPU oversubscription**, tolerated when not all VMs run at 100% simultaneously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oversubscription Ratio (vCPU)} &= \frac{10}{8} = 1.25 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall**: Students often confuse **vCPU oversubscription** with **CPU entitlement**. Entitlement is *time-share based*, not parallel-execution based. A 4-vCPU VM does not "get 4 cores" — it gets **40% of 8 pCPU time**, distributed over scheduling windows.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Type-1 vs. Type-2 Hypervisor Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Type1["TYPE-1 BARE-METAL HYPERVISOR ARCHITECTURE"]
        A1[Guest VM 1 - App + Guest OS] --> HV1[Hypervisor - Xen / ESXi / KVM]
        A2[Guest VM 2 - App + Guest OS] --> HV1
        A3[Guest VM 3 - App + Guest OS] --> HV1
        HV1 --> HW1[Physical Hardware - CPU RAM NIC Disk]
    end
    subgraph Type2["TYPE-2 HOSTED HYPERVISOR ARCHITECTURE"]
        B1[Guest VM 1 - App + Guest OS] --> HV2[Hypervisor - VirtualBox / VMware Workstation]
        B2[Guest VM 2 - App + Guest OS] --> HV2
        HV2 --> HOS[Host Operating System - Windows / Linux]
        HOS --> HW2[Physical Hardware - CPU RAM NIC Disk]
    end
    HV1:::baremetal
    HV2:::hosted
    classDef baremetal fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#0d3b8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hosted fill:#bf6c0c,stroke:#7a3d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Memory Virtualization: Two-Level Page Walk (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application in Guest VM] -->|GVA| GPT[Guest Page Table]
    GPT -->|GPA| EPT[Extended Page Table - EPT - in VMM]
    EPT -->|HPA| DRAM[Physical DRAM]
    TLB1[Guest TLB] -.->|caches GVA to GPA| GPT
    TLB2[Combined TLB - VPID] -.->|caches GVA to HPA| EPT
    style APP fill:#2d8f4e,color:#ffffff
    style DRAM fill:#8b0a1a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Live Migration Workflow (Pre-Copy Algorithm) (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Host
    participant NET as Network Link
    participant DST as Destination Host
    SRC->>SRC: Step 1 - Suspend-aware pre-copy iteration 0
    SRC->>NET: Step 2 - Transfer all memory pages
    NET->>DST: Step 3 - Receive page set A
    SRC->>SRC: Step 4 - Track dirty pages
    SRC->>NET: Step 5 - Resend dirty pages
    NET->>DST: Step 6 - Receive incremental pages
    SRC->>DST: Step 7 - Stop-and-copy final iteration
    SRC->>DST: Step 8 - Transfer CPU and device state
    DST->>DST: Step 9 - Resume VM at destination
    Note over SRC,DST: Total downtime = Final copy duration
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Full Host-Level Virtualization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VM1["VIRTUAL MACHINE 1"]
        APP1[User Application] --> LIB1[Guest Libraries]
        LIB1 --> GOS1[Guest Operating System - Linux]
    end
    subgraph VM2["VIRTUAL MACHINE 2"]
        APP2[User Application] --> LIB2[Guest Libraries]
        LIB2 --> GOS2[Guest Operating System - Windows]
    end
    subgraph VM3["VIRTUAL MACHINE 3"]
        APP3[User Application] --> LIB3[Guest Libraries]
        LIB3 --> GOS3[Guest Operating System - BSD]
    end
    GOS1 --> VMM[Hypervisor - VMM - CPU Scheduler - Memory Manager - I/O Emulator - Network Bridge]
    GOS2 --> VMM
    GOS3 --> VMM
    VMM --> DRV[Device Drivers - virtio net - virtio blk - paravirt drivers]
    DRV --> HW[Physical Server - CPU with VT-x or AMD-V - RAM with EPT - NIC with SR-IOV - SSD with TRIM]
    style VMM fill:#1f6feb,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    style HW fill:#5a1a8b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define **Host-Level Virtualization**. Differentiate between **Type-1** and **Type-2** hypervisors with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Host-level virtualization is the process of partitioning a single physical host machine into multiple, isolated virtual machines (VMs), each running its own guest operating system and applications, managed by a software layer called the *Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)* or *Hypervisor*.

**Difference Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | As an application on a host OS |
| Performance | High (no double scheduling) | Lower (host OS overhead) |
| Example | **Xen, VMware ESXi, KVM** | **Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation** |

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is **Paravirtualization**? State **two advantages** over full virtualization.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Paravirtualization is a virtualization technique in which the guest operating system is *modified* to replace non-virtualizable privileged instructions with explicit *hypercalls* directed at the hypervisor. The guest OS is "aware" that it is running virtualized.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Lower overhead** — no binary translation required, near-native CPU performance.
2. **Better I/O performance** — direct use of shared memory ring buffers (e.g., Xen grant tables, virtio).
3. **Simpler hypervisor** — VMM doesn't need to maintain shadow data structures for translated instructions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **Popek–Goldberg virtualization requirements** and discuss how modern x86 CPUs satisfy them with **Intel VT-x** and **AMD-V** extensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe **I/O virtualization techniques** (emulation, paravirtualized I/O, direct device assignment, and SR-IOV). Compare their **latency characteristics**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) — Popek–Goldberg Requirements (7 marks):**

The Popek–Goldberg theorem (1974) defines three *necessary and sufficient* conditions for a machine architecture to be efficiently virtualizable:

1. **Fidelity / Equivalence (2 marks):** Any program running under the VMM must produce results identical to running on the bare machine, *except* for timing-dependent effects.

2. **Resource Control / Efficiency (2 marks):** The VMM must retain *complete control* over all physical resources. Statistics show that no more than a bounded number of privileged instructions are emulated; "innocuous" instructions may run natively.

3. **Efficiency Criterion / Trap-and-Emulate (3 marks):** All *sensitive* instructions (those that change or reveal the system's resource configuration) must be a **subset** of *privileged* instructions. This guarantees that any attempt to execute them in user mode triggers a *trap* to the VMM, which can then emulate them.

> **Original x86 Failure:** The x86 architecture had **17 sensitive-but-non-privileged instructions** (e.g., `popf`, `pushf`, `sgdt`, `sidt`, `sldt`, `str`, `verr`, `verw`), violating the third condition. Hence pure software-based full virtualization was impossible without **binary translation** (VMware's solution).

> **Modern Solution — Intel VT-x (2 marks):** Introduces two new processor modes:
> - **VMX root mode** — for the VMM/hypervisor.
> - **VMX non-root mode** — for guest VMs.
> A new data structure, the **Virtual Machine Control Structure (VMCS)**, holds the guest/host state, and a new pair of instructions — **`VMLAUNCH`** and **`VMRESUME`** — are used to enter/exit VMs. Privileged operations in non-root mode automatically cause a **VM exit** to the root, where the hypervisor can emulate the instruction.

> **Modern Solution — AMD-V (2 marks):** AMD's counterpart uses **Nested Page Tables (NPT)** for memory and **Secure Virtual Machine (SVM)** extensions with `VMRUN`/`VMEXIT` instructions. The behavior is functionally equivalent to VT-x.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** With VT-x/AMD-V, the x86 architecture finally satisfies all three Popek–Goldberg criteria, making efficient full hardware-assisted virtualization possible.

---

**(b) — I/O Virtualization Techniques (7 marks):**

| Technique | Mechanism | Latency | Throughput | Isolation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Emulation (Pure)** | Every I/O instruction trapped and emulated by VMM | **Very High (10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ s)** | **Low** | Strong |
| **Paravirtualized I/O (virtio)** | Shared ring buffers + hypercalls (e.g., `xen-blkfront`, `virtio-net`) | **Low (10⁻⁶ s)** | **Near-native** | Strong |
| **Direct Device Assignment (PCI Passthrough / IOMMU / Intel VT-d)** | Physical NIC/HBA bound exclusively to one guest via IOMMU translation | **Near-native (10⁻⁷ s)** | **Line-rate** | Strongest |
| **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** | One physical NIC exposes many lightweight Virtual Functions (VFs) | **Near-native** | **Line-rate, hardware-partitioned** | Hardware-enforced |

> **Diagrammatic Comparison (3 marks):** The student should sketch a layered diagram showing:
> - Guest OS → Driver → (Emulation | Paravirt ring buffers | Direct IOMMU | SR-IOV VF) → Physical NIC
> Label the latency gradient visually.

> **Engineering Trade-off (1 mark):** Emulation offers maximum portability (any guest OS) but worst performance. SR-IOV offers the best performance but requires NIC hardware support and IOMMU-capable chipset. Cloud providers (AWS, Azure) prefer **SR-IOV + paravirt fallback** for high-throughput workloads.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`

**(a)** Describe the **architectural components of a host-level virtualization system** with a clear block diagram. Explain the role of the **VMM** in resource allocation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **memory virtualization using Extended Page Tables (EPT)**. How does it improve over the older **shadow paging** technique? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) — Architectural Components (7 marks):**

A host-level virtualization system has **four distinct layers** (use a block diagram, 3 marks):

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Guest VM 1     Guest VM 2     Guest VM 3      │ ← Guest Layer
│  (App+OS)       (App+OS)       (App+OS)        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  VMM / Hypervisor (Xen, KVM, ESXi)             │ ← Control Layer
│  • CPU Scheduler    • Memory Manager           │
│  • I/O Emulator     • Network Bridge           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Host OS (Type-2) or Bare-metal Firmware       │ ← OS Layer
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Physical Hardware (CPU+VT-x, RAM, NIC, Disk)  │ ← Hardware Layer
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of VMM in Resource Allocation (4 marks):**

1. **CPU Scheduling** (1 mark): The VMM schedules virtual CPUs onto physical cores using algorithms like the **Credit Scheduler (Xen)** or **CFS (KVM)**. It supports both **proportional-share** and **real-time** allocation.
2. **Memory Management** (1 mark): Allocates contiguous physical memory pages to VMs, maintains isolation, and supports features like **memory ballooning, transparent page sharing, and memory compression**.
3. **I/O Virtualization** (1 mark): Routes I/O requests to the appropriate physical device using emulated, paravirtualized, or direct-assignment strategies.
4. **Isolation & Security** (1 mark): Enforces **sandboxing** between VMs, manages **virtual networks (vSwitches)**, and implements **resource quotas/limits** to prevent noisy-neighbor effects.

---

**(b) — EPT vs. Shadow Paging (7 marks):**

> **Shadow Paging (older, 3 marks):** The VMM maintains a *shadow page table* that directly maps **Guest Virtual Address (GVA) → Host Physical Address (HPA)**. The guest's own page tables (GVA → GPA) are *hidden* and treated as data structures. Whenever the guest updates its page table, a **page fault** traps to the VMM, which updates the shadow table. This incurs:
> - High overhead (~25–40% slowdown for memory-intensive workloads)
> - Complex consistency management
> - TLB flushes on every shadow update

> **Extended Page Tables (EPT) — Intel / Nested Page Tables (NPT) — AMD (3 marks):** A hardware-supported *two-level* page walk:
> - **Level-1 (Guest Page Table):** GVA → GPA, maintained by the guest OS natively.
> - **Level-2 (EPT):** GPA → HPA, maintained by the VMM and cached in dedicated hardware.
> The CPU's Memory Management Unit (MMU) performs both walks automatically on a TLB miss. No page-fault traps to VMM for normal translations.

> **Performance Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Shadow Paging | EPT / NPT |
|---|---|---|
| Page walk overhead | Single walk (but complex update) | Two-level walk (4× native) |
| VMM intervention | On every guest page table update | Rare (only on EPT violation) |
| Memory pressure | High (duplicate tables) | Lower (dedicated EPT structure) |
| Net throughput | ~60–80% of native | **~90–97% of native** |
| TLB efficiency | Limited (single-level) | Higher (VPID tagging, large pages) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "hypervisor" with "host OS" — many students write "the VMM is the host OS." The VMM is a *control program*; on Type-2 it sits **above** the host OS.
> 2. **Do NOT state** that EPT and shadow paging are the *same thing* — EPT is hardware-assist; shadow paging is purely software emulation.
> 3. **Do NOT forget units** in numerical problems (GB vs. MB; cycles vs. ns).
> 4. **Always draw the block diagram** in 7-mark questions — examiners award **1–2 marks** for the figure alone.
> 5. **Distinguish** vCPU oversubscription (10 vCPU / 8 pCPU) from CPU time-share (40% entitlement). Marks are lost when these are conflated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Host-Level Virtualization**

- ✅ **Definition:** Decoupling OS from hardware using a **VMM**; one host → many isolated VMs.
- ✅ **Two hypervisor types:** **Type-1 (bare-metal, fast, prod)** vs. **Type-2 (hosted, slow, dev)**.
- ✅ **Popek–Goldberg theorem** — three properties: equivalence, control, efficiency.
- ✅ **CPU virtualization techniques** — Full (BT), Para (hypercalls), Hardware-assisted (VT-x/AMD-V), Hybrid.
- ✅ **Memory virtualization** — Shadow paging (old, slow) vs. **EPT/NPT** (modern, hardware).
- ✅ **I/O virtualization** — Emulation → Paravirt (virtio) → Passthrough (VT-d) → **SR-IOV** (best).
- ✅ **Key acronyms to memorize:** VMM, VM, EPT, NPT, IOMMU, VT-d, SR-IOV, virtio, hypercall, paravirt, HVM, PV.
- ✅ **Example stacks:** **Xen** (Type-1, supports both PV and HVM), **KVM** (Linux kernel module, HVM-only), **VMware ESXi** (Type-1, hybrid), **VirtualBox** (Type-2, dev).
- ✅ **Numerical must-knows:** VM Density, Server Consolidation Ratio, Memory Headroom, vCPU Oversubscription.
- ✅ **Live migration** uses the *pre-copy* algorithm to minimize downtime to **< 1 s**.
- ✅ **Real-world cloud providers** (AWS Nitro, Azure Hyper-V, Google Compute Engine) all use Type-1 hypervisors with hardware-assisted virtualization.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** When asked to "explain host-level virtualization," always begin with the **4-layer architecture diagram** — it earns easy marks and anchors the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET637 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Cloud Computing**, the **Application Level** refers to the topmost layer of the cloud computing stack where end-user software services, business logic, and application programming interfaces (APIs) are designed, deployed, scaled, and managed. It is the layer where the *actual functional purpose* of the cloud is realized — be it email delivery, video streaming, CRM, ERP, or analytics.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PEEET637 – Cloud Computing* (Module 4), the Application Level covers the following sub-domains:

1. **Cloud Application Architectures** — Monolithic, Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), Microservices, and Serverless paradigms.
2. **Application-Level Virtualization** — Containers, Docker, and Kubernetes orchestration.
3. **Application Programming Models** — MapReduce, Apache Spark, and event-driven patterns.
4. **Application Scalability & Load Balancing** — Elastic scaling, application delivery controllers (ADC).
5. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) Engineering** — Multi-tenancy, customization, and configurability.
6. **Web Services & APIs** — REST, SOAP, GraphQL, and API Gateways.
7. **Application-Level QoS** — Response time, throughput, availability, and SLA compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition Blueprint (2024 Scheme)**
> The *Application Level* in cloud computing is the abstraction layer that sits *above* the Platform and Infrastructure layers. It is responsible for delivering **on-demand, scalable, multi-tenant, and highly available** software services to geographically distributed end-users over the Internet.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine a **30-story luxury apartment building** constructed by a real-estate developer:

| Cloud Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Infrastructure (IaaS)** | The empty land, pillars, plumbing, and electrical wiring — the bare structure. |
| **Platform (PaaS)** | The interior fittings, paint, doors, and a common gym/swimming pool. |
| **Application Level (SaaS)** | The **furnished flats** that tenants actually live in — customized per family, with shared services (water, electricity) drawn from a common source. |

Just as a tenant walks in and *immediately uses* a fully furnished flat (without worrying about plumbing), a cloud user at the *Application Level* simply *consumes* ready-made software (Gmail, Salesforce, Dropbox) without managing servers, OS, or middleware.

**Three Key Properties of the Application Level** (bold the metrics):
- **Multi-tenancy ratio** (tenants per application instance)
- **Elasticity** (instances scaling from **1 to 1000** in minutes)
- **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** measured in **seconds, not hours**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Application Level" Matters in KTU Exams**
> Module 4 typically carries **20–25% weightage** in end-semester evaluations. Questions are often scenario-based: *"Design a cloud architecture for an e-commerce portal serving 10 million users."* This requires a strong grasp of *application-level* design choices.

---

## 1.3 The Cloud Application Stack — Layered View

$$
\underbrace{\text{SaaS Apps}}_{\text{Application Level}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{App Runtimes / PaaS}}_{\text{Platform Level}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Virtual Machines / Storage}}_{\text{Infrastructure Level}}
$$

The **Application Level** itself can be sub-divided into:

- **Presentation Tier** → Web UI, Mobile UI, REST endpoints
- **Logic Tier** → Business rules, microservices, serverless functions
- **Data Tier** → SQL/NoSQL databases, object stores, caches

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU 2024**: Think **"PLE"** — **P**resentation, **L**ogic, **E** (Engineering) data. This three-tier model is a **favourite viva question** for Module 4.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Patterns at the Application Level

### 2.1.1 Monolithic Architecture
A single, tightly coupled codebase where UI, business logic, and data access are bundled into one deployable unit.

**Why?** Simplifies development for small teams.
**How?** One `.war`/`.jar` file deployed to one server.
**Limitation?** Cannot scale individual components; one bug may crash the entire system.

### 2.1.2 Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)
A collection of *loosely coupled* services communicating via **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)** and standard protocols (SOAP, WSDL).

### 2.1.3 Microservices Architecture
A finer-grained evolution of SOA where each service is **independently deployable**, owns its own database, and communicates via lightweight protocols (**REST/gRPC**).

**Operational Steps in Microservices Design:**
1. **Decompose** business capabilities by *bounded context* (Domain-Driven Design).
2. **Define APIs** with OpenAPI/Swagger specifications.
3. **Choose Communication** — synchronous (REST/gRPC) or asynchronous (Kafka/RabbitMQ).
4. **Containerize** each service using Docker.
5. **Orchestrate** using Kubernetes.
6. **Observe** with distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin).

### 2.1.4 Serverless Architecture
Applications composed of **stateless functions** triggered by events. The cloud provider dynamically allocates compute resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Start Latency**: The first invocation of a serverless function after inactivity suffers a latency penalty of **200 ms – 5 s** (depending on runtime). KTU exams love this metric.

---

## 2.2 Multi-Tenancy — The SaaS Heart

**Multi-tenancy** allows a single application instance to serve multiple customers (*tenants*) while keeping their data isolated.

| Model | Description | Isolation | Cost Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared Everything** | Single DB, single schema, `tenant_id` column | Logical only | **Highest** |
| **Shared Database, Separate Schema** | One DB, one schema per tenant | Logical + Schema | High |
| **Separate Database** | One DB per tenant | Physical | **Lowest** (most isolated) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert**: *Which multi-tenancy model offers the strongest data isolation?* Answer: **Separate Database**. The follow-up: *Which is most cost-efficient?* Answer: **Shared Everything**.

---

## 2.3 Application-Level Scalability

Scalability at the application level can be **Vertical** (scale-up) or **Horizontal** (scale-out).

$$
\text{Scalability Efficiency} \; \eta_s \;=\; \frac{\text{Performance}_n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Performance}_i} \;\times\; 100\%
$$

- If $\eta_s = 100\% \Rightarrow$ **Linear scalability** (ideal).
- If $\eta_s < 100\% \Rightarrow$ **Sub-linear scalability** (overhead, contention).
- If $\eta_s > 100\% \Rightarrow$ **Super-linear** (rare; cache amplification).

---

## 2.4 Load Balancing Algorithms (Application Level)

$$
W_i \;=\; \frac{C_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j}
$$

Where:
- $W_i$ = normalized weight of server $i$
- $C_i$ = capacity metric (CPU cores, memory, throughput) of server $i$

### Common Algorithms:

| Algorithm | Selection Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Round Robin** | Sequential rotation | Homogeneous servers |
| **Weighted Round Robin** | Proportional to $C_i$ | Heterogeneous servers |
| **Least Connections** | $\min(\text{active\_conns})$ | Long-lived sessions |
| **IP Hash** | $\text{hash}(client\_ip) \bmod n$ | Sticky sessions |
| **Least Response Time** | $\min(RTT_i + \text{active\_conns}_i)$ | Latency-sensitive apps |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` symbol used in table cells** (uses `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid Markdown parser breakage).

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $T = \frac{\text{Requests Completed}}{\Delta t}$ | req/s | Higher is better |
| 2 | **Mean Response Time** | $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | ms | M/M/1 queue: $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ |
| 3 | **Utilization** | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Must be $< 1$ for stability |
| 4 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | entities | $L$=avg in system, $W$=avg time in system |
| 5 | **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | % (e.g., **99.99%**) | "Four nines" = 52.6 min/yr downtime |
| 6 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}}$ | speedup | $p$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = processors |
| 7 | **Cost per Tenant** | $C_{tenant} = \frac{C_{infra}}{N_{tenants} \cdot \eta_m}$ | ₹/₹ | $\eta_m$ = multi-tenancy efficiency |
| 8 | **Elasticity Index** | $E = \frac{T_{scale}}{T_{peak}}$ | dimensionless | $T_{scale}$ = provisioning time |
| 9 | **Cache Hit Ratio** | $H = \frac{H_{hits}}{H_{hits} + H_{misses}}$ | ratio [0,1] | Aim for $H \ge 0.9$ |
| 10 | **Session Affinity** | $\text{stickiness} = \text{hash}(\text{session\_id}) \bmod n$ | server ID | Used for stateful apps |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application-Level Cloud Use |
|---|---|
| **E-Commerce** | AWS Lambda + API Gateway for flash sales handling 100K req/s |
| **Healthcare** | HIPAA-compliant multi-tenant SaaS for Electronic Health Records |
| **Banking** | Containerized microservices for fraud detection (latency < 50 ms) |
| **Education** | Multi-tenant LMS serving 1M+ students on a single codebase |
| **IoT** | Serverless functions ingesting millions of sensor events/min |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight**: Netflix runs **700+ microservices** across AWS, processes **2 trillion events/day** using Apache Kafka, and is a *gold-standard* case study for KTU presentation answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Response Time in an M/M/1 Queueing System

An **M/M/1** queue is the canonical model for a single-server cloud application. The Markovian assumptions are:
- **M** = Markovian (Poisson) inter-arrival with rate $\lambda$
- **M** = Markovian (exponential) service with rate $\mu$
- **1** = single server

**Goal**: Derive the mean response time $\bar{R}$ in steady state.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1**: State the stability condition.

For the queue to remain bounded, the service rate must exceed the arrival rate:
$$
\lambda \;<\; \mu
$$

**Step 2**: Define the utilization factor.

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{\mu}
$$

This represents the fraction of time the server is busy.

**Step 3**: Steady-state probability of $n$ jobs in the system.

For an M/M/1 queue, the probability that the system has exactly $n$ jobs in steady state is a geometric distribution:
$$
P_n \;=\; (1 - \rho)\,\rho^{n}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

**Step 4**: Compute the expected number of jobs in the system.

$$
L \;=\; \mathbb{E}[N] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,P_n \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(1-\rho)\rho^{n}
$$

Using the identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^{n} = \frac{x}{(1-x)^{2}}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:
$$
L \;=\; (1-\rho)\,\frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}
$$

**Step 5**: Express $L$ in terms of $\lambda$ and $\mu$.

Substituting $\rho = \lambda/\mu$:
$$
L \;=\; \frac{\lambda / \mu}{1 - \lambda / \mu} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

**Step 6**: Apply Little's Law to find mean response time.

Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$, where $W$ is the mean *time* spent in the system (response time $\bar{R}$). Solving for $W$:
$$
\bar{R} \;=\; \frac{L}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{\lambda / (\mu - \lambda)}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

**Final Result**:
$$
\boxed{\;\bar{R} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: As $\lambda \to \mu$, $\bar{R} \to \infty$. This is the **queue-explosion** phenomenon — a critical concept for designing load-balanced cloud apps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law for Application-Level Parallelism

**Scenario**: An application spends fraction $p$ of its execution in parallelizable code and the remainder $(1-p)$ in serial code. What is the maximum speedup with $n$ processors?

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1**: Decompose execution time.

Let $T_1$ = total execution time on a single processor. Then:
$$
T_1 \;=\; T_{serial} + T_{parallel} \;=\; (1-p)T_1 + p\,T_1
$$

**Step 2**: Express parallel time with $n$ processors.

The serial portion remains unchanged $(1-p)T_1$. The parallel portion is divided across $n$ processors:
$$
T_n \;=\; (1-p)T_1 + \frac{p\,T_1}{n}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute speedup.

$$
S(n) \;=\; \frac{T_1}{T_n} \;=\; \frac{T_1}{(1-p)T_1 + \frac{p\,T_1}{n}} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}}
$$

**Step 4**: Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Final Result**:
$$
\boxed{\;S_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Even with *infinite* processors, speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1-p}$. If only **5%** of the application is serial ($p = 0.95$), the maximum achievable speedup is $20\times$ — no matter how many cores you add. This is the **Amdahl's bottleneck**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost Optimization for a Multi-Tenant SaaS

**Problem**: A SaaS provider serves $N$ tenants on a single application instance running on a cloud VM costing **\$C per month**. The multi-tenancy efficiency (overhead from isolation, customization) is $\eta_m$ (where $0 < \eta_m \le 1$).

**Derivation**:

**Step 1**: Effective resource cost (with overhead).

$$
C_{effective} \;=\; \frac{C}{\eta_m}
$$

**Step 2**: Per-tenant cost.

$$
C_{tenant} \;=\; \frac{C_{effective}}{N} \;=\; \frac{C}{N \cdot \eta_m}
$$

**Step 3**: Add operational cost (monitoring, support, backups) as fraction $\alpha$ of $C$:

$$
C_{total} \;=\; C \cdot (1 + \alpha)
$$

**Step 4**: Final per-tenant operational cost:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{tenant}^{final} \;=\; \frac{C \cdot (1 + \alpha)}{N \cdot \eta_m}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example** (great for KTU 14-mark answers):
> If $C = \$1000$, $N = 100$ tenants, $\eta_m = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.2$:
> $$
> C_{tenant}^{final} \;=\; \frac{1000 \cdot 1.2}{100 \cdot 0.9} \;=\; \frac{1200}{90} \;\approx\; \$13.33 \text{ per tenant/month}
> $$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application-Level Decision Table

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Architecture | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Predictable, low traffic** (< 1000 req/s) | Monolithic + single VM | Simplicity, low cost |
| **Bursty, unpredictable traffic** | Serverless (Lambda/Functions) | Pay-per-use, auto-scaling |
| **High inter-service coupling** | Modular Monolith | Easier refactoring than microservices |
| **Independent scaling per component** | Microservices + Kubernetes | Granular elasticity |
| **Strong data isolation needed** | Multi-tenancy: Separate DB | Compliance (HIPAA, GDPR) |
| **Cost-sensitive, homogeneous tenants** | Multi-tenancy: Shared DB, shared schema | Maximal density |
| **Real-time stream processing** | Apache Spark / Flink on managed clusters | Low-latency event handling |
| **Batch analytics on petabytes** | MapReduce on Hadoop / EMR | High throughput, fault tolerance |

---
